topics.title: Fundamentals of Thermodynamics Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT414 - BIOCHEMICAL THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals of Thermodynamics Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 🔬 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermodynamic System:** A precisely defined quantity of matter or a delimited region in space chosen for thermodynamic analysis, separated from the rest of the universe (called the **surroundings**) by a real or imaginary **boundary** (also termed the *envelope* or *control surface*). Everything external to this boundary constitutes the surroundings, and together, the system and surroundings form the **universe**.

In the context of **Biochemical Thermodynamics**, a system is typically defined to encompass the biological reactants, products, solvents (usually water at biological pH and temperature), and any apparatus (reactor, calorimeter, cell) under study. The choice of the system boundary is dictated by the phenomenon of interest — for example, a mitochondrion, a cell, a fermenter, or a living organism can each be modeled as a thermodynamic system.

### 🌟 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Aquarium Analogy:** Imagine a sealed glass aquarium sitting in your living room. The **aquarium (with water, fish, plants inside)** is your **system**. The **room (air, furniture, walls)** constitutes the **surroundings**. The **glass walls** are the **boundary**. If the glass is rigid and insulated, it is an **isolated system** — nothing crosses in or out. If the glass is rigid but not insulated, heat may pass through — making it a **closed system**. If the lid is open and air bubbles through, matter and energy exchange — making it an **open system**.

This simple mental model immediately clarifies why a biochemist studying a fermentation broth must decide *a priori* whether the bioreactor is open (continuously fed), closed (batch with no mass transfer but possible heat transfer), or isolated (sealed calorimeter with no exchanges).

## 🎯 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value (Bold for emphasis) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **R** | Universal gas constant | **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** |
| **g** | Standard gravity | **9.80665 m·s⁻²** |
| **Nₐ** | Avogadro's number | **6.02214076 × 10²³ mol⁻¹** |
| **T₀** | Reference temperature | **298.15 K (25 °C)** |
| **P₀** | Reference pressure | **101.325 kPa (1 atm)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The first module lays the foundation for all subsequent biochemical energetics. Mastery of system classification is *prerequisite* for understanding energy balances, entropy, free energy, and reaction spontaneity in the later modules.

## 🧩 Fundamental Components of a Thermodynamic Description

Every thermodynamic problem requires a complete specification of:

1. **The System** — *what* is being studied.
2. **The Boundary** — *where* the system ends and surroundings begin.
3. **The Surroundings** — *everything else* that may interact with the system.
4. **The State of the System** — the set of properties (T, P, V, n, U, H, S, G) that fully describe its macroscopic condition.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🔍 Classification of Thermodynamic Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme **PEBBT414** syllabus mandates a rigorous treatment of system types, as bioprocess engineering problems (fermentation, downstream processing, calorimetry) demand careful selection of the appropriate system model.

### 1. Open System (Control Volume)

- **Definition:** A system in which **both mass and energy** can cross the boundary.
- **Boundary characteristics:** Real, permeable, often a *control surface* enclosing a *control volume*.
- **Biochemical examples:** A continuous stirred-tank bioreactor (CSTR) with feed inlet and product outlet; a mammalian cell with nutrient uptake and waste excretion; a cross-flow membrane filtration unit.
- **Engineering utility:** The mass, momentum, and energy balances of biochemical engineering processes are almost always written for open systems because industrial bioreactors operate in continuous or fed-batch mode.

### 2. Closed System (Control Mass)

- **Definition:** A system in which **no mass crosses the boundary**, but energy (heat and work) may be exchanged.
- **Boundary characteristics:** Impermeable to matter, but thermally and/or mechanically interactive.
- **Biochemical examples:** A sealed batch bioreactor undergoing an exothermic enzymatic reaction; a bomb calorimeter measuring the heat of combustion of glucose; a closed reaction vial in an isothermal titration calorimeter (ITC).
- **Engineering utility:** Closed systems are mathematically simpler; the first law reduces to $\Delta U = Q - W$, making them ideal for foundational derivations and bench-scale measurements.

### 3. Isolated System

- **Definition:** A system in which **neither mass nor energy** crosses the boundary.
- **Boundary characteristics:** Rigid, impermeable, and adiabatic (perfectly insulating).
- **Biochemical examples:** A perfectly insulated Dewar calorimeter; the universe itself is the only truly isolated system.
- **Engineering utility:** Isolated systems allow conservation of total energy ($\Delta U_{total} = 0$), a powerful constraint used to compute final equilibrium states.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic Tip:** "**O**pen lets both cross; **C**losed is **C**ontent (no mass, but energetic); **I**solated is **I**mmune (nothing crosses)."

## 🌡️ Properties of a System

### Intensive Properties

- **Definition:** Properties **independent of the size or extent (mass) of the system**.
- **Examples:** Temperature ($T$), pressure ($P$), density ($\rho$), specific volume ($v$), molar concentration ($c$), chemical potential ($\mu$), refractive index.
- **Why it matters:** Two identical subsystems at equilibrium share the *same* intensive property values. This is the basis of the **Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics** and the operational definition of temperature.

### Extensive Properties

- **Definition:** Properties **proportional to the size or amount of matter** in the system.
- **Examples:** Total volume ($V$), total mass ($m$), total internal energy ($U$), total entropy ($S$), total enthalpy ($H$), Gibbs energy ($G$), number of moles ($n$).
- **Why it matters:** Extensive properties are additive across subsystems and are the natural variables in the first-law energy balance.

> [!TIP]
> **Conversion:** Any extensive property divided by mass (or moles) becomes an **intensive specific** (or **molar**) property. Example: $V \rightarrow v = V/m$ (specific volume, m³/kg) or $\bar{V} = V/n$ (molar volume, m³/mol).

## ⚖️ State, Process, Path, and Cycle

| Term | Rigorous Definition | Biochemical Context |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **State** | Condition of the system described by a set of independent properties. | The set of $(T, P, n_i, U, H, S, G, \mu_i)$ defining a biochemical reaction mixture. |
| **Process** | A change in state of the system from an initial to a final state. | Conversion of glucose + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O in cellular respiration. |
| **Path** | The specific sequence of states through which a system passes during a process. | An enzymatic pathway proceeding via enzyme-bound intermediates. |
| **Cycle** | A process whose initial and final states are identical. | A metabolic cycle such as the TCA (Krebs) cycle or the Calvin cycle. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **State Functions vs Path Functions:**
> - **State functions** (U, H, S, G): depend *only* on the state, not on how it was reached. $\oint dU = 0$.
> - **Path functions** (Q, W): depend on *how* the process is carried out. The First Law ties path functions to state-function changes.

## 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **definitive high-yield formula reference** for Module 1. These expressions are non-negotiable in any KTU 2024 Scheme ESE or internal assessment on this topic.

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Description | Units (SI) | Boundary / Validity Condition |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\Delta U = U_2 - U_1$ | Change in internal energy (state function) | J | All systems; exact differential |
| 2 | $\Delta U = Q - W$ | First Law for closed system (sign convention: W = work done *by* system) | J | Closed system, no mass flow |
| 3 | $Q = \Delta U + W$ | First Law (heat absorbed = internal energy gain + work done by system) | J | Closed system, no mass flow |
| 4 | $H \equiv U + PV$ | Definition of enthalpy | J | Any system at equilibrium |
| 5 | $\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta(PV)$ | Enthalpy change relation | J | Closed system |
| 6 | $C_p = \left(\dfrac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P$ | Heat capacity at constant pressure | J·K⁻¹ | Reversible processes |
| 7 | $C_v = \left(\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V$ | Heat capacity at constant volume | J·K⁻¹ | Reversible processes |
| 8 | $C_p - C_v = nR$ | Mayer's relation (ideal gas) | J·K⁻¹ | Ideal gas only |
| 9 | $\Delta E_{sys} = E_2 - E_1$ | Total energy change (kinetic + potential + internal) | J | Open or closed system |
| 10 | $\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \Delta \dot{H} + \Delta \dot{KE} + \Delta \dot{PE}$ | Steady-flow energy equation | W | Steady-state open system |
| 11 | $PV = nRT$ | Ideal gas equation of state | Pa·m³ | Ideal gas, low P, high T |
| 12 | $W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$ | Boundary (reversible) work | J | Quasi-static process |
| 13 | $W_{isothermal} = nRT \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$ | Isothermal reversible work (ideal gas) | J | Ideal gas, isothermal, reversible |

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign-Convention Pitfall:** In engineering thermodynamics (and KTU board answers), $W$ in $\Delta U = Q - W$ denotes **work done BY the system on the surroundings**. In physics/chemistry textbooks, the sign is often reversed: $\Delta U = Q + W$ (where $W$ is work done *on* the system). Mismatching the convention is the #1 cause of lost marks.

## 🧪 Real-World Biochemical Engineering Utility

The system concept is the gateway to:

- **Mass and energy balances** on bioreactors (Module 2–3 onward).
- **Calorimetric determination of metabolic heat** in microbial cultures.
- **Stability analysis** of pharmaceutical formulations (the system = drug + excipient, surroundings = packaging + environment).
- **Process control** in continuous bioprocesses, where the CSTR is the quintessential open system.
- **Cryopreservation modeling**, where the system is a cell suspension, the boundary is the freezing container, and the surroundings are the cryogenic bath.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🧮 Derivation 1: Boundary (Expansion) Work for a Closed System

### Physical Setup

Consider a gas enclosed in a cylinder fitted with a frictionless, movable piston of cross-sectional area $A$. The gas exerts pressure $P$ on the piston; when the piston moves a differential distance $dx$, the differential volume change is:

$$dV = A \, dx$$

### Step 1 — Elementary Work

The infinitesimal work done *by* the gas on the piston is force times displacement:

$$dW_{by} = F \, dx$$

### Step 2 — Express Force in Terms of Pressure

Since force is pressure times area ($F = PA$):

$$dW_{by} = (PA) \, dx = P \, (A \, dx) = P \, dV$$

> **Logic check:** If the gas expands ($dV > 0$), the work done by the gas is positive — consistent with our sign convention.

### Step 3 — Total Work Over a Finite Process

For a quasi-static (reversible) process from state 1 to state 2:

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

> **Validity condition:** This expression holds strictly for *reversible* processes, where the system pressure is well-defined and uniform, and the external pressure differs by an infinitesimal amount.

### Step 4 — Special Case: Isothermal Expansion of an Ideal Gas

Substitute the ideal gas law $P = \dfrac{nRT}{V}$ (with $T$ constant):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{nRT}{V} \, dV \\
&= nRT \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{dV}{V} \\
&= nRT \left[ \ln V \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} \\
&= nRT \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Using Boyle's law at constant $T$, $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$, this is equivalently:

$$W = nRT \ln\!\left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)$$

---

## 🧮 Derivation 2: First Law for a Closed System

### Statement

Energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it is only transformed.

### Step 1 — Define Total Energy

The total energy $E$ of a closed system comprises internal, kinetic, and potential contributions:

$$E = U + KE + PE = U + \frac{1}{2}mv^2 + mgz$$

### Step 2 — Energy Balance

The change in total energy equals net energy transferred as heat and work:

$$\Delta E = Q_{in} - W_{out}$$

### Step 3 — For a Stationary Closed System

If the system is stationary ($\Delta KE = 0$) and the elevation does not change ($\Delta PE = 0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta U + \Delta KE + \Delta PE &= Q - W \\
\Delta U + 0 + 0 &= Q - W \\
\Delta U &= Q - W
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the canonical form of the **First Law of Thermodynamics for a closed system** and the most-cited equation in the entire KTU 2024 PEBBT414 syllabus.

### Step 4 — Differential Form

For an infinitesimal change:

$$dU = \delta Q - \delta W$$

> **Notation caution:** $\delta Q$ and $\delta W$ are *inexact* differentials (path functions), while $dU$ is an *exact* differential (state function). This distinction is examinable.

---

## 🧮 Derivation 3: Enthalpy — Origin and Physical Meaning

### Step 1 — Start from a Constant-Pressure Process

For a closed, constant-pressure process, the boundary work is $W = P\Delta V$. Substituting into the first law:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta U &= Q_P - W \\
Q_P &= \Delta U + P \Delta V \\
Q_P &= (U_2 - U_1) + P(V_2 - V_1) \\
Q_P &= (U_2 + P_2 V_2) - (U_1 + P_1 V_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Identify the Grouping

Define the bracketed quantity as a new state function, **enthalpy** $H$:

$$H \equiv U + PV$$

### Step 3 — Final Result

For any constant-pressure process in a closed system (with only boundary work):

$$Q_P = \Delta H$$

> **Biochemical interpretation:** The heat absorbed or released by a biochemical reaction carried out in an open beaker (atmospheric pressure) at constant $T$ and $P$ equals the change in enthalpy. This is why $\Delta H$ is the natural "heat-of-reaction" descriptor in biochemistry tables.

---

## 🧮 Derivation 4: Mayer's Relation ($C_p - C_v = nR$)

### Step 1 — Enthalpy in Terms of U

$$H = U + PV = U + nRT \quad \text{(ideal gas)}$$

### Step 2 — Differentiate with Respect to T at Constant P

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P &= \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_P + nR \\
C_p &= \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_P + nR
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Use the Identity for an Ideal Gas

For an ideal gas, $U$ depends only on $T$ (Joule's law), so $\left(\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_P = \left(\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V = C_v$:

$$C_p = C_v + nR \quad \Longrightarrow \quad C_p - C_v = nR$$

> **Examination hint:** This relation is frequently asked as a 3- or 7-mark sub-question. KTU evaluators expect the explicit invocation of *Joule's law* to justify $\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_P = C_v$.

---

## 🧮 Derivation 5: Steady-Flow Energy Equation (Open System)

For a control volume operating at steady state (no accumulation of mass or energy), the rate form of the first law is:

$$\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \sum_{\text{out}} \dot{m}\!\left(h + \frac{v^2}{2} + gz\right) - \sum_{\text{in}} \dot{m}\!\left(h + \frac{v^2}{2} + gz\right)$$

For a single inlet and outlet with equal mass flow rates $\dot{m}$:

$$\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}\left[\Delta h + \frac{\Delta v^2}{2} + g\Delta z\right]$$

> **Biochemical application:** A continuous bioreactor with feed and harvest streams, neglecting kinetic and potential energy changes, gives $\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m} \Delta h$. When the reactor is well-mixed with no shaft work, this simplifies the heat-removal design calculation for exothermic fermentations.

---

## 📐 Analytical Matrix — Process Classification Summary

| Process Type | Defining Constraint | Work Expression (Ideal Gas) | Heat Expression (Ideal Gas) | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Isothermal** | $T = \text{const}$ | $W = nRT \ln(V_2/V_1)$ | $Q = W$ (since $\Delta U = 0$) | Slow enzymatic reaction in thermostat |
| **Isobaric** | $P = \text{const}$ | $W = P\Delta V = nR\Delta T$ | $Q_P = \Delta H = n C_p \Delta T$ | Reaction in open beaker at 1 atm |
| **Isochoric (Isovolumetric)** | $V = \text{const}$ | $W = 0$ | $Q_V = \Delta U = n C_v \Delta T$ | Bomb calorimeter combustion |
| **Adiabatic** | $Q = 0$ | $W = -\Delta U$ | $Q = 0$ | Rapid mixing in insulated vessel |
| **Cyclic** | $\Delta U_{\text{net}} = 0$ | $W_{\text{net}} = Q_{\text{net}}$ | $Q_{\text{net}} = W_{\text{net}}$ | Metabolic cycle (e.g., TCA) modeled abstractly |
| **Reversible (general)** | Quasi-static, frictionless | $W = \int P\,dV$ | $\delta Q_{rev} = T\,dS$ | Idealized limit case |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mastery of this table is essential:** KTU question setters in 2024–2025 have heavily favored these process classifications in both Part A (short definitions) and Part B (7-mark derivations).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 🗺️ Diagram 1: Hierarchical Classification of Thermodynamic Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["THERMODYNAMIC SYSTEM"] --> B["OPEN SYSTEM"]
    A --> C["CLOSED SYSTEM"]
    A --> D["ISOLATED SYSTEM"]

    B --> B1["Mass crosses boundary"]
    B --> B2["Energy crosses boundary"]
    B --> B3["Example: CSTR bioreactor"]

    C --> C1["No mass crosses"]
    C --> C2["Energy may cross"]
    C --> C3["Example: Batch fermenter, Bomb calorimeter"]

    D --> D1["No mass crosses"]
    D --> D2["No energy crosses"]
    D --> D3["Example: Dewar flask, Universe"]

    B1 --> E["CONTROL VOLUME APPROACH"]
    C1 --> F["CONTROL MASS APPROACH"]
    D1 --> G["CONSERVATION OF TOTAL ENERGY"]

    E --> H["Mass + Energy balances"]
    F --> I["Energy balance only"]
    G --> J["dU total equals 0"]
```

## 🔁 Diagram 2: System–Surroundings Interaction Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SYS["SYSTEM\n(Biochemical reactor)"]:::sys

    BND["BOUNDARY\n(Membrane, wall, control surface)"]:::bnd

    SUR["SURROUNDINGS\n(Bath, atmosphere, jacket)"]:::sur

    QIN["Heat Q in"]:::q
    QOUT["Heat Q out"]:::q
    WIN["Work W in"]:::w
    WOUT["Work W out"]:::w
    MIN["Mass m in"]:::m
    MOUT["Mass m out"]:::m

    SUR -->|exchanges| BND
    BND -->|exchanges| SYS

    SUR --> QIN
    QIN --> SYS
    SYS --> QOUT
    QOUT --> SUR

    SUR --> WIN
    WIN --> SYS
    SYS --> WOUT
    WOUT --> SUR

    SUR --> MIN
    MIN --> SYS
    SYS --> MOUT
    MOUT --> SUR

    classDef sys fill:#ffd9a8,stroke:#a8540c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bnd fill:#d6e0ff,stroke:#1f3a93,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sur fill:#d8f0d3,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef q fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef w fill:#e6f0ff,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef m fill:#f3e6ff,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

## ⚙️ Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture for Steady-State Mass & Energy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FEED["FEED STREAM\nMass flow rate m dot\nEnthalpy h_in\nVelocity v_in\nElevation z_in"]:::inlet

    CV["CONTROL VOLUME\nBioreactor or process unit\nOperating at STEADY STATE\ndE/dt equals 0\nMass accumulation equals 0"]:::cv

    PROD["PRODUCT STREAM\nMass flow rate m dot\nEnthalpy h_out\nVelocity v_out\nElevation z_out"]:::outlet

    QDOT["HEAT TRANSFER\nQ dot (kW)"]:::heat
    WDOT["SHAFT WORK\nW dot (kW)\nagitator, pump, compressor"]:::work

    FEED --> CV
    CV --> PROD
    QDOT --> CV
    CV --> WDOT

    CV --> BAL["ENERGY BALANCE\nQ dot minus W dot equals\nm dot times delta h\nplus KE change\nplus PE change"]:::balance

    classDef inlet fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outlet fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cv fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef heat fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef work fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef balance fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 🔄 Diagram 4: State Function vs Path Function — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ST1["STATE 1\nU1, H1, S1, G1"]:::st

    ST2["STATE 2\nU2, H2, S2, G2"]:::st

    P1["PATH A\n(reversible isothermal)"]:::path
    P2["PATH B\n(reversible adiabatic)"]:::path
    P3["PATH C\n(irreversible)"]:::path

    ST1 --> P1
    P1 --> ST2

    ST1 --> P2
    P2 --> ST2

    ST1 --> P3
    P3 --> ST2

    NOTE["State functions U, H, S, G:\nindependent of path\nPath functions Q, W:\ndepend on specific route"]:::note

    ST1 -.-> NOTE
    ST2 -.-> NOTE

    classDef st fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef path fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef note fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#424242,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading Guide for Diagrams:** Diagram 1 captures the *taxonomy* of systems (the KTU board's favorite first slide). Diagram 2 maps the *physical exchange* channels. Diagram 3 is the *engineer's block-flow* representation used in process design. Diagram 4 clarifies the subtle *state-vs-path* distinction that is worth 3–7 marks on its own in KTU papers.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a thermodynamic system. Distinguish between open, closed, and isolated systems with one bioprocess example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A thermodynamic system is a precisely specified region of the universe, separated from its surroundings by a real or imaginary boundary, on which thermodynamic analyses are performed.

| Type | Mass Exchange | Energy Exchange | Bioprocess Example |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Open system** | Yes | Yes | Continuous stirred-tank bioreactor (CSTR) with feed and harvest streams |
| **Closed system** | No | Yes | Batch bioreactor sealed after inoculation; bomb calorimeter |
| **Isolated system** | No | No | Perfectly insulated Dewar calorimeter containing a reacting sample |

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for correct distinction table, 1 mark for valid bioprocess examples.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**State the First Law of Thermodynamics for a closed system. Explain the physical significance of the sign convention $W = $ work done *by* the system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The First Law for a closed system states:

$$\Delta U = Q - W$$

where $Q$ is the heat absorbed *by* the system and $W$ is the work done *by* the system on the surroundings.

**Physical significance of the sign convention:** When the system does work on the surroundings (e.g., gas expansion lifting a piston), $W > 0$ and internal energy decreases. When the surroundings do work on the system (e.g., compression), $W < 0$ in this convention and internal energy increases. This convention is the standard adopted in engineering thermodynamics and is consistent with the I.T. sign convention followed in the KTU syllabus.

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> - **Sign convention confusion** is the single most common error. Always declare which convention you are using in the very first line of your answer.
> - **Distinguishing "heat" from "temperature"**: A system at high temperature does not necessarily *contain* high heat; heat is energy *in transit*.
> - **Forgetting units** in the final numerical answer costs a full mark. Always box your final answer with units (J, kJ, kW, etc.).
> - **Mixing intensive and extensive properties** in a single equation (e.g., adding $T$ and $U$) is a conceptual error worth 2 marks deduction.
> - **Stating "energy is conserved" without specifying the system boundary** is incomplete; conservation applies to *system + surroundings*.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Derive an expression for the boundary work done during the isothermal reversible expansion of an ideal gas from volume $V_1$ to $V_2$ at temperature $T$. Show all integration steps.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — General boundary work**

For a quasi-static reversible process, the work done by the gas on the surroundings is:

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

**Step 2 — Apply the ideal gas equation of state**

For $n$ moles of an ideal gas at temperature $T$:

$$P = \frac{nRT}{V}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the work integral**

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{nRT}{V} \, dV$$

**Step 4 — Since $T$ is constant for an isothermal process, factor it out:**

$$W = nRT \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{dV}{V}$$

**Step 5 — Perform the integration**

$$W = nRT \left[ \ln V \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} = nRT \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Alternative form using Boyle's Law**

At constant $T$, $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$, so $V_2/V_1 = P_1/P_2$:

$$W = nRT \ln\!\left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Numerical sanity check**

For an *expansion* ($V_2 > V_1$ or $P_1 > P_2$), $\ln(V_2/V_1) > 0$ and hence $W > 0$ — i.e., the gas *does* work on the surroundings. ✓

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**A 2.0 mol sample of an ideal gas expands isothermally and reversibly at 300 K from an initial volume of 5.0 L to a final volume of 15.0 L. Calculate (i) the work done by the gas, (ii) the heat absorbed by the gas, and (iii) the change in internal energy.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $n = 2.0$ mol, $T = 300$ K, $V_1 = 5.0$ L, $V_2 = 15.0$ L, $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹.

**Part (i) — Work done by the gas**

$$W = nRT \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= (2.0)(8.314)(300)\ln\!\left(\frac{15.0}{5.0}\right) \\
&= (4988.4)\ln(3.0) \\
&= (4988.4)(1.0986) \\
&\approx 5480 \text{ J} \approx \mathbf{5.48 \text{ kJ}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (ii) — Heat absorbed**

For an isothermal process involving an ideal gas, $\Delta U = 0$ (since $U$ depends only on $T$ for an ideal gas). From the first law:

$$\Delta U = Q - W \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0 = Q - W \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q = W$$

$$Q \approx \mathbf{5.48 \text{ kJ}}$$

**Part (iii) — Change in internal energy**

$$\Delta U = \mathbf{0 \text{ kJ}}$$

> **Physical interpretation:** All the heat absorbed by the gas is converted entirely into work done by the gas. The internal energy is unchanged because the temperature is constant.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define enthalpy. Starting from the first law of thermodynamics for a closed system at constant pressure, show that the heat transferred at constant pressure equals the change in enthalpy.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition**

$$H \equiv U + PV$$

**Step 2 — First Law for a closed system**

$$\Delta U = Q - W$$

**Step 3 — At constant pressure, the only work is boundary work $W = P\Delta V$:**

$$Q_P = \Delta U + P\Delta V$$

**Step 4 — Expand the right-hand side**

$$Q_P = (U_2 - U_1) + P(V_2 - V_1)$$

**Step 5 — Group terms by state**

$$Q_P = (U_2 + P_2 V_2) - (U_1 + P_1 V_1) = H_2 - H_1 = \Delta H$$

Hence, $\boxed{Q_P = \Delta H}$. ∎

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**5.0 mol of an ideal gas is heated at constant volume from 300 K to 500 K. If $C_v = \dfrac{3}{2}R$, calculate (i) the heat absorbed, (ii) the work done, and (iii) the change in enthalpy.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $n = 5.0$ mol, $V = $ constant, $T_1 = 300$ K, $T_2 = 500$ K, $C_v = \tfrac{3}{2}R$.

**Part (i) — Heat absorbed at constant volume**

For an isochoric process, $Q_V = n C_v \Delta T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_V &= (5.0)\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)(8.314)(500 - 300) \\
&= (5.0)(1.5)(8.314)(200) \\
&= \mathbf{12{,}471 \text{ J} \approx 12.47 \text{ kJ}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (ii) — Work done**

At constant volume, $dV = 0$, so:

$$W = \int P\,dV = \mathbf{0 \text{ J}}$$

**Part (iii) — Change in enthalpy**

For an ideal gas, $C_p = C_v + R = \tfrac{3}{2}R + R = \tfrac{5}{2}R$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta H &= n C_p \Delta T \\
&= (5.0)\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)(8.314)(200) \\
&= \mathbf{20{,}785 \text{ J} \approx 20.79 \text{ kJ}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification using $\Delta H = \Delta U + P\Delta V$:**

Since $V$ is constant, $P\Delta V = 0$, so $\Delta H = \Delta U = Q_V = 12.47$ kJ? ⚠️

> **Reconciliation:** Wait — $\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta(PV)$. For an ideal gas, $PV = nRT$, so $\Delta(PV) = nR\Delta T$ even at constant $V$! Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta H &= n C_v \Delta T + nR \Delta T = n(C_v + R)\Delta T = nC_p \Delta T \\
&= 20.79 \text{ kJ} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

This confirms Mayer's relation in action.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize Before Exam):**

- **System = universe − surroundings**; the boundary is the delineation.
- **Three system types** (open / closed / isolated) determined by *mass and energy permeability* — not by physical "openness" of the container.
- **Intensive properties** ($T$, $P$, $\mu$, $\rho$) are size-independent; **extensive properties** ($U$, $H$, $S$, $V$, $m$, $n$) are size-dependent and additive.
- **State functions** ($U, H, S, G, T, P, V$) → exact differentials → $\oint dX = 0$.
- **Path functions** ($Q, W$) → inexact differentials → depend on process route.
- **First Law (closed system):** $\Delta U = Q - W$ (engineering sign convention).
- **Enthalpy definition:** $H = U + PV$. At constant $P$, $Q_P = \Delta H$.
- **Mayer's relation:** $C_p - C_v = nR$ (ideal gas only).
- **Reversible boundary work:** $W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P\,dV$.
- **Isothermal reversible work (ideal gas):** $W = nRT \ln(V_2/V_1) = nRT \ln(P_1/P_2)$.
- **Joule's Law (ideal gas):** $U = U(T)$ only; $\Delta U = 0$ for isothermal processes.
- **Steady-state open system:** $\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}\Delta h + \dot{m}\Delta(v^2/2) + \dot{m}g\Delta z$.
- **Process identification:** Know by heart the four pillars — isothermal, isobaric, isochoric, adiabatic — and their respective $W$ and $Q$ expressions.
- **Zeroth Law** $\Rightarrow$ operational definition of temperature; prerequisite for the First, Second, and Third Laws.
- **Sign convention** — declare it explicitly; never mix physics ($W$ on system) and engineering ($W$ by system) conventions in the same answer.
- **Box the final answer** with correct SI units; examiners *will* deduct for missing units.
- **Biochemical relevance:** $Q_P = \Delta H$ justifies using tabulated standard enthalpies of formation ($\Delta_f H^\circ$) for reaction-heat calculations on biochemical systems at ambient conditions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Open System and Closed System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT414 - BIOCHEMICAL THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Open System and Closed System ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Foundational Definitions A **thermodynamic system** is a precisely defined region in space, containing a quantity of matter, that is selected for thermodynamic study. Everything external to this designated region is termed the **surroundings**. The real or imaginary surface that separates the system from its surroundings is the **system boundary**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The classification of systems is the *first* building block of Biochemical Thermodynamics. In living systems, almost all processes (cellular metabolism, fermentation, digestion) occur in **open systems** because mass, energy, and momentum cross the boundary continuously. ### 1.2 Three Pillars of System Classification | System Type | Mass Transfer? | Energy Transfer? | Biochemical Example | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- | | **Isolated System** | ❌ No | ❌ No | Thermally insulated sealed calorimeter | | **Closed System** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | Sealed bioreactor with heating jacket | | **Open System** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Living cell, continuous stirred-tank reactor (CSTR) | ### 1.3 Formal Definitions Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme > [!NOTE] > **Closed System (Control Mass):** A system in which **no mass** crosses the boundary, but **energy (heat and work)** may cross. The total mass of the system remains invariant with time, i.e., $m = \text{constant}$. > [!NOTE] > **Open System (Control Volume):** A system in which **both mass and energy** cross the boundary. The mass within the control volume may change with time. Open systems are essential for describing biochemical reactors, the human circulatory system, and microbial fermenters. ### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy **Closed System = Sealed Pressure Cooker** 🫙 Imagine a sealed pressure cooker on your stove. You can heat it (energy enters) and the gas pushes the safety valve (work exits), but no water vapor escapes — the mass of food and water inside is fixed. That is a **closed system**. **Open System = Flowing River** 🌊 Now imagine a section of a river. Water flows in from upstream (mass in), water flows out downstream (mass out), sunlight heats the surface (energy in), and evaporation occurs (mass and energy out). The amount of water *inside* your imaginary river segment is not constant. That is an **open system**. **Biochemical Intuition:** A human liver cell is an **open system** — glucose, oxygen, and water enter; carbon dioxide, urea, and heat leave. A sealed test tube of an enzyme solution undergoing an isothermal batch reaction behaves like a **closed system**. ### 1.5 Key Physical Quantities - **Mass of system ($m$):** measured in **kg** (SI base unit) - **Molar mass ($M$):** measured in **kg·kmol⁻¹** - **Universal gas constant ($R$):** $\mathbf{8.314 \text{ J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}}$ - **Standard pressure ($P^{\circ}$):** $\mathbf{101.325 \text{ kPa}}$ - **Standard temperature ($T^{\circ}$):** $\mathbf{298.15 \text{ K}}$ (25 °C) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Closed System — Operational Logic **Operational Steps to Identify a Closed System:** 1. **Draw the boundary** around the chosen region of matter. 2. **Probe all boundary surfaces** for mass crossing. If zero mass crosses over the time interval, it is a *closed* system. 3. **Probe for energy crossing** — heat ($Q$) and/or work ($W$) may cross. 4. **Apply the First Law for a closed system:** $$dU = \delta Q - \delta W$$ (where $U$ is the total internal energy, $\delta Q$ is the infinitesimal heat added, $\delta W$ is the infinitesimal work done *by* the system). 5. **State the invariance condition:** $m_1 = m_2 = \text{constant}$ (mass conservation inside the boundary). ### 2.2 Open System — Operational Logic **Operational Steps to Identify an Open System:** 1. **Define a control volume (CV)** — a fixed region in space (not a fixed mass of matter). 2. **Identify inlet and outlet streams** crossing the control surface (CS). 3. **Confirm that both mass and energy are exchanged** with the surroundings. 4. **Apply conservation of mass** to the CV: $$\frac{dm_{CV}}{dt} = \sum \dot{m}_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out}$$ 5. **Apply the First Law for an open system** (the energy balance equation, also called the **General Energy Equation**): $$\frac{dE_{CV}}{dt} = \dot{Q} - \dot{W} + \sum \dot{m}_{in}\left(h + \frac{v^2}{2} + gz\right)_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out}\left(h + \frac{v^2}{2} + gz\right)_{out}$$ where $h$ is specific enthalpy, $v$ is velocity, $g$ is gravitational acceleration, and $z$ is elevation. ### 2.3 Why This Matters — Engineering Utility - **Biochemical Reactors (CSTR):** Designed as open systems; substrate enters, product exits, mass varies. - **Pharmaceutical Sterilization:** Batch autoclaves are modeled as closed systems. - **Metabolic Engineering:** Cellular metabolism is an open system; flux balance analysis (FBA) uses the steady-state mass balance. - **HVAC of Clean Rooms:** Treated as open systems to control air composition, temperature, and humidity. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula Name | Equation | Unit / Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Mass conservation (general) | $\frac{dm_{CV}}{dt} = \sum \dot{m}_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out}$ | kg·s⁻¹ | | 2 | Steady-state mass balance | $\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}$ | applies when $\frac{dm_{CV}}{dt}=0$ | | 3 | Closed-system First Law | $dU = \delta Q - \delta W$ | J (extensive) | | 4 | Closed-system enthalpy form | $dH = \delta Q - V\,dP$ | J | | 5 | Open-system First Law (general) | $\frac{dE_{CV}}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{CV} - \dot{W}_{CV} + \sum \dot{m}_{i}(h_i+\frac{v_i^2}{2}+gz_i) - \sum \dot{m}_{e}(h_e+\frac{v_e^2}{2}+gz_e)$ | W (rate form) | | 6 | Steady-flow energy equation (SFEE) | $\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}\left[(h_2-h_1) + \frac{v_2^2-v_1^2}{2} + g(z_2-z_1)\right]$ | W | | 7 | Volumetric flow–molar flow link | $\dot{n} = \dot{m}/M$ | kmol·s⁻¹ | | 8 | Density–specific volume | $\rho = 1/v$ | kg·m⁻³ | | 9 | Boundary heat transfer | $\dot{Q} = U\,A\,\Delta T_{LMTD}$ | W | | 10 | Mechanical work rate (pump/compressor) | $\dot{W}_{shaft} = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)$ | W (adiabatic turbine) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memory Trick (KTU):** *"Closed = fixed Mass"* and *"Open = fixed Volume"*. Always ask: *What stays constant — the contents, or the boundary?* ### 2.5 Special Case — Steady-State Open System A **steady-state** open system has *time-invariant* properties at every point inside the CV. This does **not** mean nothing is happening — matter and energy flow through continuously — but local state variables ($T$, $P$, $\rho$, $u$, $h$) at any fixed location do not change with time. $$\left.\frac{dE_{CV}}{dt}\right|_{steady} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{Q}_{CV} - \dot{W}_{CV} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}\left(h+\frac{v^2}{2}+gz\right)_{out} - \sum \dot{m}_{in}\left(h+\frac{v^2}{2}+gz\right)_{in}$$ ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — General Mass Balance for an Open System We begin by defining a control volume $CV$ of arbitrary shape fixed in space. Let $\rho(\vec{r},t)$ be the local mass density. **Step 1 — Mass inside the CV at time $t$:* $$ m_{CV}(t) = \int_{CV} \rho(\vec{r},t)\, dV $$ **Step 2 — Rate of change of mass inside the CV (substantial derivative form): $$ \frac{dm_{CV}}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\int_{CV} \rho\, dV $$ **Step 3 — Apply the Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT) to convert the system (Lagrangian) derivative into a control-volume (Eulerian) form: $$ \frac{dm_{CV}}{dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \rho\, dV + \int_{CS} \rho(\vec{v}\cdot \hat{n})\, dA $$ **Step 4 — Interpret the two terms physically: - $\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \rho\, dV$ = rate of accumulation of mass within the CV. - $\int_{CS} \rho(\vec{v}\cdot \hat{n})\, dA$ = net rate of efflux of mass across the control surface. **Step 5 — Rewrite the surface integral as the algebraic difference between outlet and inlet flows: $$ \frac{dm_{CV}}{dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \rho\, dV + \sum \dot{m}_{out} - \sum \dot{m}_{in} $$ **Step 6 — Final mass balance for an open system: $$ \boxed{\frac{dm_{CV}}{dt} = \sum \dot{m}_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out}} $$ **Logical interpretation:** If mass enters faster than it leaves, $dm_{CV}/dt > 0$ (system is filling up). If it leaves faster than it enters, $dm_{CV}/dt < 0$ (system is draining). If they are equal, $dm_{CV}/dt = 0$ → **steady state**. --- ### 3.2 Derivation — Closed-System First Law (Energy Conservation) For a closed system, the boundary may move, but no mass crosses it. The first law states that the change in total energy equals the net energy transfer across the boundary. **Step 1 — Total energy of the closed system: $$ E = U + KE + PE = mu + \frac{1}{2}mv^2 + mgz $$ **Step 2 — Energy crossing the boundary can occur in two ways: as heat ($\delta Q$) or as work ($\delta W$). **Step 3 — By the **principle of energy conservation**: $$ dE = \delta Q - \delta W $$ **Step 4 — For a **stationary** closed system with negligible changes in macroscopic kinetic and potential energy, $dE = dU$: $$ \boxed{dU = \delta Q - \delta W} $$ **Step 5 — Use the sign convention adopted by KTU textbook (Cengel & Boles): $\delta Q$ is positive when heat is added *to* the system; $\delta W$ is positive when work is done *by* the system. **Step 6 — Express for a finite process from state 1 to state 2: $$ \Delta U = Q - W $$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation — Steady-Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) for Open Systems The SFEE is the workhorse for analyzing biochemical reactors, pumps, compressors, and heat exchangers. **Step 1 — Start from the general open-system energy balance: $$ \frac{dE_{CV}}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{CV} - \dot{W}_{CV} + \sum \dot{m}_{in}\left(h+\frac{v^2}{2}+gz\right)_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out}\left(h+\frac{v^2}{2}+gz\right)_{out} $$ **Step 2 — Apply **steady-state** condition: $\frac{dE_{CV}}{dt} = 0$. **Step 3 — Consider a single inlet (1) and single outlet (2), with $\dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_2 = \dot{m}$. **Step 4 — After eliminating the zero term: $$ 0 = \dot{Q}_{CV} - \dot{W}_{CV} + \dot{m}\left(h_1 + \frac{v_1^2}{2} + gz_1\right) - \dot{m}\left(h_2 + \frac{v_2^2}{2} + gz_2\right) $$ **Step 5 — Rearrange to obtain the classical SFEE: $$ \boxed{\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}\left[(h_2 - h_1) + \frac{v_2^2 - v_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)\right]} $$ **Step 6 — Per unit mass form (divide by $\dot{m}$): $$ q - w = (h_2 - h_1) + \frac{v_2^2 - v_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1) $$ --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style) > **Problem:** A continuous bioreactor receives a glucose feed at $0.5 \text{ kg·s}^{-1}$ and discharges effluent at the same mass flow rate at steady state. The inlet specific enthalpy is $h_1 = 250 \text{ kJ·kg}^{-1}$ and outlet is $h_2 = 180 \text{ kJ·kg}^{-1}$. The reactor loses heat to the surroundings at $25 \text{ kW}$ and a stirrer does $5 \text{ kW}$ of work on the fluid. Kinetic and potential energy changes are negligible. Calculate the net energy balance. **Solution (step-by-step):** Given: $\dot{m} = 0.5 \text{ kg·s}^{-1}$, $h_1 = 250 \text{ kJ·kg}^{-1}$, $h_2 = 180 \text{ kJ·kg}^{-1}$, $\dot{Q} = -25 \text{ kW}$ (loss), $\dot{W} = -5 \text{ kW}$ (work *on* system is negative in the Cengel convention). Apply SFEE: $$ \dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1) $$ Substitute: $$ (-25) - (-5) = 0.5 \times (180 - 250) $$ $$ -25 + 5 = 0.5 \times (-70) $$ $$ -20 = -35 \text{ kW} $$ The arithmetic is **inconsistent** — the system is *not* at steady state under these conditions. To achieve steady state, either $\dot{m}$ must change, or additional heat must be supplied. This illustrates how SFEE is used to diagnose real reactor behavior. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Comparison of System Types ```mermaid flowchart LR A0[Thermodynamic System] --> B0{Does MASS cross
the boundary?} B0 -- No --> C0{Does ENERGY cross
the boundary?} B0 -- Yes --> O0[OPEN SYSTEM
Control Volume] C0 -- No --> I0[ISOLATED SYSTEM
No interaction] C0 -- Yes --> CL0[CLOSED SYSTEM
Control Mass] O0 --> O1[Mass IN] O0 --> O2[Mass OUT] O0 --> O3[Energy IN/OUT] CL0 --> CL1[Energy IN/OUT] CL0 --> CL2[No Mass Transfer] I0 --> I1[No Mass] I0 --> I2[No Energy] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Open System Mass & Energy Flows (Bioreactor) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SURR[Surroundings] QIN[Heat Q_in] WIN[Shaft Work W_in] end subgraph CV[Control Volume: Bioreactor] MIX[Reaction Mixture
m_CV, T, P] end subgraph STREAMS[Process Streams] FIN[Inlet Stream 1
substrate] FIN2[Inlet Stream 2
air O2] FOUT[Outlet Stream
product + CO2] end FIN --> CV FIN2 --> CV CV --> FOUT QIN --> CV WIN --> CV CV --> QOUT[Heat Q_out to jacket] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Architecture — Closed System (Batch Reactor) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CS[Closed System: Sealed Batch Reactor] RX[Reaction Mass
m = constant] end H1[Q_in via heating coil] --> CS CS --> H2[W_out via stirrer shaft] CS --> H3[No mass leaves
No mass enters] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** In every open-system diagram, look for *arrows crossing the boundary*. If mass arrows cross, it is open. If only energy arrows (heat/work) cross, it is closed. No arrows at all = isolated. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** *Distinguish between an open system and a closed system with one biochemical example for each. (3 marks)* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Open System | Closed System | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Mass transfer across boundary | Yes | No | | Energy transfer across boundary | Yes | Yes | | Also known as | Control volume | Control mass | | Biochemical example | Continuous stirred-tank bioreactor (CSTR) producing ethanol | Sealed test tube of enzyme undergoing a batch reaction | *(1 mark for each correct difference + 1 mark for examples = 3 marks)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State the general mass balance equation for an open system. Under what condition does it reduce to a steady-state mass balance? (3 marks)* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The general mass balance for a control volume is: $$ \frac{dm_{CV}}{dt} = \sum \dot{m}_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out} $$ It reduces to the steady-state mass balance, $$ \sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out} $$ when the **rate of accumulation of mass inside the control volume is zero**, i.e., $dm_{CV}/dt = 0$. This means the mass inside the CV is invariant with time, although mass is still flowing through. *(1 mark for general equation, 1 mark for steady-state form, 1 mark for condition = 3 marks)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **ESE Module 1 — Internal Choice Pattern (per KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]* **Q.A (a)** Define an open system. Derive the general mass balance equation for a control volume using the Reynolds Transport Theorem. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution — Part (a):** 1. **Definition (2 marks):** An open system, also called a *control volume*, is a region in space chosen for study across whose boundary both mass and energy may flow. The mass contained within the control volume can change with time. 2. **Reynolds Transport Theorem statement (1 mark):** The RTT relates the time rate of change of an extensive property of a system to the rate of change within a control volume plus the net efflux of that property across the control surface. 3. **Application to mass (4 marks):** Mass inside the CV is the extensive property $N_{sys} = m_{CV}(t) = \int_{CV}\rho\, dV$. Applying RTT, $$ \frac{dm_{CV}}{dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV}\rho\, dV + \int_{CS}\rho(\vec{v}\cdot\hat{n})\, dA $$ The first term on the right is the rate of accumulation of mass inside the CV; the second term is the net rate at which mass leaves the CV. Rewriting the surface integral as the difference between outlet and inlet mass flow rates gives the final result: $$ \frac{dm_{CV}}{dt} = \sum \dot{m}_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out} $$ --- **Q.A (b)** A pharmaceutical batch reactor initially contains $50 \text{ kg}$ of a drug–solvent mixture. During a 30-minute isothermal process, the solvent evaporates at a constant rate of $0.02 \text{ kg·min}^{-1}$ but no mass is added. Is this an open or closed system? Justify using the mass balance. Calculate the final mass of mixture. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution — Part (b):** 1. **System identification (2 marks):** This is an **open system** (not closed) because mass *is* crossing the boundary — the solvent vapor leaves the reactor even though no liquid is added. If we treat the liquid phase alone, mass is leaving; if we treat the entire reactor contents (liquid + vapor), mass is also leaving. Hence the boundary is being crossed by mass. 2. **Mass balance (2 marks):** Apply $\frac{dm_{CV}}{dt} = \sum \dot{m}_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out}$. Here, $\dot{m}_{in} = 0$ and $\dot{m}_{out} = 0.02 \text{ kg·min}^{-1}$. $$ \frac{dm_{CV}}{dt} = 0 - 0.02 = -0.02 \text{ kg·min}^{-1} $$ 3. **Final mass calculation (3 marks):** Over a time interval $\Delta t = 30 \text{ min}$, $$ m_2 = m_1 + \frac{dm_{CV}}{dt}\cdot\Delta t = 50 + (-0.02)(30) = 50 - 0.6 = 49.4 \text{ kg} $$ The final mass of the mixture in the reactor is **49.4 kg**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]* **Q.B (a)** State the first law of thermodynamics for (i) a closed system and (ii) an open system. Derive the steady-flow energy equation (SFEE) from the general open-system energy balance. State two assumptions used. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution — Part (a):** 1. **First Law — Closed System (1.5 marks):** $$ dU = \delta Q - \delta W $$ For a finite process: $\Delta U = Q - W$. 2. **First Law — Open System (1.5 marks):** The rate form of the first law for a control volume is: $$ \frac{dE_{CV}}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{CV} - \dot{W}_{CV} + \sum \dot{m}_{in}\left(h+\frac{v^2}{2}+gz\right)_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out}\left(h+\frac{v^2}{2}+gz\right)_{out} $$ 3. **Derivation of SFEE (3 marks):** Apply steady-state ($\frac{dE_{CV}}{dt}=0$) and a single inlet/single outlet with equal mass flow rate $\dot{m}$. The equation reduces to: $$ \dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}\left[(h_2 - h_1) + \frac{v_2^2 - v_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)\right] $$ 4. **Assumptions (1 mark):** (i) Steady-state operation, (ii) single inlet and single outlet, (iii) uniform properties at each cross-section, (iv) negligible changes in kinetic and potential energy (often invoked for low-velocity reactors). --- **Q.B (b)** A continuous sterilizer in a biopharma plant processes milk at a rate of $2 \text{ kg·s}^{-1}$. The milk enters at $h_1 = 105 \text{ kJ·kg}^{-1}$ and leaves at $h_2 = 320 \text{ kJ·kg}^{-1}$. The unit is well insulated ($\dot{Q} \approx 0$) and a pump consumes $15 \text{ kW}$ of electrical power. Calculate the net heat that must be added by the steam jacket to achieve this enthalpy rise. Use the SFEE. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Given:** - $\dot{m} = 2 \text{ kg·s}^{-1}$ - $h_1 = 105 \text{ kJ·kg}^{-1}$, $h_2 = 320 \text{ kJ·kg}^{-1}$ - $\dot{Q} = 0$ (insulated) → but steam jacket supplies $\dot{Q}_{jacket}$ to be found - $\dot{W} = -15 \text{ kW}$ (work *input* to fluid is negative in Cengel convention; or stated as $\dot{W}_{shaft} = +15$ kW *into* system). **Apply SFEE (5 marks):** $$ \dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1) $$ Substituting values (in kW and kJ·kg⁻¹): $$ \dot{Q} - (-15) = 2 \times (320 - 105) $$ $$ \dot{Q} + 15 = 2 \times 215 = 430 \text{ kW} $$ $$ \dot{Q} = 430 - 15 = 415 \text{ kW} $$ **Conclusion (2 marks):** The steam jacket must supply **415 kW** of heat to raise the milk enthalpy and overcome pump losses. The energy required to heat the milk alone is $2 \times 215 = 430$ kW, of which 15 kW is supplied as shaft work and the rest as heat. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:** > 1. **Sign convention mix-up:** Students frequently lose 2–3 marks by mixing the Cengel and IUPAC sign conventions. KTU follows Cengel: $Q$ *into* the system is positive, $W$ *done by* the system is positive. > 2. **Forgetting units in SFEE:** If $\dot{m}$ is in kg·s⁻¹ and $h$ is in kJ·kg⁻¹, the result $\dot{m}\Delta h$ is automatically in kW — **no conversion needed**. Mixing units (e.g., kJ and J) costs 1 mark. > 3. **Misclassifying batch systems:** A *sealed* batch reactor with no evaporation **is** a closed system, even if it is stirred. Stirring transfers work, not mass. Many students incorrectly mark this as open. > 4. **Steady-state ≠ Equilibrium:** Steady state means *time-invariant properties*, not *thermodynamic equilibrium*. This distinction is regularly tested in 14-mark problems. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀 - ⭐ A **closed system** has fixed mass but may exchange energy as heat and work — the First Law reads $dU = \delta Q - \delta W$. - ⭐ An **open system** has a fixed *boundary in space* (control volume) through which mass and energy flow continuously — the First Law uses the SFEE in rate form. - ⭐ The **mass balance** $\frac{dm_{CV}}{dt} = \sum \dot{m}_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out}$ is the cornerstone of every open-system analysis. - ⭐ The **steady-state** assumption simplifies the mass and energy balances to algebraic equations and is valid when $dm_{CV}/dt = 0$ and $dE_{CV}/dt = 0$. - ⭐ In **biochemical engineering**, living cells, fermenters, and the human body are all modeled as **open systems**; sealed calorimeters and batch reactors are **closed systems**. - ⭐ **Sign convention (Cengel):** $Q_{in} > 0$, $W_{by\,system} > 0$, work *on* the system (e.g., pumps) is negative. - ⭐ The SFEE reduces to $\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}\Delta h$ when kinetic and potential energy changes are negligible — the most commonly used form in KTU numerical problems. - ⭐ The **Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT)** is the mathematical bridge that converts a *closed-system* (Lagrangian) derivative into an *open-system* (Eulerian) form. - ⭐ **Isolated ≠ closed**: an isolated system exchanges *neither* mass *nor* energy, the most restrictive classification. - ⭐ Memorize the units: $\dot{m}$ in kg·s⁻¹, $h$ in kJ·kg⁻¹, $\dot{Q}$ and $\dot{W}$ in kW — consistency eliminates arithmetic errors.

topics.title: State and Path Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT414 - BIOCHEMICAL THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** State and Path Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a State Function

A **State Function** (also called a *point function* or *thermodynamic property of state*) is a macroscopic property of a system whose value depends **exclusively** on the current state (i.e., the specific values of state variables like $P$, $V$, $T$, $n$, composition) of the system — and **not** on the path or history by which that state was reached.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Essence):**
> A state function is a property whose change between two equilibrium states is determined solely by the initial and final states, regardless of the process path connecting them. In mathematical terms, $\oint dF = 0$ for any closed cycle.

### 1.2 Formal Definition of a Path Function

A **Path Function** is a thermodynamic quantity whose magnitude depends on the **specific sequence of intermediate states** (the path) traversed by the system between the initial and final states. Path functions are defined only for a *process*, never for a *state*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Core Highlight:** Work ($W$) and Heat ($Q$) are the two principal path functions in classical thermodynamics. The infinitesimal amount is denoted $\delta W$ and $\delta Q$ (NOT $dW$ or $dQ$) to emphasize that they are *inexact differentials*.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Mountain Trek

Imagine you are trekking from **Base Camp** (initial state) to **Mountain Peak** (final state).

- The **altitude** of Base Camp is fixed, say **1500 m**, and the altitude of the Peak is fixed at **3000 m**. The *change in altitude* $\Delta h = 3000 - 1500 = 1500\text{ m}$, irrespective of which trail (zigzag, steep, switchback) you took. The altitude itself is a **state function**.

- The **distance walked** along the trail, however, depends entirely on the path chosen. A direct steep climb might be 5 km, while a winding switchback trail could be 12 km. The distance is a **path function**.

| Trekking Quantity | Thermodynamic Analogue | Type |
|---|---|---|
| Altitude | Internal Energy $U$ | State Function |
| Distance walked | Work $W$ | Path Function |
| Energy spent (calories) | Heat $Q$ | Path Function |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Exams:** *"State functions are SNAP — Solves Numerous Analytical Problems"* — mnemonic for: System, Number of moles, Anything path-independent. Path functions are simply $Q$ (heat) and $W$ (work).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Framework: Exact vs. Inexact Differentials

The mathematical distinction between the two is rooted in the concept of **exactness of differentials** in multivariable calculus.

#### 2.1.1 State Function — Exact Differential
For a function $F(x, y)$, the total differential is:

$$dF = \left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial x}\right)_y dx + \left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial y}\right)_x dy$$

This is an **exact differential** if the **Euler reciprocity (cross-derivative) condition** is satisfied:

$$\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial x}\right)_y\right]_x = \left[\frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial y}\right)_x\right]_x$$

**Consequence:** The line integral $\int_{A}^{B} dF = F(B) - F(A)$ depends **only** on the endpoints.

#### 2.1.2 Path Function — Inexact Differential
For an inexact differential $\delta F$ (written as $\delta$ or $\dbar$), the cross-derivative condition **fails**:

$$\left[\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right]_x \neq \left[\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right]_y$$

where $\delta F = M \, dx + N \, dy$.

**Consequence:** The integral $\int_{A}^{B} \delta F$ depends on the specific path, and a unique value of $F$ at any state cannot be defined.

### 2.2 Cyclic Property of State Functions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cyclic Theorem:** For *any* closed cycle (initial state = final state), the change in a state function is **zero**.
> $$\oint dF = 0 \quad \text{(State function)}$$
> $$\oint \delta F \neq 0 \quad \text{(Path function, in general)}$$

This is the **defining test** KTU examiners use to identify state functions in numerical problems.

### 2.3 Additivity of State Function Differentials

If $z$ and $w$ are state functions, then $F = z + w$ is also a state function, and the differentials add directly:

$$dF = dz + dw$$

This is **NOT** true for path functions. Heat and work in different paths cannot be added simply as $\delta Q + \delta W$ to give a meaningful state function — except for the **First Law** statement $dU = \delta Q + \delta W$, which defines $U$ as a state function even though $Q$ and $W$ individually are not.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Symbol | Type | Differential Notation | Cyclic Integral | Property |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Internal Energy | $U$ | **State** | $dU$ (exact) | $\oint dU = 0$ | Path-independent |
| Enthalpy | $H$ | **State** | $dH$ (exact) | $\oint dH = 0$ | $H = U + PV$ |
| Entropy | $S$ | **State** | $dS$ (exact) | $\oint dS \geq 0$ | Increases in isolated systems |
| Gibbs Free Energy | $G$ | **State** | $dG$ (exact) | $\oint dG = 0$ | $G = H - TS$ |
| Helmholtz Free Energy | $A$ | **State** | $dA$ (exact) | $\oint dA = 0$ | $A = U - TS$ |
| Temperature | $T$ | **State** | $dT$ (exact) | $\oint dT = 0$ | Intensive |
| Pressure | $P$ | **State** | $dP$ (exact) | $\oint dP = 0$ | Intensive |
| Volume | $V$ | **State** | $dV$ (exact) | $\oint dV = 0$ | Extensive |
| Heat | $Q$ | **Path** | $\delta Q$ (inexact) | $\oint \delta Q \neq 0$ | Mode of energy transfer |
| Work | $W$ | **Path** | $\delta W$ (inexact) | $\oint \delta W \neq 0$ | Mode of energy transfer |

> [!TIP]
> **Notation Mandate for KTU Exams:** Always write $dU$, $dH$, $dS$ (straight $d$) for state functions, and $\delta Q$, $\delta W$ (slashed $\delta$ or $\dbar$) for path functions. Examiners allocate marks specifically for correct notation.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Biochemical Engineering

In biochemical thermodynamics, the state-function concept is the foundation for calculating **Gibbs free energy changes** of metabolic reactions, which determine spontaneity:

$$\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$$

Since $G$, $H$, $S$, and $T$ are all state functions, $\Delta G$ for glycolysis (glucose $\to$ pyruvate) can be tabulated from standard-state values **without** knowing the complex enzymatic pathway mechanism. This is the basis of **bioenergetics tables** used in metabolic engineering.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Exact-Differential Criterion Applied to Internal Energy

We derive the formal mathematical criterion that proves $U$ is a state function. Consider $U$ as a function of any two independent state variables, say $T$ and $V$:

$$U = U(T, V)$$

**Step 1:** Write the total differential of $U$:

$$dU = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V dT + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T dV$$

**Step 2:** Identify the partial derivatives with measurable quantities. From the First Law for a constant-volume process ($\delta W = 0$, so $\delta Q = dU$), and defining heat capacity at constant volume $C_V$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V = C_V$$

**Step 3:** Apply the Euler reciprocity condition for exactness:

$$\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial V}\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V\right]_T = \left[\frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T\right]_V$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $C_V$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial C_V}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left[\frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T\right]_V$$

**Step 5:** Since the left-hand side is experimentally verifiable and the right-hand side is well-defined, $U(T, V)$ is single-valued, confirming $U$ is a state function with an **exact differential** $dU$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Explanatory Note on Step 3:** The Euler reciprocity condition is the *mathematical fingerprint* of a state function. If it is satisfied, the integral over any closed path is zero. If it is not satisfied, the function's "value" depends on the route taken — a hallmark of path dependence.

### 3.2 Derivation: Why Work is an Inexact Differential

Consider a gas expanding from volume $V_1$ to $V_2$.

**Step 1:** The pressure-volume work element is:

$$\delta W = -P_{ext} \, dV$$

**Step 2:** Total work for a finite process is:

$$W = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} P_{ext} \, dV$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate for **Path A (Isothermal Reversible Expansion of Ideal Gas):**

$$P_{ext} = \frac{nRT}{V}$$

$$W_A = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{nRT}{V} \, dV = -nRT \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate for **Path B (Expansion against constant external pressure $P_0$):**

$$W_B = -P_0 (V_2 - V_1)$$

**Step 5:** Comparison. Both paths connect the **same** $(V_1, V_2)$ states, yet:

$$W_A \neq W_B \quad \text{in general}$$

**Step 6:** Conclusion. Since the work magnitude depends on the *path*, $W$ is a **path function** and $\delta W$ is an **inexact differential**. No unique value of "work" can be assigned to a state; it is defined only for a process.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: State vs. Path Function Decision Table

| Test/Criterion | State Function $F$ | Path Function $F$ |
|---|---|---|
| Differential type | Exact ($dF$) | Inexact ($\delta F$) |
| Cyclic integral | $\oint dF = 0$ always | $\oint \delta F \neq 0$ generally |
| Value at a state | Defined | **Not** defined |
| Path reversibility independence | Yes — same $\Delta F$ for all paths | No — magnitude changes with path |
| Euler condition | $\frac{\partial M}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial N}{\partial x}$ | Fails |
| Examples in biochem | $U, H, S, G, A, T, P, V$ | $Q, W$ |
| Significance in First Law | $dU$ appears with $\delta Q + \delta W$ | $\delta Q, \delta W$ are path contributions |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: State vs Path Function Classifier

The following diagram presents a Mermaid **flowchart** that classifies any thermodynamic quantity as a state function or path function based on the cyclic-integral test and the exact-differential test.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Thermodynamic Quantity F]) --> q1{Is F defined at a single equilibrium state?}
    q1 -- Yes --> branchState[State Function Branch]
    q1 -- No --> branchPath[Path Function Branch]

    branchState --> s1[Check exactness of dF]
    s1 --> s2{Euler reciprocity satisfied?}
    s2 -- Yes --> s3[CONFIRMED STATE FUNCTION]
    s2 -- No --> s4[Re-examine variable dependence]
    s4 --> s1

    s3 --> s5[Examples: U, H, S, G, A, T, P, V]
    s5 --> s6[Cyclic integral equals zero]
    s6 --> s7[Path-independent delta F]

    branchPath --> p1[Defined only for a process]
    p1 --> p2[Symbol uses delta-bar notation]
    p2 --> p3[CONFIRMED PATH FUNCTION]
    p3 --> p4[Examples: Q and W]
    p4 --> p5[Cyclic integral nonzero in general]
    p5 --> p6[Path-dependent magnitude]

    s7 --> outcome([Result: Process-independent value of F])
    p6 --> outcome2([Result: Process-specific magnitude of F])
```

### 4.2 Schematic: Conceptual Map of State and Path Functions in Biochemical Thermodynamics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sys[Biochemical System at State 1: P1, T1, V1] --> proc[Process / Pathway]
    proc --> sys2[System at State 2: P2, T2, V2]

    sys --> sf1[Internal Energy U1]
    sys --> sf2[Enthalpy H1]
    sys --> sf3[Gibbs Energy G1]
    sys2 --> sf1b[Internal Energy U2]
    sys2 --> sf2b[Enthalpy H2]
    sys2 --> sf3b[Gibbs Energy G2]

    sf1 --> diffU[Delta U = U2 minus U1]
    sf2 --> diffH[Delta H = H2 minus H1]
    sf3 --> diffG[Delta G = G2 minus G1]

    proc --> pf1[Heat Q transferred]
    proc --> pf2[Work W done]
    pf1 --> dep1[Magnitude depends on path]
    pf2 --> dep2[Magnitude depends on path]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The left column shows that $U$, $H$, $G$ are properties of a *state* (the boxes), while the right column shows that $Q$ and $W$ are properties of a *process* (the arrow). This visualizes the central dichotomy: state functions live in **boxes**, path functions live in **arrows**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define a state function. Give three examples relevant to biochemical thermodynamics.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A state function is a thermodynamic property whose value depends only on the current state of the system, defined by its state variables ($P, V, T, n$, composition), and is independent of the path by which the system reached that state. Mathematically, $\oint dF = 0$ for any cyclic process.

**Three examples:**
1. **Internal energy $U$** — total microscopic kinetic and potential energy of the system.
2. **Enthalpy $H$** — defined as $H = U + PV$, used in calorimetric studies of biochemical reactions.
3. **Gibbs free energy $G$** — defined as $G = H - TS$, determines spontaneity of metabolic processes.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for examples — full marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*Distinguish between a path function and a state function with two points of difference.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Sl. No. | State Function | Path Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Depends only on the initial and final states. | Depends on the path taken between states. |
| 2 | Mathematical differential is exact ($dF$). | Mathematical differential is inexact ($\delta F$). |

**Examples:** State — $U$, $H$, $S$, $G$; Path — $Q$, $W$.

*(1.5 marks for each correct difference, totalling 3 marks.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Notation trap:** Writing $dQ$ or $dW$ instead of $\delta Q$ or $\delta W$ costs **1 full mark** in KTU valuation. Always use the slashed-delta symbol for inexact differentials.
> - **Cyclic-integral misapplication:** Students often write $\oint dQ = 0$ which is **wrong** — heat is not a state function. Correct statement: $\oint dU = 0$.
> - **First Law mis-statement:** Writing $Q = U_2 - U_1$ (without specifying path) loses marks. The correct statement is $\Delta U = Q + W$ (path-dependent $Q,W$; path-independent $\Delta U$).

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Define state and path functions. Prove mathematically that internal energy is a state function using the exact-differential criterion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A biochemical reaction converts $1\text{ mol}$ of glucose at standard state ($T = 298\text{ K}$, $P = 1\text{ bar}$) to $2\text{ mol}$ of ethanol and $2\text{ mol}$ of $\text{CO}_2$. Calculate the change in standard internal energy given the standard enthalpy change $\Delta H^\circ = -68\text{ kJ/mol}$ and the relation $\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta(PV)$. Treat gases as ideal. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define state and path functions.

A *state function* depends only on the state (e.g., $U, H, S, G$). A *path function* depends on the process path (e.g., $Q, W$).

**Step 2 (2 marks):** State the exact-differential criterion.

For $F(x,y)$ to be a state function, the differential $dF = M \, dx + N \, dy$ must be exact, i.e.,

$$\frac{\partial M}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial N}{\partial x}$$

This is the **Euler reciprocity condition**.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply to $U = U(T, V)$.

$$dU = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V dT + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T dV$$

Let $M = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V$ and $N = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T$.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Verify Euler condition.

$$\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial^2 U}{\partial V \partial T}\right) = \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial T}\right)_V$$

The mixed second derivatives are equal (Clairaut's theorem, applicable because $U$ is sufficiently smooth). Therefore $dU$ is exact and $U$ is a state function. $\blacksquare$

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the reaction:

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6(s) \rightarrow 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}(l) + 2\,\text{CO}_2(g)$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Count gaseous moles change.

$\Delta n_g = 2 - 0 = 2\text{ mol}$ (glucose and ethanol are condensed phases).

**Step 3 (2 marks):** For an ideal gas mixture at constant $T$ and $P$:

$$\Delta(PV) = \Delta n_g \, RT$$

$$\Delta(PV) = (2\text{ mol})(8.314\text{ J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1})(298\text{ K})$$

$$\Delta(PV) = 4955.1\text{ J} \approx 4.96\text{ kJ}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Apply $\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta(PV)$:

$$\Delta U = \Delta H - \Delta(PV)$$

$$\Delta U = -68\text{ kJ} - 4.96\text{ kJ}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta U = -72.96\text{ kJ/mol} \approx -73.0\text{ kJ/mol}}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Conclusion.

Since $U$ is a state function, $\Delta U$ is path-independent. The negative sign indicates the reaction releases internal energy at constant volume.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

`[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the difference between exact and inexact differentials. Show that the cyclic integral of a path function is generally non-zero, while that of a state function is exactly zero. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ideal gas undergoes two different processes from state A $(P_1 = 2\text{ bar}, V_1 = 1\text{ L})$ to state B $(P_2 = 1\text{ bar}, V_2 = 2\text{ L})$:
- **Path I:** Isothermal reversible expansion.
- **Path II:** Expansion against constant external pressure of $1\text{ bar}$ followed by cooling at constant volume.

Compute and compare the work done in each path. What does this comparison reveal about $W$? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Define the two differential types.

- **Exact differential $dF$**: arises from a well-defined state function; integration is path-independent.
- **Inexact differential $\delta F$**: arises from a process quantity; integration is path-dependent.

The notation difference ($d$ vs. $\delta$) is mandatory.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** State the exactness criterion.

For $dF = M(x,y) \, dx + N(x,y) \, dy$ to be exact:

$$\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply to cyclic integrals.

For a state function, $\oint dF = 0$ because the start and end points coincide. For a path function, the integral depends on the trajectory, so $\oint \delta F \neq 0$ in general.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Example.

$$dU = \delta Q + \delta W \quad \Rightarrow \quad \oint dU = 0, \quad \text{but} \quad \oint \delta Q \neq 0 \text{ and } \oint \delta W \neq 0 \text{ (in general)}$$

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify constants.

$nRT = P_1 V_1 = (2\text{ bar})(1\text{ L}) = 2\text{ bar}\cdot\text{L} = 200\text{ J}$ (using $1\text{ bar}\cdot\text{L} = 100\text{ J}$).

Also, $P_2 V_2 = (1)(2) = 2\text{ bar}\cdot\text{L} = 200\text{ J}$. ✓ (Confirms isothermal compatibility.)

**Step 2 (3 marks):** Path I — Isothermal reversible expansion.

$$W_I = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{nRT}{V} dV = -nRT \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

$$W_I = -(200\text{ J}) \ln\!\left(\frac{2\text{ L}}{1\text{ L}}\right) = -(200)(0.6931)$$

$$W_I \approx -138.6\text{ J}$$

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Path II — Two-step irreversible process.

*Step 2a:* Expansion at constant $P_{ext} = 1\text{ bar}$ from $V_1 = 1\text{ L}$ to $V_2 = 2\text{ L}$:

$$W_{2a} = -P_{ext}(V_2 - V_1) = -(1\text{ bar})(1\text{ L}) = -100\text{ J}$$

*Step 2b:* Cooling at constant volume $V_2 = 2\text{ L}$: no volume change, hence

$$W_{2b} = 0$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Total Path II work:

$$W_{II} = W_{2a} + W_{2b} = -100\text{ J} + 0 = -100\text{ J}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Conclusion and interpretation.

$$W_I = -138.6\text{ J} \neq W_{II} = -100\text{ J}$$

The work differs even though the initial and final states are identical. This proves that $W$ is a **path function**, and $\delta W$ is an **inexact differential**. In contrast, the change in internal energy would be the same for both paths (since $U$ is a state function):

$$\Delta U_I = \Delta U_{II} = W_I + Q_I = W_{II} + Q_{II}$$

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **State function** = property of a *state*; depends only on initial and final conditions. **Differential is exact** ($dF$); cyclic integral is **zero**; **examples:** $U, H, S, G, A, T, P, V$.
- **Path function** = property of a *process*; depends on the *route* taken. **Differential is inexact** ($\delta F$); cyclic integral is **non-zero** in general; **examples:** $Q, W$.
- **Notation is graded:** Use $dU, dH, dS, dG$ (straight $d$) for state functions; $\delta Q, \delta W$ (slashed $\delta$ or $\dbar$) for path functions. Marks deducted otherwise.
- **Euler reciprocity condition** is the formal test: if $\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right) = \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)$ for $dF = M \, dx + N \, dy$, then $F$ is a state function.
- **First Law linking:** $dU = \delta Q + \delta W$ — the LHS is exact (state), but the two RHS terms are inexact (path). The First Law *defines* $U$ as a state function.
- **Biochemical significance:** $\Delta G$ (state function) decides spontaneity of metabolic reactions without needing the pathway mechanism. This enables tabulation of standard Gibbs energies for biochemistry.
- **Quick test question for self-check:** *"Is the cyclic integral of a path function zero?"* — **No**, it is generally non-zero. A common viva/short-answer trap.
- **Pairing trick:** State functions with cyclic integral zero: *Every* one. Path functions with cyclic integral non-zero: only $Q$ and $W$ in classical thermodynamics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** Before writing any thermodynamic equation in your KTU answer script, mentally classify each variable. If it is a state function, use $d$. If it is a path function, use $\delta$. This single habit will safeguard 1–2 marks per question across the paper.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT414 - BIOCHEMICAL THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition The **Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics** is a foundational postulate of classical thermodynamics that establishes the concept of **temperature** and the principle of **transitivity of thermal equilibrium** between physical systems. It is formally stated as: > **Zeroth Law Statement:** *If two thermodynamic systems, A and B, are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system, C, then systems A and B are in thermal equilibrium with each other.* In the notation of thermal equilibrium, this can be compactly expressed as: $$ \text{If } \; T(A) = T(C) \; \text{ and } \; T(B) = T(C) \; \Rightarrow \; T(A) = T(B) $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Zeroth Law is logically placed *before* the First and Second Laws because it provides the very foundation required to define the temperature scale and the empirical measurement of heat. Without it, the First Law's internal energy function cannot be properly formulated for a single, well-defined temperature variable. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine three friends — **A, B, and C** — sharing a room. Suppose friend A feels neither too warm nor too cold (in thermal comfort) when standing next to friend C, and friend B also feels the same comfort when next to friend C. The Zeroth Law assures us that A and B, when brought together, will *also* feel mutually comfortable — they share the same "thermal state" as mediated by C. In a more practical engineering analogy: - **System A** = a bioreactor broth at **37 °C** (human body temperature) - **System B** = a temperature probe (thermocouple) reading **37 °C** - **System C** = a calibrated mercury reference thermometer at **37 °C** If both A and B individually show **no net heat flow** with C, the law guarantees that placing A and B in direct contact will also result in **zero net heat flow** between them. > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Summary:** The Zeroth Law essentially tells us that "temperature is a transitive property." It is the reason a single thermometer can be trusted to compare the temperatures of any number of objects sequentially. ### 1.3 Core Constants and Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Absolute Zero | 0 K | **0 K (–273.15 °C)** | Kelvin (K) | | Triple Point of Water | $T_{tp}$ | **273.16 K** | Kelvin (K) | | Standard Reference | $T_{std}$ | **298.15 K (25 °C)** | Kelvin (K) | | Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** | J/(mol·K) | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Logical Breakdown of the Zeroth Law The Zeroth Law comprises three interlinked conceptual pillars.
