Each must be fully understood for KTU ESE examination questions. - **Pillar 1 — Thermal Equilibrium Definition** - *Why:* Establishes the operational meaning of "no net heat flow." - *How:* Two systems in thermal contact exchange zero net energy in the form of heat ($Q = 0$) when their intensive thermodynamic property (temperature) is identical. - **Pillar 2 — Existence of a Thermometric Property** - *Why:* A measurable physical quantity (volume of mercury, electrical resistance, EMF, refractive index) must change predictably with temperature to act as a "temperature proxy." - *How:* For any pure substance, the function $f(P, V, T) = 0$ can be reduced (at fixed pressure) to $V = V(T)$ or $R = R(T)$. - **Pillar 3 — Transitivity** - *Why:* This is the heart of the Zeroth Law and the only novel postulate. - *How:* The existence of a universal thermal equilibrium class means that **temperature is a state function** that depends only on the internal state, not the system's material composition. ### 2.2 Mathematical Formulation Consider three systems in thermal contact via a **diathermal (thermally conducting) wall**: $$ A \leftrightarrow C \quad \text{(equilibrium)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_A = T_C $$ $$ B \leftrightarrow C \quad \text{(equilibrium)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_B = T_C $$ By **transitivity of equality** (a property of real numbers), it follows that: $$ T_A = T_B \quad \Rightarrow \quad A \leftrightarrow B \quad \text{(also in equilibrium)} $$ This defines an **equivalence relation** on the set of all thermodynamic systems, partitioning them into disjoint thermal equilibrium classes — each class characterized by a unique value of the scalar state function $T$. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Relation | Expression | Condition / Use | |---|---|---| | Zeroth Law (transitivity) | $T(A)=T(C)$ and $T(B)=T(C) \Rightarrow T(A)=T(B)$ | All pairwise systems | | Ideal Gas Temperature | $T = \dfrac{PV}{nR}$ | Defines thermodynamic $T$ scale | | Celsius Conversion | $T(^\circ C) = T(K) - 273.15$ | Engineering conversions | | Thermometric Linear Form | $T(X) = aX + b$ | Any linear thermometric property $X$ | | Thermometric General Form | $T(X) = a \cdot \ln(X) + b$ | Non-linear probes (e.g., thermocouples) | | Zero-Point Limit | $\lim_{T \to 0\,K} Q_{extractable} = 0$ | Third Law tie-in | | Thermal Contact Heat Flow | $Q = 0 \;\text{at equilibrium}$ | Diathermal walls | > [!IMPORTANT] > **No Vertical Pipe Rule:** All absolute-value and "such that" notations are written using `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $X \mid_{T}$), never the literal `|` character, to preserve markdown table integrity. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In **biochemical engineering**, the Zeroth Law underpins: - **Bioreactor Temperature Control:** PID controllers rely on the assumption that a single temperature sensor (e.g., RTD in a fermenter jacket) accurately reports the broth temperature, which is only valid *because* of transitivity. - **Sterilization Validation:** Autoclave temperature uniformity testing depends on the principle that multiple probes at different locations will agree when equilibrium is reached. - **Enzyme Kinetics Studies:** $Q_{10}$ temperature coefficients and Arrhenius plots require a well-defined, uniform temperature baseline — guaranteed by the Zeroth Law. - **Cryopreservation:** Storage of biological samples at controlled sub-zero temperatures is verified by consistent multi-probe readings. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Temperature as a State Function from the Zeroth Law We will demonstrate rigorously that the Zeroth Law implies the existence of a unique, well-defined **temperature** as a thermodynamic state variable. **Step 1 — Start with a Single Pure Substance System** For a pure substance, the equation of state relates the three measurable properties: $$ f(P, V, T) = 0 $$ Solving for $T$ at a fixed reference pressure $P_0$: $$ T = \phi(P_0, V) $$ This defines $T$ as a function of volume alone (for gases at low pressure). **Step 2 — Use a Second System as a Reference Probe** Take system $C$ to be a gas thermometer. For two different systems, $A$ and $B$, we measure their volumes $V_A$ and $V_B$ when each is in thermal equilibrium with $C$ at the same $P_0$ and $n$. $$ T_A = \phi(P_0, V_A) \qquad T_B = \phi(P_0, V_B) $$ **Step 3 — Apply the Zeroth Law to Establish Transitivity** The Zeroth Law states that if both $A \leftrightarrow C$ and $B \leftrightarrow C$ are equilibria, then $A \leftrightarrow B$ is also an equilibrium. This is only possible if the function $\phi$ returns the **same scalar value** for both systems — i.e., a single, well-defined temperature. **Step 4 — Generalize to a Universal Temperature** Because the Zeroth Law holds for *any* choice of probe $C$, the temperature function $T$ must be **independent of the substance** used as a thermometer. The function: $$ T \equiv T(\text{universal state of system}) $$ is a **universal scalar property**, the same for all systems at the same equilibrium class. Hence $T$ is a true state function. ### 3.2 Derivation: Linear Thermometric Scale Suppose the thermometric property $X$ varies linearly with temperature between two fixed points: the ice point ($T_1 = 273.15$ K) and the steam point ($T_2 = 373.15$ K). **Step 1 — Write the linear relation** $$ X = aT + b $$ **Step 2 — Apply boundary conditions** At the ice point: $X_1 = a(273.15) + b$ At the steam point: $X_2 = a(373.15) + b$ **Step 3 — Subtract to eliminate $b$** $$ X_2 - X_1 = a(373.15 - 273.15) = 100a $$ $$ a = \dfrac{X_2 - X_1}{100} $$ **Step 4 — Solve for $b$ using the ice point** $$ b = X_1 - 273.15 \, a $$ **Step 5 — Final Linear Thermometric Equation** $$ T = 273.15 + 100 \cdot \dfrac{X - X_1}{X_2 - X_1} $$ This is the **generalized Celsius-style formula** used to define any empirical temperature scale from a measured property $X$. ### 3.3 Derivation: The Ideal Gas Absolute Zero Extrapolation **Step 1 — Gay-Lussac's Law (constant-pressure process)** For an ideal gas at constant $P$ and $n$: $$ V \propto T $$ **Step 2 — Linear relationship** $$ V = V_0 \, (1 + \alpha \, t) $$ where $t$ is the Celsius temperature and $\alpha$ is the thermal expansion coefficient. **Step 3 — Experimental value of $\alpha$** For an ideal gas, $\alpha = \dfrac{1}{273.15} \; \text{K}^{-1}$. **Step 4 — Extrapolate to find the temperature at which $V = 0$** $$ 0 = V_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{t}{273.15}\right) $$ $$ t = -273.15 \; ^\circ C $$ **Step 5 — Convert to Kelvin** $$ T = t + 273.15 = 0 \; \text{K} $$ This is the **absolute zero**, an essential consequence of the linear extrapolation permitted by the Zeroth Law's transitivity property. ### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix | Concept | Foundational Principle | Mathematical Outcome | Practical Manifestation | |---|---|---|---| | Thermal equilibrium | $\Delta Q = 0$ between systems | Equal temperature | No net heat flow in bioreactor | | Transitivity | $A \sim C$ and $B \sim C \Rightarrow A \sim B$ | Equivalence relation | Single calibrated thermometer suffices | | Temperature existence | $T = T(\text{state})$ | State function | PID control possible | | Thermometric property | $X = f(T)$ | Measurable scalar | RTD, thermocouple use | | Absolute zero | $V \to 0$ as $T \to 0$ K | $0$ K lower bound | Cryogenic storage limit | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Conceptual Flow: Transitivity of Thermal Equilibrium ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["SYSTEM A
Bioreactor Broth
Initial T unknown"] nodeC["SYSTEM C
Reference Thermometer
Calibrated Probe"] nodeB["SYSTEM B
Heat Exchanger Fluid
Initial T unknown"] nodeRes["SYSTEM A AND B
Mutual Equilibrium
T A equals T B"] nodeA -- "diathermal contact
A reaches T of C" --> nodeC nodeC -- "diathermal contact
B reaches T of C" --> nodeB nodeC -- "Zeroth Law transitivity" --> nodeRes nodeA -. "inferred equilibrium" .-> nodeRes nodeB -. "inferred equilibrium" .-> nodeRes ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Temperature Measurement in a Biochemical Plant ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph proc1["PROCESS ZONE"] bioreactor["BIOREACTOR VESSEL
Working Fluid
Unknown Temperature"] end subgraph proc2["MEASUREMENT ZONE"] probe1["THERMOCOUPLE PROBE 1
Linear EMF response"] probe2["RTD PROBE 2
Resistance change"] refunit["REFERENCE UNIT C
Calibrated Standard
Defines Temperature Scale"] end subgraph proc3["VERIFICATION ZONE"] comparator["EQUILIBRIUM COMPARATOR
Applies Zeroth Law
Tests A equals B equals C"] display["DIGITAL READOUT
Displays T in Kelvin"] end bioreactor -- "diathermal wall" --> probe1 probe1 -- "calibration link" --> refunit bioreactor -- "diathermal wall" --> probe2 probe2 -- "calibration link" --> refunit probe1 -- "signal 1" --> comparator probe2 -- "signal 2" --> comparator refunit -- "reference signal" --> comparator comparator -- "validated T value" --> display ``` ### 4.3 Decision Tree: Applying the Zeroth Law in Practice ```mermaid flowchart TD start["Start: Three systems A B C given"] q1{"Is A in thermal
equilibrium with C?"} q2{"Is B in thermal
equilibrium with C?"} step1["Record T of A equals T of C"] step2["Record T of B equals T of C"] step3["Apply transitivity:
T of A equals T of B"] conclusion["Conclusion: A is in thermal
equilibrium with B by Zeroth Law"] noEq["Direct heat flow analysis required:
Zeroth Law does not apply"] start --> q1 q1 -- "Yes" --> step1 q1 -- "No" --> noEq step1 --> q2 q2 -- "Yes" --> step2 q2 -- "No" --> noEq step2 --> step3 step3 --> conclusion ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Safety Confirmation:** All node identifiers (e.g., `nodeA`, `proc1`, `bioreactor`, `comparator`) are alphanumeric and free of reserved keywords. All node labels are quoted plain text without markdown formatting tags, in accordance with the V10 compilation safeguards. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]` **Q: State the Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics. Why is it called the "Zeroth" law and not the "Fourth"?** **Model Answer:** The Zeroth Law states that *if two systems, A and B, are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system, C, then A and B are in thermal equilibrium with each other.* It is called the "Zeroth" law because it was formulated *after* the First, Second, and Third Laws had already been established, but it was recognized as a **logically prior** postulate — it must precede the others because it provides the very basis for defining temperature, which is a prerequisite for the First Law's energy formulation and the Second Law's entropy formulation. Placing it at "zero" indicates its fundamental precedence. #### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]` **Q: Differentiate between a diathermal wall and an adiabatic wall. How does this distinction relate to the Zeroth Law?** **Model Answer:** | Wall Type | Heat Transfer Property | Zeroth Law Relevance | |---|---|---| | **Diathermal** | Allows heat flow between systems | Required to test for thermal equilibrium | | **Adiabatic** | Prevents all heat flow | Isolates systems; equilibrium is trivially maintained | The Zeroth Law requires **diathermal contact** to detect thermal equilibrium. If a diathermal wall between $A$ and $C$ produces no net heat flow, the two are in thermal equilibrium. Adiabatic walls, conversely, prevent any test of equilibrium and are irrelevant to the Zeroth Law's operational statement. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model]` **(a)** *(7 Marks)* State and explain the Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics with a suitable diagram. Discuss its significance in defining the concept of temperature. **(b)** *(7 Marks)* A constant-volume gas thermometer records pressures of **$P_1 = 1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa** at the ice point and **$P_2 = 1.366 \times 10^5$ Pa** at the steam point. Determine the temperature (in °C) of a system when the thermometer reads **$P = 1.150 \times 10^5$ Pa**. **Model Solution for (a):** 1. **Statement:** If two systems A and B are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system C, then systems A and B are in thermal equilibrium with each other. 2. **Diagram Description:** Three boxes labelled A, B, and C with bidirectional arrows between A↔C and B↔C, and a dashed arrow A↔B indicating inferred equilibrium. 3. **Significance:** - Establishes the existence of a **temperature** as a state function. - Justifies the use of a **single thermometer** to measure the temperatures of multiple systems. - Provides the empirical basis for the **transitivity of thermal equilibrium**. - Enables the construction of a **universal temperature scale**. **Model Solution for (b):** Using the linear thermometric relation for a constant-volume gas thermometer: $$ T = 273.15 \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_1} $$ At $P = 1.150 \times 10^5$ Pa: $$ T = 273.15 \cdot \dfrac{1.150 \times 10^5}{1.013 \times 10^5} $$ $$ T = 273.15 \cdot (1.1353) = 310.10 \; \text{K} $$ Converting to Celsius: $$ t = T - 273.15 = 310.10 - 273.15 = 36.95 \; ^\circ C $$ $$ \boxed{t \approx 37.0 \; ^\circ C} $$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model]` **(a)** *(7 Marks)* Using the Zeroth Law, derive the existence of a universal temperature scale. Explain why the ideal gas scale is adopted as the standard. **(b)** *(7 Marks)* A platinum resistance thermometer has resistances of **$R_1 = 9.83 \; \Omega$** at the ice point and **$R_2 = 13.95 \; \Omega$** at the steam point. When inserted into a fermentation broth, the resistance is **$R = 12.10 \; \Omega$**. Calculate the temperature of the broth in °C. **Model Solution for (a):** 1. The Zeroth Law implies that systems in thermal equilibrium share a common **scalar state function** — temperature. 2. Because the law holds for **any** choice of reference probe, the temperature function must be **substance-independent** (universal). 3. **Ideal Gas Scale Justification:** - For an ideal gas: $PV = nRT$ defines $T$ without empirical calibration ambiguity. - The gas thermometer (constant volume or constant pressure) extrapolates to **absolute zero** at $-273.15 \; ^\circ C$. - It is **reproducible** across laboratories worldwide and forms the basis of the **thermodynamic temperature scale** adopted internationally. **Model Solution for (b):** Using the linear thermometric formula: $$ T = 273.15 + 100 \cdot \dfrac{R - R_1}{R_2 - R_1} $$ Substituting the values: $$ T = 273.15 + 100 \cdot \dfrac{12.10 - 9.83}{13.95 - 9.83} $$ $$ T = 273.15 + 100 \cdot \dfrac{2.27}{4.12} $$ $$ T = 273.15 + 100 \cdot (0.5509) $$ $$ T = 273.15 + 55.09 = 328.24 \; \text{K} $$ Converting to Celsius: $$ t = T - 273.15 = 328.24 - 273.15 = 55.09 \; ^\circ C $$ $$ \boxed{t \approx 55.1 \; ^\circ C} $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Common Mistake #1 — Sign Error in Extrapolation:** When deriving absolute zero, students often write $T = 0 \; K$ with the wrong sign. The correct extrapolation: $V \to 0$ as $T \to 0 \; K$, which gives $t = -273.15 \; ^\circ C$. > 2. **Common Mistake #2 — Confusing Zeroth with Third Law:** The Zeroth Law defines temperature; the Third Law states that absolute zero is unattainable. Examiners frequently award zero marks when these are conflated. > 3. **Common Mistake #3 — Unit Inconsistency:** Always state final answers with **units** (K or °C). A correct numerical value without units typically receives only 50% credit in KTU valuation. > 4. **Common Mistake #4 — Skipping the Justification for "Zeroth":** In "Why is it called Zeroth?" questions, students state the law but forget to explain its **logical priority** — losing 1–2 marks. > 5. **Common Mistake #5 — Wrong Reference Points:** In thermometric calculations, students sometimes confuse the ice point (273.15 K) with 273 K. The exact value **273.15 K** is required for full marks. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - The **Zeroth Law** establishes the **transitivity of thermal equilibrium**: $A \sim C$ and $B \sim C \Rightarrow A \sim B$. - It is the **theoretical foundation** for the concept of **temperature** as a universal, substance-independent state function. - It logically **precedes** the First, Second, and Third Laws — hence the name "Zeroth." - **Diathermal walls** are required to test thermal equilibrium; **adiabatic walls** are not relevant to the Zeroth Law. - The **ideal gas temperature scale** ($T = PV/nR$) is the practical realization of the Zeroth Law's implications. - The **absolute zero** (0 K = $-273.15 \; ^\circ C$) is found by extrapolating the linear gas law to $V = 0$ or $P = 0$. - **Linear thermometric equation:** $T = 273.15 + 100 \cdot \dfrac{X - X_1}{X_2 - X_1}$ for any empirical probe $X$. - **Constant-volume gas thermometer formula:** $T = 273.15 \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_1}$. - **Constant-pressure gas thermometer formula:** $T = 273.15 \cdot \dfrac{V}{V_1}$. - In **biochemical engineering**, the Zeroth Law is the silent assumption behind every calibrated probe in a bioreactor, autoclave, or fermenter. - **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314 \; J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}$. - **Celsius-Kelvin conversion:** $T(K) = t(^\circ C) + 273.15$. - **Exam Tip:** Always include the **equation**, the **substitution step**, and the **final boxed answer with units** for full KTU marks. ---

topics.title: Reversible and Irreversible Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT414 - BIOCHEMICAL THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reversible and Irreversible Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reversible Process (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> A **reversible process** is an idealized thermodynamic process that proceeds infinitesimally slowly through a continuous succession of equilibrium states, such that the system and its surroundings can be restored to their initial states without leaving any net change in the universe. The direction of the process can be reversed at any instant by an infinitesimal change in the driving force.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Irreversible Process (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> An **irreversible process** is a real, spontaneous process that proceeds through non-equilibrium states and cannot be reversed without leaving permanent, net changes in the system, surroundings, or both. All naturally occurring biochemical and physical processes in living cells are irreversible.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two scenarios involving a hot cup of coffee at **373 K**:

- **Reversible Analogy — The "Slow Stretching of a Spring":** Picture pulling a perfect spring outward so gradually that at every nanosecond the spring remains perfectly balanced with your hand. If you stop, the spring stays exactly where it is. Reverse the pull infinitesimally and the spring glides back, recovering all the work you put in. Nothing is lost. This is **reversibility** — every step is balanced.

- **Irreversible Analogy — The "Sudden Snap":** Now snap the spring violently. It oscillates, heats up, produces sound, and never returns to your hand on its own. The energy is dissipated as heat and sound. You cannot get the original ordered stretching motion back. This is **irreversibility** — real processes lose "useful" ability to do work.

## 1.3 Biochemical Context (KTU Board Relevance)

In **biochemical thermodynamics**, every metabolic pathway (glycolysis, Krebs cycle, oxidative phosphorylation) is intrinsically irreversible because:

- Enzymes catalyze reactions with **finite rates**.
- **Concentration gradients** across membranes dissipate spontaneously.
- **ATP hydrolysis** (ΔG°′ = **−30.5 kJ/mol**) is physiologically irreversible.
- Frictional and viscous losses occur in cytoplasmic fluid flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Highlight:**
> - Universal Gas Constant: **R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
> - Faraday Constant: **F = 96,485 C·mol⁻¹**
> - Standard Biochemical Free Energy of ATP Hydrolysis: **ΔG°′ = −30.5 kJ/mol**
> - Body Temperature Reference: **T = 310.15 K (37 °C)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

### A. Characteristics of a Reversible Process

- The driving force (e.g., pressure difference, temperature gradient) is **infinitesimal** compared to the opposing force.
- The system remains in **thermodynamic equilibrium** at every intermediate state.
- The process can be **reversed** by an infinitesimal change in conditions.
- **No dissipation** of energy occurs (no friction, no unrestrained expansion, no mixing by diffusion).
- The work obtained is the **maximum possible work**.
- The process is an **idealization** — never perfectly realized in nature, but serves as the benchmark.

### B. Characteristics of an Irreversible Process

- The driving force is **finite** (a measurable gradient).
- The system passes through **non-equilibrium states**.
- The process has a **preferred direction** dictated by the Second Law.
- **Useful work is lost** to heat, sound, friction, or molecular chaos.
- The process is **spontaneous** and **real**.
- Cannot be reversed without external energy input and net changes in the universe.

### C. Why the Distinction Matters — The "Why" Behind It

The **Second Law of Thermodynamics** states:

$$\Delta S_{\text{universe}} \geq 0$$

- For a **reversible process**: $\Delta S_{\text{universe}} = 0$
- For an **irreversible process**: $\Delta S_{\text{universe}} > 0$

Reversible processes set the **thermodynamic ceiling** for efficiency. In biochemistry, comparing actual free energy changes to the reversible limit tells us **how far a metabolic reaction is from equilibrium** — a key indicator of its regulatory role.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Variables & Units | Boundary / Validity Condition |
|---|---------|----------|-------------------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | Reversible Work (PV) | $W_{rev} = -\int_{V_i}^{V_f} P_{ext}\,dV$ | $P_{ext}$ in Pa, $V$ in m³ | Quasi-static, $P_{sys} = P_{ext}$ at all times |
| 2 | Irreversible Work | $W_{irr} = -P_{ext}(V_f - V_i)$ | Constant external pressure | Sudden expansion against $P_{ext} < P_{sys}$ |
| 3 | Work Inequality | $W_{rev} \geq W_{irr}$ | Magnitudes in Joules | Equality only when process is reversible |
| 4 | Entropy Change (System) | $dS = \dfrac{\delta q_{rev}}{T}$ | $q_{rev}$ in J, $T$ in K | State function; path-independent |
| 5 | Entropy Generation | $\Delta S_{univ} = \Delta S_{sys} + \Delta S_{surr}$ | All in J·K⁻¹ | $\geq 0$ (Second Law) |
| 6 | Reversible Criterion | $\Delta S_{univ} = 0$ | J·K⁻¹ | Ideal limit |
| 7 | Irreversible Criterion | $\Delta S_{univ} > 0$ | J·K⁻¹ | All real processes |
| 8 | Clausius Inequality | $\oint \dfrac{\delta q}{T} \leq 0$ | Cycle integral | Equality = reversible |
| 9 | Free Energy Criterion | $\Delta G < 0$ at constant $T,P$ | kJ·mol⁻¹ | Spontaneous (irreversible) |
| 10 | ATP Hydrolysis | $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -30.5$ kJ·mol⁻¹ | Biochemical standard state | $pH = 7$, $T = 298$ K |
| 11 | Biochemical Reaction Quotient | $\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ\prime} + RT \ln Q$ | kJ·mol⁻¹ | $R = 8.314 \times 10^{-3}$ kJ·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering & Biochemical Utility:**
> The reversible–irreversible distinction is the **foundation of efficiency analysis** in:
> - **Metabolic engineering** — quantifying how close an enzyme is to its thermodynamic optimum.
> - **Bioprocess design** — calculating minimum energy requirements for bioreactors.
> - **Membrane transport energetics** — distinguishing passive diffusion (irreversible) from active transport (energy-coupled, irreversible but driven).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Work from Isothermal Reversible Expansion of an Ideal Gas

### Setup
Consider $n$ moles of an ideal gas confined in a frictionless piston-cylinder at temperature $T$. The gas expands reversibly and isothermally from volume $V_i$ to $V_f$.

### Step 1 — Write the first law for a closed system

$$
\Delta U = q + w
$$

For an isothermal process with an ideal gas, internal energy depends only on temperature:

$$
\Delta U = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad q_{rev} = -w_{rev}
$$

### Step 2 — Express the reversible work differential

In a reversible process, the external pressure equals the system pressure at all times. For an ideal gas:

$$
P_{ext} = P_{sys} = \dfrac{nRT}{V}
$$

Therefore the infinitesimal reversible work done **by** the system is:

$$
\delta w_{rev} = -P_{ext}\,dV = -\dfrac{nRT}{V}\,dV
$$

### Step 3 — Integrate between initial and final volumes

$$
w_{rev} = -\int_{V_i}^{V_f} \dfrac{nRT}{V}\,dV
$$

Since $T$ is constant, it comes out of the integral:

$$
w_{rev} = -nRT \int_{V_i}^{V_f} \dfrac{1}{V}\,dV
$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the integral

$$
w_{rev} = -nRT \left[ \ln V \right]_{V_i}^{V_f}
$$

$$
w_{rev} = -nRT \ln \left( \dfrac{V_f}{V_i} \right)
$$

### Step 5 — Final Result

$$
\boxed{\,w_{rev} = -nRT \ln \left( \dfrac{V_f}{V_i} \right) = nRT \ln \left( \dfrac{V_i}{V_f} \right)\,}
$$

This is the **maximum possible work** obtainable from the expansion — the reversible benchmark.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Work Comparison — Reversible vs. Irreversible Expansion

### Irreversible Case (Constant External Pressure)

Suppose the same gas expands irreversibly against a constant external pressure $P_{ext}$ (where $P_{ext} < P_{sys,\text{initial}}$).

The work is calculated using the constant external pressure:

$$
w_{irr} = -P_{ext}(V_f - V_i)
$$

Using the ideal gas law to substitute volumes:

$$
w_{irr} = -P_{ext}\left(\dfrac{nRT}{P_f} - \dfrac{nRT}{P_i}\right)
$$

For an isothermal expansion to a final pressure $P_f = P_{ext}$:

$$
\boxed{\,w_{irr} = -P_{ext}\left(\dfrac{nRT}{P_{ext}} - \dfrac{nRT}{P_i}\right) = -nRT\left(1 - \dfrac{P_{ext}}{P_i}\right)\,}
$$

### Comparison

$$
w_{rev} - w_{irr} = -nRT\ln\left(\dfrac{P_i}{P_{ext}}\right) + nRT\left(1 - \dfrac{P_{ext}}{P_i}\right)
$$

This difference is the **lost work** — dissipated as heat to the surroundings during the irreversible expansion.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Entropy Change of the Universe for an Irreversible Process

### Step 1 — Define the universe entropy change

$$
\Delta S_{univ} = \Delta S_{sys} + \Delta S_{surr}
$$

### Step 2 — System entropy change (state function, calculated via a reversible path)

$$
\Delta S_{sys} = \int \dfrac{\delta q_{rev}}{T}
$$

For the isothermal ideal gas expansion:

$$
\Delta S_{sys} = \dfrac{q_{rev}}{T} = \dfrac{-w_{rev}}{T} = nR \ln \left( \dfrac{V_f}{V_i} \right)
$$

### Step 3 — Surroundings entropy change (real heat absorbed at constant $T$)

$$
\Delta S_{surr} = \dfrac{q_{surr}}{T} = \dfrac{-q_{sys}}{T}
$$

For irreversible expansion, $q_{sys} = -w_{irr}$, so:

$$
\Delta S_{surr} = \dfrac{w_{irr}}{T} = -\dfrac{nR}{T}\left(1 - \dfrac{P_{ext}}{P_i}\right)T = -nR\left(1 - \dfrac{P_{ext}}{P_i}\right)
$$

### Step 4 — Sum to find universe entropy change

$$
\Delta S_{univ} = nR\ln\left(\dfrac{V_f}{V_i}\right) - nR\left(1 - \dfrac{P_{ext}}{P_i}\right)
$$

Since $P_{ext} < P_i$ for expansion, the second term is **negative**, but its magnitude is **less than** the first term (logarithm grows slower than linear ratio), giving:

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta S_{univ} > 0 \quad \text{(confirms irreversibility)}\,}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Comparison

| Property | Reversible Process | Irreversible Process |
|----------|-------------------|----------------------|
| Driving Force | Infinitesimal | Finite |
| Path | Quasi-static equilibrium states | Non-equilibrium states |
| Direction | Reversible by infinitesimal change | Has preferred direction |
| Work Output | Maximum ($w_{max}$) | Less than maximum ($< w_{max}$) |
| Heat Transfer | Reversible ($q_{rev}$) | Irreversible ($q_{irr}$) |
| Entropy of Universe | $\Delta S_{univ} = 0$ | $\Delta S_{univ} > 0$ |
| Realizability | Idealized (limiting case) | All real processes |
| Biochemical Example | Hypothetical ideal enzyme catalysis | Glycolysis, ATP hydrolysis |
| Friction/Viscosity | Absent | Present |
| Mathematical Treatment | $dS = \delta q_{rev}/T$ | Clausius inequality $\oint \delta q/T \leq 0$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Classification of Thermodynamic Processes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thermodynamic Process Initiated] --> B{System stays at equilibrium at every step?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Reversible Process]
    B -- No --> D[Irreversible Process]
    C --> C1[Driving force = infinitesimal]
    C --> C2[Maximum work output]
    C --> C3[Delta S universe = 0]
    C --> C4[Idealized benchmark]
    D --> D1[Driving force = finite]
    D --> D2[Work output less than maximum]
    D --> D3[Delta S universe greater than 0]
    D --> D4[All real biological and physical processes]
    D2 --> D5[Examples ATP hydrolysis membrane diffusion enzymatic reactions]
    C4 --> C6[Examples quasi static isothermal expansion hypothetical ideal catalysis]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Reversible vs Irreversible Work Output

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SYS[System at State A]
        SA[Initial State P1 V1 T]
    end
    SA --> R1[Reversible Path infinitesimal steps]
    SA --> IR1[Irreversible Path sudden jump]
    R1 --> SB[Final State B P2 V2 T]
    IR1 --> SB
    R1 -- Wrev --> WR[Maximum Work Recoverable]
    IR1 -- Wirr --> WI[Work plus Lost Work as heat]
    WR -- Difference --> LOST[Lost Work equals T times Delta S universe]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Entropy of the Universe as a Decision Criterion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Evaluate Any Process] --> Q{Calculate Delta S universe}
    Q -- Equal to zero --> RV[Reversible Process ideal limit]
    Q -- Greater than zero --> IR[Irreversible Process spontaneous real]
    Q -- Less than zero --> IM[Impossible violates Second Law]
    IR --> EX[Examples Biochemical reactions diffusion heat conduction]
    RV --> EX2[Examples Quasi static expansion reversible heat transfer]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
**Define a reversible process. State any two characteristics.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A reversible process is an idealized process that proceeds infinitesimally slowly through a continuous series of equilibrium states, so that both the system and surroundings can be restored to their initial states without any net change in the universe. *(1 mark)*

**Two characteristics:** *(2 marks — 1 mark each)*
1. The driving force (e.g., pressure or temperature difference) is infinitesimal at every step.
2. The work done is the maximum possible, and $\Delta S_{universe} = 0$.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**Distinguish between reversible and irreversible processes with a biochemical example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Criterion | Reversible | Irreversible |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|
| Driving force | Infinitesimal | Finite |
| Path | Equilibrium states | Non-equilibrium states |
| Entropy of universe | $\Delta S_{univ} = 0$ | $\Delta S_{univ} > 0$ |

*(2 marks)*

**Biochemical Example:** The hydrolysis of ATP to ADP + Pi ($\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -30.5$ kJ/mol) is a strictly **irreversible** metabolic process under cellular conditions. *(1 mark)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students **lose 2–3 marks** by forgetting to specify the conditions of reversibility (quasi-static, no friction, equilibrium at every step).
> - Another common error is **confusing $W_{rev}$ with $W_{irr}$ signs** — the negative sign convention in $W = -\int P\,dV$ must be applied correctly.
> - In derivation problems, examiners award **partial marks only if at least three correct algebraic steps** are shown. Skipping from the integral to the final answer loses 4–5 marks instantly.
> - Always state the **Second Law justification** ($\Delta S_{univ} \geq 0$) when classifying a process — this is a 2-mark "key phrase" the examiner expects.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Part B Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the maximum work obtained during the **isothermal reversible expansion** of an ideal gas from volume $V_1$ to $V_2$ at temperature $T$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the work done in a **reversible** and **irreversible** isothermal expansion between the same initial and final states. Why is the reversible work considered the maximum? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

For an ideal gas undergoing isothermal expansion at temperature $T$:

$$
\Delta U = 0 \quad \text{(since U depends only on T for ideal gas)}
$$

First Law gives:

$$
q_{rev} = -w_{rev}
$$

For a reversible process, $P_{ext} = P_{sys} = \dfrac{nRT}{V}$. *(1 mark)*

$$
w_{rev} = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} P\,dV = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} \dfrac{nRT}{V}\,dV
$$

Taking constants out:

$$
w_{rev} = -nRT \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \dfrac{dV}{V}
$$

Integrating:

$$
w_{rev} = -nRT \left[ \ln V \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} = -nRT \ln \left( \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} \right)
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,w_{rev} = nRT \ln \left( \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} \right) = w_{max}\,}
$$

*(Breakdown: 1 mark setup, 2 marks integral setup, 2 marks integration, 1 mark final form, 1 mark conclusion)*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

In an **irreversible expansion** against a constant external pressure $P_{ext} < P_{sys}$:

$$
w_{irr} = -P_{ext}(V_2 - V_1)
$$

For the same initial and final states: *(2 marks)*

$$
w_{rev} = -nRT \ln \left( \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} \right) \quad \text{and} \quad w_{irr} = -P_{ext}(V_2 - V_1)
$$

Since $P_{ext} < P_{int}$ at every instant during the irreversible process, the magnitude of work done **by the system** in the irreversible case is **less** than in the reversible case:

$$
\vert w_{rev} \vert > \vert w_{irr} \vert
$$

Therefore $w_{rev}$ represents the **maximum work**. *(2 marks)*

**Why maximum?** Because in a reversible process, there is no dissipation — no friction, no turbulence, no unrestrained expansion. All the energy is channeled into useful work rather than being degraded to heat. *(2 marks)*

**Lost work:**

$$
w_{lost} = w_{rev} - w_{irr} = T \Delta S_{universe}
$$

*(1 mark for this conclusion)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Part B Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the criteria for reversibility and irreversibility in terms of entropy change of the universe. Derive the Clausius inequality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A biochemical reaction A → B at 310 K has $\Delta H = -40$ kJ/mol and $\Delta S = +50$ J/(mol·K). Calculate $\Delta G$ and determine whether the process is spontaneous and reversible/irreversible. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Criteria:** *(3 marks)*
- Reversible process: $\Delta S_{universe} = 0$
- Irreversible process: $\Delta S_{universe} > 0$
- Impossible process: $\Delta S_{universe} < 0$ (violates Second Law)

**Derivation of Clausius Inequality:** *(4 marks)*

For any process (reversible or irreversible), the entropy change of the system between two equilibrium states A and B is path-independent and equal to the reversible heat divided by temperature:

$$
\Delta S_{sys} = \int_A^B \dfrac{\delta q_{rev}}{T}
$$

For the same change via an irreversible path, the entropy of the universe must increase:

$$
\Delta S_{universe} = \Delta S_{sys} + \Delta S_{surr} \geq 0
$$

Since $\Delta S_{surr} = -\dfrac{q_{irr}}{T}$ (the actual heat exchanged with surroundings):

$$
\int_A^B \dfrac{\delta q_{rev}}{T} - \dfrac{q_{irr}}{T} \geq 0
$$

For a cyclic process where the system returns to its initial state ($\Delta S_{sys,\text{cycle}} = 0$):

$$
\oint \dfrac{\delta q_{irr}}{T} \leq 0
$$

This is the **Clausius Inequality**, with equality only for reversible cycles.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

Given: $T = 310$ K, $\Delta H = -40$ kJ/mol $= -40{,}000$ J/mol, $\Delta S = +50$ J/(mol·K).

Gibbs free energy equation:

$$
\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S
$$

Substituting: *(2 marks)*

$$
\Delta G = (-40{,}000) - (310)(50)
$$

$$
\Delta G = -40{,}000 - 15{,}500
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta G = -55{,}500 \text{ J/mol} = -55.5 \text{ kJ/mol}\,}
$$

*(2 marks for computation)*

**Spontaneity and Reversibility:** *(3 marks)*
- Since $\Delta G < 0$, the reaction is **spontaneous** at 310 K (physiological temperature).
- Because the process is spontaneous and produces a net increase in the entropy of the universe, it is **irreversible** — it cannot reverse itself without external energy input.
- This is consistent with $\Delta S_{universe} > 0$ for real biochemical transformations.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- A **reversible process** is an idealized, infinitely slow path through equilibrium states — a theoretical benchmark.
- An **irreversible process** is the only kind that occurs in nature and in living systems.
- **Maximum work** is obtained from a reversible process: $w_{rev} = -nRT \ln(V_f/V_i)$ for isothermal ideal-gas expansion.
- **Irreversible work** at constant external pressure: $w_{irr} = -P_{ext}(V_f - V_i)$.
- The **Clausius Inequality**: $\oint \delta q / T \leq 0$ — equality holds for reversible cycles.
- **Second Law criterion**: $\Delta S_{univ} = 0$ (reversible) versus $\Delta S_{univ} > 0$ (irreversible).
- **Lost work** in irreversible processes: $w_{lost} = T \cdot \Delta S_{universe} \geq 0$.
- All **biochemical reactions** (glycolysis, ATP hydrolysis, protein folding, membrane transport) are **irreversible**.
- The **Gibbs free energy** $\Delta G < 0$ indicates a spontaneous (irreversible) process at constant $T$ and $P$.
- **Remember the standard values:** $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹; body $T = 310.15$ K; ATP $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -30.5$ kJ/mol.
- For **KTU numerical problems**, always include units and state the assumptions (ideal gas, isothermal, closed system).
- **Sign convention:** Work done **by** the system on the surroundings is **negative** in chemistry; **positive** in physics. Confirm with the question.
- **Exam tip:** Drawing a clear $P$–$V$ diagram with the reversible curve lying **above** the irreversible line earns 1 extra mark for visualization in derivations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: First Law of Thermodynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT414 - BIOCHEMICAL THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** First Law of Thermodynamics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **First Law of Thermodynamics (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> The First Law of Thermodynamics is the law of **conservation of energy** applied to thermodynamic systems. It states that energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only be **transformed from one form to another** or **transferred between a system and its surroundings**. Mathematically, the change in the internal energy ($\Delta U$) of a closed system is equal to the heat ($q$) absorbed by the system plus the work ($w$) done on the system.

Mathematically:
$$\Delta U = q + w$$

Where:
- $\Delta U$ = Change in internal energy of the system
- $q$ = Heat exchanged between system and surroundings
- $w$ = Work exchanged between system and surroundings

> [!NOTE]
> **Internal Energy ($U$)** is a **state function** — it depends only on the current state of the system (defined by $P$, $V$, $T$, and composition) and not on the path taken to reach that state. Heat ($q$) and work ($w$) are **path functions** because their values depend on the specific process path.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bank Account of Energy**
> Think of your **internal energy ($U$)** as the balance in a bank account.
> - **Heat ($q$)** is money *deposited* or *withdrawn* into the account.
> - **Work ($w$)** is interest *credited* (work done *on* the system) or *debit charges* (work done *by* the system).
> - The **First Law** says the final balance equals the initial balance plus all deposits/withdrawals. The bank cannot magically create money — it must all be accounted for. Likewise, energy is conserved!

For a **biochemical system** (e.g., a living cell undergoing a metabolic reaction), the First Law ensures that any heat released by the reaction plus the work done by the cell exactly equals the change in the chemical/internal energy of the biomolecules involved.

### 1.3 Key Terminology and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential KTU Terms (must memorize)**
> - **System**: The specific part of the universe we are studying (e.g., a reaction mixture in a beaker).
> - **Surroundings**: Everything outside the system that can exchange energy/matter with it.
> - **State Function**: A property depending only on state, not path (e.g., $U$, $H$, $T$, $P$, $V$).
> - **Path Function**: A property depending on the process path (e.g., $q$, $w$).
> - **Isolated System**: No exchange of matter or energy (constant $U$).
> - **Closed System**: Energy exchange allowed, no matter exchange.
> - **Open System**: Both matter and energy can be exchanged.

The standard unit of energy in SI is the **Joule (J)**, with **1 cal = 4.184 J**. The standard gas constant is **$R = 8.314 \text{ J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Conceptual Foundation (Why and How)

The First Law emerges from one profound physical observation: **energy is conserved** in all physical and chemical processes. Here's the logical breakdown:

**Step 1 — Identify the energy reservoirs in a system:**
A thermodynamic system possesses energy in many forms:
- **Translational kinetic energy** of molecules
- **Rotational and vibrational energy** of molecules
- **Potential energy** from intermolecular forces
- **Chemical energy** stored in bonds
- **Nuclear energy** (typically neglected in biochemical thermodynamics)

All of these are bundled together into a single macroscopic property: **Internal Energy ($U$)**.

**Step 2 — Identify the two ways energy crosses the system boundary:**
- **Heat ($q$)**: Energy transfer due to a **temperature difference** (thermal energy flow).
- **Work ($w$)**: Energy transfer due to a **force acting through a distance** (or generalized force/displacement like $P\Delta V$).

**Step 3 — Apply the conservation principle:**
The net change in $U$ must equal the *net* energy transferred across the boundary. This gives the First Law.

### 2.2 Sign Conventions (KTU Exam Favorite!)

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign convention is the #1 source of lost marks in KTU exams. Memorize this table:**

| Quantity | Positive When | Negative When |
|----------|--------------|---------------|
| $q$ | Heat is **absorbed by** the system (endothermic) | Heat is **released by** the system (exothermic) |
| $w$ | Work is **done on** the system (compression) | Work is **done by** the system (expansion) |
| $\Delta U$ | System's internal energy **increases** | System's internal energy **decreases** |

### 2.3 Enthalpy — A Biochemical Thermodynamic Workhorse

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Most biochemical reactions (in cells, calorimeters, open beakers) occur at **constant pressure**, not constant volume. This makes internal energy inconvenient. We therefore introduce **Enthalpy ($H$)**:
> $$H = U + PV$$
> $$\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta(PV) = \Delta U + P\Delta V \quad \text{(at constant } P\text{)}$$

At constant pressure, the heat exchanged equals $\Delta H$:
$$q_P = \Delta H$$
At constant volume, the heat exchanged equals $\Delta U$:
$$q_V = \Delta U$$

### 2.4 Heat Capacities

**Heat capacity at constant volume ($C_V$):**
$$C_V = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V$$

**Heat capacity at constant pressure ($C_P$):**
$$C_P = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Vital relationship for ideal gases (derivable from First Law):**
$$C_P - C_V = R$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Print this table. It covers ~80% of First Law numerical problems in KTU exams.**

| # | Formula | Description | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|--------------------|
| 1 | $\Delta U = q + w$ | First Law (energy conservation) | Always valid for closed systems |
| 2 | $H = U + PV$ | Definition of enthalpy | State function |
| 3 | $\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta n_g RT$ | Enthalpy–internal energy relation | Ideal gas, constant $T$ and $P$ |
| 4 | $q_P = \Delta H$ | Heat at constant $P$ | Constant pressure process |
| 5 | $q_V = \Delta U$ | Heat at constant $V$ | Constant volume process |
| 6 | $w = -\int P \, dV$ | Pressure–volume work | Reversible process |
| 7 | $w = -P\Delta V$ | Work at constant external $P$ | Irreversible process |
| 8 | $w = -nRT \ln\left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$ | Isothermal reversible work | Ideal gas, $T =$ constant |
| 9 | $\Delta U = n C_V \Delta T$ | Internal energy change | Ideal gas, any process |
| 10 | $\Delta H = n C_P \Delta T$ | Enthalpy change | Ideal gas, any process |
| 11 | $C_P - C_V = R$ | Heat capacity difference | 1 mole of ideal gas |
| 12 | $\Delta U_{cyclic} = 0$ | Energy change in a cycle | State function property |
| 13 | $C = \dfrac{q}{\Delta T}$ | Heat capacity (extensive) | $C_P$ or $C_V$ as appropriate |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering and Biochemical Fields

- **Calorimetry**: Measuring $\Delta H$ of combustion for fuels (petroleum, biofuels) and metabolic reactions.
- **Refrigeration/HVAC**: Applying the First Law to compressors and condensers to compute work input.
- **Engine Design**: Computing work output from combustion of gasoline, diesel, or biofuels.
- **Bioreactor Design**: Heat balance calculations for fermentation vessels — critical for maintaining optimum temperature for enzymes.
- **Pharmaceutical Industry**: Determining enthalpy of drug dissolution to ensure stability formulations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Work Done in Reversible Isothermal Expansion of an Ideal Gas

This is a **KTU frequently asked derivation**. Let us derive $w$ for the reversible isothermal expansion of $n$ moles of an ideal gas from volume $V_1$ to $V_2$ at temperature $T$.

**Step 1 — Start with the general expression for reversible work:**
By definition, infinitesimal work done by the system against external pressure during an infinitesimal volume change $dV$ is:
$$\delta w = -P_{ext} \, dV$$

**Step 2 — Apply the reversibility condition:**
For a reversible process, the external pressure equals the internal (gas) pressure at every infinitesimal step. For an ideal gas:
$$P = \frac{nRT}{V}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the work expression:**
$$\delta w = -\frac{nRT}{V} \, dV$$

**Step 4 — Integrate between limits $V_1$ and $V_2$ (constant $T$ for isothermal):**
$$w = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{nRT}{V} \, dV$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the integral (factor out constants):**
$$w = -nRT \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{dV}{V}$$

**Step 6 — Apply standard logarithm integration:**
$$\int \frac{dV}{V} = \ln V$$

So:
$$w = -nRT \left[ \ln V \right]_{V_1}^{V_2}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute limits:**
$$w = -nRT \left( \ln V_2 - \ln V_1 \right) = -nRT \ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{w_{rev,iso} = -nRT \ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)}$$

Since for isothermal ideal gas process $\Delta U = 0$ (derived below), we have $q = -w$:
$$q_{rev,iso} = +nRT \ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Proof that $\Delta U = 0$ for Isothermal Expansion of an Ideal Gas

**Step 1 — Recall the kinetic theory assumption for an ideal gas:**
For an ideal gas, the internal energy depends *only* on temperature:
$$U = U(T)$$

So:
$$\Delta U = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V \Delta T = n C_V \Delta T$$

**Step 2 — Apply the isothermal condition:**
Since $T_1 = T_2$, we have $\Delta T = 0$.

**Step 3 — Conclude:**
$$\Delta U = n C_V (0) = 0$$

**Therefore**, for any isothermal process of an ideal gas:
$$\boxed{\Delta U = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad q = -w}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Relationship $C_P - C_V = R$ for an Ideal Gas

**Step 1 — Write $H = U + PV$, then differentiate at constant $P$:**
At constant pressure, $dP = 0$, so:
$$dH = dU + P \, dV + V \, dP = dU + P \, dV$$

**Step 2 — Divide both sides by $dT$ (at constant $P$):**
$$\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_P + P\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Step 3 — Identify the left side as $C_P$ and the first term on the right as the total derivative of $U$ with respect to $T$ (since $U$ depends only on $T$ for an ideal gas):**
$$C_P = C_V + P\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Step 4 — Apply ideal gas law $V = \dfrac{nRT}{P}$:**
$$\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{nR}{P}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute:**
$$C_P = C_V + P \cdot \frac{nR}{P} = C_V + nR$$

**Step 6 — For 1 mole ($n = 1$):**
$$\boxed{C_P - C_V = R}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Relation Between $\Delta H$ and $\Delta U$

**Step 1 — Start from $H = U + PV$ and take the change:**
$$\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta(PV)$$

**Step 2 — For a reaction at constant $T$ and $P$ involving only ideal gases:**
$$\Delta(PV) = \Delta(nRT) = RT \, \Delta n_g$$
where $\Delta n_g$ is the change in the number of gaseous moles.

**Step 3 — Final Result:**
$$\boxed{\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta n_g \, RT}$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Process-by-Process Summary

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick-Reference: First Law quantities for different processes (ideal gas)**

| Process | Condition | $\Delta U$ | $\Delta H$ | $q$ | $w$ |
|---------|-----------|------------|------------|-----|-----|
| Isothermal reversible | $T$ = constant | $0$ | $0$ | $+nRT \ln(V_2/V_1)$ | $-nRT \ln(V_2/V_1)$ |
| Isochoric (constant $V$) | $V$ = constant | $n C_V \Delta T$ | $n C_P \Delta T$ | $n C_V \Delta T$ | $0$ |
| Isobaric (constant $P$) | $P$ = constant | $n C_V \Delta T$ | $n C_P \Delta T$ | $n C_P \Delta T$ | $-P\Delta V = -nR\Delta T$ |
| Adiabatic reversible | $q = 0$ | $n C_V \Delta T$ | $n C_P \Delta T$ | $0$ | $n C_V \Delta T$ |
| Free expansion | $P_{ext} = 0$ | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of the First Law

The following Mermaid diagram maps the conceptual flow of energy through a thermodynamic system, illustrating how heat and work combine to alter internal energy — a foundational KTU visualization.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Closed Thermodynamic System] --> B[Initial State: U1]
    B --> C{Process Path}
    C --> D[Heat q absorbed from surroundings]
    C --> E[Work w done on or by system]
    D --> F[Internal Energy Final State: U2]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Conservation: Delta U equals q plus w]
    G --> H[State Functions: U and H are path independent]
    G --> I[Path Functions: q and w depend on path]
    H --> J[Enthalpy H equals U plus PV]
    J --> K[At constant P: q equals Delta H]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Enthalpy–Internal Energy Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Internal Energy U] --> B[Enthalpy H]
    B --> C[Constant Volume Heat: qV equals Delta U]
    A --> D[Constant Pressure Heat: qP equals Delta H]
    B --> D
    D --> E[Heat Capacity Cp at constant P]
    A --> C
    C --> F[Heat Capacity Cv at constant V]
    E --> G[Cp minus Cv equals R for ideal gas]
    F --> G
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Classification of Thermodynamic Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thermodynamic System] --> B[Isolated: No matter or energy exchange]
    A --> C[Closed: Energy exchange only]
    A --> D[Open: Both matter and energy exchange]
    C --> E[First Law applies: Delta U equals q plus w]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Energy conserved across all process types]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a block diagram and not a physical picture?**
> The First Law is fundamentally about *abstract* energy bookkeeping. A block diagram showing energy flow between conceptual entities (system, surroundings, internal energy, heat, work) communicates the principle more accurately than any physical drawing. Mermaid is therefore the optimal schematic tool for this KTU Module 1 topic.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

State the First Law of Thermodynamics. Explain why internal energy is a state function but heat and work are path functions.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (1 mark):** The First Law states that energy can neither be created nor destroyed. For a closed system, the change in internal energy equals the heat absorbed plus the work done on the system:
$$\Delta U = q + w$$

**Internal energy as state function (1 mark):** $U$ depends only on the **state variables** ($P$, $V$, $T$, composition). For any cyclic process, $\oint dU = 0$, so $\Delta U$ is the same regardless of path taken between two states.

**Heat and work as path functions (1 mark):** $q$ and $w$ depend on *how* the process is carried out. For example, the work done in isothermal expansion of an ideal gas from $V_1$ to $V_2$ is different in reversible vs. irreversible processes. Hence, $q$ and $w$ are path-dependent quantities.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

Define enthalpy. Show that for an ideal gas reaction at constant temperature, $\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta n_g RT$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Enthalpy is a thermodynamic state function defined as:
$$H = U + PV$$

**Derivation (2 marks):** For a reaction at constant $T$ and $P$:
$$\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta(PV)$$
For ideal gases, $PV = nRT$, so:
$$\Delta(PV) = \Delta(n_g RT) = RT \, \Delta n_g$$
Therefore:
$$\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta n_g RT$$

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — INTERNAL CHOICE**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the work done during the **reversible isothermal expansion** of $n$ moles of an ideal gas from volume $V_1$ to $V_2$ at temperature $T$. Hence, obtain the heat exchanged. (7 marks)

**(b)** A sample of **2 mol of an ideal gas** undergoes reversible isothermal expansion from $5 \text{ L}$ to $20 \text{ L}$ at $300 \text{ K}$. Calculate $w$, $q$, $\Delta U$, and $\Delta H$. ($R = 8.314 \text{ J K}^{-1}\text{mol}^{-1}$). (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to **Derivation 1** in Section 3.1 above.

- Step 1: Write $\delta w = -P_{ext} \, dV$ (1 mark)
- Step 2: Apply reversibility: $P = nRT/V$ (1 mark)
- Step 3: Substitute and integrate: $w = -nRT \ln(V_2/V_1)$ (3 marks)
- Step 4: Use $\Delta U = 0$ for isothermal ideal gas, so $q = -w$ (2 marks)

**Final answer:**
$$w_{rev,iso} = -nRT \ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right), \quad q_{rev,iso} = +nRT \ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

Given: $n = 2 \text{ mol}$, $V_1 = 5 \text{ L}$, $V_2 = 20 \text{ L}$, $T = 300 \text{ K}$.

**Step 1 — Calculate work (3 marks):**
$$w = -nRT \ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$
$$w = -(2)(8.314)(300) \ln\left(\frac{20}{5}\right)$$
$$w = -(4988.4) \ln(4) = -(4988.4)(1.3863)$$
$$\boxed{w \approx -6915 \text{ J} = -6.915 \text{ kJ}}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $\Delta U$ (1 mark):**
$$\Delta U = 0 \quad \text{(isothermal, ideal gas)}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate $q$ (2 marks):**
$$q = -w = +6.915 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate $\Delta H$ (1 mark):**
$$\Delta H = 0 \quad \text{(isothermal, ideal gas, since } H \text{ depends only on } T\text{)}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**(a)** Define **heat capacity at constant pressure ($C_P$)** and **heat capacity at constant volume ($C_V$)**. Derive the relation $C_P - C_V = R$ for one mole of an ideal gas. (7 marks)

**(b)** The combustion of 1 g of glucose releases approximately **15.6 kJ** of heat at constant volume in a bomb calorimeter at $25^{\circ}\text{C}$. Calculate $\Delta U$, $\Delta H$, and the heat that would be released at constant pressure ($R = 8.314 \text{ J K}^{-1}\text{mol}^{-1}$, molar mass of glucose = 180 g/mol). The balanced reaction is:
$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6(s) + 6\text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow 6\text{CO}_2(g) + 6\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$$
(7 marks)

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Definition and Derivation (7 marks):**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
$$C_V = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V, \quad C_P = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Derivation (5 marks):** Refer to **Derivation 3** in Section 3.3 above.
- Step 1: $dH = dU + P dV + V dP$ (1 mark)
- Step 2: Divide by $dT$ at constant $P$: $C_P = C_V + P(\partial V/\partial T)_P$ (1 mark)
- Step 3: For ideal gas $V = RT/P$, so $(\partial V/\partial T)_P = R/P$ (1 mark)
- Step 4: $C_P = C_V + P \cdot (R/P) = C_V + R$ (1 mark)
- Step 5: Final boxed result (1 mark)

$$\boxed{C_P - C_V = R}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** Heat released at constant volume = 15.6 kJ per 1 g glucose.
At constant volume, $q_V = \Delta U$, so for 1 g: $\Delta U = -15.6 \text{ kJ}$.

**Step 1 — Calculate moles of glucose (1 mark):**
$$n = \frac{1 \text{ g}}{180 \text{ g/mol}} = 5.556 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $\Delta U$ per mole (1 mark):**
$$\Delta U = \frac{-15.6 \text{ kJ}}{5.556 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol}} = -2808 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Identify $\Delta n_g$ (2 marks):**

From the balanced equation:
- Moles of gaseous products: $6 \text{ CO}_2 = 6$
- Moles of gaseous reactants: $6 \text{ O}_2 = 6$
- Glucose is solid, water is liquid — both excluded.

$$\Delta n_g = 6 - 6 = 0$$

**Step 4 — Calculate $\Delta H$ (2 marks):**
$$\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta n_g RT = -2808 + 0 = -2808 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 5 — State the constant-pressure heat (1 mark):**
At constant pressure, $q_P = \Delta H = -2808 \text{ kJ/mol}$.

For 1 g: $q_P = -15.6 \text{ kJ}$ (same magnitude since $\Delta n_g = 0$).

$$\boxed{\Delta U = \Delta H = -2808 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$

---

### **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks on First Law problems (KTU Board Examiner Observations):**
>
> 1. **Sign convention errors** — Writing $w = +P\Delta V$ instead of $w = -P\Delta V$. Always check: expansion → system does work → $w$ is *negative* under the thermodynamic convention.
> 2. **Confusing $\Delta U$ with $\Delta H$** — At constant $P$, $q = \Delta H$ (NOT $\Delta U$). At constant $V$, $q = \Delta U$.
> 3. **Forgetting the natural logarithm** — In isothermal problems, $w = -nRT \ln(V_2/V_1)$, *not* $-nRT \cdot (V_2/V_1)$.
> 4. **Incorrect $\Delta n_g$ calculation** — Only count *gaseous* moles; ignore solids and liquids.
> 5. **Mixing up $C_P$ and $C_V$ formulas** — Remember $\Delta U = nC_V\Delta T$ and $\Delta H = nC_P\Delta T$ regardless of process path (for ideal gases).
> 6. **Forgetting units** — Always report energy in **Joules or kilojoules** with correct prefixes.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — First Law of Thermodynamics**
>
> - [ ] First Law equation: $\Delta U = q + w$ (closed system)
> - [ ] State functions: $U$, $H$, $T$, $P$, $V$, $S$, $G$ — path independent
> - [ ] Path functions: $q$, $w$ — path dependent
> - [ ] Sign convention: $+q$ = heat absorbed, $+w$ = work done *on* system
> - [ ] Enthalpy: $H = U + PV$, and at constant $P$: $q_P = \Delta H$
> - [ ] For ideal gas isothermal process: $\Delta U = \Delta H = 0$, so $q = -w$
> - [ ] Reversible isothermal work: $w = -nRT \ln(V_2/V_1)$
> - [ ] Reversible adiabatic: $q = 0$, $\Delta U = w = nC_V\Delta T$
> - [ ] $\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta n_g RT$ (ideal gas, constant $T$ and $P$)
> - [ ] $C_P - C_V = R$ (per mole of ideal gas)
> - [ ] For ideal gases: $\Delta U = nC_V\Delta T$ and $\Delta H = nC_P\Delta T$ (any path)
> - [ ] Adiabatic relation: $TV^{\gamma-1} = $ constant, where $\gamma = C_P/C_V$
> - [ ] Always state units and signs explicitly in numerical answers
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internal Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT414 - BIOCHEMICAL THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Internal Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In the rigorous framework of **Classical Thermodynamics** (and by extension, **Biochemical Thermodynamics**), **Internal Energy** is defined as the **total energy contained within a thermodynamic system**, excluding the kinetic energy of the system as a whole and the potential energy due to its external position. It is a **macroscopic state function**, customarily denoted by the symbol **$U$** (sometimes $E$), and its SI unit is the **Joule (J)**.

Mathematically, the internal energy of a system is the sum of all microscopic energies of its constituent particles:

$$U = \sum_{i} E_{\text{kinetic}, i} + \sum_{i} E_{\text{potential}, i}$$

For a **biochemical system** (a single protein, a cell, a metabolic mixture, or a bioreactor), the microscopic contributions include:

- **Translational kinetic energy** of molecules (Brownian motion in cytoplasm).
- **Rotational and vibrational kinetic energy** of bonds.
- **Potential energy** stored in chemical bonds (covalent, ionic, hydrogen, van der Waals).
- **Electrostatic potential energy** of charged species (e.g., $\text{H}^+$, $\text{Ca}^{2+}$, phosphate groups).
- **Nuclear binding energy** of atoms (relevant for radioactive tracers used in PET imaging).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** Internal energy is a **State Function**, meaning $\Delta U$ depends *only* on the initial and final states of the system, not on the path taken. The differential $dU$ is therefore an **exact differential**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Energy Bank Account"

Imagine your body (a biochemical system) as a **bank account** holding energy in multiple denominations:

| Bank Account Analogy | Thermodynamic Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Cash in wallet (liquid cash) | Translational kinetic energy of molecules |
| Fixed deposits (locked-in money) | Chemical bond energy (ATP, glucose C–C bonds) |
| Gold in locker (stored value) | Potential energy of non-covalent interactions (folding energy of proteins) |
| Account balance at any instant | Total **Internal Energy ($U$)** |
| Change in balance ($\Delta$ balance) | $\Delta U$ — depends only on opening & closing balance, not the transactions in between |

Just as your bank balance changes only by the **net difference** between deposits (heat added) and withdrawals (work done), the internal energy of a system changes by the **net difference** between heat absorbed and work done by the system.

> [!NOTE]
> The **absolute value** of $U$ cannot be measured experimentally — only the **change** $\Delta U$ is accessible through calorimetry or the First Law. This is one of the most commonly tested KTU concepts.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants are central to internal energy calculations in biochemical systems:

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \ \text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$
- **Standard Molar Volume (STP):** $V_m = 22.414 \ \text{L} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1}$
- **Molar Heat Capacity at Constant Volume (ideal gas):** $C_v = \frac{3}{2}R$ (monatomic), $\frac{5}{2}R$ (diatomic)
- **Avogadro's Number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \ \text{mol}^{-1}$
- **Standard Biochemical Reference Temperature:** $T = 298.15 \ \text{K}$ (25 °C)
- **Standard Pressure:** $P = 1 \ \text{bar} = 10^5 \ \text{Pa}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Microscopic Constituents of Internal Energy

For an **ideal gas**, internal energy depends exclusively on temperature. For **real biochemical systems** (liquids, solids, dense cellular fluids), the picture is far richer:

1. **Thermal (Sensible) Energy** — Energy due to molecular motion. Function of temperature.
2. **Chemical Energy** — Energy stored in the electronic structure of atoms; released/absorbed during bond rearrangement (e.g., ATP hydrolysis, glycolysis).
3. **Configurational Energy** — Energy associated with molecular conformation (protein folding, nucleic acid base stacking).
4. **Intermolecular Potential Energy** — Van der Waals, hydrogen bonding, hydrophobic interactions.
5. **Electrostatic Energy** — Charge–charge, charge–dipole interactions in ionic solutions.

> [!NOTE]
> In **Biochemical Thermodynamics (PEBBT414)**, the chemical and configurational components dominate. This is why $\Delta U$ for a biochemical reaction is rarely zero, even at constant temperature.

---

## 2.2 The First Law of Thermodynamics (Energy Conservation)

The First Law is the operational law governing how internal energy changes:

$$\Delta U = Q - W$$

Where:
- $\Delta U$ = Change in internal energy of the system
- $Q$ = Heat absorbed **by** the system (positive when supplied)
- $W$ = Work done **by** the system (positive when system expands against surroundings)

In differential form:

$$dU = \delta Q - \delta W$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction (Frequently Tested in KTU):**
> - $dU$ is written with a **straight 'd'** because $U$ is a **state function** → exact differential.
> - $\delta Q$ and $\delta W$ are written with a **curly 'δ'** because heat and work are **path functions** → inexact differentials.

---

## 2.3 Internal Energy as a Function of State Variables

By the theorem of exact differentials, if $U$ is a function of $T$ and $V$:

$$U = U(T, V) \implies dU = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V dT + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T dV$$

Similarly, treating $U$ as a function of $T$ and $P$:

$$U = U(T, P) \implies dU = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial P}\right)_T dP$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $U = U_{\text{kin}} + U_{\text{pot}}$ | Total internal energy decomposition | J |
| 2 | $\Delta U = Q - W$ | First Law of Thermodynamics | J |
| 3 | $dU = \delta Q - \delta W$ | Differential First Law | J |
| 4 | $C_v = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V$ | Heat capacity at constant volume | J·K$^{-1}$ |
| 5 | $dU = n C_v \, dT$ | For ideal gas, $U$ depends only on $T$ | J |
| 6 | $\Delta U = n C_v \, \Delta T$ | Integrated form for ideal gas | J |
| 7 | $H = U + PV$ | Enthalpy definition | J |
| 8 | $\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta(PV)$ | Enthalpy change for any process | J |
| 9 | $\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta n_g \, R T$ | For ideal gas reactions | J |
| 10 | $\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T = 0$ | Joule's Law (ideal gas) | J·m$^{-3}$ |
| 11 | $U = U(T)$ only | For ideal gas (Joule's experiment result) | J |
| 12 | $W = \int P_{\text{ext}} \, dV$ | PV-work (expansion/compression) | J |
| 13 | $\Delta U = C_v (T_2 - T_1)$ | Constant-volume process | J |
| 14 | $\mu_J = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_U$ | **Joule Coefficient** (real gases) | K·m$^{-3}$ |
| 15 | $\mu_{JT} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_H$ | **Joule-Thomson Coefficient** | K·Pa$^{-1}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The symbols $\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T$ and $\mu_{JT}$ are **guaranteed 14-mark questions** in KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the conditions under which each is zero/non-zero.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility in Biotechnology

Internal energy is the **conceptual backbone** of nearly every downstream calculation in biochemical engineering:

- **Calorimetry:** $\Delta U$ is directly measurable in a bomb calorimeter (constant $V$) → used to compute calorific value of biomass fuels (bioethanol, biodiesel).
- **Reactor Design:** Energy balances on bioreactors (CSTR, PFR) use $\Delta U$ to predict temperature rise during exothermic fermentation.
- **Metabolic Engineering:** Cellular energy charge (ATP/ADP ratio) is a direct manifestation of internal energy redistribution.
- **Cryopreservation:** Predicting energy changes during freezing of biological tissues.
- **Pharmaceutical Stability:** Estimating shelf-life based on internal energy drift in stored drugs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: First Law of Thermodynamics from Energy Conservation

### Statement:
For a closed system undergoing a cyclic process, the net heat absorbed equals the net work done.

### Proof:

**Step 1:** Consider a closed system undergoing a cycle $1 \to 2 \to 1$. By the principle of conservation of energy, energy can neither be created nor destroyed.

**Step 2:** In path $1 \to 2$, the system absorbs heat $Q_{12}$ and does work $W_{12}$. The internal energy changes from $U_1$ to $U_2$.

$$U_2 - U_1 = Q_{12} - W_{12}$$

**Step 3:** In path $2 \to 1$ (return), the system absorbs heat $Q_{21}$ and does work $W_{21}$, with internal energy returning from $U_2$ to $U_1$:

$$U_1 - U_2 = Q_{21} - W_{21}$$

**Step 4:** Adding the two equations (since $U$ is a state function, its cyclic integral is zero):

$$\begin{aligned}
(U_2 - U_1) + (U_1 - U_2) &= (Q_{12} - W_{12}) + (Q_{21} - W_{21}) \\
0 &= (Q_{12} + Q_{21}) - (W_{12} + W_{21}) \\
\oint dU &= 0 \\
\oint \delta Q &= \oint \delta W
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** For an **open path** (not a cycle), the difference between $Q$ and $W$ depends only on initial and final states. Therefore, a state function $U$ must exist such that:

$$U_2 - U_1 = \Delta U = Q - W$$

> This is the **First Law of Thermodynamics** in its most fundamental form. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Internal Energy Change of an Ideal Gas

### Claim:
For an ideal gas, $\Delta U$ depends only on temperature, and $\Delta U = n C_v \Delta T$.

### Proof:

**Step 1:** For a closed system, the First Law in differential form is:

$$dU = \delta Q - \delta W$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the expressions for $\delta Q$ and $\delta W$ in terms of state variables. For any process:

$$\delta Q = dU + P \, dV$$

When the process is **reversible** and conducted at constant volume, $\delta W = 0$ and $dV = 0$. The heat supplied equals the change in internal energy:

$$\delta Q_V = dU$$

**Step 3:** By definition, the molar heat capacity at constant volume is:

$$C_v = \frac{1}{n}\left(\frac{\partial Q}{\partial T}\right)_V = \frac{1}{n}\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V$$

**Step 4:** Therefore, the differential change in $U$ at constant $V$ is:

$$dU = n C_v \, dT$$

**Step 5:** This is **a pure function of $T$ only** (no $V$ dependence). For an ideal gas, $C_v$ is also independent of temperature (for monoatomic and diatomic gases within moderate ranges). Therefore, integrating between limits $T_1$ and $T_2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\int_{U_1}^{U_2} dU &= n C_v \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT \\
\Delta U &= n C_v \, (T_2 - T_1) = n C_v \, \Delta T
\end{aligned}$$

> Note that this result is **independent of the path taken** — a hallmark of state functions. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Relationship Between $\Delta H$ and $\Delta U$ for Ideal Gas Reactions

### Claim:
For an ideal gas reaction at constant temperature, $\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta n_g \, R T$.

### Proof:

**Step 1:** Start from the definition of enthalpy:

$$H = U + PV$$

**Step 2:** For a process at constant pressure, the change in enthalpy is:

$$\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta(PV) = \Delta U + P\Delta V \quad \text{(constant } P\text{)}$$

**Step 3:** For an ideal gas, apply the ideal gas law $PV = nRT$. At constant $T$ and $P$:

$$\Delta(PV) = \Delta(nRT) = RT \, \Delta n \quad \text{(constant } T\text{)}$$

Where $\Delta n = n_{\text{products}} - n_{\text{reactants}}$ is the change in **gaseous** moles (denoted $\Delta n_g$).

**Step 4:** Substituting back:

$$\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta n_g \, R T$$

> **Biochemical Note:** For liquid-phase reactions (most biochemical reactions in solution), $\Delta V \approx 0$, so $\Delta H \approx \Delta U$. This is why the approximation $\Delta H \approx \Delta U$ is heavily used in enzyme kinetics and metabolism studies. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Joule's Law and the Joule Coefficient

### Claim:
For an ideal gas, $\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T = 0$ (Joule's Law).

### Proof:

**Step 1:** Consider $U$ as a function of $T$ and $V$. The total differential is:

$$dU = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V dT + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T dV$$

**Step 2:** From the First Law applied reversibly to a closed system:

$$dU = T \, dS - P \, dV$$

**Step 3:** Now express $S$ as a function of $T$ and $V$, and substitute:

$$dS = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_V dT + \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T dV$$

Using the Maxwell relation $\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$:

$$dU = T \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_V dT + \left[T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V - P\right] dV$$

**Step 4:** By the Euler reciprocity condition for exact differentials (since $U$ is a state function):

$$\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T = T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V - P$$

**Step 5:** For an ideal gas, $PV = nRT$, so $\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = \frac{nR}{V}$. Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T &= T \cdot \frac{nR}{V} - P \\
&= \frac{nRT}{V} - P \\
&= P - P \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conclusion:** For an ideal gas, internal energy is **independent of volume** at constant temperature. Hence $U = U(T)$ only. This is the celebrated **Joule's Law**. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> For **real gases** and **condensed biochemical systems** (liquids, polymers, proteins), $\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T \neq 0$. The Joule coefficient $\mu_J = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_U$ is a measure of non-ideality.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
