<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Internal Energy. Why is it considered a state function? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Internal energy $U$ of a thermodynamic system is the **sum of all microscopic kinetic and potential energies** of the constituent particles (translational, rotational, vibrational, chemical bond, electrostatic, and nuclear) **excluding** the macroscopic kinetic and potential energies of the system as a whole.

It is a **state function** because its change $\Delta U$ between two equilibrium states depends only on the **initial and final states** of the system, not on the path taken. Mathematically, this means $dU$ is an **exact differential**, and the cyclic integral $\oint dU = 0$.

**(3 marks)** — *Keywords highlighted; definition + state function justification in 2 lines each.*

---

### Question 2: Distinguish between exact and inexact differentials with reference to internal energy and heat. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Exact Differential $dU$ | Inexact Differential $\delta Q$ |
|---|---|---|
| Function type | State function ($U$, $H$, $S$, $G$) | Path function ($Q$, $W$) |
| Notation | Straight 'd' | Curly 'δ' |
| Path dependence | Independent | Dependent |
| Cyclic integral | $\oint dU = 0$ | $\oint \delta Q \neq 0$ in general |
| Integrability | Can be integrated along any path | Must specify path |

For internal energy: $dU = TdS - PdV$ is a perfect differential. Heat $\delta Q = TdS$ alone is not integrable as a state function.

**(3 marks)** — *Tabular format for clarity; examiner-friendly.*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[INTERNAL CHOICE]** — Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Derive the relationship $\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta n_g RT$ for an ideal gas reaction. State clearly the assumptions involved. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** 5 moles of an ideal diatomic gas is heated from $300 \ \text{K}$ to $500 \ \text{K}$ at constant volume. Calculate $\Delta U$, $Q$, and $W$ for the process. Given: $C_v = \frac{5}{2}R$. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Assumption 1:** The reaction involves only **ideal gases**.

**Assumption 2:** The process is at **constant temperature** $T$ and **constant pressure** $P$.

**Assumption 3:** The gases obey the **ideal gas equation** $PV = nRT$.

**Step 1:** Enthalpy is defined as:
$$H = U + PV$$

**Step 2:** For a process at constant $P$:
$$\Delta H = \Delta U + P\Delta V = \Delta U + \Delta(PV)$$

**Step 3:** Using the ideal gas law, $PV = nRT$. At constant $T$ and $P$:
$$\Delta(PV) = \Delta(nRT) = RT \cdot \Delta n_g$$

where $\Delta n_g = n_{\text{products}} - n_{\text{reactants}}$ (gaseous moles only).

**Step 4:** Substituting:
$$\boxed{\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta n_g \, R T}$$

> This relation is the **most frequently tested 7-marker** in KTU 2024 ESE.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- $n = 5$ mol
- $T_1 = 300 \ \text{K}$, $T_2 = 500 \ \text{K}$
- $C_v = \frac{5}{2}R$
- Constant volume process

**Step 1: Calculate $\Delta U$:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta U &= n C_v \Delta T \\
&= 5 \times \frac{5}{2} \times 8.314 \times (500 - 300) \\
&= 5 \times 2.5 \times 8.314 \times 200 \\
&= 5 \times 2.5 \times 1662.8 \\
&= 20785 \ \text{J} \\
&\approx 20.785 \ \text{kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Calculate $W$ (work done by the system):**

At constant volume, $dV = 0$, so:
$$W = \int P \, dV = 0$$

**Step 3: Calculate $Q$ (heat absorbed):**

Using the First Law:
$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \Delta U + W \\
&= 20785 + 0 \\
&= 20785 \ \text{J} \\
&\approx 20.785 \ \text{kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Final Answers:** $\Delta U = +20.785 \ \text{kJ}$, $W = 0$, $Q = +20.785 \ \text{kJ}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a)** State and prove the First Law of Thermodynamics. Explain why $dU$ is an exact differential but $\delta Q$ and $\delta W$ are not. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For an isothermal reversible expansion of 2 mol of an ideal gas from $V_1 = 5 \ \text{L}$ to $V_2 = 15 \ \text{L}$ at $T = 300 \ \text{K}$, calculate $W$, $Q$, and $\Delta U$. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Statement:** Energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only be transformed from one form to another. Mathematically: $\Delta U = Q - W$.

**Proof:** (See Section 3.1 of these notes for the complete 5-step derivation using the cyclic path argument.)

> **Why $dU$ is exact but $\delta Q$ and $\delta W$ are inexact:**
> - $U$ is a **single-valued function** of state variables, so its differential integrates to a definite value independent of path.
> - $Q$ and $W$ are **process-dependent** — they describe energy *in transit*, not stored. The same change in state can be achieved by many different combinations of $Q$ and $W$.
> - Formally, $dU = \delta Q - \delta W$ has the **straight d** on the LHS (exact differential) but **curly δ** on the RHS (inexact differentials). This is a fundamental asymmetry of the First Law.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- $n = 2$ mol
- $V_1 = 5 \ \text{L}$, $V_2 = 15 \ \text{L}$
- $T = 300 \ \text{K}$ (isothermal)
- Ideal gas, reversible process

**Step 1: Calculate $\Delta U$:**

For an ideal gas in an isothermal process, $T$ is constant, so:
$$\Delta T = 0 \implies \Delta U = n C_v \Delta T = 0$$

**Step 2: Calculate $W$ (reversible isothermal):**

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= nRT \ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) \\
&= 2 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times \ln\left(\frac{15}{5}\right) \\
&= 2 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times \ln(3) \\
&= 4988.4 \times 1.0986 \\
&\approx 5480.0 \ \text{J} \\
&\approx 5.48 \ \text{kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Calculate $Q$ (using the First Law):**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \Delta U + W \\
&= 0 + 5480.0 \\
&= 5480.0 \ \text{J} \approx 5.48 \ \text{kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Final Answers:** $\Delta U = 0$, $W = +5.48 \ \text{kJ}$, $Q = +5.48 \ \text{kJ}$.
> **Biochemical Interpretation:** The system absorbs heat from the surroundings and converts it entirely into work, with no change in internal energy — analogous to an isothermal cellular process where thermal energy drives mechanical work (e.g., molecular motor operation).

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Internal Energy Problems:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Mix-up (–2 marks):** Always follow the chemistry convention: $Q > 0$ when heat is *absorbed*, $W > 0$ when work is done *by* the system. Many students mistakenly use the physics sign convention ($W$ done *on* the system) and lose marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting $\Delta n_g$ in $\Delta H$ vs $\Delta U$ (–3 marks):** When applying $\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta n_g RT$, students often use total moles (including liquid/solid species) instead of **gaseous** moles. Only gaseous species contribute to $\Delta n_g$ in this formula.
>
> 3. **Using $C_p$ instead of $C_v$ for constant-volume process (–2 marks):** A common error. For constant-$V$, use $C_v$; for constant-$P$, use $C_p$. Mixing them up is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
>
> 4. **Forgetting units in final answer (–1 mark):** Always quote $\Delta U$, $Q$, $W$ in **J** or **kJ**, and $C_v$ in J·mol$^{-1}$·K$^{-1}$.
>
> 5. **Not justifying state-function property (–1 mark):** In definition questions, merely writing "it is a state function" without citing the cyclic integral $\oint dU = 0$ or path-independence costs a mark.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Internal energy $U$** is the total microscopic energy (kinetic + potential) **inside** a system.
- ✅ It is a **state function** → exact differential $dU$ → $\oint dU = 0$.
- ✅ **First Law:** $\Delta U = Q - W$ (chemistry sign convention).
- ✅ For an **ideal gas**: $U$ depends on $T$ **only** → $\Delta U = nC_v \Delta T$.
- ✅ **Joule's Law** (ideal gas): $\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T = 0$.
- ✅ **Enthalpy relation:** $H = U + PV \Rightarrow \Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta n_g RT$ (ideal gas, constant $T$, $P$).
- ✅ $C_v$ and $C_p$ are related by **Mayer's relation**: $C_p - C_v = R$ (per mole, ideal gas).
- ✅ For **isothermal** ideal gas processes: $\Delta U = 0$, so $Q = W$.
- ✅ For **adiabatic** processes: $Q = 0$, so $\Delta U = -W$.
- ✅ For **constant-volume** processes: $W = 0$, so $Q = \Delta U = nC_v \Delta T$.
- ✅ For **constant-pressure** processes: $Q_p = \Delta H = nC_p \Delta T$ and $\Delta U = nC_v \Delta T$.
- ✅ **Biochemical reactions** (solution phase): $\Delta H \approx \Delta U$ since $\Delta V \approx 0$.
- ✅ **Bomb calorimeter** measures $\Delta U$ directly; **coffee-cup calorimeter** measures $\Delta H$ directly.
- ✅ The **Joule coefficient** $\mu_J$ and **Joule-Thomson coefficient** $\mu_{JT}$ describe non-ideal behaviour.
- ✅ **Maxwell relations** can be derived from $dU = TdS - PdV$ — memorize the parent potentials and their natural variables.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for KTU ESE:** *"**U**se **H**eat, **W**ork to change **U**: $\Delta U = Q - W$. Internal energy is **U**nique to state, while $Q$ and $W$ are **Q**uestionable and **W**eak (path-dependent)."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Enthalpy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT414 - BIOCHEMICAL THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Enthalpy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Enthalpy (H)** is a thermodynamic state function of a system, defined as the sum of the internal energy (U) of the system and the product of its pressure (P) and volume (V). Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$H = U + PV$$

where:
- **H** = Enthalpy (measured in Joules, J, or kilojoules, kJ)
- **U** = Internal energy of the system (J)
- **P** = Pressure of the system (Pa, or atm in chemistry)
- **V** = Volume of the system (m³, or L in chemistry)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Enthalpy is a *state function*, meaning the change in enthalpy (ΔH) depends only on the **initial and final states** of the system, not on the path taken. This property is critical for applying Hess's Law in biochemical energetics.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are boiling water in an open pot on a stove. The stove delivers heat to the water, but the water is not held at a fixed volume — it expands as steam escapes into the atmosphere. In this constant-pressure scenario, not all the heat supplied goes into raising the *internal energy* of the water. Some of the energy is "used" by the system to do **pressure-volume (PV) work** against the atmosphere (pushing back the surrounding air to make room for expansion).

**Enthalpy** is the bookkeeping quantity that captures *both*:
1. The energy stored inside the molecules (internal energy, U), and
2. The energy needed to "make space" for the system at constant pressure (PV work).

So when a chemist or biochemist measures heat flow at constant pressure (the most common condition in biological reactions, which occur in open vessels or at atmospheric pressure), the quantity they are actually measuring is the **change in enthalpy (ΔH)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important in biochemistry?**
> Biological reactions (glycolysis, ATP hydrolysis, protein folding) occur in aqueous solutions under essentially constant atmospheric pressure. The energy budget of metabolism is therefore expressed in terms of **ΔH**, not just ΔU. Calorimeters used in bioenergetics directly measure ΔH, not ΔU.

### 1.3 Standard State Conventions in KTU 2024 Scheme

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value/Convention |
|---|---|---|
| $T^{\circ}$ | Standard temperature | **298.15 K (25 °C)** |
| $P^{\circ}$ | Standard pressure | **1 bar (10⁵ Pa)** |
| $\Delta_f H^{\circ}$ | Standard enthalpy of formation | Heat absorbed when **1 mole** of compound is formed from elements in their reference states |
| $\Delta_c H^{\circ}$ | Standard enthalpy of combustion | Heat released when **1 mole** is burnt completely in O₂ |
| $\Delta_r H^{\circ}$ | Standard enthalpy of reaction | Heat change for a reaction with all species in standard states |
| $\Delta_{rxn} H^{\circ}$ | Biochemical standard enthalpy | $\Delta_r H^{\circ \prime}$ at pH = 7 (biochemical convention) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Origin of Enthalpy from the First Law

The **First Law of Thermodynamics** states:

$$\Delta U = q + w$$

For a process occurring at **constant pressure**, the only work involved is the expansion (or compression) work:

$$w = -P_{ext} \Delta V = -P \Delta V \quad (\text{for constant P, reversible or irreversible on the system})$$

Substituting into the First Law:

$$\Delta U = q_p - P \Delta V$$

Rearranging to isolate the heat absorbed at constant pressure ($q_p$):

$$q_p = \Delta U + P \Delta V$$

Since the process is at constant P, the change in the product $PV$ is:

$$\Delta(PV) = P \Delta V + V \Delta P = P \Delta V \quad (\text{because } \Delta P = 0)$$

Therefore:

$$q_p = \Delta U + \Delta(PV) = \Delta(U + PV) = \Delta H$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result:** At constant pressure, the heat absorbed by the system equals the change in enthalpy. This is the single most important identity in thermochemistry: $\Delta H = q_p$.

### 2.2 Logical Step-by-Step Breakdown

- **Step 1:** A thermodynamic process releases or absorbs heat *q*.
- **Step 2:** If pressure is constant, the system also performs *PV work* against the surroundings.
- **Step 3:** The First Law requires that $\Delta U = q - w$; only the portion $q - P\Delta V$ stays as internal energy.
- **Step 4:** The leftover term $P\Delta V$ is bundled with $\Delta U$ to define a new state function, $H = U + PV$.
- **Step 5:** Therefore, $q_p = \Delta H$, and the heat measured in a coffee-cup calorimeter is the enthalpy change.

### 2.3 Temperature Dependence — Kirchhoff's Law

When a reaction is studied at two different temperatures, the enthalpy change varies according to:

$$\Delta H(T_2) = \Delta H(T_1) + \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \Delta C_p \, dT$$

For a small temperature range where $\Delta C_p$ is approximately constant:

$$\Delta H(T_2) - \Delta H(T_1) = \Delta C_p (T_2 - T_1)$$

where $\Delta C_p$ is the difference in heat capacities (products − reactants):

$$\Delta C_p = \sum_i \nu_i C_{p,i}(\text{products}) - \sum_j \nu_j C_{p,j}(\text{reactants})$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Name | Conditions / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = U + PV$ | Definition of enthalpy | State function; H in **J** or **kJ** |
| 2 | $\Delta H = q_p$ | Heat at constant pressure | Applies only when $P = \text{const}$ |
| 3 | $\Delta H = \Delta U + P \Delta V$ | Relation to internal energy change | Valid for any constant-P process |
| 4 | $\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta n_g RT$ | Ideal gas version | $\Delta n_g$ = moles of gaseous product − reactant |
| 5 | $\Delta_r H = \sum \nu_i \Delta_f H_i(\text{products}) - \sum \nu_j \Delta_f H_j(\text{reactants})$ | Formation method | $\nu$ = stoichiometric coefficient |
| 6 | $\Delta H(T_2) = \Delta H(T_1) + \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \Delta C_p \, dT$ | Kirchhoff's Law | $C_p$ = heat capacity at constant P (J·K⁻¹) |
| 7 | $\Delta_r H^{\circ \prime} = \Delta_r H^{\circ} + \Delta n_g RT \ln 10 \cdot (\text{pH correction})$ | Biochemical standard | pH = 7.0; $[H^+] = 10^{-7}$ M |
| 8 | $C_p - C_v = R$ (per mole of ideal gas) | Mayer's relation | For ideal gases only |

> [!NOTE]
> **Tip for KTU board exams:** Examiner *always* allocates partial credit if the units (kJ/mol) and sign convention (exothermic = negative ΔH; endothermic = positive ΔH) are correctly written. Never omit units.

### 2.5 Real-World / Engineering Utility

- **Calorimetry in food industry & pharmacology:** Bomb and flame calorimeters report $\Delta H$ of combustion; this is used to compute the caloric value of foods and metabolic fuels.
- **Process design in bioprocess engineering:** Fermenter design requires accurate $\Delta H$ values for heat removal calculations to maintain mesophilic or thermophilic culture temperatures.
- **Refrigeration and cryopreservation of biologicals (vaccines, tissues):** Enthalpy of fusion of water ($+6.01$ kJ/mol at 0 °C) governs the energy that must be removed to freeze biological samples.
- **Drug formulation:** Polymorphs of an active pharmaceutical ingredient (API) have different $\Delta_f H$, affecting solubility and shelf life.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Enthalpy from the First Law

**Starting Point:** The First Law for a closed system:

$$\Delta U = q + w$$

**Step 1 — Identify the work term.** For a mechanical (PV) process at constant external pressure:

$$w = -\int P_{ext} \, dV = -P_{ext}(V_2 - V_1) = -P \Delta V$$

> *Reasoning:* If the system expands ($\Delta V > 0$), it does positive work on the surroundings, so $w < 0$, which is correctly captured by the minus sign.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the First Law.**

$$\Delta U = q - P \Delta V$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $q$.**

$$q = \Delta U + P \Delta V$$

**Step 4 — Apply the constant-pressure constraint.** Since $P$ is held fixed, the change in the product $PV$ becomes:

$$\Delta(PV) = P \Delta V + V \Delta P = P \Delta V \quad (\text{because } \Delta P = 0)$$

**Step 5 — Recognize a state function.** Because both $U$ and $PV$ are state functions (functions of state only), their sum is also a state function. Define:

$$H \equiv U + PV$$

**Step 6 — Take the differential of H.**

$$dH = dU + d(PV) = dU + P \, dV + V \, dP$$

**Step 7 — Substitute the First Law in differential form.** From $dU = dq + dw = dq - P \, dV$:

$$dH = (dq - P \, dV) + P \, dV + V \, dP = dq + V \, dP$$

**Step 8 — Apply constant pressure.** When $dP = 0$:

$$dH = dq_p$$

Integrating from state 1 to state 2:

$$\boxed{\Delta H = q_p}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the foundational identity you will use in **every** constant-pressure thermochemistry problem.

### 3.2 Derivation of Kirchhoff's Equation

**Step 1 — Start from the definition of heat capacity at constant pressure:**

$$C_p = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Step 2 — For each species $i$ in the reaction:**

$$dH_i = C_{p,i} \, dT$$

**Step 3 — For a chemical reaction with stoichiometric coefficients $\nu_i$ (positive for products, negative for reactants):**

$$d(\Delta H) = \sum_i \nu_i \, C_{p,i} \, dT = \Delta C_p \, dT$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from $T_1$ to $T_2$:**

$$\int_{\Delta H(T_1)}^{\Delta H(T_2)} d(\Delta H) = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \Delta C_p \, dT$$

**Step 5 — Final Kirchhoff's Equation:**

$$\boxed{\Delta H(T_2) - \Delta H(T_1) = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \Delta C_p \, dT}$$

**Step 6 — Special Case:** If $\Delta C_p$ is constant over the temperature range:

$$\Delta H(T_2) = \Delta H(T_1) + \Delta C_p (T_2 - T_1)$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem:** Calculate $\Delta_r H^{\circ}$ at **373 K** for the reaction:
$$\text{H}_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(g)$$
Given: $\Delta_f H^{\circ}_{298}(\text{H}_2\text{O},g) = -241.82$ kJ/mol, $C_p(\text{H}_2) = 28.82$ J·K⁻¹·mol⁻¹, $C_p(\text{O}_2) = 29.37$ J·K⁻¹·mol⁻¹, $C_p(\text{H}_2\text{O},g) = 33.58$ J·K⁻¹·mol⁻¹.

**Step 1 — Compute $\Delta C_p$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta C_p &= C_p(\text{H}_2\text{O}) - C_p(\text{H}_2) - \tfrac{1}{2} C_p(\text{O}_2) \\
&= 33.58 - 28.82 - \tfrac{1}{2}(29.37) \\
&= 33.58 - 28.82 - 14.685 \\
&= -9.925 \;\; \text{J·K}^{-1}\text{·mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Kirchhoff's equation** (constant $\Delta C_p$ assumption):

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta H(373) &= \Delta H(298) + \Delta C_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1) \\
&= -241.82 \;\text{kJ/mol} + (-9.925 \;\text{J·K}^{-1}\text{·mol}^{-1}) \times (373 - 298) \;\text{K} \times \frac{1\;\text{kJ}}{1000\;\text{J}} \\
&= -241.82 + (-9.925)(75) / 1000 \\
&= -241.82 - 0.7444 \\
&= -242.56 \;\text{kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** $\Delta_r H^{\circ}(373\,\text{K}) = -242.56$ kJ/mol

> [!NOTE]
> The negative sign indicates an **exothermic** reaction (heat released). This result matches literature values within 0.1% — a sign that the constant-$\Delta C_p$ approximation is reasonable over this 75 K span.

### 3.4 Relationship Between $\Delta H$ and $\Delta U$ (Ideal Gases)

Starting from $H = U + PV$ and using the ideal gas law $PV = nRT$:

$$\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta(PV) = \Delta U + \Delta(nRT)$$

At constant temperature, $T$ is constant, so:

$$\Delta H = \Delta U + RT \cdot \Delta n_g$$

where $\Delta n_g$ is the change in the number of moles of *gaseous* species (liquids and solids are ignored, as their PV contribution is negligible).

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Hack:** Whenever you are given $\Delta U$ (or $\Delta E$) and asked for $\Delta H$, immediately write $\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta n_g RT$. This relation saves at least 2-3 minutes in a 14-mark problem.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Conceptual Architecture of Enthalpy

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    nodeB --> nodeE["H = U + PV"]
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF["Constant Pressure Condition"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["dP = 0"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["dH = dq at constant P"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Heat Measured in Calorimeter = delta H"]
    nodeE --> nodeJ["State Function Property"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Hess Law Applicable"]
    nodeJ --> nodeL["Path Independent"]
    nodeE --> nodeM["Enthalpy Changes"]
    nodeM --> nodeN["Formation delta fH"]
    nodeM --> nodeO["Combustion delta cH"]
    nodeM --> nodeP["Reaction delta rH"]
    nodeM --> nodeQ["Neutralization delta nH"]
    nodeN --> nodeR["Standard State 298 K 1 bar"]
    nodeO --> nodeR
    nodeP --> nodeR
    nodeQ --> nodeR
    nodeR --> nodeS["Biochemical Standard delta rH prime at pH 7"]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — Pathways Between States

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    state1["State 1 Reactants H1"] -->|Direct Path| state2["State 2 Products H2"]
    state1 -->|Path A Intermediates| state3["Intermediate State HA"]
    state3 -->|Path A continued| state2
    state1 -->|Path B Different Intermediates| state4["Intermediate State HB"]
    state4 -->|Path B continued| state2
    state2 --> nodeCalc["delta H is path independent"]

```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Enthalpy in Biochemical Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    bioA["Biochemical Reaction in Cell"] --> bioB["Constant Pressure Atmospheric"]
    bioB --> bioC["Heat Exchange qp with Surroundings"]
    bioC --> bioD["qp equals delta H reaction"]
    bioD --> bioE1["Exothermic delta H negative Energy Released"]
    bioD --> bioE2["Endothermic delta H positive Energy Absorbed"]
    bioE1 --> bioF1["ATP Synthesis Coupled to Exothermic Reactions"]
    bioE1 --> bioF2["Metabolic Heat Production in Homeotherms"]
    bioE2 --> bioF3["Photosynthesis Light Driven Endothermic"]
    bioE2 --> bioF4["Active Transport Ion Pumping"]

    bioF2 --> bioG
    bioF3 --> bioG
    bioF4 --> bioG
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagrams above map the *functional* relationships between enthalpy, its defining variables, and its biological manifestations. They are designed to help you recall the *structure* of the topic during exam revision — a quick glance at these will refresh the entire module in under 2 minutes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define enthalpy. Why is it considered a state function?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Enthalpy is defined as the total heat content of a system at constant pressure and is given by:

$$H = U + PV$$

where $U$ is the internal energy, $P$ is pressure, and $V$ is volume.

It is a **state function** because it depends only on the state variables (P, V, T, U) of the system at a given instant and not on the path or history by which that state was reached. Consequently, the change in enthalpy $\Delta H$ between two states is path-independent, which is the foundation of Hess's Law. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State and explain Kirchhoff's Law.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Kirchhoff's Law describes the temperature dependence of the enthalpy of reaction. It states that the change in enthalpy of a reaction with temperature is equal to the change in the heat capacity of the system:

$$\Delta H(T_2) = \Delta H(T_1) + \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \Delta C_p \, dT$$

where $\Delta C_p = \sum \nu_i C_{p,i}(\text{products}) - \sum \nu_j C_{p,j}(\text{reactants})$. This law is useful for computing reaction enthalpies at physiological temperatures (310 K) from tabulated data at 298 K. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` (14 marks)

**(a)** Derive the relationship $\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta n_g RT$ for an ideal gas reaction occurring at constant temperature. Discuss the conditions under which $\Delta H \approx \Delta U$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The standard enthalpy of formation of liquid water is $-285.8$ kJ/mol at 298 K, and the standard enthalpy of vaporization of water is $+44.0$ kJ/mol at 298 K. Calculate the standard enthalpy of formation of water vapor at 298 K. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Starting from the definition of enthalpy:

$$H = U + PV$$

Taking the difference between products and reactants at constant temperature:

$$\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta(PV)$$

For an ideal gas mixture, applying the ideal gas law to each gaseous species:

$$PV = nRT \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta(PV) = \Delta n_g RT$$

Substituting:

$$\boxed{\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta n_g RT}$$

**Conditions for $\Delta H \approx \Delta U$:**
- $\Delta n_g = 0$ (no change in the number of gaseous moles — common in biochemical reactions in solution where all species are aqueous).
- Reactions involving only liquids and solids (where the PV term is negligible compared to $U$).
- High-pressure or condensed-phase systems.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

The target reaction is:

$$\text{H}_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(g) \quad \Delta_f H^{\circ}(\text{H}_2\text{O},g) = \;?$$

We are given:
- (i) $\text{H}_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$ ; $\Delta H_1 = -285.8$ kJ/mol
- (ii) $\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(g)$ ; $\Delta H_2 = +44.0$ kJ/mol

The desired reaction is obtained by **adding** (i) and (ii):

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta_f H^{\circ}(\text{H}_2\text{O},g) &= \Delta H_1 + \Delta H_2 \\
&= -285.8 + 44.0 \\
&= \boxed{-241.8 \;\text{kJ/mol}}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the standard tabulated value for water vapor. **(7 marks)**

---

**Q4. Question B** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` (14 marks) — *Alternative choice*

**(a)** Starting from the First Law of Thermodynamics, derive the expression $q_p = \Delta H$ for a process carried out at constant pressure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the reaction $\text{N}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g) \rightarrow 2\text{NH}_3(g)$, the standard enthalpy of formation of NH₃(g) is $-46.0$ kJ/mol at 298 K. Using Kirchhoff's equation, calculate $\Delta_r H^{\circ}$ at 500 K. Given: $C_p(\text{N}_2) = 29.1$, $C_p(\text{H}_2) = 28.8$, $C_p(\text{NH}_3) = 35.1$ J·K⁻¹·mol⁻¹. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** First Law: $\Delta U = q + w$.

**Step 2:** For a constant-pressure process, the only work is expansion: $w = -P \Delta V$.

**Step 3:** Substituting: $\Delta U = q_p - P \Delta V$.

**Step 4:** Solving for $q_p$:

$$q_p = \Delta U + P \Delta V$$

**Step 5:** Since $P$ is constant, $\Delta(PV) = P \Delta V$, hence:

$$q_p = \Delta U + \Delta(PV) = \Delta(U + PV) = \Delta H$$

$$\boxed{q_p = \Delta H} \quad \text{(proved)}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Standard enthalpy of reaction at 298 K:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta_r H^{\circ}_{298} &= 2 \times \Delta_f H^{\circ}(\text{NH}_3) - [\Delta_f H^{\circ}(\text{N}_2) + 3 \Delta_f H^{\circ}(\text{H}_2)] \\
&= 2 \times (-46.0) - 0 - 0 \\
&= -92.0 \;\text{kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

(Formation enthalpies of elements in their standard states are zero.)

**Step 2:** Compute $\Delta C_p$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta C_p &= 2 C_p(\text{NH}_3) - [C_p(\text{N}_2) + 3 C_p(\text{H}_2)] \\
&= 2(35.1) - [29.1 + 3(28.8)] \\
&= 70.2 - [29.1 + 86.4] \\
&= 70.2 - 115.5 \\
&= -45.3 \;\text{J·K}^{-1}\text{·mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Apply Kirchhoff's equation (constant $\Delta C_p$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta_r H^{\circ}_{500} &= \Delta_r H^{\circ}_{298} + \Delta C_p \times (500 - 298) \\
&= -92.0 + (-45.3) \times (202) \times \frac{1}{1000} \\
&= -92.0 + (-9.1506) \\
&= \boxed{-101.15 \;\text{kJ/mol}}
\end{aligned}$$

The reaction is more exothermic at 500 K because the gaseous reactants have a higher heat capacity than the product ammonia. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch trap:** Students frequently leave Kirchhoff's equation with mixed units (kJ on one side, J on the other). Always convert $C_p$ from J·K⁻¹·mol⁻¹ to kJ·K⁻¹·mol⁻¹ by dividing by 1000. The KTU examiner deducts **1 full mark** for this oversight.
> 2. **Sign convention trap:** Exothermic reactions must carry a **negative** $\Delta H$. Forgetting the sign (or inverting it) is the single most common error in 14-mark problems. **0.5 to 1 mark** lost.
> 3. **State function trap:** Students sometimes add intermediate enthalpy changes in a cycle but forget to balance the stoichiometry of the desired reaction. Always cancel species on both sides of the final equation before reporting $\Delta_r H$.
> 4. **Standard state trap:** Tabulated values of $\Delta_f H^{\circ}$ apply at **298 K and 1 bar**. If the question asks for a different temperature, you *must* use Kirchhoff's equation. If you do not, expect to lose **2 marks**.
> 5. **$\Delta n_g$ trap:** Only count **gaseous** species when computing $\Delta n_g$. Aqueous, liquid, and solid species do not contribute to the PV work term.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Enthalpy** $H = U + PV$ is a state function, measured in joules (J) or kilojoules (kJ).
- At constant pressure, the heat absorbed by the system equals the enthalpy change: $\Delta H = q_p$.
- For ideal-gas reactions at constant temperature: $\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta n_g RT$.
- **Hess's Law** allows us to compute $\Delta_r H$ of any reaction as a linear combination of known reaction enthalpies — path independence is the key principle.
- **Formation method:** $\Delta_r H^{\circ} = \sum \nu_i \Delta_f H^{\circ}_i(\text{products}) - \sum \nu_j \Delta_f H^{\circ}_j(\text{reactants})$.
- **Kirchhoff's Law** gives the temperature dependence of $\Delta H$ via $\Delta C_p$.
- The **biochemical standard state** uses pH = 7.0 (denoted by a prime superscript, e.g., $\Delta_r H^{\circ \prime}$) and is the convention used in metabolic tables.
- **Sign convention:** Exothermic $\Rightarrow \Delta H < 0$; Endothermic $\Rightarrow \Delta H > 0$.
- For reactions in **condensed phases** (e.g., aqueous biochemical reactions), $\Delta H \approx \Delta U$ because $P\Delta V$ is negligibly small.
- **Mayer's relation** for ideal gases: $C_p - C_v = R$ (per mole).
- Always specify **units (kJ/mol)** and **states (g, l, s, aq)** in the final answer; KTU examiners are strict on completeness.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT414 - BIOCHEMICAL THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Flow Processes # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Flow Processes In the context of **Biochemical Thermodynamics (PEBBT414)**, a **Flow Process** is defined as a thermodynamic process in which a working fluid (the biochemical medium, broth, or solvent) continuously enters, traverses, and exits a control volume (such as a Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor, plug flow reactor, fermenter, heat exchanger, or pipe) at rates that are typically functions of time. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A flow process is any process in which mass crosses the system boundary. Such systems are termed **Open Systems** or **Control Volumes**, in contrast to **Closed Systems (Control Mass)** where no mass crosses the boundary during the process. Mathematically, a flow process is characterized by: $$\dot{m}_{in} \neq 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \dot{m}_{out} \neq 0$$ where $\dot{m}$ represents the **mass flow rate** in **kg/s** or **kg·h⁻¹**, and the system boundary is termed the **control surface**. ## 1.2 Classification of Flow Processes | **Process Type** | **Definition** | **Biochemical Engineering Example** | |---|---|---| | **Steady-State Flow** | Properties at any point in the system do not change with time | Continuous fermentation with constant feed rate | | **Unsteady-State Flow** | Properties at a given point change with time | Start-up or shut-down of a bioreactor | | **Uniform Flow** | Velocity is constant across the cross-section | Idealized pipe flow model | | **Non-Uniform Flow** | Velocity varies with position in the cross-section | Laminar flow in a tubular reactor | | **Adiabatic Flow** | No heat transfer across the control surface | Well-insulated fermentation pipeline | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Tap Water System > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Intuition:** Imagine a flowing river passing through a hydroelectric power station. The water **enters** the turbine inlet, loses some potential energy, transfers mechanical energy to the spinning turbine blades (shaft work), and **exits** as tail water. At any instant, there is water entering and leaving simultaneously — this is precisely a **flow process**. For a biochemical engineer, the same principle applies to a **continuous bioreactor**: - **Inlet stream:** Sterile substrate (glucose, salts) entering at a constant flow rate $F_{in}$ - **Reactor volume:** The **control volume** where microbial metabolism occurs - **Outlet stream:** Broth containing biomass, products, and residual substrate exiting at $F_{out}$ The key thermodynamic insight is that we cannot track a single "parcel" of fluid through the system; instead, we apply **integral balances** (mass and energy) over the **control surface**. ## 1.4 Why Flow Processes Matter in Biochemical Engineering > [!IMPORTANT] > Most industrial bioprocesses — including antibiotic production, ethanol fermentation, wastewater treatment, and enzyme manufacture — operate in **continuous flow mode** rather than batch mode. Therefore, the **First Law of Thermodynamics for Open Systems** is the most frequently applied analytical tool in biochemical engineering practice. The standard conditions used throughout this module are: - **Reference temperature:** $T_{ref} = 298.15$ **K** (25 °C) - **Standard pressure:** $P^{\circ} = 1$ **bar** = $10^{5}$ **Pa** - **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314$ **J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The First Law of Thermodynamics for Open Systems (Flow Processes) For a control volume with one inlet and one outlet, the **general energy balance** is: $$\frac{dE_{cv}}{dt} = \dot{Q} - \dot{W} + \sum_{in}\dot{m}_{i}\left(h_{i} + \frac{v_{i}^{2}}{2} + gz_{i}\right) - \sum_{out}\dot{m}_{e}\left(h_{e} + \frac{v_{e}^{2}}{2} + gz_{e}\right)$$ where each term has a specific physical interpretation: - $\frac{dE_{cv}}{dt}$ — Rate of accumulation of total energy within the control volume (kW) - $\dot{Q}$ — Net rate of heat transfer **into** the system (kW) - $\dot{W}$ — Net rate of work done **by** the system **excluding** flow work (kW) - $\dot{m}_{i}, \dot{m}_{e}$ — Mass flow rates at inlet and exit (kg·s⁻¹) - $h$ — Specific enthalpy (kJ·kg⁻¹) - $v$ — Velocity of the fluid stream (m·s⁻¹) - $z$ — Elevation above an arbitrary datum (m) - $g$ — Acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m·s⁻²** > [!NOTE] > **Key Conceptual Point:** In a flow process, the work $\dot{W}$ explicitly excludes the work required to push mass into and out of the control volume (the "flow work" $P V$). This work is already **embedded within the enthalpy** $h = u + Pv$. ## 2.2 Steady-State Flow Process (SSFP) — The Engineering Workhorse > [!IMPORTANT] > A **Steady-State Flow Process (SSFP)** is the cornerstone assumption for analyzing most continuous biochemical operations. Under SSFP conditions: > 1. The total mass within the control volume is constant > 2. Energy does not accumulate > 3. All flow rates, temperatures, and pressures at any fixed point are **time-invariant** Mathematically, the SSFP conditions translate to: $$\frac{dm_{cv}}{dt} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum\dot{m}_{i} = \sum\dot{m}_{e}$$ $$\frac{dE_{cv}}{dt} = 0$$ Under these constraints, the energy balance simplifies to the **Steady-State Flow Energy Equation**: $$\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \sum_{out}\dot{m}_{e}\left(h_{e} + \frac{v_{e}^{2}}{2} + gz_{e}\right) - \sum_{in}\dot{m}_{i}\left(h_{i} + \frac{v_{i}^{2}}{2} + gz_{i}\right)$$ ## 2.3 Special Case: Single Inlet, Single Outlet, Negligible Kinetic & Potential Energy Changes For the most common engineering scenario (a pipe, nozzle, or bioreactor with horizontal flow and small velocity change): $$\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}\left[\left(h_{2} - h_{1}\right) + \frac{v_{2}^{2} - v_{1}^{2}}{2} + g(z_{2} - z_{1})\right]$$ Dividing through by the mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ yields the **specific energy balance**: $$q - w = \Delta h + \frac{\Delta v^{2}}{2} + g\Delta z$$ ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates the equations you must memorize for the KTU Board Examination. The KTU examiner typically asks for direct formula substitution in 3-mark questions and derivation-based problems in 14-mark questions. | **Equation Name** | **Mathematical Form** | **Typical Application** | |---|---|---| | Mass Balance (SSFP) | $\sum \dot{m}_{i} = \sum \dot{m}_{e}$ | Single bioreactor with feed & effluent | | Steady-State Energy Balance | $\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}\Delta h$ | Heat exchanger in a fermentation train | | General Flow Energy Balance | $\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}\left[\Delta h + \frac{\Delta v^{2}}{2} + g\Delta z\right]$ | Pumps, compressors, turbines | | Bernoulli Equation (inviscid) | $\frac{\Delta v^{2}}{2} + g\Delta z + \frac{\Delta P}{\rho} = 0$ | Ideal fluid flow in pipes | | Mechanical Energy Balance | $\frac{\Delta v^{2}}{2} + g\Delta z + \frac{\Delta P}{\rho} + \delta w_{shaft} + F = 0$ | Real flow with friction & shaft work | | Nozzle Work (per unit mass) | $w = -\Delta h = -\Delta\left(\frac{v^{2}}{2}\right)$ | Steam nozzle in a sterilization loop | | Compressor Work | $w_{s} = -\int v\,dP$ (isentropic) | Air compressor for aeration | | Turbine Work | $w_{s} = \int v\,dP$ | Power recovery turbine | | Enthalpy of Ideal Gas | $\Delta h = C_{p}\Delta T$ | Aerobic bioreactor gas analysis | | Enthalpy of Incompressible Liquid | $\Delta h = C_{p}\Delta T + v\Delta P$ | Liquid broth in heat exchangers | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sign Convention (KTU Standard):** > - $\dot{Q} > 0$ ⇒ Heat added **to** the system > - $\dot{W} > 0$ ⇒ Work done **by** the system > - $\dot{W}_{shaft} > 0$ ⇒ Work delivered **by** the system (turbine mode) > - $\dot{W}_{shaft} < 0$ ⇒ Work required **by** the system (pump/compressor mode) ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility In a **biochemical plant**, the flow process framework is used to: 1. **Size heat exchangers** for sterilizing fermentation media (using $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}C_{p}\Delta T$) 2. **Calculate compressor power** for sparged air supply to aerobic cultures ($W = \dot{m}C_{p}\Delta T / \eta_{isentropic}$) 3. **Design bioreactor cooling jackets** by applying steady-state energy balances 4. **Optimize downstream product recovery** through evaporators and distillation columns 5. **Predict pumping requirements** for transporting viscous fermentation broths using the Mechanical Energy Balance > [!NOTE] > The **Mechanical Energy Balance (MEB)** is the practical extension of Bernoulli's equation and includes **friction losses ($F$)** and **shaft work ($w_{shaft}$)**. It is the workhorse equation for designing fluid transport systems in bioprocess plants. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Steady-State Flow Energy Equation (SSFEE) ### Starting Point: The General First Law The most general form of the First Law applied to a control volume is: $$\frac{dE_{cv}}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{cv} - \dot{W}_{cv} + \sum_{in}\dot{m}_{i}\left(h_{i} + \frac{v_{i}^{2}}{2} + gz_{i}\right) - \sum_{out}\dot{m}_{e}\left(h_{e} + \frac{v_{e}^{2}}{2} + gz_{e}\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Step 0 — Identify the system:** We define a **control volume (CV)** enclosing the device of interest (e.g., a compressor, heat exchanger, or bioreactor). The **control surface (CS)** is the imaginary boundary across which mass, heat, and work may flow. ### Step 1: Apply the Steady-State Assumption A flow process is in **steady state** when the time rate of change of mass and energy within the control volume is **zero**: $$\frac{dm_{cv}}{dt} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{dE_{cv}}{dt} = 0$$ Substituting these conditions into the general energy equation, the left-hand side vanishes: $$0 = \dot{Q}_{cv} - \dot{W}_{cv} + \sum_{in}\dot{m}_{i}\left(h_{i} + \frac{v_{i}^{2}}{2} + gz_{i}\right) - \sum_{out}\dot{m}_{e}\left(h_{e} + \frac{v_{e}^{2}}{2} + gz_{e}\right)$$ ### Step 2: Rearrange the Equation Moving the work and heat terms to one side: $$\dot{Q}_{cv} - \dot{W}_{cv} = \sum_{out}\dot{m}_{e}\left(h_{e} + \frac{v_{e}^{2}}{2} + gz_{e}\right) - \sum_{in}\dot{m}_{i}\left(h_{i} + \frac{v_{i}^{2}}{2} + gz_{i}\right)$$ This is the **Steady-State Flow Energy Equation (SSFEE)** in its most general form. ### Step 3: Simplify for a Single Inlet and Single Outlet For the most common engineering case with one inlet (state 1) and one outlet (state 2): $$\dot{Q}_{cv} - \dot{W}_{cv} = \dot{m}\left[\left(h_{2} - h_{1}\right) + \frac{v_{2}^{2} - v_{1}^{2}}{2} + g(z_{2} - z_{1})\right]$$ ### Step 4: Express in Per-Unit-Mass Form Dividing both sides by $\dot{m}$ (mass flow rate), we obtain the **specific (per kg) energy balance**: $$q - w = \Delta h + \frac{\Delta v^{2}}{2} + g\Delta z$$ where $q = \dot{Q}/\dot{m}$ (kJ·kg⁻¹) and $w = \dot{W}/\dot{m}$ (kJ·kg⁻¹). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Compact Form (KFU Board Exam Expectation):** > > $$q - w = \Delta h + \frac{\Delta v^{2}}{2} + g\Delta z$$ > > This is the **standard SSFP equation** that KTU expects students to quote in derivations and problem solutions. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation (Inviscid Flow, No Work, No Heat) ### Step 1: Start from the SSFEE For a single inlet-outlet, horizontal pipe with no shaft work and no heat transfer: $$0 - 0 = \Delta h + \frac{\Delta v^{2}}{2} + g\Delta z$$ ### Step 2: Expand the Enthalpy Term Recall that for any substance, $h = u + Pv$. For an **incompressible fluid** with constant specific volume $v$: $$\Delta h = \Delta u + \Delta(Pv) = \Delta u + v\Delta P$$ ### Step 3: Invoke the Adiabatic and Frictionless Assumptions For an inviscid (frictionless) and adiabatic flow, there is no internal energy change due to dissipation: $$\Delta u = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta h = v\Delta P = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho}$$ ### Step 4: Substitute Back into the SSFEE $$0 = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho} + \frac{\Delta v^{2}}{2} + g\Delta z$$ ### Step 5: Rearrange to Standard Bernoulli Form $$\frac{\Delta v^{2}}{2} + g\Delta z + \frac{\Delta P}{\rho} = 0$$ Or, expressed as pressures: $$P_{1} + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_{1}^{2} + \rho g z_{1} = P_{2} + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_{2}^{2} + \rho g z_{2}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** Each term in Bernoulli's equation has units of pressure (Pa): > - $P$ — **Static pressure** of the fluid > - $\frac{1}{2}\rho v^{2}$ — **Dynamic pressure** (kinetic energy per unit volume) > - $\rho g z$ — **Hydrostatic pressure** (potential energy per unit volume) > > The equation states that the total mechanical energy is **conserved** along a streamline in an inviscid, incompressible flow. --- ## 3.3 Derivation of the Mechanical Energy Balance (MEB) ### Step 1: Begin with the SSFEE $$q - w = \Delta h + \frac{\Delta v^{2}}{2} + g\Delta z$$ ### Step 2: Apply the First Law to the Fluid (Internal Energy Contribution) Using $\Delta h = \Delta u + \Delta(Pv) = \Delta u + v\Delta P$ and the relation $\Delta u = q - w_{friction}$ (the friction is irreversible work done on the fluid): $$q - (w_{shaft} + w_{friction}) = \Delta u + v\Delta P + \frac{\Delta v^{2}}{2} + g\Delta z$$ ### Step 3: Substitute $\Delta u = q - w_{friction}$ $$q - w_{shaft} - w_{friction} = (q - w_{friction}) + v\Delta P + \frac{\Delta v^{2}}{2} + g\Delta z$$ ### Step 4: Cancel $q$ and $w_{friction}$ on Both Sides $$-w_{shaft} = v\Delta P + \frac{\Delta v^{2}}{2} + g\Delta z$$ ### Step 5: Define the Friction Loss Term $F$ By definition, the friction loss per unit mass is $F = -w_{friction}$ (positive for energy dissipated). Rearranging the sign convention: $$-w_{shaft} - F = v\Delta P + \frac{\Delta v^{2}}{2} + g\Delta z$$ Or, equivalently, the **Mechanical Energy Balance**: $$\frac{\Delta v^{2}}{2} + g\Delta z + v\Delta P + w_{shaft} + F = 0$$ For incompressible fluids where $v = 1/\rho$: $$\frac{\Delta v^{2}}{2} + g\Delta z + \frac{\Delta P}{\rho} + w_{shaft} + F = 0$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Where:** > - $w_{shaft}$ — Shaft work per unit mass (positive for work done **on** the fluid, e.g., pumps) > - $F$ — Frictional loss per unit mass (always positive) > - $v$ — Specific volume (m³·kg⁻¹) > > This is the **practical equation** used by biochemical engineers for designing piping systems, calculating pump heads, and determining compressor power. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example: Cooling Coil in a Bioreactor (Step-by-Step) **Problem Statement:** A continuous stirred-tank bioreactor (CSTR) operates at steady state. A liquid broth enters at $T_{1} = 30\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ and exits at $T_{2} = 35\,^{\circ}\text{C}$. The mass flow rate is $\dot{m} = 500\,\text{kg·h}^{-1}$. The mean specific heat of the broth is $C_{p} = 4.18\,\text{kJ·kg}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$. Assume the broth is essentially water. The reactor has no moving parts, no significant elevation change, and negligible velocity change. Calculate the rate of heat removal required by the cooling coil. ### Solution: **Step 1 — Identify the Applicable Equation** For an open system at steady state with negligible $\Delta KE$ and $\Delta PE$, the SSFEE reduces to: $$\dot{Q} = \dot{m}\Delta h = \dot{m}C_{p}\Delta T$$ **Step 2 — Calculate the Temperature Change** $$\Delta T = T_{2} - T_{1} = 35 - 30 = 5\,^{\circ}\text{C} = 5\,\text{K}$$ (Note: A temperature *difference* in Celsius equals the same difference in Kelvin.) **Step 3 — Compute the Enthalpy Change** $$\Delta h = C_{p}\Delta T = 4.18 \times 5 = 20.9\,\text{kJ·kg}^{-1}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the Heat Duty** $$\dot{Q} = \dot{m}\Delta h = 500 \times 20.9 = 10{,}450\,\text{kJ·h}^{-1}$$ **Step 5 — Convert to Watts** $$\dot{Q} = \frac{10{,}450}{3600} = 2.903\,\text{kW}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Answer:** The cooling coil must remove heat at a rate of $\dot{Q} = 10{,}450\,\text{kJ·h}^{-1} = 2.903\,\text{kW}$. The negative sign convention (heat leaving the system) would yield $\dot{Q} = -2.903\,\text{kW}$ if the convention is "heat in = positive." --- ## 3.5 Worked Example: Compressor Power for Aeration **Problem Statement:** Air enters the compressor of an aerobic bioreactor at $P_{1} = 1.0\,\text{bar}$ and $T_{1} = 298\,\text{K}$. It is compressed to $P_{2} = 2.5\,\text{bar}$ and exits at $T_{2} = 453\,\text{K}$. The molar flow rate is $\dot{n} = 0.5\,\text{mol·s}^{-1}$. For air, $C_{p} = 29.1\,\text{J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$ and the gas constant is $R = 8.314\,\text{J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$. Calculate the **isentropic compressor work** per mole of air. ### Solution: **Step 1 — Recall the Isentropic Work Formula for Ideal Gas** For an ideal gas undergoing isentropic compression: $$w_{s} = -C_{p}(T_{2s} - T_{1})$$ **Step 2 — Compute the Isentropic Exit Temperature** For isentropic compression of an ideal gas with $\gamma = C_{p}/C_{v}$ and $C_{v} = C_{p} - R$: $$\gamma = \frac{C_{p}}{C_{p} - R} = \frac{29.1}{29.1 - 8.314} = \frac{29.1}{20.786} = 1.40$$ $$T_{2s} = T_{1}\left(\frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$$ $$T_{2s} = 298 \times \left(\frac{2.5}{1.0}\right)^{(1.4 - 1)/1.4} = 298 \times (2.5)^{0.2857}$$ Computing $(2.5)^{0.2857}$: $$\ln(2.5) = 0.9163, \quad 0.2857 \times 0.9163 = 0.2618, \quad e^{0.2618} = 1.2993$$ $$T_{2s} = 298 \times 1.2993 = 387.2\,\text{K}$$ **Step 3 — Calculate the Isentropic Work per Mole** $$w_{s} = -C_{p}(T_{2s} - T_{1}) = -29.1 \times (387.2 - 298) = -29.1 \times 89.2 = -2595.7\,\text{J·mol}^{-1}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the Total Compressor Power** $$\dot{W}_{s} = \dot{n} \cdot w_{s} = 0.5 \times (-2595.7) = -1297.9\,\text{W} \approx -1.30\,\text{kW}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** The negative sign indicates that work must be **supplied to** the compressor (it is a power-consuming device). The magnitude $\vert \dot{W}_{s} \vert = 1.30\,\text{kW}$ is the minimum theoretical power requirement. Actual compressors will require 10–30% more power due to mechanical and electrical inefficiencies. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Control Volume Schematic for a Generic Flow Process The following block-level functional architecture depicts the structure of a general steady-state flow process with a single inlet and single outlet, such as a bioreactor, heat exchanger, or compressor. ```mermaid flowchart LR inlet1[INLET STREAM
STATE 1] inlet2[h1, v1, z1
Mass flow rate min] inlet3[Kinetic Energy
KE1 = mdot v1 squared over 2] inlet4[Potential Energy
PE1 = mdot g z1] inlet5[Enthalpy
H1 = mdot h1] cvbox[CONTROL VOLUME
CV BOUNDARY
ENERGY ACCUMULATION ZERO
STEADY STATE] workin[Shaft Work Ws
Positive for pumps
Negative for turbines] heatin[Heat Transfer Q dot
Positive when added] outlet1[Enthalpy
H2 = mdot h2] outlet2[Kinetic Energy
KE2 = mdot v2 squared over 2] outlet3[Potential Energy
PE2 = mdot g z2] outlet4[h2, v2, z2
Mass flow rate mout] outlet5[OUTLET STREAM
STATE 2] inlet1 --> inlet2 --> inlet3 inlet3 --> inlet4 --> inlet5 --> cvbox workin --> cvbox heatin --> cvbox cvbox --> outlet1 --> outlet2 --> outlet3 --> outlet4 --> outlet5 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** Energy enters the control volume through the inlet stream (carrying enthalpy, kinetic, and potential energy) and through the heat transfer and shaft work ports. It exits through the outlet stream. Under steady-state conditions, total energy in = total energy out, yielding the SSFEE. --- ## 4.2 Classification Tree of Flow Processes ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Flow Processes in Biochemical Engineering] branch1[By Time Dependence] branch2[By Compressibility] branch3[By Geometry] ss[Steady State Flow] uss[Unsteady State Flow] ig[Ideal Gas Flow] ic[Incompressible Flow] comp[Compressible Flow] pipe[Pipe Flow] nozzle[Nozzle Diffuser] turb[Turbine Compressor] he[Heat Exchanger] bio[Bioreactor] start --> branch1 start --> branch2 start --> branch3 branch1 --> ss branch1 --> uss branch2 --> ig branch2 --> ic branch2 --> comp branch3 --> pipe branch3 --> nozzle branch3 --> turb branch3 --> he branch3 --> bio ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Relevance:** The classification tree helps identify which governing equation to apply. For example: > - **Heat Exchanger** → SSFEE with $\dot{W} = 0$ > - **Turbine** → SSFEE with $\dot{Q} = 0$ (adiabatic) > - **Compressor** → SSFEE with $\dot{Q} \neq 0$ and isentropic analysis > - **Bioreactor** → SSFEE with metabolic heat generation term --- ## 4.3 Energy Flow Through a Continuous Bioreactor ```mermaid flowchart TB feed[FEED STREAM
Sterile Substrate Solution
Temperature T1
Flow rate Fin] conv[CONTROL VOLUME
CSTR Bioreactor
Volume V constant
Microbial metabolism] prod[PRODUCT STREAM
Broth with biomass
and metabolites
Temperature T2] cool[Cooling Jacket
Q dot removal
Heat duty] ag[Agitator Power
W dot shaft input] aer[Aeration Gas
Sparged air in/out] rx[Heat of Reaction
Q dot metabolic] feed --> conv conv --> prod conv -.-> cool conv -.-> ag conv -.-> aer conv -.-> rx ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** In a real bioreactor, the **steady-state energy balance** must include not only the sensible heat change of the broth but also the **metabolic heat of microbial growth** ($Q_{metabolic}$), the **agitator power dissipation** ($W_{agitator}$), and **aeration gas enthalpy** (both inlet and outlet). The cooling jacket duty $Q_{cooling}$ is sized to balance all other terms. --- ## 4.4 Energy Transformation Cascade in a Flow Device ```mermaid flowchart LR stepA[Step 1
Identify CV and CS] stepB[Step 2
List inlet and outlet streams] stepC[Step 3
Apply steady state assumption dmcv dt equals 0] stepD[Step 4
Write general SSFEE] stepE[Step 5
Substitute properties h, v, z] stepF[Step 6
Solve for unknown Q or W] stepG[Step 7
Check sign convention and units] stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Define a steady-state flow process. State the two conditions that must be satisfied for a flow process to be considered steady-state.** **Model Answer:** A **steady-state flow process** is a process in which the fluid flows steadily through the control volume, and the **mass flow rate** and **fluid properties** (temperature, pressure, velocity, composition) at every point within the control volume remain **constant with time**. The two conditions for steady state are: 1. **Mass accumulation = 0:** $\frac{dm_{cv}}{dt} = 0$, which gives $\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}$ 2. **Energy accumulation = 0:** $\frac{dE_{cv}}{dt} = 0$ [3 Marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the two conditions with equations] --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Distinguish between a closed system and an open system. Give one bioprocess example for each.** **Model Answer:** | **Aspect** | **Closed System** | **Open System (Flow Process)** | |---|---|---| | Mass transfer across boundary | **No** | **Yes** | | Work done | Boundary work only | Shaft work + flow work | | Typical analysis | First Law for control mass | First Law for control volume | | Bioprocess example | **Batch fermentation** in a sealed vessel | **Continuous stirred-tank bioreactor (CSTR)** with feed and effluent streams | [3 Marks: 1 for closed definition + example, 1 for open definition + example, 1 for distinction] --- ## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. **Internal choice is provided**: students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **(a) Derive the steady-state flow energy equation (SSFEE) for an open system with one inlet and one outlet. Clearly state all assumptions. [7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Assumptions:** 1. The system is at **steady state** ($\frac{dm_{cv}}{dt} = 0$, $\frac{dE_{cv}}{dt} = 0$) 2. **One inlet** (state 1) and **one outlet** (state 2) 3. The fluid flows continuously across the control surface 4. Heat and work transfers occur at steady rates **Derivation:** Starting from the general First Law for a control volume: $$\frac{dE_{cv}}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{cv} - \dot{W}_{cv} + \sum_{in}\dot{m}_{i}\left(h_{i} + \frac{v_{i}^{2}}{2} + gz_{i}\right) - \sum_{out}\dot{m}_{e}\left(h_{e} + \frac{v_{e}^{2}}{2} + gz_{e}\right)$$ Applying the steady-state condition $\frac{dE_{cv}}{dt} = 0$: $$0 = \dot{Q}_{cv} - \dot{W}_{cv} + \sum_{in}\dot{m}_{i}\left(h_{i} + \frac{v_{i}^{2}}{2} + gz_{i}\right) - \sum_{out}\dot{m}_{e}\left(h_{e} + \frac{v_{e}^{2}}{2} + gz_{e}\right)$$ For a single inlet (1) and single outlet (2), and rearranging: $$\dot{Q}_{cv} - \dot{W}_{cv} = \dot{m}\left[\left(h_{2} - h_{1}\right) + \frac{v_{2}^{2} - v_{1}^{2}}{2} + g(z_{2} - z_{1})\right]$$ This is the **Steady-State Flow Energy Equation (SSFEE)**. The shaft work $\dot{W}_{cv}$ excludes the flow work $P v$, which is incorporated in the enthalpy term. **[7 Marks: 2 for assumptions, 3 for derivation steps, 2 for final equation with explanation]** --- **(b) A liquid fermentation broth with $C_{p} = 4.0\,\text{kJ·kg}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$ enters a heat exchanger at $20\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ and leaves at $80\,^{\circ}\text{C}$. The mass flow rate is $2000\,\text{kg·h}^{-1}$. The inlet pipe is at elevation $3\,\text{m}$ and the outlet at $8\,\text{m}$, with inlet velocity $1.5\,\text{m·s}^{-1}$ and outlet velocity $2.0\,\text{m·s}^{-1}$. Calculate: (i) the rate of heat transfer, and (ii) the shaft work if any. [7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $C_{p} = 4.0\,\text{kJ·kg}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$ - $T_{1} = 20\,^{\circ}\text{C}$, $T_{2} = 80\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ - $\dot{m} = 2000\,\text{kg·h}^{-1} = 2000/3600 = 0.5556\,\text{kg·s}^{-1}$ - $z_{1} = 3\,\text{m}$, $z_{2} = 8\,\text{m}$ - $v_{1} = 1.5\,\text{m·s}^{-1}$, $v_{2} = 2.0\,\text{m·s}^{-1}$ - $g = 9.81\,\text{m·s}^{-2}$ **Step 1 — Apply the SSFEE** $$\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}\left[\Delta h + \frac{\Delta v^{2}}{2} + g\Delta z\right]$$ **Step 2 — Calculate Each Energy Term** Enthalpy change (assuming incompressible liquid): $$\Delta h = C_{p}\Delta T = 4.0 \times (80 - 20) = 240\,\text{kJ·kg}^{-1}$$ Kinetic energy change: $$\frac{\Delta v^{2}}{2} = \frac{v_{2}^{2} - v_{1}^{2}}{2} = \frac{(2.0)^{2} - (1.5)^{2}}{2} = \frac{4.0 - 2.25}{2} = 0.875\,\text{J·kg}^{-1} = 0.000875\,\text{kJ·kg}^{-1}$$ Potential energy change: $$g\Delta z = 9.81 \times (8 - 3) = 49.05\,\text{J·kg}^{-1} = 0.04905\,\text{kJ·kg}^{-1}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the Total Specific Energy Change** $$\Delta h + \frac{\Delta v^{2}}{2} + g\Delta z = 240 + 0.000875 + 0.04905 = 240.050\,\text{kJ·kg}^{-1}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the Rate of Energy Transfer (assuming no shaft work, $\dot{W} = 0$)** $$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \times 240.050 = 0.5556 \times 240.050 = 133.4\,\text{kW}$$ **Step 5 — Conclusion** (i) **Rate of heat transfer:** $\dot{Q} = +133.4\,\text{kW}$ (heat must be **added** to the system) (ii) **Shaft work:** $\dot{W} = 0$ (no moving parts in a typical heat exchanger) > [!NOTE] > The kinetic and potential energy changes are **negligible** compared to the enthalpy change. This is typical for liquid flow systems and explains why $\Delta h$ dominates the energy balance in heat exchangers. **[7 Marks: 2 for setup, 3 for calculations, 1 for conclusion, 1 for units]** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — *Internal Alternative* **(a) State the Bernoulli equation. Starting from the steady-state flow energy equation, derive Bernoulli's equation for an incompressible, inviscid fluid with no shaft work. [7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Statement:** Bernoulli's equation states that for an inviscid, incompressible fluid in steady flow, the sum of the static pressure, velocity head, and elevation head is constant along a streamline: $$P + \frac{1}{2}\rho v^{2} + \rho g z = \text{constant}$$ **Derivation:** **Step 1 — Start with the SSFEE** $$\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}\left[\Delta h + \frac{\Delta v^{2}}{2} + g\Delta z\right]$$ **Step 2 — Apply the Constraints for Bernoulli's Equation** - Adiabatic: $\dot{Q} = 0$ - No shaft work: $\dot{W} = 0$ - Incompressible fluid: $\Delta h = \Delta u + v\Delta P$, with $v = 1/\rho = \text{constant}$ - Inviscid (no friction): $\Delta u = 0$ (no dissipation into internal energy) - Therefore: $\Delta h = v\Delta P = \Delta P/\rho$ **Step 3 — Substitute into SSFEE** $$0 - 0 = \dot{m}\left[\frac{\Delta P}{\rho} + \frac{\Delta v^{2}}{2} + g\Delta z\right]$$ **Step 4 — Divide by $\dot{m}$** $$\frac{\Delta P}{\rho} + \frac{\Delta v^{2}}{2} + g\Delta z = 0$$ **Step 5 — Express in Point Form** Expanding the deltas between points 1 and 2: $$\frac{P_{1} - P_{2}}{\rho} + \frac{v_{1}^{2} - v_{2}^{2}}{2} + g(z_{1} - z_{2}) = 0$$ Rearranging: $$P_{1} + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_{1}^{2} + \rho g z_{1} = P_{2} + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_{2}^{2} + \rho g z_{2}$$ This is **Bernoulli's equation** in its standard form. **[7 Marks: 1 for statement, 2 for assumptions, 3 for derivation, 1 for final form]** --- **(b) A pump transfers a fermentation broth of density $1100\,\text{kg·m}^{-3}$ at a flow rate of $5\,\text{L·s}^{-1}$ through a pipe. The pipe diameter at the inlet is $50\,\text{mm}$ and at the outlet is $30\,\text{mm}$. The pressure rise across the pump is $200\,\text{kPa}$. If the elevation difference is negligible and the pump operates adiabatically, calculate the power required by the pump. [7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $\rho = 1100\,\text{kg·m}^{-3}$ - Volumetric flow rate $Q = 5\,\text{L·s}^{-1} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^{3}\text{·s}^{-1}$ - Inlet diameter $D_{1} = 50\,\text{mm} = 0.05\,\text{m}$ - Outlet diameter $D_{2} = 30\,\text{mm} = 0.03\,\text{m}$ - Pressure rise $\Delta P = P_{2} - P_{1} = 200\,\text{kPa} = 200{,}000\,\text{Pa}$ - $\Delta z = 0$, $\dot{Q} = 0$ (adiabatic) **Step 1 — Compute the Mass Flow Rate** $$\dot{m} = \rho Q = 1100 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} = 5.5\,\text{kg·s}^{-1}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the Pipe Cross-Sectional Areas** $$A_{1} = \frac{\pi D_{1}^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.05)^{2}}{4} = 1.963 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^{2}$$ $$A_{2} = \frac{\pi D_{2}^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.03)^{2}}{4} = 7.069 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^{2}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the Flow Velocities** $$v_{1} = \frac{Q}{A_{1}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.546\,\text{m·s}^{-1}$$ $$v_{2} = \frac{Q}{A_{2}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{7.069 \times 10^{-4}} = 7.074\,\text{m·s}^{-1}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the Kinetic Energy Change** $$\frac{\Delta v^{2}}{2} = \frac{v_{2}^{2} - v_{1}^{2}}{2} = \frac{(7.074)^{2} - (2.546)^{2}}{2} = \frac{50.04 - 6.48}{2} = 21.78\,\text{J·kg}^{-1}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the Flow Work Term (per unit mass)** $$v\Delta P = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho} = \frac{200{,}000}{1100} = 181.82\,\text{J·kg}^{-1}$$ **Step 6 — Apply the SSFEE (per unit mass, $q = 0$)** $$0 - w = v\Delta P + \frac{\Delta v^{2}}{2} + g\Delta z$$ $$-w = 181.82 + 21.78 + 0 = 203.60\,\text{J·kg}^{-1}$$ $$w = -203.60\,\text{J·kg}^{-1}$$ **Step 7 — Compute the Pump Power** $$\dot{W} = \dot{m} \times w = 5.5 \times (-203.60) = -1119.8\,\text{W} \approx -1.12\,\text{kW}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** The negative sign indicates that work is **done on the system** (energy input required). The pump requires approximately **1.12 kW** of mechanical power input. This value excludes pump mechanical inefficiencies (typically 60–85% efficient), so a real pump would need $\frac{1.12}{0.75} \approx 1.49\,\text{kW}$ of electrical power. **[7 Marks: 1 for setup, 2 for velocities, 2 for energy terms, 1 for final power, 1 for interpretation]** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:** > > 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** KTU examiners are *very strict* about sign conventions. Mixing up the sign of work (turbine vs. pump) and heat (added vs. removed) is the **#1 reason for losing 2–3 marks** in 14-mark questions. **Always state your sign convention explicitly** at the start of the solution. > > 2. **Forgetting the Steady-State Assumption:** Many students write the general energy balance but forget to apply $\frac{dE_{cv}}{dt} = 0$. This results in an **incomplete SSFEE** and loses 2 marks. > > 3. **Confusing $h$ with $u$:** The enthalpy $h$ (not internal energy $u$) is the natural property for flow processes because it already includes flow work $Pv$. Using $u$ in flow equations is a **fundamental error**. > > 4. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing kJ and J, or kg/h and kg/s, within the same calculation is a common pitfall. **Convert all units to SI** (J, kg, m, s, K) before starting. > > 5. **Neglecting $\Delta KE$ and $\Delta PE$ Without Justification:** You can neglect these only when they are **demonstrably small** compared to $\Delta h$. A 1-line justification is sufficient. > > 6. **Misapplying Bernoulli:** Bernoulli's equation is for **inviscid, incompressible** flow with **no work and no heat transfer**. Using it for a real pump or compressor will lose 3–4 marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Flow Processes (Module 1)** - **Flow Process:** A process in which mass crosses the system boundary; analyzed using a **control volume**. - **Open System:** Mass transfer occurs across the boundary. **Closed System:** No mass transfer. - **Steady State (SS):** All properties at every point are **time-invariant**. The two SS conditions are $\frac{dm_{cv}}{dt} = 0$ and $\frac{dE_{cv}}{dt} = 0$. - **Steady-State Flow Energy Equation (SSFEE):** $\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}\left[\Delta h + \frac{\Delta v^{2}}{2} + g\Delta z\right]$ - **Per-unit-mass form:** $q - w = \Delta h + \frac{\Delta v^{2}}{2} + g\Delta z$ - **Enthalpy for Ideal Gas:** $\Delta h = C_{p}\Delta T$ - **Enthalpy for Incompressible Liquid:** $\Delta h = C_{p}\Delta T + v\Delta P$ (often $\Delta h \approx C_{p}\Delta T$ for liquids) - **Bernoulli Equation:** $\frac{\Delta P}{\rho} + \frac{\Delta v^{2}}{2} + g\Delta z = 0$ — applies only to **inviscid, incompressible, no-work, adiabatic** flow. - **Mechanical Energy Balance (MEB):** $\frac{\Delta v^{2}}{2} + g\Delta z + v\Delta P + w_{shaft} + F = 0$ — the practical equation for real flow with friction and shaft work. - **Sign Convention:** $\dot{Q}_{in} > 0$, $\dot{W}_{by\,system} > 0$, $w_{shaft,\,on\,fluid} > 0$ (pump mode), $F > 0$ (energy dissipated). - **Compressor Work (Isentropic):** $w_{s} = -C_{p}(T_{2s} - T_{1})$, where $T_{2s} = T_{1}\left(\frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$. - **Turbine Work:** $w_{s} = +\int v\,dP$ (energy extracted from fluid). - **Heat Exchanger:** $\dot{W} = 0$, so $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}C_{p}\Delta T$ (for liquids). - **Nozzle/Diffuser:** No work, no heat (approx.), so $\Delta h = -\frac{\Delta v^{2}}{2}$. - **Bioprocess Relevance:** Heat exchangers (sterilization), bioreactor cooling jackets, aeration compressors, broth transfer pumps, downstream product recovery columns — all analyzed using the SSFEE. - **Critical Limitation of Bernoulli:** Cannot handle viscous friction, shaft work, or heat transfer — use the **MEB** instead. - **Always convert units to SI** (J, kg, m, s, K, Pa) at the start of every numerical problem. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Tip:** When solving 14-mark problems, always structure your answer as: (1) State the given data with units, (2) Identify the governing equation, (3) Show all substitutions, (4) Box the final answer with correct units, and (5) Provide a one-sentence physical interpretation. This format aligns exactly with the KTU board examiner's marking scheme.

topics.title: Concept of Entropy and Second Law of Thermodynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT414 - BIOCHEMICAL THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concept of Entropy and Second Law of Thermodynamics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Entropy

**Entropy (S)** is a fundamental state function in thermodynamics that quantitatively measures the degree of **disorder**, **randomness**, or **molecular dispersion** of energy within a system. It is an **extensive thermodynamic property** (depends on system size) and is measured in units of **J·K⁻¹** (Joules per Kelvin) in the SI system, with the **universal gas constant R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** serving as a key reference.

Mathematically, the differential change in entropy for a **reversible process** is defined as:

$$dS = \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$$

where $\delta Q_{rev}$ is the infinitesimal reversible heat exchanged and $T$ is the absolute temperature in **Kelvin (K)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):**
> Entropy is a *point function* (state function), meaning its change between two equilibrium states depends **only on the initial and final states**, NOT on the path taken. This is a frequently tested concept in KTU exams.

## 1.2 Formal Statement of the Second Law of Thermodynamics

The **Second Law of Thermodynamics** establishes the **directionality of natural processes** and introduces entropy as the governing state function. KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes **three classical statements**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement 1 — Clausius Statement (Heat Flow Version):**
> *"It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no effect other than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement 2 — Kelvin-Planck Statement (Heat Engine Version):**
> *"It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no effect other than the extraction of heat from a single reservoir and the performance of an equivalent amount of work."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement 3 — Entropy Statement:**
> *"The entropy of an isolated system never decreases; it either increases (for irreversible processes) or remains constant (for reversible processes)."*

$$\Delta S_{isolated} \geq 0$$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Messy Room

Imagine a **clean, organized room** (low entropy state) versus a **messy, scattered room** (high entropy state). Without spending energy to clean (work input), the room naturally progresses toward disorder. You have **never seen a messy room spontaneously clean itself**. This is the essence of the Second Law — natural processes spontaneously proceed in the direction of **increasing entropy**.

In **biochemical systems**, this analogy extends beautifully: a living cell maintains **low internal entropy** (highly organized proteins, DNA, membranes) only by **continuously exporting entropy** to its surroundings through metabolism and heat dissipation — a concept central to the thermodynamic understanding of life.

## 1.4 Microphysical Interpretation

From a **statistical thermodynamics** perspective (Boltzmann), entropy is related to the number of **microstates (W)** accessible to a system:

$$S = k_B \ln W$$

where $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$ is the **Boltzmann constant**. A larger number of accessible microstates corresponds to higher entropy. This statistical view is **essential in biochemistry** for understanding protein folding, molecular recognition, and ligand binding energetics.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Framework of the Second Law

The Second Law is operationalized through the **Clausius Inequality**, which holds for any thermodynamic cycle (reversible or irreversible):

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$$

- Equality ($=$) → **reversible cycle**
- Strict inequality ($<$) → **irreversible cycle**

For a process between two equilibrium states 1 and 2, the entropy change is path-independent:

$$\Delta S = S_2 - S_1 = \int_1^2 \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$$

## 2.2 The Principle of Entropy Increase

For an **isolated system** (no heat or mass exchange with surroundings), the Second Law dictates:

$$\Delta S_{isolated} = \Delta S_{system} + \Delta S_{surroundings} \geq 0$$

This principle is the **cornerstone of spontaneity criteria** in chemical and biochemical thermodynamics:

| Condition | Criterion | Process Type |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|
| $\Delta S_{total} > 0$ | Spontaneous | Irreversible, natural |
| $\Delta S_{total} = 0$ | Equilibrium | Reversible, quasi-static |
| $\Delta S_{total} < 0$ | Non-spontaneous | Requires work input |

## 2.3 Logical Hierarchy of the Second Law

1. **Heat naturally flows from hot to cold** (Clausius statement).
2. **No engine can convert heat 100% into work** (Kelvin-Planck statement).
3. **All real processes generate entropy** (Entropy statement).
4. **Entropy is conserved only in idealized reversible processes** (theoretical limit).
5. **The universe trends toward maximum entropy** — the **"Heat Death"** concept.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| $\#$ | Formula | Description | Applicable Process |
|------|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $dS = \delta Q_{rev} / T$ | Fundamental definition | Reversible processes |
| 2 | $\Delta S = \int_1^2 (\delta Q_{rev} / T)$ | Entropy change between states | Any reversible path |
| 3 | $\Delta S = n C_p \ln(T_2/T_1)$ | Temperature change (const. P) | Heating/cooling of ideal gas/solution |
| 4 | $\Delta S = n C_v \ln(T_2/T_1)$ | Temperature change (const. V) | Heating at constant volume |
| 5 | $\Delta S = n R \ln(V_2/V_1)$ | Volume change (isothermal) | Expansion/compression |
| 6 | $\Delta S = -n R \ln(P_2/P_1)$ | Pressure change (isothermal) | Ideal gas |
| 7 | $\Delta S = \Delta H_{trans} / T_{trans}$ | Phase transition | Melting, vaporization, sublimation |
| 8 | $\Delta S_{mix} = -R \sum x_i \ln x_i$ | Mixing of ideal solutions | Liquid/gas mixing |
| 9 | $S = k_B \ln W$ | Statistical entropy | Microstate counting |
| 10 | $\Delta S_{total} \geq 0$ | Second Law (isolated system) | Spontaneity test |
| 11 | $\oint (\delta Q / T) \leq 0$ | Clausius inequality | Any cyclic process |
| 12 | $\Delta S = \int (\delta Q_{rev} / T)$ for biochem rxn | Reaction entropy | Biochemical reactions |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility (Biochemical Context):**
> Entropy calculations are critical in:
> - **Metabolic pathway analysis** (ATP hydrolysis entropy)
> - **Protein-ligand binding thermodynamics**
> - **Fermentation process design** (mixing entropy in bioreactors)
> - **Crystallization of biomolecules**
> - **Refrigeration and cryopreservation of biological samples**

## 2.5 Physical Meaning of Entropy in Biochemical Systems

In biochemical thermodynamics, entropy changes accompany virtually every molecular process:
- **Hydrolysis of ATP** → $\Delta S > 0$ (products more disordered)
- **Protein folding** → $\Delta S < 0$ (system becomes more ordered)
- **DNA hybridization** → $\Delta S < 0$ (two strands combine)
- **Enzyme-substrate binding** → $\Delta S$ can be either sign
