> 4. **Forgetting $V_m^{\text{liquid}}$ in Clausius–Clapeyron:** For solid–liquid transitions, $\Delta V$ is small but **not** negligible — do not use the simplified C–C form. Use the full equation: $dP/dT = \Delta H_{\text{fus}} / (T \Delta V_{\text{fus}})$.
> 5. **Confusing virial B in volume-form vs pressure-form:** $B_V$ (in $1/V$ expansion) differs from $B_P$ (in $P$ expansion) — $B_P = B_V / (RT)$. Check which convention your EOS uses.
> 6. **In Trouton's rule problems:** Always report the **ratio** $\Delta H_{\text{vap}}/T_b$, not just the two values separately. The comparison value is $88\,\text{J/(mol·K)}$.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1. PVT SURFACE & CRITICAL POINT**
> - Critical point = inflection of critical isotherm: $(\partial P / \partial V)_T = (\partial^2 P / \partial V^2)_T = 0$
> - Triple point is invariant for a pure substance ($F = 0$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **2. REAL-GAS EQUATIONS OF STATE**
> - **Virial EOS** (theoretically rigorous): $Z = 1 + B/V_m + C/V_m^2 + \cdots$
> - **Van der Waals** (historical): $a = 27 R^2 T_c^2 / (64 P_c)$, $b = R T_c / (8 P_c)$
> - **Redlich–Kwong**: $a = 0.42748 R^2 T_c^{2.5}/P_c$, $b = 0.08664 R T_c / P_c$
> - **SRK & PR**: Use temperature-dependent $a(T)$ and acentric factor $\omega$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **3. COMPRESSIBILITY & CORRESPONDING STATES**
> - $Z_c$ for most fluids is $\sim 0.27$–$0.29$ (experiment).
> - $Z_c = 3/8 = 0.375$ (Van der Waals prediction).
> - Principle of Corresponding States: $Z = Z(T_r, P_r, \omega)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **4. DEPARTURE FUNCTIONS (Virial Form)**
> - $H^R = P[B - T(dB/dT)]$
> - $S^R = -P(dB/dT)$
> - $V^R = B$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **5. FUGACITY & FUGACITY COEFFICIENT**
> - $\ln \phi = \int_0^P (Z-1) dP/P$
> - Virial: $\ln \phi = BP/(RT)$
> - $f = \phi P$; $f \to P$ as $P \to 0$ (ideal-gas limit).
> - Phase equilibrium: $f^{\alpha} = f^{\beta}$ (for pure component, $\alpha$ and $\beta$ phases).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **6. CLAUSIUS–CLAPEYRON**
> - General: $dP/dT = \Delta H_{\text{trans}} / (T \Delta V_{\text{trans}})$
> - For vaporization with $V_{\text{vap}} \gg V_{\text{liq}}$ and ideal-gas vapor: $\ln P^{\text{sat}} = A - B/T$
> - **Antoine** (3-parameter empirical): $\ln P^{\text{sat}} = A - B/(T+C)$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **7. PHASE RULE**
> - $F = C - P_{\text{phases}} + 2$
> - For pure substance: $F = 3 - P_{\text{phases}}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **8. TROUTON'S RULE**
> - $\Delta S_{\text{vap}} = \Delta H_{\text{vap}}/T_b \approx 88\,\text{J/(mol·K)}$
> - Water deviates ($\sim 109$) due to H-bonding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **9. NUMERIC VALUES TO MEMORIZE**
> - $R = 8.314\,\text{J/(mol·K)} = 8.314\,\text{Pa·m}^3/(\text{mol·K})$
> - $1\,\text{atm} = 1.01325 \times 10^5\,\text{Pa} = 101.325\,\text{kPa}$
> - Water triple point: $T_t = 273.16\,\text{K}$, $P_t = 611.657\,\text{Pa}$
> - $\text{CO}_2$ critical point: $T_c = 304.25\,\text{K}$, $P_c = 73.8\,\text{bar}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **10. ENGINEERING APPLICATIONS (for essay-type answers)**
> - **Steam tables & Mollier diagrams** — practical $h$, $s$ of water for power cycles.
> - **Cryogenics** — phase behavior of $\text{N}_2$, $\text{O}_2$, $\text{CH}_4$, LNG storage.
> - **Supercritical fluid extraction** — $\text{CO}_2$ above $T_c, P_c$ used in decaffeination, essential-oil extraction.
> - **Refrigeration cycles** — $\text{NH}_3$, R134a phase changes.
> - **Lyophilization (freeze-drying)** — water sublimation at low $P$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermodynamic Properties and Their Relationships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT414 - BIOCHEMICAL THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Thermodynamic Properties and Their Relationships ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **thermodynamic property** (also called a **state function** or **state variable**) is a macroscopic, physically measurable characteristic of a system whose value depends **only on the current state** of the system — described by independent variables such as temperature ($T$), pressure ($P$), volume ($V$), composition ($N_i$), and entropy ($S$) — and is independent of the *path* or *history* through which that state was attained. The eight principal thermodynamic properties studied in biochemical thermodynamics are: $$ U \;(Internal\;Energy), \quad H \;(Enthalpy), \quad S \;(Entropy) $$ $$ A \;(Helmholtz\;Free\;Energy), \quad G \;(Gibbs\;Free\;Energy) $$ $$ P, \quad V, \quad T $$ These are linked by the **fundamental property relations** and their derived **Maxwell relations**, which form the mathematical backbone of the subject. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 explicitly focuses on deriving the relationships *between* these properties (U, H, A, G) and using them to evaluate changes in systems of biochemical interest (reactions, phase equilibria, electrolyte solutions). ### 1.2 Classification of Properties | Category | Definition | Examples | |----------|------------|----------| | **Extensive** | Proportional to system size / amount of matter | $U, H, S, A, G, V$ | | **Intensive** | Independent of system size | $T, P, \rho, \mu_i$ | | **State Function** | Path-independent; exact differential | $U, H, S, A, G$ | | **Path Function** | Path-dependent; inexact differential | $\delta Q, \delta W$ | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Mountain Hike Bank Account" > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a hiker climbing a mountain. Their **altitude** at the summit is a *state function* — it doesn't matter whether they took the steep trail or the winding trail; the final altitude is the same. In contrast, the **calories burned** (work done) is a *path function* — it depends entirely on which path they took. In a biochemical system, the **Gibbs free energy change ($\Delta G$)** of a reaction is a state function: it tells us only the difference between the final (products) and initial (reactants) thermodynamic states, not the molecular route the reaction took through its transition state. This is precisely why $\Delta G$ is the single most useful quantity to predict the *spontaneity* of a biochemical reaction at constant $T$ and $P$. ### 1.4 The Four Thermodynamic Potentials — "The Big Four" > [!IMPORTANT] > The four *thermodynamic potentials* — $U$, $H$, $A$, and $G$ — are not independent energies; they are **Legendre transforms** of each other, designed to make the natural variables of a given experimental condition the "independent" variables. The **natural variables** (the variables held constant in a given process) of each potential are: - $U(S, V, N_i)$ — entropy and volume constant (isolated system) - $H(S, P, N_i)$ — entropy and pressure constant - $A(T, V, N_i)$ — temperature and volume constant - $G(T, P, N_i)$ — temperature and pressure constant (most relevant to biology!) > [!NOTE] > **Why $G(T,P,N_i)$ matters in biochemistry:** Living cells operate at approximately constant $T$ ($\approx 310 \;K$) and constant $P$ ($1 \;atm$). Hence, the Gibbs free energy is the **master potential** for biological systems. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four Fundamental Property Relations Starting from the First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics combined for a multicomponent closed system, the **fundamental energy balance** is: $$ dU = T\,dS - P\,dV + \sum_{i} \mu_i\, dN_i $$ where $\mu_i = \left( \dfrac{\partial U}{\partial N_i} \right)_{S,V,N_{j\ne i}}$ is the **chemical potential** of species $i$. By applying the definitions $H = U + PV$, $A = U - TS$, and $G = H - TS = U + PV - TS$, the remaining three **fundamental property relations** are obtained: - **Enthalpy:** $dH = T\,dS + V\,dP + \sum_i \mu_i\,dN_i$ - **Helmholtz free energy:** $dA = -S\,dT - P\,dV + \sum_i \mu_i\,dN_i$ - **Gibbs free energy:** $dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP + \sum_i \mu_i\,dN_i$ > [!NOTE] > **Why are these so powerful?** Because the differentials $dU$, $dH$, $dA$, and $dG$ are **exact differentials** (since $U$, $H$, $A$, and $G$ are state functions), the mixed second partial derivatives are independent of the order of differentiation. This mathematical truth is the **birthplace of the Maxwell relations**. ### 2.2 The Maxwell Relations Applying **Euler's reciprocity rule** ($\dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x \partial y} = \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y \partial x}$) to each of the four fundamental property relations yields **four pairs of Maxwell relations**: **From $dU$:** $$ \left( \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial V} \right)_{S,N} = -\left( \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial S} \right)_{V,N} $$ **From $dH$:** $$ \left( \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P} \right)_{S,N} = \left( \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial S} \right)_{P,N} $$ **From $dA$:** $$ \left( \dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V} \right)_{T,N} = \left( \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T} \right)_{V,N} $$ **From $dG$:** $$ \left( \dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P} \right)_{T,N} = -\left( \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_{P,N} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Note:** The Maxwell relations allow us to replace *difficult-to-measure* quantities (like $\partial S/\partial P$ or $\partial S/\partial V$) with *easily-measured* ones (like $\partial V/\partial T$ or $\partial P/\partial T$). This is the essence of "property relations" — *transforming a derivative you cannot measure into one you can*. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Relation | Natural Variables | Main Use | |---|----------|-------------------|----------| | 1 | $dU = T\,dS - P\,dV + \sum \mu_i dN_i$ | $U(S,V,N_i)$ | Isolated systems | | 2 | $dH = T\,dS + V\,dP + \sum \mu_i dN_i$ | $H(S,P,N_i)$ | Constant-P processes | | 3 | $dA = -S\,dT - P\,dV + \sum \mu_i dN_i$ | $A(T,V,N_i)$ | Constant-T,V | | 4 | $dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP + \sum \mu_i dN_i$ | $G(T,P,N_i)$ | **Biochemical reactions** | | 5 | $G = H - TS$ | — | Definition | | 6 | $A = U - TS$ | — | Definition | | 7 | $H = U + PV$ | — | Definition | | 8 | $\left(\partial S / \partial P\right)_{T,N} = -\left(\partial V / \partial T\right)_{P,N}$ | — | Maxwell (G) | | 9 | $\left(\partial S / \partial V\right)_{T,N} = \left(\partial P / \partial T\right)_{V,N}$ | — | Maxwell (A) | | 10 | $\left(\partial T / \partial P\right)_{S,N} = \left(\partial V / \partial S\right)_{P,N}$ | — | Maxwell (H) | | 11 | $\left(\partial T / \partial V\right)_{S,N} = -\left(\partial P / \partial S\right)_{V,N}$ | — | Maxwell (U) | | 12 | $\left(\partial G / \partial T\right)_{P,N} = -S$ | — | Gibbs–Helmholtz input | | 13 | $\left(\partial G / \partial P\right)_{T,N} = V$ | — | Pressure dependence | | 14 | $\left(\partial A / \partial V\right)_{T,N} = -P$ | — | Pressure from $A$ | | 15 | $\left(\partial H / \partial P\right)_{S,N} = V$ | — | Enthalpy–volume link | > [!WARNING] > **Sign convention warning:** The sign on $P$ is **negative** in $dU$ and $dA$ (compression *increases* $U$ but $-P\,dV$ for expansion *decreases* $U$), and **positive** in $dH$ and $dG$. KTU examiners frequently test this sign. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering / Biochemical Utility - **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Maxwell relations link entropy change of a drug to its volume expansion coefficient — crucial in designing stable injectable formulations. - **Food Processing:** Predicting how $G$ varies with $T$ (via $-S$) determines phase stability of emulsions and crystallization of fats. - **Cryobiology:** Property relations govern how ice formation in cells alters $\Delta G$ during freezing of biological tissues. - **Metabolic Engineering:** The Gibbs–Helmholtz equation (derived from these relations) predicts how $\Delta G$ of ATP hydrolysis varies with cellular temperature. ### 2.5 The Gibbs–Helmholtz Equation (Biochemically Critical) Combining the temperature dependence of $G$ with the definition $G = H - TS$ yields the **Gibbs–Helmholtz equation**: $$ \left[ \dfrac{\partial (\Delta G / T)}{\partial T} \right]_P = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{T^2} $$ This equation is the **workhorse for predicting the temperature dependence of biochemical reaction equilibria** and is one of the most-frequently asked derivations in KTU examinations. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Four Maxwell Relations from Exactness of Differentials **Starting Point:** Consider the Gibbs free energy $G(T, P, N_i)$ as a function of $T$, $P$, and $N_i$. Its total differential is: $$ dG = \left( \dfrac{\partial G}{\partial T} \right)_{P,N} dT + \left( \dfrac{\partial G}{\partial P} \right)_{T,N} dP + \sum_i \left( \dfrac{\partial G}{\partial N_i} \right)_{T,P,N_{j\ne i}} dN_i $$ **Step 1 — Identification of partial derivatives:** Compare with the fundamental relation $dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP + \sum_i \mu_i\,dN_i$. Equating coefficients of the independent differentials $dT$, $dP$, and $dN_i$: $$ \left( \dfrac{\partial G}{\partial T} \right)_{P,N} = -S $$ $$ \left( \dfrac{\partial G}{\partial P} \right)_{T,N} = V $$ $$ \left( \dfrac{\partial G}{\partial N_i} \right)_{T,P,N_{j\ne i}} = \mu_i $$ **Step 2 — Apply the condition for an exact differential:** Since $dG$ is exact (because $G$ is a state function), the **mixed second partial derivatives must be equal in any order**. Apply this to the $T$ and $P$ derivatives of $G$: $$ \dfrac{\partial^2 G}{\partial P \partial T} = \dfrac{\partial^2 G}{\partial T \partial P} $$ **Step 3 — Compute each mixed derivative explicitly:** From Step 1, take the derivative of $\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P,N} = -S$ with respect to $P$ at constant $T$ and $N$: $$ \dfrac{\partial^2 G}{\partial P \partial T} = -\left( \dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P} \right)_{T,N} $$ Next, take the derivative of $\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial P}\right)_{T,N} = V$ with respect to $T$ at constant $P$ and $N$: $$ \dfrac{\partial^2 G}{\partial T \partial P} = \left( \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_{P,N} $$ **Step 4 — Equate and obtain the Maxwell relation:** $$ -\left( \dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P} \right)_{T,N} = \left( \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_{P,N} $$ $$ \boxed{\; \left( \dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P} \right)_{T,N} = -\left( \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_{P,N} \;} $$ This is the **Maxwell relation derived from the Gibbs free energy** — the most biochemically relevant one. **Step 5 — Analogous derivation from $A(T, V, N)$:** Starting from $dA = -S\,dT - P\,dV + \sum_i \mu_i dN_i$: $$ \left(\dfrac{\partial A}{\partial T}\right)_{V,N} = -S, \qquad \left(\dfrac{\partial A}{\partial V}\right)_{T,N} = -P $$ $$ \dfrac{\partial^2 A}{\partial V \partial T} = -\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_{T,N} $$ $$ \dfrac{\partial^2 A}{\partial T \partial V} = -\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_{V,N} $$ $$ \boxed{\; \left( \dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V} \right)_{T,N} = \left( \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T} \right)_{V,N} \;} $$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Gibbs–Helmholtz Equation **Goal:** Derive $\left[ \dfrac{\partial (\Delta G / T)}{\partial T} \right]_P = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{T^2}$. **Step 1 — Start from the temperature dependence of $G$:** We know from the fundamental relation that $\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P = -S$. **Step 2 — Substitute $S = (H - G)/T$:** Since $G = H - TS$, we have $S = \dfrac{H - G}{T}$. **Step 3 — Write the derivative in a useful form:** $$ \left( \dfrac{\partial G}{\partial T} \right)_P = -\dfrac{H - G}{T} = \dfrac{G - H}{T} $$ **Step 4 — Divide both sides by $T$:** $$ \dfrac{1}{T} \left( \dfrac{\partial G}{\partial T} \right)_P = \dfrac{G}{T^2} - \dfrac{H}{T^2} $$ **Step 5 — Rearrange to isolate the desired derivative:** Notice the left-hand side is precisely the derivative of $G/T$ with respect to $T$ at constant $P$ (by the quotient rule): $$ \left( \dfrac{\partial (G/T)}{\partial T} \right)_P = \dfrac{1}{T} \left( \dfrac{\partial G}{\partial T} \right)_P - \dfrac{G}{T^2} $$ **Step 6 — Combine Steps 4 and 5:** $$ \left( \dfrac{\partial (G/T)}{\partial T} \right)_P = \dfrac{G}{T^2} - \dfrac{H}{T^2} - \dfrac{G}{T^2} $$ $$ \left( \dfrac{\partial (G/T)}{\partial T} \right)_P = -\dfrac{H}{T^2} $$ **Step 7 — Apply to a reaction (change from reactants to products):** $$ \boxed{\; \left[ \dfrac{\partial (\Delta G / T)}{\partial T} \right]_P = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{T^2} \;} $$ This is the **Gibbs–Helmholtz equation**, which gives the temperature coefficient of $\Delta G$ in terms of the reaction enthalpy $\Delta H$. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Expression for $(\partial U/\partial V)_T$ (Internal Pressure) **Goal:** Show that $\left(\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T = T\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V - P$. **Step 1 — Start with $U$ as a function of $T$ and $V$:** $U = U(T, V)$, so $$ dU = \left( \dfrac{\partial U}{\partial T} \right)_V dT + \left( \dfrac{\partial U}{\partial V} \right)_T dV $$ **Step 2 — But $U$ is fundamentally a function of $S$ and $V$:** $U = U(S, V)$, so $$ dU = T\,dS - P\,dV $$ **Step 3 — Express $dS$ in terms of $dT$ and $dV$:** Write $S = S(T, V)$: $$ dS = \left( \dfrac{\partial S}{\partial T} \right)_V dT + \left( \dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V} \right)_T dV $$ **Step 4 — Substitute into Step 2:** $$ dU = T \left[ \left( \dfrac{\partial S}{\partial T} \right)_V dT + \left( \dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V} \right)_T dV \right] - P\,dV $$ **Step 5 — Compare with Step 1 to identify the coefficient of $dV$ at constant $T$:** $$ \left( \dfrac{\partial U}{\partial V} \right)_T = T \left( \dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V} \right)_T - P $$ **Step 6 — Apply the Maxwell relation from $A$:** $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$. $$ \boxed{\; \left( \dfrac{\partial U}{\partial V} \right)_T = T \left( \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T} \right)_V - P \;} $$ > [!NOTE] > For an **ideal gas**, $P V = n R T$, so $\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = \dfrac{nR}{V}$, and hence $\left(\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T = T \cdot \dfrac{nR}{V} - P = 0$. This confirms **Joule's classical result** that internal energy of an ideal gas depends *only* on $T$. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Relationships at a Glance | Partial Derivative | Equals | Provenance | |--------------------|--------|------------| | $\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P$ | $-S$ | Fundamental relation of $G$ | | $\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial P}\right)_T$ | $V$ | Fundamental relation of $G$ | | $\left(\dfrac{\partial H}{\partial S}\right)_P$ | $T$ | Fundamental relation of $H$ | | $\left(\dfrac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_S$ | $V$ | Fundamental relation of $H$ | | $\left(\dfrac{\partial A}{\partial T}\right)_V$ | $-S$ | Fundamental relation of $A$ | | $\left(\dfrac{\partial A}{\partial V}\right)_T$ | $-P$ | Fundamental relation of $A$ | | $\left(\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial S}\right)_V$ | $T$ | Fundamental relation of $U$ | | $\left(\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_S$ | $-P$ | Fundamental relation of $U$ | | $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T$ | $-\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ | Maxwell ($G$) | | $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T$ | $\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$ | Maxwell ($A$) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Web of Thermodynamic Potentials The following block diagram illustrates the four thermodynamic potentials and how the Maxwell relations serve as the "bridges" connecting *difficult-to-measure* entropy derivatives to *easily-measurable* mechanical/thermal derivatives. ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef potential fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef maxwell fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef natural fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef measurable fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px U["U Internal Energy
Natural vars S V N"]:::potential H["H Enthalpy
H equals U plus PV"]:::potential A["A Helmholtz Energy
A equals U minus TS"]:::potential G["G Gibbs Free Energy
G equals H minus TS
MOST USED IN BIOCHEM"]:::potential Svar["Entropy Derivative
dS by dP or dV at T"]:::natural Vvar["Volume Derivative
dV by dT at P"]:::measurable Pvar["Pressure Derivative
dP by dT at V"]:::measurable M1["Maxwell from G
dS/dP at T equals minus dV/dT at P"]:::maxwell M2["Maxwell from A
dS/dV at T equals dP/dT at V"]:::maxwell M3["Maxwell from H
dT/dP at S equals dV/dS at P"]:::maxwell M4["Maxwell from U
dT/dV at S equals minus dP/dS at V"]:::maxwell U -->|Legendre transform| H U -->|Legendre transform| A H -->|Legendre transform| G A -->|Legendre transform| G G --> M1 A --> M2 H --> M3 U --> M4 M1 -->|converts| Svar M1 -->|to measurable| Vvar M2 -->|converts| Svar M2 -->|to measurable| Pvar ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Logic for Choosing the Right Potential ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef start fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff classDef endnode fill:#00b050,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff classDef branch fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000,color:#000000 Q["What are the constant conditions of your process?"]:::start Q1["Constant T and Constant P
Standard lab and bio conditions"]:::branch Q2["Constant T and Constant V
Sealed rigid container"]:::branch Q3["Constant S and Constant P
Adiabatic throttling"]:::branch Q4["Constant S and Constant V
Isolated system"]:::branch A1["Use GIBBS FREE ENERGY G
dG equals minus S dT plus V dP"]:::endnode A2["Use HELMHOLTZ FREE ENERGY A
dA equals minus S dT minus P dV"]:::endnode A3["Use ENTHALPY H
dH equals T dS plus V dP"]:::endnode A4["Use INTERNAL ENERGY U
dU equals T dS minus P dV"]:::endnode Q --> Q1 --> A1 Q --> Q2 --> A2 Q --> Q3 --> A3 Q --> Q4 --> A4 ``` ### 4.3 Mapping Table — Equations to Their Biochemical Applications | Relation Type | Equation Form | Biochemical Application | |---------------|---------------|--------------------------| | Pressure dependence of $G$ | $\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial P}\right)_T = V$ | Effect of blood pressure on protein folding | | Temperature dependence of $G$ | $\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P = -S$ | Thermal stability of enzymes | | Gibbs–Helmholtz | $\left[\dfrac{\partial (\Delta G/T)}{\partial T}\right]_P = -\Delta H/T^2$ | Temperature shift of $\Delta G$ in metabolic reactions | | Maxwell ($G$) | $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ | Predicting entropy change from thermal expansion | ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]` **Define a state function and a path function. Give two examples of each from biochemical thermodynamics.** **Model Answer:** A **state function** is a thermodynamic property whose value depends *only* on the current state of the system and is independent of the path by which the state was reached. Its differential is **exact** ($dF$). Examples: Internal Energy ($U$), Enthalpy ($H$), Entropy ($S$), Gibbs free energy ($G$), Helmholtz free energy ($A$). A **path function** is a thermodynamic quantity whose value depends on the *specific path* followed during a process. Its differential is **inexact** ($\delta F$). Examples: Heat ($\delta Q$), Work ($\delta W$). > The Gibbs free energy change $\Delta G$ of ATP hydrolysis ($\Delta G \approx -30.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$ under cellular conditions) is a state function — its value is the same whether ATP is hydrolysed by myosin ATPase or by Na+/K+ ATPase. --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]` **State the Maxwell relation derived from the Gibbs free energy and explain its physical significance.** **Model Answer:** $$ \left( \dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P} \right)_{T,N} = -\left( \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_{P,N} $$ **Physical significance:** The LHS is the rate of change of entropy with pressure at constant temperature — a quantity that is virtually impossible to measure directly because one cannot easily isolate the entropy change of a substance at constant $T$ while varying $P$. The RHS, however, contains the **thermal expansion coefficient** $\alpha = (1/V)(\partial V/\partial T)_P$, which is straightforwardly measured by tracking volume change versus temperature. Thus the Maxwell relation **transforms an unmeasurable entropy derivative into a measurable volumetric derivative**, enabling us to compute entropy changes of biochemical systems (proteins, membranes, electrolyte solutions) from routine $PVT$ data. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A *very common* error is **forgetting the negative sign** in $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$. The negative sign arises because $S$ appears with a minus sign in $dG$ while $V$ appears with a plus sign. A second common error is **dropping the subscript indicating which variables are held constant** — KTU examiners *will* deduct up to 2 marks for ambiguous notation. Always write full subscripts: $(\partial S / \partial P)_{T,N}$ — not $\partial S / \partial P$. --- #### **QUESTION A — (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]` **(a)** Derive the Maxwell relation $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_{T,N} = \left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_{V,N}$ starting from the fundamental property relation for the Helmholtz free energy. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Starting from the fundamental property relations, derive the **Gibbs–Helmholtz equation** $\left[\dfrac{\partial (\Delta G / T)}{\partial T}\right]_P = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{T^2}$ and hence explain its importance in predicting the temperature dependence of biochemical reaction equilibria. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 (1 mark):** The Helmholtz free energy is defined as $A = U - TS$, with natural variables $T, V, N_i$. Its total differential is: $$ dA = -S\,dT - P\,dV + \sum_i \mu_i\,dN_i $$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Compare with the general form $dA = \left(\dfrac{\partial A}{\partial T}\right)_{V,N}dT + \left(\dfrac{\partial A}{\partial V}\right)_{T,N}dV + \sum_i \left(\dfrac{\partial A}{\partial N_i}\right)_{T,V,N_{j\ne i}}dN_i$. Equating coefficients: $$ \left(\dfrac{\partial A}{\partial T}\right)_{V,N} = -S, \qquad \left(\dfrac{\partial A}{\partial V}\right)_{T,N} = -P $$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Since $dA$ is an exact differential (because $A$ is a state function), the mixed second partial derivatives are equal in any order: $$ \dfrac{\partial^2 A}{\partial V\, \partial T} = \dfrac{\partial^2 A}{\partial T\, \partial V} $$ **Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute each mixed derivative: $$ \dfrac{\partial^2 A}{\partial V\, \partial T} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial V}\left(\dfrac{\partial A}{\partial T}\right)_{V,N} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial V}\bigl(-S\bigr) = -\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_{T,N} $$ $$ \dfrac{\partial^2 A}{\partial T\, \partial V} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\dfrac{\partial A}{\partial V}\right)_{T,N} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial T}\bigl(-P\bigr) = -\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_{V,N} $$ **Step 5 (2 marks):** Equate and cancel the negatives: $$ -\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_{T,N} = -\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_{V,N} $$ $$ \boxed{\; \left( \dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V} \right)_{T,N} = \left( \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T} \right)_{V,N} \;} \quad \blacksquare $$ --- **Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 (1 mark):** From the fundamental relation for $G$, identify the temperature derivative: $$ \left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P,N} = -S $$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Use the definition $G = H - TS$ to write $S = (H - G)/T$. Substituting: $$ \left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P,N} = -\dfrac{H - G}{T} = \dfrac{G - H}{T} $$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Divide both sides by $T$: $$ \dfrac{1}{T}\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P = \dfrac{G}{T^2} - \dfrac{H}{T^2} $$ **Step 4 (2 marks):** Recognize that the LHS can be expressed as the derivative of $G/T$ via the quotient rule: $$ \left(\dfrac{\partial (G/T)}{\partial T}\right)_P = \dfrac{1}{T}\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P - \dfrac{G}{T^2} $$ Subtracting $G/T^2$ from both sides of Step 3: $$ \left(\dfrac{\partial (G/T)}{\partial T}\right)_P = \dfrac{G}{T^2} - \dfrac{H}{T^2} - \dfrac{G}{T^2} = -\dfrac{H}{T^2} $$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Apply to a reaction (products minus reactants): $$ \boxed{\; \left[ \dfrac{\partial (\Delta G / T)}{\partial T} \right]_P = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{T^2} \;} \quad \blacksquare $$ **Importance in biochemistry (1 mark):** This equation is essential because biochemical reactions occur at varying temperatures (e.g., enzyme assays at 25 °C, 30 °C, 37 °C). The equation tells us that the *sign* of $\Delta H$ dictates whether $\Delta G$ becomes more negative (more spontaneous) or more positive (less spontaneous) as $T$ increases. For example, in *denaturation* of proteins, $\Delta H > 0$ (endothermic unfolding) means $\Delta G$ of unfolding becomes more negative with temperature, correctly predicting that proteins unfold (denature) on heating. --- #### **QUESTION B — (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]` **(a)** Define enthalpy, Helmholtz free energy, and Gibbs free energy. Derive the four fundamental property relations from the First and Second Laws. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the relation $\left(\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T = T\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V - P$ and use it to prove that for an ideal gas the internal energy depends only on temperature. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks:** **Definitions (2 marks):** - **Enthalpy:** $H = U + PV$ — convenient for processes at constant pressure where $PdV$-work appears. - **Helmholtz free energy:** $A = U - TS$ — convenient for processes at constant $T$ and $V$. - **Gibbs free energy:** $G = H - TS = U + PV - TS$ — convenient for processes at constant $T$ and $P$ (biochemical standard). **Derivation of the four fundamental relations (5 marks):** **Step 1 (1 mark) — Starting point:** Combined First and Second Law for a closed multicomponent system: $$ dU = \delta Q - \delta W = T\,dS - P\,dV + \sum_i \mu_i\,dN_i \quad \text{(Relation 1)} $$ **Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Derive $dH$:** Differentiate $H = U + PV$: $$ dH = dU + P\,dV + V\,dP = T\,dS - P\,dV + \sum_i \mu_i\,dN_i + P\,dV + V\,dP $$ $$ dH = T\,dS + V\,dP + \sum_i \mu_i\,dN_i \quad \text{(Relation 2)} $$ **Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Derive $dA$:** Differentiate $A = U - TS$: $$ dA = dU - T\,dS - S\,dT = T\,dS - P\,dV + \sum_i \mu_i\,dN_i - T\,dS - S\,dT $$ $$ dA = -S\,dT - P\,dV + \sum_i \mu_i\,dN_i \quad \text{(Relation 3)} $$ **Step 4 (1 mark) — Derive $dG$:** Differentiate $G = H - TS$: $$ dG = dH - T\,dS - S\,dT = T\,dS + V\,dP + \sum_i \mu_i\,dN_i - T\,dS - S\,dT $$ $$ dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP + \sum_i \mu_i\,dN_i \quad \text{(Relation 4)} $$ --- **Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Write $U$ in terms of $T$ and $V$: $$ dU = \left(\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V dT + \left(\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T dV $$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Also, from the fundamental relation $U = U(S, V)$: $$ dU = T\,dS - P\,dV $$ **Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Express $dS$ in terms of $dT$ and $dV$: $$ dS = \left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_V dT + \left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T dV $$ **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Substitute into the fundamental relation and compare coefficients of $dV$ at constant $T$: $$ dU = T\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_V dT + T\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T dV - P\,dV $$ Comparing with Step 1, the coefficient of $dV$ at constant $T$ is: $$ \left(\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T = T\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T - P $$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Apply the Maxwell relation $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$: $$ \boxed{\; \left( \dfrac{\partial U}{\partial V} \right)_T = T \left( \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T} \right)_V - P \;} \quad \blacksquare $$ **Step 6 (1 mark) — Application to ideal gas:** For an ideal gas, $PV = nRT$, so $P = nRT/V$ and $\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = nR/V$. Substituting: $$ \left(\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T = T \cdot \dfrac{nR}{V} - P = \dfrac{nRT}{V} - \dfrac{nRT}{V} = 0 $$ Therefore, $U$ does *not* change with volume at constant $T$ for an ideal gas, proving that $U = U(T)$ only. $\blacksquare$ --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — must memorize before exam:** - **State vs Path:** $U, H, S, A, G$ are *state functions*; $Q, W$ are *path functions*. - **The Big Four Potentials** and their natural variable pairs: - $U(S, V, N)$ | $H(S, P, N)$ | $A(T, V, N)$ | $G(T, P, N)$ - **Sign of $P\,dV$:** Negative in $dU$ and $dA$; positive as $V\,dP$ in $dH$ and $dG$. - **The four Maxwell relations** (must write all four if asked): - $\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = -\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V$ - $\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_S = \left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial S}\right)_P$ - $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$ - $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ - **Partial derivatives of the Big Four:** - $(\partial U/\partial S)_V = T$, $(\partial U/\partial V)_S = -P$ - $(\partial H/\partial S)_P = T$, $(\partial H/\partial P)_S = V$ - $(\partial A/\partial T)_V = -S$, $(\partial A/\partial V)_T = -P$ - $(\partial G/\partial T)_P = -S$, $(\partial G/\partial P)_T = V$ - **Gibbs–Helmholtz equation:** $\left[\dfrac{\partial (\Delta G/T)}{\partial T}\right]_P = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{T^2}$. - **Internal energy of ideal gas:** $(\partial U/\partial V)_T = 0 \implies U = U(T)$ only. - **Definitions to be fluent with:** $H = U + PV$, $A = U - TS$, $G = H - TS$. - **KTU examiner's favourite trick:** Asking you to prove *one* Maxwell relation but the answer requires you to *first* state the fundamental relation from which it is derived, then equate mixed second partials — skipping the first step loses 1–2 marks. - **For $\mu_i$ (chemical potential) relations:** Note that each Maxwell relation also has a counterpart involving $\mu_i$; e.g., from $dG$: $\left(\dfrac{\partial \mu_i}{\partial T}\right)_{P,N} = -\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial N_i}\right)_{T,P,N_{j\ne i}}$. - **Numerical units to remember:** $G, H, U, A$ in J or kJ; $S$ in J/K; $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$; $T$ in K (always convert °C → K before substituting).

topics.title: Maxwell's Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT414 - BIOCHEMICAL THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Maxwell's Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Maxwell's Equations (Relations) in Biochemical Thermodynamics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** In the context of Biochemical Thermodynamics (PEBBT414), "Maxwell's Equations" refers to the **four Maxwell's Relations** — a set of thermodynamic identities derived from the exact-differential property of the four fundamental thermodynamic potentials. These are indispensable for computing entropy, free-energy, and reaction-equilibrium changes that are otherwise experimentally inaccessible in biochemical systems.

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Maxwell's Relations** are a set of four thermodynamic identities that interrelate the partial derivatives of the state variables *Temperature (T)*, *Pressure (P)*, *Volume (V)*, and *Entropy (S)*. They arise from the **equality of mixed second partial derivatives** (Euler's Reciprocity Theorem) of the four exact-differential thermodynamic potentials: **Internal Energy (U)**, **Enthalpy (H)**, **Helmholtz Free Energy (F)**, and **Gibbs Free Energy (G)**.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_{x} = \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_{y}
$$

whenever the differential $dz = M\,dx + N\,dy$ is exact.

The **four Maxwell's Relations** are:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_{S} = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_{V}
$$

$$
\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_{S} = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P}
$$

$$
\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_{V} = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_{T}
$$

$$
\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = -\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_{T}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic Device (KTU High-Yield):** *"UP, Down, Go Free"* — Derive from **U** (U-relation), **H** (H-relation), then **F** (F-relation), then **G** (G-relation).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **The "Currency Exchange" Analogy:** Imagine you are at an international airport currency exchange counter. You cannot directly exchange **Indian Rupees (₹)** for **Japanese Yen (¥)**. But you can convert Rupees → US Dollars → Euros → Yen, using *cross-rates*. Similarly, in thermodynamics, **entropy (S)** and **pressure (P)** or **entropy (S)** and **volume (V)** are quantities that are difficult to measure directly in a biochemical reactor. Maxwell's Relations act as the *cross-rates* that let you compute these hard-to-measure derivatives by converting them into easily measurable properties like **thermal expansion coefficient (α)**, **isothermal compressibility (κ_T)**, or **heat capacity (C_P)**.

**Why this matters in Biochemical Engineering:** When designing a bioreactor, we routinely need $\left(\partial S / \partial P\right)_T$ to predict how reaction spontaneity changes with pressure. We cannot place an entropy meter inside a cell — but we can measure volume changes with a high-pressure densitometer. Maxwell's Relation *#4* converts the unmeasurable entropy derivative into the measurable volume derivative. **The same principle underlies pressure-induced protein denaturation studies and high-pressure food preservation engineering.**

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following measurable material properties are *gateways* to applying Maxwell's Relations in biochemical systems:

- **Thermal expansion coefficient:** $\alpha = \dfrac{1}{V}\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ — units: **K⁻¹**
- **Isothermal compressibility:** $\kappa_T = -\dfrac{1}{V}\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T$ — units: **Pa⁻¹**
- **Isobaric heat capacity:** $C_P = \left(\dfrac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P = T\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P$ — units: **J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
- **Isochoric heat capacity:** $C_V = \left(\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V = T\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_V$ — units: **J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
- **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314$ **J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** = $8.314 \times 10^{-2}$ **bar·L·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Foundation: The Exact-Differential Principle

A thermodynamic state function $Z(X, Y)$ has an *exact* differential. If we have:

$$
dZ = M\,dX + N\,dY
$$

then **Euler's Reciprocity Theorem** guarantees:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial Y}\right)_{X} = \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial X}\right)_{Y}
$$

This single mathematical identity, when applied to each of the four thermodynamic potentials, generates the four Maxwell's Relations. *No new physics is required — only calculus.*

## 2.2 The Four Thermodynamic Potentials and Their Differentials

| Potential | Differential Form | Natural Variables | Measurable Coefficients Extracted |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Internal Energy** $U$ | $dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$ | $(S, V)$ | $\left(\partial U/\partial S\right)_V = T$; $\left(\partial U/\partial V\right)_S = -P$ |
| **Enthalpy** $H = U + PV$ | $dH = T\,dS + V\,dP$ | $(S, P)$ | $\left(\partial H/\partial S\right)_P = T$; $\left(\partial H/\partial P\right)_S = V$ |
| **Helmholtz Energy** $F = U - TS$ | $dF = -S\,dT - P\,dV$ | $(T, V)$ | $\left(\partial F/\partial T\right)_V = -S$; $\left(\partial F/\partial V\right)_T = -P$ |
| **Gibbs Energy** $G = H - TS$ | $dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$ | $(T, P)$ | $\left(\partial G/\partial T\right)_P = -S$; $\left(\partial G/\partial P\right)_T = V$ |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **examinable verbatim** at KTU. Memorize the **signs**, **variables held constant**, and the **source potential** for each relation.

| # | Maxwell's Relation | Source Potential | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MR-1** | $\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_{S} = -\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_{V}$ | $U(S, V)$ | Connects isentropic cooling/heating to thermal pressure |
| **MR-2** | $\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_{S} = \left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P}$ | $H(S, P)$ | Used in Joule–Thomson expansion analysis |
| **MR-3** | $\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_{V} = \left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_{T}$ | $F(T, V)$ | Predicts entropy change during isothermal volume change |
| **MR-4** | $\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = -\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_{T}$ | $G(T, P)$ | Predicts pressure dependence of reaction spontaneity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Negative-Sign Rule:** MR-1 and MR-4 carry a **minus sign**; MR-2 and MR-3 do not. This is because the mixed second derivative of $U$ and $G$ contains a negative second cross-term in their respective differentials ($-P\,dV$ in $dU$ and $-S\,dT$ in $dG$).

## 2.4 Why Maxwell's Relations are Engineering-Critical

- **Pressure Effects in Bioreactors:** MR-4 gives $\left(\partial S / \partial P\right)_T = -\alpha V$. At 1000 bar (deep-sea microorganisms), the entropy of unfolding changes significantly, directly affecting $\Delta G$ of protein denaturation.
- **High-Pressure Processing (HPP) of Food:** MR-3 is used to compute entropy and free-energy changes during pressure-induced inactivation of *E. coli* and *Listeria*.
- **Cryopreservation of Biologics:** MR-2 and MR-4 jointly predict how Gibbs energy of ice formation varies with pressure and temperature.
- **Non-Ideal Gas Behaviour in Fermentation:** The virial-coefficient corrections to $\alpha$ and $\kappa_T$ flow directly into Maxwell-derived identities like $C_P - C_V = \alpha^2 V T / \kappa_T$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility — Biochemical Case Study

Consider an enzyme reactor at **300 K** and **1 bar** containing an aqueous protein solution with $\alpha = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ **K⁻¹** and $V_m = 18$ **cm³·mol⁻¹**. The pressure-dependence of the unfolding entropy is:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = -\alpha V = -(2.5 \times 10^{-4})(18 \times 10^{-6}) = -4.5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ J·K}^{-1}\text{·Pa}^{-1}
$$

This seemingly small value becomes significant at industrial pressures (50–600 MPa in HPP), shifting $\Delta G_{unfold}$ by several kJ·mol⁻¹ — enough to drive pressure-induced denaturation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of MR-1 from Internal Energy $U$

**Starting point:** The fundamental thermodynamic identity:

$$
dU = T\,dS - P\,dV
$$

**Step 1 — Identify the partial derivatives.** Treat $U$ as $U(S, V)$ and read off:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial S}\right)_{V} = T \qquad \text{and} \qquad \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_{S} = -P
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Euler's Reciprocity Theorem.** Since $dU$ is exact, mixed second derivatives are equal:

$$
\frac{\partial^2 U}{\partial S\,\partial V} = \frac{\partial^2 U}{\partial V\,\partial S}
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate the first coefficient w.r.t. $V$ and the second w.r.t. $S$:**

$$
\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial V}\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial S}\right)_{V}\right]_{S} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_{S}
$$

$$
\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial S}\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_{S}\right]_{V} = \left(\frac{\partial (-P)}{\partial S}\right)_{V} = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_{V}
$$

**Step 4 — Equate the two expressions:**

$$
\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_{S} = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_{V}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert $\left(\partial P / \partial S\right)_V$ into the standard form.** Using the chain rule, the natural conjugate is $\left(\partial P / \partial T\right)_V$:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_{V} = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_{V}\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial S}\right)_{V} = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_{V} \cdot \frac{1}{(\partial S/\partial T)_V}
$$

For a *single-variable* conversion, applying Euler's reciprocity on the *reciprocal* derivative pair yields the **standard form of MR-1**:

$$
\boxed{\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_{S} = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_{V}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of MR-4 from Gibbs Energy $G$ (Most-Used Relation in Biochemical Engineering)

**Starting point:**

$$
dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP
$$

**Step 1 — Read off the partial derivatives:**

$$
\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = -S \qquad \text{and} \qquad \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = V
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Euler's Reciprocity Theorem to the exact differential $dG$:**

$$
\frac{\partial^2 G}{\partial P\,\partial T} = \frac{\partial^2 G}{\partial T\,\partial P}
$$

**Step 3 — Cross-differentiate each coefficient:**

$$
\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial P}\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P}\right]_{T} = \left(\frac{\partial (-S)}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = -\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_{T}
$$

$$
\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial P}\right)_{T}\right]_{P} = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P}
$$

**Step 4 — Equate:**

$$
\boxed{-\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P} \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = -\alpha V}
$$

This is **MR-4**, the workhorse of pressure-dependent reaction-equilibrium calculations.

## 3.3 Worked Example #1: Derivation of $C_P - C_V = \dfrac{\alpha^2 V T}{\kappa_T}$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Problem:** This composite identity is *derived* using Maxwell's Relations and the definitions of $\alpha$, $\kappa_T$, and $C_P, C_V$. It is asked almost every KTU cycle.

**Step 1 — Start with the definitions:**

$$
C_P = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_{P} \qquad C_V = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_{V}
$$

**Step 2 — Express both in terms of entropy using the fundamental identities:**

$$
C_P = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_{P} \qquad C_V = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the difference:**

$$
C_P - C_V = T\left[\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_{P} - \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_{V}\right]
$$

**Step 4 — Use a triple-product / chain-rule identity for entropy as a function of $T$ and $V$ (or $T$ and $P$):**

We treat $S = S(T, V)$ for the second term, then convert $(T, V)$ to $(T, P)$:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_{V} = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_{P} + \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_{T}\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_{V}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_{P} - \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_{V} = -\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_{T}\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_{V}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into Step 3:**

$$
C_P - C_V = -T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_{T}\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_{V}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply MR-4** to replace $\left(\partial S / \partial P\right)_T$:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = -\alpha V
$$

Substituting:

$$
C_P - C_V = -T(-\alpha V)\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_{V} = \alpha V T \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_{V}
$$

**Step 7 — Apply MR-3** to convert $\left(\partial P / \partial T\right)_V$:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_{V} = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_{T}
$$

And since $\left(\partial S / \partial V\right)_T = \alpha / \kappa_T$ (a standard identity; can be derived independently from MR-3 + the $\alpha, \kappa_T$ definitions):

$$
\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_{V} = \frac{\alpha}{\kappa_T}
$$

**Step 8 — Final substitution:**

$$
\boxed{C_P - C_V = \frac{\alpha^2 V T}{\kappa_T}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Sanity Check:** For an **ideal gas**, $\alpha = 1/T$ and $\kappa_T = 1/P$, so $C_P - C_V = (1/T^2) \cdot V T \cdot P = PV/T = nR$. The Mayer relation is recovered — confirming the derivation.

## 3.4 Worked Example #2: Entropy Change of an Incompressible Liquid with Pressure

**Problem:** Derive an expression for $\left(\partial S / \partial P\right)_T$ for a liquid whose volume is approximately independent of pressure.

**Step 1 — Start with MR-4:**

$$
\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = -\alpha V
$$

**Step 2 — For an *incompressible* liquid, $V \approx \text{constant}$ at fixed mass, but $\alpha \neq 0$:**

$$
\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_{T} \approx -\alpha V
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate isothermally from $P_1$ to $P_2$:**

$$
\Delta S = \int_{P_1}^{P_2} -\alpha V\,dP
$$

If $\alpha$ and $V$ are taken as approximately constant over the pressure range (valid for water up to ~100 MPa):

$$
\boxed{\Delta S \approx -\alpha V (P_2 - P_1)}
$$

**Numerical Application:** For water at 298 K with $\alpha = 2.57 \times 10^{-4}$ **K⁻¹** and $V_m = 18$ **cm³·mol⁻¹**, a pressure jump from **1 bar to 1000 bar** gives:

$$
\Delta S = -(2.57 \times 10^{-4})(18 \times 10^{-6})(1000 \times 10^5 - 1 \times 10^5) \approx -4.6 \text{ J·K}^{-1}\text{·mol}^{-1}
$$

This entropy decrease contributes to the *ordering* of water structure under pressure, relevant to pressure-induced protein denaturation.

## 3.5 Worked Example #3: Derivation of the Joule–Thomson Coefficient Expression

**Problem:** Show that the Joule–Thomson coefficient $\mu_{JT} = \left(\partial T / \partial P\right)_H$ can be expressed using $C_P$, $V$, and $T$.

**Step 1 — Use the cyclic relation for state functions** (treating $T = T(H, P)$):

$$
\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_{H} = -\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial H}\right)_{P}\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_{T}
$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that $\left(\partial H / \partial T\right)_P = C_P$, so $\left(\partial T / \partial H\right)_P = 1/C_P$:**

$$
\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_{H} = -\frac{1}{C_P}\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_{T}
$$

**Step 3 — From $dH = T\,dS + V\,dP$, at constant $T$:**

$$
\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_{T} + V
$$

**Step 4 — Apply MR-4:**

$$
\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = -\alpha V
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute:**

$$
\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = -T\alpha V + V = V(1 - \alpha T)
$$

**Step 6 — Final result:**

$$
\boxed{\mu_{JT} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_{H} = \frac{V(\alpha T - 1)}{C_P}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **inversion temperature** of a real gas is obtained by setting $\mu_{JT} = 0$, giving $T_{inv} = 1/\alpha$. This is the theoretical basis for designing throttling processes in cryogenic and gas-separation industries — directly traceable to MR-4.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Strategy:** Because Maxwell's Relations are a *mathematical network* connecting eight thermodynamic partial derivatives, the most informative visualization is a **block-level functional map** showing how each Maxwell's Relation *routes* a non-measurable derivative to a measurable material property.

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Maxwell's Relations Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PotU["Source Potential: U(S,V)"]
        dU["dU = T dS - P dV"]
    end

    subgraph PotH["Source Potential: H(S,P)"]
        dH["dH = T dS + V dP"]
    end

    subgraph PotF["Source Potential: F(T,V)"]
        dF["dF = -S dT - P dV"]
    end

    subgraph PotG["Source Potential: G(T,P)"]
        dG["dG = -S dT + V dP"]
    end

    MR1["MR-1: dT/dV at S = -dP/dT at V"]
    MR2["MR-2: dT/dP at S = dV/dT at P"]
    MR3["MR-3: dP/dT at V = dS/dV at T"]
    MR4["MR-4: dV/dT at P = -dS/dP at T"]

    MeasA["Measurable: alpha = thermal expansion coefficient"]
    MeasB["Measurable: kappaT = isothermal compressibility"]
    MeasC["Measurable: CP = isobaric heat capacity"]
    MeasD["Measurable: CV = isochoric heat capacity"]

    App1["App: Pressure-induced protein unfolding"]
    App2["App: Joule-Thomson expansion in cryogenics"]
    App3["App: CP minus CV identity derivation"]
    App4["App: Bioreactor pressure-effect analysis"]

    PotU --> MR1
    PotH --> MR2
    PotF --> MR3
    PotG --> MR4

    MR1 --> MeasC
    MR2 --> MeasA
    MR3 --> MeasB
    MR4 --> MeasA

    MR4 --> App1
    MR2 --> App2
    MR1 --> App3
    MR4 --> App4

    MeasA -. provides alpha .-> MR4
    MeasB -. provides kappaT .-> MR3
    MeasC -. provides CP .-> MR2
    MeasD -. provides CV .-> MR1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — The Sign Convention Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StartExact["Exact Differential: dZ = M dx + N dy"]
    ThmEuler["Euler Reciprocity: dM/dy = dN/dx"]
    Q1{"Is negative sign present in differential?"}
    SignYes["Apply MINUS sign in Maxwell Relation"]
    SignNo["NO minus sign in Maxwell Relation"]

    StartExact --> ThmEuler
    ThmEuler --> Q1

    Q1 -->|dU has -P dV| SignYes
    Q1 -->|dH has +V dP| SignNo
    Q1 -->|dF has -P dV| SignYes
    Q1 -->|dG has -S dT| SignYes

    SignYes --> OutMR["MR-1, MR-3, MR-4 carry minus sign"]
    SignNo --> OutMR2["MR-2 carries NO minus sign"]

    OutMR --> Final["MR-1: minus; MR-3: minus; MR-4: minus; MR-2: plus"]
    OutMR2 --> Final
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Reading Note:** The sign convention in MR-1 and MR-4 is the **single most-lost-mark** item in KTU valuation. Always cross-check by writing out the mixed-second-derivative equality explicitly before committing to the sign.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
**State the four Maxwell's Relations in thermodynamics. Identify which thermodynamic potential each is derived from.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- MR-1: $\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_{S} = -\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_{V}$ — derived from **Internal Energy $U$**. **(1 mark)**
- MR-2: $\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_{S} = \left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P}$ — derived from **Enthalpy $H$**. **(1 mark)**
- MR-3: $\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_{V} = \left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_{T}$ — derived from **Helmholtz Energy $F$**. **(0.5 mark)**
- MR-4: $\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = -\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_{T}$ — derived from **Gibbs Energy $G$**. **(0.5 mark)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**The thermal expansion coefficient of liquid water at 298 K is $2.57 \times 10^{-4}$ K⁻¹ and its molar volume is $18.0$ cm³·mol⁻¹. Using a Maxwell's Relation, compute the isothermal change in entropy per mole when water is compressed from 1 bar to 100 bar.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Apply **MR-4**: $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = -\alpha V$. **(1 mark)**
Integrate (assuming $\alpha, V$ constant):

$$
\Delta S = -\alpha V (P_2 - P_1) = -(2.57 \times 10^{-4})(18.0 \times 10^{-6})(100 \times 10^5 - 1 \times 10^5)
$$

$$
= -(2.57 \times 10^{-4})(18.0 \times 10^{-6})(99 \times 10^5) \approx -0.458 \text{ J·K}^{-1}\text{·mol}^{-1}
$$

**Numerical result: $\Delta S \approx -0.46$ J·K⁻¹·mol⁻¹**. **(2 marks — 1 mark setup, 1 mark arithmetic)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Maxwell's Relations as Computational Tools**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive MR-1 and MR-4 from their respective thermodynamic potentials. Clearly state the mathematical theorem you invoke. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the definitions of the thermal expansion coefficient $\alpha$ and the isothermal compressibility $\kappa_T$, use Maxwell's Relations to derive the identity

$$
C_P - C_V = \frac{\alpha^2 V T}{\kappa_T}
$$

**(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Derivation of MR-1 (3.5 marks):**

Starting from $dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$, identify $\left(\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial S}\right)_V = T$ and $\left(\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_S = -P$. **(0.5 mark)**

Apply **Euler's Reciprocity Theorem** for the exact differential $dU$: $\dfrac{\partial^2 U}{\partial V\,\partial S} = \dfrac{\partial^2 U}{\partial S\,\partial V}$. **(1 mark)**

Cross-differentiation yields: $\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = -\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V$. **(1 mark)**

Convert $\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V$ using the **inverse-derivative identity** to obtain the standard form $\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$. **(1 mark)**

**Derivation of MR-4 (3.5 marks):**

Starting from $dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$, identify $\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P = -S$ and $\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial P}\right)_T = V$. **(0.5 mark)**

Apply Euler's Reciprocity: $\dfrac{\partial^2 G}{\partial P\,\partial T} = \dfrac{\partial^2 G}{\partial T\,\partial P}$. **(1 mark)**

Cross-differentiation gives: $-\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = \left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$. **(1 mark)**

Rearrange: $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\alpha V$. **(1 mark)**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1** — Write $C_P = T\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P$ and $C_V = T\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_V$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2** — Form the difference:
$C_P - C_V = T\left[\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P - \left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_V\right]$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3** — Use the chain rule (treating $S = S(T,P)$ but switching one to $(T,V)$ via the cyclic identity):
$\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_V = \left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P + \left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T \left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4** — Apply **MR-4** for $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\alpha V$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 5** — Apply **MR-3** to convert $\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = \left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \dfrac{\alpha}{\kappa_T}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 6** — Combine:
$C_P - C_V = -T(-\alpha V)\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{\kappa_T}\right) = \dfrac{\alpha^2 V T}{\kappa_T}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 7** — **Sanity check (ideal gas):** $\alpha = 1/T$, $\kappa_T = 1/P$ gives $C_P - C_V = R$. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: Applied Biochemical Thermodynamics**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Starting from the Gibbs–Helmholtz equation, show that the pressure-dependence of the equilibrium constant $K$ of a biochemical reaction (e.g., protein unfolding) is governed by the reaction volume $\Delta V$. Express the result in terms of $\alpha$ and $V_m$ using a Maxwell's Relation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A protein unfolds with $\Delta V_{unfold} = +50$ cm³·mol⁻¹ at 298 K. Estimate the shift in $\Delta G$ when the pressure is raised from 1 bar to 1000 bar. Discuss the physical significance of the sign. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1** — Gibbs–Helmholtz: $\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln K$. At equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0$, so $\ln K = -\Delta G^\circ / RT$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2** — Differentiate w.r.t. $P$ at constant $T$:
$\left(\dfrac{\partial \ln K}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\dfrac{1}{RT}\left(\dfrac{\partial \Delta G^\circ}{\partial P}\right)_T$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3** — From $dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$: $\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial P}\right)_T = V$. Therefore $\left(\dfrac{\partial \Delta G^\circ}{\partial P}\right)_T = \Delta V$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4** — Combine: $\left(\dfrac{\partial \ln K}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\dfrac{\Delta V}{RT}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 5** — For the *unfolding* reaction, the *opposite* sign convention gives the pressure-shift in unfolding free energy. Using $\Delta V = \alpha V_m \Delta T$-equivalent (isobaric thermal expansion), apply **MR-4**: $\left(\dfrac{\partial \Delta S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\Delta \alpha V_m$. **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 6** — State the final form: $K$ shifts by the factor $\exp\left(-\Delta V \Delta P / RT\right)$ — the basis of high-pressure biochemistry. **(0.5 mark)**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1** — Integrate the relation from Part (a) at constant $T$:

$$
\Delta G(P_2) - \Delta G(P_1) = \int_{P_1}^{P_2} \Delta V\,dP \approx \Delta V (P_2 - P_1)
$$

**(1 mark)** (assuming $\Delta V$ is pressure-independent over the range — valid up to ~100 MPa for most proteins).

**Step 2** — Convert units: $\Delta V = 50 \times 10^{-6}$ m³·mol⁻¹, $\Delta P = (1000 - 1) \times 10^5$ Pa $\approx 10^8$ Pa. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3** — Compute:

$$
\Delta\Delta G = (50 \times 10^{-6})(1.0 \times 10^8) = 5000 \text{ J·mol}^{-1} = +5.0 \text{ kJ·mol}^{-1}
$$

**(2 marks for arithmetic; 1 mark for correct sign)**

**Step 4** — Physical interpretation: The positive $\Delta V$ of unfolding means the *unfolded* state has a larger molar volume. **Increasing pressure favours the smaller-volume (folded) state** (Le Chatelier's principle). However, the *increase* in $\Delta G$ on pressurization in this convention (using $\Delta G$ of unfolding) means unfolding is *less favourable* at high $P$ — i.e., the protein is *stabilized* against unfolding by pressure. This is the molecular basis of barostability. **(2 marks for discussion)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Valuation Warning):**
> 1. **Sign Errors in MR-1 and MR-4:** Most students forget the minus sign. Examiners *immediately* deduct **1 mark** for each sign error.
> 2. **Variable-Held-Constant Notations:** Writing $\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)$ *without* the subscript $S$ loses **0.5 mark per relation**.
> 3. **Confusion Between $\alpha$ Definitions:** $\alpha = \dfrac{1}{V}\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ is **not** the same as $\dfrac{\partial \ln V}{\partial T}$. Use the dimensionless $\alpha$ when substituting.
> 4. **Ideal-Gas Assumption:** Do **not** apply $PV = nRT$ identities for $C_P - C_V$ if the problem states a real gas or liquid. Always go through the $\alpha$-$\kappa_T$ route.
> 5. **Inversion of $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T$ vs. $\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_T$:** These are **not equal**; they are reciprocals scaled by the cross-derivative. Show the conversion explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rapid-Revision Checklist — Maxwell's Relations**

- **Origin:** Exact-differential property + Euler's Reciprocity Theorem (equality of mixed second partial derivatives).
- **Four Potentials:** $U(S,V)$, $H(S,P)$, $F(T,V)$, $G(T,P)$.
- **Four Relations — Memorize with Signs:**
   - $U \to \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_{S} = -\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_{V}$ **(−)**
   - $H \to \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_{S} = \left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P}$ **(+)** — only positive sign
   - $F \to \left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_{V} = \left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_{T}$ **(+)**
   - $G \to \left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = -\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_{T}$ **(−)**
- **The Workhorse:** MR-4 — most-frequently used in biochemical pressure-equilibrium problems.
- **Measurable Bridge:** $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\alpha V$ links the unmeasurable entropy derivative to the measurable thermal expansion coefficient.
- **Composite Identity:** $C_P - C_V = \dfrac{\alpha^2 V T}{\kappa_T}$ — KTU favourite derivation.
- **Joule–Thomson Coefficient:** $\mu_{JT} = \dfrac{V(\alpha T - 1)}{C_P}$.
- **Biochemical Applications:** Pressure-dependent protein unfolding, enzyme reactor design, high-pressure food processing, cryopreservation of biologics.
- **Sign Convention Rule:** "If the second term in the differential is negative ($-P\,dV$ in $dU$ and $dF$; $-S\,dT$ in $dG$), the resulting Maxwell's Relation carries a minus sign. If positive (like $+V\,dP$ in $dH$), the relation is positive."
- **Subscript Discipline:** Always state *what is held constant* — missing subscripts cost 0.5 mark each at KTU valuation.
- **Sanity Check:** All four relations must reduce to the Mayer relation ($C_P - C_V = R$) for an ideal gas.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gibbs-Helmholtz Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT414 - BIOCHEMICAL THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gibbs-Helmholtz Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Gibbs-Helmholtz Equation** is a fundamental thermodynamic relation that expresses the **temperature dependence of the Gibbs free energy change** ($\Delta G$) of a process in terms of the **enthalpy change** ($\Delta H$) of that process. It is mathematically derived by combining the first and second laws of thermodynamics and provides a direct link between the energetics of a reaction and its thermal sensitivity.

For a process occurring at constant pressure, the Gibbs-Helmholtz equation can be expressed in two principal forms:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Differential Form:**
> $$\left(\frac{\partial (\Delta G / T)}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = -\frac{\Delta H}{T^{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative Form (after dividing by T):**
> $$\left(\frac{\partial (\Delta G / T)}{\partial (1/T)}\right)_{P} = \Delta H$$

These equations are indispensable in **biochemical thermodynamics** because they allow us to predict how the spontaneity ($\Delta G < 0$) of a metabolic reaction, protein folding, ligand binding, or enzyme-catalyzed transformation changes with physiological temperature ($T \approx 298$ K to $310$ K).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Thermos of Chemical Reactions"

Imagine a chemical reaction as a **leaky thermos flask** holding energy. The total energy in the flask is $\Delta H$ (enthalpy, like the "total heat content"). However, only a portion of this energy is **available to do useful work** — that portion is $\Delta G$ (Gibbs free energy). The unusable portion is $T\Delta S$ (entropic loss to the surroundings).

Now, the thermos has a **temperature knob**. As you turn the knob (change $T$), the amount of *available* energy ($\Delta G$) shifts — even if the total heat ($\Delta H$) stays roughly constant. The Gibbs-Helmholtz equation is essentially the **"calibration curve"** of this knob: it tells you exactly how the available work energy changes per degree of temperature change.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Biochemical Engineering Students:**
> In living systems, body temperature is held near **37 °C (310.15 K)**. The Gibbs-Helmholtz equation is critical in designing **bioreactors, fermentation processes, and enzyme reactors** where small temperature shifts can flip a reaction from spontaneous ($\Delta G < 0$) to non-spontaneous ($\Delta G > 0$).

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$
- **Standard Temperature (Biochemistry):** $T = 298.15 \text{ K}$ (25 °C) — the conventional reference for tabulated $\Delta G^{\circ}$ values
- **Physiological Temperature:** $T = 310.15 \text{ K}$ (37 °C) — relevant for in-vivo biochemical processes
- **Standard Pressure:** $P = 1 \text{ bar} = 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$ (IUPAC, since 1982)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The "Why" Behind the Equation

The Gibbs-Helmholtz equation is **not** an independent law of thermodynamics. It is a mathematical consequence of combining three fundamental relations:

1. **Definition of Gibbs Free Energy:**
$$G = H - TS$$

2. **First Law for closed systems at constant pressure:** $dH = dq_{P}$ (heat exchanged at constant pressure equals enthalpy change).

3. **Second Law / Clausius inequality:** $dS \geq dq_{rev}/T$, with equality holding for reversible processes.

By taking the differential of $G$ and substituting these constraints, we obtain the equation. The crucial point is that the Gibbs-Helmholtz equation **does not require knowledge of the third-law entropy** explicitly — it relates $\Delta G$ directly to the more easily measured quantity $\Delta H$.

---

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

- **Step 1 — Start from the fundamental definition:**
$$G = H - TS$$
This identifies $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ for a process.

- **Step 2 — Differentiate $G/T$ with respect to $T$ at constant $P$:**
The choice of differentiating the ratio $G/T$ (rather than $G$ alone) is a clever mathematical move that eliminates the explicit dependence on $\Delta S$, leaving only $\Delta H$ in the final result.

- **Step 3 — Apply the cyclic identity** of partial derivatives to ensure thermodynamic consistency, which guarantees that the equation is exact and path-independent.

- **Step 4 — Integrate** between two temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$ (assuming $\Delta H$ is approximately constant over the range — a fair assumption for most biochemical reactions in a narrow temperature window).

- **Step 5 — Solve for the unknown $\Delta G$** at the desired temperature, given a known reference $\Delta G$ at another temperature.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains all KTU-board-essential formulas for this topic. Memorize these for the ESE.**

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Units | Conditions / Notes |
|---|--------------|------------------------|-------|--------------------|
| 1 | Gibbs-Helmholtz (Differential) | $\left(\dfrac{\partial (\Delta G / T)}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{T^{2}}$ | J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ | Constant pressure; general form |
| 2 | Gibbs-Helmholtz (Inverse-T) | $\left(\dfrac{\partial (\Delta G / T)}{\partial (1/T)}\right)_{P} = \Delta H$ | J·mol⁻¹ | Constant pressure; useful for graphical analysis |
| 3 | Integrated Form (ΔH constant) | $\dfrac{\Delta G_{2}}{T_{2}} - \dfrac{\Delta G_{1}}{T_{1}} = \Delta H \left(\dfrac{1}{T_{2}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{1}}\right)$ | J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ | $\Delta H$ assumed independent of $T$ |
| 4 | Standard Form Integrated | $\dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}_{2}}{T_{2}} - \dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}_{1}}{T_{1}} = \Delta H^{\circ}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{2}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{1}}\right)$ | J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ | For standard-state conditions |
| 5 | Van't Hoff Form (alternative) | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{K_{2}}{K_{1}}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{2}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{1}}\right)$ | Dimensionless ratio | From $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$ substitution |
| 6 | Relation to Entropy | $\left(\dfrac{\partial \Delta G}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = -\Delta S$ | J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ | Direct consequence; useful for sign analysis |
| 7 | Gibbs-Helmholtz for G (pure) | $\left[\dfrac{\partial (G/T)}{\partial T}\right]_{P} = -\dfrac{H}{T^{2}}$ | J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ | Single-substance form (no $\Delta$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on table syntax:** All absolute-value or magnitude indicators (if any) are written using $\vert \cdot \vert$ to avoid breaking markdown table parsing.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Gibbs-Helmholtz equation is extensively used in:

- **Bioreactor Design:** Predicting how the thermodynamic feasibility of fermentation reactions (e.g., ethanol production by *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*) shifts with operating temperature.
- **Pharmaceutical Stability Studies:** Determining the shelf life of protein drugs by analyzing how $\Delta G$ of denaturation changes with temperature.
- **Enzyme Engineering:** Designing thermophilic enzymes (operating at 60–100 °C) by understanding how $\Delta H$ of folding dictates the $\Delta G$ landscape.
- **Metabolic Pathway Analysis:** Estimating the equilibrium constants of biochemical reactions at physiological temperature from standard-tabulated values at 25 °C.
- **Food Processing:** Calculating energy requirements for sterilization/pasteurization processes that inactivate microbial enzymes.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Differential Form of the Gibbs-Helmholtz Equation

**Starting Point:** The definition of Gibbs free energy for a pure substance:
$$G = H - TS$$

**Step 1 — Form the ratio $G/T$:** This is a deliberate mathematical construction. We divide both sides by $T$ to prepare for a temperature derivative that will yield a simple, useful result.

$$\frac{G}{T} = \frac{H}{T} - S$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate both sides with respect to $T$ at constant $P$:**

For the left-hand side, we apply the **quotient rule** of differentiation:
$$\left[\frac{\partial (G/T)}{\partial T}\right]_{P} = \frac{1}{T}\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P} - \frac{G}{T^{2}}$$

For the right-hand side, we differentiate each term:
- Derivative of $H/T$ at constant $P$: $\dfrac{1}{T}\left(\dfrac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_{P} - \dfrac{H}{T^{2}}$
- Derivative of $S$ at constant $P$: $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_{P}$

**Step 3 — Equate LHS and RHS:**

$$\frac{1}{T}\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P} - \frac{G}{T^{2}} = \frac{1}{T}\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_{P} - \frac{H}{T^{2}} - \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_{P}$$

**Step 4 — Use the fundamental relation** $\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = -S$ (this is one of the four Maxwell-derived relations from $dG = VdP - SdT$). Substituting:

$$\frac{1}{T}(-S) - \frac{G}{T^{2}} = \frac{1}{T}\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_{P} - \frac{H}{T^{2}} - \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_{P}$$

**Step 5 — Recognize that** $\left(\dfrac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = C_{P}$ and $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = \dfrac{C_{P}}{T}$ (from $dH = C_{P}\,dT$ and $dS = C_{P}\,dT/T$ at constant $P$). Thus the $C_{P}$ terms cancel exactly:

$$-\frac{S}{T} - \frac{G}{T^{2}} = -\frac{H}{T^{2}}$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange** by substituting back $G = H - TS$, which gives $S = (H-G)/T$:

$$-\frac{(H-G)/T}{T} - \frac{G}{T^{2}} = -\frac{H}{T^{2}}$$

$$-\frac{H}{T^{2}} + \frac{G}{T^{2}} - \frac{G}{T^{2}} = -\frac{H}{T^{2}}$$

This identity confirms the consistency. The **useful rearrangement** is obtained by isolating the derivative:

$$\left[\frac{\partial (G/T)}{\partial T}\right]_{P} = -\frac{H}{T^{2}}$$

**Step 7 — Apply to a chemical process** (subtract the reactant equation from the product equation):

$$\left[\frac{\partial (\Delta G / T)}{\partial T}\right]_{P} = -\frac{\Delta H}{T^{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **final differential form of the Gibbs-Helmholtz equation** — a high-yield KTU result.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Integrated Form

**Starting Point:** Assume $\Delta H$ is approximately **independent of temperature** over the range $T_1$ to $T_2$ (valid for most biochemical reactions in a 50–100 K window).

$$\left[\frac{\partial (\Delta G / T)}{\partial T}\right]_{P} = -\frac{\Delta H}{T^{2}}$$

**Step 1 — Separate variables** by multiplying both sides by $dT$:

$$d\!\left(\frac{\Delta G}{T}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H}{T^{2}}\,dT$$

**Step 2 — Integrate** between the limits $(T_1, \Delta G_1)$ and $(T_2, \Delta G_2)$:

$$\int_{\Delta G_1 / T_1}^{\Delta G_2 / T_2} d\!\left(\frac{\Delta G}{T}\right) = -\Delta H \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integrals:**

The left side gives:
$$\frac{\Delta G_{2}}{T_{2}} - \frac{\Delta G_{1}}{T_{1}}$$

The right side gives (using $\int T^{-2} dT = -1/T$):
$$-\Delta H \left[-\frac{1}{T}\right]_{T_1}^{T_2} = -\Delta H \left(-\frac{1}{T_{2}} + \frac{1}{T_{1}}\right) = \Delta H \left(\frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Final integrated form:**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\Delta G_{2}}{T_{2}} - \frac{\Delta G_{1}}{T_{1}} = \Delta H \left(\frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}}\right)\;}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Van't Hoff Form (KTU High-Yield Connection)

**Starting Point:** The standard relation between Gibbs free energy and the equilibrium constant:
$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$$

**Step 1 — Substitute** into the integrated Gibbs-Helmholtz equation:

$$\frac{-RT_{2} \ln K_{2}}{T_{2}} - \frac{-RT_{1} \ln K_{1}}{T_{1}} = \Delta H^{\circ}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}}\right)$$

$$-R \ln K_{2} + R \ln K_{1} = \Delta H^{\circ}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange:**

$$R \ln\!\left(\frac{K_{1}}{K_{2}}\right) = \Delta H^{\circ}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Multiply** by $-1/R$ to obtain the conventional Van't Hoff form:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{K_{2}}{K_{1}}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This shows that the **Van't Hoff equation is a direct corollary of the Gibbs-Helmholtz equation** — a frequently tested KTU relationship.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical)

**Problem:** For the ATP hydrolysis reaction, $\Delta G^{\circ}_{298} = -30.5$ kJ/mol and $\Delta H^{\circ} = -20.1$ kJ/mol. Calculate $\Delta G^{\circ}$ at body temperature $T = 310$ K. (Assume $\Delta H$ is constant.)

**Solution:**

Given:
- $T_1 = 298$ K, $\Delta G_1 = -30.5$ kJ/mol, $\Delta H = -20.1$ kJ/mol
- $T_2 = 310$ K, $\Delta G_2 = ?$

Using the integrated form:
$$\frac{\Delta G_{2}}{T_{2}} = \frac{\Delta G_{1}}{T_{1}} + \Delta H\left(\frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}}\right)$$

Substitute:
$$\frac{\Delta G_{2}}{310} = \frac{-30.5}{298} + (-20.1)\left(\frac{1}{310} - \frac{1}{298}\right)$$

Evaluate term by term:
$$\frac{-30.5}{298} = -0.10235 \text{ kJ/(mol·K)}$$

$$\frac{1}{310} - \frac{1}{298} = 0.003226 - 0.003356 = -0.0001302 \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$(-20.1) \times (-0.0001302) = +0.002617 \text{ kJ/(mol·K)}$$

Therefore:
$$\frac{\Delta G_{2}}{310} = -0.10235 + 0.002617 = -0.09973 \text{ kJ/(mol·K)}$$

$$\Delta G_{2} = 310 \times (-0.09973) = -30.92 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Physical interpretation:** ATP hydrolysis becomes **slightly more exergonic** at body temperature — meaning the cell has marginally more free energy available to drive coupled biosynthetic reactions. This is physiologically significant and is why biological systems maintain elevated temperatures.
