```mermaid
    B --> D["Count Composition Variables: P times C-1"]
    C --> D
    D --> E["Add 2 External Variables T and P"]
    E --> F["Total Variables: P C-1 + 2"]
    F --> G["Count Equilibrium Constraints: mu of each component equal in all phases"]
    G --> H["Total Constraints: C times P-1"]
    H --> I["Subtract Constraints from Variables"]
    I --> J["F = C - P + 2"]
    J --> K{"F equals Zero?"}
    K -->|"Yes"| L["Invariant System: Triple Point, Eutectic Point"]
    K -->|"F equals One"| M["Univariant System: Phase Boundary Curve"]
    K -->|"F equals Two"| N["Bivariant System: Phase Region Area"]
    K -->|"F greater than 2"| O["Higher Order: 3D Phase Volume"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: One-Component Phase Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOW_TEMP["Low Temperature Region"]
        A1["SOLID PHASE: Ice"]
    end
    subgraph MID_TEMP["Intermediate Temperature Region"]
        B1["LIQUID PHASE: Water"]
    end
    subgraph HIGH_TEMP["High Temperature Region"]
        C1["VAPOR PHASE: Steam"]
    end
    subgraph CRITICAL["Critical Point Region"]
        D1["Supercritical Fluid"]
    end
    AB["Boundary: Melting Curve"] --> A1
    AB --> B1
    BC["Boundary: Vaporization Curve"] --> B1
    BC --> C1
    CD["Boundary: Sublimation Curve"] --> A1
    CD --> C1
    TP["Triple Point T equals 273.16 K"] -.-> AB
    TP -.-> BC
    TP -.-> CD
    CP["Critical Point T equals 647 K"] --> D1
    BC --> CP
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Identifying the Number of Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Start: System Identified"] --> Q1{"Are there chemical reactions?"}
    Q1 -->|"No"| R1["C = number of chemical species"]
    Q1 -->|"Yes"| R2["Count species minus number of independent reactions"]
    R1 --> Q2{"Are there any constraints like stoichiometric ratios?"}
    R2 --> Q2
    Q2 -->|"No"| F1["Final C value"]
    Q2 -->|"Yes"| R3["C = species - constraints - reactions"]
    R3 --> F1
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Two-Component Eutectic System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph PHASE_DIAG["Binary Eutectic Phase Diagram Architecture"]
        TOP["L region: Liquid Solution"]
        MID1["Alpha plus L region: Solid A in Liquid"]
        MID2["L plus Beta region: Liquid in Solid B"]
        BOT["Alpha plus Beta region: Two Solid Phases"]
    end
    TOP -->|"Cooling"| MID1
    MID1 -->|"Eutectic Reaction at TE"| BOT
    TOP -->|"Cooling"| MID2
    MID2 -->|"Eutectic Reaction at TE"| BOT
    E["Eutectic Point at TE, XE"] -.-> MID1
    E -.-> MID2
    E -.-> BOT
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]

**State and explain the Gibbs Phase Rule. Define each term with one suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statement:** The Gibbs Phase Rule is a thermodynamic relationship that defines the degrees of freedom $F$ in a heterogeneous system at equilibrium.

**Mathematical Form:**

$$F = C - P + 2$$

**Definitions with Examples:**

- **$F$ (Degrees of Freedom / Variance):** The minimum number of independent intensive variables (such as temperature, pressure, and composition) that must be specified to define the state of the system completely. **Example:** For a pure liquid water system in equilibrium with its vapor, $F = 1$, meaning only one variable (e.g., temperature) needs to be fixed; the corresponding pressure (vapor pressure) is then automatically determined.

- **$C$ (Number of Components):** The minimum number of independent chemical species required to express the composition of all phases of the system. **Example:** For a solution of ethanol and water, $C = 2$ (ethanol and water are the components; hydrates or azeotropes are not counted as separate components).

- **$P$ (Number of Phases):** A physically distinct, homogeneous, and mechanically separable part of the system, bounded by sharp interfaces. **Example:** In the triple point of water, $P = 3$ (ice, liquid water, and water vapor coexist simultaneously).

- **$+2$:** Represents the two external intensive variables — **Temperature ($T$)** and **Pressure ($P$)** — that can be independently varied.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]

**Differentiate between a phase and a component with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Difference Table:**

| **Aspect** | **Phase** | **Component** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Definition** | A homogeneous, physically distinct, and mechanically separable part of a system. | The minimum number of independent chemical constituents required to express the composition of all phases. |
| **Nature** | Physical concept based on state of matter and structure. | Chemical concept based on species identity. |
| **Example 1** | Ice, liquid water, and water vapor are three distinct phases of $H_2O$. | For the water–ethanol system, the components are $H_2O$ and $C_2H_5OH$ (i.e., $C = 2$). |
| **Example 2** | Rhombic sulfur and monoclinic sulfur are two allotropic solid phases. | For a $NaCl$–$H_2O$ aqueous solution, the components are $NaCl$ and $H_2O$ (i.e., $C = 2$). |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Choice A]

**(a)** Derive the Gibbs Phase Rule from fundamental thermodynamic principles. State clearly the assumptions made. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A system consists of $NH_4Cl$ in equilibrium with its dissociation products $NH_3$ and $HCI$ in the vapor phase. Calculate the degrees of freedom $(F)$ and comment on the result. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Derivation

**Assumptions of the Phase Rule:**

1. The system is at **thermodynamic equilibrium** (mechanical, thermal, and chemical).
2. The phases are in intimate physical contact so that equilibrium is established.
3. The effect of **gravity, electrical, magnetic, and surface tension forces** is neglected.
4. Pressure and temperature are uniform throughout the system.

**Derivation:**

Consider a system with $C$ components distributed among $P$ phases at equilibrium. The state of each phase is described by its temperature, pressure, and composition.

**Step 1 — Total variables to specify the system:**

- Pressure (common to all phases): **1 variable**
- Temperature (common to all phases): **1 variable**
- Composition of each phase: In any phase, the mole fractions of $C$ components must satisfy the constraint $\sum x_i = 1$, so only $(C-1)$ mole fractions are independent. For $P$ phases:

$$\text{Composition variables} = P(C - 1)$$

**Total variables:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= P(C-1) + 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Equilibrium constraints:**

For the system to be in equilibrium, the chemical potential of each component must be equal in every phase. For component $i$:

$$\mu_i^{\alpha} = \mu_i^{\beta} = \mu_i^{\gamma} = \ldots = \mu_i^{P}$$

This gives $(P-1)$ independent equalities for each of the $C$ components.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total constraints} = C(P-1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Net degrees of freedom:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= P(C-1) + 2 - C(P-1) \\
&= PC - P + 2 - CP + C \\
&= -P + 2 + C \\
&= C - P + 2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; F = C - P + 2 \;}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b): $NH_4Cl$ Dissociation Problem

**Given:** The dissociation reaction is:

$$NH_4Cl_{(s)} \rightleftharpoons NH_{3(g)} + HCl_{(g)}$$

**Step 1 — Identify the chemical species:**

The species present in the system are: $NH_4Cl$, $NH_3$, $HCl$. Thus, the number of species $S = 3$.

**Step 2 — Determine the number of components ($C$):**

For a system with chemical reactions, the number of components is:

$$C = S - R$$

where $S$ is the number of species and $R$ is the number of independent reactions.

Here, $S = 3$ and $R = 1$ (one dissociation reaction). Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 3 - 1 = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the number of phases ($P$):**

- Solid $NH_4Cl$: **1 phase**
- Vapor mixture of $NH_3$ and $HCl$: **1 phase**

Therefore, $P = 2$.

**Step 4 — Apply the phase rule:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= C - P + 2 \\
F &= 2 - 2 + 2 \\
F &= 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Comment on the result:**

The system has **2 degrees of freedom** (bivariant). This means that both temperature and pressure (or temperature and composition) can be independently varied without disturbing the number of phases in equilibrium. In the vapor phase, the stoichiometric constraint requires that the mole fractions of $NH_3$ and $HCI$ remain equal (since they are produced in a 1:1 ratio), which reduces the independent composition variables but does not change the formal value of $F = 2$ for the overall system.

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Choice B — Internal Choice Alternative]

**(a)** With the help of a neatly labeled $P$–$T$ phase diagram of the water system, discuss the significance of the triple point, critical point, and the various phase regions. Apply the phase rule at each labeled point and region. (7 Marks)

**(b)** What is the reduced phase rule? Under what conditions is it applicable? Calculate the degrees of freedom for a binary eutectic system ($Pb$–$Ag$) at the eutectic composition. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Water System Phase Diagram

**Description of the Diagram:**

The $P$–$T$ phase diagram of water is divided by three curves meeting at a single point (the **triple point**):

- **Curve OA** (Vaporization curve): Equilibrium between liquid water and vapor.
- **Curve OB** (Sublimation curve): Equilibrium between ice and vapor.
- **Curve OC** (Melting/Fusion curve): Equilibrium between ice and liquid water.

**Significance of Key Points:**

- **Triple Point (O):** $T = 273.16\ \text{K}$, $P = 611.657\ \text{Pa}$. All three phases — ice, liquid water, and water vapor — coexist in equilibrium.

  Applying the phase rule: $C = 1$, $P = 3$:
  
  $$F = 1 - 3 + 2 = 0 \quad \text{(Invariant system)}$$

- **Critical Point (C):** $T_c = 647\ \text{K}$, $P_c = 22.06\ \text{MPa}$. Above this point, the liquid and vapor phases become indistinguishable (supercritical fluid).

  Applying the phase rule: $C = 1$, $P = 2$ (liquid and vapor on the verge of merging):
  
  $$F = 1 - 2 + 2 = 1$$

**Phase Regions and Phase Rule Application:**

- **Region: Solid (Ice) only** — $C = 1$, $P = 1$, $F = 1 - 1 + 2 = 2$ (Bivariant — both $T$ and $P$ can be varied independently).
- **Region: Liquid (Water) only** — $C = 1$, $P = 1$, $F = 1 - 1 + 2 = 2$ (Bivariant).
- **Region: Vapor only** — $C = 1$, $P = 1$, $F = 1 - 1 + 2 = 2$ (Bivariant).
- **Curve: Liquid ↔ Vapor (OA)** — $C = 1$, $P = 2$, $F = 1 - 2 + 2 = 1$ (Univariant — specifying $T$ fixes $P$).
- **Curve: Solid ↔ Vapor (OB)** — $C = 1$, $P = 2$, $F = 1 - 2 + 2 = 1$ (Univariant).
- **Curve: Solid ↔ Liquid (OC)** — $C = 1$, $P = 2$, $F = 1 - 2 + 2 = 1$ (Univariant).
- **Triple Point (O)** — $C = 1$, $P = 3$, $F = 0$ (Invariant).

**Anomalous Behavior of Water:**

The melting curve OC has a **negative slope** ($dP/dT < 0$), unlike most substances. This is because ice is **less dense** than liquid water ($\Delta V_m < 0$ for melting). The Clapeyron equation $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{fus}}{T \cdot \Delta V_m}$ therefore gives a negative value.

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Reduced Phase Rule and Eutectic Application

**Reduced Phase Rule — Definition:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reduced Phase Rule:** When the pressure is held constant (e.g., in an open system at atmospheric pressure), the phase rule simplifies to $F = C - P + 1$, as the pressure variable is removed from the set of independently variable parameters.

**Conditions of Applicability:**

- Applicable to **condensed systems** where vapor pressure effects are negligible.
- Applicable when the system is studied under **isobaric conditions** (constant pressure).
- Useful for **metallurgical systems** (alloys, solders) and **molten salt equilibria** where atmospheric pressure is essentially invariant.

**Lead–Silver ($Pb$–$Ag$) Eutectic System:**

The $Pb$–$Ag$ system forms a **simple eutectic** with no intermediate compounds or solid solutions. The eutectic occurs at:

- $T_E = 303\ ^\circ\text{C} = 576\ \text{K}$
- $x_{Ag} = 2.6\ \text{at\%}$ (eutectic composition)

**Degrees of Freedom at the Eutectic Point:**

- Number of components: $C = 2$ ($Pb$ and $Ag$).
- Number of phases at eutectic: $P = 3$ (solid $Pb$ + solid $Ag$ + liquid melt of eutectic composition).

Applying the **reduced phase rule** (constant pressure):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= C - P + 1 \\
F &= 2 - 3 + 1 \\
F &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The eutectic point is **invariant** under constant pressure. This means that the eutectic temperature and composition are uniquely fixed — neither can be altered without losing one of the three coexisting phases. This is the basis for using eutectic mixtures (e.g., Wood's metal, Rose's metal) as low-melting solders and thermal fuses.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (and How to Avoid Them):**
>
> 1. **Confusing the "+2" with number of components:** The "+2" is **not** a variable; it represents the two external intensive variables (Temperature and Pressure). Many students write the formula incorrectly as $F = C - P + 2$ where "2" is misinterpreted as a third component.
>
> 2. **Counting ions as components:** For a $NaCl$–$H_2O$ system, the components are $NaCl$ and $H_2O$ (i.e., $C = 2$). Students often incorrectly count $Na^+$, $Cl^-$, and $H_2O$ as three components.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to subtract reactions in reactive systems:** For $CaCO_3 \rightleftharpoons CaO + CO_2$, $C = 3 - 1 = 2$, **not** $C = 3$. Always subtract the number of independent reactions.
>
> 4. **Miscounting phases in alloys:** Each distinct solid phase (e.g., $\alpha$-brass vs. $\beta$-brass) counts as a separate phase.
>
> 5. **Wrong application of reduced vs. general phase rule:** If the problem specifies "atmospheric pressure" or "condensed system", use $F = C - P + 1$. Otherwise, use the general $F = C - P + 2$.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Things You Must Remember:**
>
> - **Gibbs Phase Rule (General):** $F = C - P + 2$
> - **Reduced Phase Rule (Condensed):** $F = C - P + 1$
> - **Reactive Phase Rule:** $F = C - P + 2 - R$, where $C = S - R$
> - **"+"** is **always** assigned to $T$ and $P$ (the two external intensive variables).
> - **Phase = physical state** (same chemical composition can have multiple phases, e.g., ice/water/vapor).
> - **Component = minimum independent chemical species** (not ions, not reaction products).
> - **Triple point:** $F = 0$ (invariant) — all three phases coexist at a unique $(T, P)$.
> - **Critical point:** End of the vaporization curve where the meniscus disappears.
> - **Eutectic point:** $F = 0$ at constant pressure — fixed melting composition.
> - **Clapeyron Equation:** $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{trans}}{T \cdot \Delta V_m}$
> - **Clausius–Clapeyron Equation:** $\ln P = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T} + C$
> - **Water is anomalous:** $\rho_{ice} < \rho_{water}$, so the melting curve has **negative slope**.
> - **Biochemical relevance:** Protein crystallization, lyophilization, ATPE, membrane phase transitions.
> - **KTU Board Pattern:** Always **define every variable** and provide an **example** for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vapour-Liquid Equilibrium
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT414 - BIOCHEMICAL THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Vapour-Liquid Equilibrium

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Vapour-Liquid Equilibrium (VLE)** is the thermodynamic state in which a liquid mixture and its coexisting vapour phase are in **mechanical, thermal, and chemical equilibrium**, meaning there is no net interphase transfer of mass or energy across the phase boundary.

For a multi-component system at VLE, three simultaneous equality conditions must be satisfied:

$$
T^{L} = T^{V}, \quad P^{L} = P^{V}, \quad \mu_{i}^{L} = \mu_{i}^{V} \quad \forall i \in \{1, 2, \dots, C\}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The equality of chemical potentials ($\mu_i^L = \mu_i^V$) for *every* component $i$ is the **fundamental criterion** for VLE. From this single statement, *all* engineering VLE equations (Raoult's law, modified Raoult's law, Henry's law, K-values) are derived.

## 1.2 The Gibbs Phase Rule

The degrees of freedom ($F$) in a VLE system are governed by the **Gibbs Phase Rule**:

$$
F = C - P + 2
$$

where:
- $C$ = number of components
- $P$ = number of coexisting phases
- The constant **2** represents the two independent intensive variables ($T$ and $P$) for non-reactive systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Special VLE Case:** For a binary system ($C = 2$) with two phases ($P = 2$), we get $F = 2$. Specifying **one** variable (e.g., $T$) fixes the system completely, giving a unique pair $(P, x_i, y_i)$.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Boiling Tea Kettle

Imagine a closed pressure cooker with water and a tiny amount of tea-vapour inside. At first, water evaporates and accumulates vapour above the liquid. After some time, evaporation and condensation rates **balance exactly** — the system has reached VLE.

- The macroscopic observables (temperature, pressure, composition) become **constant in time**.
- Microscopically, molecules still cross the interface both ways, but their **net flux is zero**.
- If we heat further, the equilibrium shifts (Le Chatelier's principle) — more liquid vaporises until a new VLE state is reached.

In a **biochemical** context, this is identical to what happens in bioreactors during sterilisation, fermentation gas exchange, or solvent recovery in downstream processing — only the species are biomolecules, water, ethanol, $\text{CO}_2$, or $\text{O}_2$.

## 1.4 Why VLE is Indispensable in Biochemical Engineering

| Engineering Operation | Relevance of VLE |
|---|---|
| Distillation of bioethanol | Separation by boiling-point differences |
| Fermenter head-space gas analysis | $\text{O}_2$ / $\text{CO}_2$ partial pressures govern mass transfer |
| Solvent extraction / recovery | Selectivity depends on activity coefficients |
| Sterilisation in autoclaves | Saturated steam properties are VLE data |
| Freeze-drying (lyophilisation) | Sublimation–vapour equilibrium of water–ice |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard State Reminder:** In KTU 2024, the *pure-component liquid at the system T and P* is the standard state for activity coefficients. Activities of pure liquids equal unity ($a_i^{\text{pure}} = 1$). Keep this in mind — it is asked repeatedly in past papers.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Building Up VLE — Step-by-Step Logic

### Step 1 — Starting from Chemical-Potential Equality

The defining criterion $\mu_i^L(T, P, x_i) = \mu_i^V(T, P, y_i)$ is recast, for component $i$, into the **fugacity equality**:

$$
\hat{f}_i^L = \hat{f}_i^V
$$

This single equation is the *mother equation* of all VLE relations.

### Step 2 — Expressing Fugacities via Convenient Reference States

Using the definition of fugacity coefficient $\hat{\phi}_i$ and activity coefficient $\gamma_i$:

$$
\hat{f}_i^V = y_i \, \hat{\phi}_i \, P
$$

$$
\hat{f}_i^L = x_i \, \gamma_i \, f_i^{L,\text{pure}}
$$

Setting these equal gives the **General VLE Equation (the master equation)**:

$$
y_i \, \hat{\phi}_i \, P = x_i \, \gamma_i \, f_i^{L,\text{pure}}
$$

### Step 3 — Why We "Why" Each Side

- **Why $y_i \hat{\phi}_i P$?** Because in the vapour phase, the most natural variables are partial pressure $y_i P$ and a correction factor $\hat{\phi}_i$ that accounts for vapour-phase non-ideality.
- **Why $x_i \gamma_i f_i^{L,\text{pure}}$?** Because in the liquid phase, we compare to the **pure-component liquid** at the same $T$ and $P$; $\gamma_i = 1$ means "ideal-liquid behaviour".

### Step 4 — Three Limiting (Engineering) Cases

By applying the general equation under different assumptions, KTU 2024 derives three famous engineering laws:

**Case A — Ideal Vapour + Ideal Liquid → Raoult's Law**
(Valid for chemically similar species like benzene–toluene at moderate pressures)

$$
y_i \, P = x_i \, P_i^{\text{sat}}
$$

**Case B — Ideal Vapour + Non-Ideal Liquid → Modified Raoult's Law**
(Valid for most biochemical systems: water–ethanol, water–acetic acid)

$$
y_i \, P = x_i \, \gamma_i \, P_i^{\text{sat}}
$$

**Case C — Ideal Vapour + Highly Dilute Solute → Henry's Law**
(Valid for sparingly soluble gases like $\text{O}_2$ in fermentation broth)

$$
y_i \, P = x_i \, \mathcal{H}_i
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Trap:** When both phases are non-ideal, you **must** include both $\hat{\phi}_i$ (vapour) and $\gamma_i$ (liquid). Do *not* blindly apply Raoult's law for bio-molecules.

## 2.2 K-Value (Equilibrium Ratio) — The Distillation Workhorse

The **K-value** is defined as the ratio of vapour to liquid mole fractions:

$$
K_i \equiv \frac{y_i}{x_i}
$$

From modified Raoult's law (low-pressure case, $\hat{\phi}_i \approx 1$, $f_i^{L,\text{pure}} \approx P_i^{\text{sat}}$):

$$
K_i = \frac{\gamma_i \, P_i^{\text{sat}}(T)}{P}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If $K_i = 1$, component $i$ distributes equally; if $K_i > 1$, it prefers the vapour; if $K_i < 1$, it prefers the liquid.

## 2.3 Bubble-Point and Dew-Point Equations (KTU 2024 Favourites)

**Bubble-Point Equation** (find $T$ or $P$ given $\{x_i\}$):

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{C} y_i = \sum_{i=1}^{C} K_i \, x_i = 1
$$

**Dew-Point Equation** (find $T$ or $P$ given $\{y_i\}$):

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{C} x_i = \sum_{i=1}^{C} \frac{y_i}{K_i} = 1
$$

## 2.4 Relative Volatility ($\alpha$) — Distillation Design Parameter

For binary systems:

$$
\alpha_{12} = \frac{K_1}{K_2} = \frac{y_1 / x_1}{y_2 / x_2}
$$

A larger $\alpha_{12}$ means easier separation. When $\alpha_{12} = 1$, separation by simple distillation becomes impossible (an **azeotrope** has formed).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master this table. Every KTU 2024 VLE numerical answer uses at least one of these identities.

| # | Equation / Identity | Engineering Meaning | Validity Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\hat{f}_i^L = \hat{f}_i^V$ | Master VLE criterion | Always |
| 2 | $F = C - P + 2$ | Gibbs Phase Rule | Non-reactive systems |
| 3 | $y_i P = x_i P_i^{\text{sat}}$ | Raoult's Law | Ideal liquid + ideal vapour |
| 4 | $y_i P = x_i \gamma_i P_i^{\text{sat}}$ | Modified Raoult's Law | Non-ideal liquid, low $P$ |
| 5 | $y_i P = x_i \mathcal{H}_i$ | Henry's Law | Dilute solute (bio-gases) |
| 6 | $K_i = \gamma_i P_i^{\text{sat}} / P$ | K-value expression | Low-pressure, ideal vapour |
| 7 | $\sum_i K_i x_i = 1$ | Bubble-point equation | At dew-point incipient |
| 8 | $\sum_i y_i / K_i = 1$ | Dew-point equation | At bubble-point incipient |
| 9 | $\alpha_{ij} = K_i / K_j$ | Relative volatility | Binary or pseudo-binary |
| 10 | $\ln \gamma_i = \dfrac{A}{R T}\left(x_j^2 - x_k^2\right)$ | Margules 2-suffix | Binary symmetric |
| 11 | $\displaystyle \sum_i x_i \, d\ln \gamma_i = \dfrac{V^L}{RT}\, dP - \dfrac{H^E}{RT^2}\, dT$ | Gibbs–Duhem (liquid) | Consistency test basis |
| 12 | $\ln P = A - \dfrac{B}{T + C}$ | Antoine Equation (vapour pressure) | Pure components |

## 2.6 Activity Coefficient Models (Common in Biochemical VLE)

Because biological mixtures are *almost never ideal*, $\gamma_i$ must be predicted. The standard KTU 2024 models are:

**Margules (2-suffix, binary):**

$$
\ln \gamma_1 = A \, x_2^2, \quad \ln \gamma_2 = A \, x_1^2
$$

**Van Laar:**

$$
\ln \gamma_1 = \dfrac{A}{\left(1 + \dfrac{A \, x_1}{B \, x_2}\right)^2}, \quad \ln \gamma_2 = \dfrac{B}{\left(1 + \dfrac{B \, x_2}{A \, x_1}\right)^2}
$$

**Wilson Equation (most popular for bio-mixtures like water–ethanol):**

$$
\ln \gamma_1 = -\ln\!\left(x_1 + \Lambda_{12}\, x_2\right) + x_2 \!\left[\dfrac{\Lambda_{12}}{x_1 + \Lambda_{12} x_2} - \dfrac{\Lambda_{21}}{x_2 + \Lambda_{21} x_1}\right]
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Master VLE Equation from Chemical-Potential Equality

We start with the strict equilibrium condition and arrive at the working engineering form used throughout the syllabus.

### Starting Point

For component $i$:

$$
\mu_i^{L}(T, P, \{x_j\}) = \mu_i^{V}(T, P, \{y_j\})
$$

### Step A — Introduce Fugacity Definition

By definition, $\mu_i = \mu_i^{\circ}(T) + RT \ln \hat{f}_i / f_i^{\circ}$, so equality of $\mu_i$ reduces to equality of $\hat{f}_i$:

$$
\hat{f}_i^L(T, P, \{x_j\}) = \hat{f}_i^V(T, P, \{y_j\})
$$

### Step B — Express $\hat{f}_i^V$ Using Fugacity Coefficient

The vapour-phase fugacity is written as partial pressure times the fugacity coefficient:

$$
\hat{f}_i^V = y_i \, \hat{\phi}_i \, P
$$

### Step C — Express $\hat{f}_i^L$ via the Lewis–Randall Standard State

For the liquid phase, the standard state is the pure-liquid $i$ at system $T, P$:

$$
\hat{f}_i^L = x_i \, \gamma_i \, f_i^{L,\text{pure}}(T, P)
$$

### Step D — Equate

$$
y_i \, \hat{\phi}_i \, P = x_i \, \gamma_i \, f_i^{L,\text{pure}}(T, P)
$$

This is the **master VLE equation** (KTU 2024 derivation complete). $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Raoult's Law from the Master Equation (Low-Pressure Ideal Case)

For a **low-pressure system with both phases ideal**:
- Vapour: $\hat{\phi}_i = 1$
- Liquid: $\gamma_i = 1$ and $f_i^{L,\text{pure}} = P_i^{\text{sat}}(T)$ (Lewis–Randall rule + Poynting correction $\approx 1$ at low $P$).

Substituting into the master equation:

$$
y_i \cdot 1 \cdot P = x_i \cdot 1 \cdot P_i^{\text{sat}}(T)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,y_i = \dfrac{x_i \, P_i^{\text{sat}}(T)}{P}\,}
$$

This is **Raoult's Law**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Modified Raoult's Law (Non-Ideal Liquid)

Same low-pressure assumption ($\hat{\phi}_i = 1$, $f_i^{L,\text{pure}} \approx P_i^{\text{sat}}$), but now $\gamma_i \neq 1$:

$$
y_i \, P = x_i \, \gamma_i \, P_i^{\text{sat}}(T)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,y_i = \dfrac{x_i \, \gamma_i \, P_i^{\text{sat}}(T)}{P}\,}
$$

**Modified Raoult's Law** is the workhorse for **water–ethanol**, **water–acetic acid**, and most bio-separations. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Bubble-Point Equation

For a liquid mixture of known composition $\{x_i\}$ at temperature $T$, the vapour it produces is at pressure $P$ with composition $\{y_i\}$. From modified Raoult's law:

$$
y_i = \dfrac{x_i \, \gamma_i \, P_i^{\text{sat}}(T)}{P}
$$

The mole fractions of the vapour must sum to unity:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{C} y_i = \sum_{i=1}^{C} \dfrac{x_i \, \gamma_i \, P_i^{\text{sat}}(T)}{P} = 1
$$

Solving for $P$:

$$
\boxed{\,P_{\text{bubble}} = \sum_{i=1}^{C} x_i \, \gamma_i \, P_i^{\text{sat}}(T)\,}
$$

If the liquid is ideal ($\gamma_i = 1$):

$$
P_{\text{bubble}} = \sum_{i=1}^{C} x_i \, P_i^{\text{sat}}(T)
$$

This is the **isothermal bubble-pressure equation**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.5 Derivation of the Dew-Point Equation (Analytical Matrix)

Given a vapour of composition $\{y_i\}$, the incipient liquid that condenses has composition $\{x_i\}$. From modified Raoult's law:

$$
x_i = \dfrac{y_i \, P}{\gamma_i \, P_i^{\text{sat}}(T)}
$$

Imposing $\sum x_i = 1$:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{C} \dfrac{y_i \, P}{\gamma_i \, P_i^{\text{sat}}(T)} = 1
$$

Solving for $P$:

$$
\boxed{\,P_{\text{dew}} = \left[\sum_{i=1}^{C} \dfrac{y_i}{\gamma_i \, P_i^{\text{sat}}(T)}\right]^{-1}\,}
$$

For the ideal case ($\gamma_i = 1$):

$$
P_{\text{dew}} = \left[\sum_{i=1}^{C} \dfrac{y_i}{P_i^{\text{sat}}(T)}\right]^{-1}
$$

$\blacksquare$

---

## 3.6 Derivation of Relative Volatility for Binary Ideal Systems

For a binary ideal system, $K_i = P_i^{\text{sat}} / P$. Hence:

$$
\alpha_{12} = \dfrac{K_1}{K_2} = \dfrac{P_1^{\text{sat}}}{P_2^{\text{sat}}}
$$

Combining with Raoult's law:

$$
\dfrac{y_1}{1 - y_1} = \alpha_{12} \, \dfrac{x_1}{1 - x_1}
$$

Solving for $y_1$:

$$
\boxed{\,y_1 = \dfrac{\alpha_{12} \, x_1}{1 + (\alpha_{12} - 1) \, x_1}\,}
$$

This is the **equilibrium curve** (x-y diagram) for an ideal binary. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical — Bubble Point of a Bioethanol–Water Mixture

**Problem (KTU 2024 numerical style):** A liquid mixture contains $x_{\text{ethanol}} = 0.20$ and $x_{\text{water}} = 0.80$. At $T = 351.4 \text{ K}$, $P_{\text{ethanol}}^{\text{sat}} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$ and $P_{\text{water}}^{\text{sat}} = 47.18 \text{ kPa}$. The activity coefficients at this composition are $\gamma_{\text{EtOH}} = 1.95$ and $\gamma_{\text{water}} = 1.05$. Calculate the bubble pressure.

### Solution

Using the bubble-pressure formula derived above:

$$
P_{\text{bubble}} = \sum_i x_i \, \gamma_i \, P_i^{\text{sat}}
$$

For ethanol:

$$
P_{\text{EtOH, contrib}} = 0.20 \times 1.95 \times 101.325 = 39.52 \text{ kPa}
$$

For water:

$$
P_{\text{water, contrib}} = 0.80 \times 1.05 \times 47.18 = 39.63 \text{ kPa}
$$

Total:

$$
P_{\text{bubble}} = 39.52 + 39.63 = \boxed{\,79.15 \text{ kPa}\,}
$$

This result lies **below** pure water's vapour pressure at 351.4 K, indicating a strong negative deviation from Raoult's law — characteristic of the ethanol–water azeotrope. $\blacksquare$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of VLE Calculations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System Specification]:::box --> B{Identify Components and Phases}
    B --> C{Calculate C via Phase Rule}
    C --> D{Vapour Pressure of Each Component}
    D --> E[Antoine Equation]
    E --> F{Select Thermodynamic Model}
    F --> F1[Raoult's Law - Ideal]
    F --> F2[Modified Raoult's Law - Non-ideal]
    F --> F3[Henry's Law - Dilute Solute]
    F1 --> G[Compute K-values]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H{Choose Calculation Type}
    H --> H1[Bubble-Point Pressure or Temperature]
    H --> H2[Dew-Point Pressure or Temperature]
    H --> H3[Flash Vaporisation]
    G --> I[Apply Material Balance and Phase Sum]
    I --> J[Iterate to Convergence]
    J --> K[Final Equilibrium State: T, P, xi, yi]
    
    classDef box fill:#e0f0ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000;
```

**Reading the flow:**
- Begin with the **input specification** (T, P, or composition).
- The **Antoine equation** gives pure-component $P_i^{\text{sat}}(T)$.
- Choose the **model** based on ideal / non-ideal / dilute character of the mixture.
- Compute **K-values**, then close the **summation equations** $\sum K_i x_i = 1$ or $\sum y_i / K_i = 1$.
- Iterate if a specified variable (typically $T$) is unknown.

## 4.2 Schematic — P-x-y and T-x-y Phase Envelopes (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Bin["Binary VLE Phase Behaviour"]
    direction TB
    P1[Pure Component A P_A^sat T]
    P2[Pure Component B P_B^sat T]
    P1 --> Pxy["P-x-y Diagram: Liquid Line and Dew Line"]
    P2 --> Pxy
    Pxy --> C1[Above Dew Line: Superheated Vapour]
    Pxy --> C2[Between Lines: Two-Phase Region]
    Pxy --> C3[Below Liquid Line: Subcooled Liquid]
    end

    subgraph Tin["T-x-y Diagram"]
    direction TB
    T1[Bubble Point Curve]
    T2[Dew Point Curve]
    T1 --> Txy[T-x-y Diagram]
    T2 --> Txy
    Txy --> D1[Below Bubble Line: Liquid Only]
    Txy --> D2[Between Curves: Two Phase L plus V]
    Txy --> D3[Above Dew Line: Vapour Only]
    end

    Bin --> E[Critical Point: P-x-y and T-x-y Merge]
    Tin --> E
    E --> F[Use Antoine plus Activity Models for Prediction]
```

**Key takeaways from the schematic:**
- The region **enclosed** between the bubble-point and dew-point curves is the **two-phase (VLE) region**.
- Outside this region, only a single phase exists.
- The critical point marks where the two curves meet (no two-phase region beyond).

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Algorithm for Iterative Bubble-Point $T$ Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Given Liquid Composition xi and Pressure P] --> B[Initialise T using Weighted Average of Saturation Temps]
    B --> C[Compute P_i^sat T using Antoine Equation]
    C --> D[Look up or Compute gamma_i from Activity Model]
    D --> E[Evaluate Sum K_i times x_i]
    E --> F{Sum equals 1 within Tolerance?}
    F -- No --> G[Adjust T upward if sum greater than 1, Downward otherwise]
    G --> C
    F -- Yes --> H[Converged: Output T_bubble and y_i]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why this works:** From $K_i = \gamma_i P_i^{\text{sat}}(T)/P$, the function $f(T) = \sum K_i x_i - 1$ is *monotonic*. So the iteration always converges — Newton–Raphson or simple bisection both succeed.

---
