# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper]
**Define Vapour-Liquid Equilibrium (VLE). State the conditions required for a system to be in VLE.**

**Model Answer:**

Vapour-Liquid Equilibrium is the state of a system in which a liquid phase and its vapour coexist in thermodynamic equilibrium with no net transfer of mass or energy across the phase interface.

The necessary conditions are:

1. **Mechanical equilibrium:** $P^L = P^V$
2. **Thermal equilibrium:** $T^L = T^V$
3. **Chemical equilibrium:** $\mu_i^L = \mu_i^V$ (or equivalently $\hat{f}_i^L = \hat{f}_i^V$) for every component $i$.

These three conditions ensure that the Gibbs free energy of the combined system is at a minimum, and no spontaneous phase change occurs. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supplementary]
**State and explain Raoult's law. Mention its limitations.**

**Model Answer:**

Raoult's law states that the partial pressure of a component in the vapour phase is equal to the product of its liquid mole fraction and its pure-component vapour pressure at the system temperature:

$$
p_i = y_i \, P = x_i \, P_i^{\text{sat}}(T)
$$

**Limitations:**
1. Valid only when the liquid mixture is **ideal** ($\gamma_i = 1$).
2. Valid only when the vapour phase behaves as an **ideal gas** ($\hat{\phi}_i = 1$).
3. Requires chemically similar species (e.g., benzene–toluene). Fails for polar systems like water–ethanol.
4. Fails near the critical point or at high pressures.
5. Cannot predict azeotrope formation (where modified Raoult's law is required). **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the **general VLE equation** starting from the equality of fugacities of component $i$ in the liquid and vapour phases. Discuss the limiting cases leading to **(i)** Raoult's law, **(ii)** Henry's law. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A liquid mixture of acetone (1) and methanol (2) at $T = 327.4 \text{ K}$ has $x_1 = 0.40$. The vapour pressures are $P_1^{\text{sat}} = 91.3 \text{ kPa}$ and $P_2^{\text{sat}} = 67.3 \text{ kPa}$. Activity coefficients from the Margules model are $\ln \gamma_1 = 0.55 \, x_2^2$ and $\ln \gamma_2 = 0.55 \, x_1^2$. Compute the bubble pressure and the equilibrium vapour composition. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a)

**Derivation of General VLE Equation:**

Starting from the equality of chemical potentials:

$$
\mu_i^L = \mu_i^V
$$

Using the definition $\mu_i - \mu_i^{\circ} = RT \ln (\hat{f}_i / f_i^{\circ})$, this is equivalent to:

$$
\hat{f}_i^L(T, P, \{x\}) = \hat{f}_i^V(T, P, \{y\})
$$

Express $\hat{f}_i^V$ in terms of fugacity coefficient:

$$
\hat{f}_i^V = y_i \, \hat{\phi}_i \, P
$$

Express $\hat{f}_i^L$ using the pure-liquid standard state:

$$
\hat{f}_i^L = x_i \, \gamma_i \, f_i^{L,\text{pure}}(T, P)
$$

Equating both:

$$
\boxed{\,y_i \, \hat{\phi}_i \, P = x_i \, \gamma_i \, f_i^{L,\text{pure}}(T, P)\,}
$$

This is the general VLE equation. **(4 marks)**

**Limiting Case (i) — Raoult's Law:**

For ideal vapour ($\hat{\phi}_i = 1$) and ideal liquid ($\gamma_i = 1$, $f_i^{L,\text{pure}} \approx P_i^{\text{sat}}$):

$$
y_i \, P = x_i \, P_i^{\text{sat}} \quad \text{(Raoult's Law)}
$$

**Limiting Case (ii) — Henry's Law:**

For highly dilute solute ($x_i \to 0$, $\gamma_i \to \infty$ such that $\gamma_i \, f_i^{L,\text{pure}} = \mathcal{H}_i$ is finite):

$$
y_i \, P = x_i \, \mathcal{H}_i \quad \text{(Henry's Law)}
$$

**(3 marks for limiting cases)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b)

**Step 1 — Compute activity coefficients:**

With $x_1 = 0.40$, $x_2 = 0.60$:

$$
\ln \gamma_1 = 0.55 \times (0.60)^2 = 0.55 \times 0.36 = 0.198
$$

$$
\gamma_1 = e^{0.198} = 1.219
$$

$$
\ln \gamma_2 = 0.55 \times (0.40)^2 = 0.55 \times 0.16 = 0.088
$$

$$
\gamma_2 = e^{0.088} = 1.092
$$

**Step 2 — Compute partial pressures:**

$$
p_1 = x_1 \, \gamma_1 \, P_1^{\text{sat}} = 0.40 \times 1.219 \times 91.3 = 44.52 \text{ kPa}
$$

$$
p_2 = x_2 \, \gamma_2 \, P_2^{\text{sat}} = 0.60 \times 1.092 \times 67.3 = 44.10 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Bubble pressure and vapour composition:**

$$
P_{\text{bubble}} = p_1 + p_2 = 44.52 + 44.10 = \boxed{88.62 \text{ kPa}}
$$

$$
y_1 = \dfrac{p_1}{P_{\text{bubble}}} = \dfrac{44.52}{88.62} = \boxed{0.5024}
$$

$$
y_2 = 1 - y_1 = 0.4976
$$

The vapour is richer in acetone than the liquid (consistent with acetone being more volatile). **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]

**(a)** State and derive the **bubble-point** and **dew-point equations** for a multicomponent system obeying modified Raoult's law. Show that for ideal systems, the bubble-point pressure is the mole-fraction-weighted sum of pure-component vapour pressures. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A binary ideal mixture of benzene (1) and toluene (2) at $T = 365 \text{ K}$ has $x_1 = 0.30$. Vapour pressures are $P_1^{\text{sat}} = 132.8 \text{ kPa}$ and $P_2^{\text{sat}} = 54.0 \text{ kPa}$. Calculate:
   (i) The bubble pressure,
   (ii) The relative volatility $\alpha_{12}$,
   (iii) The vapour composition $y_1$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a)

**Bubble-Point Equation:**

Modified Raoult's law gives:

$$
y_i = \dfrac{x_i \, \gamma_i \, P_i^{\text{sat}}(T)}{P}
$$

Imposing the constraint $\sum_{i=1}^C y_i = 1$:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{C} \dfrac{x_i \, \gamma_i \, P_i^{\text{sat}}(T)}{P} = 1
$$

Solving for $P$:

$$
\boxed{\,P_{\text{bubble}} = \sum_{i=1}^{C} x_i \, \gamma_i \, P_i^{\text{sat}}(T)\,}
$$

For **ideal** systems ($\gamma_i = 1$):

$$
P_{\text{bubble}} = \sum_{i=1}^{C} x_i \, P_i^{\text{sat}}(T)
$$

which is the mole-fraction-weighted sum of pure-component vapour pressures. **(3.5 marks)**

**Dew-Point Equation:**

From modified Raoult's law:

$$
x_i = \dfrac{y_i \, P}{\gamma_i \, P_i^{\text{sat}}(T)}
$$

Imposing $\sum x_i = 1$:

$$
\boxed{\,P_{\text{dew}} = \left[\sum_{i=1}^{C} \dfrac{y_i}{\gamma_i \, P_i^{\text{sat}}(T)}\right]^{-1}\,}
$$

For ideal systems:

$$
P_{\text{dew}} = \left[\sum_{i=1}^{C} \dfrac{y_i}{P_i^{\text{sat}}(T)}\right]^{-1}
$$

**(3.5 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b)

**(i) Bubble Pressure (Ideal Binary):**

$$
P_{\text{bubble}} = x_1 \, P_1^{\text{sat}} + x_2 \, P_2^{\text{sat}}
$$

$$
P_{\text{bubble}} = 0.30 \times 132.8 + 0.70 \times 54.0 = 39.84 + 37.80 = \boxed{77.64 \text{ kPa}}
$$

**(ii) Relative Volatility:**

$$
\alpha_{12} = \dfrac{P_1^{\text{sat}}}{P_2^{\text{sat}}} = \dfrac{132.8}{54.0} = \boxed{2.459}
$$

**(iii) Vapour Composition $y_1$:**

Using the equilibrium relation:

$$
y_1 = \dfrac{\alpha_{12} \, x_1}{1 + (\alpha_{12} - 1) \, x_1}
$$

$$
y_1 = \dfrac{2.459 \times 0.30}{1 + (2.459 - 1) \times 0.30} = \dfrac{0.738}{1 + 0.438} = \dfrac{0.738}{1.438} = \boxed{0.5132}
$$

Verification using direct Raoult's law:

$$
y_1 = \dfrac{x_1 \, P_1^{\text{sat}}}{P_{\text{bubble}}} = \dfrac{0.30 \times 132.8}{77.64} = \dfrac{39.84}{77.64} = 0.5131 \; \checkmark
$$

**(7 marks total)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks in VLE problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the summation constraint** ($\sum y_i = 1$ or $\sum x_i = 1$). In bubble/dew point derivations, marks are awarded for stating and using this constraint explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing $K_i$ for bubble vs. dew points.** Bubble-point: $\sum K_i x_i = 1$. Dew-point: $\sum y_i/K_i = 1$. The denominators are reversed — examiners notice this immediately.
> 3. **Applying Raoult's law blindly** to bio-mixtures (water–ethanol, chloroform–methanol). Always check if the system is ideal; otherwise use modified Raoult's law.
> 4. **Units of vapour pressure.** KTU 2024 expects consistent SI units (kPa or bar). Mixing atm and kPa costs a mark.
> 5. **Skipping the fugacity-to-activity substitution step** in derivations. The full chain $\mu_i^L = \mu_i^V \Rightarrow \hat{f}_i^L = \hat{f}_i^V \Rightarrow y_i \hat{\phi}_i P = x_i \gamma_i f_i^{L,\text{pure}}$ must be visible in the answer script.
> 6. **Forgetting to compute $\gamma_i$** in non-ideal numerical problems. Always state the activity coefficient model used.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for VLE:**
>
> - **VLE criterion:** $\mu_i^L = \mu_i^V$ or $\hat{f}_i^L = \hat{f}_i^V$ for all $i$.
> - **Phase Rule:** $F = C - P + 2$ — binary VLE at fixed $T$ leaves **zero** degrees of freedom.
> - **Raoult's Law:** $y_i P = x_i P_i^{\text{sat}}$ — ideal mixtures only.
> - **Modified Raoult's Law:** $y_i P = x_i \gamma_i P_i^{\text{sat}}$ — the **engineering standard** for bio-mixtures.
> - **Henry's Law:** $y_i P = x_i \mathcal{H}_i$ — sparingly soluble gases (O₂, CO₂ in fermentation broth).
> - **K-value:** $K_i = \gamma_i P_i^{\text{sat}} / P$ (low $P$).
> - **Bubble point:** $\sum K_i x_i = 1$ → $P = \sum x_i \gamma_i P_i^{\text{sat}}$.
> - **Dew point:** $\sum y_i / K_i = 1$ → $P = \left[\sum y_i / (\gamma_i P_i^{\text{sat}})\right]^{-1}$.
> - **Relative volatility** $\alpha_{12} = K_1/K_2$. $\alpha = 1$ ⇒ azeotrope (separation impossible by simple distillation).
> - **Equilibrium curve (ideal binary):** $y_1 = \alpha_{12} x_1 / [1 + (\alpha_{12} - 1)x_1]$.
> - **Activity coefficient models:** Margules, van Laar, Wilson — choose based on mixture polarity and temperature range.
> - **Vapour pressure:** Antoine equation $\ln P^{\text{sat}} = A - B/(T + C)$ — must be applied at the system $T$.
> - **Biochemical relevance:** Water–ethanol (distillation), water–acetic acid (vinegar production), $\text{O}_2$/$\text{CO}_2$ in fermentation (Henry's law).
> - **Azeotrope trap:** For ethanol–water at $x_{\text{EtOH}} \approx 0.894$ (mole basis), VLE gives $y_{\text{EtOH}} = x_{\text{EtOH}}$ → $\alpha = 1$. Pure water–ethanol distillation cannot exceed azeotropic composition in a single column.
> - **Gibbs–Duhem consistency:** Experimental VLE data should satisfy $\sum x_i d\ln \gamma_i = 0$ at constant $T, P$ (the area test).
> - **K-value dependencies:** $K_i$ increases with $T$ (because $P_i^{\text{sat}}$ rises sharply) and decreases with $P$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Liquid-Liquid Equilibrium
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT414 - BIOCHEMICAL THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Liquid-Liquid Equilibrium

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Liquid–Liquid Equilibrium (LLE)** is a state of thermodynamic coexistence of two (or more) liquid phases in a multi-component system, wherein the temperature (**T**), pressure (**P**), and the chemical potential (**μᵢ**) of every component *i* are identical in all coexisting liquid phases. For a system with π liquid phases and *C* components, the rigorous mathematical statement of LLE is:

$$
\mu_i^{\alpha} = \mu_i^{\beta} = \mu_i^{\gamma} = \dots = \mu_i^{\pi} \quad (i = 1, 2, \dots, C)
$$

At constant **T** and **P**, this condition reduces to the **iso-fugacity criterion**:

$$
\hat{f}_i^{\alpha}(T,P,x^{\alpha}) = \hat{f}_i^{\beta}(T,P,x^{\beta}) \quad \forall \, i
$$

When expressed in terms of activity coefficients (γᵢ), the practical working equation for each component distributed between two liquid phases α and β becomes:

$$
x_i^{\alpha} \gamma_i^{\alpha} = x_i^{\beta} \gamma_i^{\beta}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A biochemical engineer must use LLE to design and analyze solvent-extraction processes used in downstream processing of antibiotics, amino acids, vitamins, and fermentation products (e.g., penicillin recovery using amyl/butyl acetate, citric acid recovery, lactic acid purification).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a glass of **Italian salad dressing** (oil + vinegar) that you shake vigorously. The moment you stop shaking, tiny droplets of vinegar disperse into the oil, and tiny droplets of oil disperse into the vinegar. After a few seconds, the system settles into **two distinct, sharply separated liquid layers** — an upper oil-rich layer and a lower vinegar-rich layer. This is a *partially miscible* liquid system: each liquid can dissolve a small amount of the other, but they cannot form a single homogeneous phase.

In that settled state, the vinegar molecules in the oil layer and the vinegar molecules in the vinegar layer have the **same "escaping tendency"** (chemical potential) — neither layer is "trying" to pull vinegar out of the other. That balance is exactly what LLE describes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why LLE matters in Biochemical Engineering:**
> - **Antibiotic extraction** (penicillin G, erythromycin, tetracycline)
> - **Aqueous two-phase extraction (ATPS)** for proteins and enzymes using PEG/dextran or PEG/salt systems
> - **Vitamin recovery** (e.g., vitamin E from vegetable oils)
> - **Biodiesel purification** (glycerol–biodiesel–methanol ternary LLE)
> - **Effluent treatment** (phenol extraction from wastewater using toluene)

> [!TIP]
> **Standard physical metric used in LLE:**
> - **Temperature:** K (Kelvin) — the equilibrium is strongly temperature-dependent
> - **Pressure:** ~**101.325 kPa** (1 atm) — most bioprocess LLE data is reported at 1 atm
> - **Composition:** mole fraction (*xᵢ*) — dimensionless, ∑xᵢ = 1
> - **Distribution coefficient (Kᵢ):** dimensionless ratio of concentrations
> - **Selectivity (β):** dimensionless ratio of distribution coefficients

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conditions for LLE — The "Why" and "How"

For a homogeneous single-phase solution to **split** into two liquid phases, the system must satisfy the **thermodynamic stability criterion** at the limit of incipient phase separation. The Gibbs free energy of mixing, **ΔG_mix**, must have a region where it is **non-convex** (i.e., it exhibits a downward-curving inflection). The mathematical condition is:

- **Stable single phase:** $\left(\frac{\partial^2 \Delta G_{mix}}{\partial x^2}\right)_{T,P} > 0$

- **Unstable (spontaneous splitting):** $\left(\frac{\partial^2 \Delta G_{mix}}{\partial x^2}\right)_{T,P} < 0$

- **Spinodal (limit of stability):** $\left(\frac{\partial^2 \Delta G_{mix}}{\partial x^2}\right)_{T,P} = 0$

The locus of spinodal points (plotted in T-x space) gives the **spinodal curve**, while the actual two-phase envelope (the boundary between the single-phase and two-phase regions) is the **binodal curve**. The gap between the spinodal and binodal is called the **metastable region**.

### Key Thermodynamic Steps to Establish LLE

1. **Step 1 — Equate chemical potentials** of each component across the α and β phases at constant T and P.
2. **Step 2 — Introduce fugacity coefficients** ($\hat{\phi}_i$) for vapor-phase treatment, or **activity coefficients** ($\gamma_i$) for liquid-phase treatment.
3. **Step 3 — Choose a model** for $\gamma_i$ (ideal, Margules, van Laar, Wilson, NRTL, UNIQUAC, UNIFAC).
4. **Step 4 — Solve the material balance + iso-fugacity equations simultaneously** to obtain the equilibrium compositions $x_i^{\alpha}$ and $x_i^{\beta}$.
5. **Step 5 — Verify stability** using the second-derivative test on $\Delta G_{mix}$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Equation / Concept** | **Mathematical Form** | **Variables & Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Iso-fugacity criterion | $\hat{f}_i^{\alpha} = \hat{f}_i^{\beta}$ | $\hat{f}_i$ = fugacity (Pa) | Master equilibrium equation |
| Liquid-phase equilibrium | $x_i^{\alpha}\gamma_i^{\alpha} = x_i^{\beta}\gamma_i^{\beta}$ | $x_i$ = mole fraction, $\gamma_i$ = activity coefficient (–) | Practical LLE solver |
| Nernst Distribution Law (ideal, dilute) | $K_D = \dfrac{C_i^{\alpha}}{C_i^{\beta}}$ | $K_D$ = partition coefficient (–) | Solvent extraction design |
| Modified Nernst (with dissociation) | $K_D' = K_D \cdot \dfrac{[H^+]}{[H^+] + K_a}$ | $K_a$ = acid dissociation constant | pH-sensitive extractions |
| Selectivity | $\beta_{ij} = \dfrac{K_{D,i}}{K_{D,j}} = \dfrac{(x_i^{\alpha}/x_i^{\beta})}{(x_j^{\alpha}/x_j^{\beta})}$ | $\beta$ = selectivity (–); $\beta > 1$ ⇒ separation feasible | Choice of solvent |
| NRTL activity coefficient | $\ln \gamma_i = \dfrac{\sum_j x_j \tau_{ji} G_{ji}}{\sum_k x_k G_{ki}} + \sum_j \dfrac{x_j G_{ij}}{\sum_k x_k G_{kj}} \left( \tau_{ij} - \dfrac{\sum_m x_m \tau_{mj} G_{mj}}{\sum_k x_k G_{kj}} \right)$ | $\tau_{ij}$, $G_{ij} = \exp(-\alpha_{ij}\tau_{ij})$ | Most common LLE correlation |
| UNIQUAC activity coefficient | $\ln \gamma_i = \ln \dfrac{\Phi_i}{x_i} + \dfrac{z}{2}q_i \ln \dfrac{\theta_i}{\Phi_i} + l_i - \dfrac{\Phi_i}{x_i}\sum_j x_j l_j - q_i'\ln\left( \sum_j \theta_j' \tau_{ij} \right) + q_i - q_i' \sum_j \dfrac{\theta_j' \tau_{ij}}{\sum_k \theta_k' \tau_{kj}}$ | $\Phi_i$, $\theta_i$ = segment and surface area fractions | Polymer + solvent LLE |
| Plait point (binary) | $\left(\dfrac{\partial^3 G_m}{\partial x^3}\right)_{T,P} = 0$ at the critical composition | – | Upper limit of two-phase region |
| Lever rule (mass balance) | $\dfrac{n^{\alpha}}{n^{\beta}} = \dfrac{\text{length of tie line opposite to } \alpha}{\text{length of tie line opposite to } \beta}$ | $n$ = moles | Compute phase amounts |
| Upper Critical Solution Temp. (UCST) | max T at which LLE exists | T_c (K) | Energy-driven miscibility gap |
| Lower Critical Solution Temp. (LCST) | min T at which LLE exists | T_c (K) | Hydrogen-bonded systems (e.g., water–nicotine) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility of LLE Equations:**
> - **NRTL** is the *workhorse* equation for LLE in process simulators (Aspen Plus, ChemCAD, DWSIM) because it has **three adjustable parameters per binary pair**, easily fits strongly non-ideal LLE data, and is fully predictive in the **miscibility-gap** region.
> - **UNIQUAC** is preferred for systems containing a **polymer + solvent** (relevant in ATPS for protein recovery).
> - The **lever rule** is used in the lab to compute how much extract phase is produced per kg of feed in single-stage extraction.

## 2.3 Critical Phenomena and Phase Diagrams

- **UCST (Upper Critical Solution Temperature):** Above the UCST, the two liquids are completely miscible; below it, they split. Example: water–phenol system at **T_c ≈ 65.85 °C**.
- **LCST (Lower Critical Solution Temperature):** Below the LCST, miscible; above it, immiscible. Example: water–triethylamine, water–nicotine.
- **Closed-loop miscibility gap:** A few systems (e.g., water–nicotine) exhibit *both* UCST and LCST, forming a closed miscibility dome.
- **Plait point:** The single point where the binodal and spinodal curves meet and the two liquid phases become identical in composition.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Equivalence of Chemical Potential and Iso-fugacity Criteria

We start from the fundamental definition of chemical potential for component *i* in a non-ideal liquid phase:

$$
\mu_i = \mu_i^{\circ} + RT \ln \hat{a}_i
$$

where $\hat{a}_i$ is the activity of *i* in the solution. In a two-phase LLE system (α and β), equality of chemical potential at equilibrium gives:

$$
\mu_i^{\circ,\alpha} + RT \ln \hat{a}_i^{\alpha} = \mu_i^{\circ,\beta} + RT \ln \hat{a}_i^{\beta}
$$

Rearranging the two standard-state terms:

$$
\mu_i^{\circ,\alpha} - \mu_i^{\circ,\beta} = RT \ln \left( \dfrac{\hat{a}_i^{\beta}}{\hat{a}_i^{\alpha}} \right)
$$

But $\hat{a}_i = \gamma_i x_i$ (Lewis–Randall reference state), so the difference in standard chemical potentials is absorbed into a *modified* partition coefficient. Taking the *exponential* of both sides and using the identity $\hat{f}_i = \hat{\phi}_i x_i P$ for vapor-like reference states, we obtain the **iso-fugacity** form. For pure liquid reference:

$$
\hat{f}_i^{\alpha} = \gamma_i^{\alpha} x_i^{\alpha} f_i^{\circ} \quad ; \quad \hat{f}_i^{\beta} = \gamma_i^{\beta} x_i^{\beta} f_i^{\circ}
$$

Since the pure-component standard-state fugacity $f_i^{\circ}$ is the same on both sides, equating gives:

$$
\boxed{\,x_i^{\alpha} \gamma_i^{\alpha} = x_i^{\beta} \gamma_i^{\beta}\,}
$$

This is the **practical LLE equation** that every KTU problem uses.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Nernst Distribution Law from Iso-fugacity

For a solute *i* distributed between two **immiscible** liquid phases (carrier solvent S and extractant E) in the **dilute limit** ($\gamma_i \to 1$):

$$
x_i^{\alpha} \cdot 1 = x_i^{\beta} \cdot 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_i^{\alpha} = x_i^{\beta}
$$

This would predict no separation, contradicting experiment. The resolution is to recognize that in the dilute limit, the **reference states differ** because each phase has a different pure-solute standard state. The rigorous form is:

$$
K_{D,i} = \dfrac{x_i^{\alpha}}{x_i^{\beta}} = \dfrac{\gamma_i^{\beta,\infty}}{\gamma_i^{\alpha,\infty}} \cdot \dfrac{f_i^{\circ,\beta}}{f_i^{\circ,\alpha}}
$$

The two right-hand terms are **constant at fixed T and P**, so we group them into a single experimentally measurable constant $K_D$. Expressed in terms of molar concentrations $C_i$ (rather than mole fractions), for dilute solutions where $C_i \approx \rho_i x_i$:

$$
\boxed{\,K_{D,i} = \dfrac{C_i^{\alpha}}{C_i^{\beta}} = \text{constant at fixed } T,P\,}
$$

This is **Nernst's Distribution Law** (1891).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Modified Distribution Law with Dissociation

Suppose solute HA is a weak acid that **partially dissociates** in the aqueous (β) phase:

$$
HA \rightleftharpoons H^+ + A^-
$$

The apparent distribution coefficient measured experimentally is the ratio of **total** analytical concentration of HA (both molecular and dissociated forms) in the α phase to the total in the β phase:

$$
K_D' = \dfrac{C_{HA}^{\alpha}}{C_{HA}^{\beta,total}} = \dfrac{C_{HA}^{\alpha}}{C_{HA}^{\beta} + C_{A^-}^{\beta}}
$$

Let $\alpha$ be the degree of dissociation of HA in the aqueous phase. Then $C_{HA}^{\beta} = (1-\alpha)C_{HA}^{\beta,total}$ and $C_{A^-}^{\beta} = \alpha C_{HA}^{\beta,total}$. Substituting:

$$
C_{HA}^{\beta,total} = (1-\alpha)C_{HA}^{\beta,total} + \alpha C_{HA}^{\beta,total} \quad \text{(identity check)}
$$

The intrinsic partition coefficient (for *molecular* HA only) is $K_D = C_{HA}^{\alpha}/C_{HA}^{\beta}$, so $C_{HA}^{\alpha} = K_D \cdot (1-\alpha) C_{HA}^{\beta,total}$. Therefore:

$$
K_D' = \dfrac{K_D (1-\alpha) C_{HA}^{\beta,total}}{C_{HA}^{\beta,total}} = K_D (1-\alpha)
$$

For a weak acid with dissociation constant $K_a = \alpha^2 C_{HA}^{\beta,total}/(1-\alpha)$ (at low concentration $C$), solving for $(1-\alpha)$ yields:

$$
(1-\alpha) = \dfrac{[H^+]}{[H^+] + K_a}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\boxed{\,K_D' = K_D \cdot \dfrac{[H^+]}{[H^+] + K_a}\,}
$$

This is the **modified Nernst law** and is the basis of all **pH-shift extraction** strategies used in biochemical engineering (e.g., penicillin G extraction — at low pH, HA stays molecular and extracts into the solvent; at high pH, it ionizes and stays in water).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Stability Criterion and Spinodal Equation

For a binary mixture of components 1 and 2, the molar Gibbs energy of mixing is:

$$
\Delta G_m = RT \left( x_1 \ln x_1 + x_2 \ln x_2 \right) + RT \left( x_1 \ln \gamma_1 + x_2 \ln \gamma_2 \right)
$$

The stability condition for a single homogeneous phase requires:

$$
\left( \dfrac{\partial^2 \Delta G_m}{\partial x_1^2} \right)_{T,P} > 0
$$

Differentiating the **ideal** part twice gives $\dfrac{\partial^2}{\partial x_1^2}\left[ x_1\ln x_1 + (1-x_1)\ln(1-x_1) \right] = \dfrac{1}{x_1} + \dfrac{1}{1-x_1} = \dfrac{1}{x_1 x_2}$. For the **excess** part, the second derivative is:

$$
\left( \dfrac{\partial^2 G^E / RT}{\partial x_1^2} \right) = 1 + \dfrac{\partial \ln \gamma_1}{\partial x_1} - \dfrac{\partial \ln \gamma_2}{\partial x_1}
$$

The **spinodal** (incipient instability) is therefore defined by:

$$
\boxed{\,\dfrac{1}{x_1 x_2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 (G^E/RT)}{\partial x_1^2} = 0\,}
$$

The **binodal** is defined by the equality of chemical potentials, $\mu_1^{\alpha} = \mu_1^{\beta}$ and $\mu_2^{\alpha} = \mu_2^{\beta}$. The two curves coincide only at the **plait point**, which mathematically corresponds to:

$$
\left( \dfrac{\partial^3 \Delta G_m}{\partial x_1^3} \right)_{T,P} = 0
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Liquid–Liquid Equilibrium Solver Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed Mixture zF_i] --> B{Single-Phase Stability Test}
    B -- Stable --> C[Single Liquid Phase] 
    B -- Unstable --> D[LLE Solver Loop]
    D --> E[Guess Tie-Line Endpoint x_alpha]
    E --> F[Compute Activity Coeffs gamma_i]
    F --> G{NRTL or UNIQUAC Model}
    G --> H[Evaluate K_D_i equals x_beta gamma_beta over x_alpha gamma_alpha]
    H --> I[Update Tie-Line via Newton-Raphson]
    I --> J{Convergence Check}
    J -- No --> E
    J -- Yes --> K[Final Equilibrium x_alpha and x_beta]
    K --> L[Compute Selectivity beta_ij and Recovery]
    L --> M[Output to Extraction Column Design]
    M --> N[Penicillin or Citric Acid Recovery Process]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Phase Envelope of a Partially Miscible Binary System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T_x_Diagram["T-x Phase Diagram - Water and Phenol System"]
        A1[Pure Water x1 equals 1] --> B1[Binodal Curve - Upper Branch]
        A1 --> C1[Binodal Curve - Lower Branch]
        B1 --> D1[UCST Plait Point T_c equals 65.85 C]
        C1 --> D1
        D1 --> E1[Single Phase Region Above UCST]
        B1 --> F1[Two Phase LLE Region]
        C1 --> F1
        F1 --> G1[Tie Line Connects x_alpha and x_beta at fixed T]
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Steps in a Single-Stage Liquid–Liquid Extraction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed F contains Solute and Carrier] --> B[Mix with Solvent S in Mixer]
    B --> C[Allow Phase Separation in Settler]
    C --> D[Top Phase: Extract rich in Solute]
    C --> E[Bottom Phase: Raffinate poor in Solute]
    D --> F[Distillation or pH Shift to Recover Solute]
    E --> G[Recycle Carrier or Treat Waste]
    F --> H[Pure Product]
    G --> H
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read the Mermaid diagrams above:**
> - **Diagram 1 (Solver architecture):** Shows the iterative algorithm used by process simulators (Aspen, DWSIM) to compute LLE compositions. Notice the Newton-Raphson feedback loop until convergence on tie lines.
> - **Diagram 2 (T-x envelope):** Standard LLE phase diagram for a binary showing the **UCST plait point** at the top of the dome. Below the binodal, two phases coexist; the horizontal line at a given T is the **tie line**.
> - **Diagram 3 (Process flow):** Functional steps of a bench-scale single-stage extraction — the most common LLE operation in biochemical engineering.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** State and explain Nernst's Distribution Law. Mention the conditions under which it is valid.

**Model Answer:**

Nernst's Distribution Law (1891) states that *a solute distributes itself between two immiscible (or partially miscible) liquid phases in a constant ratio of concentrations at constant temperature*.

$$
K_D = \dfrac{C_i^{\alpha}}{C_i^{\beta}} = \text{constant at fixed } T
$$

**Conditions for validity:**
1. The solute must be in the **same molecular form** in both phases (no dissociation, no association, no chemical reaction).
2. The two solvents must be **mutually immiscible** (or only slightly soluble in each other).
3. The solute must be in **dilute solution** (typically < 0.1 M) so that activity coefficients approach unity.
4. Temperature must be **constant** throughout the equilibrium.

If any condition is violated, the **modified Nernst law** is used.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define (a) distribution coefficient, (b) selectivity, and (c) plait point in the context of liquid–liquid extraction.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Distribution Coefficient (K_D):** The ratio of the equilibrium concentration of a solute in the extract (α) phase to its concentration in the raffinate (β) phase. It is a **dimensionless** number; values > 1 indicate preferential affinity for the extract phase.

$$
K_D = \dfrac{C_{solute}^{\alpha}}{C_{solute}^{\beta}}
$$

**(b) Selectivity (β_ij):** A measure of the *separation efficiency* of a solvent for solute *i* relative to an unwanted component *j*. It is the ratio of distribution coefficients:

$$
\beta_{ij} = \dfrac{K_{D,i}}{K_{D,j}}
$$

A solvent is *viable* only if **β_ij > 1**; the higher the value, the easier the separation in fewer stages.

**(c) Plait Point:** The unique point on the binodal curve of a ternary (or higher) system where the two equilibrium liquid phases become **identical in composition** (i.e., the tie-line length shrinks to zero). Mathematically, it is the point where $\left(\partial^3 G_m / \partial x^3\right)_{T,P} = 0$. The plait point represents the **upper limit of the two-phase region**.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the **modified Nernst distribution law** for a weak acid HA that dissociates in the aqueous phase. Show clearly all the intermediate steps and the final expression relating the apparent distribution coefficient $K_D'$ to the intrinsic $K_D$ and the dissociation constant $K_a$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] An antibiotic (weak acid, $K_a = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$) is to be extracted from an aqueous fermentation broth (pH = 5.0) into amyl acetate at 25 °C. The intrinsic distribution coefficient $K_D = 12$. Calculate the **apparent distribution coefficient** $K_D'$. Comment on the implications for extraction efficiency.

**Model Solution to (a):**

Refer to **Section 3.3 — Derivation 3** above. The result is:

$$
K_D' = K_D \cdot \dfrac{[H^+]}{[H^+] + K_a}
$$

*Step 1:* Write the dissociation equilibrium: $HA \rightleftharpoons H^+ + A^-$, with $K_a = [H^+][A^-]/[HA]$.
*Step 2:* Express the analytical (total) concentration in the aqueous phase as $C_{HA}^{total} = [HA] + [A^-] = [HA](1 + K_a/[H^+])$.
*Step 3:* Define the degree of dissociation: $\alpha = [A^-]/C_{HA}^{total}$, so $(1-\alpha) = [H^+]/([H^+] + K_a)$.
*Step 4:* Since only the *molecular* form partitions into the organic phase, $C_{HA}^{org} = K_D \cdot [HA] = K_D (1-\alpha) C_{HA}^{aq,total}$.
*Step 5:* Therefore $K_D' = C_{HA}^{org}/C_{HA}^{aq,total} = K_D (1-\alpha) = K_D [H^+]/([H^+] + K_a)$. $\blacksquare$

**Model Solution to (b):**

Given: $K_a = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$, pH = 5.0, $K_D = 12$.

Step 1: Compute $[H^+]$ from pH:
$$
[H^+] = 10^{-pH} = 10^{-5.0} = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol/L}
$$

Step 2: Substitute into the modified Nernst equation:
$$
K_D' = 12 \times \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{-5}}{1.0 \times 10^{-5} + 2.5 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

Step 3: Evaluate the denominator:
$$
1.0 \times 10^{-5} + 2.5 \times 10^{-3} = 2.51 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/L}
$$

Step 4: Compute the ratio:
$$
K_D' = 12 \times \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{-5}}{2.51 \times 10^{-3}} = 12 \times 3.984 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0478
$$

**Result:** $K_D' \approx 0.048$

**Comment:** Since $K_D' \ll 1$, the antibiotic remains **almost entirely in the aqueous phase** at pH 5.0. To extract efficiently, the pH must be lowered to **~2.0–2.5** (about 1.5 units below $pK_a = 2.6$), where the acid stays in its molecular HA form and partitions strongly into the organic phase. This is the principle of **pH-shift extraction** used in commercial penicillin recovery.

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Alternative Module Choice]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **NRTL activity coefficient model** for liquid–liquid equilibrium. State its mathematical form, define all parameters, and discuss why it is the *preferred* model for strongly non-ideal LLE systems in biochemical engineering.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For a ternary system of water (1) – acetic acid (2) – benzene (3) at 25 °C, the NRTL binary parameters are $\tau_{12} = 1.30$, $\tau_{21} = 0.95$, $\alpha_{12} = 0.30$, $\tau_{23} = 1.80$, $\tau_{32} = 0.40$, $\alpha_{23} = 0.30$, $\tau_{13} = 2.40$, $\tau_{31} = 1.20$, $\alpha_{13} = 0.20$. Verify the iso-fugacity condition for component 2 (acetic acid) at the experimentally measured compositions $x_2^{\alpha} = 0.06$ (aqueous phase) and $x_2^{\beta} = 0.10$ (benzene phase) using the local-composition form of NRTL (assume $G_{ij} = \exp(-\alpha_{ij}\tau_{ij})$).

**Model Solution to (a):**

The **Non-Random Two-Liquid (NRTL)** model, developed by Renon and Prausnitz (1968), is a local-composition model for the excess Gibbs energy. The activity coefficient of component *i* in a multi-component mixture is:

$$
\ln \gamma_i = \dfrac{\sum_j x_j \tau_{ji} G_{ji}}{\sum_k x_k G_{ki}} + \sum_j \dfrac{x_j G_{ij}}{\sum_k x_k G_{kj}} \left[ \tau_{ij} - \dfrac{\sum_m x_m \tau_{mj} G_{mj}}{\sum_k x_k G_{kj}} \right]
$$

where $G_{ij} = \exp(-\alpha_{ij} \tau_{ij})$, $\tau_{ij} = a_{ij}/RT$ is a dimensionless interaction parameter, and $\alpha_{ij}$ is the non-randomness parameter (typically 0.20 – 0.47).

**Why NRTL is preferred for LLE in biochemical engineering:**
1. **Three parameters per binary pair** ($\tau_{ij}$, $\tau_{ji}$, $\alpha_{ij}$), giving enough flexibility to fit strong non-idealities including **partial miscibility gaps**.
2. It is **continuous across the entire composition range**, including the **two-phase region**, unlike Wilson's model.
3. It is **applicable to partially miscible systems** (e.g., water–toluene, water–amyl acetate) — essential for LLE.
4. It captures the **local composition effect**: a molecule of type *i* in a sea of *j* has a *different* local environment than the bulk average — a physically realistic assumption for hydrogen-bonded bioprocess solvents. $\blacksquare$

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1:** Calculate all $G_{ij}$ values using $G_{ij} = \exp(-\alpha_{ij}\tau_{ij})$:
- $G_{12} = \exp(-0.30 \times 1.30) = \exp(-0.39) = 0.6771$
- $G_{21} = \exp(-0.30 \times 0.95) = \exp(-0.285) = 0.7519$
- $G_{23} = \exp(-0.30 \times 1.80) = \exp(-0.54) = 0.5827$
- $G_{32} = \exp(-0.30 \times 0.40) = \exp(-0.12) = 0.8869$
- $G_{13} = \exp(-0.20 \times 2.40) = \exp(-0.48) = 0.6188$
- $G_{31} = \exp(-0.20 \times 1.20) = \exp(-0.24) = 0.7866$

**Step 2:** State the iso-fugacity condition for component 2:
$$
x_2^{\alpha} \gamma_2^{\alpha} = x_2^{\beta} \gamma_2^{\beta}
$$

**Step 3:** Identify the phase compositions. In the aqueous phase (α), the bulk composition is mostly water + acid: $x_1^{\alpha} \approx 0.94$, $x_2^{\alpha} = 0.06$, $x_3^{\alpha} \approx 0$ (since benzene is the extract phase, essentially absent from the aqueous phase).

**Step 4:** In the benzene phase (β): $x_1^{\beta} \approx 0$ (water immiscible with benzene), $x_2^{\beta} = 0.10$, $x_3^{\beta} \approx 0.90$.

**Step 5:** Approximate the activity coefficients in each phase using the **dilute-solute form** of NRTL (where one solvent dominates):

In aqueous phase ($\alpha$, dominated by component 1 = water, with trace acid 2):
$$
\ln \gamma_2^{\alpha} \approx \tau_{21} \cdot \dfrac{G_{21}}{x_1 G_{11} + x_2 G_{21}} = \tau_{21} \cdot G_{21} = 0.95 \times 0.7519 = 0.7143
$$

In benzene phase ($\beta$, dominated by component 3 = benzene, with trace acid 2):
$$
\ln \gamma_2^{\beta} \approx \tau_{23} \cdot G_{23} = 1.80 \times 0.5827 = 1.0489
$$

**Step 6:** Compute $\gamma_2$:
- $\gamma_2^{\alpha} = \exp(0.7143) = 2.043$
- $\gamma_2^{\beta} = \exp(1.0489) = 2.854$

**Step 7:** Check iso-fugacity:
- LHS: $x_2^{\alpha} \gamma_2^{\alpha} = 0.06 \times 2.043 = 0.1226$
- RHS: $x_2^{\beta} \gamma_2^{\beta} = 0.10 \times 2.854 = 0.2854$

**Result:** LHS = 0.123, RHS = 0.285. The two values are **not equal**, indicating either (a) the given $x_2^{\alpha}, x_2^{\beta}$ are *not* the true equilibrium tie-line endpoints (the data needs to be solved iteratively), or (b) the simplifying assumptions (negligible cross-solubility, dilute-solute form) introduce error. The proper procedure is to **iterate** by adjusting $x_2^{\alpha}$ until the iso-fugacity condition is satisfied — this is exactly what the Newton-Raphson loop in **Diagram 1 (Section 4.1)** performs. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the dissociation term in the modified Nernst law** when the solute is a weak acid/base. Many students write $K_D' = K_D$ and lose 3–4 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $K_D$ with selectivity $\beta$** — $K_D$ is for a *single* component, $\beta$ is a *ratio* of two $K_D$'s.
> 3. **Unit mistakes in pH calculations** — $[H^+] = 10^{-pH}$ in mol/L, not in mol/m³.
> 4. **Not using the correct reference state** for activity coefficients. KTU problems usually assume **Lewis–Randall** with pure-component fugacity $f_i^{\circ}$ cancelling out.
> 5. **Neglecting to specify the temperature** in distribution-coefficient answers — $K_D$ is **strongly T-dependent**; stating it without mentioning "at 25 °C" loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Drawing binodal/spinodal curves on the wrong axis** — the *binodal* lies in the **two-phase region**, the *spinodal* lies *inside* the binodal. Mixing them up is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 7. **Forgetting to apply the lever rule** when asked "what fraction of the raffinate is obtained?" — the lever rule uses **tie-line lengths**, not compositions.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LLE = liquid–liquid equilibrium**, defined by the condition $\mu_i^{\alpha} = \mu_i^{\beta}$ for all components *i*, leading to the practical equation $x_i^{\alpha} \gamma_i^{\alpha} = x_i^{\beta} \gamma_i^{\beta}$.
- **Nernst's Distribution Law** $K_D = C_i^{\alpha}/C_i^{\beta}$ applies only to **dilute, non-dissociating, non-associating** solutes between **immiscible** solvents.
- **Modified Nernst Law** for weak acids: $K_D' = K_D \cdot [H^+]/([H^+] + K_a)$; for weak bases: $K_D' = K_D \cdot K_a/(K_a + [H^+])$. This is the heart of **pH-shift extraction** in antibiotic recovery.
- **Selectivity** $\beta_{ij} = K_{D,i}/K_{D,j}$ must be **> 1** for a feasible extraction; higher $\beta$ means fewer equilibrium stages.
- **NRTL model** is the **preferred LLE correlation** in biochemical engineering because it handles **partially miscible** systems and has **three parameters per binary pair**; Wilson's model is *not* valid for LLE because it cannot represent partial miscibility.
- **Binodal curve** = boundary of the two-phase region; **spinodal curve** = limit of metastability; **plait point** = where they meet, defined by $\partial^3 G_m / \partial x^3 = 0$.
- **UCST** systems (water–phenol, water–triethylamine) and **LCST** systems (water–nicotine) reflect whether the mixing process is **enthalpy-dominated** (UCST) or **entropy-dominated** (LCST).
- **Lever rule** converts tie-line geometry into **molar phase amounts**: $n^{\alpha}/n^{\beta} = \text{(opposite tie-line length)}$.
- **Aqueous Two-Phase Systems (ATPS)** — PEG/dextran or PEG/salt — are a special form of LLE used in **protein and enzyme purification** because of their **mild, biocompatible** environment.
- In **process simulators** (Aspen, DWSIM, ChemCAD), the default LLE algorithm is a **Newton–Raphson iteration on the NRTL tie-line equations**, with the **UNIQUAC** model as an alternative for polymer-containing systems.
- Always quote $K_D$ values **at the stated temperature** (typically 25 °C); for penicillin G, $K_D \approx 30$–$50$ in butyl acetate at 0 °C, but only $\sim 12$ at 25 °C.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties of Solutions and Chemical Reaction Equilibria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT414 - BIOCHEMICAL THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Properties of Solutions and Chemical Reaction Equilibria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Ideal Solutions — Formal KTU Definition

An **ideal solution** is a homogeneous binary or multi-component liquid mixture in which the intermolecular forces between unlike molecules (A–B) are identical in magnitude to the average of the forces between like molecules (A–A and B–B). Under this condition, the **enthalpy of mixing** is zero ($\Delta H_{mix} = 0$) and the **volume of mixing** is zero ($\Delta V_{mix} = 0$).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Raoult's Law, Henry's Law, and Colligative Properties are core to Module 3 and are tested every semester.

### 1.2 Real Solutions — Formal KTU Definition

A **real solution** deviates from ideality because A–B interactions differ from A–A and B–B interactions. These deviations are quantified by **activity coefficients** ($\gamma_i$) and the **excess thermodynamic functions** ($G^{E}, H^{E}, S^{E}$).

### 1.3 Chemical Reaction Equilibrium — Formal KTU Definition

**Chemical reaction equilibrium** is the macroscopic state of a closed reacting system at a specified temperature and pressure where the forward and reverse reaction rates are equal, the **Gibbs free energy change is zero** ($\Delta G = 0$), and the **time-averaged composition** no longer changes.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Bus Stop Equilibrium"**
> Imagine a narrow bridge where students from College A walk east-to-west and students from College B walk west-to-east. At first, you see a net flow. But once a steady crowd is on the bridge, the number passing each direction per minute is **equal** — even though students are still moving. This is **dynamic equilibrium**: macroscopic stillness, microscopic activity.
>
> - **Ideal solution** = a perfectly mixed tea where sugar molecules experience identical forces from water and from each other.
> - **Real solution** = salted water, where Na⁺ and Cl⁻ strongly attract water (negative deviation from Raoult's Law).
> - **Biochemical example** = ATP ⇌ ADP + Pi in the cell is never truly "still" — flux continues, but the ratio stays constant at physiological conditions.

### 1.5 Key Standard Constants

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \, \text{J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1} = 0.08206 \, \text{L·atm mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$
- **Faraday's Constant:** $F = 96485 \, \text{C mol}^{-1}$
- **Standard State:** $T^{\circ} = 298.15 \, \text{K}$, $P^{\circ} = 1 \, \text{bar} = 10^{5} \, \text{Pa}$
- **Standard Free Energy Convention:** $\Delta G^{\circ}_{f}$ of elements in their reference form = **0 kJ/mol**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Raoult's Law — The Cornerstone of Solution Thermodynamics

**Statement:** The partial vapor pressure of component $i$ in an ideal solution equals the product of its mole fraction and the pure component vapor pressure.

$$ P_i = x_i \cdot P_i^{*} $$

**Logic Steps:**
- Molecules escape from the liquid surface proportional to their surface concentration.
- In an ideal solution, the escaping tendency of $i$ is reduced **only** by dilution (no chemical interaction effect).
- The total pressure follows **Dalton's Law of additive partial pressures**: $P_{total} = \sum P_i$.

### 2.2 Henry's Law — Volatile Solutes in Dilute Solution

$$ P_i = K_{H,i} \cdot x_i $$

- $K_H$ is the **Henry's Law constant** (pressure unit).
- Applies when the solute is volatile but the solvent is non-volatile (e.g., O₂ in water, CO₂ in blood plasma — a **biochemically critical** relationship).

### 2.3 Colligative Properties — They Depend Only on the *Number* of Solute Particles

| Property | Formula | Linear Constant | KTU 2024 Symbol |
|---|---|---|---|
| Relative lowering of vapor pressure | $\dfrac{P^{*} - P}{P^{*}} = x_{solute}$ | — | — |
| Boiling Point Elevation | $\Delta T_b = K_b \cdot m$ | $K_b = \dfrac{R T_b^{*2} M_{solvent}}{1000 \, \Delta H_{vap}}$ | $K_b$ |
| Freezing Point Depression | $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$ | $K_f = \dfrac{R T_f^{*2} M_{solvent}}{1000 \, \Delta H_{fus}}$ | $K_f$ |
| Osmotic Pressure (van't Hoff) | $\pi = i \, C \, R \, T$ | — | $\pi$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $i$ is the **van't Hoff factor** representing the *degree of dissociation* (e.g., $i = 2$ for NaCl, $i = 3$ for CaCl₂). This is essential for **biochemical salt solutions** like physiological saline (0.9% NaCl).

### 2.4 Chemical Equilibrium — Master Equations

**Equilibrium Constant (concentration basis):**
$$ K_c = \prod_{i} [C_i]^{\nu_i} $$

**Equilibrium Constant (pressure basis):**
$$ K_p = \prod_{i} (P_i)^{\nu_i} $$

**Linking $K_p$ and $K_c$:**
$$ K_p = K_c (RT)^{\Delta n_g} $$

**Master Gibbs Free Energy Relationship:**
$$ \Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q $$

**At equilibrium ($\Delta G = 0$, $Q = K$):**
$$ \Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K $$

### 2.5 Temperature Dependence — The van't Hoff Equation

$$ \frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R T^{2}} $$

**Integrated form (constant $\Delta H^{\circ}$):**
$$ \ln \left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right) $$

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Summary

| Equation Name | Compact Form | Boundary / Validity | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Raoult's Law | $P_i = x_i P_i^{*}$ | Ideal solution | Pa / bar |
| Henry's Law | $P_i = K_{H,i} x_i$ | Dilute, volatile solute | Pa / bar |
| BPE | $\Delta T_b = K_b m$ | Non-volatile solute | K |
| FPD | $\Delta T_f = K_f m$ | Non-volatile solute | K |
| Osmotic Pressure | $\pi = i C R T$ | Dilute solution | Pa |
| Equilibrium Criterion | $\Delta G = 0$ | Closed system, $T, P$ fixed | J mol⁻¹ |
| Free Energy ↔ K | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$ | All temperatures | J mol⁻¹ |
| $K_p$ ↔ $K_c$ | $K_p = K_c(RT)^{\Delta n}$ | Ideal gases only | dimensionless |
| van't Hoff | $\dfrac{d \ln K}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R T^{2}}$ | $\Delta H^{\circ}$ constant | K⁻¹ |
| Le Chatelier | $\Delta G_{rxn} < 0 \Rightarrow$ forward | Spontaneous direction | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility (Biochemical Context):** These equations govern **dialysis design**, **blood osmolality calculations** (~285–295 mOsm/kg), **freeze-drying (lyophilization) of biopharmaceuticals**, **fermentation reactor pressure control**, and **enzyme reactor yield optimization** in downstream processing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — The Osmotic Pressure Equation ($\pi = CRT$)

We consider a semipermeable membrane separating pure solvent from a solution. The chemical potential of the solvent on the pure solvent side at pressure $P$ must equal that on the solution side at pressure $P + \pi$ at equilibrium.

**Step 1: Chemical potential of pure solvent (side 1):**
$$ \mu_{solvent}^{(1)} = \mu^{*}(T, P) $$

**Step 2: Chemical potential of solvent in solution (side 2):**
$$ \mu_{solvent}^{(2)} = \mu^{*}(T, P + \pi) + RT \ln x_{solvent} $$

**Step 3: Apply the equilibrium condition $\mu^{(1)} = \mu^{(2)}$:**
$$ \mu^{*}(T, P) = \mu^{*}(T, P + \pi) + RT \ln x_{solvent} $$

**Step 4: Expand $\mu^{*}(T, P + \pi)$ as a Taylor series (small $\pi$):**
$$ \mu^{*}(T, P + \pi) \approx \mu^{*}(T, P) + \pi \overline{V}_{solvent} $$

where $\overline{V}_{solvent}$ is the partial molar volume of the solvent.

**Step 5: Substitute back and cancel $\mu^{*}(T,P)$:**
$$ 0 = \pi \overline{V}_{solvent} + RT \ln x_{solvent} $$

**Step 6: Use the approximation $\ln x_{solvent} = \ln(1 - x_{solute}) \approx -x_{solute}$ for dilute solution:**
$$ 0 = \pi \overline{V}_{solvent} - RT \, x_{solute} $$

**Step 7: For dilute solution, $x_{solute} \approx \dfrac{n_{solute}}{n_{solvent}}$ and $C = \dfrac{n_{solute}}{V_{solution}}$:**
$$ \pi = \frac{RT \, n_{solute}}{n_{solvent} \, \overline{V}_{solvent}} = \frac{RT \, n_{solute}}{V_{solution}} = CRT $$

**Final Result:** $\boxed{\pi = CRT}$ (with van't Hoff factor $i$: $\pi = iCRT$)

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Relationship $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$

**Step 1: For a general reaction $aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$, the reaction Gibbs energy is:**
$$ \Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q $$

where the reaction quotient is:
$$ Q = \frac{a_C^{c} \cdot a_D^{d}}{a_A^{a} \cdot a_B^{b}} $$

**Step 2: At equilibrium, by definition, the reaction has zero driving force:**
$$ \Delta G_{eq} = 0 $$

**Step 3: At equilibrium, $Q = K$ (the activities become equilibrium activities). Substituting:**
$$ 0 = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln K $$

**Step 4: Rearrange to isolate $\Delta G^{\circ}$:**
$$ \boxed{\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K} $$

**Step 5 (corollary):** Therefore, the criterion for spontaneity of reaction in any state is:
$$ \Delta G = RT \ln \left( \frac{Q}{K} \right) $$

- If $Q < K \Rightarrow \Delta G < 0 \Rightarrow$ **forward direction spontaneous**
- If $Q > K \Rightarrow \Delta G > 0 \Rightarrow$ **reverse direction spontaneous**
- If $Q = K \Rightarrow \Delta G = 0 \Rightarrow$ **equilibrium**

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — The van't Hoff Equation

**Step 1: Start with the Gibbs–Helmholtz equation at constant pressure:**
$$ \frac{\partial (\Delta G^{\circ}/T)}{\partial T}\bigg|_{P} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^{2}} $$

**Step 2: Substitute $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$:**
$$ \frac{\partial (-R \ln K)}{\partial T}\bigg|_{P} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^{2}} $$

**Step 3: The factor $-R$ is constant, so it comes out of the partial derivative:**
$$ -R \frac{d \ln K}{dT} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^{2}} $$

**Step 4: Cancel the negative signs:**
$$ \boxed{\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R T^{2}}} $$

**Step 5 (Integration at constant $\Delta H^{\circ}$):**
$$ \int_{K_1}^{K_2} d \ln K = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^{2}} $$

$$ \ln \frac{K_2}{K_1} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention Pitfall (KTU Examiners Love This):**
> - **Endothermic** ($\Delta H^{\circ} > 0$): $K$ **increases** with $T$.
> - **Exothermic** ($\Delta H^{\circ} < 0$): $K$ **decreases** with $T$.
> - This is the thermodynamic basis of **Le Chatelier's Principle** for temperature changes.

---

### 3.4 Derivation IV — Relation Between $K_p$ and $K_c$

**Step 1: For an ideal gas, partial pressure is $P_i = C_i RT$.**

**Step 2: Substitute into the $K_p$ definition:**
$$ K_p = \prod_i (P_i)^{\nu_i} = \prod_i (C_i RT)^{\nu_i} $$

**Step 3: Separate the concentration and $(RT)$ terms:**
$$ K_p = \left[\prod_i (C_i)^{\nu_i}\right] \cdot (RT)^{\sum \nu_i} $$

**Step 4: The first bracket is $K_c$, and the exponent $\sum \nu_i = \Delta n_g$ (change in gaseous moles):**
$$ \boxed{K_p = K_c (RT)^{\Delta n_g}} $$

**Special Cases:**
- $\Delta n_g = 0$ (e.g., $\text{H}_2 + \text{I}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{HI}$): $K_p = K_c$
- $\Delta n_g > 0$ (e.g., $\text{CaCO}_3 \rightleftharpoons \text{CaO} + \text{CO}_2$): $K_p = K_c (RT)^{+1}$
- $\Delta n_g < 0$ (e.g., $\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3$): $K_p = K_c (RT)^{-2}$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Logic of Solution Behavior and Equilibrium

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Liquid Mixture] --> B{Are A-B interactions equal to A-A and B-B?}
    B -- YES --> C[Ideal Solution]
    B -- NO --> D[Real Solution]

    C --> E[Raoult's Law: Pi = xi Pi*]
    C --> F[Delta H_mix = 0, Delta V_mix = 0]
    C --> G[Colligative Properties valid]

    D --> H[Henry's Law: Pi = KH xi]
    D --> I[Activity coeff gamma is non-unity]
    D --> J[Excess Functions GE, HE, SE exist]

    G --> K[Applications: Osmometry, Cryoscopy]
    E --> K

    L[Closed Reacting System] --> M[Reaction Quotient Q vs Equilibrium K]
    M -- Q less than K --> N[Forward shift, Delta G less than 0]
    M -- Q greater than K --> O[Reverse shift, Delta G greater than 0]
    M -- Q equals K --> P[Equilibrium, Delta G equals 0]
    P --> Q[Delta G zero equals minus RT ln K]
    Q --> R[Temperature change shifts K via van't Hoff]
    R --> S[Le Chatelier: System opposes the change]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Free Energy & Equilibrium Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS_A[INPUTS]
        A1[Standard free energy Delta G zero] --> A2[Reactant activity aA, aB]
        B1[Product activity aC, aD] --> A2
        A2 --> A3[Reaction quotient Q]
    end

    subgraph CORE_PROCESS[CORE THERMODYNAMIC ENGINE]
        A3 --> B2[Compute Delta G equals Delta G zero plus RT ln Q]
        B2 --> B3{Delta G sign}
        B3 -- less than 0 --> B4[Spontaneous forward reaction]
        B3 -- greater than 0 --> B5[Spontaneous reverse reaction]
        B3 -- equals 0 --> B6[Equilibrium State]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS_B[OUTPUTS]
        B4 --> C1[Reactants consumed]
        B5 --> C2[Products consumed]
        B6 --> C3[Compute K from Delta G zero equals minus RT ln K]
        C3 --> C4[Apply van't Hoff for T dependence]
    end
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Chart — Ideal vs Real Solution

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IDEAL_CASE[IDEAL SOLUTION]
        I1[A-B interactions equal to A-A, B-B]
        I2[Delta H mix equals 0]
        I3[Raoult's law strictly valid]
        I4[Activity coefficient equals 1]
    end

    subgraph REAL_CASE[REAL SOLUTION]
        R1[A-B interactions different from A-A, B-B]
        R2[Delta H mix non-zero]
        R3[Raoult's law fails]
        R4[Activity coefficient not equal to 1]
    end

    subgraph BIO_APPS[BIOCHEMICAL EXAMPLES]
        I1 --> X1[Sugar in water dilute]
        R1 --> X2[Protein in saline]
        R1 --> X3[DNA in Tris buffer]
        R1 --> X4[Oxygen in blood plasma]
    end
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State and explain Raoult's Law. Mention two of its limitations.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** Raoult's Law states that the partial vapor pressure of a component in an ideal solution is equal to the product of its mole fraction in the solution and the vapor pressure of the pure component at the same temperature.

$$ P_i = x_i P_i^{*} $$

**Explanation:** The law assumes that the intermolecular forces between unlike molecules (A–B) are equal to those between like molecules (A–A and B–B). This implies $\Delta H_{mix} = 0$ and $\Delta V_{mix} = 0$.

**Limitations:**
1. **Non-ideality:** Fails for polar + non-polar mixtures (e.g., acetone + benzene) and for electrolytes where A–B interactions differ.
2. **Concentration dependence:** Valid only for the solvent in dilute solutions; the solute obeys Henry's Law instead.
3. **Association/Dissociation:** Fails for systems with hydrogen bonding (e.g., chloroform + acetone exhibits negative deviation).

**Mark Distribution:** Statement (1) + Equation (1) + Limitations (1).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between $\Delta G$, $\Delta G^{\circ}$, and $\Delta G^{\ddagger}$ (activation free energy) with respect to a chemical reaction.**

**Model Answer:**

| Symbol | Meaning | Condition |
|---|---|---|
| $\Delta G$ | Instantaneous Gibbs free energy change of reaction at the current state | Depends on actual activities ($Q$) |
| $\Delta G^{\circ}$ | Standard Gibbs free energy change when all reactants and products are at unit activity | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$ at any $T$ |
| $\Delta G^{\ddagger}$ | Free energy of activation at the transition state | Governs **reaction rate** (kinetics) |

**Key Distinction:** $\Delta G$ and $\Delta G^{\circ}$ are **thermodynamic** quantities (govern *spontaneity* and *equilibrium position*). $\Delta G^{\ddagger}$ is a **kinetic** quantity (governs *how fast* the reaction proceeds via Eyring equation: $k = \frac{k_B T}{h} e^{-\Delta G^{\ddagger}/RT}$).

In biochemical context, enzymes work by **lowering $\Delta G^{\ddagger}$** without affecting $\Delta G^{\circ}$ or $K_{eq}$.

**Mark Distribution:** Definitions (2) + Distinction (1).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**Q. (a)** Derive the relationship between the equilibrium constant $K$ and the standard Gibbs free energy change $\Delta G^{\circ}$. Explain how the temperature dependence of $K$ is given by the van't Hoff equation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the reaction $\text{N}_2\text{O}_4(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NO}_2(g)$, the equilibrium constant $K_p$ is **0.148** at **298 K** and **0.360** at **308 K**. Calculate:
- (i) The standard enthalpy change $\Delta H^{\circ}$ of the reaction. **(3 Marks)**
- (ii) The standard entropy change $\Delta S^{\circ}$ at 298 K. **(2 Marks)**
- (iii) The standard Gibbs free energy change $\Delta G^{\circ}$ at 298 K. **(2 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

For a general reaction at any instant:
$$ \Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q $$

At equilibrium, the criterion is $\Delta G = 0$ and $Q = K$:

$$ 0 = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln K $$

$$ \boxed{\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K} \quad \text{(3 marks)} $$

Using the Gibbs–Helmholtz relation:
$$ \frac{\partial (\Delta G^{\circ}/T)}{\partial T}\bigg|_{P} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^{2}} $$

Substituting $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$:
$$ \frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{RT^{2}} \quad \text{(2 marks)} $$

Integrating (constant $\Delta H^{\circ}$):
$$ \ln \left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right) \quad \text{(2 marks)} $$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Calculation of $\Delta H^{\circ}$:**

$$ \ln \left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = \ln \left(\frac{0.360}{0.148}\right) = \ln(2.432) = 0.8887 $$

$$ \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{308} - \frac{1}{298} = \frac{298 - 308}{308 \times 298} = \frac{-10}{91784} = -1.0894 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{K}^{-1} $$

$$ 0.8887 = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{8.314}(-1.0894 \times 10^{-4}) $$

$$ \Delta H^{\circ} = \frac{0.8887 \times 8.314}{1.0894 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{7.389}{1.0894 \times 10^{-4}} $$

$$ \boxed{\Delta H^{\circ} = +67{,}830 \, \text{J mol}^{-1} \approx +67.8 \, \text{kJ mol}^{-1}} $$

**(1 mark)**

The positive value confirms the reaction is **endothermic** (consistent with $K$ increasing with $T$).

**(ii) Calculation of $\Delta S^{\circ}$ at 298 K:**

First compute $\Delta G^{\circ}$ at 298 K:
$$ \Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K = -(8.314)(298) \ln(0.148) $$
$$ = -(8.314)(298)(-1.9105) = +4730.7 \, \text{J mol}^{-1} $$

Then:
$$ \Delta S^{\circ} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ} - \Delta G^{\circ}}{T} = \frac{67830 - 4730.7}{298} $$

$$ \boxed{\Delta S^{\circ} = +211.6 \, \text{J K}^{-1}\text{mol}^{-1}} $$

**(1 mark)**

The positive $\Delta S^{\circ}$ is expected because $\Delta n_g = +1$ (gas molecules increase).

**(iii) Calculation of $\Delta G^{\circ}$ at 298 K (already shown above):**

$$ \boxed{\Delta G^{\circ} = +4.73 \, \text{kJ mol}^{-1}} $$

**(1 mark)**

Since $\Delta G^{\circ} > 0$, the reaction is **non-spontaneous** in the forward direction under standard conditions — Le Chatelier suggests heating will favor products.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q. (a)** Define an ideal solution. State Raoult's Law and Henry's Law. Show that Raoult's Law is a special case of Henry's Law. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A solution of **2.0 g** of a non-volatile, non-electrolyte solute in **50 g** of water (molar mass = 18 g/mol) freezes at **$-1.20^{\circ}$C**. Given $K_f$ for water = **$1.86 \, \text{K kg mol}^{-1}$**, calculate:
- (i) The molar mass of the solute. **(3 Marks)**
- (ii) The boiling point of the solution. **(2 Marks)** [Given: $K_b$ for water = **$0.512 \, \text{K kg mol}^{-1}$**]
- (iii) The osmotic pressure of the solution at **$27^{\circ}$C**, assuming the solution density is **$1.0 \, \text{g cm}^{-3}$**. **(2 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Theory (7 Marks):**

**Ideal Solution Definition (2 marks):** A solution where the intermolecular forces between unlike molecules (A–B) are the same as between like molecules (A–A, B–B). Thus, $\Delta H_{mix} = 0$ and $\Delta V_{mix} = 0$.

**Raoult's Law (1.5 marks):** $P_i = x_i P_i^{*}$ — applicable to the **solvent** in ideal/dilute solutions.

**Henry's Law (1.5 marks):** $P_i = K_{H,i} x_i$ — applicable to the **solute** in dilute solutions.

**Raoult's as a special case of Henry's (2 marks):**

For an **ideal** solution at the limit $x_{solvent} \to 1$, the solvent's vapor pressure follows Raoult's Law:
$$ P_{solvent} = x_{solvent} P_{solvent}^{*} $$

In a **dilute** solution, if we write the solute's partial pressure as:
$$ P_{solute} = K_{H,solute} \cdot x_{solute} $$

In the limit of an **ideal solution**, $K_{H,solute} = P_{solute}^{*}$ (the pure solute hypothetical vapor pressure), so the equation reduces to:
$$ P_{solute} = x_{solute} P_{solute}^{*} $$

This is **Raoult's Law applied to the solute**. Therefore, Raoult's Law is the **limit of Henry's Law when the proportionality constant $K_H$ equals the pure component vapor pressure $P^*$**, which occurs in an ideal solution. **(2 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

**(i) Molar mass of solute (3 marks):**

Given: mass of solute $w_2 = 2.0 \, \text{g}$, mass of solvent $w_1 = 50 \, \text{g} = 0.050 \, \text{kg}$, $\Delta T_f = 1.20 \, \text{K}$.

$$ \Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m = K_f \cdot \frac{w_2 / M_2}{w_1} $$

$$ 1.20 = 1.86 \times \frac{2.0 / M_2}{0.050} $$

$$ 1.20 = 1.86 \times \frac{40}{M_2} = \frac{74.4}{M_2} $$

$$ M_2 = \frac{74.4}{1.20} $$

$$ \boxed{M_2 = 62.0 \, \text{g mol}^{-1}} $$

**(ii) Boiling point of solution (2 marks):**

$$ \Delta T_b = K_b \cdot m = 0.512 \times \frac{40}{62.0} = 0.330 \, \text{K} $$

$$ \boxed{T_b = 100.33^{\circ}\text{C}} $$

**(iii) Osmotic pressure (2 marks):**

Volume of solution:
$$ V = \frac{\text{total mass}}{\text{density}} = \frac{(50 + 2.0) \, \text{g}}{1.0 \, \text{g/cm}^{3}} = 52.0 \, \text{cm}^{3} = 52.0 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{L} = 5.20 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^{3} $$

Moles of solute:
$$ n = \frac{2.0}{62.0} = 0.03226 \, \text{mol} $$

Molarity:
$$ C = \frac{0.03226}{5.20 \times 10^{-5}} \times 10^{3} = \frac{0.03226}{5.20 \times 10^{-5}} = 620.4 \, \text{mol m}^{-3} $$

Temperature: $T = 27 + 273 = 300 \, \text{K}$

$$ \pi = CRT = 620.4 \times 8.314 \times 300 = 1.547 \times 10^{6} \, \text{Pa} $$

$$ \boxed{\pi \approx 1.55 \, \text{MPa} \, \approx 15.3 \, \text{atm}} $$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign of $\Delta G$ vs $\Delta G^{\circ}$:** Students often confuse them. $\Delta G$ decides the *direction* (depends on $Q$); $\Delta G^{\circ}$ decides the *position* (depends on $K$).
> 2. **Forgetting $i$ factor:** For NaCl, $i = 2$ (nearly, ignoring ion pairing). In biochemical problems, never use $i = 1$ for strong electrolytes.
> 3. **Units of $K_H$:** Must match $P_i$ units — converting atm to bar requires multiplication by 1.01325.
> 4. **van't Hoff sign:** $K$ **increases** with $T$ for **endothermic** reactions, **decreases** for **exothermic** — examiners test this directly.
> 5. **Le Chatelier trap:** Pressure change affects $K$ only when $\Delta n_g \neq 0$. Pressure change does NOT change $K$ itself, only the equilibrium **position**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- [ ] **Raoult's Law:** $P_i = x_i P_i^{*}$ — valid for **solvent** in ideal solutions.
- [ ] **Henry's Law:** $P_i = K_H x_i$ — valid for **solute** in dilute solutions.
- [ ] **Colligative properties** depend only on solute **particle count**, not identity.
- [ ] **BPE:** $\Delta T_b = K_b m$ ; **FPD:** $\Delta T_f = K_f m$ ; **Osmotic:** $\pi = iCRT$.
- [ ] **van't Hoff factor $i$:** $i = 1 + \alpha(n-1)$ where $\alpha$ is degree of dissociation.
- [ ] **Equilibrium criterion:** $\Delta G = 0$ ; **Equilibrium constant relation:** $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$.
- [ ] **Spontaneity test:** $Q < K \Rightarrow$ forward ; $Q > K \Rightarrow$ reverse ; $Q = K \Rightarrow$ equilibrium.
- [ ] **van't Hoff equation:** $\dfrac{d \ln K}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R T^{2}}$.
- [ ] **$K_p$ vs $K_c$:** $K_p = K_c (RT)^{\Delta n_g}$.
- [ ] **Le Chatelier:** Endothermic reactions favor products at higher $T$; Exothermic favor products at lower $T$.
- [ ] **Biochemical applications:** dialysis, blood osmolality, lyophilization, fermentation, enzyme kinetics.
- [ ] **Always quote temperature** when reporting $K$ — $K$ is **not** a universal constant!
- [ ] **Standard state convention:** 1 bar, 1 M (or unit activity), 298.15 K (for tabulation only).
- [ ] **Activity** replaces concentration in real solutions: $a_i = \gamma_i \cdot c_i / c^{\circ}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fugacity of Pure Gases, Liquids and Solids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT414 - BIOCHEMICAL THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fugacity of Pure Gases, Liquids and Solids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In classical thermodynamics, the chemical potential $\mu$ of a pure real (non-ideal) substance cannot be expressed in the same convenient logarithmic form as it is for an **ideal gas**. To overcome this, **Gilbert N. Lewis (1901)** introduced a fictitious but mathematically rigorous thermodynamic property called **Fugacity** (symbol: $f$), derived from the Latin word *fugere*, meaning *"to flee"* or *"to escape"*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> **Fugacity ($f$)** is a measure of the *escaping tendency* or *effective partial molar Gibbs free energy* of a real gas, liquid, or solid. It is defined through the exact differential relation:
> $$dG = V\,dP - S\,dT$$
> and the chemical potential equation:
> $$\mu_i = \mu_i^{\circ} + RT \ln \frac{f_i}{f_i^{\circ}}$$
> where $f_i^{\circ}$ is the fugacity at a defined standard state.

## 1.2 The Problem Fugacity Solves

For an **ideal gas**, the chemical potential at temperature $T$ is:
$$\mu^{ig}(T,P) = \mu^{\circ}(T, P^{\circ}) + RT \ln \frac{P}{P^{\circ}}$$

However, for **real gases, liquids, and solids**, this ideal logarithmic form fails because of intermolecular forces, finite molecular volume, and phase-specific behavior. **Fugacity replaces pressure** in thermodynamic equations so that the same mathematical form can be retained, even for non-ideal systems.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Crowded Room"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine a crowded lecture hall (a real gas under high pressure). Each student (molecule) wants to "escape" through the door. The **door's opening pressure** is the *pressure*, but the *real ease of escape* depends on:
> - How tightly packed students are (intermolecular forces / compressibility)
> - How much space they already occupy (excluded volume)
> - The temperature (kinetic energy of escape attempts)
>
> **Fugacity** = the *effective* opening that allows the same number of students to flee, as would escape from an *idealized* uncrowded hall (ideal gas). It is therefore *not* the real pressure, but a **corrected escape tendency** that makes the math universal.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$
- **Standard Pressure:** $P^{\circ} = 1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- **Reference Temperature:** $T$ (system temperature)
- **Standard State Fugacity for Gases:** $f^{\circ} = 1 \text{ bar}$
- **Standard State Fugacity for Liquids/Solids:** $f^{\circ} = \text{Pure liquid/solid at } 1 \text{ bar and system temperature}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fugacity Coefficient ($\phi$)

The relationship between fugacity and pressure is captured by the **fugacity coefficient** $\phi$:

$$\phi \equiv \frac{f}{P}$$

### Logical Breakdown:

1. **At low pressure (ideal gas limit):** Real gas behaves ideally → intermolecular forces vanish → $f \rightarrow P$ → therefore $\phi \rightarrow 1$.
2. **At moderate/high pressure:** Molecules repel and attract each other → $\phi \neq 1$.
3. **For a gas:** $\phi$ is a function of **reduced temperature ($T_r$)** and **reduced pressure ($P_r$)**.
4. **For a liquid/solid:** $\phi$ is usually defined through the **Poynting correction factor**.

## 2.2 Why Do We Need Fugacity? (The "How")

- **Preserves the structure** of all ideal-gas thermodynamic equations for real systems.
- Allows calculation of **vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE)** using $f_i^{\text{vapor}} = f_i^{\text{liquid}}$.
- Acts as the **thermodynamic bridge** between chemical potential and measurable $P,V,T$ data.
- Essential for **reaction equilibrium constants** and **phase equilibria** in biochemical engineering.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|--------------------|
| 1 | General definition | $d\mu = RT\,d\ln f$ (at constant $T$) | Foundation equation |
| 2 | Chemical potential via fugacity | $\mu = \mu^{\circ} + RT \ln \dfrac{f}{f^{\circ}}$ | Universal form |
| 3 | Fugacity coefficient | $\phi = \dfrac{f}{P}$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Ideal gas limit | $\phi = 1$ as $P \rightarrow 0$ | Boundary condition |
| 5 | From residual Gibbs energy | $\ln \phi = \dfrac{G^{R}}{RT} = \int_{0}^{P} \dfrac{Z-1}{P}\,dP$ | Generalized |
| 6 | From compressibility factor | $\ln \phi = \int_{0}^{P} \dfrac{Z-1}{P}\,dP$ | At constant $T$ |
| 7 | Virial equation form | $\ln \phi = \dfrac{BP}{RT} + \dfrac{C-\dfrac{B^2}{RT}P^2}{2(RT)^2} + \cdots$ | Truncated virial EOS |
| 8 | Pure liquid fugacity | $f_i^{L} = \phi_i^{\text{sat}}\,P_i^{\text{sat}}\,\exp\!\left[\dfrac{V_i^{L}(P - P_i^{\text{sat}})}{RT}\right]$ | Poynting correction |
| 9 | Pure solid fugacity | $f_i^{S} = \phi_i^{\text{sat}}\,P_i^{\text{sat}}\,\exp\!\left[\dfrac{V_i^{S}(P - P_i^{\text{sub}})}{RT}\right]$ | Sublimation-based |
| 10 | Temperature dependence | $\dfrac{\partial \ln f}{\partial T}\bigg\vert_{P} = -\dfrac{H(T,P) - H^{\circ}(T)}{RT^{2}} = -\dfrac{\bar{H}^{R}}{RT^{2}}$ | Used for enthalpy corrections |
| 11 | Pressure dependence | $\dfrac{\partial \ln f}{\partial P}\bigg\vert_{T} = \dfrac{V}{RT}$ | Exact |
| 12 | Standard state (gas) | $f^{\circ} = 1 \text{ bar}$ | At system $T$ |
| 13 | Standard state (condensed) | $f^{\circ} = \text{pure condensed phase at } 1 \text{ bar},\,T$ | Hypothetical for supercritical solutes |
| 14 | Fugacity of pure solid | $f^{S}(T,P) = f^{\text{sat}}(T)\,\exp\!\left[\dfrac{V^{S}(P - P^{\text{sub}})}{RT}\right]$ | At $T < T_{\text{triple}}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The exponential term in (8), (9), and (14) is the **Poynting correction**. For solids and liquids at low pressures, $V^{S}$ and $V^{L}$ are very small, so the exponential is $\approx 1$. Always check whether pressure deviation from saturation is significant before including it.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Chemical Reactor Design:** Computing fugacities is critical for non-ideal reactor modeling, particularly in high-pressure polymerization (e.g., polyethylene production at $P = 1000\text{–}3000 \text{ atm}$).
- **Biochemical Engineering:** Enzyme-catalyzed reactions in supercritical CO₂ require fugacity-based equilibrium constants.
- **Distillation Column Design:** Bubble-point and dew-point calculations for non-ideal mixtures use **$\phi-\phi$** or **$\phi-\gamma$** approaches based on fugacity.
- **Crystallization & Pharmaceutical Industry:** Solid fugacity governs polymorph stability in drug formulations.
- **Refrigeration & LNG Processing:** Liquid fugacity determines vapor pressures of cryogenic fluids.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Fugacity from the Fundamental Equation

We begin with the exact differential for a pure substance:
$$dG = V\,dP - S\,dT$$

At constant temperature $T$, this reduces to:
$$dG \big\vert_{T} = V\,dP$$

For one mole, $G = \mu$, so:
$$d\mu \big\vert_{T} = V\,dP$$

For an **ideal gas**, $V = \dfrac{RT}{P}$, giving:
$$d\mu^{ig} \big\vert_{T} = \dfrac{RT}{P}\,dP = RT\,d\ln P$$

Integrating between a reference state $(\mu^{\circ}, P^{\circ})$ and state $(\mu, P)$:
$$\mu^{ig}(T,P) - \mu^{ig}(T,P^{\circ}) = RT \ln \frac{P}{P^{\circ}}$$

### The "Trick" of Lewis:
For a **real gas**, we *replace* $P$ with a new property $f$ such that the **same mathematical form** holds:
$$\mu(T,P) - \mu^{\circ}(T) = RT \ln \frac{f}{f^{\circ}}$$

By definition of fugacity, both equations must be **consistent at the ideal-gas limit** ($P \rightarrow 0$):
$$\lim_{P \to 0} \frac{f}{P} = 1$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of $\ln \phi$ from Residual Gibbs Energy

The residual Gibbs energy is defined as:
$$G^{R}(T,P) \equiv G^{R}(T,P) - G^{ig}(T,P)$$

At constant $T$:
$$dG^{R} = V\,dP - \dfrac{RT}{P}\,dP = \left(V - \dfrac{RT}{P}\right)dP$$

Define compressibility factor $Z = \dfrac{PV}{RT}$, so $V = \dfrac{ZRT}{P}$:
$$dG^{R} = \left(\dfrac{ZRT}{P} - \dfrac{RT}{P}\right)dP = \dfrac{RT(Z-1)}{P}\,dP$$

Integrating from $P = 0$ (where $G^{R} = 0$ and $\phi = 1$) to $P$:
$$\frac{G^{R}}{RT} = \int_{0}^{P} \frac{Z-1}{P}\,dP$$

But since:
$$\mu - \mu^{ig} = RT \ln \frac{f}{P} = RT \ln \phi = G^{R}$$

we arrive at the **master equation**:

$$\boxed{\;\ln \phi = \frac{G^{R}}{RT} = \int_{0}^{P} \frac{Z-1}{P}\,dP\;}$$

### Conversion Logic Step-by-Step:
- **Step 1:** Real gas molar volume $V = ZRT/P$ from definition of $Z$.
- **Step 2:** Subtract ideal gas volume $RT/P$ to get excess volume.
- **Step 3:** Multiply by $dP$ to get differential residual Gibbs energy.
- **Step 4:** Integrate from $P = 0$ (ideal limit) to target $P$.
- **Step 5:** Recognize $G^{R}/RT$ equals $\ln(f/P) = \ln\phi$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation Using the Virial Equation of State

The virial EOS truncated at the second coefficient is:
$$Z = 1 + \frac{BP}{RT}$$

Substitute into the master equation:
$$\ln \phi = \int_{0}^{P} \frac{1}{P}\left(\frac{BP}{RT}\right)dP$$

Factor out constants:
$$\ln \phi = \frac{B}{RT}\int_{0}^{P} dP$$

Integrate:
$$\boxed{\;\ln \phi = \frac{BP}{RT}\;}$$

For higher virial coefficients (third virial $C$):
$$Z = 1 + \frac{BP}{RT} + \frac{(C-B^2)P^2}{(RT)^2}$$

Then:
$$\ln \phi = \frac{BP}{RT} + \frac{(C - B^2)}{(RT)^2}\cdot\frac{P^2}{2} + \cdots$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Fugacity of a Pure Liquid (Poynting Correction)

Consider pure liquid at temperature $T$ and pressure $P$, in equilibrium with its vapor at saturation pressure $P^{\text{sat}}$.

Since liquid and vapor fugacities are equal at equilibrium:
$$f^{L}(T,P) = f^{V}(T,P)$$

The vapor fugacity at $(T, P^{\text{sat}})$ is:
$$f^{V}(T, P^{\text{sat}}) = \phi^{\text{sat}}\,P^{\text{sat}}$$

We must now raise the pressure from $P^{\text{sat}}$ to $P$, using $\left(\dfrac{\partial \ln f}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = \dfrac{V}{RT}$:

$$\ln \frac{f^{L}(T,P)}{f^{L}(T,P^{\text{sat}})} = \int_{P^{\text{sat}}}^{P} \frac{V^{L}}{RT}\,dP$$

Assuming $V^{L}$ (liquid molar volume) is independent of pressure:
$$\ln \frac{f^{L}(T,P)}{\phi^{\text{sat}}\,P^{\text{sat}}} = \frac{V^{L}(P - P^{\text{sat}})}{RT}$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\;f^{L}(T,P) = \phi^{\text{sat}}\,P^{\text{sat}}\,\exp\!\left[\frac{V^{L}(P - P^{\text{sat}})}{RT}\right]\;}$$

### Engineering Significance:
- At $P$ close to $P^{\text{sat}}$, the exponential $\approx 1$.
- The Poynting correction becomes critical only for **liquids under very high pressure** (e.g., deep-sea hydrothermal chemistry, hydraulic systems).

---

## 3.5 Derivation of Fugacity of a Pure Solid

For a pure solid below its triple point, we proceed similarly using sublimation pressure $P^{\text{sub}}$:

$$f^{S}(T,P) = P^{\text{sub}}(T)\,\phi^{\text{sub}}\,\exp\!\left[\frac{V^{S}(P - P^{\text{sub}})}{RT}\right]$$

For most practical purposes:
$$\phi^{\text{sub}} \approx 1 \quad \text{(since } P^{\text{sub}} \ll 1 \text{ bar)}$$

So the working form is:
$$\boxed{\;f^{S}(T,P) \approx P^{\text{sub}}(T)\,\exp\!\left[\frac{V^{S}(P - P^{\text{sub}})}{RT}\right]\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation of Solid Fugacity:**
> The fugacity of a solid at temperature $T$ and pressure $P$ is essentially determined by its **sublimation pressure** (the ease with which molecules escape the solid lattice directly into vapor), corrected for any pressure deviation from that sublimation equilibrium using the small but finite molar volume of the solid.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Fugacity Conceptual Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real Substance: Gas, Liquid, or Solid] --> B{Is the system ideal?}
    B -- Yes, P approaches 0 --> C[Ideal Gas Reference State]
    B -- No, finite P --> D[Define Fugacity f via Lewis]
    D --> E[Fugacity Coefficient phi = f over P]
    E --> F[Compute ln phi from residual Gibbs GR]
    F --> G{Choose Equation of State}
    G --> G1[Virial EOS]
    G --> G2[Peng Robinson EOS]
    G --> G3[Soave Redlich Kwong EOS]
    G1 --> H[Integrate Z minus 1 over P]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Fugacity Value f = phi times P]
    C --> J[Standard State f deg equals 1 bar]
    I --> K{Phase of Substance}
    K --> K1[Gas: f equals phi times P]
    K --> K2[Liquid: Poynting Correction Applied]
    K --> K3[Solid: Sublimation Pressure Dominates]
    K1 --> L[Engineering Application]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    L --> L1[Reactor Design]
    L --> L2[Distillation Columns]
    L --> L3[Supercritical Extraction]
    L --> L4[Pharmaceutical Polymorph Stability]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Fugacity Determination Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input Parameters"]
        I1[Temperature T]
        I2[Pressure P]
        I3[Molar Volume V]
        I4[Compressibility Factor Z]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS["Thermodynamic Engine"]
        P1[Apply Master Equation ln phi equals integral of Z minus 1 over P]
        P2[Integrate from P equals 0 to target P]
        P3{Evaluate phi}
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["Output Fugacity"]
        O1[phi value]
        O2[Multiply by P to obtain f]
        O3[Apply Poynting correction if liquid or solid]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P1
    I4 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
    O3 --> FINAL[Final Fugacity f T P]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Phase Diagram: Fugacity Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Fugacity Determination] --> G{Gaseous Phase}
    G --> G1[Use Virial or Cubic EOS]
    G1 --> G2[Compute ln phi from integral]
    G2 --> G3[Final f gas equals phi times P]

    START --> L{Liquid Phase}
    L --> L1[Identify saturation pressure P sat]
    L1 --> L2[Compute phi sat at low P sat]
    L2 --> L3[Apply Poynting exponential correction]
    L3 --> L4[Final f liquid equals phi sat times P sat times EXP]

    START --> S{Solid Phase}
    S --> S1[Identify sublimation pressure P sub]
    S1 --> S2[Assume phi sub approx 1]
    S2 --> S3[Apply solid Poynting correction with V S]
    S3 --> S4[Final f solid equals P sub times EXP]
```
