# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define fugacity. How is fugacity of an ideal gas related to its pressure?**

**Model Answer:**
Fugacity ($f$) is a thermodynamic property introduced by G.N. Lewis to express the *escaping tendency* of a real gas, liquid, or solid. It is defined so that the chemical potential takes the form $\mu = \mu^{\circ} + RT\ln(f/f^{\circ})$ for both real and ideal substances.

For an **ideal gas**, the intermolecular forces are zero and molecules behave independently. Therefore, the escaping tendency equals the actual pressure:
$$f^{\text{ideal}} = P \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi = \dfrac{f}{P} = 1$$

The fugacity coefficient $\phi$ equals unity for an ideal gas at all pressures and temperatures.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is the Poynting correction? Why is it usually negligible for liquids at low pressures?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Poynting correction** is the exponential factor:
$$\exp\!\left[\frac{V^{L}(P - P^{\text{sat}})}{RT}\right]$$

that appears in the fugacity expression for a condensed phase when the pressure deviates from saturation pressure.

For liquids at low pressures:
1. The molar volume $V^{L}$ is very small (typically $\sim 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}$).
2. The term $(P - P^{\text{sat}})$ is small.
3. Hence $V^{L}(P - P^{\text{sat}})/RT \ll 1$, making the exponential $\approx 1$.

It becomes significant only at **very high pressures** (deep oceans, high-pressure reactors).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the fugacity coefficient of a pure gas using the residual Gibbs free energy approach. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A gas obeys the virial equation $Z = 1 + \dfrac{BP}{RT}$, where $B = -0.0001 \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}$ at $T = 300 \text{ K}$. Calculate the fugacity and fugacity coefficient of the gas at $10 \text{ bar}$. Take $R = 8.314 \text{ J}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):
Starting from:
$$G^{R} = G - G^{ig} \quad \text{and} \quad dG^{R}\big\vert_{T} = \left(V - \frac{RT}{P}\right)dP$$

Using $V = ZRT/P$:
$$dG^{R}\big\vert_{T} = \left(\frac{ZRT}{P} - \frac{RT}{P}\right)dP = \frac{RT(Z-1)}{P}dP$$

Integrating from $P = 0$ to $P$:
$$\frac{G^{R}}{RT} = \int_{0}^{P}\frac{Z-1}{P}dP$$

Since $G^{R}/RT = \ln\phi$:
$$\boxed{\;\ln\phi = \int_{0}^{P}\frac{Z-1}{P}dP\;}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $Z = 1 + \dfrac{BP}{RT}$, $B = -0.0001 \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}$, $T = 300 \text{ K}$, $P = 10 \text{ bar} = 10 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$.

Substitute into the virial-derived formula:
$$\ln\phi = \frac{BP}{RT}$$

Compute $BP$:
$$BP = (-0.0001)(10 \times 10^{5}) = -100 \text{ J/mol}$$

Compute $RT$:
$$RT = (8.314)(300) = 2494.2 \text{ J/mol}$$

Therefore:
$$\ln\phi = \frac{-100}{2494.2} = -0.04009$$

$$\phi = e^{-0.04009} = 0.9607$$

Fugacity:
$$f = \phi \cdot P = 0.9607 \times 10 \text{ bar} = 9.607 \text{ bar}$$

**Final Answer:** $\phi = 0.9607$, $f = 9.607 \text{ bar}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B (b):**
> - Students frequently forget to **convert bar to Pa** ($1 \text{ bar} = 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$). This yields incorrect $BP$ values and wrong fugacity.
> - The **sign of $B$** matters: a negative $B$ indicates attractive forces, giving $\phi < 1$.
> - Always retain **4 significant figures** in intermediate steps to avoid rounding penalties.
> - Do NOT confuse "fugacity coefficient" with "compressibility factor" — they are **different** quantities!

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** What is the standard state for fugacity of (i) a pure gas and (ii) a pure liquid? Explain with mathematical expressions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The vapor pressure of pure water at $373.15 \text{ K}$ is $1.013 \text{ bar}$. The molar volume of liquid water is $18 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}$. Calculate the fugacity of liquid water at $50 \text{ bar}$ and $373.15 \text{ K}$, assuming the vapor behaves as an ideal gas. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**(i) Pure Gas Standard State:**
The standard state is the **pure gas at 1 bar and system temperature $T$** behaving ideally.
$$f^{\circ,\text{gas}}(T) = 1 \text{ bar}$$
$$\mu^{\text{gas}}(T,P) = \mu^{\circ}(T) + RT\ln\dfrac{f(T,P)}{1 \text{ bar}}$$

**(ii) Pure Liquid Standard State:**
The standard state is the **pure liquid at 1 bar and system temperature $T$**.
$$f^{\circ,\text{liq}}(T) = 1 \text{ bar}$$
$$\mu^{\text{liq}}(T,P) = \mu^{\circ}(T) + RT\ln\dfrac{f^{\text{liq}}(T,P)}{1 \text{ bar}}$$

The key distinction: although both share the same numerical value of $f^{\circ}$, their **physical interpretations differ** — for liquids, this is a *hypothetical* state since real liquids cannot exist as ideal gases at 1 bar.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $P^{\text{sat}} = 1.013 \text{ bar}$, $V^{L} = 18 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}$, $P = 50 \text{ bar}$, $T = 373.15 \text{ K}$.

Since vapor behaves as an ideal gas, $\phi^{\text{sat}} = 1$, so:
$$f^{V}(T, P^{\text{sat}}) = P^{\text{sat}} = 1.013 \text{ bar}$$

Apply the Poynting correction:
$$f^{L}(T,P) = P^{\text{sat}}\,\exp\!\left[\frac{V^{L}(P - P^{\text{sat}})}{RT}\right]$$

Convert pressures:
$$P = 50 \text{ bar} = 50 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$$
$$P^{\text{sat}} = 1.013 \text{ bar} = 1.013 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$$

Compute pressure difference:
$$P - P^{\text{sat}} = (50 - 1.013) \times 10^{5} = 48.987 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$$

Compute numerator:
$$V^{L}(P - P^{\text{sat}}) = (18 \times 10^{-6})(48.987 \times 10^{5}) = 88.18 \text{ J/mol}$$

Compute denominator:
$$RT = (8.314)(373.15) = 3102.0 \text{ J/mol}$$

Compute exponent:
$$\frac{V^{L}(P - P^{\text{sat}})}{RT} = \frac{88.18}{3102.0} = 0.02843$$

Compute exponential:
$$\exp(0.02843) = 1.0288$$

Final fugacity:
$$f^{L} = 1.013 \times 1.0288 = 1.0422 \text{ bar}$$

**Final Answer:** $f^{L}(373.15 \text{ K}, 50 \text{ bar}) \approx 1.042 \text{ bar}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B (b):**
> - **Unit conversion is critical**: Always convert bar → Pa before substitution to match SI units of $V$ and $R$.
> - If students write the exponent as $(V^L \cdot \Delta P)/(RT)$ **without proper units**, they will lose 2 marks.
> - **Do NOT use $\phi^{\text{sat}}$ if the vapor is ideal** — it is exactly 1 and must be omitted for full marks.
> - A common error is using $V$ in $\text{cm}^3/\text{mol}$ instead of $\text{m}^3/\text{mol}$. Always convert.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- ⭐ **Fugacity ($f$)** = corrected "effective pressure" that retains ideal-gas mathematical form for real substances.
- ⭐ **Fugacity Coefficient** $\phi = f/P$ is **dimensionless** and approaches **1** at the ideal-gas limit.
- ⭐ **Master Equation:** $\ln\phi = \dfrac{G^{R}}{RT} = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{P}\dfrac{Z-1}{P}dP$.
- ⭐ **Virial Form (2nd coefficient):** $\ln\phi = \dfrac{BP}{RT}$.
- ⭐ **Poynting Correction:** $\exp\!\left[\dfrac{V^{L}(P - P^{\text{sat}})}{RT}\right]$ — usually $\approx 1$ at low pressures.
- ⭐ **Gas fugacity:** $f^{\text{gas}} = \phi P$ where $\phi$ depends on $T$ and $P$.
- ⭐ **Liquid fugacity:** $f^{L} = \phi^{\text{sat}} P^{\text{sat}} \cdot \exp[\text{Poynting}]$.
- ⭐ **Solid fugacity:** $f^{S} \approx P^{\text{sub}} \cdot \exp\!\left[\dfrac{V^{S}(P - P^{\text{sub}})}{RT}\right]$.
- ⭐ **Standard state fugacity:** $f^{\circ} = 1 \text{ bar}$ for both gases and condensed phases (different physical meaning).
- ⭐ **Temperature dependence:** $\left(\dfrac{\partial \ln f}{\partial T}\right)_P = -\dfrac{\bar{H}^{R}}{RT^{2}}$.
- ⭐ **Pressure dependence:** $\left(\dfrac{\partial \ln f}{\partial P}\right)_T = \dfrac{V}{RT}$.
- ⭐ **Always convert bar → Pa** when using SI units for $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$.
- ⭐ **Phase Equilibrium Condition:** $f_i^{\alpha} = f_i^{\beta}$ at constant $T$ and $P$.
- ⭐ **Sign convention for virial $B$:** Negative $B$ (attractive forces) → $\phi < 1$; Positive $B$ (repulsive) → $\phi > 1$.
- ⭐ **KTU favorite exam topics:** Poynting correction derivations, virial-based fugacity calculations, and comparing gas vs. liquid fugacity at saturation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Partial Molar Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT414 - BIOCHEMICAL THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Partial Molar Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Partial Molar Property ($\bar{M}_i$):** A partial molar property of component $i$ in a mixture is rigorously defined as the change in a given extensive thermodynamic property of the system per mole of component $i$ added, when temperature, pressure, and the moles of all other components are held constant.

Mathematically, for any extensive property $M$ (such as Volume $V$, Enthalpy $H$, Entropy $S$, Gibbs Energy $G$, etc.):

$$\bar{M}_i = \left( \frac{\partial M_{\text{total}}}{\partial n_i} \right)_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}}$$

Where:
- $M_{\text{total}}$ = Extensive property of the mixture (depends on system size)
- $\bar{M}_i$ = Partial molar property of component $i$ (an **intensive** property)
- $n_i$ = Number of moles of component $i$
- Subscript $n_{j \neq i}$ denotes that moles of all other components are held constant

The most commonly encountered partial molar property in biochemical engineering is the **partial molar volume** ($\bar{V}_i$), which represents the effective volumetric contribution of species $i$ to the total solution volume.

> [!NOTE]
> A special and critically important identity exists for the partial molar Gibbs energy:
>
> $$\bar{G}_i = \left( \frac{\partial G}{\partial n_i} \right)_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}} = \mu_i$$
>
> This establishes the link between partial molar properties and the **chemical potential** — the fundamental driver of mass transfer, reaction spontaneity, and phase equilibrium in biological systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stadium Crowd" Intuition

Imagine a packed cricket stadium in Thiruvananthapuram during an India–Australia T20 match.

- The **total noise level** is the *extensive property* — it scales with the number of people.
- Each individual fan's *isolated* shout is meaningless; what matters is their **effective contribution** when embedded in the cheering crowd.
- When you add one more fan, the change in total noise is **not** simply that fan's individual shout volume. The new fan gets "carried away" by the crowd, or alternatively dampens the noise by absorbing sound. This effective contribution is the **partial molar property**.

In a similar fashion, when you add one mole of ethanol to water:
- You **do not** simply add 58.4 cm³ (the pure molar volume of ethanol).
- Due to hydrogen bonding disruption and electrostriction, the *actual* volume change may be far less (around 56 cm³ in dilute solution).
- This *actual volumetric contribution* is the **partial molar volume of ethanol** in that mixture.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Anchor for KTU Exams:** Partial molar property = "How does the *whole* react when *one* changes?" — Just like asking how a symphony's mood shifts when one violinist changes their volume knob.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard state reference pressure:** $P^{\circ} = 1$ **bar** (= $10^5$ **Pa**)
- **Standard temperature for biochemical data:** $T = 298.15$ **K** (= $25^{\circ}$**C**)
- **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314$ **J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
- **Faraday's constant:** $F = 96485$ **C·mol⁻¹** (relevant for electrochemical bio-thermodynamics)

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation — The Total Differential Framework

Any extensive thermodynamic property $M$ of a multi-component system is a function of state variables:

$$M = M(T, P, n_1, n_2, \ldots, n_k)$$

The total differential is constructed by holding each independent variable constant in turn:

$$dM = \underbrace{\left( \frac{\partial M}{\partial T} \right)_{P, n} dT}_{\text{Thermal effect}} + \underbrace{\left( \frac{\partial M}{\partial P} \right)_{T, n} dP}_{\text{Pressure effect}} + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \underbrace{\left( \frac{\partial M}{\partial n_i} \right)_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}} dn_i}_{\text{Compositional effect}}$$

By definition, the compositional coefficient is the **partial molar property**:

$$\bar{M}_i = \left( \frac{\partial M}{\partial n_i} \right)_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}}$$

## 2.2 The Three Cardinal Rules of Partial Molar Properties

### Rule 1 — The Summability (Additivity) Rule
At fixed $T$ and $P$, integrating the total differential at constant composition yields:

$$M_{\text{total}}(T, P, n_1, n_2, \ldots, n_k) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i \, \bar{M}_i(T, P, x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_k)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Interpretation:** The total extensive property of a mixture equals the **mole-weighted sum** of the partial molar properties. This is *not* a mole-weighted sum of pure-component molar properties (which would only hold for ideal solutions).

### Rule 2 — Intensity and State-Function Nature
- $\bar{M}_i$ is an **intensive** property (independent of system size).
- It is a **state function**, depending only on $T$, $P$, and composition.
- It can be positive, negative, or zero depending on interactions (e.g., $\bar{V}_{\text{MgSO}_4}$ is negative in concentrated aqueous solution due to electrostriction).

### Rule 3 — The Gibbs–Duhem Constraint
For any closed system at constant $T$ and $P$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i \, d\bar{M}_i = 0$$

In terms of mole fractions ($x_i = n_i / n_{\text{total}}$), the **mole-fraction form** is:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i \, d\bar{M}_i = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Constraint"?** The Gibbs–Duhem equation states that the partial molar properties of all components are **not independent**. If you know the partial molar property of one component and the equation of state, you can compute the others. This is a powerful **engineering shortcut**.

## 2.3 Specific Partial Molar Properties of Biochemical Interest

| Symbol | Name | Defining Equation | Engineering/Bio Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\bar{V}_i$ | Partial Molar Volume | $\left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial n_i} \right)_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}}$ | Cell volume regulation, density of culture media, polymer packing |
| $\bar{U}_i$ | Partial Molar Internal Energy | $\left( \frac{\partial U}{\partial n_i} \right)_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}}$ | Energetics of solvation, calorimetric analyses |
| $\bar{H}_i$ | Partial Molar Enthalpy | $\left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial n_i} \right)_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}}$ | Heat of mixing in bioreactors, dilution enthalpies |
| $\bar{S}_i$ | Partial Molar Entropy | $\left( \frac{\partial S}{\partial n_i} \right)_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}}$ | Conformational entropy of biomolecules, mixing entropy |
| $\bar{G}_i = \mu_i$ | Partial Molar Gibbs Energy (Chemical Potential) | $\left( \frac{\partial G}{\partial n_i} \right)_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}}$ | Reaction spontaneity, membrane transport, phase equilibrium |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Context / Application | Units (SI) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\bar{M}_i = \left( \frac{\partial M}{\partial n_i} \right)_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}}$ | Universal definition of partial molar property | J·mol⁻¹, m³·mol⁻¹, etc. |
| 2 | $M = \sum_i n_i \bar{M}_i$ | Summability / Additivity rule | Same as $M$ |
| 3 | $\sum_i n_i \, d\bar{M}_i = 0$ (at const $T$, $P$) | Gibbs–Duhem equation (mole form) | Same as $\bar{M}_i$ |
| 4 | $\sum_i x_i \, d\bar{M}_i = 0$ (at const $T$, $P$) | Gibbs–Duhem equation (mole-fraction form) | Same as $\bar{M}_i$ |
| 5 | $\mu_i = \bar{G}_i = \bar{H}_i - T \bar{S}_i$ | Fundamental relation for chemical potential | J·mol⁻¹ |
| 6 | $\left( \frac{\partial \mu_i}{\partial P} \right)_{T, n} = \bar{V}_i$ | Pressure dependence of chemical potential | m³·mol⁻¹ |
| 7 | $\left( \frac{\partial \mu_i}{\partial T} \right)_{P, n} = -\bar{S}_i$ | Temperature dependence of chemical potential | J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ |
| 8 | $V_{\phi, 2} = \dfrac{V_{\text{soln}} - n_1 V_{m, 1}^{\circ}}{n_2}$ | Apparent molar volume of solute (component 2) | m³·mol⁻¹ |
| 9 | $\bar{V}_2 = V_{\phi, 2} + n_2 \left( \dfrac{\partial V_{\phi, 2}}{\partial n_2} \right)_{T, P, n_1}$ | Link between apparent and partial molar volume | m³·mol⁻¹ |
| 10 | $\bar{V}_i = V_m^{\circ} + \sum_j \left( n_j \dfrac{\partial V_m}{\partial n_i} \right)_{T, P, n_{k \neq i}}$ | Slope-intercept (tangent) method | m³·mol⁻¹ |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Biochemical Engineering

1. **Bioreactor Design:** Accurate volume calculations for culture media (LB broth, defined media) require partial molar volumes, as nutrient addition is **not** strictly additive.
2. **Downstream Processing:** Crystallization and precipitation yields depend on chemical-potential-driven supersaturation — a direct application of $\bar{G}_i$.
3. **Membrane Transport:** Passive diffusion, active transport, and osmosis are all governed by the gradient of $\mu_i$ across the membrane.
4. **Protein Stability & Solvation:** Partial molar volume changes upon protein unfolding reveal hydration-shell reorganization; partial molar heat capacity ($C_{p,\phi}$) quantifies hydrophobic effects.
5. **Formulation of Injectables / Pharmaceuticals:** Partial molar properties govern tonicity, osmolality, and pH-buffer capacity in IV formulations.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Summability Rule and the Gibbs–Duhem Equation

**Starting Point:** For a $k$-component system at equilibrium, the total differential of any extensive property $M$ is:

$$dM = \left( \frac{\partial M}{\partial T} \right)_{P, n} dT + \left( \frac{\partial M}{\partial P} \right)_{T, n} dP + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \bar{M}_i \, dn_i$$

**Step 1 — Restrict to constant $T$ and $P$:**

$$(dM)_{T, P} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \bar{M}_i \, dn_i$$

**Step 2 — Integrate along a path of constant composition** (so all $\bar{M}_i$ remain constant). Scale every $n_i$ by a factor $\lambda$ from 0 to 1:

$$\int_0^M dM' = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \bar{M}_i \int_0^{n_i} dn_i'$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integrals** (linear scaling since $\bar{M}_i$ is constant along the path):

$$M = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i \, \bar{M}_i$$

This is the **Summability Rule** — confirmed. ✓

**Step 4 — Now take the total differential of the result** (allowing $T$, $P$, and composition to vary):

$$dM = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i \, d\bar{M}_i + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \bar{M}_i \, dn_i$$

**Step 5 — Subtract the original total differential** (Step 0):

$$0 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i \, d\bar{M}_i \quad \text{(at constant } T, P\text{)}$$

**This is the Gibbs–Duhem Equation** — rigorously derived. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Division by $n_{\text{total}} = \sum n_i$** converts to the mole-fraction form:
>
> $$\sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i \, d\bar{M}_i = 0 \quad \text{(at constant } T, P\text{)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Partial Molar Volume from Volume–Composition Data (Slope-Intercept Method)

**Setup:** Consider a binary solution of components 1 (solvent) and 2 (solute). Let $n$ be the total moles, $x_1$ and $x_2$ the mole fractions, with $x_1 + x_2 = 1$.

**Step 1 — Express the mean molar volume:**

$$V_m = \frac{V_{\text{total}}}{n_{\text{total}}} = \frac{V_{\text{total}}}{n_1 + n_2}$$

So the total volume becomes:

$$V_{\text{total}} = (n_1 + n_2) \, V_m$$

**Step 2 — Apply the summability rule** to the volume:

$$V_{\text{total}} = n_1 \bar{V}_1 + n_2 \bar{V}_2$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for $V_{\text{total}}$:**

$$(n_1 + n_2) V_m = n_1 \bar{V}_1 + n_2 \bar{V}_2$$

**Step 4 — Divide through by $(n_1 + n_2)$** to obtain the mean molar volume in terms of mole fractions:

$$V_m = x_1 \bar{V}_1 + x_2 \bar{V}_2$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate $V_m$ with respect to $x_1$ at constant $T$ and $P$** (using $x_2 = 1 - x_1$, so $dx_2 = -dx_1$):

$$\frac{dV_m}{dx_1} = \bar{V}_1 + x_1 \frac{d\bar{V}_1}{dx_1} - \bar{V}_2 + (1 - x_1) \frac{d\bar{V}_2}{dx_1}$$

Wait — we need the **Gibbs–Duhem** constraint for the binary case: $x_1 d\bar{V}_1 + x_2 d\bar{V}_2 = 0$, which gives $x_1 \frac{d\bar{V}_1}{dx_1} + (1 - x_1) \frac{d\bar{V}_2}{dx_1} = 0$.

**Step 6 — Apply the constraint and simplify:**

$$\frac{dV_m}{dx_1} = \bar{V}_1 - \bar{V}_2$$

**Step 7 — Solve the simultaneous system:**

From Step 4: $\bar{V}_1 = (V_m - x_2 \bar{V}_2)/x_1$ — substitute into the slope equation:

$$\frac{dV_m}{dx_1} = \frac{V_m - x_2 \bar{V}_2}{x_1} - \bar{V}_2$$

Solving for $\bar{V}_1$ and $\bar{V}_2$:

$$\boxed{\bar{V}_1 = V_m + x_2 \frac{dV_m}{dx_2} = V_m - x_2 \frac{dV_m}{dx_1}}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{V}_2 = V_m - x_1 \frac{dV_m}{dx_1}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Geometric Interpretation:** Plot $V_m$ (y-axis) versus $x_1$ (x-axis). At a chosen composition $x_1$, draw the **tangent line** to the curve. The **y-intercept** (at $x_1 = 0$) is $\bar{V}_2$, and the **y-intercept** (at $x_1 = 1$) is $\bar{V}_1$. This is the famous **"Method of Intercepts"** by Lewis & Randall.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Apparent Molar Volume and Its Link to Partial Molar Volume

**Setup:** For a binary solution, define the apparent molar volume of solute (component 2):

$$V_{\phi, 2} = \frac{V_{\text{total}} - n_1 V_{m, 1}^{\circ}}{n_2}$$

where $V_{m, 1}^{\circ}$ is the **molar volume of pure component 1**.

**Step 1 — Express the total volume using the summability rule:**

$$V_{\text{total}} = n_1 \bar{V}_1 + n_2 \bar{V}_2$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for the solute contribution:**

$$V_{\text{total}} - n_1 \bar{V}_1 = n_2 \bar{V}_2$$

So:

$$V_{\phi, 2} = \frac{n_1 (\bar{V}_1 - V_{m, 1}^{\circ})}{n_2} + \bar{V}_2$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate $V_{\phi, 2}$ with respect to $n_2$ at constant $T$, $P$, and $n_1$:**

$$\left( \frac{\partial V_{\phi, 2}}{\partial n_2} \right)_{T, P, n_1} = \frac{n_1}{n_2} \left( \frac{\partial \bar{V}_1}{\partial n_2} \right)_{T, P, n_1} + \left( \frac{\partial \bar{V}_2}{\partial n_2} \right)_{T, P, n_1}$$

**Step 4 — Use the Gibbs–Duhem equation** $n_1 d\bar{V}_1 + n_2 d\bar{V}_2 = 0$ at constant $T$, $P$:

$$n_1 \left( \frac{\partial \bar{V}_1}{\partial n_2} \right)_{T, P, n_1} = -n_2 \left( \frac{\partial \bar{V}_2}{\partial n_2} \right)_{T, P, n_1}$$

Substituting:

$$\left( \frac{\partial V_{\phi, 2}}{\partial n_2} \right)_{T, P, n_1} = -\left( \frac{\partial \bar{V}_2}{\partial n_2} \right)_{T, P, n_1} + \left( \frac{\partial \bar{V}_2}{\partial n_2} \right)_{T, P, n_1} = 0$$

Wait — that simplification requires a closer look. Let's re-derive more carefully:

Starting from $V_{\text{total}} = n_1 \bar{V}_1 + n_2 \bar{V}_2$ and the definition $V_{\phi, 2} = (V_{\text{total}} - n_1 V_{m,1}^{\circ})/n_2$:

$$V_{\text{total}} = n_1 V_{m, 1}^{\circ} + n_2 V_{\phi, 2}$$

Differentiate at constant $T$, $P$, $n_1$:

$$\left( \frac{\partial V_{\text{total}}}{\partial n_2} \right)_{T, P, n_1} = \bar{V}_2 = V_{\phi, 2} + n_2 \left( \frac{\partial V_{\phi, 2}}{\partial n_2} \right)_{T, P, n_1}$$

**The final link** (the relation we sought):

$$\boxed{\bar{V}_2 = V_{\phi, 2} + n_2 \left( \frac{\partial V_{\phi, 2}}{\partial n_2} \right)_{T, P, n_1}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the name "Apparent"?** $V_{\phi, 2}$ treats the solute *as if* it occupied its own pure-component molar volume in the solvent; the **correction term** $n_2 (\partial V_{\phi, 2} / \partial n_2)$ accounts for solute–solute interactions and the solvent's structural response. This is the experimentalist's workhorse relation.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A binary solution contains $n_1 = 2$ mol water and $n_2 = 1$ mol ethanol. The mean molar volume of the solution is observed to be $V_m = 24.0$ cm³·mol⁻¹, and the slope of the $V_m$ vs $x_1$ curve at this composition is $dV_m/dx_1 = -8.0$ cm³·mol⁻¹. Pure water has $V_{m, 1}^{\circ} = 18.07$ cm³·mol⁻¹. Find the partial molar volumes and the apparent molar volume of ethanol.

**Step 1 — Compute the mole fractions:**

$$x_1 = \frac{2}{3} = 0.6667, \quad x_2 = \frac{1}{3} = 0.3333$$

**Step 2 — Use the slope-intercept formula:**

$$\bar{V}_1 = V_m - x_2 \frac{dV_m}{dx_1} = 24.0 - (0.3333)(-8.0) = 24.0 + 2.667 = 26.67 \text{ cm}^3 \cdot \text{mol}^{-1}$$

$$\bar{V}_2 = V_m - x_1 \frac{dV_m}{dx_1} = 24.0 - (0.6667)(-8.0) = 24.0 + 5.333 = 29.33 \text{ cm}^3 \cdot \text{mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the apparent molar volume of ethanol:**

$$V_{\phi, 2} = \frac{n_1 \bar{V}_1 + n_2 \bar{V}_2 - n_1 V_{m,1}^{\circ}}{n_2} = \frac{(2)(26.67) + (1)(29.33) - (2)(18.07)}{1}$$

$$V_{\phi, 2} = 53.34 + 29.33 - 36.14 = 46.53 \text{ cm}^3 \cdot \text{mol}^{-1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** Pure ethanol's molar volume $\approx 58.4$ cm³·mol⁻¹. The apparent value (46.53) is *lower* due to water structuring around ethanol's OH group — physically reasonable. ✓

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define partial molar property. Derive the mathematical expression for partial molar volume.**

**Model Answer:**

Partial molar property $\bar{M}_i$ of a component $i$ in a mixture is defined as the rate of change of the total extensive property $M$ of the system with respect to the number of moles of component $i$, holding temperature $T$, pressure $P$, and moles of all other components $n_{j \neq i}$ constant:

$$\bar{M}_i = \left( \frac{\partial M}{\partial n_i} \right)_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}}$$

For volume, $M = V$, so:

$$\bar{V}_i = \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial n_i} \right)_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}}$$

It represents the effective volumetric contribution of one mole of component $i$ to the total volume of the mixture at the specified $T$, $P$, and composition. The units are m³·mol⁻¹ (or cm³·mol⁻¹ in CGS). **[3 marks]**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and explain the physical significance of the Gibbs–Duhem equation.**

**Model Answer:**

The Gibbs–Duhem equation for a $k$-component system at constant $T$ and $P$ is:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i \, d\bar{M}_i = 0 \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i \, d\bar{M}_i = 0$$

**Physical Significance:**

1. **Interdependence constraint:** The partial molar properties of all components in a mixture are *not* independent — a change in one is compensated by changes in the others such that the weighted sum vanishes.
2. **Determinism of mixture behavior:** If the partial molar property of $(k-1)$ components is known as a function of composition, the partial molar property of the $k^{\text{th}}$ component can be calculated.
3. **Inaccessible-state filter:** It shows that we cannot independently vary the chemical potential of all components — a foundational principle in phase-rule and reaction-equilibrium theory.
4. **Engineering utility:** Used in vapor–liquid equilibrium calculations (e.g., Wilson, NRTL, UNIQUAC models) to relate activity coefficients of components. **[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Forgetting to specify constant $T$, $P$ in partial derivatives → **−1 mark**
> - Confusing partial molar property with pure-component molar property (e.g., writing $\bar{V}_i = V_{m, i}^{\circ}$ in non-ideal mixtures) → **−2 marks**
> - Mixing up $\bar{V}_1$ and $\bar{V}_2$ in the Method of Intercepts (right intercept = component 1, left intercept = component 2) → **−2 marks**
> - Dropping the Gibbs–Duhem constraint while deriving the slope equation → **−1 to 2 marks**
> - Using mean molar volume $V_m$ in place of partial molar volume in the sum rule → **−2 marks**

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the Gibbs–Duhem equation starting from the fundamental definition of partial molar property.**

**Model Solution:**

Let $M$ be an extensive property of a $k$-component system at equilibrium, with $M = M(T, P, n_1, n_2, \ldots, n_k)$.

**Step 1: Write the total differential:**

$$dM = \left( \frac{\partial M}{\partial T} \right)_{P, n} dT + \left( \frac{\partial M}{\partial P} \right)_{T, n} dP + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \bar{M}_i \, dn_i \quad \text{...(1)}$$

where $\bar{M}_i = (\partial M / \partial n_i)_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}}$.

**Step 2: Restrict to constant $T$ and $P$:**

$$(dM)_{T, P} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \bar{M}_i \, dn_i \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 3: Integrate along a path of constant composition** (so each $\bar{M}_i$ is constant, and we scale all $n_i$ by the same factor from 0 to 1):

$$\int_0^M dM' = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \bar{M}_i \int_0^{n_i} dn_i' \quad \Rightarrow \quad M = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i \bar{M}_i \quad \text{...(3)}$$

**Step 4: Differentiate Equation (3)** allowing $T$, $P$, and composition to vary:

$$dM = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i \, d\bar{M}_i + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \bar{M}_i \, dn_i + \left( \frac{\partial M}{\partial T} \right)_{P, n} dT + \left( \frac{\partial M}{\partial P} \right)_{T, n} dP \quad \text{...(4)}$$

**Step 5: Subtract Equation (1) from Equation (4):**

$$0 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i \, d\bar{M}_i + 0 \quad \text{(at constant } T, P\text{)}$$

$$\boxed{\sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i \, d\bar{M}_i = 0 \quad \text{at constant } T, P}$$

This is the **Gibbs–Duhem equation**. Dividing by $n_{\text{total}} = \sum n_i$ yields the mole-fraction form: $\sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i \, d\bar{M}_i = 0$. **[7 marks]**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**For a binary solution of acetone(1) and chloroform(2) at 298 K, the total volume is given empirically as $V = 50.0 + 30.0\,x_1 - 8.0\,x_1^2$ cm³ (where $x_1$ is the mole fraction of acetone, and the total moles $n = 1$ mol for the standard basis). Determine the partial molar volumes of both components at $x_1 = 0.5$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the mean molar volume** (since $n_{\text{total}} = 1$ mol, $V_m = V$):

$$V_m = 50.0 + 30.0\,x_1 - 8.0\,x_1^2$$

**Step 2: Compute the slope** $dV_m / dx_1$:

$$\frac{dV_m}{dx_1} = 30.0 - 16.0\,x_1$$

**Step 3: Evaluate at $x_1 = 0.5$:**

$$V_m(0.5) = 50.0 + 30.0(0.5) - 8.0(0.5)^2 = 50.0 + 15.0 - 2.0 = 63.0 \text{ cm}^3 \cdot \text{mol}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{dV_m}{dx_1}\bigg|_{0.5} = 30.0 - 16.0(0.5) = 30.0 - 8.0 = 22.0 \text{ cm}^3 \cdot \text{mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 4: Apply the Method of Intercepts formulas:**

$$\bar{V}_1 = V_m - x_2 \frac{dV_m}{dx_1} = 63.0 - (0.5)(22.0) = 63.0 - 11.0 = 52.0 \text{ cm}^3 \cdot \text{mol}^{-1}$$

$$\bar{V}_2 = V_m - x_1 \frac{dV_m}{dx_1} = 63.0 - (0.5)(22.0) = 63.0 - 11.0 = 52.0 \text{ cm}^3 \cdot \text{mol}^{-1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** At $x_1 = 0.5$, by symmetry of the quadratic expression, the partial molar volumes are equal — a direct consequence of the chosen composition. ✓

**Verification via Summability Rule:**

$$x_1 \bar{V}_1 + x_2 \bar{V}_2 = (0.5)(52.0) + (0.5)(52.0) = 52.0 \text{ cm}^3 \cdot \text{mol}^{-1}$$

Hmm — this doesn't match $V_m = 63.0$! Let me re-examine the intercept formulas.

**Corrected approach (using the proper intercept identities):** The relation is $V_m = x_1 \bar{V}_1 + x_2 \bar{V}_2$ (always true) and the slope relation is $dV_m/dx_1 = \bar{V}_1 - \bar{V}_2$ (when Gibbs–Duhem holds). So we have the system:

$$V_m = x_1 \bar{V}_1 + (1-x_1)\bar{V}_2, \quad \frac{dV_m}{dx_1} = \bar{V}_1 - \bar{V}_2$$

Solving simultaneously: $\bar{V}_1 = V_m + (1-x_1) \frac{dV_m}{dx_1}$ and $\bar{V}_2 = V_m - x_1 \frac{dV_m}{dx_1}$.

**Re-evaluate at $x_1 = 0.5$:**

$$\bar{V}_1 = 63.0 + (0.5)(22.0) = 63.0 + 11.0 = 74.0 \text{ cm}^3 \cdot \text{mol}^{-1}$$

$$\bar{V}_2 = 63.0 - (0.5)(22.0) = 63.0 - 11.0 = 52.0 \text{ cm}^3 \cdot \text{mol}^{-1}$$

**Summability check:**

$$x_1 \bar{V}_1 + x_2 \bar{V}_2 = (0.5)(74.0) + (0.5)(52.0) = 37.0 + 26.0 = 63.0 \text{ cm}^3 \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} = V_m \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answer:** $\bar{V}_1 = 74.0$ cm³·mol⁻¹ (acetone), $\bar{V}_2 = 52.0$ cm³·mol⁻¹ (chloroform). **[7 marks]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Set]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the apparent molar volume concept. Derive the relationship between apparent molar volume and partial molar volume of the solute in a binary solution.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** For a binary solution (solvent = 1, solute = 2), the apparent molar volume of the solute is defined as:

$$V_{\phi, 2} = \frac{V_{\text{total}} - n_1 V_{m, 1}^{\circ}}{n_2}$$

where $V_{m, 1}^{\circ}$ is the molar volume of *pure* solvent and $V_{\text{total}}$ is the observed total volume of the solution.

**Physical Meaning:** It represents the volume that one mole of solute *appears* to occupy in the solution, *if* the solvent retained its pure-state molar volume. The deviation of $V_{\phi, 2}$ from the true partial molar volume $\bar{V}_2$ encodes solute–solute interactions and solvent structural rearrangement.

**Derivation of the Link:**

**Step 1:** Apply the summability rule for total volume:

$$V_{\text{total}} = n_1 \bar{V}_1 + n_2 \bar{V}_2 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the apparent molar volume definition:

$$V_{\phi, 2} = \frac{n_1 \bar{V}_1 + n_2 \bar{V}_2 - n_1 V_{m, 1}^{\circ}}{n_2} = \frac{n_1 (\bar{V}_1 - V_{m, 1}^{\circ})}{n_2} + \bar{V}_2 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate Equation (ii) with respect to $n_2$ at constant $T$, $P$, $n_1$:

$$\left( \frac{\partial V_{\phi, 2}}{\partial n_2} \right)_{T, P, n_1} = \frac{n_1}{n_2} \left( \frac{\partial \bar{V}_1}{\partial n_2} \right)_{T, P, n_1} + \left( \frac{\partial \bar{V}_2}{\partial n_2} \right)_{T, P, n_1} \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the Gibbs–Duhem equation for binary volume: $n_1 d\bar{V}_1 + n_2 d\bar{V}_2 = 0$ at constant $T$, $P$. Thus:

$$n_1 \left( \frac{\partial \bar{V}_1}{\partial n_2} \right)_{T, P, n_1} = -n_2 \left( \frac{\partial \bar{V}_2}{\partial n_2} \right)_{T, P, n_1} \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute (iv) into (iii):

$$\left( \frac{\partial V_{\phi, 2}}{\partial n_2} \right)_{T, P, n_1} = -\left( \frac{\partial \bar{V}_2}{\partial n_2} \right)_{T, P, n_1} + \left( \frac{\partial \bar{V}_2}{\partial n_2} \right)_{T, P, n_1} = 0 \quad \text{...(v)}$$

**Step 6:** Wait — this cancellation requires a more careful path. Re-derive directly: Differentiate (i) at constant $T$, $P$, $n_1$:

$$\left( \frac{\partial V_{\text{total}}}{\partial n_2} \right)_{T, P, n_1} = \bar{V}_2 = V_{\phi, 2} + n_2 \left( \frac{\partial V_{\phi, 2}}{\partial n_2} \right)_{T, P, n_1} \quad \text{...(vi)}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\bar{V}_2 = V_{\phi, 2} + n_2 \left( \frac{\partial V_{\phi, 2}}{\partial n_2} \right)_{T, P, n_1}}$$

This is the **key operational relation**: experimentally measure $V_{\phi, 2}$ at various $n_2$, plot vs $n_2$, take the tangent slope at the desired $n_2$, and compute $\bar{V}_2$. **[7 marks]**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the significance of partial molar properties in biochemical engineering with at least four specific examples.**

**Model Answer:**

Partial molar properties are the *lingua franca* of multi-component thermodynamic analysis. In biochemical engineering, where reaction mixtures contain water, sugars, salts, proteins, lipids, and metabolites, they are indispensable. **[7 marks]**

**1. Bioreactor Volume and Media Design:**
The total working volume of a stirred-tank bioreactor is not the sum of pure-component volumes. Adding 10 g/L glucose to water contributes a *partial molar volume* of glucose (≈ 112 cm³/mol at infinite dilution) that depends on concentration due to glucose–glucose and glucose–water interactions. Accurate partial molar volume data are essential for **volumetric yield calculations**, **fed-batch feeding strategies**, and **scale-up**.

**2. Membrane Transport and Osmosis:**
The driving force for passive diffusion of oxygen, nutrients, or drugs across a cell membrane is the gradient of **chemical potential** $\mu_i = \bar{G}_i$. The Nernst–Planck equation and the Kedem–Katchalsky equations for membrane transport both invoke partial molar quantities. Without these, one cannot predict whether a metabolite will accumulate inside a cell or efflux out.

**3. Protein Folding and Solvation Thermodynamics:**
The partial molar heat capacity $\bar{C}_{p, \text{protein}}$ and partial molar volume $\bar{V}_{\text{protein}}$ change sharply upon protein unfolding. The *negative* $\Delta \bar{V}$ upon folding reflects the collapse of hydrophobic hydration shells. **Pressure-jump and temperature-jump experiments** rely on these partial molar quantities to extract volumetric and enthalpic contributions to protein stability.

**4. Crystallization and Precipitation:**
Supersaturation — the thermodynamic driver of protein crystallization, antibody precipitation, and biomineralization — is governed by differences in **chemical potential** of the solute between the solution and the solid phase:

$$\Delta \mu_2 = \mu_2^{\text{solution}} - \mu_2^{\text{solid}} = RT \ln a_2^{\text{solution}}$$

Controlling $\bar{G}_2$ via pH, ionic strength, and precipitant concentration is the bedrock of downstream bioprocessing.

**5. Fermentation Energetics and Metabolic Flux:**
Apparent molar enthalpies of substrates and products enable **energy-balance closure** in fermentation processes. The heat evolved per mole of biomass formed is computed from partial molar enthalpies of glucose consumption and ethanol/lactate production — a direct application of Hess's law generalized to mixtures.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Treating biological mixtures as ideal (using pure-component molar properties instead of partial molar properties) can introduce **5–15% errors** in volume, enthalpy, and free-energy balances. This is often enough to mis-design a bioreactor or mis-predict a crystallization yield.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Partial Molar Properties**

- [x] **Universal Definition:** $\bar{M}_i = (\partial M / \partial n_i)_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}}$ — always state the constant variables explicitly in the subscript.
- [x] **Intensive Nature:** $\bar{M}_i$ is intensive; $M$ itself is extensive.
- [x] **Summability Rule:** $M = \sum_i n_i \bar{M}_i$ — the *defining identity* of mixture thermodynamics.
- [x] **Chemical Potential Identity:** $\mu_i \equiv \bar{G}_i = (\partial G / \partial n_i)_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}}$ — the gateway to all equilibrium theory.
- [x] **Gibbs–Duhem Equation:** $\sum_i x_i d\bar{M}_i = 0$ at constant $T$, $P$ — the constraint that ties all $\bar{M}_i$ together.
- [x] **Method of Intercepts (Binary):** $\bar{V}_1 = V_m + x_2 (dV_m/dx_1)$ and $\bar{V}_2 = V_m - x_1 (dV_m/dx_1)$ — the *right intercept* (at $x_1 = 1$) gives $\bar{V}_1$, the *left intercept* (at $x_1 = 0$) gives $\bar{V}_2$.
- [x] **Apparent → Partial:** $\bar{V}_2 = V_{\phi, 2} + n_2 (\partial V_{\phi, 2} / \partial n_2)_{T, P, n_1}$ — the experimentalist's workhorse equation.
- [x] **Pressure dependence of $\mu_i$:** $(\partial \mu_i / \partial P)_{T, n} = \bar{V}_i$.
- [x] **Temperature dependence of $\mu_i$:** $(\partial \mu_i / \partial T)_{P, n} = -\bar{S}_i$.
- [x] **Ideal-solution limit:** $\bar{M}_i = M_{m, i}^{\circ}$ (pure-component molar property) — true *only* for ideal mixtures; biochemical systems are almost never ideal.
- [x] **Units Vigilance:** $\bar{V}_i$ in m³·mol⁻¹ or cm³·mol⁻¹; $\bar{H}_i, \bar{G}_i$ in J·mol⁻¹; $\bar{S}_i$ in J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹; $\bar{C}_{p, i}$ in J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹.
- [x] **Sign Convention:** $\bar{V}_i$ can be **negative** (e.g., for ions with strong electrostriction — Mg²⁺, SO₄²⁻ in water).
- [x] **Biochemical Hot-Spots:** Partial molar volume of water, ethanol, glucose, urea, salts; chemical potentials of O₂, CO₂, H⁺, glucose in metabolism.
- [x] **Most-tested relations in KTU ESE:** (1) Derive Gibbs–Duhem, (2) Method of Intercepts numerical, (3) Apparent ↔ Partial link, (4) Sign and units of partial molar volume in ionic solutions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chemical Potential
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT414 - BIOCHEMICAL THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Chemical Potential

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chemical Potential (μᵢ)** of the *i*-th component in a thermodynamic system is formally defined as the **rate of change of the Gibbs free energy (G)** of the system with respect to the number of moles (nᵢ) of that component, while keeping **temperature (T)**, **pressure (P)**, and the moles of all other components (nⱼ, j ≠ i) constant.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$
\mu_i = \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial n_i}\right)_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}}
$$

This makes chemical potential a **partial molar quantity** — specifically, the **partial molar Gibbs free energy**. The general partial molar property $\bar{M_i}$ of any extensive thermodynamic property $M$ is defined analogously, and chemical potential is the partial molar version of $G$.

### Why is it called "potential"?

The term *potential* is chosen deliberately, by direct analogy to gravitational, electrical, or mechanical potentials. Just as water flows from a region of high gravitational potential to low gravitational potential, and electric charge flows from high electrical potential to low electrical potential, **matter spontaneously migrates from a region of high chemical potential to a region of low chemical potential**. It is the *driving force* for mass transport, phase change, and chemical reaction.

### Sign Convention of Driving Force

For any spontaneous process at constant T and P:

$$
\sum_i \mu_i \, dn_i < 0
$$

This inequality is the heart of chemical thermodynamics and is the foundation of the criteria for **physical equilibrium**, **phase equilibrium**, and **chemical equilibrium** in biochemical systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Water-Tank" Analogy for Chemical Potential**

Imagine a city built on a hillside with two large water tanks, A and B, at the same temperature (T) and pressure (P). Both tanks contain water (a pure substance) but tank A is on a higher floor than tank B. The water in tank A has a *higher gravitational potential* per unit mass than tank B.

When the connecting valve is opened, water **spontaneously flows from A → B**, even though both contain the same chemical species (H₂O). No external work is required; in fact, work could be extracted as the water falls.

Now extend this to chemistry:

- Replace "height" with **chemical potential (μ)**.
- Replace "water molecules" with **molecules of species i** (e.g., glucose, ATP, Na⁺).
- Replace "two tanks" with **two compartments** (e.g., inside a cell vs. outside, or reactant vs. product side of a biochemical reaction).

**Molecules flow from regions of high chemical potential to regions of low chemical potential — spontaneously.** Equilibrium is reached when μ is uniform everywhere (just as water levels equalize between two connected tanks).

### Real-World Biochemical Examples

| Biological Process | Driving Force (Δμ) |
|---|---|
| Diffusion of O₂ from lungs to blood | μ(O₂, lung) > μ(O₂, blood) |
| ATP hydrolysis powering muscle contraction | μ(ATP) > μ(ADP + Pᵢ) |
| Active transport of Na⁺/K⁺ across membranes | Electrochemical potential gradient |
| Substrate binding to enzyme active site | Difference in μ between bound and free states |

## 1.3 Relationship to the Fundamental Thermodynamic Equation

For a multi-component, multi-phase system, the fundamental equation of thermodynamics takes the form:

$$
dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \mu_i \, dn_i
$$

This equation makes explicit the three "natural variables" of G: T, P, and the mole numbers nᵢ. Each term has units of energy: **Joules (J)** or **calories (cal)** in CGS, with the standard SI unit of energy.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard-State Reference Values Used Throughout KTU Problems:**
> - **Standard pressure:** P° = **1 bar** = 10⁵ Pa (since 1990 IUPAC recommendation)
> - **Standard temperature:** T° = **298.15 K** = 25 °C
> - **Universal gas constant:** R = **8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹** = 1.987 cal mol⁻¹ K⁻¹
> - **Faraday constant:** F = **96,485 C mol⁻¹** = 96.485 kJ mol⁻¹ V⁻¹
> - **Boltzmann constant:** k_B = **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J K⁻¹**

These constants are **bolded** as they are universally used in the formula sheet that follows.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Chemical Potential Concept

### Step 1 — The Definition Re-examined

Starting from the total differential of G for a single-component system:

$$
dG = \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P,n} dT + \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial P}\right)_{T,n} dP
$$

For a multi-component system, the chain rule expands this to include mole number variations:

$$
dG = \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P,n} dT + \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial P}\right)_{T,n} dP + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial n_i}\right)_{T,P,n_{j \neq i}} dn_i
$$

The third term coefficient, by definition, is μᵢ.

### Step 2 — Why Partial, Not Total Derivative?

If you used the total derivative (∂G/∂nᵢ), the result would not be a state function of the *i*-th component alone — it would be contaminated by changes in other components. The partial derivative isolates the **specific contribution** of component *i* while all other variables are held fixed. This is essential for describing mixtures where interactions between components matter.

### Step 3 — Pressure Dependence of μ

From the fundamental relation:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial \mu_i}{\partial P}\right)_{T, n} = \left(\frac{\partial^2 G}{\partial P \, \partial n_i}\right) = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial n_i}\right)_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}} = \bar{V_i}
$$

where $\bar{V_i}$ is the **partial molar volume**. Integrating at constant T and composition:

$$
\mu_i(T, P) = \mu_i^\circ(T, P^\circ) + \int_{P^\circ}^{P} \bar{V_i}\, dP
$$

For an incompressible liquid or solid (or a dilute biochemical solution), $\bar{V_i}$ is approximately constant:

$$
\mu_i(T, P) \approx \mu_i^\circ + \bar{V_i}(P - P^\circ)
$$

This shows μ increases with pressure — high-pressure environments (e.g., deep-sea organisms) shift chemical potentials significantly.

### Step 4 — Temperature Dependence of μ

Similarly, from the fundamental relation:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial \mu_i}{\partial T}\right)_{P, n} = -\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial n_i}\right)_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}} = -\bar{S_i}
$$

At constant T and P, integrating from T° to T (with reference μ° at T°):

$$
\frac{\mu_i(T)}{T} = \frac{\mu_i^\circ}{T^\circ} - \bar{S_i}^\circ \ln\!\left(\frac{T}{T^\circ}\right) - \int_{T^\circ}^{T} \frac{1}{T} \int_{T^\circ}^{T} \frac{\bar{C}_{P,i}}{T}\, dT\, dT
$$

For most biochemical KTU problems, a simpler linearized form suffices:

$$
\mu_i(T) \approx \mu_i^\circ - \bar{S_i}^\circ (T - T^\circ)
$$

---

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table. Every KTU question on Module 3 will draw from these expressions.**

| # | Formula | Expression | Typical Use / Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Partial molar Gibbs energy (definition) | $\mu_i = \left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial n_i}\right)_{T,P,n_{j \neq i}}$ | Foundational definition |
| 2 | Fundamental equation of G | $dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP + \sum_i \mu_i\, dn_i$ | Multi-component open system |
| 3 | Pressure dependence | $d\mu_i = \bar{V_i}\, dP$ at constant T | High-pressure biochemistry |
| 4 | Temperature dependence | $d\mu_i = -\bar{S_i}\, dT$ at constant P | Thermophile biology |
| 5 | Ideal pure gas | $\mu(T,P) = \mu^\circ(T) + RT \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P^\circ}\right)$ | Pure-gas reference state |
| 6 | Ideal gas mixture | $\mu_i = \mu_i^\circ + RT \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_i}{P^\circ}\right) = \mu_i^\circ + RT \ln(y_i P / P^\circ)$ | Dalton's law + Raoult's limit |
| 7 | Real gas (fugacity form) | $\mu_i = \mu_i^\circ + RT \ln\!\left(\dfrac{f_i}{f^\circ}\right) = \mu_i^\circ + RT \ln(\phi_i y_i P / P^\circ)$ | High-pressure deviations |
| 8 | Fugacity coefficient | $\phi_i = f_i / (y_i P)$ | Measure of non-ideality |
| 9 | Ideal-dilute (Henry's law) solution | $\mu_i = \mu_i^{*} + RT \ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_i}{C^\circ}\right)$ | Solute at low concentration |
| 10 | Ideal solution (Raoult's law) | $\mu_i = \mu_i^{*} + RT \ln(x_i)$ | Solvent and solute both obey |
| 11 | Real solution (activity) | $\mu_i = \mu_i^\circ + RT \ln(a_i)$ | General non-ideal case |
| 12 | Activity coefficient | $\gamma_i = a_i / x_i$ (Raoult basis) or $\gamma_i = a_i / (C_i / C^\circ)$ (Henry basis) | Quantifies deviation from ideality |
| 13 | Electrochemical potential | $\tilde{\mu}_i = \mu_i + z_i F \phi$ | Ions in electric field (membrane transport) |
| 14 | Gibbs–Duhem equation | $\sum_i n_i\, d\mu_i = -S\,dT + V\,dP$ at constant composition changes | Relates changes in all μᵢ |
| 15 | Reaction Gibbs energy | $\Delta_r G = \sum_i \nu_i \mu_i$ | Drives biochemical reaction |
| 16 | Equilibrium condition | $\Delta_r G = 0 \Leftrightarrow \prod_i a_i^{\nu_i} = K$ | Standard condition at equilibrium |
| 17 | Equilibrium constant relation | $\Delta_r G^\circ = -RT \ln K$ | Connects K to standard free energy |
| 18 | Osmotic pressure (van't Hoff) | $\pi = RT C$ (ideal-dilute) | Colloid/biochemical solutions |

**Note on notation:** $\mu_i^\circ$ is the standard chemical potential (pure substance at T°, P°); $\mu_i^{*}$ is the Henry's-law reference (hypothetical pure solute with infinite-dilution behavior); $z_i$ is the charge number of an ion; $\phi$ is the electric potential (in volts); $x_i$ is the mole fraction.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Biochemical Utility

| Domain | Why Chemical Potential Matters |
|---|---|
| **Pharmaceutical industry** | Drug solubility, partition coefficients (log P), receptor binding — all governed by Δμ between phases |
| **Bioprocess engineering** | Fermentation yields depend on how Δμ of substrate/glucose drives microbial growth |
| **Membrane separation** | Reverse osmosis, dialysis, ultrafiltration rely on chemical potential gradients |
| **Bioprocess scale-up** | Reactor design requires knowing how μ of substrates/products changes with concentration |
| **Medical devices** | Hemodialysis machines exploit concentration gradients (chemical potential differences) of urea/creatinine |
| **Food preservation** | Osmotic pressure of high-salt/sugar brines dehydrates microbial cells by reducing their water μ |
| **Enzyme kinetics** | The driving force ΔG‡ (activation) and ΔᵣG (reaction) determine reaction rates in metabolic pathways |
| **Cell biology** | ATP synthesis by ATP-synthase is driven by the proton electrochemical potential (proton-motive force) |

---

## 2.4 The Gibbs–Duhem Equation — A Critical Constraint

The Gibbs–Duhem equation is a **constraint** that any thermodynamic system must satisfy. It states that the chemical potentials of components in a mixture are not all independent — if you change one, the others must adjust in a coupled way.

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i\, d\mu_i = -S\,dT + V\,dP
$$

At constant T and P:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i\, d\mu_i = 0 \quad \text{(isothermal, isobaric)}
$$

For a binary solution, this becomes:

$$
x_1\, d\mu_1 + x_2\, d\mu_2 = 0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Biological significance:** If you increase the chemical potential of one species in a cell (e.g., glucose uptake), the chemical potentials of all other species **must** change to compensate. This is why the cell spends energy (ATP) to maintain non-equilibrium steady states.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Chemical Potential of an Ideal Gas

**Starting point:** For a pure ideal gas at constant T, the molar Gibbs energy G_m depends only on P. We have:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial G_m}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = V_m = \frac{RT}{P}
$$

where the ideal gas law $PV_m = RT$ has been used.

**Step 1 — Set up the integral.**

To find G_m(P), integrate from the standard pressure P° to the final pressure P at constant T:

$$
\int_{G_m^\circ}^{G_m} dG_m = \int_{P^\circ}^{P} \frac{RT}{P'}\, dP'
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the right-hand integral.**

Since R and T are constant, they factor out:

$$
\int_{P^\circ}^{P} \frac{RT}{P'}\, dP' = RT \int_{P^\circ}^{P} \frac{dP'}{P'} = RT \left[\ln P'\right]_{P^\circ}^{P} = RT \ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P^\circ}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Assemble the result.**

$$
G_m(T,P) = G_m^\circ(T) + RT \ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P^\circ}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Identify chemical potential with molar Gibbs energy.**

For a pure substance, μ = G_m. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\mu(T,P) = \mu^\circ(T) + RT \ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P^\circ}\right)}
$$

This is the **single most important equation for KTU Module 3**, and it forms the template for all other forms below.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Chemical Potential of an Ideal Gas in a Mixture

For component *i* in an ideal-gas mixture, the **partial pressure** P_i replaces the total pressure P. By Dalton's law:

$$
P_i = y_i P
$$

Substituting into the pure-gas result:

$$
\mu_i(T, P) = \mu_i^\circ(T) + RT \ln\!\left(\frac{y_i P}{P^\circ}\right)
$$

For a mixture, the standard state μ_i° is defined as the pure component *i* at T° and P°, behaving as an ideal gas.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Chemical Potential of a Real Gas (Fugacity Form)

**Motivation:** Real gases deviate from ideality at high P. We retain the **mathematical form** of the ideal-gas expression but replace pressure P with **fugacity f**, which has units of pressure:

$$
\mu_i^{\text{real}} = \mu_i^\circ + RT \ln\!\left(\frac{f_i}{f^\circ}\right)
$$

**Definition of fugacity** (Lewis, 1901) — chosen so that, as P → 0, f/P → 1 (ideal-gas limit):

$$
\lim_{P \to 0} \frac{f_i}{P_i} = 1
$$

**Fugacity coefficient:**

$$
\phi_i = \frac{f_i}{P_i} = \frac{f_i}{y_i P}
$$

For an ideal gas, φᵢ = 1. For a real gas, φᵢ ≠ 1, and its value depends on the equation of state.

**Temperature dependence of fugacity:**

$$
\left(\frac{\partial \ln f_i}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = \frac{\bar{H_i}^\circ - \bar{H_i}}{RT^2} = -\frac{\Delta \bar{H}_{\text{dep}}}{RT^2}
$$

where $\Delta \bar{H}_{\text{dep}}$ is the molar enthalpy of departure from ideality (zero for an ideal gas).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Chemical Potential in an Ideal Solution (Raoult's Law)

**Starting point:** Raoult's law states that the partial pressure of component *i* above an ideal solution is:

$$
P_i = x_i P_i^{\text{pure}}(T)
$$

The chemical potential of the vapor in equilibrium with the solution (assumed ideal gas) is:

$$
\mu_i^{\text{vap}} = \mu_i^{\circ,\text{vap}} + RT \ln\!\left(\frac{P_i}{P^\circ}\right)
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute Raoult's law.**

$$
\mu_i^{\text{vap}} = \mu_i^{\circ,\text{vap}} + RT \ln\!\left(\frac{x_i P_i^{\text{pure}}}{P^\circ}\right)
$$

$$
\mu_i^{\text{vap}} = \mu_i^{\circ,\text{vap}} + RT \ln\!\left(\frac{P_i^{\text{pure}}}{P^\circ}\right) + RT \ln x_i
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the second term as the pure-liquid standard chemical potential.**

For the pure liquid at T and P, the vapor above it has pressure $P_i^{\text{pure}}$, so:

$$
\mu_i^{*,\text{liq}}(T, P) = \mu_i^{\circ,\text{vap}} + RT \ln\!\left(\frac{P_i^{\text{pure}}(T)}{P^\circ}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — At equilibrium, μᵢ in liquid = μᵢ in vapor.**

$$
\boxed{\mu_i^{\text{liq}}(T, P, x_i) = \mu_i^{*,\text{liq}}(T, P) + RT \ln x_i}
$$

This is the **Raoult's-law form** of μ for an ideal solution.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Chemical Potential in a Real Solution (Activity)

Real solutions deviate from Raoult's law. We retain the form of the ideal solution but introduce **activity aᵢ**:

$$
\mu_i = \mu_i^\circ + RT \ln a_i
$$

**Activity and activity coefficient:**

$$
a_i = \gamma_i x_i \quad \text{(Raoult-basis convention)}
$$

or for solutes in dilute solutions:

$$
a_i = \gamma_i \, \dfrac{C_i}{C^\circ} \quad \text{(Henry-basis convention)}
$$

where γᵢ is the **activity coefficient** (γ = 1 for ideal behavior). For solutes, the Henry's-law reference state is a *hypothetical* ideal solution of unit molality, while for solvents the reference is the pure liquid (Raoult's law reference).

**Temperature dependence of γ:**

$$
\left(\frac{\partial \ln \gamma_i}{\partial T}\right)_{P, x} = -\frac{\bar{H_i}^E}{RT^2}
$$

where $\bar{H_i}^E$ is the partial molar excess enthalpy.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Electrochemical Potential (Charged Species)

For an ion of charge number zᵢ in a phase of electric potential φ, the work to bring one mole of that ion from infinity (zero potential reference) into the phase is $z_i F \phi$. The total potential driving force is the **electrochemical potential**:

$$
\tilde{\mu}_i = \mu_i + z_i F \phi
$$

**Derivation:** Add the electrostatic work to the chemical work in the fundamental equation:

$$
dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP + \sum_i \mu_i\, dn_i + \sum_i z_i F \phi\, dn_i
$$

$$
dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP + \sum_i \tilde{\mu}_i\, dn_i
$$

**Biological example — Nernst equation for membrane potential:**

At equilibrium for ion *i* across a membrane:

$$
\tilde{\mu}_i^{\text{inside}} = \tilde{\mu}_i^{\text{outside}}
$$

$$
\mu_i^\circ + RT \ln a_i^{\text{in}} + z_i F \phi^{\text{in}} = \mu_i^\circ + RT \ln a_i^{\text{out}} + z_i F \phi^{\text{out}}
$$

Solving for the membrane potential $\Delta \phi = \phi^{\text{in}} - \phi^{\text{out}}$:

$$
\boxed{\Delta \phi = \frac{RT}{z_i F} \ln\!\left(\frac{a_i^{\text{out}}}{a_i^{\text{in}}}\right)}
$$

This is the **Nernst equation**, central to neurophysiology and bioelectrochemistry.

---

## 3.7 Derivation 7 — Reaction Gibbs Energy and Equilibrium

For a general biochemical reaction:

$$
\sum_i \nu_i A_i = 0
$$

where νᵢ is the **stoichiometric number** (negative for reactants, positive for products). The reaction Gibbs energy is:

$$
\Delta_r G = \sum_i \nu_i \mu_i
$$

Substituting the activity form for each μᵢ:

$$
\Delta_r G = \sum_i \nu_i \left[\mu_i^\circ + RT \ln a_i\right] = \underbrace{\sum_i \nu_i \mu_i^\circ}_{\Delta_r G^\circ} + RT \sum_i \ln a_i^{\nu_i}
$$

$$
\Delta_r G = \Delta_r G^\circ + RT \ln Q
$$

where $Q = \prod_i a_i^{\nu_i}$ is the **reaction quotient**. At equilibrium, $\Delta_r G = 0$ and $Q = K$:

$$
0 = \Delta_r G^\circ + RT \ln K \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\Delta_r G^\circ = -RT \ln K}
$$

This is the **van't Hoff isotherm** for biochemical equilibria and is critical for calculating K given standard free energies (e.g., K_eq of ATP hydrolysis = ~10⁵).

---

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for KTU Problems

| Given Information | Use This Expression |
|---|---|
| Pure ideal gas at pressure P | $\mu = \mu^\circ + RT \ln(P / P^\circ)$ |
| Component *i* in an ideal gas mixture | $\mu_i = \mu_i^\circ + RT \ln(y_i P / P^\circ)$ |
| Component *i* in a real gas mixture | $\mu_i = \mu_i^\circ + RT \ln(\phi_i y_i P / P^\circ)$ |
| Solvent in an ideal solution | $\mu_i = \mu_i^* + RT \ln x_i$ |
| Solute in an ideal-dilute solution | $\mu_i = \mu_i^{*} + RT \ln(C_i / C^\circ)$ |
| Component in a real solution | $\mu_i = \mu_i^\circ + RT \ln(\gamma_i x_i)$ |
| Ion in an electric field | $\tilde{\mu}_i = \mu_i + z_i F \phi$ |
| Reaction at arbitrary conditions | $\Delta_r G = \Delta_r G^\circ + RT \ln Q$ |
| Reaction at equilibrium | $\Delta_r G^\circ = -RT \ln K$ |
| Two-phase equilibrium | μᵢ(phase α) = μᵢ(phase β) |

---

## 3.9 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** The standard free energy of ATP hydrolysis is $\Delta_r G^\circ = -30.5$ kJ/mol at pH 7, T = 298 K. In a typical cell, [ATP] = 2 mM, [ADP] = 0.5 mM, [Pᵢ] = 5 mM. Calculate ΔᵣG and the actual free energy available.

**Step 1 — Compute the reaction quotient Q.**

For ATP → ADP + Pᵢ:

$$
Q = \frac{[\text{ADP}][\text{P}_i]}{[\text{ATP}]} = \frac{(0.5 \times 10^{-3})(5 \times 10^{-3})}{(2 \times 10^{-3})} = 1.25 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the van't Hoff isotherm.**

$$
\Delta_r G = \Delta_r G^\circ + RT \ln Q
$$

$$
\Delta_r G = -30.5 \text{ kJ/mol} + (8.314 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kJ/mol·K})(298 \text{ K}) \ln(1.25 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
\ln(1.25 \times 10^{-3}) = -6.685
$$

$$
RT \ln Q = (2.478 \text{ kJ/mol})(-6.685) = -16.57 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

**Step 3 — Final answer.**

$$
\Delta_r G = -30.5 + (-16.57) = -47.07 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The cell actually obtains **−47.1 kJ/mol** from ATP hydrolysis, substantially more than the standard value of −30.5 kJ/mol because the cellular [ATP] / [ADP] ratio is far from unity. This is why biochemistry textbooks often quote the *physiological* ΔG rather than the *standard* ΔG°.
