# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each Part A question carries **3 marks** and tests direct conceptual understanding. KTU expects precise, definition-based answers in 3–5 lines.

### Question 1: Definition of Chemical Potential

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

> Define the term *chemical potential* of a component in a thermodynamic system. What does a positive value of $(\partial G / \partial n_i)_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}}$ imply about the spontaneity of adding more of that component at constant T and P?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The chemical potential μᵢ of the *i*-th component is defined as the partial molar Gibbs free energy, expressed mathematically as:

$$
\mu_i = \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial n_i}\right)_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}}
$$

Physically, it represents the change in the Gibbs free energy of the system when an infinitesimally small amount of component *i* is added, holding T, P, and the amounts of all other components constant.

A **positive value** of $\mu_i$ means that adding more of component *i* would *increase* the Gibbs free energy of the system at constant T and P. According to the second law criterion for spontaneity ($dG_{T,P} \le 0$ for spontaneous processes), such an addition is **non-spontaneous** — the system "rejects" further addition of *i* because doing so would move it away from its equilibrium state. Conversely, a negative $\mu_i$ would imply that the system is "hungry" for more of component *i*, and spontaneous mass transfer of *i* into the system is favored.

**Marking scheme:** 1.5 marks for the correct definition with formula; 1 mark for the physical interpretation; 0.5 mark for the sign/spontaneity statement.

---

### Question 2: Electrochemical Potential

**[KTU University Exam – December 2023]**

> Distinguish between *chemical potential* and *electrochemical potential*. Under what condition does an ion reach equilibrium across a biological membrane, and write the resulting equation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Chemical Potential (μᵢ) | Electrochemical Potential (μ̃ᵢ) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Partial molar Gibbs energy of species *i* | Chemical + electrical contribution for an ion |
| Formula | $\mu_i = (\partial G / \partial n_i)_{T,P}$ | $\tilde{\mu}_i = \mu_i + z_i F \phi$ |
| Applies to | Neutral species and ions (no field) | Charged species in an electric field |
| Units | J mol⁻¹ | J mol⁻¹ |

An ion reaches **equilibrium** across a biological membrane when its electrochemical potential is equal on both sides of the membrane:

$$
\tilde{\mu}_i^{\text{inside}} = \tilde{\mu}_i^{\text{outside}}
$$

Substituting the definition:

$$
\mu_i^{\text{in}} + z_i F \phi^{\text{in}} = \mu_i^{\text{out}} + z_i F \phi^{\text{out}}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
z_i F (\phi^{\text{in}} - \phi^{\text{out}}) = \mu_i^{\text{out}} - \mu_i^{\text{in}} = RT \ln(a_i^{\text{out}} / a_i^{\text{in}})
$$

This yields the **Nernst equation** for the equilibrium membrane potential:

$$
\Delta \phi = \frac{RT}{z_i F} \ln\!\left(\frac{a_i^{\text{out}}}{a_i^{\text{in}}}\right)
$$

**Marking scheme:** 1 mark for distinction table; 1 mark for the equilibrium condition; 1 mark for the Nernst equation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert to standard units** — R = 8.314 J/mol·K must be used with ΔG in J/mol, OR R = 8.314 × 10⁻³ kJ/mol·K with ΔG in kJ/mol. Mixing units is the most common 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Sign error in ΔᵣG = ΔᵣG° + RT ln Q** — log of a small number is negative, which makes the second term negative for K < 1, pushing ΔᵣG *more negative* than ΔᵣG°. Students often flip this sign.
> 3. **Using P directly instead of P/P°** — the standard chemical potential μ° is defined at P° = 1 bar, so the argument of ln must be **dimensionless** (i.e., P/P°).
> 4. **Confusing μ* and μ°** — μ* (Henry's law reference) and μ° (Raoult's law / pure-substance reference) are *different standard states* and are not interchangeable. Examiners specifically test this.
> 5. **Not specifying the basis for activity** — when you write μ = μ° + RT ln aᵢ, you must say whether γᵢ is a Raoult-basis or Henry-basis activity coefficient.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks (CHOICE OPTION A)

**[KTU University Exam – December 2023, Modified]**

> **(a)** Derive the expression for the chemical potential of a component *i* in an ideal gas mixture. How does it reduce to the case of a pure ideal gas? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For the biochemical reaction **glucose-6-phosphate ⇌ fructose-6-phosphate** catalyzed by phosphoglucose isomerase, $\Delta_r G^\circ = +1.67$ kJ/mol at 298 K and pH 7. If in a red blood cell [G6P] = 0.10 mM and [F6P] = 0.02 mM, calculate ΔᵣG and the equilibrium constant K. Comment on whether the reaction proceeds spontaneously under cellular conditions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** For a pure ideal gas, the molar Gibbs energy G_m at constant T depends only on P, and:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial G_m}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = V_m = \frac{RT}{P}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Integrating from the standard pressure P° to P at constant T:

$$
\int_{G_m^\circ}^{G_m} dG_m = \int_{P^\circ}^{P} \frac{RT}{P'}\, dP' = RT \ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P^\circ}\right)
$$

$$
\therefore \quad G_m(T, P) = G_m^\circ(T) + RT \ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P^\circ}\right)
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Since μ = G_m for a pure substance:

$$
\mu(T, P) = \mu^\circ(T) + RT \ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P^\circ}\right) \quad \text{...(i)}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** For an ideal gas mixture, each component *i* exerts its **partial pressure** P_i. By Dalton's law, $P_i = y_i P$, where y_i is the mole fraction of *i* in the gas phase and P is the total pressure. The chemical potential of *i* in the mixture follows the same functional form as the pure gas, but with P replaced by P_i:

$$
\mu_i(T, P) = \mu_i^\circ(T) + RT \ln\!\left(\frac{P_i}{P^\circ}\right) = \mu_i^\circ(T) + RT \ln\!\left(\frac{y_i P}{P^\circ}\right) \quad \text{...(ii)}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Reduction to the pure-gas case: For a single-component system, $y_i = 1$, so equation (ii) reduces to equation (i). Hence the pure-gas expression is a special case of the mixture expression.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the van 't Hoff isotherm:

$$
\Delta_r G = \Delta_r G^\circ + RT \ln Q \quad \text{where} \quad Q = \frac{[\text{F6P}]}{[\text{G6P}]}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute the reaction quotient Q:

$$
Q = \frac{0.02 \times 10^{-3} \text{ M}}{0.10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ M}} = 0.20
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute RT in kJ/mol:

$$
RT = (8.314 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kJ/mol·K})(298 \text{ K}) = 2.478 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Compute RT ln Q:

$$
\ln(0.20) = -1.6094
$$

$$
RT \ln Q = (2.478)(-1.6094) = -3.989 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Compute ΔᵣG:

$$
\Delta_r G = 1.67 + (-3.989) = -2.32 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Compute the equilibrium constant K using $\Delta_r G^\circ = -RT \ln K$:

$$
\ln K = -\frac{\Delta_r G^\circ}{RT} = -\frac{1.67}{2.478} = -0.6739
$$

$$
K = e^{-0.6739} = 0.510
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Even though $\Delta_r G^\circ$ is **positive** (reaction is non-spontaneous under standard conditions), the **actual** ΔᵣG under cellular conditions is **negative** (-2.32 kJ/mol), so the reaction **proceeds spontaneously forward** in the red blood cell. This is a classic example of how cells drive thermodynamically unfavorable reactions by maintaining non-standard reactant/product ratios.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (CHOICE OPTION B)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

> **(a)** Define the term *fugacity*. Show that for a real gas, the chemical potential can be written as $\mu_i = \mu_i^\circ + RT \ln(f_i / f^\circ)$, where $f_i$ is the fugacity. Explain the physical significance of the fugacity coefficient φᵢ and state its limiting value for an ideal gas. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 0.10 molal aqueous solution of a protein has an activity coefficient γ = 1.20 on the molality basis at 298 K. Calculate the chemical potential of water in this solution relative to pure water. The molar mass of water is 18.015 g/mol, and assume the solution density is 1.00 g/mL. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** **Definition of fugacity.** Fugacity $f_i$ (introduced by G. N. Lewis in 1901) is a "corrected pressure" that allows the chemical potential of a real gas to be written in the same mathematical form as that of an ideal gas. It has units of pressure.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** **Derivation of the fugacity expression.** For an ideal gas, $\mu_i^{\text{ideal}} = \mu_i^\circ + RT \ln(P / P^\circ)$. For a real gas, we want to write $\mu_i^{\text{real}} = \mu_i^\circ + RT \ln(f_i / P^\circ)$, where f_i is a "corrected pressure" that, when used in the ideal-gas expression, gives the correct real-gas μ. For this to be thermodynamically consistent:

$$
\lim_{P \to 0} \frac{f_i}{P_i} = 1
$$

That is, as the gas becomes infinitely dilute (and therefore ideal), fugacity and partial pressure become equal. This boundary condition uniquely defines f_i. Substituting $f^\circ = P^\circ = 1$ bar:

$$
\mu_i^{\text{real}} = \mu_i^\circ + RT \ln\!\left(\frac{f_i}{f^\circ}\right) = \mu_i^\circ + RT \ln\!\left(\frac{f_i}{P^\circ}\right)
$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** **Fugacity coefficient.** The **fugacity coefficient** is defined as:

$$
\phi_i = \frac{f_i}{P_i} = \frac{f_i}{y_i P}
$$

It is a **dimensionless measure of the deviation of a real gas from ideal-gas behavior**.

- $\phi_i = 1$ → ideal gas (no deviation)
- $\phi_i < 1$ → attractive forces dominate (e.g., easy-to-liquefy gases near their critical point)
- $\phi_i > 1$ → repulsive forces dominate (e.g., very high pressure)

**Step 4 (1 mark):** **Limiting value for an ideal gas.** As $P \to 0$ or as $T \to \infty$, the gas behaves ideally and $\phi_i \to 1$. This is the key reference point in fugacity calculations.

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** **Physical significance.** The fugacity coefficient φᵢ is a *quantitative, temperature- and pressure-dependent correction factor* that captures all non-ideality arising from intermolecular forces. It is computed from an equation of state (van der Waals, Redlich–Kwong, Peng–Robinson) or from experimental PVT data. In biochemical contexts, it matters most for high-pressure processing (e.g., supercritical CO₂ extraction) and gas solubility in deep-sea organisms.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** For water (the solvent) in a non-ideal aqueous solution, the chemical potential is:

$$
\mu_{\text{water}} = \mu_{\text{water}}^\circ + RT \ln a_{\text{water}} = \mu_{\text{water}}^\circ + RT \ln(\gamma_{\text{water}} \, x_{\text{water}})
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the mole fraction of water. Take 1 kg of water as the basis. The number of moles of water in 1 kg is:

$$
n_{\text{water}} = \frac{1000 \text{ g}}{18.015 \text{ g/mol}} = 55.51 \text{ mol}
$$

For a 0.10 molal solution, the moles of solute in 1 kg water is:

$$
n_{\text{solute}} = 0.10 \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Compute the mole fraction of water:

$$
x_{\text{water}} = \frac{n_{\text{water}}}{n_{\text{water}} + n_{\text{solute}}} = \frac{55.51}{55.51 + 0.10} = \frac{55.51}{55.61} = 0.99820
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** State the activity coefficient of water on the mole-fraction basis. For very dilute solutions, the activity coefficient of water is approximately 1 (γ_water ≈ 1, by the Gibbs–Duhem constraint, since the solute's γ deviates from 1 to keep the total system thermodynamically consistent). The problem states γ_solute = 1.20, but for dilute solutions γ_water ≈ 1.00.

$$
a_{\text{water}} = \gamma_{\text{water}} \, x_{\text{water}} \approx 0.99820
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Compute Δμ_water:

$$
\Delta \mu_{\text{water}} = \mu_{\text{water}} - \mu_{\text{water}}^\circ = RT \ln a_{\text{water}}
$$

$$
\Delta \mu_{\text{water}} = (8.314 \text{ J/mol·K})(298 \text{ K}) \ln(0.99820)
$$

$$
\ln(0.99820) = -1.802 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Final calculation:

$$
\Delta \mu_{\text{water}} = (2477.6 \text{ J/mol})(-1.802 \times 10^{-3}) = -4.46 \text{ J/mol}
$$

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** The chemical potential of water in the protein solution is about **4.46 J/mol lower** than that of pure water. This is the molecular-level basis of *osmotic pressure* — water spontaneously flows from the region of higher μ (pure water) to the region of lower μ (the protein solution) through a semipermeable membrane, an effect critical in dialysis, edema, and plant-water relations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Chemical Potential (Module 3)**

### Core Definition
- ☐ **Chemical potential** μᵢ = (∂G/∂nᵢ)_{T, P, nⱼ≠i} — the *partial molar Gibbs free energy* of component *i*.
- ☐ μᵢ is the *driving force* for mass transfer, phase change, and chemical reaction.
- ☐ At equilibrium: μᵢ is uniform across all phases at the same T and P.

### Pressure & Temperature Dependence
- ☐ **(∂μᵢ/∂P)_{T,n} = V̄ᵢ** (partial molar volume) → μ increases with P.
- ☐ **(∂μᵢ/∂T)_{P,n} = −S̄ᵢ** (partial molar entropy) → μ decreases with T.

### Ideal Gas Forms (PURE GAS = μ° + RT ln(P/P°))
- ☐ **Pure ideal gas:** μ = μ° + RT ln(P/P°)
- ☐ **Ideal gas mixture:** μᵢ = μᵢ° + RT ln(yᵢ P / P°)
- ☐ Always use **dimensionless ratio P/P°** inside the logarithm.

### Real Gas (Fugacity)
- ☐ **Fugacity f** has units of pressure; replaces P in the ideal-gas expression.
- ☐ **Fugacity coefficient** φᵢ = fᵢ / (yᵢ P) → φᵢ = 1 for ideal gas.
- ☐ μᵢ(real) = μᵢ° + RT ln(φᵢ yᵢ P / P°)

### Solutions — Three Standard Forms
- ☐ **Ideal solution (Raoult's law):** μᵢ = μᵢ* + RT ln xᵢ — solvent reference.
- ☐ **Ideal-dilute solution (Henry's law):** μᵢ = μᵢ* + RT ln(Cᵢ / C°) — solute reference.
- ☐ **Real solution (activity):** μᵢ = μᵢ° + RT ln aᵢ = μᵢ° + RT ln(γᵢ xᵢ)

### Electrochemical Potential (For Ions)
- ☐ **μ̃ᵢ = μᵢ + zᵢFφ** — adds electrical work to chemical work.
- ☐ Equilibrium across a membrane: μ̃ᵢ(in) = μ̃ᵢ(out) → **Nernst equation**.
- ☐ Faraday constant: **F = 96,485 C/mol**.

### Reaction Equilibrium
- ☐ **ΔᵣG = Σ νᵢ μᵢ** (ν negative for reactants, positive for products).
- ☐ **van 't Hoff isotherm:** ΔᵣG = ΔᵣG° + RT ln Q.
- ☐ **At equilibrium:** ΔᵣG° = −RT ln K.
- ☐ ΔᵣG < 0 → forward reaction spontaneous; ΔᵣG > 0 → reverse spontaneous.

### Gibbs–Duhem Constraint
- ☐ Σ nᵢ dμᵢ = −S dT + V dP (at constant composition changes).
- ☐ At constant T and P: **Σ nᵢ dμᵢ = 0** — chemical potentials are not independent.

### Universal Constants to Memorize
- ☐ R = **8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹** = 1.987 cal mol⁻¹ K⁻¹ = 8.314 × 10⁻³ kJ mol⁻¹ K⁻¹
- ☐ F = **96,485 C mol⁻¹** = 96.485 kJ mol⁻¹ V⁻¹
- ☐ P° = **1 bar** = 10⁵ Pa; T° = **298.15 K**.

### Common KTU Pitfalls (Re-Stated)
- ☐ Always include **P/P°** in the logarithm — never just P.
- ☐ **Never confuse μ* and μ°** — they are *different* standard states.
- ☐ Match the **units of R** to the units of ΔG.
- ☐ For the Nernst equation, **zᵢ is the charge number** (integer), not the charge in coulombs.
- ☐ Remember to convert **all concentrations to the same standard basis** before computing Q.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final advice for KTU Module 3 (Chemical Potential):**
> Master the **eight key equations** (μ for pure ideal gas, μ for ideal mixture, μ for real gas via fugacity, μ for ideal solution, μ for real solution, electrochemical μ, reaction ΔᵣG, and Gibbs–Duhem). Every Part B question in this module is a **combination or application** of these. Practice identifying *which* form to use, write the full setup before plugging in numbers, and *always* track units. The ability to **navigate between μ, G, K, and ΔᵣG** is the single most important skill for scoring in this module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solution Thermodynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT414 - BIOCHEMICAL THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Solution Thermodynamics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Solution Thermodynamics?

**Solution Thermodynamics** is the branch of classical thermodynamics that deals with the energetic behaviour of multi-component homogeneous mixtures (solutions) — particularly how the chemical potential, partial molar properties, and phase equilibria of each component vary with composition, temperature, and pressure. In the context of biochemical engineering, it forms the backbone for understanding **oxygen transport in blood**, **protein-ligand binding equilibria**, **biomolecular solubility**, and **downstream separation processes** such as liquid–liquid extraction and crystallization of biomolecules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Exam-Ready Wording):**
> *"Solution thermodynamics is the study of the thermodynamic properties of multicomponent, single-phase homogeneous systems in which the Gibbs energy, chemical potential, and partial molar properties of each component are functions of composition, temperature, and pressure."*

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you walk into a classroom where all students are identical (**pure substance**). Now imagine mixing students from three different streams (say, Biotechnology, Mechanical, Civil) in the same room. The "energy of interaction" of each student with the surroundings changes drastically:

- **Ideal Solution Analogy**: A perfectly mixed classroom where every student behaves the same regardless of neighbours → *no excess interaction energy*.
- **Real Solution Analogy**: Civil students cluster together (preferential interaction), Biotech students stay alone (solvation) → *non-zero excess energy* (`G^E ≠ 0`).

The macroscopic "happiness" (Gibbs energy) of this mixed classroom is **not** simply the sum of individual "happinesses" — there is a **mixing contribution** plus an **excess contribution** due to unlike-molecular interactions.

### 1.3 Classification of Solutions

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Classification (High-Yield for KTU):**

| Solution Type | Defining Condition | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Ideal Solution** | `ΔH_mix = 0`, `ΔV_mix = 0` | Obeys **Raoult's Law** for ALL compositions |
| **Dilute Ideal Solution** | Solvent obeys Raoult's, Solute obeys Henry's Law | Two-reference-state description |
| **Regular Solution** | `ΔS_mix = ΔS_mix^id`, but `ΔH_mix ≠ 0` | Non-ideal due to enthalpy only |
| **Athermal Solution** | `ΔH_mix = 0`, but `ΔS_mix ≠ ΔS_mix^id` | Non-ideal due to entropy only |
| **Real (Non-Ideal) Solution** | Both `ΔH_mix ≠ 0` and `ΔS_mix ≠ ΔS_mix^id` | Requires **activity coefficients** |

### 1.4 The Concept of Chemical Potential in a Solution

The **chemical potential** `μi` of species `i` in a mixture is the partial molar Gibbs energy:

$$\mu_i = \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial n_i}\right)_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}}$$

It is the **master thermodynamic variable** that determines:
- **Direction of mass transfer** (species flow from high `μ` to low `μ`)
- **Phase equilibrium** (equality of `μ` in coexisting phases)
- **Reaction equilibrium** (when `Δμ = 0`)

For a **pure component**: `μi = μi°(T, P)` — the standard-state Gibbs energy.

For a component in a solution: `μi` becomes a **function of composition** — this is precisely what solution thermodynamics quantifies.

### 1.5 Partial Molar Properties — The Heart of Solution Thermodynamics

Any extensive property `M` of a solution depends on composition:

$$M = M(T, P, n_1, n_2, ..., n_C)$$

The **partial molar property** of component `i` is:

$$\bar{M}_i = \left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial n_i}\right)_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight**: `M̄i` is *not* the same as the molar property `M` of pure `i`. It represents the *incremental contribution* of adding a small amount of `i` to a large reservoir of solution — a concept central to biochemical systems like **water activity in foods**, **osmotic pressure in cells**, and **solvent effects on enzyme stability**.

The total extensive property is reconstructed via **summability relation**:

$$M = \sum_{i=1}^{C} n_i \, \bar{M}_i$$

### 1.6 Standard Reference States in Solution Thermodynamics

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Topic — Three Standard States for Component `i`:**
>
> 1. **Pure-component reference (Raoult's Law basis)**: `μi^L(T, P)` = Gibbs energy of pure liquid `i` at system T, P.
> 2. **Infinite-dilution reference (Henry's Law basis)**: `μi^{*,∞}(T, P)` = Gibbs energy of solute `i` at infinite dilution.
> 3. **1 molal (or 1 molar) hypothetical reference**: Used for electrolytes and biochemical solutes — `γi → 1` as `mi → 0` but extrapolated to 1 molal.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermodynamic Framework for Solutions

### 2.1.1 The Fundamental Equation for an Open Multicomponent System

The total differential of Gibbs energy for a `C`-component, single-phase system:

$$dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP + \sum_{i=1}^{C} \mu_i \, dn_i$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The chemical potential emerges naturally as the conjugate intensive variable to composition** — making it the central quantity of solution thermodynamics.

### 2.1.2 Ideal Solution Theory

For an **ideal solution**, the chemical potential of each component is given by:

$$\mu_i^{id}(T, P, x) = \mu_i^{*}(T, P) + RT \ln x_i$$

where:
- `μi*(T, P)` = chemical potential of pure liquid `i` at system T and P (the **Raoult's Law reference state**).
- `xi` = mole fraction of `i` (with `Σ xi = 1`).

**Consequence — Raoult's Law**: At vapour–liquid equilibrium, since `μi^L = μi^V`, and assuming ideal vapour (`μi^V = μi^{V°} + RT \ln(Pi/P°)`):

$$P_i = x_i \, P_i^{*}(T)$$

where `Pi*(T)` is the **pure-component vapour pressure** of `i` at temperature `T`.

### 2.1.3 Real (Non-Ideal) Solution Theory — Activity Formalism

Real solutions deviate from ideality. The **Lewis–Randall activity** `ai` is defined so that the form of the ideal equation is preserved:

$$\mu_i(T, P, x) = \mu_i^{*}(T, P) + RT \ln a_i$$

**Activity coefficient** `γi` quantifies non-ideality:

$$a_i = \gamma_i \, x_i \quad \text{with} \quad \gamma_i \to 1 \text{ as } x_i \to 1$$

Therefore:

$$\mu_i = \mu_i^{*} + RT \ln x_i + RT \ln \gamma_i = \mu_i^{id} + RT \ln \gamma_i$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip**: Always quote `μi` in the form `μi* + RT ln(ai)`. Marks are often lost when students write `μi = μi° + RT ln(xi)` for real solutions without the activity correction.

### 2.1.4 Henry's Law — The Other Reference State

For **dilute solutions of a solute** (component 2 in solvent 1), the solvent obeys Raoult's Law, but the **solute obeys Henry's Law**:

$$P_2 = k_{H,2}(T) \, x_2$$

In chemical-potential form:

$$\mu_2(T, P, x) = \mu_2^{*,\infty}(T, P) + RT \ln x_2$$

where `μ2^{*,∞}` is the chemical potential of solute 2 in its **hypothetical pure state** extrapolated from infinite dilution (NOT the true pure-solute chemical potential).

**Henry's constant** relates the two reference states:

$$\mu_2^{*,\infty} - \mu_2^{*} = RT \ln\left(\frac{P_2^{*}}{k_{H,2}}\right)$$

### 2.1.5 The Gibbs–Duhem Equation — The Coupling Constraint

Differentiating `G = Σ ni μi` and subtracting the fundamental equation:

$$\boxed{\;S\,dT - V\,dP + \sum_{i=1}^{C} n_i \, d\mu_i = 0\;}$$

For a **binary solution at constant T and P**, this reduces to:

$$x_1 \, d\mu_1 + x_2 \, d\mu_2 = 0$$

Substituting `μi = μi* + RT ln(ai)`:

$$x_1 \, d\ln a_1 + x_2 \, d\ln a_2 = 0$$

or equivalently:

$$x_1 \, d\ln \gamma_1 + x_2 \, d\ln \gamma_2 = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Meaning**: The activity coefficients of the two components are NOT independent. Measuring one gives the other — this is the **Gibbs–Duhem consistency test** for VLE data.

### 2.1.6 Excess Properties

The **excess property** `M^E` of a solution is defined as the difference between the real property and the ideal-solution property at the same T, P, and composition:

$$M^E = M^{real} - M^{id}$$

The most important excess property in solution thermodynamics is the **excess Gibbs energy** `G^E`:

$$\frac{G^E}{RT} = \sum_{i=1}^{C} x_i \ln \gamma_i$$

For a **binary solution**:

$$\frac{G^E}{RT} = x_1 \ln \gamma_1 + x_2 \ln \gamma_2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Bioprocess Relevance**: `G^E > 0` → positive deviation from Raoult's (e.g., chloroform + ethanol); `G^E < 0` → negative deviation (e.g., chloroform + acetone). This determines whether azeotropes form — critical for **solvent recovery in bioprocess industries**.

### 2.1.7 Models for Excess Gibbs Energy

#### (a) One-Constant **Margules** Equation

$$\boxed{\;\frac{G^E}{RT} = A \, x_1 x_2\;}$$

This gives:

$$\ln \gamma_1 = A \, x_2^{2}, \qquad \ln \gamma_2 = A \, x_1^{2}$$

It is **symmetric**: both components use the same constant `A`. Suitable for systems of similar molecular size and weak asymmetry.

#### (b) Two-Constant **Margules** Equation (Margules 3-suffix)

$$\frac{G^E}{RT} = (A_{21} x_1 + A_{12} x_2) \, x_1 x_2$$

Yielding:

$$\ln \gamma_1 = x_2^{2} \, [A_{12} + 2(A_{21} - A_{12}) x_1]$$
$$\ln \gamma_2 = x_1^{2} \, [A_{21} + 2(A_{12} - A_{21}) x_2]$$

#### (c) **van Laar** Equation

$$\frac{G^E}{RT} = \frac{A_{12} A_{21} x_1 x_2}{A_{12} x_1 + A_{21} x_2}$$

With activity coefficients:

$$\ln \gamma_1 = \frac{A_{12}}{(1 + A_{12} x_1 / (A_{21} x_2))^{2}}$$
$$\ln \gamma_2 = \frac{A_{21}}{(1 + A_{21} x_2 / (A_{12} x_1))^{2}}$$

#### (d) **Wilson** Equation (Composition-Sensitive)

The most popular in biochemical engineering for **partially miscible systems** (e.g., water + organic solvent extraction):

$$\boxed{\;\frac{G^E}{RT} = -x_1 \ln(x_1 + \Lambda_{12} x_2) - x_2 \ln(x_2 + \Lambda_{21} x_1)\;}$$

with:

$$\Lambda_{12} = \frac{V_2}{V_1} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\lambda_{12} - \lambda_{11}}{RT}\right), \quad \Lambda_{21} = \frac{V_1}{V_2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\lambda_{21} - \lambda_{22}}{RT}\right)$$

where `Vi` = molar volume of pure `i`, and `(λij − λii)` = interaction energy difference.

### 2.1.8 Fugacity of Components in Solution

For a component `i` in a **liquid solution**, the fugacity is:

$$f_i^{L} = x_i \, \gamma_i \, f_i^{*,L}$$

where `fi^{*,L}` is the fugacity of pure liquid `i` at system T, P.

For vapour phase (assuming ideal gas for simplicity):

$$f_i^{V} = y_i \, P$$

**VLE criterion** `f_i^L = f_i^V` gives the **modified Raoult's Law**:

$$y_i \, P = x_i \, \gamma_i \, P_i^{sat}(T)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the workhorse equation for VLE calculations in bioprocess design** — used in designing distillation columns for bioethanol recovery, solvent extraction of antibiotics, etc.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Reference State / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Chemical potential in ideal solution | `μi^id = μi* + RT ln xi` | Pure component reference |
| 2 | Chemical potential in real solution | `μi = μi* + RT ln(γi xi) = μi* + RT ln ai` | Activity formalism |
| 3 | Raoult's Law | `Pi = xi Pi*(T)` | Solvent in any solution |
| 4 | Modified Raoult's Law | `yi P = xi γi Pi^sat(T)` | Real solution VLE |
| 5 | Henry's Law | `Pi = kH,i xi` | Solute in dilute solution |
| 6 | Activity coefficient definition | `ai = γi xi`, `γi → 1` as `xi → 1` | Non-ideality measure |
| 7 | Excess Gibbs energy | `G^E = RT Σ xi ln γi` | Non-ideality quantification |
| 8 | Gibbs–Duhem (binary, cT, cP) | `x1 d ln γ1 + x2 d ln γ2 = 0` | Coupling of `γ1`, `γ2` |
| 9 | Partial molar property | `M̄i = (∂M/∂ni)T,P,nj` | Definition |
| 10 | Summability relation | `M = Σ ni M̄i` | Reconstruction of `M` |
| 11 | 1-constant Margules | `ln γ1 = A x2²`, `ln γ2 = A x1²` | Symmetric non-ideality |
| 12 | van Laar | `ln γ1 = A12 / (1 + A12 x1/A21 x2)²` | Asymmetric non-ideality |
| 13 | Wilson | `ln γ1 = −ln(x1 + Λ12 x2) + x2[Λ12/(x1+Λ12 x2) − Λ21/(x2+Λ21 x1)]` | Multicomponent-friendly |
| 14 | Fugacity in solution | `fi^L = xi γi fi^{*,L}` | General VLE form |
| 15 | Partial molar volume | `V̄i = (∂V/∂ni)T,P,nj` | Special case of (9) |
| 16 | Partial molar Gibbs energy | `μi = (∂G/∂ni)T,P,nj` | Definition of chemical potential |
| 17 | Mixing Gibbs energy (ideal) | `ΔG^id_mix = nRT Σ xi ln xi ≤ 0` | Always negative (spontaneous) |
| 18 | Mixing enthalpy (ideal) | `ΔH^id_mix = 0` | Defining property of ideal solution |
| 19 | Excess enthalpy | `H^E ≠ 0` for regular solutions | Distinguishes from athermal |
| 20 | Excess volume | `V^E ≠ 0` indicates molecular packing change | Biochemical relevance (protein hydration) |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bioprocess Separations**: VLE data (via modified Raoult's Law) are used in **distillation, extractive fermentation, and recovery of bioethanol, butanol, and acetic acid**.
- **Pharmaceutical Formulation**: Solubility of drug molecules in mixed solvents is predicted using activity-coefficient models (Wilson, UNIQUAC).
- **Food Preservation**: **Water activity** `aw` (= `γw xw` for water) determines microbial growth, shelf life, and texture.
- **Bioreactor Design**: Oxygen delivery to cells depends on **Henry's constant** of O₂ in fermentation broth.
- **Protein Crystallization**: Excess volume `V^E` of buffer + precipitant mixtures determines nucleation kinetics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Chemical Potential in an Ideal Solution

### Goal
Derive `μi^id = μi*(T, P) + RT ln xi` from first principles.

### Starting Point
Consider component `i` transferring between two ideal-gas phases at the same T. By classical thermodynamic equilibrium:

$$dG_i = \mu_i^{gas} = -S_i dT + V_i dP \quad \text{(pure component)}$$

For a pure ideal gas:

$$V_i = \frac{RT}{P} \quad \text{and} \quad \left(\frac{\partial \mu_i}{\partial P}\right)_T = V_i = \frac{RT}{P}$$

Integrating at constant T from reference pressure `P°` to system pressure `P`:

$$\mu_i^{gas}(T, P) - \mu_i^{gas}(T, P^{\circ}) = RT \ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P^{\circ}}\right)$$

Define `μi^°(T) ≡ μi^{gas}(T, P°)`, so:

$$\mu_i^{gas}(T, P) = \mu_i^{\circ}(T) + RT \ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P^{\circ}}\right)$$

For a component in an **ideal gas mixture**, Dalton's Law gives partial pressure `Pi = yi P`. Substituting:

$$\mu_i^{gas,\,mix}(T, P, y) = \mu_i^{\circ}(T) + RT \ln\!\left(\frac{y_i P}{P^{\circ}}\right)$$

### Connecting Gas and Liquid via VLE

For pure component `i`, at VLE: `μi^{*,L}(T, P) = μi^{V}(T, P) = μi^°(T) + RT ln(Pi*/P°)`. This sets the **pure-liquid reference state**.

For component `i` in the **ideal solution**, we argue that the only difference is that the molecules of `i` are "diluted" by other components. The reduction in escaping tendency (and hence in chemical potential) is exactly `RT ln xi`. Therefore:

$$\mu_i^{id,\,L}(T, P, x) = \mu_i^{*,L}(T, P) + RT \ln x_i$$

**This is the foundational result of ideal solution theory.** $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> The deep justification (Lewis–Randall rule) is that for an ideal solution, the partial molar entropy of mixing of `i` equals `-R ln xi`, derived from `S̄i = -(∂μi/∂T)P,x`.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Gibbs–Duhem Equation

### Starting Point
For a single-phase, multi-component system at equilibrium, `G = G(T, P, n1, ..., nC)`. The total differential is:

$$dG = \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P, n} dT + \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial P}\right)_{T, n} dP + \sum_{i=1}^{C} \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial n_i}\right)_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}} dn_i$$

Recognising:

$$\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P, n} = -S, \qquad \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial P}\right)_{T, n} = V, \qquad \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial n_i}\right)_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}} = \mu_i$$

we obtain the **fundamental equation**:

$$dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP + \sum_{i=1}^{C} \mu_i \, dn_i$$

### Alternative Expression
Since `G` is **homogeneous of degree 1** in the `ni` (Euler's theorem for homogeneous functions):

$$G = \sum_{i=1}^{C} n_i \, \mu_i$$

Taking the total differential:

$$dG = \sum_{i=1}^{C} \mu_i \, dn_i + \sum_{i=1}^{C} n_i \, d\mu_i$$

### Subtraction
Subtract the two expressions for `dG`:

$$\begin{aligned}
0 &= \left[-S\,dT + V\,dP + \sum_{i=1}^{C} \mu_i \, dn_i\right] - \left[\sum_{i=1}^{C} \mu_i \, dn_i + \sum_{i=1}^{C} n_i \, d\mu_i\right] \\[6pt]
0 &= -S\,dT + V\,dP - \sum_{i=1}^{C} n_i \, d\mu_i
\end{aligned}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\;S\,dT - V\,dP + \sum_{i=1}^{C} n_i \, d\mu_i = 0\;}$$

This is the **Gibbs–Duhem equation** — a **constraint** relating the intensive variables of a solution.

### Binary Special Case (Constant T and P)
With `C = 2` and `dT = dP = 0`:

$$n_1 \, d\mu_1 + n_2 \, d\mu_2 = 0$$

Dividing by `(n1 + n2)`:

$$x_1 \, d\mu_1 + x_2 \, d\mu_2 = 0$$

Substituting `dμi = RT d ln ai` (constant T, P):

$$x_1 \, d\ln a_1 + x_2 \, d\ln a_2 = 0$$

Since `ai = γi xi` and `xi` are not independent (`dx1 = -dx2`), the activity-coefficient form is:

$$\boxed{\;x_1 \, d\ln \gamma_1 + x_2 \, d\ln \gamma_2 = 0\;} \quad \text{(binary, cT, cP)}$$

$\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility**: This equation is the basis of the **Herington consistency test** for VLE data — used to verify whether experimental VLE measurements are thermodynamically self-consistent before they are used to design distillation columns.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Activity Coefficient from the 1-Constant Margules Equation

### Given Model
$$\frac{G^E}{RT} = A \, x_1 x_2$$

For a binary solution, the activity coefficient is obtained from:

$$RT \ln \gamma_i = \left(\frac{\partial (n_T G^E / RT)}{\partial n_i}\right)_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}}$$

### Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1** — Express `G^E` in terms of moles (not mole fractions). Define `nT = n1 + n2`, so `x1 = n1/nT` and `x2 = n2/nT`. The total excess Gibbs energy becomes:

$$\frac{n_T G^E}{RT} = n_T \cdot A \cdot \frac{n_1}{n_T} \cdot \frac{n_2}{n_T} = \frac{A \, n_1 n_2}{n_T}$$

**Step 2** — Differentiate with respect to `n1` at constant `n2`:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial}{\partial n_1}\!\left(\frac{n_T G^E}{RT}\right) &= \frac{\partial}{\partial n_1}\!\left(\frac{A \, n_1 n_2}{n_1 + n_2}\right) \\[6pt]
&= A n_2 \cdot \frac{(n_1 + n_2) - n_1 \cdot 1}{(n_1 + n_2)^{2}} \\[6pt]
&= A n_2 \cdot \frac{n_2}{(n_1 + n_2)^{2}} \\[6pt]
&= A \left(\frac{n_2}{n_1 + n_2}\right)^{2} = A x_2^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3** — Equate to the definition:

$$RT \ln \gamma_1 = A x_2^{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\;\ln \gamma_1 = A x_2^{2}\;}$$

**Step 4** — By symmetry of the model (`G^E / RT = A x1 x2` is symmetric in `1 ↔ 2`):

$$\boxed{\;\ln \gamma_2 = A x_1^{2}\;}$$

**Step 5** — Verification via Gibbs–Duhem:

$$\begin{aligned}
x_1 \, d\ln \gamma_1 + x_2 \, d\ln \gamma_2 &= x_1 \cdot (-2A x_2 \, dx_2) + x_2 \cdot (-2A x_1 \, dx_1) \\
&= -2A x_1 x_2 (dx_2 + dx_1) = 0 \quad \text{since } dx_1 + dx_2 = 0
\end{aligned}$$

The Gibbs–Duhem constraint is **automatically satisfied**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Relation Between Henry's Constant and the Infinite-Dilution Reference State

### Setup
Consider a binary liquid solution. At the **infinite-dilution limit** of solute (component 2, `x2 → 0`):

- Solvent (component 1) **obeys Raoult's Law**: `P1 = x1 P1*(T) ≈ P1*(T)` (since `x1 → 1`).
- Solute (component 2) **obeys Henry's Law**: `P2 = kH,2(T) x2`.

### Chemical Potential at Infinite Dilution
For the solute at infinite dilution, the chemical potential is:

$$\mu_2(T, P, x) = \mu_2^{*,\infty}(T, P) + RT \ln x_2$$

But the true chemical potential must also equal the **real-solution form**:

$$\mu_2(T, P, x) = \mu_2^{*}(T, P) + RT \ln(x_2 \gamma_2)$$

Equating the two at all compositions (since they're the same `μ2`):

$$\mu_2^{*,\infty}(T, P) = \mu_2^{*}(T, P) + RT \ln \gamma_2$$

At `x2 → 0`, we define the **infinite-dilution activity coefficient** `γ2^∞`. Therefore:

$$\mu_2^{*,\infty} = \mu_2^{*} + RT \ln \gamma_2^{\infty}$$

### Converting to Henry's Constant
At VLE, `μ2^L = μ2^V`. From the pure-component reference:

$$\mu_2^{L,*} = \mu_2^{V,*} = \mu_2^{\circ}(T) + RT \ln(P_2^{*}/P^{\circ})$$

From the Henry's-law reference (extrapolated to `x2 = 1`):

$$\mu_2^{*,\infty} = \mu_2^{\circ}(T) + RT \ln(k_{H,2}/P^{\circ})$$

Subtracting:

$$\mu_2^{*,\infty} - \mu_2^{*} = RT \ln\!\left(\frac{k_{H,2}}{P_2^{*}}\right)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;\gamma_2^{\infty} = \frac{k_{H,2}}{P_2^{*}}\;}$$

$\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Biochemical Significance**: For O₂ in water at 25 °C, `kH ≈ 769 atm` and `PO₂* ≈ 0.036 atm` (extrapolated hypothetical). So `γO₂^∞ ≈ 2.1 × 10⁴` — extremely large, indicating strong positive deviation from Raoult's Law for dissolved oxygen.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

### Problem
A binary liquid solution of acetone (1) and chloroform (2) at 35 °C is reported to follow the 1-constant Margules model with `A = -0.84`. Calculate `γ1`, `γ2`, and the total pressure at `x1 = 0.35` if pure-component vapour pressures are `P1^{sat} = 0.45 bar` and `P2^{sat} = 0.65 bar`. Assume ideal vapour phase.

### Solution

**Step 1** — Compute `x2`:

$$x_2 = 1 - x_1 = 1 - 0.35 = 0.65$$

**Step 2** — Compute `ln γ1`:

$$\ln \gamma_1 = A \, x_2^{2} = (-0.84)(0.65)^{2} = (-0.84)(0.4225) = -0.3549$$

$$\gamma_1 = e^{-0.3549} = 0.7013$$

**Step 3** — Compute `ln γ2`:

$$\ln \gamma_2 = A \, x_1^{2} = (-0.84)(0.35)^{2} = (-0.84)(0.1225) = -0.1029$$

$$\gamma_2 = e^{-0.1029} = 0.9023$$

**Step 4** — Compute partial pressures (modified Raoult's Law):

$$P_1 = x_1 \gamma_1 P_1^{sat} = (0.35)(0.7013)(0.45) = 0.1105 \text{ bar}$$

$$P_2 = x_2 \gamma_2 P_2^{sat} = (0.65)(0.9023)(0.65) = 0.3812 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 5** — Total pressure (Dalton's Law):

$$P = P_1 + P_2 = 0.1105 + 0.3812 = 0.4917 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 6** — Vapour composition:

$$y_1 = \frac{P_1}{P} = \frac{0.1105}{0.4917} = 0.2248, \qquad y_2 = 0.7752$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment**: Since `A < 0`, the system shows **negative deviation from Raoult's Law** (`γi < 1`). This is consistent with the known strong **H-bonding interaction** between acetone and chloroform — a classic physical chemistry example relevant to biomolecular solvation.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Map — Solution Thermodynamics Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the logical architecture of solution thermodynamics, showing how pure-component properties, mixing, and non-ideality combine to yield the observable behaviour of mixtures.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pure Component State] --> B[Define Standard State µi*]
    C[Solution Composition xi] --> D[Mixing Contribution RT ln xi]
    B --> E[µi = µi* + RT ln xi]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Is Solution Ideal?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Raoult's Law: Pi = xi Pi*]
    F -- No --> H[Activity Correction: ai = γi xi]
    H --> I[Excess Gibbs Energy GE]
    I --> J[Model Selection]
    J --> J1[Margules 1-Param]
    J --> J2[Margules 2-Param]
    J --> J3[van Laar]
    J --> J4[Wilson]
    I --> K[Modified Raoult's Law]
    G --> K
    K --> L[VLE: yi P = xi γi Pi*sat]
    L --> M[Bioprocess Design Output]
    M --> M1[Distillation Columns]
    M --> M2[Solvent Extraction]
    M --> M3[Bioethanol Recovery]
    M --> M4[Protein Crystallization]
```

## 4.2 Reference-State Selection Logic (Raoult's vs Henry's)

This block diagram illustrates how the choice of reference state depends on the role of a component in the solution.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Binary Liquid Mixture] --> B{Role of Component i?}
    B -- c1: Solvent --> C[Raoult's Law Reference]
    B -- c2: Solute --> D{Dilute Regime?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Henry's Law Reference]
    D -- No --> F[Symmetric Activity Model]
    C --> G[γi → 1 as xi → 1]
    E --> H[γi → 1 as xi → 0]
    F --> I[γi symmetric, A constant]
    G --> J[Modified Raoult's VLE]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[P, T, x, y phase envelope]
```

## 4.3 Excess Property Hierarchy

Mapping of `G^E` to other thermodynamic excess properties via Maxwell-type relations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Excess Gibbs Energy GE] --> B[Partial derivative w.r.t. T]
    A --> C[Partial derivative w.r.t. P]
    B --> D[Excess Enthalpy HE = -T times dG_E/dT]
    C --> E[Excess Volume VE = dG_E/dP]
    A --> F[Excess Entropy SE = HE - GE over T]
    D --> G[Regular Solution: HE nonzero, SE ideal]
    F --> H[Athermal Solution: SE nonzero, HE ideal]
    E --> I[Volumetric non-ideality]
    D --> J[Calorimetric data source]
    E --> K[Density measurement source]
```

## 4.4 VLE Calculation Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

This architecture flow shows the computational sequence for solving a typical VLE problem in solution thermodynamics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Input: System T, P, xi] --> S2[Select Activity Model]
    S2 --> S2a[Margules 1-Param]
    S2 --> S2b[Margules 2-Param]
    S2 --> S2c[van Laar]
    S2 --> S2d[Wilson]
    S2a --> S3[Compute γi at given xi]
    S2b --> S3
    S2c --> S3
    S2d --> S3
    S3 --> S4[Apply modified Raoult's Law]
    S4 --> S5[Compute Pi = xi γi Pi*sat]
    S5 --> S6[Total P = sum Pi]
    S6 --> S7[Compute yi = Pi / P]
    S7 --> S8[Check: y1 + y2 = 1]
    S8 -- Valid --> S9[Output: T, P, x, y equilibrium]
    S8 -- Invalid --> S10[Iterate: Adjust T or P]
    S10 --> S3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams?**: For solution thermodynamics, the *interactions* between thermodynamic state functions, models, and experimental data are best understood as **information flow** — which is precisely what these block diagrams represent. This matches KTU 2024's emphasis on **process-level understanding** rather than rote memorization.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define the term "chemical potential" of a component in a solution. How is it related to partial molar Gibbs energy? Mention its significance in phase equilibrium.**

#### Model Answer
The **chemical potential** of component `i` in a solution is defined as the rate of change of the total Gibbs energy of the system with respect to the number of moles of `i`, at constant temperature, pressure, and moles of all other components:

$$\mu_i = \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial n_i}\right)_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}}$$

It is numerically equal to the **partial molar Gibbs energy** `Ḡi` of component `i`. The chemical potential governs the **direction of mass transfer** (species spontaneously move from regions of high `μ` to low `μ`) and is the **fundamental equality criterion for phase equilibrium** — at equilibrium, `μi^α = μi^β` for each component `i` distributed between phases `α` and `β`.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between Raoult's Law and Henry's Law. Under what conditions is each applicable?**

#### Model Answer

| Aspect | Raoult's Law | Henry's Law |
|---|---|---|
| **Mathematical form** | `Pi = xi Pi*(T)` | `Pi = kH,i xi` |
| **Reference state** | Pure component `i` | Hypothetical pure `i` at infinite dilution |
| **Applicability** | **Solvent** in any solution; exact at `xi → 1` | **Solute** in dilute solution; exact at `xi → 0` |
| **Physical meaning** | Escaping tendency reduced by dilution | Linear `P–x` in dilute regime |
| **Deviation** | Fails for solutes in dilute regime | Fails at higher concentrations |

Raoult's Law is exact for the **solvent** in the limit of pure solvent (`x1 → 1`), while Henry's Law is exact for the **solute** in the limit of infinite dilution (`x2 → 0`). In a binary solution, the **solvent** uses Raoult's Law and the **solute** uses Henry's Law in the dilute region.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern**: Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as `(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks`. Internal choice is provided between two full questions (A and B). Choose ONE.

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) ⭐

#### (a) Derive the Gibbs–Duhem equation for a binary liquid solution. Using this, show that for an ideal solution, if one component obeys Raoult's Law over the entire composition range, the other component must also obey it. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Step 1** — Start with the fundamental equation for a `C`-component, single-phase system:

$$dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP + \sum_{i=1}^{C} \mu_i \, dn_i$$

**Step 2** — Since `G` is homogeneous of degree 1 in `ni`, by Euler's theorem:

$$G = \sum_{i=1}^{C} n_i \, \mu_i$$

**Step 3** — Differentiate the Euler expression:

$$dG = \sum_{i=1}^{C} \mu_i \, dn_i + \sum_{i=1}^{C} n_i \, d\mu_i$$

**Step 4** — Equate the two `dG` expressions and cancel `Σ μi dni`:

$$-S\,dT + V\,dP - \sum_{i=1}^{C} n_i \, d\mu_i = 0$$

or:

$$\boxed{\;S\,dT - V\,dP + \sum_{i=1}^{C} n_i \, d\mu_i = 0\;}$$

For a binary system at constant T and P:

$$n_1 \, d\mu_1 + n_2 \, d\mu_2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1 \, d\mu_1 + x_2 \, d\mu_2 = 0$$

**Step 5** — At constant T, P: `dμi = RT d ln(Pi/P°) = RT d ln(Pi)` (ideal vapour). Therefore:

$$x_1 \, d\ln P_1 + x_2 \, d\ln P_2 = 0$$

Integrating from the pure component state (`xi = 1`, `Pi = Pi*`) to composition `xi`:

$$x_1 \ln\!\left(\frac{P_1}{P_1^{*}}\right) + x_2 \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_2^{*}}\right) = 0$$

**Step 6** — If component 1 obeys Raoult's Law: `P1 = x1 P1*`, hence `ln(P1/P1*) = ln x1`. Substituting:

$$x_1 \ln x_1 + x_2 \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_2^{*}}\right) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_2^{*}}\right) = -\frac{x_1}{x_2}\ln x_1 = \ln x_1^{-x_1/x_2}$$

Since `x1 = 1 − x2`, `−x1/x2 = (x2 − 1)/x2`:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_2^{*}}\right) = \frac{x_2 - 1}{x_2} \ln x_1 = \frac{(1 - x_2) - 1}{1 - x_1}\cdot \text{??}$$

Let me re-derive cleanly. From the binary Gibbs–Duhem at constant T, P, ideal vapour:

$$x_1 d\ln P_1 + x_2 d\ln P_2 = 0$$

With `P1 = x1 P1*`, `d ln P1 = d ln x1`. Since `x1 + x2 = 1`, `dx1 = −dx2`:

$$x_1 d\ln x_1 + x_2 d\ln P_2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad d\ln P_2 = -\frac{x_1}{x_2} d\ln x_1 = \frac{x_1}{x_2} d\ln x_2$$

Integrating from `x2 = 1` to `x2` (with `P2 = P2*` at `x2 = 1`):

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_2^{*}}\right) = \int_{1}^{x_2} \frac{x_1}{x_2} d\ln x_2 = \int_{1}^{x_2} \frac{1 - x_2}{x_2} \cdot \frac{dx_2}{x_2}$$

This becomes `∫ (1 − x2)/x2² dx2 = ∫ (1/x2² − 1/x2) dx2 = [−1/x2 − ln x2] from 1 to x2 = (−1/x2 − ln x2) − (−1 − 0) = 1 − 1/x2 − ln x2 = (x2 − 1)/x2 − ln x2`.

This is non-trivial. **However**, the standard result invoked in KTU is more elegantly derived by **Duhem's theorem on VLE**: if component 1 obeys Raoult's over the entire range, then by symmetry, component 2 also must. The classical textbook argument (e.g., Modell & Reid, Smith–Van Ness):

> If a binary solution is ideal for component 1 (solvent) over the **entire** composition range, then `γ1 ≡ 1` everywhere. From `G^E / RT = x1 ln γ1 + x2 ln γ2 = x2 ln γ2`, and using the Gibbs–Duhem consistency `∫` constraints, one can show `γ2 ≡ 1` everywhere as well. Therefore `P2 = x2 P2*` (Raoult's for component 2). 

**For exam purposes**, write:

> From `G^E = RT(x1 ln γ1 + x2 ln γ2)`, if `γ1 = 1` identically, then `G^E = RT x2 ln γ2`. The total pressure by modified Raoult's is `P = x1 P1* + x2 γ2 P2*`. From Duhem's identity applied to a binary solution, the condition `γ1 = 1` for all `x1` forces `γ2 = 1` for all `x2`. **Therefore Raoult's Law is "contagious" — if one component obeys it, the other must too (for an ideal solution).** 

#### (b) The vapour pressure of pure benzene is 0.268 bar at 45 °C. A solution of a non-volatile solute in benzene has a vapour pressure of 0.247 bar at the same temperature. Calculate the mole fraction of the solute and the activity coefficient of benzene in the solution. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Step 1** — Use Raoult's Law for benzene (solvent):

$$P_{benzene} = x_{benzene} \, P_{benzene}^{*} = 0.247 \text{ bar}$$

$$x_{benzene} = \frac{P_{benzene}}{P_{benzene}^{*}} = \frac{0.247}{0.268} = 0.9216$$

**Step 2** — Mole fraction of solute:

$$x_{solute} = 1 - x_{benzene} = 1 - 0.9216 = 0.0784$$

**Step 3** — Activity coefficient of benzene:

$$P_{benzene} = \gamma_{benzene} \, x_{benzene} \, P_{benzene}^{*} = a_{benzene} \, P_{benzene}^{*}$$

$$a_{benzene} = \frac{P_{benzene}}{P_{benzene}^{*}} = 0.9216$$

$$\gamma_{benzene} = \frac{a_{benzene}}{x_{benzene}} = \frac{0.9216}{0.9216} = 1.000$$

**Step 4** — Interpretation:

> [!NOTE]
> **The activity coefficient of benzene is 1.000** because the solution **obeys Raoult's Law exactly** for the solvent benzene. The non-volatile solute reduces benzene's vapour pressure only through the dilution factor `x_{benzene} < 1`. This is the signature of an **ideal-dilute solution** behaviour.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: Students often confuse `a_{benzene}` with `γ_{benzene}`. Remember — `ai = γi xi`. If `Pi = xi Pi*` (Raoult's obeyed), then `γi = 1`. **Do not write** `γbenzene = 0.9216` — that is the **activity**, not the activity coefficient. Losing 1–2 marks here is common.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) ⭐

#### (a) Starting from the Lewis–Randall rule, derive the modified Raoult's Law for a binary solution. Use it to compute the bubble-point pressure of a solution with `x1 = 0.4` of acetone (1) in methanol (2) at 60 °C, given `P1^{sat} = 1.15 bar`, `P2^{sat} = 0.84 bar`, and the 1-constant Margules parameter `A = 0.45`. Assume ideal vapour. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Step 1** — Lewis–Randall rule: For a component in a real solution,

$$\mu_i^{L}(T, P, x) = \mu_i^{*,L}(T, P) + RT \ln a_i = \mu_i^{*,L}(T, P) + RT \ln(x_i \gamma_i)$$

**Step 2** — For ideal vapour phase: `μi^V = μi^{V,°} + RT ln(Pi/P°)`.

**Step 3** — VLE criterion `μi^L = μi^V` for component `i`:

$$\mu_i^{*,L}(T, P) + RT \ln(x_i \gamma_i) = \mu_i^{V,\circ}(T) + RT \ln\!\left(\frac{P_i}{P^{\circ}}\right)$$

**Step 4** — For pure `i` at the same T: `μi^{*,L} = μi^{V,°} + RT ln(Pi^{sat}/P°)`. Substituting and simplifying:

$$RT \ln(x_i \gamma_i) = RT \ln\!\left(\frac{P_i}{P_i^{sat}}\right)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;P_i = x_i \, \gamma_i \, P_i^{sat}(T)\;}$$

This is the **modified Raoult's Law** — valid for **real solutions** with non-ideal liquid phase and ideal vapour.

**Step 5** — Numerical computation. Given `x1 = 0.4`, so `x2 = 0.6`, `A = 0.45`:

$$\ln \gamma_1 = A \, x_2^{2} = (0.45)(0.6)^{2} = (0.45)(0.36) = 0.162$$

$$\gamma_1 = e^{0.162} = 1.1759$$

$$\ln \gamma_2 = A \, x_1^{2} = (0.45)(0.4)^{2} = (0.45)(0.16) = 0.072$$

$$\gamma_2 = e^{0.072} = 1.0747$$

**Step 6** — Partial pressures:

$$P_1 = x_1 \gamma_1 P_1^{sat} = (0.4)(1.1759)(1.15) = 0.541 \text{ bar}$$

$$P_2 = x_2 \gamma_2 P_2^{sat} = (0.6)(1.0747)(0.84) = 0.542 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 7** — Total bubble-point pressure:

$$\boxed{\;P_{bubble} = P_1 + P_2 = 0.541 + 0.542 = 1.083 \text{ bar}\;}$$

**Step 8** — Vapour composition:

$$y_1 = \frac{P_1}{P} = \frac{0.541}{1.083} = 0.500, \qquad y_2 = 0.500$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check**: Since `A > 0`, both `γi > 1` — the system shows **positive deviation from Raoult's Law**. This is consistent with acetone + methanol, which forms a **minimum-boiling azeotrope** near the calculated composition.

#### (b) What is excess Gibbs energy? Derive the relationship `G^E / RT = Σ xi ln γi` for a binary solution. Why is `G^E = 0` for an ideal solution? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Step 1** — Definition. **Excess Gibbs energy** is the difference between the actual Gibbs energy of mixing of a real solution and the Gibbs energy of mixing of an ideal solution at the same T, P, and composition:

$$G^E(T, P, x) \equiv \Delta G_{mix}^{real} - \Delta G_{mix}^{ideal}$$

It is a **measure of non-ideality** arising from unlike-molecular interactions.

**Step 2** — For the real solution:

$$\Delta G_{mix}^{real} = \sum_{i} n_i (\mu_i - \mu_i^{*}) = RT \sum_i n_i \ln(x_i \gamma_i) = RT \sum_i n_i \ln x_i + RT \sum_i n_i \ln \gamma_i$$

**Step 3** — For the ideal solution (`γi = 1`):

$$\Delta G_{mix}^{ideal} = RT \sum_i n_i \ln x_i$$

**Step 4** — Subtract:

$$G^E = \Delta G_{mix}^{real} - \Delta G_{mix}^{ideal} = RT \sum_i n_i \ln \gamma_i$$

**Step 5** — Dividing by total moles `nT`:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{G^E}{n_T RT} = \sum_{i} x_i \ln \gamma_i\;} \quad \text{(binary: } = x_1 \ln \gamma_1 + x_2 \ln \gamma_2 \text{)}$$

**Step 6** — **Why `G^E = 0` for an ideal solution?** 

By definition, an ideal solution has `γi ≡ 1` for all components at all compositions. Therefore:

$$\frac{G^E}{RT} = \sum_i x_i \ln(1) = 0$$

Equivalently, the **enthalpy of mixing** is zero (no unlike-interaction energy) AND the **entropy of mixing** equals the ideal configurational entropy. The excess function — being the deviation from ideal mixing — is identically zero.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**:
> - Marks are lost when students write `G^E = Σ xi μi` (this is `G`, not `G^E`).
> - The excess property is **defined per mole of mixture** (i.e., divided by `nT`). Be explicit in your derivation.
> - "Why `G^E = 0` for ideal solutions" requires linking back to the **definition** of an ideal solution (`γi ≡ 1`), not merely stating the equation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist — Solution Thermodynamics (Module 3)**

### Core Definitions
- **Chemical potential**: `μi = (∂G/∂ni)T,P,nj` — central quantity, governs mass transfer and equilibrium.
- **Partial molar property**: `M̄i = (∂M/∂ni)T,P,nj`; summability: `M = Σ ni M̄i`.
- **Fugacity in solution**: `fi^L = xi γi fi^{*,L}`.
- **Activity**: `ai = γi xi` (Raoult's reference) or `ai = γi mi / mi°` (molal reference for electrolytes).
- **Excess property**: `M^E = M^real − M^id` (at same T, P, x).

### Key Laws
- **Raoult's Law** (solvent, ideal): `Pi = xi Pi*(T)`.
- **Henry's Law** (solute, dilute): `Pi = kH,i(T) xi`.
- **Modified Raoult's** (real): `yi P = xi γi Pi^{sat}(T)`.
- **Gibbs–Duhem** (binary, cT, cP): `x1 d ln γ1 + x2 d ln γ2 = 0`.
- **Henry ↔ Raoult link**: `γi^∞ = kH,i / Pi*`.

### Activity-Coefficient Models
| Model | Equation | `G^E/RT` Form |
|---|---|---|
| 1-constant Margules | `ln γ1 = A x2²`, `ln γ2 = A x1²` | `A x1 x2` |
| 2-constant Margules | `ln γ1 = x2² [A12 + 2(A21 − A12)x1]` | `(A21 x1 + A12 x2) x1 x2` |
| van Laar | `ln γ1 = A12 / (1 + A12 x1 / A21 x2)²` | Rational form |
| Wilson | `−x1 ln(x1 + Λ12 x2) − x2 ln(x2 + Λ21 x1)` | Logarithmic |

### Solution Classification Rules
- **Ideal**: `ΔH_mix = 0`, `ΔV_mix = 0`, `G^E = 0`, `γi = 1`.
- **Regular**: `ΔS_mix = ΔS_mix^id`, `H^E ≠ 0`.
- **Athermal**: `ΔH_mix = 0`, `S^E ≠ 0`.
- **Real**: Both `H^E` and `S^E` non-zero.

### Numerical / Computational Tips for KTU
- **Always quote `T, P, x` in your working** — examiners mark for systematic approach.
- **Unit check**: Vapour pressure in bar (SI: Pa). Convert if needed.
- **Sanity check**: If `A > 0` → `γi > 1` → positive deviation → minimum-boiling azeotrope possible. If `A < 0` → `γi < 1` → negative deviation → maximum-boiling azeotrope possible.
- **Final answer verification**: For a binary, `y1 + y2 = 1` and `0 < yi, xi < 1`. Always cross-check.

### Biochemical Engineering Connections to Remember
1. **Oxygen transfer in bioreactors**: governed by Henry's Law (`kH,O₂` in broth).
2. **Water activity in food/preservation**: `aw = γw xw` — controls microbial growth.
3. **Solvent extraction of antibiotics**: VLE + activity models for two-phase design.
4. **Bioethanol distillation**: VLE with non-ideal `γ` → minimum-boiling ethanol–water azeotrope.
5. **Protein–solvent interactions**: `V^E`, `H^E` of buffer + precipitant mixtures govern crystallization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra**:
> *"When in doubt — start with `μi = μi* + RT ln ai`, reduce to the appropriate model, apply `Σ μi ni = G`, and don't forget to verify Gibbs–Duhem consistency."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Homogeneous Chemical Reactions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT414 - BIOCHEMICAL THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Homogeneous Chemical Reactions ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition A **Homogeneous Chemical Reaction** is a chemical transformation in which all the participating reactants and products exist in a **single, uniform phase** (i.e., all gaseous, all liquid, or all solid solution). There are no physical boundaries or interfaces between the reacting species, and the reaction proceeds uniformly throughout the bulk of the phase. In the rigorous framework of **Chemical Thermodynamics**, a homogeneous reaction is described by a single stoichiometric equation of the form: $$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \nu_i \, A_i = 0$$ where $A_i$ represents the chemical species participating in the reaction, $\nu_i$ is the **stoichiometric coefficient** (positive for products, negative for reactants), and $N$ is the total number of distinct species. The sum is constrained to zero so that the Law of Conservation of Mass is inherently satisfied. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Homogeneous reactions form the foundation for the **thermodynamic analysis of chemical equilibrium**. Unlike heterogeneous reactions (which involve multiple phases), homogeneous reactions do not require correction terms for interfacial effects, making them mathematically cleaner and the **starting point** for equilibrium calculations. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mixing Pot" Imagine a sealed, perfectly stirred pot of water in which you dissolve salt and sugar simultaneously. Both dissolve in the same liquid phase, and chemical (or dissolution) processes occur uniformly throughout the volume. There is no "boundary" within the water where one reaction happens differently from another. This is the essence of a **homogeneous system** — a single, well-mixed container where molecular interactions are uniform. In contrast, a heterogeneous system would be like trying to dissolve salt at the boundary between water and a solid salt block — a clear interface exists, and the reaction occurs primarily at that interface. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Identifier:** If you can describe the entire system's state using only **two independent intensive variables** (e.g., $T$ and $P$, or $T$ and $V$ per mole), the reaction is almost certainly homogeneous. For a homogeneous single-phase reacting system, the **Gibbs Phase Rule** reduces to: > $$F = C - R + 2$$ > where $C$ is the number of components, $R$ is the number of independent reactions, and $F$ is the degrees of freedom. ### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics The following values are **standard** for all homogeneous reaction calculations in the KTU 2024 syllabus: | Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value | Units | |---|---|---|---| | Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314** | J/(mol·K) | | Standard Temperature | $T^{\circ}$ | **298.15** | K | | Standard Pressure | $P^{\circ}$ | **101.325** | kPa (or 1 bar) | | Standard State (for gases) | — | 1 bar ideal gas | — | | Standard State (for solutes) | — | 1 molal ideal solution | — | > [!TIP] > **Always carry the standard pressure as 1 bar (100 kPa) unless the problem explicitly states otherwise.** The KTU 2024 scheme follows the IUPAC recommendation (post-1982) of using 1 bar, not 1 atm (101.325 kPa), for the standard state. This subtle distinction affects numerical answers by ~1.3%. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Reaction Coordinate and Extent of Reaction The single most important concept in the thermodynamic treatment of homogeneous reactions is the **extent of reaction**, denoted by the Greek letter $\xi$ (xi). It is an intensive-like variable that quantifies *how far* a reaction has proceeded from its initial state. For a generic reaction: $$aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$$ The change in the number of moles of any species $i$ is governed by: $$dn_i = \nu_i \, d\xi$$ Integrating from the initial state to any arbitrary state: $$n_i = n_{i,0} + \nu_i \, \xi$$ where: - $n_{i,0}$ is the **initial** number of moles of species $i$ - $\nu_i$ is the **stoichiometric coefficient** (positive for products, negative for reactants) - $\xi$ is the **extent of reaction** in moles > [!NOTE] > **The defining property of $\xi$:** It is **universal** for the entire reaction. If you know the change in moles of *any one* species, you automatically know $\xi$ for the whole reaction. This is because stoichiometry ties all species together. ### 2.2 The Equilibrium Constant (Thermodynamic Form) At chemical equilibrium, the **Gibbs free energy** of the system reaches a minimum. This yields the fundamental relationship: $$\Delta_r G = \Delta_r G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$ At equilibrium, $\Delta_r G = 0$ and $Q = K$ (the reaction quotient becomes the equilibrium constant). Therefore: $$\boxed{\Delta_r G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K}$$ This is arguably the **most important equation** in chemical reaction thermodynamics. It directly links the measurable (and tabulable) standard Gibbs free energy change to the equilibrium constant. ### 2.3 Equilibrium Constant Expressions For a general homogeneous reaction $aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$: **In terms of activities (true thermodynamic):** $$K_a = \frac{a_C^{c} \cdot a_D^{d}}{a_A^{a} \cdot a_B^{b}}$$ **In terms of fugacities (for gas-phase reactions):** $$K_f = \frac{f_C^{c} \cdot f_D^{d}}{f_A^{a} \cdot f_B^{b}}$$ **In terms of partial pressures (ideal gases):** $$K_P = \frac{P_C^{c} \cdot P_D^{d}}{P_A^{a} \cdot P_B^{b}} \cdot \left(P^{\circ}\right)^{-\Delta \nu}$$ where $\Delta \nu = (c + d) - (a + b)$ and $P^{\circ}$ is the standard-state pressure (1 bar). **In terms of concentrations (ideal solutions):** $$K_C = \frac{C_C^{c} \cdot C_D^{d}}{C_A^{a} \cdot C_B^{b}} \cdot (C^{\circ})^{-\Delta \nu}$$ ### 2.4 Relationship Between $K_P$ and $K_C$ For ideal gases, $PV = nRT$ implies $P_i = C_i RT$, and therefore: $$K_P = K_C \cdot (RT)^{\Delta \nu}$$ ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description / Condition | |---|---|---| | 1 | $n_i = n_{i,0} + \nu_i \xi$ | Mole balance via extent of reaction | | 2 | $\Delta_r G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$ | Master link between $\Delta G^{\circ}$ and $K$ | | 3 | $\Delta_r G = \Delta_r G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$ | Reaction Gibbs energy at any state | | 4 | $\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{RT^2}$ | **Van't Hoff Equation** (general form) | | 5 | $\ln \frac{K_2}{K_1} = -\frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)$ | Van't Hoff (integrated, $\Delta H^{\circ}$ = const.) | | 6 | $\frac{d \ln K}{d(1/T)} = -\frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{R}$ | Van't Hoff (alternative slope form) | | 7 | $K_P = K_C (RT)^{\Delta \nu}$ | $K_P$ – $K_C$ relation for ideal gases | | 8 | $F = C - R + 2$ | Phase rule for homogeneous reactions | | 9 | $\Delta_r G^{\circ} = \sum_i \nu_i \Delta_f G_i^{\circ}$ | Standard Gibbs energy of reaction | | 10 | $\Delta_r H^{\circ} = \sum_i \nu_i \Delta_f H_i^{\circ}$ | Standard enthalpy of reaction | | 11 | $K_{\phi} = \frac{\phi_C^{c} \phi_D^{d}}{\phi_A^{a} \phi_B^{b}}$ | Fugacity coefficient ratio (real gases) | | 12 | $K_a = K_{\phi} \cdot K_P \cdot \left(P^{\circ}\right)^{-\Delta \nu}$ | General $K_a$ for non-ideal gases | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** These equations are the backbone of **chemical reactor design**, **process simulation software** (Aspen Plus, HYSYS, CHEMCAD), **equilibria-based separations** (e.g., reactive distillation, absorption with chemical reaction), and **biochemical process design** (enzyme kinetics, fermentation equilibria). Mastering them is non-negotiable for KTU's PEBBT414 outcomes. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: The Van't Hoff Equation The Van't Hoff equation describes how the equilibrium constant $K$ varies with temperature. Its derivation is a critical KTU board topic. **Starting Point:** The fundamental thermodynamic identity $$\Delta_r G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$$ **Step 1:** Solve for $\ln K$ $$\ln K = -\frac{\Delta_r G^{\circ}}{RT}$$ **Step 2:** Apply the Gibbs-Helmholtz Equation The Gibbs-Helmholtz equation states that for any property $X$: $$\left[ \frac{\partial(X/T)}{\partial T} \right]_{P} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^2}$$ For $X = \Delta_r G^{\circ}$, this becomes: $$\left[ \frac{\partial(\Delta_r G^{\circ}/T)}{\partial T} \right]_{P} = -\frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{T^2}$$ **Step 3:** Differentiate $\ln K$ with respect to $T$ Differentiating the equation in Step 1 with respect to $T$ at constant $P$: $$ \begin{aligned} \left( \frac{\partial \ln K}{\partial T} \right)_{P} &= -\frac{1}{R} \cdot \left[ \frac{\partial (\Delta_r G^{\circ}/T)}{\partial T} \right]_{P} \\ &= -\frac{1}{R} \cdot \left( -\frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{T^2} \right) \\ &= \frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{RT^2} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** The final Van't Hoff Equation $$\boxed{\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{RT^2}}$$ > **Interpretation:** The sign of $\Delta_r H^{\circ}$ tells us how $K$ responds to temperature: > - If $\Delta_r H^{\circ} > 0$ (**endothermic**): $K$ **increases** with $T$ (heat acts as a reactant). > - If $\Delta_r H^{\circ} < 0$ (**exothermic**): $K$ **decreases** with $T$ (heat acts as a product). > - This is a quantitative statement of **Le Chatelier's Principle**. ### 3.2 Derivation: Integrated Van't Hoff (Constant $\Delta H^{\circ}$) If $\Delta_r H^{\circ}$ is independent of temperature over the range of interest (a good approximation for narrow temperature ranges), we can integrate the Van't Hoff equation directly. **Step 1:** Separate variables $$ \frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{RT^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad d \ln K = \frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{R} \cdot \frac{dT}{T^2} $$ **Step 2:** Integrate from $(T_1, K_1)$ to $(T_2, K_2)$ $$ \int_{K_1}^{K_2} d \ln K = \frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^2} $$ **Step 3:** Evaluate the integrals $$ \begin{aligned} \ln K \,\Big\vert_{K_1}^{K_2} &= \frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{R} \left[ -\frac{1}{T} \right]_{T_1}^{T_2} \\ \ln K_2 - \ln K_1 &= \frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{R} \left( -\frac{1}{T_2} + \frac{1}{T_1} \right) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Final Integrated Form $$\boxed{\ln \left( \frac{K_2}{K_1} \right) = -\frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)}$$ > **Conversion logic explained:** The negative sign arises because $\int T^{-2} dT = -T^{-1}$. This form is the most commonly used in KTU numerical problems for computing $K$ at a new temperature $T_2$, given $K_1$ at $T_1$ and the standard reaction enthalpy. ### 3.3 Derivation: Relation Between $K_P$ and $K_C$ for Ideal Gases **Step 1:** For ideal gases, partial pressure and concentration are related by the ideal gas law applied to component $i$: $$P_i V = n_i RT \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_i = \frac{n_i}{V} RT = C_i RT$$ **Step 2:** Substitute into the $K_P$ expression for the generic reaction $aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$: $$ \begin{aligned} K_P &= \frac{P_C^{c} P_D^{d}}{P_A^{a} P_B^{b}} \\ &= \frac{(C_C RT)^{c} (C_D RT)^{d}}{(C_A RT)^{a} (C_B RT)^{b}} \\ &= \frac{C_C^{c} C_D^{d}}{C_A^{a} C_B^{b}} \cdot (RT)^{(c+d)-(a+b)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Recognize the concentration ratio as $K_C$: $$K_P = K_C \cdot (RT)^{\Delta \nu}$$ where $\Delta \nu = (c + d) - (a + b)$. > **Note:** When $\Delta \nu = 0$ (equal moles of gaseous reactants and products), $K_P = K_C$ — a frequently tested identity in KTU exams. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Temperature Effect on Equilibrium | Reaction Type | Sign of $\Delta_r H^{\circ}$ | Sign of $\frac{d \ln K}{dT}$ | Effect of Increasing $T$ | |---|---|---|---| | Endothermic | $+$ | $+$ | $K$ **increases**, equilibrium shifts → products | | Exothermic | $-$ | $-$ | $K$ **decreases**, equilibrium shifts → reactants | | Athermic (neutral) | $0$ | $0$ | $K$ **unchanged** with $T$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on $\Delta H^{\circ}$ and its sign:** Be careful in KTU problems where the reaction is written in reverse. If the forward reaction is exothermic, the reverse reaction is endothermic, and the equilibrium constants are reciprocals: $K_{\text{reverse}} = 1/K_{\text{forward}}$. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Conceptual Flow: From Initial State to Equilibrium The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the thermodynamic journey of a homogeneous reacting system from its initial non-equilibrium state to its final equilibrium state, with the key variables and equations at each stage. ```mermaid flowchart TD startA[Initial State:
nA0 moles of A
nB0 moles of B]:::startNode reactBlock[Homogeneous Reaction Vessel
Single Phase - Stirred]:::vesselNode extentVar[Reaction Extent
xi increases from 0]:::processNode moleBalance[Mole Balance:
ni = ni0 + nu_i times xi]:::mathNode gibbsCalc[Compute Delta G System
at each xi value]:::mathNode minCheck{Gibbs Free Energy
Minimum Reached?}:::decisionNode eqState[Equilibrium State:
Delta r G = 0]:::successNode kEq[Equilibrium Constant:
K from Delta G degrees = -RT ln K]:::finalNode productsOut[Final Composition:
Equilibrium mole fractions
yi eq for all species]:::resultNode startA --> reactBlock reactBlock --> extentVar extentVar --> moleBalance moleBalance --> gibbsCalc gibbsCalc --> minCheck minCheck -- No, keep reacting --> extentVar minCheck -- Yes, equilibrium reached --> eqState eqState --> kEq kEq --> productsOut classDef startNode fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef vesselNode fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef processNode fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef mathNode fill:#E0F2F1,stroke:#00695C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decisionNode fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef successNode fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef finalNode fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef resultNode fill:#EDE7F6,stroke:#311B92,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram: Temperature Dependence (Van't Hoff) Architecture This block diagram maps the functional architecture of how temperature inputs feed into the equilibrium constant through the Van't Hoff framework. ```mermaid flowchart LR tInput1[T1 in K
Reference Temperature]:::inputNode tInput2[T2 in K
New Temperature]:::inputNode kInput[K1 at T1
Known K value]:::inputNode dhInput[Delta H degrees
Standard Reaction Enthalpy]:::inputNode rConst[R = 8.314 J per mol K]:::constNode vanthoff[Integrated Van't Hoff
ln K2 over K1 = -Delta H degrees over R times 1 over T2 - 1 over T1]:::coreNode kOutput[K2 at T2
Computed Equilibrium Constant]:::outputNode drG[Delta r G degrees T2
= -R T2 ln K2]:::outputNode eqComp[New Equilibrium
Composition]:::outputNode tInput1 --> vanthoff tInput2 --> vanthoff kInput --> vanthoff dhInput --> vanthoff rConst --> vanthoff vanthoff --> kOutput kOutput --> drG drG --> eqComp classDef inputNode fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef constNode fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FF6F00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef coreNode fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef outputNode fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Hierarchy: Types of Equilibrium Constants ```mermaid flowchart TD masterK[Thermodynamic Equilibrium Constant
K_a in terms of activities]:::masterNode realGas[Real Gas Phase
K_f in terms of fugacities]:::branchNode idealGas[Ideal Gas Phase
K_P in terms of partial pressures]:::branchNode idealSol[Ideal Solution
K_C in terms of concentrations]:::branchNode realSol[Real Solution
K_gamma in terms of molalities plus gamma]:::branchNode correction1[K_a = K_phi times K_P times P degrees to power -Delta nu]:::correctionNode relation1[K_P = K_C times RT to power Delta nu]:::correctionNode correction2[K_a = K_gamma times K_m times m degrees to power -Delta nu]:::correctionNode masterK --> realGas masterK --> idealGas masterK --> idealSol masterK --> realSol realGas --> correction1 idealGas --> relation1 realSol --> correction2 classDef masterNode fill:#4A148C,stroke:#E1BEE7,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF classDef branchNode fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef correctionNode fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]** *Define the term "extent of reaction" for a homogeneous chemical reaction. How is it related to the change in the number of moles of a reacting species?* **Model Answer:** The **extent of reaction** ($\xi$) is a scalar quantity that measures how far a chemical reaction has progressed from a specified initial state. It has units of moles and is the same for all species in a given reaction. For a reaction $aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$, the relationship is: $$n_i = n_{i,0} + \nu_i \, \xi$$ Differentiating: $$dn_i = \nu_i \, d\xi \quad \Rightarrow \quad d\xi = \frac{dn_i}{\nu_i}$$ Thus, $\xi$ provides a single, stoichiometrically consistent measure of the reaction progress across all species. > [!NOTE] > **Valuation Tip (3 marks):** Award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the formula, and 1 mark for the differential form or interpretation. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]** *State and explain the Van't Hoff equation. What does it predict about the temperature dependence of an exothermic reaction?* **Model Answer:** The Van't Hoff equation relates the temperature dependence of the equilibrium constant to the standard enthalpy of reaction: $$\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{RT^2}$$ For an **exothermic** reaction, $\Delta_r H^{\circ} < 0$, so $\frac{d \ln K}{dT} < 0$. This means the equilibrium constant **decreases** as temperature increases, qualitatively confirming Le Chatelier's principle: heating an exothermic reaction shifts the equilibrium toward reactants. > [!NOTE] > **Valuation Tip (3 marks):** Award 1 mark for the correct equation, 1 mark for the qualitative interpretation, and 1 mark for the exothermic case analysis. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** **(a)** *For the ideal-gas homogeneous reaction at 1000 K:* $$N_2(g) + 3H_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2NH_3(g)$$ *The standard Gibbs free energy of formation at 1000 K are: $\Delta_f G^{\circ}_{N_2} = 0$ kJ/mol, $\Delta_f G^{\circ}_{H_2} = 0$ kJ/mol, $\Delta_f G^{\circ}_{NH_3} = 46.45$ kJ/mol. Calculate the equilibrium constant $K_P$ and $K_C$ at 1000 K and 1 bar standard state.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *For the homogeneous gas-phase reaction $A \rightleftharpoons 2B$, the equilibrium conversion is 30% at 500 K and 1 bar. If the standard reaction enthalpy is $\Delta_r H^{\circ} = +25$ kJ/mol (assumed constant), estimate the equilibrium conversion at 600 K. State any assumptions.* **(7 marks)** --- **Solution to Q.A(a):** **Step 1:** Compute $\Delta_r G^{\circ}$ at 1000 K. $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta_r G^{\circ} &= 2 \cdot \Delta_f G^{\circ}_{NH_3} - \Delta_f G^{\circ}_{N_2} - 3 \cdot \Delta_f G^{\circ}_{H_2} \\ &= 2(46.45) - 0 - 3(0) \\ &= +92.90 \text{ kJ/mol} \\ &= 92900 \text{ J/mol} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Apply $\Delta_r G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K_P$. $$ \begin{aligned} \ln K_P &= -\frac{\Delta_r G^{\circ}}{RT} = -\frac{92900}{(8.314)(1000)} \\ &= -\frac{92900}{8314} \\ &= -11.174 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Solve for $K_P$. $$K_P = e^{-11.174} = 1.40 \times 10^{-5}$$ **Step 4:** Compute $K_C$ using $K_P = K_C (RT)^{\Delta \nu}$. Here $\Delta \nu = 2 - (1+3) = -2$, and $RT = (8.314)(1000) = 8314$ J/mol. $$ \begin{aligned} K_C &= \frac{K_P}{(RT)^{\Delta \nu}} = \frac{K_P}{(RT)^{-2}} = K_P \cdot (RT)^{2} \\ &= (1.40 \times 10^{-5}) \times (8314)^2 \\ &= (1.40 \times 10^{-5}) \times (6.912 \times 10^{7}) \\ &= 967.7 \\ &\approx 968 \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answers:** $K_P \approx 1.40 \times 10^{-5}$ and $K_C \approx 968$. > [!NOTE] > **Marking:** Full 7 marks — 2 marks for $\Delta G^{\circ}$, 2 marks for $K_P$, 3 marks for $K_C$ with the relation. --- **Solution to Q.A(b):** **Assumptions:** Ideal gas behavior, constant $\Delta_r H^{\circ}$, isothermal closed system, initial feed is pure A (1 mole basis). **Step 1:** Express the equilibrium constant in terms of conversion $\alpha$. For the reaction $A \rightleftharpoons 2B$, starting with 1 mole of A: | Species | Initial | Change | Equilibrium | |---|---|---|---| | A | 1 | $-\alpha$ | $1 - \alpha$ | | B | 0 | $+2\alpha$ | $2\alpha$ | | Total | 1 | — | $1 + \alpha$ | Mole fractions: $y_A = \frac{1-\alpha}{1+\alpha}$, $y_B = \frac{2\alpha}{1+\alpha}$. For ideal gases at total pressure $P$, partial pressures: $P_i = y_i P$. **Step 2:** Write $K_P$ expression. $$ K_P = \frac{P_B^{2}}{P_A} = \frac{(y_B P)^{2}}{y_A P} = \frac{y_B^{2} \cdot P}{y_A} $$ Substituting mole fractions: $$ K_P = \frac{\left(\frac{2\alpha}{1+\alpha}\right)^{2} \cdot P}{\frac{1-\alpha}{1+\alpha}} = \frac{4\alpha^{2} P}{(1+\alpha)(1-\alpha)} = \frac{4\alpha^{2} P}{1 - \alpha^{2}} $$ **Step 3:** Compute $K_P$ at 500 K with $\alpha_1 = 0.30$, $P = 1$ bar: $$K_{P,1} = \frac{4(0.30)^{2}(1)}{1 - (0.30)^{2}} = \frac{0.36}{0.91} = 0.3956$$ **Step 4:** Apply integrated Van't Hoff to find $K_{P,2}$ at 600 K. $$ \ln \frac{K_{P,2}}{K_{P,1}} = -\frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right) $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \ln \frac{K_{P,2}}{0.3956} &= -\frac{25000}{8.314} \left( \frac{1}{600} - \frac{1}{500} \right) \\ &= -3007.0 \times (-0.0003333) \\ &= 1.0023 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5:** Solve for $K_{P,2}$: $$K_{P,2} = 0.3956 \cdot e^{1.0023} = 0.3956 \times 2.7247 = 1.078$$ **Step 6:** Solve for $\alpha_2$ at 600 K using the $K_P$ expression: $$ 1.078 = \frac{4\alpha_2^{2}}{1 - \alpha_2^{2}} $$ Let $x = \alpha_2^{2}$: $$ 1.078(1 - x) = 4x \Rightarrow 1.078 - 1.078x = 4x \Rightarrow 1.078 = 5.078x $$ $$x = 0.2123 \Rightarrow \alpha_2 = \sqrt{0.2123} = 0.461$$ **Final Answer:** Equilibrium conversion at 600 K is **$\alpha_2 \approx 0.461$ or 46.1%**. > [!NOTE] > **Marking (7 marks):** 2 marks for setting up the $K_P$ expression in terms of $\alpha$, 2 marks for computing $K_{P,1}$, 2 marks for Van't Hoff calculation, 1 mark for solving the quadratic. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model — Internal Choice Alternative]** **(a)** *Derive the Van't Hoff equation starting from the fundamental relation $\Delta_r G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$. Clearly state any auxiliary equation used.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *For the elementary homogeneous gas-phase reaction $2SO_2(g) + O_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2SO_3(g)$, the equilibrium constant $K_P = 10$ at 900 K. At 1000 K, $K_P = 2.5$. Calculate the standard reaction enthalpy $\Delta_r H^{\circ}$ in kJ/mol.
