Comment on whether the reaction is exothermic or endothermic.* **(7 marks)** --- **Solution to Q.B(a):** **Starting Point:** The fundamental identity linking standard Gibbs energy and equilibrium constant is: $$\Delta_r G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K \quad \text{...(1)}$$ Rearranging: $$\ln K = -\frac{\Delta_r G^{\circ}}{RT} \quad \text{...(2)}$$ **Auxiliary Equation Used:** The **Gibbs-Helmholtz Equation**: $$\left[ \frac{\partial(\Delta_r G^{\circ}/T)}{\partial T} \right]_{P} = -\frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{T^2} \quad \text{...(3)}$$ **Derivation:** Differentiate equation (2) with respect to $T$ at constant $P$: $$ \left( \frac{\partial \ln K}{\partial T} \right)_{P} = -\frac{1}{R} \left[ \frac{\partial (\Delta_r G^{\circ}/T)}{\partial T} \right]_{P} $$ Substitute equation (3) into the right-hand side: $$ \left( \frac{\partial \ln K}{\partial T} \right)_{P} = -\frac{1}{R} \cdot \left( -\frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{T^2} \right) $$ $$ \boxed{\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{RT^2}} $$ This is the **Van't Hoff Equation**, which quantitatively describes how the equilibrium constant changes with temperature, governed by the sign and magnitude of $\Delta_r H^{\circ}$. **Marking (7 marks):** 2 marks for starting equation, 2 marks for the Gibbs-Helmholtz equation statement, 2 marks for differentiation steps, 1 mark for final boxed result. --- **Solution to Q.B(b):** **Given:** - Reaction: $2SO_2 + O_2 \rightleftharpoons 2SO_3$ - $K_{P,1} = 10$ at $T_1 = 900$ K - $K_{P,2} = 2.5$ at $T_2 = 1000$ K - Assume $\Delta_r H^{\circ}$ is constant over this range. **Step 1:** Apply the integrated Van't Hoff equation: $$\ln \frac{K_{P,2}}{K_{P,1}} = -\frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)$$ **Step 2:** Compute the left-hand side: $$ \ln \frac{2.5}{10} = \ln(0.25) = -1.3863 $$ **Step 3:** Compute the temperature term: $$ \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{1000} - \frac{1}{900} = 0.001000 - 0.001111 = -1.111 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1} $$ **Step 4:** Solve for $\Delta_r H^{\circ}$: $$ -1.3863 = -\frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{8.314} \cdot (-1.111 \times 10^{-4}) $$ $$ -1.3863 = \frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{8.314} \cdot (1.111 \times 10^{-4}) $$ $$ \Delta_r H^{\circ} = \frac{-1.3863 \times 8.314}{1.111 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{-11.524}{1.111 \times 10^{-4}} $$ $$ \Delta_r H^{\circ} = -103{,}720 \text{ J/mol} \approx -103.7 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ **Step 5:** Comment on the sign. Since $\Delta_r H^{\circ} < 0$, the reaction is **exothermic** in the forward direction. This is consistent with the observation that $K_P$ **decreased** from 10 (at 900 K) to 2.5 (at 1000 K) as temperature increased — Le Chatelier's principle: heating an exothermic reaction shifts equilibrium toward reactants. > [!NOTE] > **Marking (7 marks):** 2 marks for the correct formula, 2 marks for substitution, 2 marks for numerical answer, 1 mark for the exothermic comment. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in KTU Board Exams (PEBBT414):** > 1. **Sign error in stoichiometric coefficients:** Products get **positive** $\nu_i$, reactants get **negative** $\nu_i$. Many students invert this. > 2. **Forgetting the standard state correction in $K_P$:** When using $K_a = K_{\phi} K_P (P^{\circ})^{-\Delta \nu}$, students often omit the $(P^{\circ})^{-\Delta \nu}$ factor, leading to wrong units. > 3. **Using 1 atm (101.325 kPa) instead of 1 bar (100 kPa) as the standard state:** Post-1982 IUPAC convention requires 1 bar. KTU strictly follows this. Marks are deducted for using the older convention. > 4. **Unit inconsistency in Van't Hoff:** If $\Delta_r H^{\circ}$ is in kJ/mol, divide $R$ accordingly (use 0.008314 kJ/mol·K), or convert $\Delta_r H^{\circ}$ to J/mol first. > 5. **Mixing $K_P$ and $K_C$ in the same equation:** Always check the units. $K_P$ is dimensionless when partial pressures are divided by $P^{\circ}$. > 6. **Reversing the reaction in the middle of a problem:** $K_{\text{reverse}} = 1/K_{\text{forward}}$, and $\Delta_r H^{\circ}_{\text{reverse}} = -\Delta_r H^{\circ}_{\text{forward}}$. Many students forget to flip the sign. > 7. **Not specifying the assumption of constant $\Delta H^{\circ}$:** If you use the integrated form of Van't Hoff, you **must** state the assumption that $\Delta_r H^{\circ}$ is temperature-independent over the range. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember **Rapid Revision Checklist:** - A **homogeneous reaction** has all reactants and products in a single phase. - The **extent of reaction** $\xi$ is a unified measure of reaction progress: $n_i = n_{i,0} + \nu_i \xi$. - The **master equation** is $\Delta_r G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$. It connects thermodynamics (tabulated $\Delta G^{\circ}$ values) to chemical equilibrium (measurable $K$). - The **reaction quotient** $Q$ extends the equilibrium constant expression to non-equilibrium states: $\Delta_r G = \Delta_r G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$. - The **Van't Hoff equation** is $\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{RT^2}$. Its integrated form (constant $\Delta H^{\circ}$) is $\ln(K_2/K_1) = -(\Delta_r H^{\circ}/R)(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$. - For ideal gases: $K_P = K_C (RT)^{\Delta \nu}$. When $\Delta \nu = 0$, $K_P = K_C$. - For non-ideal gases: $K_a = K_{\phi} K_P (P^{\circ})^{-\Delta \nu}$, with the standard state $P^{\circ} = 1$ bar. - **Le Chatelier's Principle** is quantified by the sign of $\Delta_r H^{\circ}$ in the Van't Hoff equation. - The **Phase Rule** for a homogeneous reacting system reduces to $F = C - R + 2$ (no interfacial phases). - **Always declare assumptions**: ideal behavior, constant $\Delta H^{\circ}$, isothermal conditions, and standard state. - **Unit discipline**: $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) — keep $\Delta H^{\circ}$ and $\Delta G^{\circ}$ in J/mol for direct substitution, or convert $R$ to kJ/(mol·K). - Equilibrium constants are **dimensionless** only when activities/pressures are referenced to the standard state. $K_P$ from raw partial pressures carries units of (bar)$^{\Delta \nu}$. - The sign convention for $\nu_i$ is critical: **negative** for reactants, **positive** for products, summing to zero in a balanced equation.

topics.title: Effect of Pressure and Temperature on Equilibrium Constant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT414 - BIOCHEMICAL THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Effect of Pressure and Temperature on Equilibrium Constant

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **equilibrium constant** ($K$) of a chemical reaction quantifies the ratio of product activities to reactant activities at equilibrium. For a generic reversible reaction:

$$aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$$

the thermodynamic equilibrium constant is rigorously defined as:

$$K = \frac{a_C^{\,c} \; a_D^{\,d}}{a_A^{\,a} \; a_B^{\,b}}$$

where $a_i$ denotes the **activity** of species $i$ at equilibrium, referenced to a chosen standard state.

For ideal-gas systems the activities are replaced by fugacities, and for ideal solutions by molalities or mole fractions. The numerical value of $K$ depends explicitly on **temperature** and (for reactions in condensed phases subjected to very high pressure) on **pressure**, but is *independent* of the initial composition or total pressure for ideal-gas reactions. This subtle distinction—pressure shifting *composition* versus pressure shifting *the constant itself*—is a frequent point of confusion in KTU examinations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The two canonical variables that govern the magnitude of $K$ are **temperature** and (in the case of real gases or condensed phases) **pressure**. Composition variables (concentrations, partial pressures, mole fractions) only shift the *position* of equilibrium; they do not alter $K$ itself.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — A Mechanical Spring in a Heated Room

Imagine a perfectly elastic spring sitting on a table. The **spring constant** $k$ (analogous to $K$) is a property of the metal itself. Now consider two perturbations:

- **You push the spring sideways with your hand (analogy: pressure/composition change).** The spring compresses by some amount, but as soon as you remove your hand, it returns to its original equilibrium length. The spring constant $k$ has not changed. *In the same way, changing total pressure (for ideal gases) does not change $K$; it merely shifts the equilibrium composition.*

- **You heat the entire room (analogy: temperature change).** The thermal energy alters the interatomic bond stiffness — the spring constant itself changes! *In the same way, temperature is the only intensive variable that fundamentally changes the value of $K$ for an ideal-gas reaction.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used Throughout This Module:**
> - Universal gas constant: **$R = 8.314 \; \text{J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$**
> - Standard pressure: **$P^{\circ} = 1 \; \text{bar} = 10^{5} \; \text{Pa}$**
> - Standard temperature: **$T^{\circ} = 298.15 \; \text{K}$**
> - 1 calorie = **$4.184 \; \text{J}$** (useful for thermochemical data)

## 1.3 Why the Distinction Matters in Biochemical Thermodynamics

In living systems, most reactions occur in **aqueous (condensed) phase** under nearly isobaric, isothermal conditions. Therefore, for biochemical equilibria, the *pressure dependence* of $K$ is usually negligible (the partial molar volumes $\bar{V}_i$ are small, and biological pressures are modest). The *temperature dependence*, however, is profoundly important — every $0.1 \,^\circ\text{C}$ change in body temperature alters the equilibrium position of metabolic reactions, and thermophilic enzymes have evolved specifically to retain function across a wider temperature range.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The Two Influences Decoupled

We treat pressure and temperature effects separately because they enter the thermodynamic master equation through *different* partial derivatives.

### 2.1.1 Temperature Effect — The Master Tool is the van't Hoff Equation

Starting from the fundamental relation $\Delta_r G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$, and recalling that the **Gibbs–Helmholtz equation** gives:

$$\left( \frac{\partial(\Delta_r G^{\circ}/T)}{\partial T} \right)_{P} = -\frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{T^{2}}$$

Substituting $\Delta_r G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$ and differentiating yields the **van't Hoff equation** in its differential form:

$$\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{RT^{2}}$$

This is the single most important equation in this module. The sign of $\Delta_r H^{\circ}$ determines whether $K$ *increases* or *decreases* with temperature:

| Type of Reaction | Sign of $\Delta_r H^{\circ}$ | Behaviour of $K$ with $\uparrow T$ | Physical Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Exothermic | $\Delta_r H^{\circ} < 0$ | $K$ **decreases** | Heat is a "product"; adding it shifts equilibrium backward |
| Endothermic | $\Delta_r H^{\circ} > 0$ | $K$ **increases** | Heat is a "reactant"; adding it shifts equilibrium forward |

### 2.1.2 Pressure Effect — A Subtler Story

The formal pressure dependence of $K$ (in terms of fugacity) is given by:

$$\left( \frac{\partial \ln K}{\partial P} \right)_{T} = -\frac{\Delta_r V^{\circ}}{RT}$$

where $\Delta_r V^{\circ} = \sum_i \nu_i \bar{V}_i^{\circ}$ is the **standard reaction volume** (the algebraic sum of stoichiometric coefficients times partial molar volumes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Distinction for KTU 2024:**
> - For **ideal-gas reactions**: $K_P$ and $K_C$ are *related* by $K_P = K_C (RT)^{\Delta n_g}$, but neither depends on total pressure.
> - For **condensed-phase or real-gas reactions**: $K$ itself can shift with pressure, and the magnitude is governed by $\Delta_r V^{\circ}$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Conditions | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Thermodynamic definition of $K$ | $K = \exp\!\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta_r G^{\circ}}{RT}\right)$ | Always at standard state | Bridge between $K$ and $\Delta_r G^{\circ}$ |
| 2 | van't Hoff (differential form) | $\dfrac{d \ln K}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{RT^{2}}$ | Constant $P$, $\Delta_r H^{\circ}$ assumed $\approx$ const. | Qualitative temperature effect |
| 3 | van't Hoff (integrated form) | $\ln\!\!\left(\dfrac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | $\Delta_r H^{\circ}$ constant over $[T_1, T_2]$ | Numerical calculation of $K$ at new $T$ |
| 4 | van't Hoff (with $C_P$ correction) | $\ln K = -\dfrac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}_{\,0}}{R}\dfrac{1}{T} + \dfrac{\Delta_r C_P}{R}\ln T + \text{const.}$ | $\Delta_r C_P = \sum_i \nu_i C_{P,i}$ | Wide temperature ranges |
| 5 | Pressure effect on $K$ | $\left(\dfrac{\partial \ln K}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\dfrac{\Delta_r V^{\circ}}{RT}$ | Any phase | Condensed-phase & real-gas reactions |
| 6 | $K_P$–$K_C$ relationship | $K_P = K_C \,(RT)^{\Delta n_g}$ | Ideal gases only; $\Delta n_g = \sum \nu_i$ (gas) | Converting between forms |
| 7 | $K_P$–$K_x$ relationship | $K_P = K_x \,(P)^{\Delta n_g}$ | Ideal-gas mixture, total $P$ | Effect of total pressure on *composition* |
| 8 | Reaction isotherm (Leffler) | $\Delta_r G = \Delta_r G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$ | At any stage of reaction | Direction of spontaneity |
| 9 | Gibbs–Helmholtz | $\left[\dfrac{\partial(\Delta_r G^{\circ}/T)}{\partial T}\right]_P = -\dfrac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{T^{2}}$ | Foundation for van't Hoff | Derivation starting point |
| 10 | Kirchhoff's law | $\Delta_r H^{\circ}(T_2) = \Delta_r H^{\circ}(T_1) + \displaystyle\int_{T_1}^{T_2}\!\Delta_r C_P \, dT$ | For temperature-corrected $\Delta H$ | When $\Delta H$ varies with $T$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, the symbol $\Delta n_g$ denotes *change in the number of moles of gas* (products minus reactants), and $\nu_i$ is the stoichiometric coefficient of species $i$ (positive for products, negative for reactants in the $\Delta_r V^{\circ}$ expression).

## 2.3 Engineering & Biochemical Utility

- **Industrial reactor design:** The van't Hoff equation is the design basis for *exothermic equilibrium-limited reactors* (e.g., ammonia synthesis, methanol production). Operators raise temperature to increase rate, but the equilibrium shifts unfavourably — a classic *yield vs. rate* trade-off resolved by Le Chatelier's principle.
- **Pharmaceutical stability:** Drug degradation pathways (oxidation, hydrolysis) have temperature-dependent equilibrium constants; Arrhenius-type extrapolation predicts shelf life.
- **Metabolic regulation:** Enzyme-catalysed reactions in vivo operate near equilibrium for many steps; small local $T$ changes (e.g., fever, hypothermia) shift the equilibrium position, which is why core body temperature is so tightly regulated ($\pm 0.5^\circ$C).
- **Geochemistry & oceanography:** Pressure-dependent equilibria govern carbonate speciation in deep oceans and the formation of high-pressure mineral phases in Earth's mantle.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — The van't Hoff Equation (Differential Form)

**Starting Point:** The fundamental link between equilibrium constant and standard Gibbs energy:

$$\Delta_r G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$$

**Step 1 — Divide both sides by $T$** to prepare for the Gibbs–Helmholtz substitution:

$$\frac{\Delta_r G^{\circ}}{T} = -R \ln K$$

**Step 2 — Take the partial derivative with respect to $T$ at constant $P$** of both sides:

$$\left[ \frac{\partial}{\partial T}\!\left(\frac{\Delta_r G^{\circ}}{T}\right) \right]_P = -R \left(\frac{\partial \ln K}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Gibbs–Helmholtz equation** to the left-hand side:

$$\left[ \frac{\partial}{\partial T}\!\left(\frac{\Delta_r G^{\circ}}{T}\right) \right]_P = -\frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{T^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Equate the two expressions** for $\partial(\Delta_r G^{\circ}/T) / \partial T$:

$$-\frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{T^{2}} = -R \left(\frac{\partial \ln K}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $d \ln K / dT$** by multiplying both sides by $-1/R$:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{RT^{2}}\;}$$

This is the **van't Hoff equation** in differential form. The logic chain is: *definition of $K$ via $\Delta_r G^{\circ}$* $\rightarrow$ *Gibbs–Helmholtz* $\rightarrow$ *van't Hoff*.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Integrated van't Hoff Equation

**Assumption:** $\Delta_r H^{\circ}$ is independent of $T$ over the range of interest (valid for moderate temperature ranges, typically $< 100 \; \text{K}$).

**Step 1 — Separate variables** in the differential van't Hoff equation:

$$d \ln K = \frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{R} \cdot \frac{dT}{T^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate both sides** between state 1 $(T_1, K_1)$ and state 2 $(T_2, K_2)$:

$$\int_{K_1}^{K_2} d \ln K = \frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the left integral** (it is exact, $d \ln K$):

$$\ln K_2 - \ln K_1 = \ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the right integral** using $\displaystyle\int T^{-2} \, dT = -T^{-1}$:

$$\frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{R} \left[ -\frac{1}{T} \right]_{T_1}^{T_2} = \frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{R}\!\left(-\frac{1}{T_2} + \frac{1}{T_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Combine the two results** to obtain the **integrated van't Hoff equation**:

$$\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right) = \frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\;}$$

**Equivalent form** (often seen in textbooks):

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = \frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{R}\!\left(\frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_1 T_2}\right)$$

**Conversion logic note:** The third form is obtained by combining the two fractions over a common denominator $T_1 T_2$, since $1/T_1 - 1/T_2 = (T_2 - T_1)/(T_1 T_2)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check using physical intuition:** Suppose $\Delta_r H^{\circ} > 0$ (endothermic) and $T_2 > T_1$. Then the right side is positive (numerator positive, denominator positive), so $\ln(K_2/K_1) > 0$, implying $K_2 > K_1$. ✓ Endothermic reactions are favoured by higher $T$ — consistent with Le Chatelier's principle.

## 3.3 Derivation III — The $K_P$–$K_C$ Relationship (Ideal Gases)

**Step 1 — Write the two equilibrium constants** in terms of concentrations and partial pressures:

$$K_C = \prod_i [C_i]^{\nu_i} \qquad \text{and} \qquad K_P = \prod_i (P_i)^{\nu_i}$$

**Step 2 — Use the ideal-gas law** to relate partial pressure of species $i$ to its concentration:

$$P_i = \frac{n_i RT}{V} = [C_i] \, RT$$

**Step 3 — Substitute** $P_i = [C_i] \, RT$ into $K_P$:

$$K_P = \prod_i \big( [C_i] \, RT \big)^{\nu_i} = \prod_i [C_i]^{\nu_i} \cdot \prod_i (RT)^{\nu_i}$$

**Step 4 — Recognise that** $\prod_i [C_i]^{\nu_i} = K_C$:

$$K_P = K_C \cdot (RT)^{\sum_i \nu_i}$$

**Step 5 — Define** $\Delta n_g = \sum_i \nu_i$ (sum of stoichiometric coefficients of *gaseous* species only, products minus reactants):

$$\boxed{\;K_P = K_C \,(RT)^{\Delta n_g}\;}$$

**Conversion logic note:** The exponent $\Delta n_g$ counts *only* gas-phase species; pure solids and liquids are excluded because their "concentrations" are constants folded into $K$.

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Pressure Dependence of $K$ (General Case)

**Step 1 — Start from** $\ln K = -\Delta_r G^{\circ}/(RT)$ and differentiate at constant $T$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial \ln K}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\frac{1}{RT}\!\left(\frac{\partial \Delta_r G^{\circ}}{\partial P}\right)_T$$

**Step 2 — Use the relation** $\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial P}\right)_T = V$ applied to each species in the standard state. For the *reaction*:

$$\left(\frac{\partial \Delta_r G^{\circ}}{\partial P}\right)_T = \Delta_r V^{\circ} = \sum_i \nu_i \bar{V}_i^{\circ}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute** back into the derivative:

$$\boxed{\;\left(\frac{\partial \ln K}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\frac{\Delta_r V^{\circ}}{RT}\;}$$

**Conversion logic note:** For an ideal-gas reaction, $\bar{V}_i^{\circ} = RT/P$ and the equation reduces to the trivial statement that $\ln K$ is independent of $P$ (the $P$-dependence of $\Delta_r V^{\circ}$ exactly cancels). This is the *thermodynamic proof* that ideal-gas $K$ values are pressure-independent.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** For the reaction $\text{N}_2\text{O}_4(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\,\text{NO}_2(g)$, $K_P = 0.150$ at $298 \; \text{K}$. Given $\Delta_r H^{\circ} = +57.20 \; \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ (assume constant), calculate $K_P$ at $350 \; \text{K}$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify given quantities:**

$$K_1 = 0.150, \quad T_1 = 298 \; \text{K}, \quad T_2 = 350 \; \text{K}, \quad \Delta_r H^{\circ} = 57200 \; \text{J mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the integrated van't Hoff equation:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = \frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the temperature bracket:**

$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{298} - \frac{1}{350} = 0.003356 - 0.002857 = 4.985 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the prefactor** $\Delta_r H^{\circ}/R$:

$$\frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{R} = \frac{57200}{8.314} = 6880.4 \; \text{K}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{0.150}\right) = 6880.4 \times 4.985 \times 10^{-4} = 3.430$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $K_2$:**

$$\frac{K_2}{0.150} = e^{3.430} = 30.89 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad K_2 = 0.150 \times 30.89 = 4.63$$

**Interpretation:** Heating shifts equilibrium toward $\text{NO}_2$ (the dissociation is endothermic, so $K$ rises with $T$). ✓ Physically consistent.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Factors Governing the Equilibrium Constant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Equilibrium Constant K]:::root
    B[Temperature T]:::hot
    C[Pressure P]:::cool
    D[Standard State]:::state
    E[Reaction Enthalpy dH]:::hot
    F[Reaction Volume dV]:::cool
    G[van't Hoff Equation]:::eq
    H[General Pressure Equation]:::eq
    I[Composition shift only]:::neutral
    J[Independence for ideal gases]:::neutral

    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D
    B --> E
    B --> G
    C --> F
    C --> H
    E --> G
    F --> H
    H --> I
    H --> J

    classDef root fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#0a1929,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef hot fill:#c0392b,stroke:#7b241c,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef cool fill:#2874a6,stroke:#1a5276,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef state fill:#7d6608,stroke:#4a3c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef eq fill:#196f3d,stroke:#0e4023,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef neutral fill:#566573,stroke:#2c3e50,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Thermodynamic Derivation Flow — From $\Delta G^{\circ}$ to van't Hoff

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Definition of K: K equals exp of negative dG standard over RT]:::start
    node2[Divide both sides by T]:::step
    node3[Differentiate with respect to T at constant P]:::step
    node4[Apply Gibbs Helmholtz Equation]:::step
    node5[Equate and solve for d ln K over dT]:::finish
    node6[Separate variables]:::step
    node7[Integrate from T1 to T2]:::step
    node8[Integrated van't Hoff Equation]:::final

    node1 --> node2 --> node3 --> node4 --> node5 --> node6 --> node7 --> node8

    classDef start fill:#5b2c6f,stroke:#3b1a4b,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef step fill:#21618c,stroke:#154360,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef finish fill:#1e8449,stroke:#145a32,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef final fill:#b9770e,stroke:#7d4e00,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — What Changes $K$ vs. What Shifts the Position of Equilibrium?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Perturbation applied to system at equilibrium]:::start
    q1{Type of perturbation?}:::q
    p1[Change total pressure or volume]:::p
    p2[Change concentration or partial pressure of a species]:::p
    p3[Change temperature]:::p
    p4[Add a catalyst]:::p
    o1[For ideal gases: K unchanged; equilibrium COMPOSITION shifts to reduce delta n gas]:::o
    o2[K unchanged; equilibrium COMPOSITION shifts to consume added species]:::o
    o3[K CHANGES according to van't Hoff equation; new equilibrium established]:::o
    o4[K unchanged; only rate of attainment of equilibrium changes]:::o

    start --> q1
    q1 -->|Pressure or Volume| p1 --> o1
    q1 -->|Composition| p2 --> o2
    q1 -->|Temperature| p3 --> o3
    q1 -->|Catalyst| p4 --> o4

    classDef start fill:#34495e,stroke:#1b2631,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef q fill:#7e5109,stroke:#4a2f00,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef p fill:#2874a6,stroke:#1a5276,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef o fill:#117a65,stroke:#0b5345,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.4 Schematic — van't Hoff Plot (Arrhenius-type for Equilibrium)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axis[Coordinate system]:::axis
    q1{Is reaction exothermic or endothermic?}:::q
    exo[Slope NEGATIVE: dH less than zero: K decreases as T rises]:::exo
    endo[Slope POSITIVE: dH greater than zero: K increases as T rises]:::endo
    formula[Slope equals negative dH standard over R from y axis ln K vs 1 over T]:::eq

    axis --> q1
    q1 -->|Exothermic| exo
    q1 -->|Endothermic| endo
    exo --> formula
    endo --> formula

    classDef axis fill:#4a235a,stroke:#2c0f37,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef q fill:#7e5109,stroke:#4a2f00,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef exo fill:#1f618d,stroke:#143f5e,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef endo fill:#922b21,stroke:#5b1914,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef eq fill:#1e8449,stroke:#145a32,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the van't Hoff plot:** A plot of $\ln K$ (vertical axis) versus $1/T$ (horizontal axis) gives a straight line with **slope $= -\Delta_r H^{\circ}/R$** and **intercept $= \Delta_r S^{\circ}/R$**. This single graph encodes *both* the enthalpy and entropy of the reaction — a powerful diagnostic tool emphasised in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**State and explain the van't Hoff equation. How does it explain the variation of the equilibrium constant with temperature?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **van't Hoff equation** in differential form is:

$$\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{RT^{2}}$$

where $\Delta_r H^{\circ}$ is the standard enthalpy of reaction and $R$ is the universal gas constant.

**Explanation (2 marks):** For an *exothermic* reaction, $\Delta_r H^{\circ} < 0$, so $d \ln K / dT < 0$, meaning $K$ **decreases** as $T$ increases. For an *endothermic* reaction, $\Delta_r H^{\circ} > 0$, so $K$ **increases** with $T$. This is a quantitative statement of **Le Chatelier's principle** applied to heat as a pseudo-reactant (for endothermic) or pseudo-product (for exothermic) reactions.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]
**For an ideal-gas reaction, what is the relationship between $K_P$ and $K_C$? When does $K_P = K_C$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The relationship for ideal gases is:

$$K_P = K_C \,(RT)^{\Delta n_g}$$

where $\Delta n_g$ is the change in the number of gaseous moles (products minus reactants). 

**When equal (1 mark):** $K_P = K_C$ when $\Delta n_g = 0$, i.e., when the total moles of gaseous products equals the total moles of gaseous reactants (e.g., $\text{H}_2 + \text{I}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\,\text{HI}$).

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Pattern:** The question paper presents one question of 14 marks with two alternative choices (A and B). You must answer *only one*. Each choice typically has sub-parts (a) carrying 7 marks and (b) carrying 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]

**(a)** Starting from the relation $\Delta_r G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$, derive the **integrated form of the van't Hoff equation**, clearly stating the assumption used. (7 marks)

**(b)** For the gaseous reaction $\text{PCl}_5(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{PCl}_3(g) + \text{Cl}_2(g)$, the equilibrium constant $K_P = 0.612$ at $500 \; \text{K}$. If $\Delta_r H^{\circ} = +92.5 \; \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ (assumed constant), calculate:
- (i) $K_P$ at $600 \; \text{K}$ (4 marks)
- (ii) The standard Gibbs energy change $\Delta_r G^{\circ}$ at $500 \; \text{K}$ and at $600 \; \text{K}$ (3 marks)

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Start from the definition:
$$\Delta_r G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Divide by $T$:
$$\frac{\Delta_r G^{\circ}}{T} = -R \ln K$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Differentiate w.r.t. $T$ at constant $P$:
$$\left[ \frac{\partial}{\partial T}\!\left(\frac{\Delta_r G^{\circ}}{T}\right) \right]_P = -R \left(\frac{\partial \ln K}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply the Gibbs–Helmholtz equation to the LHS:
$$\left[ \frac{\partial}{\partial T}\!\left(\frac{\Delta_r G^{\circ}}{T}\right) \right]_P = -\frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{T^{2}}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Equate and rearrange:
$$\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{RT^{2}}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** State the assumption — $\Delta_r H^{\circ}$ is independent of $T$ over the range considered.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Separate variables and integrate:
$$\int_{K_1}^{K_2} d \ln K = \frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{R}\int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^{2}} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) $K_P$ at $600 \; \text{K}$ (4 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the integrated van't Hoff equation:
$$\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = \frac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Substitute numerical values:
$$\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{0.612}\right) = \frac{92500}{8.314}\!\left(\frac{1}{500} - \frac{1}{600}\right)$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Evaluate:
$$\frac{92500}{8.314} = 11125.8 \; \text{K} \qquad \frac{1}{500} - \frac{1}{600} = \frac{600 - 500}{500 \times 600} = \frac{100}{300000} = 3.333 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute and exponentiate:
$$\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{0.612}\right) = 11125.8 \times 3.333 \times 10^{-4} = 3.709 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{K_2}{0.612} = e^{3.709} = 40.83$$
$$\boxed{\;K_P(600\;\text{K}) = 0.612 \times 40.83 = 24.99 \approx 25.0\;}$$

**(ii) $\Delta_r G^{\circ}$ at both temperatures (3 marks):**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** At $500 \; \text{K}$:
$$\Delta_r G^{\circ}(500) = -RT \ln K = -(8.314)(500) \ln(0.612) = -4157 \times (-0.4912) = +2042 \; \text{J mol}^{-1} = +2.04 \; \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** At $600 \; \text{K}$:
$$\Delta_r G^{\circ}(600) = -(8.314)(600) \ln(25.0) = -4988.4 \times 3.219 = -16059 \; \text{J mol}^{-1} = -16.06 \; \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$$

**Physical interpretation:** $\Delta_r G^{\circ}$ becomes more negative at higher $T$, consistent with the endothermic reaction becoming more spontaneous (more product-favoured) at elevated $T$. ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** Discuss the **effect of pressure on the equilibrium constant** for:
- (i) An ideal-gas reaction (4 marks)
- (ii) A reaction in the condensed phase or involving real gases (3 marks)

**(b)** For the water-gas shift reaction $\text{CO}(g) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2(g) + \text{H}_2(g)$ at $1000 \; \text{K}$:
- (i) Derive the relationship between $K_P$ and $K_C$ and state the numerical value of the exponent (3 marks)
- (ii) If $K_C = 1.40$ at $1000 \; \text{K}$, calculate $K_P$ (in bar units). What does the numerical value of $K_P$ tell you about the position of equilibrium? (4 marks)

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Effect of Pressure (7 marks):**

**(i) Ideal-gas reaction (4 marks):**

For an ideal-gas reaction, the partial molar volume of each species is $\bar{V}_i = RT/P$. The standard reaction volume is:

$$\Delta_r V^{\circ} = \sum_i \nu_i \frac{RT}{P} = \frac{RT}{P}\sum_i \nu_i = \frac{RT \,\Delta n_g}{P}$$

Substituting into the general pressure-dependence equation:

$$\left(\frac{\partial \ln K}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\frac{\Delta_r V^{\circ}}{RT} = -\frac{\Delta n_g}{P}$$

But since $K_P$ for ideal gases is defined in terms of fugacities, the $P$-dependence arising from $\Delta n_g \neq 0$ is *exactly* absorbed into the standard-state definition. **Net result:** $K_P$ is rigorously *independent* of total pressure for ideal-gas reactions. Pressure changes shift the *equilibrium composition* (mole fractions) but not the value of $K_P$.

**(ii) Condensed-phase / real-gas reaction (3 marks):**

For liquids, solids, or real gases, the partial molar volumes $\bar{V}_i^{\circ}$ are nearly constant and small (typically $10$–$100 \; \text{cm}^3/\text{mol}$). The relation:

$$\left(\frac{\partial \ln K}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\frac{\Delta_r V^{\circ}}{RT}$$

implies that even at $P = 1000 \; \text{bar}$, $\Delta_r V^{\circ} \approx 50 \; \text{cm}^3/\text{mol}$ gives:

$$\Delta \ln K \approx -\frac{50 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^3/\text{mol} \times 1000 \times 10^{5} \; \text{Pa}}{8.314 \times 298} \approx -2.0$$

i.e., $K$ can change by a factor of $\sim 7$. So pressure can be a *significant* variable for condensed-phase and real-gas equilibria (e.g., polymerisation, protein folding, diamond–graphite equilibrium).

---

**Part (b) — Water-Gas Shift Reaction (7 marks):**

**(i) Relationship between $K_P$ and $K_C$ (3 marks):**

For ideal gases, $K_P = K_C (RT)^{\Delta n_g}$. Counting only gas-phase species:

- Reactants: 1 mol CO$(g)$ + 1 mol H$_2$O$(g)$ = 2 mol
- Products: 1 mol CO$_2(g)$ + 1 mol H$_2(g)$ = 2 mol
- $\Delta n_g = 2 - 2 = 0$

Therefore:

$$K_P = K_C (RT)^{0} = K_C$$

**The exponent is zero** because the reaction conserves the total number of gas moles.

**(ii) Calculation and interpretation (4 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Apply $K_P = K_C$:

$$K_P = 1.40$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Calculate $\Delta_r G^{\circ}$:

$$\Delta_r G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K_P = -(8.314)(1000)\ln(1.40) = -8314 \times 0.3365 = -2797 \; \text{J mol}^{-1} = -2.80 \; \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Interpretation:

Since $K_P = 1.40 > 1$ at $1000 \; \text{K}$ and $\Delta_r G^{\circ} < 0$, the equilibrium lies *slightly in favour of the products* (CO$_2$ + H$_2$) under standard conditions. However, because $K_P$ is close to 1, a substantial amount of reactants remains at equilibrium — the reaction is *reversible* and not strongly product-favoured at this temperature. Industrial processes operate at $1000$–$1300 \; \text{K}$ for acceptable rates while accepting this moderate equilibrium conversion.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Pitfalls on This Topic:**
> 1. **Sign error in integrated van't Hoff** — confusing the form $\ln(K_2/K_1) = -\Delta_r H^{\circ}/R \cdot (1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$ with the *positive* form. The most reliable method: derive it from the differential form each time; never memorise two versions.
> 2. **Using the *total* moles** (not just gas-phase moles) in $\Delta n_g$ when solids or liquids are present. For example, in $\text{CaCO}_3(s) \rightleftharpoons \text{CaO}(s) + \text{CO}_2(g)$, $\Delta n_g = 1$, *not* 3.
> 3. **Forgetting the assumption** that $\Delta_r H^{\circ}$ is constant — the integrated van't Hoff is only exact when $\Delta C_P = 0$ for the reaction. Examiners deduct 1 mark if the assumption is not explicitly stated.
> 4. **Confusing $K$ dependence with composition dependence** — many students incorrectly write that $K$ changes with pressure. For ideal gases, $K$ does *not* change with $P$; only the *position* of equilibrium (mole fractions) changes.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency in $R$** — using $R = 1.987 \; \text{cal mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$ with $\Delta_r H^{\circ}$ in kJ. Always check units *before* substituting.
> 6. **Misreading the van't Hoff plot** — slope is $-\Delta_r H^{\circ}/R$ (negative for exothermic). Students often state the slope is $+\Delta_r H^{\circ}/R$.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Van't Hoff equation (differential):** $\dfrac{d \ln K}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{RT^{2}}$ — the central result of this module.
- **Van't Hoff equation (integrated):** $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = \dfrac{\Delta_r H^{\circ}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$, valid when $\Delta_r H^{\circ}$ is constant.
- **Temperature is the only intensive variable that fundamentally changes $K$** for ideal-gas reactions.
- **Endothermic reactions** ($\Delta_r H^{\circ} > 0$): $K$ rises with $T$.
- **Exothermic reactions** ($\Delta_r H^{\circ} < 0$): $K$ falls with $T$.
- **Van't Hoff plot** of $\ln K$ vs. $1/T$ is a straight line with slope $-\Delta_r H^{\circ}/R$ and intercept $\Delta_r S^{\circ}/R$.
- **$K_P$–$K_C$ relationship:** $K_P = K_C (RT)^{\Delta n_g}$ — only gas-phase moles count in $\Delta n_g$.
- **$K_P = K_C$** when $\Delta n_g = 0$.
- **$K_x$ shifts with total pressure:** $K_P = K_x (P)^{\Delta n_g}$ — so changing $P$ changes $x$ when $\Delta n_g \neq 0$, but $K_P$ itself is unchanged (ideal gases).
- **General pressure dependence:** $\left(\dfrac{\partial \ln K}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\dfrac{\Delta_r V^{\circ}}{RT}$ — important for condensed-phase and real-gas systems, often negligible for biochemical aqueous reactions at ambient pressure.
- **Pressure affects composition, not $K$** (for ideal gases) — this is the most heavily tested conceptual distinction in KTU exams.
- **Catalysts do not change $K$** — they only accelerate the rate of reaching equilibrium.
- **Sign convention for $\nu_i$:** positive for products, negative for reactants in the $\Delta_r V^{\circ}$ and $\Delta n_g$ sums.
- **Always state the constant-$\Delta_r H^{\circ}$ assumption** explicitly when using the integrated van't Hoff equation.
- **Watch the units of $R$** — match with the units of $\Delta_r H^{\circ}$ (J or kJ; cal or kcal).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Activity Coefficient
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT414 - BIOCHEMICAL THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Activity Coefficient

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In non-ideal solutions (which includes virtually every real biochemical system, especially those containing salts, proteins, nucleic acids, or charged metabolites), the simple molar concentration of a species no longer adequately describes its **effective** contribution to the thermodynamic properties of the system. To preserve the elegant mathematical structure of ideal thermodynamics, Lewis and Randall introduced the concept of **activity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ACTIVITY (a):** A dimensionless, "effective" concentration that, when substituted into the equations valid for ideal solutions, allows those same equations to correctly describe the thermodynamic behavior of **real** solutions.

The **activity coefficient** ($\gamma$) is the dimensionless proportionality constant that connects the activity of a species to its actual analytical (molar) concentration:

$$a_i = \gamma_i \, c_i$$

or in terms of molality:

$$a_i = \gamma_i \, m_i$$

where:
- $a_i$ = activity of species *i* (dimensionless)
- $\gamma_i$ = activity coefficient of species *i* (dimensionless)
- $c_i$ = molar concentration (mol/L) or $m_i$ = molality (mol/kg solvent)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard State Convention:** In the infinitely dilute (ideal-dilute) reference state, $\gamma_i \rightarrow 1$, so $a_i \rightarrow c_i$. This is the boundary condition that anchors the entire activity formalism.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Crowded Elevator" Intuition

Imagine a **small lift (elevator)** with a maximum capacity of **8 people**, and imagine trying to push your way through a *crowded* lift versus an *empty* lift:

- In an **empty lift** (analogous to an *infinitely dilute, ideal solution*), you can move freely in any direction. Every person behaves independently. This is the **ideal regime** — concentration equals activity.
- In a **packed lift** (analogous to a *concentrated, non-ideal electrolyte solution* containing proteins, salts, ATP, etc.), every person is jostling against every other person. Your *effective* freedom of movement (your **activity**) is much smaller than the fact that you are "there" (your **concentration**).

**The activity coefficient $\gamma < 1$ tells you precisely *how much* the surrounding molecular crowd is suppressing the effective thermodynamic behavior of a given species.** Conversely, in some specialized systems (e.g., hydrophobic solutes in certain solvents), $\gamma > 1$, indicating the solute is "pushed out" of the solution.

## 1.3 Why Activity Coefficients Matter in Biochemistry

In living systems (cells, blood, fermentation broths), the ionic strength can exceed **0.1 to 0.5 M** due to dissolved salts, proteins, and metabolites. At such strengths, using raw concentrations in equations like:

- Nernst equation (membrane potentials)
- Equilibrium constants for biochemical reactions
- Henderson–Hasselbalch (buffer pH)
- Solubility product ($K_{sp}$) of biominerals

can introduce errors of **20–300%**. The activity coefficient corrects for the electrostatic and excluded-volume interactions that pervade all real biological media.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard constants referenced in this topic:**
> - Permittivity of free space: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m
> - Elementary charge: $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C
> - Boltzmann constant: $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
> - Avogadro's number: $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ /mol
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mean Ionic Activity Coefficient

For a single salt that dissociates as:

$$M_{\nu_+} X_{\nu_-} \rightarrow \nu_+ M^{z_+} + \nu_- X^{z_-}$$

the **mean ionic activity** $a_\pm$ and **mean ionic activity coefficient** $\gamma_\pm$ are *measurable* quantities (unlike the individual ionic activities, which are not independently measurable). They are defined by:

$$a_\pm = (\gamma_\pm \, m_\pm)$$

where the mean ionic molality is:

$$m_\pm = (m_+^{\nu_+} \, m_-^{\nu_-})^{1/(\nu_+ + \nu_-)}$$

and the mean ionic activity coefficient satisfies:

$$\gamma_\pm = (\gamma_+^{\nu_+} \, \gamma_-^{\nu_-})^{1/(\nu_+ + \nu_-)}$$

This geometric-mean definition is **not arbitrary** — it is the only combination that makes the chemical potential of the dissolved salt expressible in a single, self-consistent equation.

## 2.2 Ionic Strength

The **ionic strength** $I$ is a measure of the total electrostatic "crowding" produced by all ions in solution:

$$I = \frac{1}{2} \sum_i m_i \, z_i^2$$

- $m_i$ = molality of ion *i*
- $z_i$ = charge number of ion *i*
- The squaring of charge reflects the fact that electrostatic interaction energy scales with the **product** of charges.

> [!NOTE]
> For a pure $z:z$ electrolyte of molality $m$, this collapses to $I = m \cdot z^2$. For example, 0.1 M NaCl gives $I = 0.1$ M, but 0.1 M MgCl$_2$ gives $I = 0.3$ M.

## 2.3 The Debye–Hückel Theory — Three Levels

### 2.3.1 Debye–Hückel Limiting Law (DHLL)
Valid at **very low ionic strength** ($I < 10^{-3}$ mol/kg):

$$\log_{10} \gamma_\pm = -A \, \vert z_+ z_- \vert \sqrt{I}$$

### 2.3.2 Extended (Davies) Debye–Hückel Equation
Valid at **moderate ionic strength** ($I < 0.1$ mol/kg) — most useful for biochemical problems:

$$\log_{10} \gamma_\pm = -\frac{A \, z^2 \sqrt{I}}{1 + B \, a \, \sqrt{I}}$$

where for aqueous solutions at **298 K**:
- $A \approx 0.509$ (mol/kg)$^{-1/2}$
- $B \approx 0.328$ (mol/kg)$^{-1/2}$·Å$^{-1}$
- $a$ = effective ion-size parameter in Å (typically 3–9 Å)

### 2.3.3 Specific Ion Interaction Theory (Pitzer / Bromley)
Used at high ionic strength ($I > 0.1$ M); requires empirical ion-pair coefficients. Beyond KTU Module 3 scope but worth knowing it exists.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Symbols & Conditions | Engineering / Bio Use |
|---|----------|----------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | $a_i = \gamma_i \, m_i$ | Activity definition; ideal limit $\gamma \to 1$ | All real-solution equilibria |
| 2 | $\mu_i = \mu_i^\circ + RT \ln a_i$ | Chemical potential with activity | Foundation of bio-thermo |
| 3 | $I = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum m_i z_i^2$ | Ionic strength; molal basis | Salt/buffer quantification |
| 4 | $m_\pm = (m_+^{\nu_+} m_-^{\nu_-})^{1/\nu}$ | Mean ionic molality; $\nu = \nu_+ + \nu_-$ | Strong-electrolyte work |
| 5 | $\gamma_\pm = (\gamma_+^{\nu_+} \gamma_-^{\nu_-})^{1/\nu}$ | Mean ionic activity coefficient | Experimental data fitting |
| 6 | $\log_{10}\gamma_\pm = -A \vert z_+ z_- \vert \sqrt{I}$ | **DHLL**; $I < 10^{-3}$ mol/kg; $A_{298K} = 0.509$ | Dilute biochemical buffers |
| 7 | $\log_{10}\gamma_\pm = -\dfrac{A z^2 \sqrt{I}}{1 + B a \sqrt{I}}$ | **Extended DH**; $I < 0.1$ mol/kg; $a \approx 4$–$9$ Å | Physiological ionic strength |
| 8 | $\gamma_\pm^{DHLL} \to e^{-A z^2 \sqrt{I} \ln 10}$ | Natural-log form via $2.303 \log_{10}$ | Hand derivations |
| 9 | $pH_{actual} = pK_a + \log \dfrac{a_{A^-}}{a_{HA}}$ | True Henderson–Hasselbalch | Biobuffer design |
| 10 | $\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$ where $Q$ uses activities | Reaction Gibbs energy in real media | Metabolic energetics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Always use **natural logarithm (ln)** when the constant in front is $RT$, and **log base 10** when the constant in front involves $2.303 \, RT$ or $A$. Mixing them is the #1 source of valuation marks lost in KTU scripts.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical formulation:** Predicting solubility and stability of charged drug molecules in saline infusion fluids.
- **Downstream bioprocessing:** Designing ion-exchange chromatography — protein binding depends on local $\gamma_\pm$ near the resin surface.
- **Membrane biophysics:** Nernst potentials across neuronal membranes require $\gamma_\pm$ for accuracy.
- **Fermentation engineering:** Maintaining enzyme activity requires predicting how added salts (NaCl, ammonium sulfate) shift $\gamma$ and hence $K_{eq}$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Connecting $\gamma_\pm$ to Individual Ionic Coefficients

We start with the chemical potential of the dissolved salt $M_{\nu_+} X_{\nu_-}$ written using its constituent ions:

$$\mu_{salt} = \nu_+ \mu_+ + \nu_- \mu_-$$

**Step 1.** Expand each $\mu$ using the activity formalism (ideal-dilute reference state):

$$\mu_+ = \mu_+^\circ + RT \ln a_+ = \mu_+^\circ + RT \ln(\gamma_+ \, m_+)$$

$$\mu_- = \mu_-^\circ + RT \ln a_- = \mu_-^\circ + RT \ln(\gamma_- \, m_-)$$

**Step 2.** Substitute back into the expression for $\mu_{salt}$:

$$\mu_{salt} = \nu_+ \mu_+^\circ + \nu_- \mu_-^\circ + RT \left[ \nu_+ \ln(\gamma_+ m_+) + \nu_- \ln(\gamma_- m_-) \right]$$

**Step 3.** Combine the standard-state terms into a single salt reference:

$$\mu_{salt}^\circ = \nu_+ \mu_+^\circ + \nu_- \mu_-^\circ$$

**Step 4.** Use log rules: $\nu \ln(x) = \ln(x^\nu)$. The bracketed term becomes:

$$RT \ln\left[ (\gamma_+ m_+)^{\nu_+} (\gamma_- m_-)^{\nu_-} \right]$$

**Step 5.** Group the activities and molalities separately:

$$RT \ln\left[ (\gamma_+^{\nu_+} \gamma_-^{\nu_-}) \, (m_+^{\nu_+} m_-^{\nu_-}) \right]$$

**Step 6.** Recognize that the total stoichiometric coefficient is $\nu = \nu_+ + \nu_-$. For consistency with a single chemical potential expression, the argument of the log must be raised to the power $1/\nu$:

$$\mu_{salt} = \mu_{salt}^\circ + RT \, \nu \, \ln\left[ (\gamma_+^{\nu_+} \gamma_-^{\nu_-})^{1/\nu} (m_+^{\nu_+} m_-^{\nu_-})^{1/\nu} \right]$$

**Step 7.** Define the bracketed quantity as the **mean ionic activity**:

$$a_\pm = (\gamma_+^{\nu_+} \gamma_-^{\nu_-})^{1/\nu} (m_+^{\nu_+} m_-^{\nu_-})^{1/\nu}$$

**Step 8.** Separate the coefficient and concentration parts. The mean ionic activity coefficient is therefore:

$$\gamma_\pm = (\gamma_+^{\nu_+} \gamma_-^{\nu_-})^{1/(\nu_+ + \nu_-)}$$

which is the standard textbook result. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — A KTU-Style Calculation

> **Problem.** A buffer contains 0.10 mol/kg NaCl, 0.05 mol/kg Na$_2$HPO$_4$, and 0.02 mol/kg KH$_2$PO$_4$ at 298 K. Estimate the mean ionic activity coefficient of NaCl in this solution using the **Extended Debye–Hückel** equation with $a = 4$ Å.

### Step 1 — Compute Ionic Strength

List all ions with their molalities and charges:

| Ion | $m_i$ (mol/kg) | $z_i$ | $z_i^2$ | $m_i z_i^2$ |
|-----|----------------|-------|---------|-------------|
| Na$^+$ (from NaCl) | 0.10 | +1 | 1 | 0.10 |
| Cl$^-$ | 0.10 | −1 | 1 | 0.10 |
| Na$^+$ (from Na$_2$HPO$_4$) | 2 × 0.05 = 0.10 | +1 | 1 | 0.10 |
| HPO$_4^{2-}$ | 0.05 | −2 | 4 | 0.20 |
| K$^+$ | 0.02 | +1 | 1 | 0.02 |
| H$_2$PO$_4^-$ | 0.02 | −1 | 1 | 0.02 |

**Step 2.** Sum the last column:

$$\sum m_i z_i^2 = 0.10 + 0.10 + 0.10 + 0.20 + 0.02 + 0.02 = 0.54$$

**Step 3.** Apply the ionic strength definition:

$$I = \frac{1}{2}(0.54) = 0.27 \text{ mol/kg}$$

**Step 4.** Apply the Extended Debye–Hückel equation. For NaCl, $z_+ = z_- = 1$, so $z_+ z_- = 1$ and $z^2 = 1$:

$$\log_{10} \gamma_\pm = -\frac{A \sqrt{I}}{1 + B a \sqrt{I}}$$

**Step 5.** Substitute values ($A = 0.509$, $B = 0.328$ Å$^{-1}$·mol$^{-1/2}$·kg$^{1/2}$, $a = 4$ Å, $I = 0.27$):

$$\sqrt{I} = \sqrt{0.27} = 0.5196$$

$$B a \sqrt{I} = 0.328 \times 4 \times 0.5196 = 0.6818$$

$$1 + B a \sqrt{I} = 1.6818$$

$$\frac{A \sqrt{I}}{1 + B a \sqrt{I}} = \frac{0.509 \times 0.5196}{1.6818} = \frac{0.2645}{1.6818} = 0.1573$$

**Step 6.** Take the negative and exponentiate:

$$\log_{10} \gamma_\pm = -0.1573$$

$$\gamma_\pm = 10^{-0.1573} = 0.696$$

**Result:** $\boxed{\gamma_\pm(\text{NaCl}) \approx 0.696}$ — a substantial deviation from ideality, confirming that physiological ionic strengths strongly suppress the *effective* concentration of NaCl.

## 3.3 Limiting-Case Sanity Check

At very low $I$ ($I \to 0$), the denominator $1 + B a \sqrt{I} \to 1$, and the Extended DH equation **reduces exactly** to the DH Limiting Law:

$$\log_{10} \gamma_\pm \;\xrightarrow{I\to 0}\; -A \vert z_+ z_- \vert \sqrt{I}$$

This consistency check confirms that the two equations are hierarchical, not contradictory — a frequent KTU viva question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Hierarchy of the Activity Formalism

The following block-flow diagram shows how raw concentration is progressively "corrected" through the activity formalism, ending at the form actually used in thermodynamic equations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Analytical Concentration m_i] --> B[Identify Charge z_i]
    B --> C[Compute Ionic Strength I]
    C --> D{Select Ionic Strength Regime}
    D -->|I less than 1e-3| E[Debye-Huckel Limiting Law]
    D -->|I less than 0.1| F[Extended Debye-Huckel]
    D -->|I greater than 0.1| G[Pitzer or Bromley]
    E --> H[Mean Ionic Activity Coeff gamma_pm]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Multiply by Molality to get Activity]
    I --> J[Substitute into Nernst or Equilibrium Equation]
    J --> K[Accurate Real Solution Property]
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — From Salt Dissociation to Measurable $\gamma_\pm$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT STAGE]
        A1[Salt Formula M_nu_plus X_nu_minus]
        A2[Ion Charges z_plus z_minus]
        A3[Total Molality m]
    end
    subgraph COMPUTE[COMPUTATION STAGE]
        B1[Compute Ionic Strength I]
        B2[Pick Ion Size Parameter a]
        B3[Apply Extended DH Equation]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT STAGE]
        C1[Mean Ionic Activity Coefficient gamma_pm]
        C2[Mean Ionic Activity a_pm]
        C3[Chemical Potential mu_salt]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B3
    A2 --> B3
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    A1 --> C2
    A3 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow for Choosing the Correct Activity Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Have solution composition] --> Q1{Is ionic strength known?}
    Q1 -->|No| S1[Measure via conductivity]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is I less than 1e-3?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| P1[Use DH Limiting Law]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is I less than 0.1?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| P2[Use Extended Debye-Huckel with a]
    Q3 -->|No| P4[Use Pitzer Specific Ion Theory]
    P1 --> R[Compute gamma_pm]
    P2 --> R
    P4 --> R
    R --> X[Output gamma_pm to Nernst or K_eq equation]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the activity coefficient of an electrolyte. State the Debye–Hückel Limiting Law. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The activity coefficient $\gamma_i$ of a species $i$ is the dimensionless factor relating its activity $a_i$ to its molality $m_i$ via $a_i = \gamma_i m_i$. It quantitatively corrects the ideal-dilute assumption by accounting for non-ideal interactions in real solutions.

The **Debye–Hückel Limiting Law** gives the mean ionic activity coefficient at very low ionic strength ($I < 10^{-3}$ mol/kg):

$$\log_{10} \gamma_\pm = -A \vert z_+ z_- \vert \sqrt{I}$$

where $A$ is the Debye–Hückel constant ($A = 0.509$ kg$^{1/2}$·mol$^{-1/2}$ in water at 298 K), $z_+$ and $z_-$ are ion charges, and $I$ is ionic strength.

*(Word count sufficient for 3 marks; mark split: 1.5 for definition + 1.5 for the law.)*

### Q2. What is ionic strength? How is it computed for a 0.05 M solution of AlCl$_3$ that dissociates completely? `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Ionic strength $I$ is a measure of the total electrostatic intensity produced by ions in solution:

$$I = \frac{1}{2} \sum_i m_i z_i^2$$

For 0.05 M AlCl$_3$, the dissociation gives [Al$^{3+}$] = 0.05 M and [Cl$^-$] = 0.15 M:

$$I = \frac{1}{2} [ (0.05)(3)^2 + (0.15)(1)^2 ] = \frac{1}{2} (0.45 + 0.15) = 0.30 \text{ M}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently forget that AlCl$_3$ produces **4 ions per formula unit** (1 + 3), giving $I = 6m$ instead of $I = 5m$. Always count total dissociated ions and square *each* charge.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the mean ionic activity coefficient $\gamma_\pm$ for a binary electrolyte $A_{v_+} B_{v_-}$ that dissociates completely. (7 marks)

**(b)** Calculate the mean ionic activity coefficient of a 0.01 mol/kg aqueous solution of CaCl$_2$ at 298 K using the Debye–Hückel Limiting Law. Given $A = 0.509$ kg$^{1/2}$·mol$^{-1/2}$. (7 marks)

### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**Step 1.** Write the dissociation:
$A_{v_+} B_{v_-} \rightarrow v_+ A^{z_+} + v_- B^{z_-}$

**Step 2.** Salt chemical potential equals sum of ion chemical potentials:
$\mu_{salt} = v_+ \mu_+ + v_- \mu_-$

**Step 3.** Substitute $\mu_i = \mu_i^\circ + RT \ln(\gamma_i m_i)$ for each ion and combine standard states into $\mu_{salt}^\circ = v_+ \mu_+^\circ + v_- \mu_-^\circ$. Using $\nu \ln x = \ln x^\nu$, the log term becomes $RT \ln[(\gamma_+ m_+)^{v_+}(\gamma_- m_-)^{v_-}]$.

**Step 4.** Factor molality and coefficient parts separately and define:

$$m_\pm = (m_+^{v_+} m_-^{v_-})^{1/\nu}, \quad \gamma_\pm = (\gamma_+^{v_+} \gamma_-^{v_-})^{1/\nu}$$

where $\nu = v_+ + v_-$. The salt chemical potential becomes:

$$\mu_{salt} = \mu_{salt}^\circ + \nu RT \ln(\gamma_\pm m_\pm)$$

This is the required result. (7 marks — full marks require showing the log-grouping steps explicitly.)

### Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**Step 1.** Compute ionic strength. For CaCl$_2$: $v_+ = 1$, $v_- = 2$, molality $m = 0.01$ mol/kg. So $m_+ = 0.01$, $m_- = 0.02$. Charges: $z_+ = +2$, $z_- = -1$.

$$I = \frac{1}{2}\sum m_i z_i^2 = \frac{1}{2}[(0.01)(4) + (0.02)(1)] = \frac{1}{2}(0.06) = 0.03 \text{ mol/kg}$$

**Step 2.** Apply DHLL:

$$\log_{10} \gamma_\pm = -A \vert z_+ z_- \vert \sqrt{I} = -(0.509)(2)(1)\sqrt{0.03}$$

$\sqrt{0.03} = 0.1732$

$$\log_{10} \gamma_\pm = -0.509 \times 2 \times 0.1732 = -0.1763$$

**Step 3.** Exponentiate:

$$\gamma_\pm = 10^{-0.1763} = 0.666$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{\gamma_\pm(\text{CaCl}_2) \approx 0.666}$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Define ionic strength. The intracellular fluid contains 0.14 M K$^+$, 0.012 M Na$^+$, 0.10 M Cl$^-$ and 0.025 M HPO$_4^{2-}$. Compute the ionic strength. (7 marks)

**(b)** State and explain the extended Debye–Hückel equation. Why does the activity coefficient of an ion decrease with increasing ionic strength? (7 marks)

### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**Step 1.** Apply $I = \frac{1}{2} \sum m_i z_i^2$:

$$I = \frac{1}{2} \left[ (0.14)(1)^2 + (0.012)(1)^2 + (0.10)(1)^2 + (0.025)(2)^2 \right]$$

$$I = \frac{1}{2} [ 0.14 + 0.012 + 0.10 + 0.100 ] = \frac{1}{2}(0.352) = 0.176 \text{ M}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{I = 0.176 \text{ M}}$

### Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**Statement:** The Extended Debye–Hückel equation is:

$$\log_{10} \gamma_\pm = -\frac{A \, z^2 \sqrt{I}}{1 + B \, a \sqrt{I}}$$

**Explanation:**
- $A$ and $B$ are temperature- and solvent-dependent constants ($A \approx 0.509$, $B \approx 0.328$ Å$^{-1}$·mol$^{-1/2}$·kg$^{1/2}$ in water at 298 K).
- $z$ is the ion charge.
- $a$ is the effective hydrated ionic radius (Å).
- The denominator $1 + B a \sqrt{I}$ accounts for the finite size of ions, making the equation valid up to $I \approx 0.1$ M (vs. only $10^{-3}$ M for DHLL).

**Why $\gamma$ decreases with $I$:** Each ion in solution is surrounded by an *ionic atmosphere* — a net excess of oppositely charged ions and a deficit of like-charged ions — that screens its electrostatic influence. As $I$ rises, this atmosphere becomes denser and screens the central ion more effectively, reducing its *effective* freedom of motion and hence its activity. Mathematically, $\sqrt{I}$ appears in the numerator with a negative sign, so $\gamma$ falls monotonically as $I$ increases.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Units of $\gamma$:** Examiners *will* deduct a mark if you write $\gamma$ with units. It is **strictly dimensionless**.
> 2. **Sign of $\gamma_\pm$:** $\gamma_\pm < 1$ for electrolytes in normal aqueous solution; never negative. Writing $\gamma < 0$ indicates a sign error worth **2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing molality vs. molarity:** Extended DH uses **molality** (mol/kg solvent). Conversion $m \approx c / \rho$ is needed only for very concentrated solutions.
> 4. **Forgetting to multiply by ½:** Half of all ionic-strength errors come from omitting the $1/2$ factor — examiners report this annually.
> 5. **Mixing ln and log:** Use $\log_{10}$ when $A$ is dimensionless (DH equation); use $\ln$ when writing chemical potentials with $RT$. Mixing them = 1 mark lost per occurrence.

## 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

- **Activity $a$:** effective concentration = $\gamma \cdot m$; dimensionless; $\gamma \to 1$ as solution becomes infinitely dilute.
- **Activity coefficient $\gamma$:** ratio of activity to molality; measures deviation from ideality; always dimensionless.
- **Mean ionic activity coefficient $\gamma_\pm$:** geometric mean of individual ionic coefficients weighted by stoichiometry: $\gamma_\pm = (\gamma_+^{v_+} \gamma_-^{v_-})^{1/\nu}$.
- **Mean ionic molality $m_\pm$:** geometric mean of individual ionic molalities with the same exponent weighting.
- **Ionic strength $I$:** $I = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum m_i z_i^2$; quantifies total electrostatic intensity.
- **DH Limiting Law (DHLL):** $\log_{10}\gamma_\pm = -A \vert z_+ z_- \vert \sqrt{I}$; valid for $I < 10^{-3}$ mol/kg.
- **Extended Debye–Hückel:** adds denominator $1 + B a \sqrt{I}$; valid for $I < 0.1$ mol/kg — the workhorse for biochemical buffers.
- **Davies equation:** another common empirical form valid up to $I \approx 0.5$ M.
- **Ionic atmosphere:** cloud of opposite charge around each ion — physical origin of activity-coefficient reduction.
- **Biochemical relevance:** essential for Nernst equation (membrane potentials), $K_{eq}$, buffer pH, protein solubility, enzyme $K_m$ corrections.
- **Common numerical values:** $A_{298K} = 0.509$ kg$^{1/2}$·mol$^{-1/2}$; $B_{298K} = 0.328$ Å$^{-1}$·kg$^{1/2}$·mol$^{-1/2}$; $a \approx 4$ Å for most hydrated monovalent ions.
- **KTU hot traps to avoid:** mixing ln/log; reporting units on $\gamma$; omitting the $1/2$ in ionic strength; using $c$ (molarity) instead of $m$ (molality) in DH equations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ionic Equilibria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT414 - BIOCHEMICAL THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Ionic Equilibria ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Ionic Equilibria** is the branch of physical chemistry that deals with the quantitative study of the dissociation (or association) of electrolytes in aqueous solution and the dynamic equilibrium established between the ionized and unionized forms of the species. In biochemical thermodynamics, ionic equilibria is the cornerstone for understanding **enzyme kinetics, protein folding, membrane transport, blood buffering, and metabolic regulation**. Every biochemical reaction in living systems occurs in an aqueous ionic medium, making the study of dissociation constants, pH, and buffer systems absolutely essential. ### 1.2 Key Terminology | Term | Definition | |------|------------| | **Electrolyte** | A substance that dissociates into ions in solution, conducting electricity | | **Strong Electrolyte** | Dissociates completely (≈100%) in aqueous solution (e.g., NaCl, HCl, KOH) | | **Weak Electrolyte** | Dissociates only partially, establishing a true equilibrium (e.g., CH₃COOH, NH₄OH) | | **Degree of Dissociation (α)** | Fraction of total molecules that ionize at equilibrium: $\alpha = \frac{\text{Number of molecules dissociated}}{\text{Total number of molecules initially}}$ | | **Ostwald's Dilution Law** | The relationship that quantitatively links the degree of dissociation of a weak electrolyte with its concentration | | **Ionic Product of Water (Kw)** | The product of H⁺ and OH⁻ ion concentrations in pure water at a given temperature | | **Solubility Product (Ksp)** | The equilibrium constant for a sparingly soluble salt's dissolution process | | **Common Ion Effect** | Suppression of dissociation of a weak electrolyte by adding a strong electrolyte sharing a common ion | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Reference Values (KTU Board Favorites):** > - Ionic product of water at **25 °C**: $K_w = 1.0 \times 10^{-14}$ > - Dissociation constant of acetic acid: $K_a = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ > - Dissociation constant of ammonia: $K_b = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ > - $[\text{H}^+]$ in pure water = $[\text{OH}^-]$ = $1.0 \times 10^{-7}$ M ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Molecular Dance Imagine a crowded dance floor where couples enter, dance briefly, and then separate, but some of the separated dancers eventually pair up again. The number of couples dancing at any moment is the **degree of dissociation**. A strong electrolyte is like a venue where every couple splits up immediately upon entering (NaCl → Na⁺ + Cl⁻), while a weak electrolyte is like a venue where only a small fraction of couples split up, and the rest stay paired. In **ionic equilibria**, we are not looking at a static "amount dissolved" — we are looking at a **dynamic tug-of-war** between two opposing processes: 1. **Forward reaction**: Molecules → Ions (dissociation) 2. **Reverse reaction**: Ions → Molecules (re-association) At equilibrium, the **rate of dissociation = rate of re-association**, giving us a constant ratio known as the **equilibrium constant** ($K_a$, $K_b$, or $K_{sp}$). ### 1.4 The pH Scale in Biology The **pH scale** (Søren Sørensen, 1909) is the most-used metric in biochemical thermodynamics: $$\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[\text{H}^+]$$ $$\text{pOH} = -\log_{10}[\text{OH}^-]$$ $$\text{pH} + \text{pOH} = 14 \quad (\text{at } 25\,°\text{C})$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Biological pH Hotspots (Memorize These for KTU):** > - Blood plasma: **pH 7.35–7.45** > - Cytosol: **pH ~7.2** > - Lysosomes: **pH ~4.5** > - Gastric juice: **pH ~1.5** > - Pancreatic juice: **pH ~8.0** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Electrolytes #### 2.1.1 Strong Electrolytes - Dissociate **completely** in aqueous solution. - Examples: All Group 1 salts (NaCl, KBr), most mineral acids (HCl, HNO₃, H₂SO₄), and strong bases (NaOH, KOH). - No equilibrium constant is needed because the reaction effectively goes to completion. - Conductivity does **not** change appreciably upon dilution (already fully dissociated). #### 2.1.2 Weak Electrolytes - Dissociate **partially**, reaching a dynamic equilibrium. - Examples: Weak acids (CH₃COOH, HCN, H₂CO₃), weak bases (NH₄OH), and water. - Governed by **equilibrium constants** ($K_a$ or $K_b$). - Conductivity **increases** with dilution (Le Chatelier's principle shifts equilibrium toward ionization). > [!NOTE] > **Why does dilution increase dissociation?** > When you add water, the concentration of ions falls. By Le Chatelier's principle, the equilibrium shifts to produce *more* ions to partially compensate — i.e., more molecules dissociate, raising $\alpha$. ### 2.2 Ostwald's Dilution Law (1888) Wilhelm Ostwald established the mathematical relationship between the degree of dissociation ($\alpha$) and concentration ($C$) for a weak electrolyte. **For a weak binary electrolyte AB** (like CH₃COOH): $$\text{AB} \rightleftharpoons \text{A}^+ + \text{B}^-$$ At equilibrium, if $C$ is the initial concentration and $\alpha$ is the degree of dissociation: - Concentration of undissociated AB = $C(1-\alpha)$ - Concentration of A⁺ = $C\alpha$ - Concentration of B⁻ = $C\alpha$ The dissociation constant is: $$K = \frac{[\text{A}^+][\text{B}^-]}{[\text{AB}]} = \frac{C\alpha \cdot C\alpha}{C(1-\alpha)} = \frac{C\alpha^2}{1-\alpha}$$ **Two special cases:** | Condition | Approximation | Simplified Form | |-----------|--------------|-----------------| | Very dilute solution (α ≪ 1) | $1-\alpha \approx 1$ | $K \approx C\alpha^2 \Rightarrow \alpha \approx \sqrt{K/C}$ | | α not negligible | Full expression | $K = \dfrac{C\alpha^2}{1-\alpha}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Insight:** $\alpha$ is **inversely proportional to $\sqrt{C}$** for dilute solutions. As $C \to 0$, $\alpha \to 1$ (complete dissociation at infinite dilution — confirmed experimentally by Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions). ### 2.3 Acid-Base Equilibria #### 2.3.1 Brønsted-Lowry Definition (Most Relevant to Biochemistry) - **Acid**: Proton (H⁺) donor - **Base**: Proton (H⁺) acceptor For a weak acid HA: $$\text{HA} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_3\text{O}^+ + \text{A}^-$$ $$K_a = \frac{[\text{H}_3\text{O}^+][\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$ For its conjugate base A⁻: $$\text{A}^- + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{HA} + \text{OH}^-$$ $$K_b = \frac{[\text{HA}][\text{OH}^-]}{[\text{A}^-]}$$ **Crucial relationship:** $$K_a \times K_b = K_w = 1.0 \times 10^{-14} \text{ (at 25 °C)}$$ $$\text{p}K_a + \text{p}K_b = 14$$ #### 2.3.2 Lewis Definition - **Acid**: Electron-pair acceptor - **Base**: Electron-pair donor - Includes species without H⁺ (e.g., BF₃, AlCl₃) ### 2.4 Buffer Solutions A **buffer** resists pH change upon addition of small amounts of strong acid or base. It consists of: - A **weak acid** + its **conjugate base** (acidic buffer) - A **weak base** + its **conjugate acid** (basic buffer) **Henderson-Hasselbalch Equation** (Most tested formula in KTU biochemistry): $$\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log_{10}\left(\frac{[\text{salt}]}{[\text{acid}]}\right) = \text{p}K_a + \log_{10}\left(\frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}\right)$$ **Buffer Capacity** ($\beta$): The amount (in moles) of strong acid or base required to change the pH of 1 L of buffer by 1 unit. $$\beta = \frac{dn}{d(\text{pH})}$$ Maximum buffer capacity occurs at **pH = pKa** (when [salt] = [acid]). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Effective Buffer Range:** $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a \pm 1$ ### 2.5 Hydrolysis of Salts Salts of strong acids and strong bases do **not** hydrolyze (pH = 7). Salts of weak acids/bases do hydrolyze: | Salt Type | Example | Nature | pH | |-----------|---------|--------|-----| | Strong acid + Strong base | NaCl | Neutral | 7 | | Strong acid + Weak base | NH₄Cl | Acidic | < 7 | | Weak acid + Strong base | CH₃COONa | Basic | > 7 | | Weak acid + Weak base | CH₃COONH₄ | Depends on $K_a$ vs $K_b$ | ≈ 7 if $K_a = K_b$ | For a salt of weak acid + strong base (e.g., CH₃COONa): $$h = \sqrt{\frac{K_w}{K_a \cdot C}} \quad \text{(degree of hydrolysis)}$$ ### 2.6 Solubility Product & Common Ion Effect For a sparingly soluble salt $\text{A}_m\text{B}_n$: $$\text{A}_m\text{B}_n(s) \rightleftharpoons m\text{A}^{n+} + n\text{B}^{m-}$$ $$K_{sp} = [\text{A}^{n+}]^m [\text{B}^{m-}]^n$$ **Common Ion Effect:** Adding a salt with a common ion decreases the solubility of the sparingly soluble salt. For AgCl in presence of NaCl: $$K_{sp} = [\text{Ag}^+][\text{Cl}^-]$$ If [Cl⁻] from NaCl = $x$ and [Ag⁺] = $s$ (solubility): $$K_{sp} = s(s+x) \approx s \cdot x \quad \text{(if } x \gg s\text{)}$$ ### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---------|---------|---------------| | 1 | pH | $\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[\text{H}^+]$ | Dimensionless; [H⁺] in mol/L | | 2 | pOH | $\text{pOH} = -\log_{10}[\text{OH}^-]$ | Dimensionless | | 3 | Ionic Product of Water | $K_w = [\text{H}^+][\text{OH}^-] = 1.0 \times 10^{-14}$ | At 25 °C | | 4 | pH + pOH | $\text{pH} + \text{pOH} = 14$ | At 25 °C | | 5 | Ostwald's Dilution Law | $K = \dfrac{C\alpha^2}{1-\alpha}$ | Weak binary electrolyte | | 6 | Dilute Approximation | $\alpha = \sqrt{K/C}$ | Valid when $\alpha \ll 1$ | | 7 | Acid Dissociation | $K_a = \dfrac{[\text{H}^+][\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$ | For HA ⇌ H⁺ + A⁻ | | 8 | Base Dissociation | $K_b = \dfrac{[\text{BH}^+][\text{OH}^-]}{[\text{B}]}$ | For B + H₂O ⇌ BH⁺ + OH⁻ | | 9 | Conjugate Pair | $K_a \cdot K_b = K_w$ | $\text{p}K_a + \text{p}K_b = 14$ | | 10 | Henderson-Hasselbalch | $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}\right)$ | Buffer equation | | 11 | Hydrolysis (weak acid + strong base salt) | $h = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_w}{K_a C}}$ | Degree of hydrolysis | | 12 | Hydrolysis pH | $\text{pH} = 7 + \dfrac{1}{2}\text{p}K_a + \dfrac{1}{2}\log C$ | For salt of WA + SB | | 13 | Solubility Product | $K_{sp} = [\text{A}^{n+}]^m [\text{B}^{m-}]^n$ | For A$_m$B$_n$ | | 14 | Molar Solubility | $s = \sqrt{K_{sp}}$ | For 1:1 salt (e.g., AgCl) | | 15 | Common Ion Solubility | $s = K_{sp}/[\text{common ion}]$ | Approximation | | 16 | Buffer Capacity | $\beta = 2.303 \cdot C \cdot \dfrac{K_a [\text{H}^+]}{(K_a + [\text{H}^+])^2}$ | Maximum at pH = pKa | | 17 | Polyprotic Acid (1st pH) | $\text{pH} \approx \dfrac{1}{2}(\text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{a2})$ | Amphiprotic species | > **Engineering Utility:** Ionic equilibria governs corrosion control, water purification, pharmaceutical formulation, electrochemical batteries, bioprocess engineering, blood gas analysis in clinical biochemistry, and enzyme assay design. **Every** pH measurement in a biochemical lab is an application of these equilibrium principles. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Ostwald's Dilution Law (Exhaustive) **Setup:** Consider a weak binary electrolyte AB dissolved in water. Let the initial molar concentration be $C$ mol/L. **Step 1:** Write the dissociation equation with stoichiometry. $$\text{AB} \rightleftharpoons \text{A}^+ + \text{B}^-$$ **Step 2:** Construct an ICE (Initial-Change-Equilibrium) table. | Species | AB | A⁺ | B⁻ | |---------|-----|------|------| | Initial (mol/L) | $C$ | 0 | 0 | | Change | $-C\alpha$ | $+C\alpha$ | $+C\alpha$ | | Equilibrium | $C(1-\alpha)$ | $C\alpha$ | $C\alpha$ | **Step 3:** Write the equilibrium constant expression. $$K = \frac{[\text{A}^+][\text{B}^-]}{[\text{AB}]}$$ **Step 4:** Substitute equilibrium concentrations from the ICE table. $$K = \frac{(C\alpha)(C\alpha)}{C(1-\alpha)} = \frac{C^2\alpha^2}{C(1-\alpha)}$$ **Step 5:** Simplify algebraically. $$K = \frac{C\alpha^2}{1-\alpha} \quad \text{...(Ostwald's Dilution Law — full form)}$$ **Step 6:** Apply the dilute-solution approximation. When $\alpha \ll 1$ (i.e., the electrolyte is very weak OR the solution is very dilute), $1-\alpha \approx 1$. $$K \approx C\alpha^2$$ **Step 7:** Solve for $\alpha$. $$\alpha^2 = \frac{K}{C} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = \sqrt{\frac{K}{C}}$$ **Step 8:** Solve for $C$ in terms of $\alpha$ (useful for KTU numericals). $$C = \frac{K}{\alpha^2}$$ **Step 9:** Take the negative logarithm to obtain the "dilution law in p-form": $$-\log C = -\log K - 2\log \alpha$$ $$\text{p}K_a - 2\log\alpha = -\log C$$ > **Numerical interpretation:** A **tenfold dilution** (C decreases by 10×) causes $\alpha$ to increase by $\sqrt{10} \approx 3.16$ times. This is the heart of why weak-electrolyte conductivity changes with dilution while strong-electrolyte conductivity barely changes. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of the Henderson-Hasselbalch Equation **Step 1:** Start from the acid dissociation expression. $$K_a = \frac{[\text{H}^+][\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$ **Step 2:** Rearrange to isolate $[\text{H}^+]$. $$[\text{H}^+] = K_a \cdot \frac{[\text{HA}]}{[\text{A}^-]}$$ **Step 3:** Take $-\log_{10}$ of both sides. $$-\log[\text{H}^+] = -\log K_a - \log\frac{[\text{HA}]}{[\text{A}^-]}$$ **Step 4:** Recognize that $\text{pH} = -\log[\text{H}^+]$ and $\text{p}K_a = -\log K_a$. $$\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a - \log\frac{[\text{HA}]}{[\text{A}^-]}$$ **Step 5:** Use the logarithm identity $-\log(x/y) = \log(y/x)$. $$\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log\frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$ $$\boxed{\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log_{10}\left(\frac{[\text{salt}]}{[\text{acid}]}\right)}$$ **Step 6:** Limiting cases. - When $[\text{A}^-] = [\text{HA}] \Rightarrow \text{pH} = \text{p}K_a$ (half-equivalence point of titration). - When $[\text{A}^-] \gg [\text{HA}] \Rightarrow \text{pH} \gg \text{p}K_a$ (fully deprotonated regime). --- ### 3.3 Derivation of Hydrolysis Constant & pH of Salt Solution **Problem:** Determine the pH of an aqueous solution of sodium acetate (CH₃COONa) of concentration $C$ mol/L. **Step 1:** Identify the hydrolysis equilibrium. Acetate ion is the conjugate base of a weak acid. $$\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^- + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} + \text{OH}^-$$ **Step 2:** Write the hydrolysis constant $K_h$. $$K_h = \frac{[\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}][\text{OH}^-]}{[\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^-]}$$ **Step 3:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $[\text{H}^+]$ and use $K_w = [\text{H}^+][\text{OH}^-]$ and $K_a = [\text{H}^+][\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}]$. $$K_h = \frac{[\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}]}{[\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^-][\text{H}^+]} \cdot [\text{H}^+][\text{OH}^-] = \frac{K_w}{K_a}$$ **Step 4:** Let $h$ = degree of hydrolysis. ICE table for $C$ mol/L CH₃COONa: | Species | CH₃COO⁻ | CH₃COOH | OH⁻ | |---------|---------|---------|------| | Initial | $C$ | 0 | 0 | | Change | $-Ch$ | $+Ch$ | $+Ch$ | | Eq. | $C(1-h)$ | $Ch$ | $Ch$ | **Step 5:** Substitute into $K_h$. $$K_h = \frac{Ch \cdot Ch}{C(1-h)} = \frac{Ch^2}{1-h}$$ **Step 6:** Since $h$ is small for typical salt concentrations, $1-h \approx 1$: $$K_h \approx Ch^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad h = \sqrt{\frac{K_h}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{K_w}{K_a \cdot C}}$$ **Step 7:** Calculate $[\text{OH}^-] = Ch$: $$[\text{OH}^-] = C \cdot \sqrt{\frac{K_w}{K_a \cdot C}} = \sqrt{\frac{K_w \cdot C}{K_a}}$$ **Step 8:** Take negative logarithms. $$\text{pOH} = \frac{1}{2}(\text{p}K_w - \text{p}K_a - \log C)$$ **Step 9:** Convert to pH using $\text{pH} = 14 - \text{pOH}$ (at 25 °C). $$\boxed{\text{pH} = 7 + \frac{1}{2}\text{p}K_a + \frac{1}{2}\log C}$$ --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** Calculate the pH of a buffer containing 0.20 M acetic acid and 0.30 M sodium acetate. Given $K_a = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Compute $\text{p}K_a$. $$\text{p}K_a = -\log(1.8 \times 10^{-5}) = 5 - \log 1.8 = 5 - 0.2553 = 4.745$$ **Step 2:** Apply Henderson-Hasselbalch. $$\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log\frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$ $$\text{pH} = 4.745 + \log\frac{0.30}{0.20}$$ **Step 3:** Evaluate the logarithm. $$\log\frac{0.30}{0.20} = \log 1.5 = 0.1761$$ **Step 4:** Final answer. $$\text{pH} = 4.745 + 0.176 = 4.921$$ **Step 5:** Verify buffer range: $4.745 \pm 1 = 3.745$ to $5.745$. The calculated pH = 4.92 lies inside this range — buffer is effective. ✓ **Step 6 (Challenge):** If 0.01 mol of HCl is added to 1 L of this buffer, what is the new pH? - HCl converts 0.01 mol of CH₃COO⁻ to CH₃COOH. - New [CH₃COO⁻] = 0.30 − 0.01 = 0.29 M - New [CH₃COOH] = 0.20 + 0.01 = 0.21 M $$\text{pH}_{\text{new}} = 4.745 + \log\frac{0.29}{0.21} = 4.745 + \log 1.381 = 4.745 + 0.140 = 4.885$$ The pH only dropped by 0.036 units — illustrating the **buffering power**. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flow: Ionic Equilibrium Master Framework ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Electrolyte in Water] --> B{Type?} B -- Strong --> C[Complete Dissociation
No Equilibrium Constant
NaCl, HCl, NaOH] B -- Weak --> D[Partial Dissociation
True Equilibrium] D --> E[Acid HA] D --> F[Base BOH] E --> G[Ka Expression] F --> H[Kb Expression] G --> I[Ka x Kb = Kw] H --> I I --> J[Buffer System
HA + A- Conjugate Pair] J --> K[Henderson Hasselbalch
pH = pKa + log A-/HA] K --> L[Buffer Capacity max at pH = pKa] D --> M[Salt Hydrolysis] M --> N[WA + SB Salt Basic
SA + WB Salt Acidic] D --> O[Sparingly Soluble Salt] O --> P[Ksp Expression] P --> Q[Common Ion Effect] ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Buffer Action in Blood ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[Metabolic Input] A1[CO2 from Cellular Respiration] A2[Lactic Acid from Anaerobic Metabolism] A3[Dietary Acid Load] end subgraph BUFFER[Bicarbonate Buffer System H2CO3 HCO3-] B1[Carbonic Acid H2CO3
Carbonic Anhydrase] B2[Bicarbonate Ion HCO3-] B1 --> B2 B2 --> B1 end subgraph RESPONSE[Physiological Compensation] C1[Renal Regulation
HCO3- Reabsorption] C2[Respiratory Regulation
