Ventilation Rate] end A1 --> BUFFER A2 --> BUFFER A3 --> BUFFER BUFFER --> OUTPUT OUTPUT[Stable Blood pH 7.35 to 7.45] --> C1 OUTPUT --> C2 C1 --> BUFFER C2 --> BUFFER ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree: Solving Ionic Equilibrium Problems ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[KTU Problem Given] --> Q1{What is asked?} Q1 -- pH or pOH --> Q2{Given Ka, Kb or C?} Q1 -- Degree of Dissociation --> Q3[Use Ostwalds Law K = C alpha squared over 1 minus alpha] Q1 -- Solubility --> Q4[Use Ksp Expression] Q1 -- Buffer pH --> Q5[Use Henderson Hasselbalch] Q2 -- Strong Acid or Base --> P1[Complete Dissociation pH = minus log C] Q2 -- Weak Acid --> P2[Solve Ka = H plus A minus over HA] Q2 -- Weak Base --> P3[Solve Kb and convert via Kw] Q5 --> P4[Identify conjugate pair ratio] P4 --> P5[Plug into HH equation] ``` ### 4.4 Titration Curve Schematic (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[pH starts at 1.5 strong acid] --> B[Gradual rise with weak base addition] B --> C[Half Equivalence Point pH = pKa] C --> D[Steep rise near equivalence] D --> E[Equivalence Point pH greater than 7 for WA SB] E --> F[Plateau at high pH] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define ionic product of water. How is it related to pH and pOH at 25 °C?* **Model Answer:** The ionic product of water ($K_w$) is the product of the molar concentrations of hydrogen and hydroxide ions in pure water (or any dilute aqueous solution) at a given temperature: $$K_w = [\text{H}^+][\text{OH}^-] = 1.0 \times 10^{-14} \text{ at } 25\,°\text{C}$$ Relation to pH and pOH: $$\text{pH} = -\log[\text{H}^+], \quad \text{pOH} = -\log[\text{OH}^-]$$ Taking $-\log$ of $K_w$: $$-\log K_w = -\log[\text{H}^+] - \log[\text{OH}^-]$$ $$14 = \text{pH} + \text{pOH} \quad \text{(at 25 °C)}$$ --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State Ostwald's dilution law. Write its mathematical expression for a weak binary electrolyte.* **Model Answer:** Ostwald's dilution law states that *for a weak electrolyte, the degree of dissociation ($\alpha$) is inversely proportional to the square root of concentration, and the dissociation constant remains constant at a given temperature.* For a weak binary electrolyte $\text{AB} \rightleftharpoons \text{A}^+ + \text{B}^-$, the mathematical expression is: $$K = \frac{C\alpha^2}{1-\alpha}$$ where $C$ = initial concentration, $\alpha$ = degree of dissociation, and $K$ = dissociation constant. For very dilute solutions where $\alpha \ll 1$, the equation reduces to: $$\alpha = \sqrt{\frac{K}{C}}$$ --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]* **(a)** Derive the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation starting from the expression for the dissociation constant of a weak acid. Discuss its significance in biological buffer systems with at least two examples. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** The pH of human blood is maintained at 7.4 by the bicarbonate buffer system. If the concentration of H₂CO₃ in blood is $1.4 \times 10^{-3}$ M and the pKa of carbonic acid is 6.1, calculate the concentration of bicarbonate ion required. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution for (a):** **Derivation:** Step 1: For a weak acid HA dissociating in water: $$\text{HA} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{A}^-$$ $$K_a = \frac{[\text{H}^+][\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$ Step 2: Rearranging: $$[\text{H}^+] = K_a \cdot \frac{[\text{HA}]}{[\text{A}^-]}$$ Step 3: Taking $-\log$ on both sides: $$-\log[\text{H}^+] = -\log K_a - \log\frac{[\text{HA}]}{[\text{A}^-]}$$ Step 4: Since $\text{pH} = -\log[\text{H}^+]$ and $\text{p}K_a = -\log K_a$: $$\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log\frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$ This is the **Henderson-Hasselbalch equation**. **Biological Significance:** 1. **Bicarbonate buffer in blood:** Maintains blood pH at 7.4, essential for oxygen transport by hemoglobin. Equation: $\text{pH} = 6.1 + \log\frac{[\text{HCO}_3^-]}{[\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3]}$ 2. **Phosphate buffer in cells:** Maintains intracellular pH near 7.2. Equation: $\text{pH} = 7.2 + \log\frac{[\text{HPO}_4^{2-}]}{[\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4^-]}$ 3. **Amino acid buffers:** Proteins contain ionizable side chains (histidine pKa ≈ 6.0) that buffer cytosolic pH changes. **Model Solution for (b):** Given: $\text{pH} = 7.4$, $\text{p}K_a = 6.1$, $[\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3] = 1.4 \times 10^{-3}$ M. Apply Henderson-Hasselbalch: $$\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log\frac{[\text{HCO}_3^-]}{[\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3]}$$ $$7.4 = 6.1 + \log\frac{[\text{HCO}_3^-]}{1.4 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$\log\frac{[\text{HCO}_3^-]}{1.4 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.3$$ $$\frac{[\text{HCO}_3^-]}{1.4 \times 10^{-3}} = 10^{1.3} = 19.95$$ $$[\text{HCO}_3^-] = 1.4 \times 10^{-3} \times 19.95 = 2.79 \times 10^{-2} \text{ M} \approx 0.028 \text{ M}$$ **Answer:** The bicarbonate ion concentration required is approximately **0.028 M** (28 mM), which matches the physiological value. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]* **(a)** What is the common ion effect? Explain with a suitable example and derive the relationship between solubility and common ion concentration. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** The solubility product of AgCl is $1.8 \times 10^{-10}$ at 25 °C. Calculate its molar solubility in (i) pure water and (ii) 0.1 M NaCl solution. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution for (a):** **Definition:** The common ion effect is the suppression of the dissociation of a weak electrolyte (or the reduction in solubility of a sparingly soluble salt) by the addition of a strong electrolyte that shares a common ion. **Example:** AgCl in NaCl solution. - AgCl: $\text{AgCl}(s) \rightleftharpoons \text{Ag}^+ + \text{Cl}^-$ - NaCl: $\text{NaCl} \to \text{Na}^+ + \text{Cl}^-$ (fully dissociated) - The added Cl⁻ from NaCl shifts the AgCl equilibrium to the left, reducing solubility. **Derivation:** Let $s$ = solubility of AgCl in presence of common ion. If the concentration of common ion (Cl⁻ from NaCl) is $x$: $$K_{sp} = [\text{Ag}^+][\text{Cl}^-] = (s)(s+x)$$ When $x \gg s$ (typical case): $$K_{sp} \approx s \cdot x \quad \Rightarrow \quad s = \frac{K_{sp}}{x}$$ **Model Solution for (b):** **(i) Solubility in pure water:** For AgCl: $K_{sp} = [\text{Ag}^+][\text{Cl}^-] = s \cdot s = s^2$ $$s = \sqrt{K_{sp}} = \sqrt{1.8 \times 10^{-10}}$$ $$s = 1.34 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol/L}$$ **(ii) Solubility in 0.1 M NaCl:** Common ion [Cl⁻] = 0.1 M. Let solubility of AgCl = $s$. Then: $$[\text{Ag}^+] = s, \quad [\text{Cl}^-] = 0.1 + s \approx 0.1 \text{ (since } s \ll 0.1\text{)}$$ $$K_{sp} = s \times 0.1 = 1.8 \times 10^{-10}$$ $$s = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{-10}}{0.1} = 1.8 \times 10^{-9} \text{ mol/L}$$ **Comparison:** Solubility decreases from $1.34 \times 10^{-5}$ M to $1.8 \times 10^{-9}$ M — a **7,400-fold decrease** due to the common ion effect. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit mismatch**: Always quote concentrations in mol/L (M) when using equilibrium constants. Never mix g/L with $K_{sp}$ in (mol/L)² form. > 2. **Approximation abuse**: The $1-\alpha \approx 1$ approximation is valid only when $\alpha < 0.05$ (i.e., 5%). For $\alpha$ between 5–20%, you MUST use the full quadratic. > 3. **Temperature dependence**: $K_w = 1.0 \times 10^{-14}$ **only at 25 °C**. At body temperature (37 °C), $K_w \approx 2.4 \times 10^{-14}$ — relevant for biochemical calculations. > 4. **Conjugate pair confusion**: For the conjugate base A⁻, $K_b$ refers to the reaction with water (forming OH⁻), not direct neutralization. > 5. **Henderson-Hasselbalch misuse**: HH is for **buffers** (significant [HA] and [A⁻]). It is **not** valid for strong acids, strong bases, or very dilute weak-acid solutions where dissociation is nearly complete. > 6. **Forgetting log base**: In KTU exams, always use $\log_{10}$ explicitly. Some students use $\ln$ (natural log) and lose full marks. > 7. **Solubility vs molar solubility**: $K_{sp}$ units depend on stoichiometry. For CaF₂ (1:2 salt), $K_{sp} = 4s^3$, **not** $s^2$. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Ionic product of water** at 25 °C: $K_w = 1.0 \times 10^{-14}$; pH + pOH = 14 - ✅ **Ostwald's Dilution Law**: $K = C\alpha^2/(1-\alpha)$; for dilute solutions $\alpha = \sqrt{K/C}$ - ✅ **Strong electrolytes** dissociate completely; **weak electrolytes** establish equilibrium - ✅ **Acid-base conjugate relationship**: $K_a \times K_b = K_w$ and $\text{p}K_a + \text{p}K_b = 14$ - ✅ **Henderson-Hasselbalch equation**: $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log([\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}])$ — memorize this - ✅ **Maximum buffer capacity** at pH = pKa; effective range is $\text{p}K_a \pm 1$ - ✅ **Hydrolysis of salt of weak acid + strong base**: $\text{pH} = 7 + \frac{1}{2}\text{p}K_a + \frac{1}{2}\log C$ - ✅ **Solubility product** for $\text{A}_m\text{B}_n$: $K_{sp} = [\text{A}]^m [\text{B}]^n$ - ✅ **Common ion effect** suppresses dissociation/solubility - ✅ **Biological pH values** to memorize: blood 7.4, cytosol 7.2, lysosome 4.5, stomach 1.5 - ✅ **Key buffer systems**: Bicarbonate (blood), Phosphate (cells), Protein (intracellular) - ✅ **KTU must-derive**: Ostwald's law, Henderson-Hasselbalch, hydrolysis pH - ✅ **Quadratic requirement**: When $\alpha > 0.05$, use full form $C\alpha^2 + K\alpha - K = 0$

topics.title: Dissociation Equilibria of Acids and Bases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT414 - BIOCHEMICAL THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dissociation Equilibria of Acids and Bases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Dissociation Equilibria of Acids and Bases** refers to the dynamic, reversible chemical equilibrium established when a Brønsted–Lowry acid donates a proton (H⁺) or a Brønsted–Lowry base accepts a proton in an aqueous (or biochemical) medium. At equilibrium, the rates of the forward (dissociation) and reverse (association) reactions become equal, and the system is characterized by a thermodynamically meaningful **equilibrium constant (Keq)** — for acids this is the **acid dissociation constant (Ka)** and for bases, the **base dissociation constant (Kb)**.

Mathematically, for a generic monoprotic weak acid HA dissociating in water:

$$\text{HA} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_3\text{O}^+ + \text{A}^-$$

the equilibrium constant expression is:

$$K_a = \frac{[\text{H}_3\text{O}^+][\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, this topic is treated thermodynamically: every dissociation constant is a direct link to the standard Gibbs free energy change through the relation $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_a$. This couples Module 3 directly to Module 1 (Thermodynamic Laws) and Module 2 (Energetics of Biochemical Reactions).

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Acid–Base Theory

| Theory | Acid Definition | Base Definition | KTU Relevance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Arrhenius (1884)** | Releases H⁺ in water | Releases OH⁻ in water | Historical context only |
| **Brønsted–Lowry (1923)** | **Proton (H⁺) donor** | **Proton (H⁺) acceptor** | Core for Module 3 derivations |
| **Lewis (1923)** | Electron-pair acceptor | Electron-pair donor | Brief mention; not derivation focus |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Proton Relay Race" 🏃

Imagine a relay race where the baton is a **proton (H⁺)**. The acid is the runner who currently holds the baton. The base is the runner waiting to receive it. At equilibrium, the baton is being passed back and forth so frequently that **statistically, you can predict exactly what fraction of the runners are holding the baton at any instant** — that fraction is governed by $K_a$.

- A **strong acid** = a sprinter who drops the baton the moment the race begins (fully dissociated, $K_a \to \infty$).
- A **weak acid** = a reluctant runner who only passes the baton to a few teammates ($K_a$ is small, e.g., $10^{-5}$).
- **Water** is the "neutral track" — it can act as either donor (acid) or acceptor (base), which is why it is called **amphiprotic** ($K_w = 10^{-14}$ at **25 °C**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Self-Ionization of Water (Autoprotolysis):** $\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_3\text{O}^+ + \text{OH}^-$, with $K_w = [\text{H}_3\text{O}^+][\text{OH}^-] = 1.0 \times 10^{-14}$ at 25 °C. This constant is the master anchor of the entire pH scale and is **temperature-dependent** (it rises to ~$10^{-12}$ at body temperature 37 °C).

## 1.4 Conjugate Acid–Base Pairs — The "Family Tree"

A Brønsted–Lowry acid and the species formed after it loses a proton are called a **conjugate acid–base pair**. The relationship is symmetrical:

$$\underbrace{\text{HA}}_{\text{acid}} \rightleftharpoons \underbrace{\text{H}^+}_{\text{proton}} + \underbrace{\text{A}^-}_{\text{conjugate base}}$$

The **stronger the acid, the weaker its conjugate base** (and vice versa). This is captured quantitatively by:

$$K_a \times K_b = K_w = 1.0 \times 10^{-14} \quad \text{(at 25 °C)}$$

where $K_b$ is the dissociation constant of the conjugate base of HA.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Dissociation is an Equilibrium Problem (Not Just a Reaction)

A weak acid in water does not "complete" the reaction — it reaches a steady state where:

$$\text{Rate}_{\text{forward}} = \text{Rate}_{\text{reverse}}$$

From **thermodynamic principles** (Module 1), the equilibrium constant is governed by:

$$\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_a$$

- If $\Delta G^\circ < 0$, the reaction favors products → $K_a > 1$ → strong acid.
- If $\Delta G^\circ > 0$, the reaction favors reactants → $K_a < 1$ → weak acid.

At **standard biochemical conditions** (pH 7, 25 °C, 1 atm), the transformed constant is $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -RT \ln K_a$.

## 2.2 The pH Scale — A Logarithmic Convenience

The **pH** was introduced by Søren Sørensen (1909) to convert unwieldy concentrations into manageable numbers:

$$\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[\text{H}_3\text{O}^+] \quad ; \quad \text{pOH} = -\log_{10}[\text{OH}^-] \quad ; \quad \text{p}K_a = -\log_{10} K_a$$

Because $K_w$ is constant, taking negative logs gives:

$$\text{pH} + \text{pOH} = \text{p}K_w = 14 \quad \text{(at 25 °C)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** pH is **dimensionless** but you must always specify the temperature when quoting pH or pKw. At body temperature (37 °C), pKw ≈ 13.62, so neutral pH ≈ 6.8, **not 7.0**.

## 2.3 Strong vs. Weak Electrolytes

- **Strong acids** (HCl, HBr, HI, HNO₃, HClO₄, H₂SO₄ in first dissociation): completely dissociated; $[\text{H}^+] = C_0$ (initial concentration).
- **Weak acids** (CH₃COOH, HCOOH, H₃PO₄, HF, most biological acids): partially dissociated; use ICE table + $K_a$ expression.

## 2.4 Degree of Dissociation (α)

For a weak acid HA of initial concentration $C$:

| Species | HA | H₃O⁺ | A⁻ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Initial (I) | $C$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| Change (C) | $-C\alpha$ | $+C\alpha$ | $+C\alpha$ |
| Equilibrium (E) | $C(1-\alpha)$ | $C\alpha$ | $C\alpha$ |

Substituting into the $K_a$ expression:

$$K_a = \frac{(C\alpha)(C\alpha)}{C(1-\alpha)} = \frac{C\alpha^2}{1-\alpha}$$

## 2.5 Ostwald's Dilution Law (1894)

**Wilhelm Ostwald** recognized that for a weak acid, the degree of dissociation $\alpha$ depends on concentration. Re-arranging the equation above:

$$K_a = \frac{C\alpha^2}{1-\alpha}$$

For **dilute solutions** ($\alpha \ll 1$, so $1-\alpha \approx 1$):

$$\alpha \approx \sqrt{\frac{K_a}{C}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad [\text{H}^+] = C\alpha \approx \sqrt{K_a \cdot C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** As you dilute a weak acid ($C \to 0$), $\alpha \to 1$ — eventually the acid is fully dissociated. This is why dilution increases the fractional dissociation, even though $[\text{H}^+]$ decreases.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Boundary / Limit |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $K_a = \dfrac{[\text{H}_3\text{O}^+][\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$ | Acid dissociation constant | Valid for weak acids only |
| 2 | $K_b = \dfrac{[\text{OH}^-][\text{HA}]}{[\text{A}^-]}$ | Base dissociation constant | Valid for weak bases only |
| 3 | $K_a \cdot K_b = K_w = 1.0 \times 10^{-14}$ | Conjugate pair relationship | At **25 °C** |
| 4 | $\text{pH} = -\log[\text{H}_3\text{O}^+]$ | Definition of pH | $[\text{H}_3\text{O}^+]$ in mol/L |
| 5 | $\text{pH} + \text{pOH} = 14$ | Water ion product log-form | At 25 °C only |
| 6 | $\text{p}K_a = \text{pH} - \log\dfrac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$ | Henderson–Hasselbalch | Valid for $\dfrac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]} \in [0.1, 10]$ |
| 7 | $\alpha = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_a}{C}}$ | Ostwald's dilution law (dilute) | $\alpha \ll 1$ required |
| 8 | $[\text{H}^+] = \sqrt{K_a \cdot C}$ | $[\text{H}^+]$ for weak monoprotic acid | $\alpha \ll 1$ required |
| 9 | $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_a$ | Thermodynamic link | $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹ |
| 10 | $\beta = 2.303 \, C \, \dfrac{K_a[\text{H}^+]}{(K_a + [\text{H}^+])^2}$ | Buffer capacity (Van Slyke) | Maximum at $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a$ |

## 2.7 Henderson–Hasselbalch Equation (1908–1916)

For a buffer consisting of a weak acid HA and its conjugate base A⁻ (e.g., CH₃COOH/CH₃COO⁻), the pH is governed by:

$$\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log_{10}\frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$

This equation is the **single most tested formula** in KTU Module 3 problems. It tells you:
- When $[\text{A}^-] = [\text{HA}]$, $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a$ (the **half-equivalence point** of a titration).
- When $[\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}] = 10$, $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + 1$.
- When $[\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}] = 0.1$, $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a - 1$.
- The buffer works effectively within ±1 pH unit of $\text{p}K_a$.

## 2.8 Buffer Capacity (β)

The **buffer capacity** is the amount of strong acid or base (in moles) needed to change the pH of 1 L of solution by one unit:

$$\beta = \frac{dn_{base}}{d(\text{pH})} = 2.303 \, C_{\text{total}} \frac{K_a[\text{H}^+]}{(K_a + [\text{H}^+])^2}$$

$\beta$ is **maximum** at $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a$ and approaches zero at the extremes of the pH scale. This is why **biological buffers** (phosphate at pH 7.4, carbonate at pH 6.3, Tris at pH 7.1, HEPES at pH 7.5) are chosen so that their $\text{p}K_a$ is close to the desired working pH.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biochemical Significance:** Human blood maintains pH 7.40 ± 0.05 using the **carbonic acid/bicarbonate** buffer ($\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3/\text{HCO}_3^-$, $\text{p}K_{a1} = 6.35$). A shift outside 7.0–7.8 causes **acidosis** or **alkalosis**, which is life-threatening — the Henderson–Hasselbalch equation quantifies the body's compensatory mechanisms.

## 2.9 Polyprotic Acids

Acids with multiple dissociable protons (e.g., H₃PO₄, H₂CO₃, citric acid, amino acids) have **multiple $K_a$ values** ($K_{a1} > K_{a2} > K_{a3}$), typically separated by 4–5 orders of magnitude. Each dissociation step is governed by its own equilibrium:

$$\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{PO}_4^- \quad (K_{a1} = 7.1 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4^- \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{HPO}_4^{2-} \quad (K_{a2} = 6.2 \times 10^{-8})$$

$$\text{HPO}_4^{2-} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{PO}_4^{3-} \quad (K_{a3} = 4.5 \times 10^{-13})$$

For **amino acids** (zwitterions), this leads to the **isoelectric point (pI)**:

$$\text{pI} = \frac{\text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{a2}}{2}$$

(For neutral amino acids like glycine, $\text{pI} \approx 6.06$.)

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering & Bio-Utility

| Field | Application | Equilibrium Used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Biomedical Engineering** | Hemodialysis, blood-gas analyzers | $\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3 / \text{HCO}_3^-$ buffer |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Drug absorption (weak acid/base, Henderson–Hasselbalch) | pKa of drug vs. GI tract pH |
| **Fermentation Tech** | Microbial media design | Phosphate, citrate buffers |
| **Food Engineering** | Preservation, taste pH control | Acetic, citric, lactic acid |
| **Environmental Engg** | Acid rain (pH < 5.6), lake buffering | $\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3 / \text{HCO}_3^-$ |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Acid Dissociation Constant from First Principles

We start with a generic weak monoprotic acid HA in aqueous solution at constant temperature $T$.

**Step 1:** Write the balanced equilibrium reaction.

$$\text{HA} (aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O} (l) \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_3\text{O}^+ (aq) + \text{A}^- (aq)$$

**Step 2:** Identify the activities. Since water is a pure liquid, its activity is 1 (by convention). For dilute aqueous solutions, activities ≈ molar concentrations.

**Step 3:** By definition of the equilibrium constant:

$$K_a = \frac{a_{\text{H}_3\text{O}^+} \cdot a_{\text{A}^-}}{a_{\text{HA}}} = \frac{[\text{H}_3\text{O}^+][\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$

**Step 4:** Link to thermodynamics (Module 1). For any reaction at equilibrium:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$

At equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0$ and $Q = K$, hence $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_a$.

**Step 5:** Rewrite in terms of $\text{p}K_a$. Taking $-\log_{10}$ of both sides:

$$-\log_{10} K_a = -\log_{10}\left(e^{-\Delta G^\circ/RT}\right) = \frac{\Delta G^\circ}{2.303 \, RT}$$

$$\boxed{\text{p}K_a = \frac{\Delta G^\circ}{2.303 \, RT}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the equation that links the **macroscopic thermodynamic quantity** ($\Delta G^\circ$) to the **microscopic equilibrium constant** ($K_a$). At $T = 298.15$ K and $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹, the conversion factor is $2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15 = 5708$ J mol⁻¹ ≈ **5.71 kJ mol⁻¹ per pKa unit**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Ostwald's Dilution Law (Exact Form)

**Step 1:** Set up the ICE (Initial–Change–Equilibrium) table for HA of initial concentration $C$ mol/L dissociating to the extent $\alpha$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{HA} \quad &\rightleftharpoons \quad \text{H}^+ + \text{A}^- \\
\text{Initial:}\quad &C \quad\quad\quad 0 \quad\quad 0 \\
\text{Change:}\quad &-C\alpha \quad +C\alpha \quad +C\alpha \\
\text{Equilibrium:}\quad &C(1-\alpha) \quad\quad C\alpha \quad\quad C\alpha
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the $K_a$ expression.

$$K_a = \frac{(C\alpha)(C\alpha)}{C(1-\alpha)} = \frac{C\alpha^2}{1-\alpha}$$

**Step 3:** Cross-multiply to get the quadratic in $\alpha$:

$$K_a(1-\alpha) = C\alpha^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C\alpha^2 + K_a\alpha - K_a = 0$$

**Step 4:** Apply the quadratic formula $\alpha = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$:

$$\alpha = \frac{-K_a + \sqrt{K_a^2 + 4 C K_a}}{2C}$$

(taking the positive root since $\alpha > 0$).

**Step 5:** For **dilute solutions** where $\alpha \ll 1$ (i.e., $1 - \alpha \approx 1$):

$$K_a \approx C\alpha^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha \approx \sqrt{\frac{K_a}{C}}$$

**Step 6:** Therefore the hydrogen-ion concentration in dilute solution:

$$[\text{H}^+] = C\alpha \approx \sqrt{K_a \cdot C}$$

$$\boxed{[\text{H}^+] = \sqrt{K_a \cdot C} \quad \text{(valid for weak acid, } \alpha \ll 1\text{)}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Henderson–Hasselbalch Equation (Rigorous)

**Step 1:** Start with the $K_a$ expression for HA:

$$K_a = \frac{[\text{H}^+][\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to isolate $[\text{H}^+]$:

$$[\text{H}^+] = K_a \cdot \frac{[\text{HA}]}{[\text{A}^-]}$$

**Step 3:** Take $-\log_{10}$ of both sides:

$$-\log[\text{H}^+] = -\log K_a - \log\frac{[\text{HA}]}{[\text{A}^-]}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the left side as pH and the first term on the right as $\text{p}K_a$:

$$\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a - \log\frac{[\text{HA}]}{[\text{A}^-]}$$

**Step 5:** Use the logarithm identity $-\log(x/y) = \log(y/x)$:

$$\boxed{\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log_{10}\frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The equation is **dimensionally consistent** because the ratio $[\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}]$ is dimensionless. The **Lawrence Joseph Henderson** originally derived it in 1908, and **Karl Albert Hasselbalch** (a Danish physician) reformulated it in terms of pH in 1916 for blood plasma studies.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Relationship Between $K_a$ and $K_b$ of a Conjugate Pair

**Step 1:** Write the two equilibria.

$$\text{HA} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{A}^- \quad \text{with} \quad K_a = \frac{[\text{H}^+][\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$

$$\text{A}^- + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{HA} + \text{OH}^- \quad \text{with} \quad K_b = \frac{[\text{HA}][\text{OH}^-]}{[\text{A}^-]}$$

**Step 2:** Multiply the two expressions.

$$K_a \cdot K_b = \frac{[\text{H}^+][\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]} \cdot \frac{[\text{HA}][\text{OH}^-]}{[\text{A}^-]} = [\text{H}^+][\text{OH}^-]$$

**Step 3:** Recognize the right side as the **ionic product of water**, $K_w$.

$$\boxed{K_a \cdot K_b = K_w = 1.0 \times 10^{-14} \quad \text{(at 25 °C)}}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to $\text{p}K$ form: $\text{p}K_a + \text{p}K_b = \text{p}K_w = 14$.

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Numerical illustration:** Acetic acid has $K_a = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$. Therefore the acetate ion has $K_b = K_w / K_a = 10^{-14} / (1.8 \times 10^{-5}) = 5.56 \times 10^{-10}$. Hence $\text{p}K_a = 4.74$ and $\text{p}K_b = 9.26$. Notice that $\text{p}K_a + \text{p}K_b = 14.00$. ✓

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Titration Curve (Buffer Region & Half-Equivalence)

Consider titrating a weak acid HA (concentration $C_0$, volume $V_0$) with a strong base NaOH of concentration $C_b$.

**Step 1:** At any point before equivalence, the moles of A⁻ formed equal the moles of NaOH added, and the moles of HA remaining equal $C_0 V_0 - C_b V_b$.

$$[\text{A}^-] = \frac{C_b V_b}{V_0 + V_b} \quad ; \quad [\text{HA}] = \frac{C_0 V_0 - C_b V_b}{V_0 + V_b}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into Henderson–Hasselbalch.

$$\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log\frac{C_b V_b}{C_0 V_0 - C_b V_b}$$

**Step 3:** At the **half-equivalence point**, $C_b V_b = \frac{1}{2} C_0 V_0$:

$$\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log\frac{1/2 \, C_0 V_0}{1/2 \, C_0 V_0} = \text{p}K_a + \log(1) = \text{p}K_a$$

$$\boxed{\text{pH}_{\text{half-eq}} = \text{p}K_a}$$

This is the **experimental method** to determine $\text{p}K_a$ from a titration curve.

**Step 4:** At the **equivalence point**, all HA has been converted to A⁻. The pH is now governed by the **hydrolysis of A⁻**:

$$K_b = \frac{K_w}{K_a} = \frac{[\text{OH}^-]^2}{C_{\text{eq}}}$$

where $C_{\text{eq}} = \dfrac{C_0 V_0}{V_0 + V_{b,\text{eq}}}$ is the diluted conjugate base concentration. So:

$$[\text{OH}^-] = \sqrt{K_b \cdot C_{\text{eq}}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{pH} = 14 - \text{pOH}$$

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Hydrolysis of a Salt of a Weak Acid and Strong Base

A salt like **sodium acetate (CH₃COONa)** dissociates completely, but the acetate ion hydrolyzes:

$$\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^- + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} + \text{OH}^-$$

Using $K_b = K_w / K_a$ and ICE:

$$K_b = \frac{[\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}][\text{OH}^-]}{[\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^-]} = \frac{x^2}{C - x}$$

For $x \ll C$:

$$[\text{OH}^-] = \sqrt{K_b \cdot C} = \sqrt{\frac{K_w \cdot C}{K_a}}$$

$$\text{pOH} = \tfrac{1}{2}(\text{p}K_w - \text{p}K_a - \log C) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{pH} = 7 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{p}K_a + \tfrac{1}{2}\log C$$

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Calculate the pH of a 0.10 M acetic acid solution. Given $K_a = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ at 25 °C.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Write the equilibrium and $K_a$ expression.

$$\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_3\text{COO}^- + \text{H}^+ \quad ; \quad K_a = \frac{[\text{H}^+][\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^-]}{[\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}]}$$

**Step 2:** ICE table.

$$\begin{aligned}
[\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}]_i &= 0.10 \text{ M} \\
[\text{H}^+]_i &= 0 \\
[\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^-]_i &= 0 \\
\text{At equilibrium:}\quad [\text{H}^+] &= [\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^-] = x \\
[\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}] &= 0.10 - x
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute.

$$1.8 \times 10^{-5} = \frac{x^2}{0.10 - x}$$

**Step 4:** Approximation. If $x \ll 0.10$, then $0.10 - x \approx 0.10$:

$$1.8 \times 10^{-5} \approx \frac{x^2}{0.10} \quad \Rightarrow \quad x^2 = 1.8 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 5:** Solve.

$$x = \sqrt{1.8 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.342 \times 10^{-3} \text{ M}$$

**Step 6:** Check approximation: $x / 0.10 = 1.34\% \ll 5\%$ ✓ (Approximation valid.)

**Step 7:** Calculate pH.

$$\text{pH} = -\log(1.342 \times 10^{-3}) = 2.87$$

$$\boxed{\text{pH} = 2.87}$$

**Step 8 (Bonus — Exact check using full quadratic):**
$x^2 + (1.8 \times 10^{-5})x - (1.8 \times 10^{-6}) = 0$. Solving: $x = 1.341 \times 10^{-3}$ M → pH = 2.873. Same answer to 3 sig figs. ✓

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Brønsted–Lowry Conjugate Pair Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    acid1[ACID HA] --donates proton to--> base1[BASE A minus]
    base1 --reverse reaction is protonation--> acid1
    acid1 --conjugate base is--> base1
    base1 --conjugate acid is--> acid1
    acid1 --equilibrium governed by--> Ka1[Ka constant]
    base1 --equilibrium governed by--> Kb1[Kb constant]
    Ka1 --product with--> Kb1
    Kb1 --equals--> Kw1[Kw equals 1e-14 at 25C]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Buffer Action Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    buf1[Buffer System] --> buf2[Weak Acid HA Pool]
    buf1 --> buf3[Conjugate Base A minus Pool]
    buf2 -. when acid added consumes H plus .-> buf3
    buf3 -. when base added consumes OH minus .-> buf2
    Hplus1[Added Strong Acid H plus] --> buf3
    OHminus1[Added Strong Base OH minus] --> buf2
    buf1 --> phconst[pH remains stable]
    phconst --> phval[pH equals pKa plus log of base to acid ratio]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Titration Curve Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    regionA[Region 1 Initial pH] --> regionB[Region 2 Buffer Plateau]
    regionB --> regionC[Region 3 Half Equivalence pH equals pKa]
    regionC --> regionD[Region 4 Equivalence Point pH greater than 7 for weak acid]
    regionD --> regionE[Region 5 Excess Base Plateau]
    regionA --low pH strong acid region--> regionB
    regionB --gradual rise--> regionC
    regionC --inflection midpoint--> regionD
    regionD --sharp rise--> regionE
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Hierarchy of Acid–Base Constants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    master[Master Constant Kw at Temperature T] --> strong[Strong Acids Ka tends to infinity]
    master --> weak[Weak Acids Ka less than 1]
    strong --> sa1[HCl, HNO3, HClO4 fully dissociated]
    weak --> wa1[Monoprotic CH3COOH HF HCN]
    weak --> poly[Polyprotic H3PO4 H2CO3]
    poly --> pKa1[Ka1 largest]
    poly --> pKa2[Ka2 smaller]
    poly --> pKa3[Ka3 smallest]
    poly --> pIcalc[For amino acids pI equals average of relevant pKa values]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure interpretation:** These block diagrams abstract the molecular processes into functional flows. In a KTU examination, drawing a labeled buffer-action or titration-curve block diagram can earn full marks (2–3 marks) for the structured question on buffer systems.
