# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the temperature** when quoting $K_w = 10^{-14}$. At 37 °C, pKw = 13.62 — many students lose 1 mark here.
> 2. **Approximation validity:** The $1 - \alpha \approx 1$ assumption in Ostwald's law is valid only when $\alpha < 0.05$ (5%). If your $\alpha$ comes out > 5%, you must solve the quadratic — failure to do so loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Henderson–Hasselbalch misuse:** Don't apply it outside the range $0.1 \leq [\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}] \leq 10$. At the extreme ends of titration, use the full equilibrium expression.
> 4. **Confusing $K_a$ with $K_b$:** Always verify whether you are given the acid or the base constant before plugging into $K_a \cdot K_b = K_w$.
> 5. **Unit errors:** $K_a$, $K_b$, $K_w$ are dimensionless (true equilibrium constants use activities) but numerically equal to concentration-based expressions for dilute solutions. pH is dimensionless — do not write "M" or "mol/L" with pH.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define the terms **acid dissociation constant (Ka)** and **pKa**. How is pKa related to the standard Gibbs free energy change of the dissociation reaction?

**Model Answer:**

The **acid dissociation constant ($K_a$)** is the equilibrium constant for the dissociation of a weak acid HA in water:

$$K_a = \frac{[\text{H}_3\text{O}^+][\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$

**pKa** is defined as the negative base-10 logarithm of $K_a$:

$$\text{p}K_a = -\log_{10} K_a$$

The thermodynamic relationship is:

$$\Delta G^\circ = 2.303 \, RT \, \text{p}K_a$$

At 25 °C (298.15 K), $2.303 \, RT = 5.708$ kJ mol⁻¹, so:

$$\Delta G^\circ = (5.708 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}) \times \text{p}K_a$$

A larger pKa corresponds to a **less favorable** dissociation (smaller $K_a$, positive $\Delta G^\circ$), meaning a weaker acid.

> **[3 marks: 1 mark definition of Ka, 1 mark definition of pKa, 1 mark relationship with ΔG°]**

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** State the **Henderson–Hasselbalch equation** and explain its significance in the preparation of a buffer of desired pH.

**Model Answer:**

The Henderson–Hasselbalch equation relates the pH of a buffer to the ratio of conjugate base to acid:

$$\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log_{10}\frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$

**Significance:**

1. **Buffer design:** To prepare a buffer at a specific pH, select an acid whose $\text{p}K_a$ is within ±1 unit of the desired pH, then adjust the $[\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}]$ ratio accordingly.
2. **pKa determination:** In a titration, the half-equivalence point yields $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a$.
3. **Biological application:** Used to predict the charge state of amino acid side chains, drugs, and proteins at physiological pH 7.4.

**Example:** To prepare a buffer at pH 4.74 (pKa of acetic acid), use equal molar concentrations of CH₃COOH and CH₃COONa.

> **[3 marks: 1 mark statement, 1 mark significance, 1 mark example]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each, with two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. You must answer **either** the OR choice given below.

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q (a) [7 marks]:** Derive the **Henderson–Hasselbalch equation** starting from the equilibrium expression for a weak acid HA. State clearly all assumptions used in the derivation.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1:** Write the dissociation equilibrium and its $K_a$ expression.

$$\text{HA} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{A}^- \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_a = \frac{[\text{H}^+][\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to isolate $[\text{H}^+]$:

$$[\text{H}^+] = K_a \cdot \frac{[\text{HA}]}{[\text{A}^-]}$$

**Step 3:** Take $-\log_{10}$ of both sides:

$$-\log[\text{H}^+] = -\log K_a - \log\frac{[\text{HA}]}{[\text{A}^-]}$$

**Step 4:** Identify terms. By definition, $-\log[\text{H}^+] = \text{pH}$ and $-\log K_a = \text{p}K_a$:

$$\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a - \log\frac{[\text{HA}]}{[\text{A}^-]}$$

**Step 5:** Apply logarithm identity $-\log(x/y) = \log(y/x)$:

$$\boxed{\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log_{10}\frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. The system is at **thermodynamic equilibrium** (so $K_a$ is valid).
2. **Activities ≈ concentrations** (valid for dilute solutions, ionic strength < 0.1 M).
3. The **ratio $[\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}]$** is not perturbed significantly by added small amounts of strong acid/base (this is the buffer condition).
4. **Temperature is constant** at 25 °C (so that $K_w = 10^{-14}$).
5. The **autoionization of water** contribution to $[\text{H}^+]$ is negligible compared to the acid's contribution (valid when $[\text{HA}] \gg 10^{-7}$ M).

> **[7 marks: 3 marks derivation steps, 2 marks final equation, 2 marks assumptions]**

---

**Q (b) [7 marks]:** A buffer solution is prepared by mixing 200 mL of 0.20 M CH₃COOH with 300 mL of 0.15 M CH₃COONa. Calculate (i) the pH of the buffer, and (ii) the pH after adding 50 mL of 0.10 M HCl to 100 mL of this buffer. Given $K_a = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ for acetic acid, $\text{p}K_a = 4.74$.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) pH of the original buffer:**

**Step 1:** Calculate final concentrations after mixing (total volume = 200 + 300 = 500 mL).

$$[\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}] = \frac{0.20 \times 200}{500} = 0.080 \text{ M}$$

$$[\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^-] = \frac{0.15 \times 300}{500} = 0.090 \text{ M}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Henderson–Hasselbalch.

$$\text{pH} = 4.74 + \log\frac{0.090}{0.080} = 4.74 + \log(1.125)$$

$$\text{pH} = 4.74 + 0.0512 = 4.79$$

**(ii) pH after adding HCl:**

**Step 1:** Work with 100 mL of buffer. Initial moles in 100 mL:

$$n_{\text{HA}} = 0.080 \times 0.100 = 0.0080 \text{ mol}$$

$$n_{\text{A}^-} = 0.090 \times 0.100 = 0.0090 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2:** Moles of HCl added:

$$n_{\text{HCl}} = 0.10 \times 0.050 = 0.0050 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 3:** Reaction: $\text{A}^- + \text{H}^+ \to \text{HA}$. The added H⁺ converts A⁻ to HA.

$$n_{\text{HA,new}} = 0.0080 + 0.0050 = 0.0130 \text{ mol}$$

$$n_{\text{A}^-, \text{new}} = 0.0090 - 0.0050 = 0.0040 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 4:** Total volume = 100 + 50 = 150 mL = 0.150 L. (Ratios cancel in log, so volumes not needed for the ratio.)

$$\text{pH} = 4.74 + \log\frac{0.0040}{0.0130} = 4.74 + \log(0.3077)$$

$$\text{pH} = 4.74 + (-0.512) = 4.23$$

$$\boxed{\text{pH}_{\text{original}} = 4.79, \quad \text{pH}_{\text{after HCl}} = 4.23}$$

> **[7 marks: 2 marks part (i) setup, 2 marks part (i) answer, 1 mark part (ii) stoichiometry, 2 marks part (ii) final pH]**

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q (a) [7 marks]:** State and derive **Ostwald's dilution law** for a weak monoprotic acid. How does the degree of dissociation change with (i) decreasing concentration and (ii) increasing temperature?

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** Ostwald's dilution law relates the dissociation constant ($K_a$) of a weak acid to its degree of dissociation ($\alpha$) and concentration ($C$):

$$K_a = \frac{C\alpha^2}{1-\alpha}$$

For dilute solutions ($\alpha \ll 1$):

$$\alpha = \sqrt{\frac{K_a}{C}}$$

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Consider a weak acid HA of initial concentration $C$ dissociating to extent $\alpha$. ICE table:

$$\begin{aligned}
[\text{HA}]_e &= C(1-\alpha) \\
[\text{H}^+]_e &= C\alpha \\
[\text{A}^-]_e &= C\alpha
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into $K_a$ expression.

$$K_a = \frac{(C\alpha)(C\alpha)}{C(1-\alpha)} = \frac{C\alpha^2}{1-\alpha}$$

**Step 3:** For $\alpha \ll 1$, $1 - \alpha \approx 1$, so:

$$\alpha \approx \sqrt{\frac{K_a}{C}}$$

**(i) Effect of decreasing concentration:**
From $\alpha = \sqrt{K_a/C}$, as $C$ decreases, $\alpha$ increases. **In the limit $C \to 0$, $\alpha \to 1$** (full dissociation). This is why highly diluted weak acids behave like strong acids.

**(ii) Effect of increasing temperature:**
From the van 't Hoff equation (Module 1):

$$\frac{d \ln K_a}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2}$$

Dissociation of weak acids is **endothermic** ($\Delta H^\circ > 0$), so increasing $T$ increases $K_a$, which in turn increases $\alpha$. Therefore, **$\alpha$ increases with temperature**.

> **[7 marks: 1 mark statement, 3 marks derivation, 1 mark concentration effect, 2 marks temperature effect with van 't Hoff]**

---

**Q (b) [7 marks]:** The pH of a 0.050 M solution of a weak monoprotic acid HA is 3.00. Calculate (i) the acid dissociation constant $K_a$, (ii) the degree of dissociation $\alpha$, and (iii) the $\text{p}K_b$ of its conjugate base $\text{A}^-$. Given $K_w = 1.0 \times 10^{-14}$ at 25 °C.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Calculate $K_a$:**

**Step 1:** From pH 3.00:

$$[\text{H}^+] = 10^{-3.00} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ M}$$

**Step 2:** For a weak acid, $[\text{H}^+] = C\alpha$:

$$\alpha = \frac{[\text{H}^+]}{C} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{0.050} = 0.020$$

**Step 3:** Apply $K_a = C\alpha^2 / (1-\alpha)$:

$$K_a = \frac{0.050 \times (0.020)^2}{1 - 0.020} = \frac{0.050 \times 4.0 \times 10^{-4}}{0.980}$$

$$K_a = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-5}}{0.980} = 2.04 \times 10^{-5}$$

**(ii) Degree of dissociation:**

$$\boxed{\alpha = 0.020 \text{ or } 2.0\%}$$

**(iii) Calculate $\text{p}K_b$:**

**Step 1:** Use the conjugate pair relation $K_a \cdot K_b = K_w$:

$$K_b = \frac{K_w}{K_a} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-14}}{2.04 \times 10^{-5}} = 4.90 \times 10^{-10}$$

**Step 2:** Convert to $\text{p}K_b$:

$$\text{p}K_b = -\log_{10}(4.90 \times 10^{-10}) = 9.31$$

$$\boxed{K_a = 2.04 \times 10^{-5}, \quad \alpha = 0.020, \quad \text{p}K_b = 9.31}$$

**Verification:** $\text{p}K_a + \text{p}K_b = -\log(2.04 \times 10^{-5}) + 9.31 = 4.69 + 9.31 = 14.00$ ✓

> **[7 marks: 2 marks (i), 1 mark (ii), 2 marks (iii) setup, 1 mark (iii) calculation, 1 mark verification]**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

- ✅ **Brønsted–Lowry** defines acids as proton donors and bases as proton acceptors. The acid and the species formed after losing H⁺ are a **conjugate pair**.
- ✅ **$K_a$** = equilibrium constant for acid dissociation; **pKa = $- \log K_a$**. Larger pKa → weaker acid.
- ✅ **$K_a \cdot K_b = K_w = 10^{-14}$** at 25 °C; hence **pKa + pKb = 14**.
- ✅ **pH = $- \log[\text{H}_3\text{O}^+]$**; pH + pOH = 14 (at 25 °C only).
- ✅ For a weak monoprotic acid, $[\text{H}^+] = \sqrt{K_a \cdot C}$ **only when** $\alpha < 5\%$.
- ✅ **Henderson–Hasselbalch:** $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log([\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}])$; valid for $0.1 \leq \text{ratio} \leq 10$.
- ✅ At the **half-equivalence point** of a titration, **pH = pKa** — this is how $\text{p}K_a$ is measured experimentally.
- ✅ **Buffer capacity $\beta$** is maximum at $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a$ and falls to zero at the extremes.
- ✅ **Thermodynamic link:** $\Delta G^\circ = 2.303 \, RT \, \text{p}K_a$ (at 25 °C, coefficient ≈ 5.71 kJ mol⁻¹).
- ✅ **Biological buffers:** Phosphate (pKa 7.20, blood pH 7.40), Carbonate (pKa 6.35), Tris (pKa 8.06), HEPES (pKa 7.55).
- ✅ For **polyprotic acids**, $K_{a1} > K_{a2} > K_{a3}$, typically by 4–5 orders of magnitude.
- ✅ For **amino acids**, isoelectric point $\text{pI} = (\text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{a2})/2$ for neutral amino acids.
- ✅ **Common KTU mistake to avoid:** Always state the temperature; always check the 5% approximation; always verify Henderson–Hasselbalch ratio limits.

> [!TIP]
> **Final-Exam Quick-Revision Trick:** Memorize the 10 formulas in the Section 2.7 cheat sheet. In a 14-mark question, simply stating the correct formula and identifying which one applies to the problem can secure 2–3 marks even before calculation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Henry's Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT414 - BIOCHEMICAL THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Henry's Law** ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 📘 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Henry's Law** is a thermodynamic equilibrium principle that quantifies the solubility of a gas in a liquid. Formulated by the English chemist **William Henry in 1803**, the law states: > *"At a constant temperature, the partial pressure of a gas above a liquid solution is directly proportional to the mole fraction of that gas dissolved in the solution, provided the solution is sufficiently dilute and no chemical reaction occurs between the gas and the solvent."* Mathematically, the most rigorous representation is: $$P_A = k_H^A \cdot x_A$$ Where: - $P_A$ = Partial pressure of gas $A$ in the gas phase (in **atm** or **Pa**) - $x_A$ = Mole fraction of gas $A$ dissolved in the liquid phase - $k_H^A$ = **Henry's Law Constant** (units of pressure) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Henry's Law is a limiting law valid only for **ideal-dilute solutions**. It is the thermodynamic foundation for gas-liquid equilibria in biochemical reactors, oxygen transport in blood, and fermentation broths where dissolved oxygen is critical. --- ### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy — The Soda Bottle Experiment Imagine a sealed bottle of carbonated soft drink. Inside the bottle, **CO₂ gas is dissolved in water under high pressure**. The dissolved CO₂ molecules are "trapped" because the high pressure above the liquid forces them to stay in solution. - **The moment you open the bottle** → pressure drops → bubbles of CO₂ violently escape → because Henry's Law dictates that *less pressure = less dissolved gas*. - **The moment you shake the bottle** → equilibrium is disturbed → excess dissolved CO₂ rapidly fizzes out. - **If you keep the bottle unopened for days at constant temperature** → equilibrium is maintained → a fixed (predictable) amount of CO₂ remains dissolved. > This everyday phenomenon is the operational basis of **Henry's Law** in action. > [!NOTE] > **Biochemical Relevance:** Your lungs operate on the same principle. In the alveoli, the partial pressure of **O₂ ≈ 104 mmHg** and **CO₂ ≈ 40 mmHg**. Blood plasma uses Henry's Law to dissolve and transport these respiratory gases. --- ### 🎯 Key Standard Metrics | Metric | Value | Context | |---|---|---| | **Henry's Constant for O₂ in water (25°C)** | $\approx 4.34 \times 10^4$ **atm** | Standard biochemical reference | | **Henry's Constant for CO₂ in water (25°C)** | $\approx 1.67 \times 10^2$ **atm** | Standard biochemical reference | | **Molarity of pure water at 25°C** | **55.5 mol/L** | Critical for unit conversion | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 🔬 Thermodynamic Logic Steps (Why and How) Henry's Law emerges from a single fundamental principle: **equality of chemical potential at phase equilibrium**. Let's break it down: **Step 1 — Equilibrium Condition** At the gas-liquid interface, the chemical potential of gas $A$ in the gas phase must equal its chemical potential in the dissolved (liquid) state: $$\mu_A^{gas}(T, P_A) = \mu_A^{liq}(T, x_A)$$ **Step 2 — Express Gas Phase Chemical Potential (Ideal Gas Assumption)** Using the standard thermodynamic expression for an ideal gas: $$\mu_A^{gas} = \mu_A^{\circ, gas} + RT \ln\!\left(\frac{P_A}{P^{\circ}}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Express Liquid Phase Chemical Potential (Ideal-Dilute Solution)** For a solute in a dilute solution, Raoult's Law analogue applies: $$\mu_A^{liq} = \mu_A^{*, liq} + RT \ln(x_A)$$ **Step 4 — Combine the Two Expressions** Setting the two equal and rearranging gives the linear relationship between $P_A$ and $x_A$: $$P_A = k_H^A \cdot x_A$$ **Step 5 — Interpret the Constant** The Henry's Law constant contains all the thermodynamic "fingerprint" of the gas-solvent pair: $$k_H^A = P^{\circ} \cdot \exp\!\left[\frac{\mu_A^{*, liq} - \mu_A^{\circ, gas}}{RT}\right]$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why it matters:** A *small* $k_H^A$ means the gas is *highly soluble* (e.g., NH₃ in water). A *large* $k_H^A$ means *low solubility* (e.g., O₂, N₂ in water). This single constant controls bioreactor oxygenation, gas stripping, and aeration efficiency. --- ### 🧮 The Complete Set of Forms of Henry's Law Because biochemical engineers work with **concentrations (mol/L)** more often than mole fractions, Henry's Law can be rewritten in three equivalent forms: | Form | Equation | Constant Symbol | Units of Constant | |---|---|---|---| | **Pressure Form** | $P_A = k_H^A \cdot x_A$ | $k_H^A$ | **atm** (or Pa) | | **Concentration–Pressure Form** | $C_A = k_H^{cp} \cdot P_A$ | $k_H^{cp}$ | **mol·L⁻¹·atm⁻¹** | | **Concentration–Concentration Form** | $C_A = k_H^{cc} \cdot C_A^{w}$ | $k_H^{cc}$ | **dimensionless** | Where $C_A^w$ is the equilibrium concentration of the same gas in **pure water** at the same temperature and partial pressure. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conversion Logic:** In dilute aqueous solutions, the mole fraction can be approximated as $x_A \approx C_A / 55.5$ mol/L (using water's molarity). This links the three constants together (see full derivation in Section 3). --- ### 📊 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Revision Ready) | # | Equation | Symbolic Form | Primary Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Henry's Law (pressure form) | $P_A = k_H^A \cdot x_A$ | Mole-fraction based calculations | | 2 | Solubility expression | $x_A = P_A / k_H^A$ | Solving for dissolved fraction | | 3 | Concentration form | $C_A = k_H^{cp} \cdot P_A$ | Bioreactor oxygen calculations | | 4 | Henry's Constant for water | $k_H^{cp} = \dfrac{55.5 \text{ mol/L}}{k_H^A}$ | Unit conversion | | 5 | Van't Hoff (Temp. dependence) | $\dfrac{d \ln k_H}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta_{sol} \bar{H}}{RT^2}$ | Temperature correction | | 6 | Integrated Van't Hoff | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_H(T_2)}{k_H(T_1)}\right) = \dfrac{\Delta_{sol} \bar{H}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | Temperature shift problems | | 7 | Combined Henry's + Dalton | $P_A = y_A \cdot P_{total}$ | Gas mixture problems | | 8 | Modified Raoult's blend | $P_A = y_A P_{total} = x_A \gamma_A P_A^{sat}$ | General VLE reference | > [!NOTE] > **For KTU valuation:** The examiner will *always* give full credit if you state the **temperature, dilute solution, and no chemical reaction** assumptions alongside the law. --- ### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Application | |---|---| | **Bioreactor Design** | Predicting dissolved O₂ concentration as a function of air sparging pressure | | **Blood–Gas Analysis** | Arterial $P_{O_2}$, $P_{CO_2}$ measurements in clinical diagnostics | | **Carbonated Beverages** | Quantifying fizz-loss kinetics during storage | | **Anaesthesia** | Determining partial pressures of inhaled anaesthetic gases | | **Environmental Engineering** | Predicting volatile pollutant dissolution in lakes/rivers | | **Decompression Sickness** | Modelling N₂ release from blood during diver ascent | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 🔁 Derivation 1 — Henry's Law from First Principles of Chemical Potential **Objective:** Starting from chemical potential equality, derive the linear $P_A$–$x_A$ relationship. **Step 1: Establish the equilibrium condition.** At the gas-liquid interface, thermodynamic equilibrium demands: $$\mu_A^{gas}(T, P_A) = \mu_A^{liq}(T, x_A)$$ **Step 2: Express the gas-phase chemical potential.** Assuming ideal gas behavior: $$\mu_A^{gas}(T, P_A) = \mu_A^{\circ, gas}(T) + RT \ln\!\left(\frac{P_A}{P^{\circ}}\right)$$ **Step 3: Express the liquid-phase chemical potential.** For a solute in an ideal-dilute solution (Henry's Law regime): $$\mu_A^{liq}(T, x_A) = \mu_A^{*, liq}(T) + RT \ln(x_A)$$ **Step 4: Equate the two expressions.** Substituting Step 2 and Step 3 into Step 1: $$\mu_A^{\circ, gas}(T) + RT \ln\!\left(\frac{P_A}{P^{\circ}}\right) = \mu_A^{*, liq}(T) + RT \ln(x_A)$$ **Step 5: Isolate the logarithmic terms on one side.** $$RT \ln\!\left(\frac{P_A}{P^{\circ}}\right) - RT \ln(x_A) = \mu_A^{*, liq}(T) - \mu_A^{\circ, gas}(T)$$ **Step 6: Factor out $RT$ and combine the logarithms.** $$RT \ln\!\left(\frac{P_A}{P^{\circ} \cdot x_A}\right) = \mu_A^{*, liq}(T) - \mu_A^{\circ, gas}(T)$$ **Step 7: Divide both sides by $RT$ to isolate the natural log.** $$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_A}{P^{\circ} \cdot x_A}\right) = \frac{\mu_A^{*, liq}(T) - \mu_A^{\circ, gas}(T)}{RT}$$ **Step 8: Exponentiate both sides to remove the logarithm.** $$\frac{P_A}{P^{\circ} \cdot x_A} = \exp\!\left[\frac{\mu_A^{*, liq}(T) - \mu_A^{\circ, gas}(T)}{RT}\right]$$ **Step 9: Rearrange to obtain the standard Henry's Law form.** $$P_A = \left\{P^{\circ} \cdot \exp\!\left[\frac{\mu_A^{*, liq}(T) - \mu_A^{\circ, gas}(T)}{RT}\right]\right\} \cdot x_A$$ **Step 10: Recognize the bracketed term as Henry's Constant.** $$\boxed{P_A = k_H^A \cdot x_A, \quad \text{where} \quad k_H^A = P^{\circ} \cdot \exp\!\left[\frac{\mu_A^{*, liq}(T) - \mu_A^{\circ, gas}(T)}{RT}\right]}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical insight:** $k_H^A$ is **temperature-dependent** because the exponential contains $T$ explicitly. This is why the gas solubility in your cold drink is *higher* than in your warm drink. --- ### 🔁 Derivation 2 — Interconversion of Henry's Law Constants **Objective:** Derive the exact relationship between $k_H^A$ (pressure form) and $k_H^{cp}$ (concentration form) for dilute aqueous solutions. **Step 1: Start with the mole-fraction form of Henry's Law.** $$P_A = k_H^A \cdot x_A$$ **Step 2: Express the mole fraction in terms of molar concentrations.** By definition: $$x_A = \frac{n_A}{n_A + n_{water}}$$ **Step 3: Apply the dilute-solution approximation.** Since the dissolved gas concentration is far smaller than pure water's molarity: $$n_A \ll n_{water} \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_A \approx \frac{n_A}{n_{water}}$$ **Step 4: Convert moles to concentration using solution volume.** Let $V$ be the solution volume: $$n_A = C_A \cdot V, \quad n_{water} = \frac{\rho_{water} \cdot V}{M_{water}}$$ **Step 5: Compute the pure water molarity.** For water at 25°C with density $\rho_{water} = 1$ g/mL and molar mass $M_{water} = 18$ g/mol: $$C_{water} = \frac{\rho_{water}}{M_{water}} = \frac{1000 \text{ g/L}}{18 \text{ g/mol}} \approx 55.5 \text{ mol/L}$$ **Step 6: Substitute back into the mole-fraction approximation.** $$x_A \approx \frac{C_A \cdot V}{55.5 \cdot V} = \frac{C_A}{55.5}$$ **Step 7: Substitute this into Henry's Law from Step 1.** $$P_A = k_H^A \cdot \left(\frac{C_A}{55.5}\right)$$ **Step 8: Solve for $C_A$ in terms of $P_A$.** $$C_A = \left(\frac{55.5}{k_H^A}\right) \cdot P_A$$ **Step 9: Define the concentration-pressure Henry's Constant.** $$\boxed{k_H^{cp} = \frac{55.5 \text{ mol/L}}{k_H^A} \quad \text{and} \quad C_A = k_H^{cp} \cdot P_A}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Numerical check:** For $O_2$ in water at 25°C, $k_H^A = 4.34 \times 10^4$ atm. Therefore: > $k_H^{cp} = 55.5 / (4.34 \times 10^4) \approx 1.28 \times 10^{-3}$ mol·L⁻¹·atm⁻¹ ✓ (matches standard literature values) --- ### 🔁 Derivation 3 — Temperature Dependence via Van't Hoff Equation **Objective:** Quantify how Henry's constant changes with temperature. **Step 1: Recall the definition of $k_H^A$ as a function of $T$.** $$k_H^A = P^{\circ} \cdot \exp\!\left[\frac{\mu_A^{*, liq}(T) - \mu_A^{\circ, gas}(T)}{RT}\right]$$ **Step 2: Take the natural logarithm of both sides.** $$\ln k_H^A = \ln P^{\circ} + \frac{\mu_A^{*, liq}(T) - \mu_A^{\circ, gas}(T)}{RT}$$ **Step 3: Differentiate with respect to temperature $T$.** Note that $\ln P^{\circ}$ is constant and using the Gibbs–Helmholtz relation $\frac{d(\mu/RT)}{dT} = -\frac{\bar{H}}{RT^2}$: $$\frac{d \ln k_H^A}{dT} = -\frac{\bar{H}_A^{*, liq} - \bar{H}_A^{\circ, gas}}{RT^2}$$ **Step 4: Recognize the numerator as the enthalpy of solution (gas dissolving into liquid).** $$\Delta_{sol} \bar{H} = \bar{H}_A^{*, liq} - \bar{H}_A^{\circ, gas}$$ **Step 5: Write the final Van't Hoff expression for Henry's constant.** $$\boxed{\frac{d \ln k_H^A}{dT} = \frac{\Delta_{sol} \bar{H}}{RT^2}}$$ **Step 6: Integrate between two temperatures** (assuming $\Delta_{sol} \bar{H}$ is roughly constant over the range): $$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_H^A(T_2)}{k_H^A(T_1)}\right) = \frac{\Delta_{sol} \bar{H}}{R} \left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering takeaway:** Since gas dissolution is **typically exothermic** ($\Delta_{sol} \bar{H} < 0$), increasing $T$ *increases* $k_H^A$ → meaning gas becomes *less soluble*. This is the thermodynamic reason why warm bioreactors need *more* vigorous aeration than cold ones. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 🎨 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Henry's Law in a Bioreactor Context ```mermaid graph TD node1[Compressed Air Supply
P total atmospheric] --> node2[Gas Filter and Sterilization] node2 --> node3[Sparger Ring
Located at Reactor Bottom] node3 --> node4[Gas Liquid Interface
Rising Bubbles] node4 --> node5[Broth Liquid Phase
Contains Dissolved O2] node4 --> node6[Gas Phase Headspace
Partial Pressure P_O2] node5 --> node7[Microbial Cells
O2 Consumption Sink] node6 --> node8[Henry Equilibrium Condition
P O2 equals kH times xO2] node5 --> node8 node8 --> node9[Sensor DO Probe
Reads Dissolved O2 C] node9 --> node10[PID Controller
Adjusts Air Flow Rate] node10 --> node1 style node1 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd style node8 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px style node9 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 style node10 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#d84315 ``` ### 📖 Reading the Diagram | Node | Represents | Biochemical Interpretation | |---|---|---| | **node1** | Compressed air source | Bulk gas supply with known composition | | **node3** | Sparger | Mechanical device that disperses gas as fine bubbles | | **node4** | Gas-liquid interface | The microscopic zone where Henry's Law is locally satisfied | | **node6** | Headspace gas | Defines $P_{O_2}$ — the driving force for dissolution | | **node8** | Henry Equilibrium | The thermodynamic "heart" of the system: $P_A = k_H \cdot x_A$ | | **node9** | DO sensor | Real-time measurement of $C_{O_2}$ in the broth | | **node10** | PID controller | Industrial feedback loop that maintains optimal $C_{O_2}$ | > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain how Henry's Law applies to bioreactor oxygenation," **draw a similar block diagram** with the equilibrium box highlighted. Examiners award full design marks for such visual reasoning. ### 🔄 Process Flow Logic 1. **Gas enters** at high pressure (node1 → node3). 2. **Bubbles rise** through the broth, creating fresh gas-liquid interfaces (node4). 3. **At each interface**, Henry's Law instantaneously governs the local equilibrium (node8). 4. **Cells consume O₂** continuously, disturbing the equilibrium (node7). 5. **Sensors detect** the dissolved O₂ level (node9) and adjust the air supply (node10). 6. **The system settles** into a **steady state** where mass transfer rate = consumption rate. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- #### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` > **State Henry's Law. What are the three essential conditions for its validity?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Statement (1 Mark):** *Henry's Law states that at constant temperature, the partial pressure of a gas in equilibrium with a dilute solution is directly proportional to its mole fraction in the solution: $P_A = k_H^A \cdot x_A$.* > > **Conditions (2 Marks):** > 1. **Constant temperature** — $k_H$ depends strongly on $T$. > 2. **Dilute solution** — the dissolved gas mole fraction must be very small ($x_A \ll 1$). > 3. **No chemical reaction** — the gas must not react with the solvent (e.g., $CO_2$ + $H_2O \to H_2CO_3$ violates Henry's Law at high concentrations). --- #### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` > **Explain the significance of Henry's Law constant in the context of dissolved oxygen in fermentation broths.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Definition (1 Mark):** The Henry's Law constant $k_H^{O_2} \approx 4.34 \times 10^4$ atm at 25°C represents the ratio of partial pressure to mole fraction for dissolved oxygen. > > **Significance (2 Marks):** *(i) It quantitatively predicts the dissolved oxygen concentration $C_{O_2} = (P_{O_2}/k_H) \times 55.5$ for any air-sparging condition. (ii) Its temperature dependence (governed by Van't Hoff equation) dictates that **warm fermenters require higher sparging rates** to maintain aerobic conditions for microbial growth.* --- ### 📚 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` > **(a)** Derive Henry's Law starting from the equality of chemical potentials at phase equilibrium. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** The partial pressure of $O_2$ in a fermenter's headspace is maintained at **0.21 atm** (typical of air) at **T = 310 K** (37°C). The Henry's Law constant for $O_2$ in water at 310 K is $k_H^{O_2} = 5.84 \times 10^4$ atm. Calculate the **dissolved oxygen concentration (DO)** in mol/L. Given: water molarity at 310 K is approximately **55.0 mol/L**. **(7 Marks)** --- ##### **Solution to A(a): Derivation (7 Marks)** > **Assumptions (1 Mark):** > - Gas phase behaves ideally. > - Solution is dilute (Henry's Law regime). > - Temperature is constant. > - No chemical reaction between gas and solvent. > > **Derivation Steps (6 Marks):** > > **Step 1 — Equilibrium criterion:** At gas-liquid interface: > > $$\mu_A^{gas}(T, P_A) = \mu_A^{liq}(T, x_A)$$ > > **Step 2 — Gas-phase expression:** > > $$\mu_A^{gas}(T, P_A) = \mu_A^{\circ, gas}(T) + RT \ln\!\left(\frac{P_A}{P^{\circ}}\right)$$ > > **Step 3 — Liquid-phase expression (ideal-dilute):** > > $$\mu_A^{liq}(T, x_A) = \mu_A^{*, liq}(T) + RT \ln(x_A)$$ > > **Step 4 — Equate and rearrange:** > > $$RT \ln\!\left(\frac{P_A}{P^{\circ}}\right) - RT \ln(x_A) = \mu_A^{*, liq}(T) - \mu_A^{\circ, gas}(T)$$ > > **Step 5 — Combine logs:** > > $$RT \ln\!\left(\frac{P_A}{P^{\circ} \cdot x_A}\right) = \Delta\mu^{\circ}(T)$$ > > **Step 6 — Exponentiate:** > > $$P_A = P^{\circ} \cdot e^{\Delta\mu^{\circ}/RT} \cdot x_A$$ > > **Step 7 — Identify Henry's constant:** > > $$\boxed{P_A = k_H^A \cdot x_A, \quad k_H^A = P^{\circ} \cdot e^{\Delta\mu^{\circ}/RT}}$$ --- ##### **Solution to A(b): Numerical Problem (7 Marks)** > **Given:** > - $P_{O_2} = 0.21$ atm > - $T = 310$ K > - $k_H^{O_2} = 5.84 \times 10^4$ atm > - $C_{water} \approx 55.0$ mol/L > > **Step 1 — Apply mole-fraction Henry's Law to find $x_{O_2}$ (2 Marks):** > > $$x_{O_2} = \frac{P_{O_2}}{k_H^{O_2}} = \frac{0.21}{5.84 \times 10^4} = 3.596 \times 10^{-6}$$ > > **Step 2 — Use dilute-solution approximation (2 Marks):** > > $$x_{O_2} \approx \frac{C_{O_2}}{C_{water}}$$ > > **Step 3 — Solve for dissolved oxygen concentration (2 Marks):** > > $$C_{O_2} = x_{O_2} \cdot C_{water} = (3.596 \times 10^{-6}) \times (55.0)$$ > > $$C_{O_2} = 1.978 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/L}$$ > > **Step 4 — Express in mg/L (1 Mark):** (Molar mass of $O_2$ = 32 g/mol) > > $$C_{O_2} = 1.978 \times 10^{-4} \times 32 = 6.33 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/L} = 6.33 \text{ mg/L}$$ > > **Final Answer:** $\boxed{C_{O_2} \approx 1.98 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/L} \approx 6.33 \text{ mg/L}}$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]` > **(a)** With neat derivations, show the interconversion between the three forms of Henry's Law constant: $k_H^A$, $k_H^{cp}$, and $k_H^{cc}$. Use the dilute-solution approximation in aqueous systems. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** The Henry's Law constant for $CO_2$ in water at **298 K** is **$1.67 \times 10^2$ atm**. The enthalpy of solution of $CO_2$ in water is $\Delta_{sol} \bar{H} = -19.5$ kJ/mol. Calculate the Henry's Law constant at **T = 308 K** using the Van't Hoff equation. Given: $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹. **(7 Marks)** --- ##### **Solution to B(a): Interconversion (7 Marks)** > **Step 1 — Start with the pressure form (1 Mark):** > > $$P_A = k_H^A \cdot x_A$$ > > **Step 2 — Apply the dilute-solution approximation for water (2 Marks):** > > $$x_A \approx \frac{C_A}{C_{water}} = \frac{C_A}{55.5}$$ > > **Step 3 — Derive $k_H^{cp}$ relationship (2 Marks):** > > $$P_A = k_H^A \cdot \frac{C_A}{55.5} \Rightarrow C_A = \left(\frac{55.5}{k_H^A}\right) P_A$$ > > $$\therefore k_H^{cp} = \frac{55.5 \text{ mol/L}}{k_H^A} \quad \text{(units: mol·L}^{-1}\text{·atm}^{-1}\text{)}$$ > > **Step 4 — Derive $k_H^{cc}$ relationship (2 Marks):** The dimensionless form uses the equilibrium concentration in pure water $C_A^w$ at the same $P_A$: > > $$C_A^w = k_H^{cp} \cdot P_A = \frac{55.5}{k_H^A} \cdot P_A$$ > > $$\therefore C_A = k_H^{cc} \cdot C_A^w \quad \text{where} \quad k_H^{cc} = \frac{C_A}{C_A^w}$$ > > For gases that do not react with water, $k_H^{cc} \approx 1$ (Henry's Law is symmetric for inert gases). --- ##### **Solution to B(b): Temperature Correction (7 Marks)** > **Given:** > - $k_H^A(T_1) = 1.67 \times 10^2$ atm at $T_1 = 298$ K > - $\Delta_{sol} \bar{H} = -19.5$ kJ/mol $= -19500$ J/mol > - $T_2 = 308$ K > - $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ > > **Step 1 — Write the integrated Van't Hoff equation (2 Marks):** > > $$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_H(T_2)}{k_H(T_1)}\right) = \frac{\Delta_{sol} \bar{H}}{R} \left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$ > > **Step 2 — Compute the temperature-difference factor (2 Marks):** > > $$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{298} - \frac{1}{308} = \frac{308 - 298}{298 \times 308} = \frac{10}{91784}$$ > > $$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = 1.0895 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$ > > **Step 3 — Compute the right-hand side (1 Mark):** > > $$\frac{\Delta_{sol} \bar{H}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) = \frac{-19500}{8.314} \times 1.0895 \times 10^{-4}$$ > > $$= -2345.4 \times 1.0895 \times 10^{-4} = -0.2556$$ > > **Step 4 — Solve for $k_H(T_2)$ (1 Mark):** > > $$k_H(T_2) = k_H(T_1) \cdot e^{-0.2556} = 1.67 \times 10^2 \times 0.7744$$ > > $$\boxed{k_H^{CO_2}(308 \text{ K}) \approx 129.3 \text{ atm}}$$ > > **Step 5 — Interpret the result (1 Mark):** As temperature increased from 298 K to 308 K, $k_H$ **decreased** from 167 atm to 129.3 atm. This is **counter-intuitive at first** — but for $CO_2$, the negative $\Delta_{sol} \bar{H}$ means the exothermic dissolution becomes less favorable at higher T, so the *effective solubility constant* in pressure units actually changes. *(Note: The decrease reflects the changing standard state reference; the practical observation is that $CO_2$ becomes less soluble in warmer water.)* --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Traps in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Forgetting the temperature assumption.** Writing Henry's Law without stating "at constant temperature" → lose **1 mark** in Part A. > 2. **Mixing up units.** Using $k_H$ in atm but plugging in concentration without converting via 55.5 mol/L → entire Part B(b) numerical problem goes wrong. **Always declare the form and units of $k_H$ before using it.** > 3. **Confusing Raoult's and Henry's Laws.** Raoult's = $P_A = x_A P_A^{sat}$ (for **solvent**). Henry's = $P_A = x_A k_H$ (for **solute/dissolved gas**). Examiners **specifically test this distinction** for 2 marks. > 4. **Ignoring dilute-solution limit.** Applying Henry's Law to highly concentrated solutions (e.g., $CO_2$ in carbonated water at > 5 mol/L) → significant error; this is a **favourite 1-mark trap question**. > 5. **Sign error in Van't Hoff.** The sign of $\Delta_{sol} \bar{H}$ directly determines whether solubility increases or decreases with T. Mixing up the sign → lose 2-3 marks in derivation. > 6. **Forgetting to convert to mg/L.** Examiners often want both mol/L and mg/L. Omitting the conversion is a **silent mark-loss trap** worth 1 mark. --- ### 🧾 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Henry's Law statement:** $P_A = k_H^A \cdot x_A$ at **constant T**, in a **dilute solution**, with **no chemical reaction**. - ✅ **Three equivalent forms:** pressure ($P_A = k_H^A x_A$), concentration-pressure ($C_A = k_H^{cp} P_A$), and concentration-concentration ($C_A = k_H^{cc} C_A^w$). - ✅ **Key conversion for water:** $k_H^{cp} = 55.5 \text{ mol/L} / k_H^A$. - ✅ **Standard values to memorize:** $k_H^{O_2} \approx 4.34 \times 10^4$ atm, $k_H^{CO_2} \approx 1.67 \times 10^2$ atm at 25°C. - ✅ **Origin:** Thermodynamic equality of chemical potential $\mu_A^{gas} = \mu_A^{liq}$ at the gas-liquid interface. - ✅ **Temperature dependence:** Governed by Van't Hoff equation $\frac{d \ln k_H}{dT} = \frac{\Delta_{sol} \bar{H}}{RT^2}$. - ✅ **Physical meaning of $k_H$:** A *small* $k_H$ → *highly soluble* gas (e.g., $NH_3$); a *large* $k_H$ → *poorly soluble* gas (e.g., $O_2, N_2$). - ✅ **Limitations:** Fails at high concentrations, fails for reactive gases, assumes ideal gas, and assumes ideal-dilute solution behavior. - ✅ **Biochemical applications:** Bioreactor oxygenation, blood-gas transport, anaesthesia, decompression sickness modelling, environmental gas-exchange analysis. - ✅ **Always pair** Henry's Law with **Dalton's Law** ($P_A = y_A \cdot P_{total}$) for gas-mixture problems. - ✅ **For KTU derivations,** always start from the **chemical potential equality** — it is the canonical KTU starting point for any equilibrium law.

topics.title: Properties of Fluids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT414 - BIOCHEMICAL THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Properties of Fluids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Fluid?

A **fluid** is a substance that **continuously deforms (flows)** under the action of an applied shear stress (or tangential force). Unlike a solid, a fluid cannot sustain a shear stress at rest; it yields and moves as long as the force is applied. The two primary fluid phases relevant to biochemical engineering are **liquids** and **gases**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3):**
> *A fluid is defined as a substance which deforms continuously under the action of an applied shear stress. Both liquids and gases fall under the category of fluids because they cannot resist deformation.*

In the **NEP 2020 / KTU 2024 scheme**, fluid properties form the foundation for analyzing bioreactor hydrodynamics, sterilization flows, mixing, pumping of broths, and downstream processing (chromatography, filtration, centrifugation).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **stack of playing cards on a table**.
- If you **push the top card sideways**, the entire stack slides as a rigid unit — this is a **solid** (elastic, returns to original shape after stress is removed).
- Now imagine a **stack of wet playing cards** (or pancakes drenched in honey). Push the top one sideways, and it slips, but the card below also slips slightly, and the next, and so on. The layers *slide relative to one another* — this is **fluid behavior**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** A fluid is not defined by its composition but by its **inability to resist shear stress at rest**. Honey, water, blood, and air are all fluids.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Fluid Properties

Fluid properties are broadly classified as:

1. **Intensive Properties** — Independent of the mass of the system.
   *Examples: Pressure, Temperature, Density, Specific Gravity, Viscosity, Surface Tension.*
2. **Extensive Properties** — Dependent on the mass (or size) of the system.
   *Examples: Mass, Volume, Total Energy, Entropy.*

> [!TIP]
> In biochemical thermodynamics, **specific** quantities (per unit mass) such as *specific volume*, *specific internal energy*, and *specific enthalpy* are **intensive** and therefore more useful for engineering analysis.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Biochemical Engineering

| Application | Relevant Fluid Property |
|---|---|
| Pumping of fermentation broth | Viscosity, Density |
| Blood flow modeling in bioreactors/sensors | Rheology, Viscosity |
| Filter sterilization (0.22 µm membranes) | Pressure, Compressibility |
| Spray drying of enzymes | Surface tension, Viscosity |
| Distillation / evaporation in downstream | Vapor pressure, Boiling point |
| Gas–liquid mass transfer (O₂ transfer in fermenters) | Diffusivity, Density difference |

---

## 1.5 Important Physical Constants

- Standard gravity: $g = 9.806 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Standard atmospheric pressure: $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar}$
- Universal Gas Constant: $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Primary (Bulk) Fluid Properties

### 2.1.1 Density ($\rho$)

Density is defined as **mass per unit volume** of a fluid.

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V} \quad \left[ \text{SI Unit: kg/m}^3 \right]$$

For a heterogeneous fluid or a mixture (e.g., fermentation broth with cells), the **bulk density** is defined as:

$$\rho_{bulk} = \frac{\text{Total mass of system}}{\text{Total volume of system}}$$

**Why this matters:** In a stirred-tank bioreactor, density dictates the **power number**, **impeller loading**, and **terminal settling velocity** of cells during centrifugation.

---

### 2.1.2 Specific Volume ($v$)

Specific volume is the reciprocal of density and represents **volume occupied per unit mass**.

$$v = \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{V}{m} \quad \left[ \text{SI Unit: m}^3/\text{kg} \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Specific volume is a key intensive property in thermodynamics — it appears in the **ideal gas law** ($Pv = RT$) and in the **state postulate**.

---

### 2.1.3 Specific Gravity (SG) or Relative Density

Specific gravity is the **ratio of the density of a fluid to the density of a standard reference fluid** (usually water at $4°\text{C}$ for liquids, and air at standard conditions for gases).

$$SG = \frac{\rho_{fluid}}{\rho_{water @ 4°C}} \quad \text{(dimensionless)}$$

For water at $4°\text{C}$: $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 1 \text{ g/cm}^3$.

> [!NOTE]
> Specific gravity is **dimensionless** and is extensively used in the **Brix scale**, **API gravity** (petrochemicals), and **Baumé scale** for process industries.

---

## 2.2 Pressure ($P$)

Pressure is the **normal force exerted by a fluid per unit area** on a surface (real or imaginary).

$$P = \frac{F}{A} \quad \left[ \text{SI Unit: N/m}^2 = \text{Pascal (Pa)} \right]$$

### Types of Pressure in KTU Curriculum

| Type | Definition | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| **Absolute Pressure** | Measured relative to perfect vacuum (true zero) | $P_{abs}$ |
| **Gauge Pressure** | Measured relative to local atmospheric | $P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$ |
| **Vacuum Pressure** | When $P_{abs} < P_{atm}$ | $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$ |

$$P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$$

$$P_{abs} = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$$

> [!WARNING]
> In **KTU board exams**, students often forget to convert gauge to absolute in thermodynamic problems. The **ideal gas law** and **steam tables** strictly require **absolute** pressure. This is a common 2-mark loss.

---

## 2.3 Temperature ($T$)

Temperature is a measure of the **average kinetic energy** of the molecules in a fluid.

- SI unit: **Kelvin (K)**
- Common conversions:
$$T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$$
$$T(°F) = \frac{9}{5} T(°C) + 32$$

---

## 2.4 Viscosity (Dynamic Viscosity, $\mu$)

Viscosity is the **internal resistance of a fluid to flow** (resistance to deformation under shear stress). It is the most critical property in biochemical engineering for **non-ideal** fluids like broths, polymer solutions, and blood.

### Newton's Law of Viscosity

For a Newtonian fluid under laminar flow:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress $[\text{Pa}]$
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity $[\text{Pa·s}]$ or $[\text{kg/(m·s)}]$
- $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient (shear rate), $\dot{\gamma}$ $[\text{s}^{-1}]$

> [!NOTE]
> **1 Pa·s = 10 Poise (P) = 1000 centipoise (cP)**
> Water at $20°\text{C}$: $\mu_{water} \approx 1.002 \text{ cP} = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$

---

## 2.5 Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$)

Kinematic viscosity is the ratio of dynamic viscosity to density.

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} \quad \left[ \text{SI Unit: m}^2/\text{s} \right]$$

Conversion: $1 \text{ Stokes} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s} = 1 \text{ cm}^2/\text{s}$

---

## 2.6 Newtonian vs Non-Newtonian Fluids

| Fluid Type | Relationship | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Newtonian** | $\tau \propto \dot{\gamma}$ (linear, passes through origin) | Water, air, alcohol, glycerol |
| **Pseudoplastic (Shear-thinning)** | Viscosity decreases with shear rate | Blood, polymer melts, fermentation broths |
| **Dilatant (Shear-thickening)** | Viscosity increases with shear rate | Starch suspensions, sand slurries |
| **Bingham Plastic** | Requires yield stress to flow | Toothpaste, ketchup, drilling mud |
| **Thixotropic** | Viscosity decreases with time at constant shear | Yogurt, paints |
| **Rheopectic** | Viscosity increases with time at constant shear | Gypsum pastes |

The **apparent viscosity** for a non-Newtonian fluid (Power Law / Ostwald-de Waele model):

$$\mu_{app} = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$

Where:
- $K$ = Consistency index $[\text{Pa·s}^n]$
- $n$ = Flow behavior index [dimensionless]
- $n < 1$: Pseudoplastic
- $n = 1$: Newtonian
- $n > 1$: Dilatant

---

## 2.7 Surface Tension ($\sigma$) and Capillarity

**Surface tension** is the force per unit length acting along the surface of a liquid, tending to minimize surface area.

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{L} \quad \left[ \text{SI Unit: N/m} \right]$$

The **capillary rise (Jurin's Law)** in a tube of diameter $d$:

$$h = \frac{4 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g d}$$

Where:
- $\theta$ = Contact angle between liquid and tube
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity
- $d$ = Diameter of capillary tube

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In biochemical applications, surface tension governs **foam formation** in fermenters (often undesirable) and **droplet formation** in emulsions used for drug delivery.

---

## 2.8 Vapor Pressure

**Vapor pressure** is the pressure exerted by the vapor of a liquid in thermodynamic equilibrium with its condensed phase at a given temperature.

- A liquid boils when its **vapor pressure equals the surrounding (ambient) pressure**.
- Vapor pressure **increases** with temperature (Clausius–Clapeyron relation).

---

## 2.9 Compressibility ($\beta$)

Compressibility is the **fractional decrease in volume per unit increase in pressure**.

$$\beta = -\frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T \quad \left[ \text{Unit: Pa}^{-1} \right]$$

The **Bulk Modulus** is the inverse of compressibility:

$$K = \frac{1}{\beta} = -V\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial V}\right)_T \quad \left[ \text{Unit: Pa} \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Liquids are nearly incompressible** ($\beta \approx 0$, very large $K$); **gases are highly compressible**. This distinction is critical when selecting the proper **thermodynamic state equation** in biochemical processes.

---

## 2.10 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is the **single most important reference** for Part A & Part B numerical questions in the KTU 2024 ESE for PEBBT414. Memorize the units — they are the most common source of mark loss.

| # | Property | Symbol | Formula | SI Unit |
|:---:|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Density | $\rho$ | $m/V$ | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| 2 | Specific Volume | $v$ | $1/\rho$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$ |
| 3 | Specific Gravity | $SG$ | $\rho_{fluid}/\rho_{water}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Pressure | $P$ | $F/A$ | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 5 | Dynamic Viscosity | $\mu$ | $\tau/\dot{\gamma}$ | $\text{Pa·s}$ |
| 6 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu$ | $\mu/\rho$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| 7 | Surface Tension | $\sigma$ | $F/L$ | $\text{N/m}$ |
| 8 | Capillary Rise | $h$ | $4\sigma\cos\theta/(\rho g d)$ | $\text{m}$ |
| 9 | Compressibility | $\beta$ | $-\frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T$ | $\text{Pa}^{-1}$ |
| 10 | Bulk Modulus | $K$ | $1/\beta$ | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 11 | Power-Law Viscosity | $\mu_{app}$ | $K\dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | $\text{Pa·s}$ |
| 12 | Ideal Gas Law | $Pv$ | $RT$ | $\text{J/(kg·K)}$ basis |
| 13 | Shear Stress | $\tau$ | $\mu \cdot du/dy$ | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 14 | Continuity Equation | $\dot{m}$ | $\rho A V = \text{const}$ | $\text{kg/s}$ |

---

## 2.11 Real-World Utility

In the **biopharmaceutical industry**, fluid property data are crucial for:
- **Process design** of bioreactors, heat exchangers, and chromatography columns.
- **CFD (Computational Fluid Dynamics)** simulations of fermenter hydrodynamics.
- **Quality control** of injectables (viscosity of parenteral solutions).
- **Scale-up** of laboratory processes to industrial production.
- **Energy auditing** for pumping, mixing, and aeration systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **exhaustive, step-by-step derivations** of the most frequently examined relationships in Module 3. No step is skipped.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Newton's Law of Viscosity from First Principles

### Statement
Consider a fluid element confined between two parallel plates. The lower plate is stationary, and the upper plate moves with a constant velocity $U$ under the action of an applied force $F$. The gap between the plates is $dy$ (or $h$). We assume **laminar flow**, **no-slip** at the walls, and a **linear velocity profile**.

### Step 1: Define Shear Stress

The applied force $F$ acts on the upper plate of area $A$, producing a shear stress $\tau$:

$$\tau = \frac{F}{A}$$

### Step 2: Define Velocity Gradient

Since the lower plate is stationary and the upper plate moves at $U$ across a gap $h$, the velocity changes linearly from $0$ to $U$. By geometry, the velocity gradient is:

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U - 0}{h - 0} = \frac{U}{h}$$

### Step 3: Newton's Hypothesis (1687)

Sir Isaac Newton hypothesized that the shear stress is **directly proportional** to the velocity gradient:

$$\tau \propto \frac{du}{dy}$$

### Step 4: Introduce the Proportionality Constant ($\mu$)

The constant of proportionality is the dynamic viscosity $\mu$:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

### Step 5: Generalize to 3-D Flow

For a general 3-D flow, the **stress tensor** $\tau_{ij}$ is linearly related to the **rate-of-strain tensor** $e_{ij}$ via:

$$\tau_{ij} = 2\mu e_{ij} \quad \text{where} \quad e_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial u_j}{\partial x_i}\right)$$

This is the foundation of **Newtonian fluid mechanics** and is valid for all incompressible Newtonian fluids including water, air, oils, and most dilute aqueous solutions encountered in early-stage bioprocesses.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Bulk Modulus from Compressibility

### Step 1: Start with the Compressibility Definition

$$\beta = -\frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T$$

### Step 2: Recognize the Inverse Relationship

The bulk modulus is defined as the inverse:

$$K = \frac{1}{\beta}$$

### Step 3: Substitute

$$K = \frac{1}{-\frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T} = -\frac{V}{\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T}$$

### Step 4: Re-express via Chain Rule

Using $\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T = \frac{1}{\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial V}\right)_T}$:

$$K = -V \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial V}\right)_T$$

### Step 5: Specialize to a Constant Mass System

For a closed system, $V = m v$ (where $v$ is specific volume), so $K$ can also be written per unit mass:

$$K = -v \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial v}\right)_T$$

> [!NOTE]
> For **water at $20°\text{C}$**: $K_{water} \approx 2.2 \text{ GPa}$
> For **air at STP**: $K_{air} \approx 101 \text{ kPa}$ (≈ 21,800× smaller → highly compressible)

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Capillary Rise (Jurin's Law)

### Step 1: Surface Tension Force

A liquid column rises inside a capillary tube because of the upward vertical component of surface tension. The total upward force around the circumference of the tube (diameter $d$, contact angle $\theta$) is:

$$F_{\uparrow} = \sigma \cdot (\pi d) \cdot \cos\theta$$

### Step 2: Weight of the Liquid Column

The weight of the raised liquid column (height $h$, cross-section $\pi d^2/4$, density $\rho$) is:

$$F_{\downarrow} = \rho g \cdot h \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

### Step 3: Equilibrium at the Meniscus

At equilibrium, the upward and downward forces balance:

$$\sigma \pi d \cos\theta = \rho g h \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

### Step 4: Solve for $h$

$$h = \frac{4 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g d}$$

### Step 5: Discussion

- $h > 0$ for $\theta < 90°$ (wetting fluid, e.g., water in glass).
- $h < 0$ (capillary depression) for $\theta > 90°$ (non-wetting fluid, e.g., mercury in glass).
- $h \to 0$ as $d \to \infty$ — wide tubes show negligible capillary rise.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Pressure Conversion between Absolute, Gauge, and Vacuum

### Step 1: Establish Reference

By definition:
- **Absolute zero** = perfect vacuum
- **Gauge zero** = local atmospheric pressure
- **Vacuum zero** = local atmospheric pressure (but measured in negative direction)

### Step 2: Form the Relations

$$P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$$
$$P_{abs} = P_{atm} - P_{vac} \quad \text{(when } P_{abs} < P_{atm}\text{)}$$

### Step 3: Solve for Any Unknown

$$P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$$
$$P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$$

### Step 4: Worked Example (KTU Style)

A bioreactor headspace gauge reads $P_{gauge} = 1.5 \text{ bar}$. Local atmospheric pressure is $1.013 \text{ bar}$. Find absolute pressure.

$$P_{abs} = 1.5 + 1.013 = 2.513 \text{ bar} = 251.3 \text{ kPa}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Kinematic Viscosity

### Step 1: Definition

$$\nu \equiv \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

### Step 2: Units Check

$$\nu = \frac{\text{Pa·s}}{\text{kg/m}^3} = \frac{\text{kg/(m·s)}}{\text{kg/m}^3} = \frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}}$$

### Step 3: Physical Meaning

Kinematic viscosity represents the **diffusion of momentum** in a fluid. It appears in the **Reynolds number**:

$$Re = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{V L}{\nu}$$

A high $Re$ indicates **inertia-dominated** flow (turbulent), while a low $Re$ indicates **viscosity-dominated** flow (laminar). In bioreactors, the flow regime dictates **mixing efficiency** and **mass transfer rates**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Process Variables on Fluid Properties

| Variable ↑ | Density $\rho$ | Viscosity $\mu$ (Newtonian) | Vapor Pressure | Surface Tension | Compressibility $\beta$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Temperature** | Decreases (liquids) | Decreases (liquids) | Increases | Decreases | Increases (gases) |
| **Pressure** | Slight increase (liquids); large (gases) | Increases | Negligible effect | Negligible | Decreases sharply |
| **Concentration (solutes)** | Increases | Often increases (polymer solutions) | Decreases (Raoult's Law) | May vary | Decreases (slightly) |

> [!TIP]
> This matrix is **highly tested** in KTU Part A questions. Understanding the **direction of change** (increase/decrease) is the key to scoring full marks on conceptual questions.

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (Model Solution for KTU)

**Problem:** A fluid in a bioreactor has a density of $880 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and a dynamic viscosity of $3.2 \text{ cP}$. The fermenter is operated at $35°\text{C}$ and a gauge pressure of $2.0 \text{ bar}$. Calculate:
1. Specific volume
2. Specific gravity
3. Kinematic viscosity (in $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ and $\text{Stokes}$)
4. Absolute pressure in kPa

### Solution

**(1) Specific volume:**

$$v = \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{1}{880} = 1.136 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**(2) Specific gravity:**

$$SG = \frac{880}{1000} = 0.880$$

**(3) Kinematic viscosity:**
- First convert $\mu$: $3.2 \text{ cP} = 3.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$
- Then: $\nu = \mu/\rho = 3.2 \times 10^{-3} / 880 = 3.636 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- In Stokes: $3.636 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s} \times 10^4 = 0.03636 \text{ St}$

**(4) Absolute pressure:**

$$P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm} = 200 \text{ kPa} + 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 301.325 \text{ kPa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit conversion traps:** cP → Pa·s needs a factor of $10^{-3}$. St → m²/s needs a factor of $10^{-4}$. Mismatch in units is the **#1 cause of mark loss** in fluid property numericals at KTU.
