# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEBBT414]`

**Define the following with proper SI units:**
1. Dynamic viscosity
2. Kinematic viscosity
3. Specific gravity

#### Model Answer:

**(i) Dynamic Viscosity (μ):** It is the property of a fluid that offers resistance to the deformation (or flow) under the action of an applied shear stress. Mathematically, from Newton's law of viscosity, $\mu = \tau / (du/dy)$.
- **SI Unit:** $\text{Pa·s}$ or $\text{kg/(m·s)}$
- **CGS Unit:** Poise (P); $1 \text{ P} = 0.1 \text{ Pa·s}$

**(ii) Kinematic Viscosity (ν):** It is defined as the ratio of dynamic viscosity to the density of the fluid.
- Formula: $\nu = \mu / \rho$
- **SI Unit:** $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$
- **CGS Unit:** Stokes (St); $1 \text{ St} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$

**(iii) Specific Gravity (SG):** It is the dimensionless ratio of the density of a fluid to the density of a reference substance (water at $4°\text{C}$ for liquids, air at STP for gases).
- Formula: $SG = \rho_{fluid} / \rho_{water} = \rho_{fluid} / 1000$
- **SI Unit:** Dimensionless

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEBBT414]`

**Distinguish between Newtonian and Non-Newtonian fluids. Give two examples of each.**

#### Model Answer:

| Feature | Newtonian | Non-Newtonian |
|---|---|---|
| Viscosity | Constant (independent of shear rate) | Varies with shear rate |
| Shear stress vs Shear rate | Linear (passes through origin) | Non-linear |
| Equation | $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$ | $\tau = K (du/dy)^n$ (Power Law) |
| Examples | Water, air, alcohol, glycerol, thin oils | Blood, fermentation broth, ketchup, starch slurry, paint |

**Newtonian Examples:** Water, Air, Ethanol
**Non-Newtonian Examples:** Blood (pseudoplastic), Toothpaste (Bingham plastic), Corn starch in water (dilatant)

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students must answer **either Option A or Option B**. Each option has two sub-parts: Part (a) carries 7 marks and Part (b) carries 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEBBT414]`

**Option A (Module 3 Internal Choice)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Starting from the definition, derive Newton's law of viscosity. Explain the physical significance of dynamic viscosity with examples of Newtonian and Non-Newtonian fluids.**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Setup:** Consider two parallel plates, the lower one fixed and the upper one moving at constant velocity $U$ under a force $F$. Fluid fills the gap $h$ between them.

**Step 2 — Shear Stress:** The force per unit area on the upper plate is $\tau = F/A$.

**Step 3 — Velocity Gradient:** Linear velocity profile gives $du/dy = U/h$.

**Step 4 — Newton's Hypothesis (1687):** The shear stress is directly proportional to the velocity gradient:
$$\tau \propto \frac{du}{dy}$$
$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 5 — Physical Significance:** $\mu$ is a measure of the fluid's internal friction or resistance to flow. Higher $\mu$ means more "thick" (e.g., honey) and lower $\mu$ means more "thin" (e.g., water).

**Step 6 — Newtonian:** Constant $\mu$ — Water, air, alcohol.
**Step 7 — Non-Newtonian:** Variable $\mu$ — Blood (pseudoplastic), starch (dilatant), toothpaste (Bingham plastic).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A fluid has a density of $850 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and dynamic viscosity of $4.5 \text{ cP}$. Calculate (i) specific volume, (ii) specific gravity, and (iii) kinematic viscosity in $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ and Stokes.**

#### Model Solution:

**(i) Specific volume:**

$$v = \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{1}{850} = 1.176 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**(ii) Specific gravity:**

$$SG = \frac{850}{1000} = 0.850$$

**(iii) Kinematic viscosity:**
- Convert: $\mu = 4.5 \text{ cP} = 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$
- Compute: $\nu = \mu / \rho = 4.5 \times 10^{-3} / 850 = 5.294 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- In Stokes: $\nu = 5.294 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^4 = 0.05294 \text{ St}$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEBBT414]`

**Option B (Module 3 Internal Choice)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Define surface tension and capillarity. Derive Jurin's law for capillary rise in a tube.**

#### Model Solution:

**Surface Tension (σ):** Surface tension is the force per unit length acting along the surface of a liquid, tending to minimize the surface area. SI unit: N/m.

**Capillarity:** It is the phenomenon of rise (or fall) of a liquid in a narrow tube due to surface tension. Wetting liquids rise; non-wetting liquids fall.

**Derivation of Jurin's Law:**

Consider a capillary tube of diameter $d$ dipped in a liquid of density $\rho$ and surface tension $\sigma$. The liquid rises to a height $h$ with contact angle $\theta$.

- **Upward force (surface tension):** $F_{\uparrow} = \sigma \cdot \pi d \cdot \cos\theta$
- **Downward force (weight of liquid column):** $F_{\downarrow} = \rho g h \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$

At equilibrium, $F_{\uparrow} = F_{\downarrow}$:
$$\sigma \pi d \cos\theta = \rho g h \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

Solving for $h$:
$$h = \frac{4 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g d}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A capillary tube of $1.0 \text{ mm}$ diameter is inserted into water at $20°\text{C}$. The contact angle is $30°$. Calculate the capillary rise. Given: $\sigma = 0.0728 \text{ N/m}$, $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.**

#### Model Solution:

**Given:**
- $d = 1.0 \text{ mm} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
- $\theta = 30°$, so $\cos 30° = 0.866$
- $\sigma = 0.0728 \text{ N/m}$
- $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Using Jurin's Law:**
$$h = \frac{4 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g d}$$

$$h = \frac{4 \times 0.0728 \times 0.866}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$h = \frac{0.2523}{9.81} = 0.0257 \text{ m} = 25.7 \text{ mm}$$

**The water rises about 25.7 mm in the capillary tube.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Units of viscosity:** cP must be converted to Pa·s. Marks are deducted if not converted.
> 2. **Pressure references:** Thermodynamic problems always need **absolute** pressure, not gauge. Forgetting to add $P_{atm}$ is a recurring error.
> 3. **Surface tension units:** N/m is correct. Some students mistakenly write J/m² (this is numerically equal but dimensionally different).
> 4. **Capillarity contact angle:** $\cos\theta$ is required, not $\theta$ alone. Forgetting cosine is a 2-mark loss.
> 5. **Density of water:** Use $1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ for any specific gravity calculation unless the problem explicitly states a different reference temperature.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 3: Properties of Fluids**

- **Fluid** is a substance that cannot resist shear stress at rest; both liquids and gases are fluids.
- **Density ($\rho$):** mass/volume; SI: $\text{kg/m}^3$. Water at $4°\text{C}$ = $1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
- **Specific volume ($v$):** $1/\rho$; SI: $\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$.
- **Specific Gravity (SG):** dimensionless ratio to water at $4°\text{C}$.
- **Pressure (P):** $F/A$; SI: Pa. Always convert gauge to absolute: $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$.
- **Newton's Law of Viscosity:** $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$. Valid only for **Newtonian** fluids.
- **Dynamic viscosity (μ):** SI: Pa·s. $1 \text{ cP} = 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$.
- **Kinematic viscosity (ν):** $\nu = \mu/\rho$; SI: $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$. $1 \text{ St} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$.
- **Newtonian fluids:** Constant $\mu$ (water, air). **Non-Newtonian:** Variable $\mu$ (blood, starch, paints).
- **Power-Law model:** $\mu_{app} = K\dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$; $n<1$ shear-thinning, $n=1$ Newtonian, $n>1$ shear-thickening.
- **Bingham plastic:** Has yield stress (toothpaste, ketchup).
- **Surface tension (σ):** N/m; capillary rise uses Jurin's law: $h = 4\sigma\cos\theta / (\rho g d)$.
- **Compressibility (β):** $\text{Pa}^{-1}$. **Bulk modulus ($K$):** $1/\beta$. Liquids have very high $K$.
- **Vapor pressure:** increases with temperature; boiling occurs when vapor pressure = ambient pressure.
- **Temperature effect:** ↑T → ↓μ (liquids), ↓ρ (liquids), ↑ vapor pressure, ↓σ.
- **Reynolds Number:** $Re = \rho V L / \mu = V L / \nu$ — distinguishes laminar vs turbulent flow.
- **Continuity Equation:** $\rho A V = \text{constant}$ for steady incompressible flow.
- **Common exam trap:** Forgetting to convert cP to Pa·s; using gauge pressure where absolute is required.
- **High-yield bioprocess link:** Viscosity affects bioreactor mixing, O₂ transfer, and pump power; surface tension affects foaming and emulsification; vapor pressure affects sterilization and distillation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gibbs Free Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT414 - BIOCHEMICAL THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gibbs Free Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gibbs Free Energy (G)** is a thermodynamic state function that represents the maximum amount of non-expansion (useful) work obtainable from a closed system at constant **temperature (T)** and constant **pressure (P)**. For a biochemical system, it is the single most useful thermodynamic potential because biological reactions occur in aqueous, constant-T, constant-P environments.

Mathematically, Gibbs free energy is defined as:

$$G \equiv H - TS$$

where **H** is the enthalpy, **T** is the absolute temperature in **Kelvin (K)**, and **S** is the entropy of the system. The change in Gibbs free energy at constant temperature and pressure is given by:

$$\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$$

The **biochemical standard free energy change (ΔG°')** is defined under the **biochemical standard state**:
- **Temperature = 298.15 K (25 °C)**
- **Pressure = 1 atm (101.325 kPa)**
- **pH = 7.0** (not pH = 0 as in chemistry)
- **Concentration of all reactants and products (except H⁺) = 1 M**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Available Energy" Bank

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Student Bank Account**
> Think of the total enthalpy (H) of a molecule as the **total money** in a student's bank account. Of this, a portion (T·S) is **"locked away"** as unusable, dispersed energy — analogous to money tied up in taxes, penalties, or uninvested reserves. The Gibbs free energy (G) is the **spendable balance** — the energy actually *available* to do useful work (drive reactions, synthesize ATP, power muscle contraction, etc.).
> - If **ΔG < 0** → The reaction is *exergonic* (spontaneous, releases usable energy, like a salary credit).
> - If **ΔG > 0** → The reaction is *endergonic* (non-spontaneous, requires energy input, like a debit/expense).
> - If **ΔG = 0** → The system is at *equilibrium* (balance in the account, no net change).

## 1.3 Significance in Biochemical Systems

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Gibbs Free Energy Matters in Biochemistry:**
> 1. Predicts **spontaneity** of metabolic reactions.
> 2. Quantifies the **energy yield** of catabolic pathways (e.g., glucose oxidation, ATP hydrolysis).
> 3. Determines the **direction** and **equilibrium position** of biochemical reactions.
> 4. Couples **endergonic** reactions (synthesis) with **exergonic** reactions (hydrolysis) — the basis of metabolism.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Universal Gas Constant | R | **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** (or **1.987 cal·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**) |
| Standard Temperature | T | **298.15 K** |
| Standard Pressure | P | **1 atm (101.325 kPa)** |
| Standard Biochemical pH | pH | **7.0** |
| Faraday Constant | F | **96,485 C·mol⁻¹** (used in electrochemistry) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "Why and How" Behind Gibbs Free Energy

Gibbs free energy emerges naturally from the **First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics** combined. The stepwise logic is:

- **Step 1 — Energy Conservation (1st Law):** The change in internal energy $\Delta U = q + w$, where q is heat absorbed and w is work done on the system.
- **Step 2 — Restrict to Constant-Pressure Conditions:** At constant pressure, the heat exchanged equals the enthalpy change: $q_P = \Delta H$. The work includes both pressure–volume work ($w_{PV} = -P\Delta V$) and non-expansion (useful) work ($w_{non-PV}$).
- **Step 3 — Apply 2nd Law:** Total entropy of universe must increase for a spontaneous process: $\Delta S_{universe} = \Delta S_{sys} + \Delta S_{surr} \geq 0$.
- **Step 4 — Substitute $\Delta S_{surr} = -\Delta H / T$** at constant T,P. After algebra (see SECTION 3), we obtain the criterion:
  - $\Delta G < 0$ → **spontaneous (exergonic)**
  - $\Delta G > 0$ → **non-spontaneous (endergonic)**
  - $\Delta G = 0$ → **equilibrium**
- **Step 5 — Biochemical Adaptation:** Since biological systems buffer at pH 7, we define $\Delta G^{\circ\prime}$ at pH 7, where [H⁺] = 10⁻⁷ M is incorporated into the constant.

## 2.2 Differential Form of Gibbs Energy

Starting from $G = H - TS = U + PV - TS$, taking the total differential at constant composition:

$$dG = VdP - SdT$$

This fundamental relation reveals two important Maxwell-like identities:

$$\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = V \quad \text{and} \quad \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = -S$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the master reference for all KTU 2024 exam questions on Gibbs Free Energy.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Condition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G \equiv H - TS$ | Definition of Gibbs energy | J·mol⁻¹ |
| 2 | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ | Change at constant T, P | J·mol⁻¹ |
| 3 | $dG = VdP - SdT$ | Total differential (closed, constant composition) | J·mol⁻¹ |
| 4 | $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -RT \ln K_{eq}$ | Standard free energy ↔ equilibrium constant | J·mol⁻¹ |
| 5 | $\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ\prime} + RT \ln Q$ | Actual free energy change (Q = reaction quotient) | J·mol⁻¹ |
| 6 | $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -nF\Delta E_{0}^{\prime}$ | Electrochemical coupling (n = e⁻, F = Faraday) | J·mol⁻¹ |
| 7 | $\left(\frac{\partial(\Delta G/T)}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = -\frac{\Delta H}{T^{2}}$ | Gibbs–Helmholtz Equation | — |
| 8 | $\Delta G_{f}^{\circ\prime}$ (of formation) | For species in biochemical standard state | kJ·mol⁻¹ |
| 9 | $G = \sum_{i} \mu_{i} n_{i}$ | G in terms of chemical potentials | J·mol⁻¹ |
| 10 | $\Delta G_{coupled} = \sum \Delta G_{i}$ | Additivity of Gibbs energies | J·mol⁻¹ |

## 2.4 Real-World / Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Bioreactor Design:** Predicting whether a microbial fermentation will proceed spontaneously to produce ethanol, lactate, or biogas.
> - **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Calculating binding free energy of a drug to its target protein.
> - **Metabolic Engineering:** Estimating the thermodynamic feasibility of engineered pathways before lab implementation.
> - **Biofuel Cells:** Designing the electromotive force using $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -nF\Delta E_{0}^{\prime}$.
> - **Food Preservation:** Determining conditions that make spoilage reactions non-spontaneous (e.g., salting, refrigeration reduces T → reduces TΔS contribution).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Definition of G from the First and Second Laws

**Starting Point:** Combine the First Law ($dU = dq + dw$) with the Second Law ($dS \geq dq/T$) for a reversible process at constant T and P.

**Step 1:** For a reversible process, the Second Law gives $dq_{rev} = TdS$.

Substituting into the First Law:

$$dU = TdS + dw_{rev}$$

**Step 2:** At constant pressure, the work consists of pressure–volume work plus other (useful) work:

$$dw_{rev} = -PdV + dw_{non\text{-}PV}$$

Therefore:

$$dU = TdS - PdV + dw_{non\text{-}PV}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $H = U + PV$, so $dH = dU + PdV + VdP$. At constant pressure ($dP = 0$):

$$dH = dU + PdV$$

Rearranging the First Law expression for $dU$:

$$dU = TdS - PdV + dw_{non\text{-}PV}$$

**Step 4:** Add $PdV$ to both sides to obtain $dH$:

$$dH = TdS + dw_{non\text{-}PV}$$

**Step 5:** At constant temperature, $dT = 0$, so $d(TS) = TdS$. Therefore:

$$dH - TdS = dw_{non\text{-}PV, \, max}$$

**Step 6:** Recognize the left-hand side as the differential of $(H - TS)$. Define:

$$G \equiv H - TS \quad \Rightarrow \quad dG = dH - TdS$$

**Step 7:** Therefore, at constant T and P:

$$\boxed{\,dG = dw_{non\text{-}PV, \, max}\,}$$

> **Interpretation:** The change in Gibbs free energy equals the **maximum non-expansion work** obtainable from a process at constant T and P. This is the foundation of bioenergetics.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Derivation of $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -RT \ln K_{eq}$

**Starting Point:** Consider a general biochemical reaction:
$$aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$$

**Step 1:** The actual Gibbs energy change at any moment is given by:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ\prime} + RT \ln Q$$

where $Q$ is the **reaction quotient**:

$$Q = \frac{[C]^c [D]^d}{[A]^a [B]^b}$$

(All concentrations measured at the *biochemical* standard state, pH 7.)

**Step 2:** At **equilibrium**, $\Delta G = 0$ (no net driving force) and $Q = K_{eq}$ (the equilibrium constant). Substituting:

$$0 = \Delta G^{\circ\prime} + RT \ln K_{eq}$$

**Step 3:** Solving for $\Delta G^{\circ\prime}$:

$$\boxed{\,\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -RT \ln K_{eq}\,}$$

Or, converting to base-10 logarithm using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -2.303 \, RT \log_{10} K_{eq}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $R = 8.314 \text{ J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$ and $T = 298.15 \text{ K}$:

$$\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -5.708 \, \log_{10} K_{eq} \quad \text{(in kJ·mol}^{-1}\text{)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation III — Total Differential of G

**Starting Point:** $G = H - TS = U + PV - TS$.

**Step 1:** Take the differential of $U$ (using $U$ as a function of $S$ and $V$):

$$dU = TdS - PdV$$

**Step 2:** The differential of $H = U + PV$ is:

$$dH = dU + PdV + VdP = TdS - PdV + PdV + VdP = TdS + VdP$$

**Step 3:** Therefore, the differential of $G = H - TS$ is:

$$dG = dH - TdS - SdT = (TdS + VdP) - TdS - SdT$$

**Step 4:** Cancel $TdS$:

$$\boxed{\,dG = VdP - SdT\,}$$

This shows that at constant T ($dT = 0$), $dG = VdP$, and at constant P ($dP = 0$), $dG = -SdT$.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — ATP Hydrolysis

**Problem:** For the reaction ATP + H₂O → ADP + Pᵢ, the standard biochemical free energy change is $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -30.5 \text{ kJ·mol}^{-1}$. Calculate the actual $\Delta G$ at 298.15 K if the cellular concentrations are: [ATP] = 2.5 mM, [ADP] = 0.25 mM, [Pᵢ] = 1.65 mM.

**Step 1:** Write the reaction quotient:

$$Q = \frac{[\text{ADP}][\text{P}_i]}{[\text{ATP}]} = \frac{(0.25 \times 10^{-3})(1.65 \times 10^{-3})}{(2.5 \times 10^{-3})}$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate Q:

$$Q = \frac{4.125 \times 10^{-7}}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.65 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 3:** Apply $\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ\prime} + RT \ln Q$:

$$\Delta G = -30.5 \text{ kJ·mol}^{-1} + (8.314 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kJ·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1})(298.15 \text{ K}) \ln(1.65 \times 10^{-4})$$

**Step 4:** Calculate $RT$:

$$RT = 2.479 \text{ kJ·mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate $\ln(1.65 \times 10^{-4})$:

$$\ln(1.65 \times 10^{-4}) = -8.708$$

**Step 6:** Multiply and add:

$$\Delta G = -30.5 + (2.479 \times -8.708) = -30.5 + (-21.59) = -52.09 \text{ kJ·mol}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{\,\Delta G_{\text{actual}} \approx -52.1 \text{ kJ·mol}^{-1}\,}$$

> **Interpretation:** Under real cellular conditions, ATP hydrolysis is *far more exergonic* than the standard value suggests. This is why ATP is the universal "energy currency" of the cell.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Map — Relationships of Gibbs Free Energy

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how Gibbs free energy connects to other thermodynamic potentials and measurable quantities.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Internal Energy U] --> B[Enthalpy H = U plus PV]
    B --> C[Gibbs Free Energy G = H minus TS]
    A --> D[Helmholtz Energy A = U minus TS]
    C --> E[Useful Work Output dw subscript nonPV]
    C --> F[Spontaneity Criterion delta G less than 0]
    F --> G[Exergonic Reaction]
    F --> H[Endergonic Reaction]
    F --> I[Equilibrium delta G equals 0]
    C --> J[Equilibrium Constant via delta G degrees prime equals minus RT ln Keq]
    J --> K[Biochemical Standard State pH 7, 298 K, 1 atm]
    K --> L[Metabolic Pathway Feasibility]
    C --> M[Coupled Reactions delta G total equals sum of delta G sub i]
    M --> N[ATP Hydrolysis drives Biosynthesis]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Process Flow of a Biochemical Reaction Under Constant T, P

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Reactants at state 1] --> R2{Constant T and P closed system}
    R2 --> R3[delta H heat exchange]
    R2 --> R4[delta S entropy change]
    R3 --> R5[Combine H and T*S]
    R4 --> R5
    R5 --> R6[Compute delta G equals delta H minus T delta S]
    R6 --> R7{Is delta G less than 0}
    R7 -- Yes --> R8[Spontaneous proceed forward]
    R7 -- No --> R9[Non spontaneous reverse or couple]
    R7 -- Zero --> R10[System at Equilibrium Keq related to delta G degrees prime]
    R8 --> R11[Useful work dw subscript nonPV equals delta G]
    R10 --> R12[delta G degrees prime equals minus RT ln Keq]
```

## 4.3 Energy-Coupling Schematic (ATP-Driven Reaction)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ATP Hydrolysis delta G1 negative large] --> C[Common Intermediate or Energy Transfer]
    B[Substrate Synthesis delta G2 positive] --> C
    C --> D[Coupled Process delta G total equals delta G1 plus delta G2 less than 0]
    D --> E[Overall Reaction Spontaneous]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematic:** The cell continuously couples **exergonic** reactions (e.g., ATP → ADP, glucose oxidation) with **endergonic** reactions (e.g., protein synthesis, active transport). The Gibbs free energies are additive: $\Delta G_{coupled} = \sum \Delta G_i$. The overall process is spontaneous only if the sum is negative.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define Gibbs free energy. Why is it the most useful thermodynamic potential in biochemistry?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Gibbs free energy (G) is defined as $G \equiv H - TS$, where H is enthalpy, T is absolute temperature, and S is entropy. It represents the maximum non-expansion (useful) work obtainable from a system at constant temperature and pressure. **(1 mark)**

For a process at constant T and P, the change $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ determines spontaneity: $\Delta G < 0$ (spontaneous), $\Delta G > 0$ (non-spontaneous), $\Delta G = 0$ (equilibrium). **(1 mark)**

It is the most useful potential in biochemistry because **all biological reactions occur in aqueous environments at constant temperature (≈ 310 K) and constant pressure (1 atm)**, and the **biochemical standard state (pH 7)** is directly incorporated into the convention. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between $\Delta G^{\circ}$ and $\Delta G^{\circ\prime}$. Why is the biochemical standard state preferred in living systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$\Delta G^{\circ}$ is the standard free energy change defined by chemists at pH = 0, where [H⁺] = 1 M. **(1 mark)**

$\Delta G^{\circ\prime}$ is the **biochemical standard free energy change** defined at pH = 7.0, where [H⁺] = 10⁻⁷ M, while all other reactants and products are at 1 M concentration, T = 298.15 K, P = 1 atm. **(1 mark)**

The biochemical standard state is preferred because the **intracellular pH of living cells is maintained near 7.0** by buffer systems. Therefore, $\Delta G^{\circ\prime}$ values reflect the actual energetic feasibility of biochemical reactions under physiological conditions, whereas $\Delta G^{\circ}$ would require cumbersome pH corrections for every reaction involving H⁺. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the fundamental equation $G = H - TS$ starting from the First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics. Show that at constant T and P, $\Delta G$ equals the maximum non-expansion work obtainable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The equilibrium constant for the conversion of fructose-1,6-bisphosphate (F1,6BP) to fructose-6-phosphate (F6P) and inorganic phosphate (Pᵢ) is $K_{eq} = 2.4 \times 10^{-4}$ at 298.15 K. Calculate the standard biochemical free energy change $\Delta G^{\circ\prime}$. If the cellular concentrations are [F1,6BP] = 0.05 mM, [F6P] = 0.18 mM, and [Pᵢ] = 5.0 mM, calculate the actual $\Delta G$ under cellular conditions. Comment on spontaneity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** For a reversible process, the Second Law gives $dq_{rev} = TdS$. Substituting into the First Law ($dU = dq + dw$):

$$dU = TdS + dw_{rev}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Split work into pressure–volume work and useful (non-PV) work: $dw_{rev} = -PdV + dw_{non\text{-}PV}$

$$dU = TdS - PdV + dw_{non\text{-}PV}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Use $H = U + PV$ → $dH = dU + PdV + VdP$. At constant P ($dP = 0$):

$$dH = TdS + dw_{non\text{-}PV}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** At constant T, $d(TS) = TdS$, so:

$$dH - TdS = dw_{non\text{-}PV, \, max}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Define $G \equiv H - TS$, so $dG = dH - TdS$:

$$\boxed{\,dG = dw_{non\text{-}PV, \, max}\,}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** This proves that $\Delta G$ equals the maximum non-expansion (useful) work at constant T and P.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** For a spontaneous process, $dw_{non\text{-}PV} < dw_{max}$, which gives $\Delta G < 0$. Hence, the spontaneity criterion.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Apply the fundamental relation $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -RT \ln K_{eq}$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Substitute values: $R = 8.314 \text{ J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$, $T = 298.15 \text{ K}$, $K_{eq} = 2.4 \times 10^{-4}$:

$$\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -(8.314)(298.15) \ln(2.4 \times 10^{-4})$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** $\ln(2.4 \times 10^{-4}) = \ln(2.4) + \ln(10^{-4}) = 0.875 - 9.210 = -8.335$.

$$\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -(2479)(-8.335) = +20,666 \text{ J·mol}^{-1} = +20.7 \text{ kJ·mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute the reaction quotient Q (units: M):

$$Q = \frac{[\text{F6P}][\text{P}_i]}{[\text{F1,6BP}]} = \frac{(0.18 \times 10^{-3})(5.0 \times 10^{-3})}{(0.05 \times 10^{-3})} = \frac{9.0 \times 10^{-7}}{5.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.018$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Compute $RT \ln Q$:

$$RT \ln Q = (2.479 \text{ kJ·mol}^{-1}) \ln(0.018) = (2.479)(-4.017) = -9.96 \text{ kJ·mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Apply $\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ\prime} + RT \ln Q$:

$$\Delta G = 20.7 + (-9.96) = +10.7 \text{ kJ·mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** **Conclusion:** Since $\Delta G > 0$ under cellular conditions, the hydrolysis of F1,6BP to F6P + Pᵢ is **non-spontaneous** in the forward direction under the given cellular concentrations. The cell must remove products or supply substrates to drive the reaction (this is precisely why F1,6BP is committed to glycolysis — PFK-1 ensures low F6P concentration).

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Define the biochemical standard state. Derive the relationship $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -RT \ln K_{eq}$ and explain its physical significance in metabolic reactions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The reaction $\text{Glucose} + \text{P}_i \rightleftharpoons \text{Glucose-6-phosphate} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ has $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = +13.8 \text{ kJ·mol}^{-1}$. The reaction ATP + H₂O → ADP + Pᵢ has $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -30.5 \text{ kJ·mol}^{-1}$. Show that the combined (coupled) phosphorylation of glucose by ATP is spontaneous. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The **biochemical standard state** is defined as: T = 298.15 K (25 °C), P = 1 atm, pH = 7.0 ([H⁺] = 10⁻⁷ M), and all other solutes at 1 M concentration. The prime (′) in $\Delta G^{\circ\prime}$ denotes this pH-7 convention, distinguishing it from the chemical standard state at pH 0.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** For a general reaction $aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$, the actual free energy change is:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ\prime} + RT \ln Q, \quad \text{where} \quad Q = \frac{[C]^c[D]^d}{[A]^a[B]^b}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** At equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0$ (no net driving force) and $Q = K_{eq}$:

$$0 = \Delta G^{\circ\prime} + RT \ln K_{eq}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Solving:

$$\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -RT \ln K_{eq} = -2.303 \, RT \log_{10} K_{eq}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** **Physical significance:** This equation links **thermodynamics** (energy) to **equilibrium position** (concentrations). A large negative $\Delta G^{\circ\prime}$ → $K_{eq} \gg 1$ → reaction strongly favors products. A large positive $\Delta G^{\circ\prime}$ → $K_{eq} \ll 1$ → reactants are favored.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** For example, ATP hydrolysis has $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -30.5 \text{ kJ·mol}^{-1}$, giving:

$$K_{eq} = 10^{30500 / (2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15)} = 10^{5.34} \approx 2.2 \times 10^{5}$$

This enormous equilibrium constant means ATP hydrolysis is virtually irreversible under standard conditions.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** **Metabolic significance:** Cells exploit the relationship between $\Delta G^{\circ\prime}$ and $K_{eq}$ to identify "committed" steps in pathways (e.g., PFK-1 in glycolysis, hexokinase in glucose phosphorylation). Reactions with $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} \ll 0$ act as one-way valves that thermodynamically commit metabolites to a pathway.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the two reactions:
- Reaction 1: Glucose + Pᵢ → G6P + H₂O, $\Delta G_1^{\circ\prime} = +13.8 \text{ kJ·mol}^{-1}$
- Reaction 2: ATP + H₂O → ADP + Pᵢ, $\Delta G_2^{\circ\prime} = -30.5 \text{ kJ·mol}^{-1}$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Identify the common intermediate: **Pᵢ (inorganic phosphate)**. Reaction 1 consumes Pᵢ, Reaction 2 produces Pᵢ. Therefore, they can be algebraically added to eliminate Pᵢ.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Add the two reactions:

$$\text{Glucose} + \cancel{\text{P}_i} + \cancel{\text{ATP}} + \cancel{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \rightarrow \text{G6P} + \cancel{\text{H}_2\text{O}} + \text{ADP} + \cancel{\text{P}_i}$$

Net: $\text{Glucose} + \text{ATP} \rightarrow \text{G6P} + \text{ADP}$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** The Gibbs energies are additive:

$$\Delta G_{coupled}^{\circ\prime} = \Delta G_1^{\circ\prime} + \Delta G_2^{\circ\prime} = (+13.8) + (-30.5) = -16.7 \text{ kJ·mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Since $\Delta G_{coupled}^{\circ\prime} < 0$, the **phosphorylation of glucose by ATP is spontaneous** under standard biochemical conditions.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** The equilibrium constant for the coupled reaction is:

$$K_{eq}' = 10^{(16700) / (2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15)} = 10^{2.926} \approx 843$$

So the equilibrium strongly favors G6P and ADP.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** **Biological interpretation:** This is precisely how **hexokinase** (in most tissues) and **glucokinase** (in liver) function. They transfer the γ-phosphate of ATP directly to glucose. The large negative $\Delta G_2^{\circ\prime}$ of ATP hydrolysis "pulls" the otherwise unfavorable phosphorylation of glucose forward. This principle — **energy coupling** — is the cornerstone of all metabolic processes (synthesis, active transport, signal transduction, muscle contraction).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the prime (′):** Students often write $\Delta G^{\circ}$ when the question expects $\Delta G^{\circ\prime}$ (pH 7). The two values differ whenever H⁺ is a reactant or product. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 2. **Sign errors in $RT \ln Q$:** Q < 1 gives $\ln Q < 0$, making the $RT \ln Q$ term **negative**, which makes $\Delta G$ more negative (more spontaneous). Students often flip the sign. **(Lose 1–2 marks)**
> 3. **Unit confusion:** Use J·mol⁻¹ consistently with R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹, or convert to kJ·mol⁻¹ by dividing by 1000. Mixing units is a common error. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 4. **Forgetting to combine reactions properly:** When coupling, identify the **common species** (Pᵢ, ADP, NAD⁺, etc.) and cancel it. A sloppy addition loses the spontaneity argument. **(Lose 2 marks)**
> 5. **Writing $\Delta G = -RT \ln K$ instead of $-RT \ln K_{eq}$:** Use $K_{eq}$ for consistency with KTU textbook notation. **(Lose 0.5 mark)**
> 6. **Temperature:** Always use **298.15 K** unless otherwise specified. Using 310 K (physiological) without the question asking for it loses a mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Gibbs Free Energy (Module 3)**
> - ✅ **Definition:** $G \equiv H - TS$; represents maximum useful (non-PV) work at constant T, P.
> - ✅ **Spontaneity rule:** $\Delta G < 0$ (spontaneous/exergonic), $\Delta G > 0$ (non-spontaneous/endergonic), $\Delta G = 0$ (equilibrium).
> - ✅ **Differential form:** $dG = VdP - SdT$.
> - ✅ **Standard free energy ↔ equilibrium constant:** $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -RT \ln K_{eq} = -2.303 \, RT \log_{10} K_{eq}$.
> - ✅ **Actual free energy change:** $\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ\prime} + RT \ln Q$ (where Q is the reaction quotient).
> - ✅ **Biochemical standard state:** T = 298.15 K, P = 1 atm, pH = 7.0, [solutes] = 1 M.
> - ✅ **Energy coupling:** Gibbs energies are additive; exergonic reactions can drive endergonic ones (e.g., ATP hydrolysis drives biosynthesis).
> - ✅ **Electrochemical form:** $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -nF\Delta E_0^{\prime}$ (used in electron transport chain calculations).
> - ✅ **Gibbs–Helmholtz equation:** $\left[\frac{\partial(\Delta G/T)}{\partial T}\right]_P = -\frac{\Delta H}{T^2}$ (relates $\Delta G$ to $\Delta H$ and T).
> - ✅ **ATP hydrolysis benchmark:** $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -30.5 \text{ kJ·mol}^{-1}$; under cellular conditions, actual $\Delta G \approx -50 \text{ to } -60 \text{ kJ·mol}^{-1}$.
> - ✅ **Constants to memorize:** R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ (= 1.987 cal·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹); F = 96,485 C·mol⁻¹; 2.303 RT = 5.708 kJ·mol⁻¹ at 298 K.
> - ✅ **Sign convention of Q:** Q < 1 → $\ln Q < 0$ → reaction shifts forward; Q > 1 → $\ln Q > 0$ → reaction shifts backward.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Entropy and Heat Capacity Relation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT414 - BIOCHEMICAL THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Entropy and Heat Capacity Relation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Heat Capacity?

The **heat capacity (C)** of a system is formally defined as the infinitesimal amount of heat (δQ) that must be supplied to a system to raise its temperature by one degree (typically one Kelvin or one degree Celsius). Mathematically, it is the ratio of the heat absorbed to the resulting temperature change.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> Heat capacity is the heat required to raise the temperature of a body (or system) by one degree. Since heat (Q) is a path-dependent quantity, the value of heat capacity depends on the conditions (path) under which the heat transfer occurs.

The general mathematical expression is:

$$C = \lim_{\Delta T \to 0} \frac{\delta Q}{\Delta T} = \frac{\delta Q}{dT}$$

The **SI unit** of heat capacity is **Joules per Kelvin (J K⁻¹)** or equivalently **J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹** when expressed on a per-mole basis (molar heat capacity).

> [!NOTE]
> **Molar Heat Capacity (Cm):** It is the heat capacity of one mole of a substance. For engineering calculations in biochemical systems, molar quantities are almost always preferred over specific (per-kg) quantities because chemical reactions are quantified in moles.

## 1.2 Two Principal Varieties: Cp and Cv

Since heat is path-dependent, two principal heat capacities are defined depending on whether pressure or volume is held constant:

**A. Heat Capacity at Constant Volume (Cv):**
This is the heat required to raise the temperature of a system by one degree while keeping its **volume constant**. The corresponding heat is dQv, and:

$$C_v = \left(\frac{\partial Q}{\partial T}\right)_V = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V$$

By the First Law of Thermodynamics at constant volume (where no PV-work is done, dW = 0), δQ = dU. Therefore, the heat absorbed at constant volume is exactly equal to the change in internal energy.

**B. Heat Capacity at Constant Pressure (Cp):**
This is the heat required to raise the temperature of a system by one degree while keeping its **pressure constant**.

$$C_p = \left(\frac{\partial Q}{\partial T}\right)_P = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

At constant pressure, δQ = dH (because dH = dU + PdV + VdP, and dP = 0, so dH = dU + PdV = dQ). Hence, Cp is the rate of change of enthalpy with temperature at constant pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Significance:** Cp is ALWAYS greater than Cv. At constant pressure, the system must absorb extra heat to perform expansion work (PΔV) against the surroundings IN ADDITION to raising its internal energy. This is why Cp > Cv for all substances (except at absolute zero where they approach equality for ideal gases).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Sponge and the Balloon

Imagine you are warming two identical containers of water — one in a **rigid steel can** (constant volume) and one in a **flexible balloon** (constant pressure, expanding freely).

- In the **steel can**, all the heat you supply goes directly into increasing the kinetic energy (temperature) of the water molecules. The walls do not move, so no energy is wasted on expansion. This corresponds to **Cv**.

- In the **balloon**, you must supply heat to do two jobs: (1) raise the water's internal kinetic energy (just like the can), AND (2) push back the atmosphere as the balloon expands. The heat capacity is therefore higher — this is **Cp**.

The "extra" heat going into the expansion work is the reason why Cp > Cv, and the difference is exactly the work of expansion:

$$C_p - C_v = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P - \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V$$

For an **ideal gas**, this simplifies to the famous **Mayer's relation**:

$$C_p - C_v = R = 8.314 \text{ J K}^{-1}\text{ mol}^{-1}$$

where **R** is the **Universal Gas Constant**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Biochemical Context:** Most biochemical reactions occur in aqueous solutions at essentially constant pressure (atmospheric pressure) and very small volume changes. Hence, **Cp** is the experimentally and practically relevant heat capacity in biochemistry, and the **Entropy–Heat Capacity relation** is most commonly written in terms of Cp.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Entropy is Linked to Heat Capacity — The Logical Foundation

The **Second Law of Thermodynamics** defines entropy (S) for a reversible process as:

$$dS = \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$$

This definition shows that entropy change is fundamentally tied to heat flow. Heat capacity, being a measure of how much heat a system absorbs per unit temperature change, therefore becomes the natural bridge between thermal energy input and entropy change.

**The Core Logical Chain:**

1. Heat supplied → Temperature change (defined by heat capacity)
2. Heat supplied → Entropy change (defined by Second Law)
3. Therefore: Heat capacity → Entropy change directly

This intrinsic link gives rise to the **Entropy–Heat Capacity Relation**, which is the topic of this module.

## 2.2 Temperature Dependence of Heat Capacity

Heat capacity is **NOT a constant** for real substances. It varies with temperature. For accurate thermodynamic calculations (especially in biochemical calorimetry over a wide temperature range), this variation must be accounted for using empirical polynomial equations.

### 2.2.1 Power Series Form (Common for Engineering Gases)

$$C_p = a + bT + cT^2$$

### 2.2.2 Shomate-Style Form (Common for Condensed Phases & NIST Data)

$$C_p = a + bT + \frac{c}{T^2}$$

In the Shomate form, the cT⁻² term is particularly useful for capturing low-temperature behavior, while in the power series form, the cT² term captures high-temperature behavior.

### 2.2.3 Kistiakowsky-Type Approximation (Used in Some Estimation Methods)

For organic compounds in the gas phase, a useful approximate rule is the **Kistiakowsky rule** for Cp at 298 K:

$$C_p \text{ (J K}^{-1}\text{ mol}^{-1}) \approx \sum (\text{atomic/group contributions})$$

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, students are expected to recognize the **functional forms** of heat capacity equations and be able to integrate them to obtain entropy changes. Numerical a, b, c values are typically given in the problem.

## 2.3 The Fundamental Entropy–Heat Capacity Equations

### 2.3.1 At Constant Pressure (Most Common in Biochemistry)

Starting from the definition of Cp and the Second Law:

$$dS = \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = \frac{C_p \, dT}{T} \quad \text{(at constant P)}$$

For a finite temperature change from T₁ to T₂:

$$\Delta S = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{C_p}{T} \, dT$$

### 2.3.2 At Constant Volume

$$dS = \frac{C_v \, dT}{T} \quad \text{(at constant V)}$$

$$\Delta S = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{C_v}{T} \, dT$$

### 2.3.3 For Ideal Gases (Combined P-V-T Process)

For a reversible change in an ideal gas from state (T₁, P₁) to (T₂, P₂):

$$\Delta S = C_p \ln\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

$$\Delta S = C_v \ln\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + R \ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

These are the two most-tested formulas in the KTU Part B questions.

## 2.4 Standard Entropy (S°) and the Third Law

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Third Law of Thermodynamics (Nernst Heat Theorem):** The entropy of a perfectly crystalline pure substance at absolute zero (T = 0 K) is zero. This provides an absolute reference point for entropy.

This allows the calculation of **absolute entropies** (not just changes) at any temperature T by integrating heat capacity from 0 K:

$$S^\circ(T) = \int_0^T \frac{C_p}{T} \, dT = \int_0^T C_p \, d(\ln T)$$

The **standard molar entropy S°** at 298.15 K is the absolute entropy of one mole of a substance in its standard state at 25°C and 1 bar. Tabulated values (in J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹) are available in thermodynamic data tables.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / When to Use | Units |
|---|---------|--------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $C = \frac{\delta Q}{dT}$ | General heat capacity definition | J K⁻¹ |
| 2 | $C_v = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V$ | Constant-volume heat capacity | J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹ |
| 3 | $C_p = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P$ | Constant-pressure heat capacity | J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹ |
| 4 | $C_p - C_v = R$ | Mayer's relation (ideal gases only) | J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹ |
| 5 | $dS = \frac{C_p \, dT}{T}$ | Entropy change (constant P) | J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹ |
| 6 | $dS = \frac{C_v \, dT}{T}$ | Entropy change (constant V) | J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹ |
| 7 | $\Delta S = C_p \ln\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)$ | Isothermal P constant, T change | J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹ |
| 8 | $\Delta S = C_p \ln\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | Ideal gas, general P-T change | J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹ |
| 9 | $\Delta S = C_v \ln\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + R \ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$ | Ideal gas, general V-T change | J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹ |
| 10 | $C_p = a + bT + cT^2$ | Empirical T-dependence (power form) | J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹ |
| 11 | $C_p = a + bT + cT^{-2}$ | Empirical T-dependence (Shomate form) | J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹ |
| 12 | $S^\circ(T) = \int_0^T \frac{C_p}{T} dT$ | Absolute entropy from Third Law | J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹ |
| 13 | $\Delta S_{phase} = \frac{\Delta H_{phase}}{T_{phase}}$ | Entropy of phase transition | J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Entropy–Heat Capacity relation is the operational basis of **Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC)** — a foundational technique in biochemistry and biophysics for measuring protein folding/unfolding thermodynamics, lipid phase transitions, and DNA melting. The area under a Cp vs. T curve, when divided by T (and integrated), directly yields the entropy change.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: dS = Cp dT / T (The Foundational Identity)

This is the single most important derivation in this module. It connects the two great laws of thermodynamics through heat capacity.

**Starting Point: The First Law of Thermodynamics**

For a closed system undergoing a reversible process:

$$dU = \delta Q_{rev} - \delta W_{rev}$$

For a system doing only PV-work (the most common case in biochemical thermodynamics):

$$\delta W_{rev} = P \, dV$$

Therefore:

$$dU = \delta Q_{rev} - P \, dV$$

**Step 1: Express dU in terms of U(T, V)**

Since U is a state function of T and V, its total differential is:

$$dU = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V dT + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T dV$$

By the **definition of Cv**:

$$C_v = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V$$

Substituting:

$$dU = C_v \, dT + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T dV$$

**Step 2: Equate the Two Expressions for dU**

$$C_v \, dT + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T dV = \delta Q_{rev} - P \, dV$$

Solving for δQrev:

$$\delta Q_{rev} = C_v \, dT + \left[ \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T + P \right] dV$$

**Step 3: Apply the Second Law**

$$dS = \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = \frac{C_v}{T} dT + \frac{1}{T} \left[ \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T + P \right] dV$$

**Step 4: Impose the Constant-Pressure Condition**

At constant pressure, dP = 0. This is the practical condition for most biochemical processes. The coefficient in front of dT must therefore be evaluated at dP = 0. Using the chain rule, the entropy differential becomes:

$$dS = \frac{C_p}{T} dT \quad \text{(at constant P)}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\,dS = \frac{C_p \, dT}{T}\,}$$

**Interpretation:** The entropy change per unit temperature change is simply the heat capacity divided by the absolute temperature. The higher the heat capacity (more "thermal inertia" of the system) and the lower the temperature, the larger the entropy change for a given temperature step.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Entropy Change for a Temperature Change (Constant Pressure)

To go from a differential to a finite entropy change, we integrate between T₁ and T₂.

**Step 1: Set Up the Integral**

From dS = Cp dT / T:

$$\Delta S = S_2 - S_1 = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{C_p}{T} dT$$

**Step 2: If Cp is Constant (Simplest Case)**

Pull Cp out of the integral (since it does not depend on T):

$$\Delta S = C_p \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T} = C_p \left[ \ln T \right]_{T_1}^{T_2}$$

$$\boxed{\,\Delta S = C_p \ln\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) \quad \text{(constant Cp, constant P)}\,}$$

**Step 3: If Cp Varies Linearly (Cp = a + bT)**

$$\Delta S = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{a + bT}{T} dT = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \left(\frac{a}{T} + b\right) dT$$

$$= a \left[ \ln T \right]_{T_1}^{T_2} + b \left[ T \right]_{T_1}^{T_2}$$

$$\boxed{\,\Delta S = a \ln\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + b(T_2 - T_1) \quad \text{(Cp = a + bT)}\,}$$

**Step 4: If Cp Varies as Shomate Form (Cp = a + bT + cT⁻²)**

$$\Delta S = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{a + bT + cT^{-2}}{T} dT = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \left(\frac{a}{T} + b + \frac{c}{T^3}\right) dT$$

$$= a \ln\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + b(T_2 - T_1) - \frac{c}{2}\left(\frac{1}{T_2^2} - \frac{1}{T_1^2}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention Check:** If T₂ > T₁ (heating), then ΔS > 0 (entropy increases). This is consistent with the physical intuition that adding heat to a system increases its molecular disorder.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Entropy of an Ideal Gas for a General P-T Change

This is the workhorse formula for ideal gas problems in KTU exams.

**Step 1: Set Up the Total Differential of S(T, P)**

Since S is a state function of T and P:

$$dS = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T dP$$

**Step 2: Evaluate the First Partial Derivative**

From dS = Cp dT / T at constant P:

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{C_p}{T}$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the Second Partial Derivative Using a Maxwell Relation**

A standard Maxwell relation derived from the Gibbs free energy gives:

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

For an ideal gas, V = nRT / P, so:

$$\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{nR}{P}$$

Therefore:

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\frac{nR}{P}$$

**Step 4: Combine and Integrate**

$$dS = \frac{C_p}{T} dT - \frac{nR}{P} dP$$

Integrating from state 1 (T₁, P₁) to state 2 (T₂, P₂):

$$\Delta S = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{C_p}{T} dT - \int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{nR}{P} dP$$

If Cp is approximately constant over the temperature range:

$$\boxed{\,\Delta S = C_p \ln\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - nR \ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \quad \text{(ideal gas, constant Cp)}\,}$$

**Per-mole version** (dividing by n, and replacing nR with R for one mole):

$$\Delta S_m = C_p \ln\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Kirchhoff's Equation (for ΔH(T) — Foundation for ΔS via Cp)

Although strictly a derivation for enthalpy change, **Kirchhoff's equation** is included here because it is the conceptual twin of the Entropy–Heat Capacity relation and frequently tested together.

**Step 1: Define Heat Capacity Difference**

$$\Delta C_p = C_p(\text{products}) - C_p(\text{reactants})$$

**Step 2: Use the Identity d(ΔH)/dT = ΔCp**

From H(T) = H(298) + ∫Cp dT, differentiate both sides of the reaction:

$$\frac{d(\Delta H)}{dT} = \Delta C_p$$

**Step 3: Integrate from 298 K to T**

$$\Delta H(T) = \Delta H(298) + \int_{298}^{T} \Delta C_p \, dT$$

For a constant ΔCp:

$$\boxed{\,\Delta H(T) = \Delta H(298) + \Delta C_p (T - 298)\,}$$

**Step 4: Analogous Equation for Entropy**

By identical logic (since dS = Cp dT / T):

$$\Delta S(T) = \Delta S(298) + \int_{298}^{T} \frac{\Delta C_p}{T} dT$$

For a constant ΔCp:

$$\boxed{\,\Delta S(T) = \Delta S(298) + \Delta C_p \ln\left(\frac{T}{298}\right)\,}$$

This pair of equations is essential for calculating thermodynamic quantities at non-standard temperatures (e.g., body temperature 310 K, instead of standard 298 K).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Worked Example

**Problem:** The molar heat capacity of a certain protein in solution is given by Cp (J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹) = 5000 + 10T, where T is in Kelvin. Calculate the entropy change when this protein is heated from 300 K to 350 K at constant pressure.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the heat capacity function. Here, a = 5000, b = 10, c = 0.

$$C_p = 5000 + 10T \quad \text{J K}^{-1}\text{ mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the entropy change formula with variable Cp:

$$\Delta S = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{C_p}{T} dT = \int_{300}^{350} \frac{5000 + 10T}{T} dT$$

**Step 3:** Split the integrand:

$$\Delta S = \int_{300}^{350} \left(\frac{5000}{T} + 10\right) dT$$

**Step 4:** Integrate term by term:

$$\Delta S = 5000 \left[ \ln T \right]_{300}^{350} + 10 \left[ T \right]_{300}^{350}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the limits:

$$\Delta S = 5000 \ln\left(\frac{350}{300}\right) + 10(350 - 300)$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate numerically:

$$\frac{350}{300} = 1.1\overline{6}, \quad \ln(1.1\overline{6}) \approx 0.15415$$

$$\Delta S = 5000 \times 0.15415 + 10 \times 50$$

$$\Delta S = 770.77 + 500$$

$$\boxed{\,\Delta S \approx 1270.77 \text{ J K}^{-1}\text{ mol}^{-1}\,}$$

**Verification:** The result is positive (heating → entropy increases ✓). The magnitude is reasonable for a large biomolecule (proteins have Cp values several thousand J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹, much larger than small molecules due to their complex internal degrees of freedom).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow: From Heat Input to Entropy Change

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the logical chain from heat capacity measurement to entropy calculation, which is the core operational flow used in **Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC)** and related biochemical techniques.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System at Temperature T1] --> B[Heat Input deltaQ]
    B --> C[Heat Capacity Cp governs temperature response]
    C --> D[Temperature rises to T2]
    A --> E[Entropy S1 at T1]
    D --> F[Entropy S2 at T2]
    E --> G[Second Law dS = deltaQrev / T]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Integrate Cp dT / T from T1 to T2]
    H --> I[Final Entropy Change deltaS]
    I --> J{Is Cp constant?}
    J -->|Yes| K[deltaS = Cp ln T2 / T1]
    J -->|No| L[Integrate a/T plus b plus c/T^3]
    L --> M[deltaS = a ln T2/T1 plus b T2 minus T1 plus c/2 times 1/T1^2 minus 1/T2^2]
    K --> N[Output in J K^-1 mol^-1]
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture: Variable Heat Capacity Integration Pathways

This second diagram maps out the complete decision tree for handling different forms of heat capacity functions encountered in KTU problems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Cp as a function of T] --> B{Functional form?}
    B -->|Constant| C[Cp = constant]
    B -->|Linear| D[Cp = a + bT]
    B -->|Quadratic| E[Cp = a + bT + cT^2]
    B -->|Shomate| F[Cp = a + bT + cT to power -2]
    C --> G[deltaS = Cp times ln T2 over T1]
    D --> H[deltaS = a ln T2 over T1 plus b times T2 minus T1]
    E --> I[deltaS = a ln T2 over T1 plus b times T2 minus T1 plus c by 2 times T2 squared minus T1 squared]
    F --> J[deltaS = a ln T2 over T1 plus b times T2 minus T1 plus c by 2 times 1 over T1 squared minus 1 over T2 squared]
    G --> K[Add any phase transition contributions: deltaH phase / T phase]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Final deltaS with sign check: positive for heating]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Interconnection Between U, H, S, Cp, and Cv

This diagram shows how the various thermodynamic properties and heat capacities are mathematically interconnected, which is essential for understanding the entire module's framework.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Internal Energy U] -->|constant V| B[Cv = dU by dT]
    C[Enthalpy H] -->|constant P| D[Cp = dH by dT]
    A -->|H = U + PV| C
    B -->|Mayer Cp minus Cv equals R for ideal gas| D
    A -->|Second Law dS = deltaQ / T| E[Entropy S]
    C -->|Second Law at constant P| E
    D -->|dS = Cp dT over T| E
    B -->|dS = Cv dT over T| E
    E -->|Third Law S zero equals zero at zero K| F[Absolute Entropy S degree T]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each arrow in the flowcharts represents a mathematical relationship (an equation or identity). Following the arrows from "Given" to "Final Answer" gives the student a structured problem-solving roadmap for KTU exam questions.
