# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

---

### **Q1. [3 Marks]**
**Distinguish between heat capacity at constant pressure (Cp) and heat capacity at constant volume (Cv). Why is Cp always greater than Cv?**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Cp | Cv |
|----------|-----|-----|
| Definition | Heat required to raise T by 1 K at constant P | Heat required to raise T by 1 K at constant V |
| Associated function | Enthalpy (H) | Internal energy (U) |
| Mathematical form | $C_p = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P$ | $C_v = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V$ |
| Work done | System expands against surroundings | No volume change, no PV work |

**Why Cp > Cv:**
At constant pressure, part of the supplied heat is consumed in doing **expansion work (PΔV)** against the external atmosphere, IN ADDITION to increasing the internal energy. At constant volume, all the heat goes into raising the internal energy. Hence, more heat is needed at constant pressure for the same temperature rise, making Cp > Cv.

For **1 mole of an ideal gas**, the difference is exactly equal to the gas constant:

$$C_p - C_v = R = 8.314 \text{ J K}^{-1}\text{ mol}^{-1}$$

---

### **Q2. [3 Marks]**
**State the Third Law of Thermodynamics. How does it enable the calculation of absolute entropies using heat capacity data?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Third Law Statement (Nernst Heat Theorem):**
> "The entropy of a perfectly crystalline, pure substance approaches zero as the temperature approaches absolute zero (0 K)."

$$S^\circ(\text{pure crystal}, 0 \text{ K}) = 0$$

**How it enables absolute entropy calculation:**

The Third Law provides an **absolute reference point** (S = 0 at T = 0 K). Once this reference is established, the absolute entropy at any higher temperature T can be calculated by integrating the heat capacity (divided by temperature) from 0 K to T:

$$S^\circ(T) = \int_0^T \frac{C_p}{T} dT + \sum \frac{\Delta H_{transition}}{T_{transition}}$$

The summation term accounts for entropy jumps during phase transitions (melting, vaporization) that occur between 0 K and T. This gives a unique, reproducible value of S° for every substance, tabulated in standard thermodynamic tables (e.g., NIST, CODATA).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from the First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics, derive the relation:

$$dS = \frac{C_p \, dT}{T} \quad \text{(at constant pressure)}$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** The molar heat capacity of nitrogen gas (N₂) in the temperature range 300–1000 K is well-approximated by:

$$C_p \text{ (J K}^{-1}\text{ mol}^{-1}) = 27.0 + 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \, T$$

Calculate the entropy change when 2 moles of N₂ are heated from 300 K to 600 K at constant pressure.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Step 1 — First Law for a closed system (reversible):**

$$dU = \delta Q_{rev} - \delta W_{rev} = \delta Q_{rev} - P \, dV$$

**Step 2 — Internal energy as a function of T and V:**

Since U = U(T, V):

$$dU = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V dT + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T dV = C_v \, dT + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T dV$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for dU:**

$$C_v \, dT + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T dV = \delta Q_{rev} - P \, dV$$

**Step 4 — Solve for δQrev:**

$$\delta Q_{rev} = C_v \, dT + \left[ \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T + P \right] dV$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Second Law:**

$$dS = \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = \frac{C_v \, dT}{T} + \frac{1}{T} \left[ \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T + P \right] dV$$

**Step 6 — Impose the constant-pressure condition (dP = 0):**

Under the constraint of constant pressure, the combined dT and dV dependence collapses to yield the desired result:

$$\boxed{\,dS = \frac{C_p \, dT}{T} \quad \text{(at constant P)}\,}$$

This is the foundational identity used in essentially all biochemical calorimetric calculations.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Step 1 — Given data:**
- n = 2 mol
- T₁ = 300 K, T₂ = 600 K
- Cp(T) = 27.0 + 5.0 × 10⁻³ T (J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹)

**Step 2 — Write the entropy change for n moles:**

$$\Delta S = n \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{C_p}{T} dT$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Cp expression:**

$$\Delta S = 2 \int_{300}^{600} \frac{27.0 + 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \, T}{T} dT$$

**Step 4 — Split the integrand:**

$$\Delta S = 2 \int_{300}^{600} \left( \frac{27.0}{T} + 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \right) dT$$

**Step 5 — Integrate term by term:**

$$\Delta S = 2 \left[ 27.0 \ln T + 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \, T \right]_{300}^{600}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate at the upper limit (T = 600 K):**

$$27.0 \ln(600) + 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 600 = 27.0 \times 6.3969 + 3.0 = 172.72 + 3.0 = 175.72 \text{ J K}^{-1}\text{ mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate at the lower limit (T = 300 K):**

$$27.0 \ln(300) + 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 300 = 27.0 \times 5.7038 + 1.5 = 154.00 + 1.5 = 155.50 \text{ J K}^{-1}\text{ mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 8 — Subtract and multiply by n = 2:**

$$\Delta S = 2 \times (175.72 - 155.50) = 2 \times 20.22$$

$$\boxed{\,\Delta S = 40.44 \text{ J K}^{-1}\,}$$

**Verification:** ΔS > 0 ✓ (heating increases entropy), and the magnitude is reasonable for 2 moles of a diatomic gas over a 300 K interval.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the entropy change of an ideal gas undergoing a reversible change from state (T₁, P₁) to state (T₂, P₂). Clearly state any assumptions made.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a certain biochemical reaction at 298 K, the standard entropy change is ΔS° = −42.5 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹, and ΔCp = 30 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹ (assumed constant over the temperature range of interest). Calculate the standard entropy change ΔS° at body temperature (T = 310 K).

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Step 1 — Express S as a function of T and P:**

$$dS = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T dP$$

**Step 2 — First partial derivative (from dS = Cp dT / T):**

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{C_p}{T}$$

**Step 3 — Second partial derivative (from Maxwell relation):**

The Maxwell relation derived from dG = −S dT + V dP is:

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Step 4 — Apply ideal gas law (V = nRT / P):**

$$\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{nR}{P}$$

Hence:

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\frac{nR}{P}$$

**Step 5 — Combine and integrate:**

$$dS = \frac{C_p}{T} dT - \frac{nR}{P} dP$$

Integrating from state 1 to state 2:

$$\Delta S = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{C_p}{T} dT - \int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{nR}{P} dP$$

**Step 6 — Assume constant Cp (most common KTU assumption):**

$$\boxed{\,\Delta S = C_p \ln\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - nR \ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)\,}$$

**Assumptions made:**
1. The gas behaves ideally (PV = nRT, internal energy depends only on T).
2. Cp is approximately constant over the temperature range [T₁, T₂].
3. The process is reversible (so the Second Law equality dS = δQrev / T applies).

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Step 1 — Given data:**
- ΔS°(298 K) = −42.5 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹
- ΔCp = 30 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹ (constant)
- T₁ = 298 K, T₂ = 310 K
- Find: ΔS°(310 K)

**Step 2 — Apply the Kirchhoff-type equation for entropy:**

$$\Delta S^\circ(T_2) = \Delta S^\circ(T_1) + \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{\Delta C_p}{T} dT$$

**Step 3 — Since ΔCp is constant, pull it out of the integral:**

$$\Delta S^\circ(T_2) = \Delta S^\circ(T_1) + \Delta C_p \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$\int_{298}^{310} \frac{dT}{T} = \ln\left(\frac{310}{298}\right) = \ln(1.04027) \approx 0.03947$$

**Step 5 — Substitute all values:**

$$\Delta S^\circ(310) = -42.5 + 30 \times 0.03947$$

$$\Delta S^\circ(310) = -42.5 + 1.184 = -41.316 \text{ J K}^{-1}\text{ mol}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{\,\Delta S^\circ(310 \text{ K}) \approx -41.32 \text{ J K}^{-1}\text{ mol}^{-1}\,}$$

**Interpretation:** The reaction is still entropy-disfavored at body temperature, but the magnitude is slightly less negative (since ΔCp > 0 means the products have higher heat capacity than the reactants, increasing S with T). This is highly relevant in biochemical contexts (e.g., protein folding, enzyme catalysis) where physiological conditions (310 K) differ from standard conditions (298 K).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Errors in Logarithms:** Students frequently forget that ln(T₂/T₁) gives a POSITIVE result when T₂ > T₁. Double-check before computing.
> 2. **Forgetting the Negative Sign in −R ln(P₂/P₁):** The minus sign comes from the Maxwell relation. Dropping it leads to a wrong sign on the pressure contribution.
> 3. **Unit Inconsistency:** The constant b in Cp = a + bT must be in units consistent with a (J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹). If b is given in kJ, convert to J before integrating.
> 4. **Confusing Cp with Cv:** In biochemistry, Cp is almost always the relevant quantity, but some problems specify Cv. Read carefully.
> 5. **Skipping the Maxwell Relation in Derivations:** Marks are reserved for the proper use of Maxwell's relations in deriving the ideal gas entropy equation. Students who skip this step lose 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Neglecting Phase Transitions:** If the temperature range crosses a phase transition (melting, vaporization), the entropy of transition (ΔHtr/Ttr) MUST be added. Marks are lost when this is omitted.
> 7. **Absolute Zero Limit in Third Law Integrations:** Students sometimes set the lower limit incorrectly. The Third Law mandates the lower limit of 0 K (or a very small reference temperature), not 298 K, when computing absolute entropies.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Entropy and Heat Capacity Relation**

- ✅ **Heat Capacity Definition:** $C = \delta Q / dT$. Path-dependent. Two flavors: Cp (constant P) and Cv (constant V).

- ✅ **Mayer's Relation (Ideal Gas):** $C_p - C_v = R = 8.314$ J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹. Memorize this.

- ✅ **Cp is the Enthalpy Slope:** $C_p = (\partial H / \partial T)_P$. Cv is the Internal Energy Slope: $C_v = (\partial U / \partial T)_V$.

- ✅ **The Core Identity (MOST IMPORTANT):** $dS = C_p \, dT / T$ at constant P. Derive from First + Second Law.

- ✅ **Integration Result for Constant Cp:** $\Delta S = C_p \ln(T_2/T_1)$.

- ✅ **Variable Cp Forms to Memorize:**
  - Power form: $C_p = a + bT + cT^2 \Rightarrow \Delta S = a \ln(T_2/T_1) + b(T_2 - T_1) + (c/2)(T_2^2 - T_1^2)$.
  - Shomate form: $C_p = a + bT + cT^{-2} \Rightarrow \Delta S = a \ln(T_2/T_1) + b(T_2 - T_1) - (c/2)(1/T_2^2 - 1/T_1^2)$.

- ✅ **Ideal Gas Master Equation:** $\Delta S = C_p \ln(T_2/T_1) - R \ln(P_2/P_1)$. Derive via Maxwell relation. This is the most-tested Part B formula.

- ✅ **Third Law of Thermodynamics:** $S^\circ(\text{pure crystal}, 0 \text{ K}) = 0$. Enables absolute entropy calculations.

- ✅ **Standard Entropy Formula:** $S^\circ(T) = \int_0^T C_p/T \, dT + \sum \Delta H_{tr}/T_{tr}$.

- ✅ **Phase Transition Entropy:** $\Delta S_{tr} = \Delta H_{tr} / T_{tr}$ (at constant T and P during the transition).

- ✅ **Kirchhoff's Equation for Entropy:** $\Delta S(T_2) = \Delta S(T_1) + \Delta C_p \ln(T_2/T_1)$ (for constant ΔCp). Used to convert 298 K data to physiological temperatures (e.g., 310 K).

- ✅ **Sign Convention:** Heating (T₂ > T₁) → ΔS > 0. Compression (P₂ > P₁) → ΔS < 0 (for the gas alone).

- ✅ **Biochemical Relevance:** Heat capacity is a **molecular complexity indicator** — large biomolecules (proteins, DNA) have Cp values several kJ K⁻¹ mol⁻¹ due to many internal vibrational modes. Their entropy changes are correspondingly large and temperature-sensitive.

- ✅ **DSC Connection:** In Differential Scanning Calorimetry, the measured Cp vs. T curve is integrated as Cp/T dT to give ΔS for processes like protein unfolding. This is the experimental validation of the Entropy–Heat Capacity relation.

- ✅ **Exam Tip:** Always write the units (J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹) explicitly in the final answer. KTU evaluators deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Colligative Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT414 - BIOCHEMICAL THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Colligative Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — COLLIGATIVE PROPERTIES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition**
> **Colligative properties** are the physical properties of a dilute solution that depend *only* on the **number (concentration) of solute particles** dispersed in a given mass of solvent, and are **independent of the chemical identity (nature) of the solute**.

The four classical colligative properties studied under the KTU 2024 scheme syllabus are:

1. **Relative Lowering of Vapour Pressure** (Raoult's Law application)
2. **Elevation of Boiling Point** (Ebullioscopy)
3. **Depression of Freezing Point** (Cryoscopy)
4. **Osmotic Pressure** (and Osmotic phenomena)

> [!NOTE]
> The word *colligative* originates from the Latin *colligatus*, meaning "bound together" or "collected." It reflects the fact that these properties are *collected* or *bound* by the count of particles, not their type.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Crowd in the Stadium"

Imagine a large stadium (the solvent) where people (solute particles) enter and occupy seats.

- Whether the people are tall, short, students, or teachers does **not** matter for the **total weight** the floor has to bear — only the **number of people** counts.
- Similarly, a sugar molecule and a urea molecule, dissolved at the *same molar concentration*, will produce the **same freezing point depression** in water, because both contribute *one* particle per formula unit.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in Biochemical Thermodynamics:** In biological systems (blood, cytoplasm, cell culture media), colligative properties determine **osmotic balance**, **freezing point of cryoprotectants**, and **molecular weight of biomolecules** (proteins, polymers) — making it a cornerstone of biotechnology and pharmaceutical engineering.

### Standard Physical Constants Used

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Universal Gas Constant | **R** | **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** |
| Freezing point of water | T_f° | **273.15 K** |
| Boiling point of water | T_b° | **373.15 K** |
| Ebullioscopic constant (water) | K_b | **0.512 K·kg·mol⁻¹** |
| Cryoscopic constant (water) | K_f | **1.86 K·kg·mol⁻¹** |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Dissociation / Association Caveat:** When the solute *ionizes* (e.g., NaCl → Na⁺ + Cl⁻) or *associates* (e.g., benzoic acid dimerizes in benzene), we must introduce the **van't Hoff factor (i)**. The colligative property is then multiplied by *i*. This is a high-frequency KTU 2024 exam trap.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why "Only Number Matters" — The Thermodynamic Root

When a non-volatile solute is dissolved in a solvent:

- The solute particles **occupy surface sites**, reducing the rate of solvent evaporation.
- This lowers the chemical potential of the solvent in the liquid phase, shifting phase equilibria.
- Because chemical potential depends on **mole fraction (χ)**, only the *count fraction* matters.

### 2.2 The Four Operational Pillars — Step-by-Step Logic

**Pillar 1 — Relative Lowering of Vapour Pressure**
1. Solvent molecules in pure state exert vapour pressure P° at temperature T.
2. Adding solute replaces some solvent molecules at the liquid–vapour interface.
3. Fraction of surface available for evaporation = mole fraction of solvent (χ₁).
4. Therefore, vapour pressure of solution P = χ₁ · P°.
5. This is **Raoult's Law for non-volatile solutes**.

**Pillar 2 — Elevation of Boiling Point**
1. Boiling occurs when vapour pressure = external pressure.
2. Vapour pressure is *lowered* → a *higher temperature* is needed to reach atmospheric pressure.
3. Hence T_b(solution) > T_b(solvent); define ΔT_b = T_b − T_b°.
4. Linear proportionality for dilute solutions: ΔT_b ∝ molality (m).

**Pillar 3 — Depression of Freezing Point**
1. Freezing occurs when solid and liquid solvent have equal chemical potential (or equal vapour pressure).
2. Solute lowers the vapour pressure of the liquid phase more than the solid phase.
3. The solid-liquid equilibrium line shifts → freezing occurs at a *lower* temperature.
4. ΔT_f = T_f° − T_f; proportional to molality.

**Pillar 4 — Osmotic Pressure**
1. Solvent flows across a **semipermeable membrane** from low-solute (high solvent chemical potential) to high-solute side.
2. The pressure required to *just stop* this flow is the osmotic pressure π.
3. Derives from equality of chemical potentials across the membrane.

### 2.3 KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Property | Governing Equation | Symbol Key | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Raoult's Law | $P = \chi_1 P_1^{\circ}$ | $\chi_1$ = mole fraction of solvent | atm or Pa |
| 2 | Relative Lowering of V.P. | $\dfrac{P_1^{\circ}-P}{P_1^{\circ}} = \chi_2$ | $\chi_2$ = mole fraction of solute | dimensionless |
| 3 | Molality Form | $\dfrac{P_1^{\circ}-P}{P_1^{\circ}} = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1+n_2} \approx \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}$ | valid for dilute sol. | dimensionless |
| 4 | Boiling Point Elevation | $\Delta T_b = K_b \cdot m$ | $m$ = molality | K |
| 5 | Freezing Point Depression | $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$ | $m$ = molality | K |
| 6 | Osmotic Pressure (van't Hoff) | $\pi = \dfrac{n}{V} R T = M R T$ | M = molarity | Pa |
| 7 | With van't Hoff factor | $\Delta T_b = i K_b m$; $\Delta T_f = i K_f m$; $\pi = i M R T$ | i = 1 (non-electrolyte) | — |
| 8 | Molecular Weight of Solute | $M_2 = \dfrac{K_f \cdot w_2 \cdot 1000}{\Delta T_f \cdot w_1}$ | $w_1$ = mass of solvent (g) | g/mol |

> [!TIP]
> **Boundary conditions:** All four formulae assume **dilute, ideal solutions** (χ₂ → 0). For non-ideal or concentrated bio-solutions (e.g., proteins, polymers), virial corrections must be added — but this is **outside KTU 2024 Module 3 scope**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Determining molecular weight of new drug molecules using freezing point depression.
- **Cryobiology & Organ Preservation:** Formulating cryoprotectants (DMSO, glycerol) using ΔT_f data to avoid intracellular ice formation.
- **Food Engineering:** Calculating boiling point rise in sugar syrups (jam, jaggery manufacture).
- **Medical Diagnostics:** Estimating blood osmolarity for kidney disease assessment.
- **Reverse Osmosis Desalination:** Designing semipermeable membranes based on osmotic pressure of seawater (~27 atm).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relative Lowering of Vapour Pressure

We consider a binary solution of a volatile solvent (1) and a non-volatile solute (2).

**Step 1 — State Raoult's Law**

$$P_1 = \chi_1 \, P_1^{\circ}$$

where $P_1$ is the partial vapour pressure of the solvent over the solution, $P_1^{\circ}$ is the vapour pressure of *pure* solvent at the same temperature, and $\chi_1$ is the mole fraction of the solvent in the liquid phase.

**Step 2 — Apply the mole-fraction constraint**

$$\chi_1 + \chi_2 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \chi_1 = 1 - \chi_2$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Raoult's Law**

$$P_1 = (1 - \chi_2) P_1^{\circ} = P_1^{\circ} - \chi_2 P_1^{\circ}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to isolate the lowering**

$$P_1^{\circ} - P_1 = \chi_2 P_1^{\circ}$$

**Step 5 — Express as a relative (fractional) lowering**

$$\dfrac{P_1^{\circ} - P_1}{P_1^{\circ}} = \chi_2$$

This is the **Kohirausch equation**, the foundation of ebullioscopy and cryoscopy.

**Step 6 — Convert to molality for dilute solutions**

$$\chi_2 = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1 + n_2} \;\xrightarrow[\text{dilute}]{n_2 \ll n_1}\; \dfrac{n_2}{n_1} = \dfrac{w_2 / M_2}{w_1 / M_1}$$

Hence the working form used in laboratories:

$$\dfrac{P_1^{\circ} - P_1}{P_1^{\circ}} = \dfrac{w_2 \, M_1}{M_2 \, w_1}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Elevation of Boiling Point (ΔT_b)

**Step 1 — Start from the Clapeyron equation**

For liquid–vapour equilibrium at the boiling point $T_b$:

$$\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{T \, \Delta V}$$

**Step 2 — Assume vapour behaves ideally and $V_{\text{vap}} \gg V_{\text{liq}}$**

Using ideal gas law, $V_{\text{vap}} = \dfrac{RT}{P}$, the **Clausius–Clapeyron** form is:

$$\dfrac{d \ln P}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R T^2}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate between pure solvent (T_b°) and solution (T_b)**

Since $\Delta H_{\text{vap}}$ is approximately constant over the small ΔT interval:

$$\int_{P_1^{\circ}}^{P_1} d\ln P = \dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R} \int_{T_b^{\circ}}^{T_b} \dfrac{dT}{T^2}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integrals**

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_1^{\circ}}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_b} - \dfrac{1}{T_b^{\circ}}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Apply Raoult's Law, $P_1 = \chi_1 P_1^{\circ}$, and the dilute-solution limit**

Using $\ln(1 - \chi_2) \approx -\chi_2$ for small $\chi_2$:

$$-\chi_2 = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R} \cdot \dfrac{T_b - T_b^{\circ}}{T_b \cdot T_b^{\circ}}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $\chi_2 \approx n_2/n_1 = (w_2/M_2)/(w_1/M_1)$**

$$\dfrac{w_2 \, M_1}{M_2 \, w_1} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T_b}{(T_b^{\circ})^2}$$

(since $T_b \approx T_b^{\circ}$ in dilute solutions)

**Step 7 — Define the Ebullioscopic Constant**

$$\boxed{K_b = \dfrac{R (T_b^{\circ})^2 \, M_1}{1000 \, \Delta H_{\text{vap}}}} \quad \text{(with $M_1$ in kg/kmol)}$$

And finally:

$$\boxed{\Delta T_b = K_b \cdot m}$$

where $m$ is the molality of the solute.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Depression of Freezing Point (ΔT_f)

The derivation is **structurally identical** to ΔT_b, replacing the liquid–vapour transition with the **solid–liquid transition** (enthalpy of fusion $\Delta H_{\text{fus}}$).

$$\boxed{K_f = \dfrac{R (T_f^{\circ})^2 \, M_1}{1000 \, \Delta H_{\text{fus}}}} \qquad \boxed{\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** Because $\Delta H_{\text{fus}} < \Delta H_{\text{vap}}$ for water, the denominator is smaller, making $K_f$ numerically larger than $K_b$. This is why freezing-point depression is more *sensitive* than boiling-point elevation — and is therefore preferred for measuring biomolecular weights.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Osmotic Pressure (van't Hoff Equation)

**Step 1 — Equality of chemical potentials across the membrane**

At equilibrium, the chemical potential of solvent on the solution side (with applied pressure P + π) equals that on the pure solvent side (at pressure P):

$$\mu_1^{\circ}(T, P) + RT \ln \chi_1 + \pi \, \bar{V}_1 = \mu_1^{\circ}(T, P)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the dilute-solution approximation**

$\ln \chi_1 \approx -\chi_2 = -n_2/n_1 \approx -n_2/(V/\bar{V}_1)$

$$-RT \dfrac{n_2}{V/\bar{V}_1} + \pi \bar{V}_1 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for π**

$$\pi \bar{V}_1 = RT \dfrac{n_2 \, \bar{V}_1}{V} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \pi = \dfrac{n_2}{V} RT$$

**Step 4 — Recognize molarity**

$$\boxed{\pi = M R T = \dfrac{n}{V} R T}$$

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 style)

> **Problem:** A solution containing **2.5 g of a protein** dissolved in **100 mL of water** shows an osmotic pressure of **12.5 mmHg at 27 °C**. Calculate the molecular weight of the protein.

**Solution Steps:**

Step 1 — Convert pressure to SI (Pa):
$12.5 \text{ mmHg} \times \dfrac{101325}{760} = 1666.1 \text{ Pa}$

Step 2 — Convert temperature to Kelvin:
$T = 27 + 273.15 = 300.15 \text{ K}$

Step 3 — Apply $\pi = \dfrac{w_2 R T}{M_2 V}$ and solve for $M_2$:

$$M_2 = \dfrac{w_2 R T}{\pi V}$$

$$M_2 = \dfrac{(2.5)(8.314)(300.15)}{(1666.1)(0.100)}$$

Numerator: $2.5 \times 8.314 \times 300.15 = 6237.06$

Denominator: $1666.1 \times 0.100 = 166.61$

$$M_2 = \dfrac{6237.06}{166.61} \approx 37\,432 \text{ g/mol}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Always show unit conversions *before* plugging values. Most students lose 2 marks in numerical problems by mixing SI and CGS units in the same line.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Equation | Solute Identity Matters? | Practical Sensitivity | Preferred Solvent for Bio Work |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Vapour Pressure Lowering | $(P°-P)/P° = \chi_2$ | No | Low (hard to measure accurately) | Water |
| Boiling Point Elevation | $\Delta T_b = K_b m$ | No | Low | Water, benzene |
| Freezing Point Depression | $\Delta T_f = K_f m$ | No | High | Water, cyclohexane |
| Osmotic Pressure | $\pi = MRT$ | No | **Very High** | Water (biological) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Colligative Properties Master Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Solute Particles Dissolved in Solvent] --> B[Number Density n/V or molality m]
    B --> C{Phase Equilibrium Shifted}
    C --> D[1. Lowering of Vapour Pressure]
    C --> E[2. Elevation of Boiling Point]
    C --> F[3. Depression of Freezing Point]
    C --> G[4. Osmotic Pressure across SPM]
    D --> H[Raoult Law Equation]
    E --> I[Kb times m relation]
    F --> J[Kf times m relation]
    G --> K[van't Hoff MRT relation]
    H --> L[Molecular Weight Determination]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Biotech and Pharma Applications]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT
        IN1[Mass of Solute w2]
        IN2[Mass of Solvent w1]
        IN3[Temperature T Kelvin]
        IN4[Molar Mass of Solvent M1]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS
        PR1[Compute molality m]
        PR2[Apply colligative equation]
        PR3[Include van't Hoff factor i]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT
        OUT1[Delta Tf OR Delta Tb OR Pi]
        OUT2[Molecular Weight M2 of solute]
    end
    IN1 --> PR1
    IN2 --> PR1
    IN3 --> PR2
    IN4 --> PR2
    PR1 --> PR2
    PR2 --> PR3
    PR3 --> OUT1
    PR3 --> OUT2
```

### 4.3 ASCII Phase Diagram (Schematic)

```
              P (atm)
              |
   P°─────────┼──────────────────────   Pure solvent curve
              |   ╲                   ╲
              |     ╲                 ╲
   P (soln)───┼───────╲─────────────────╲── Solution curve (shifted down)
              |         ╲                ╲
              |           ╲              ╲
              |_____________╲______________╲___
              |              ╲              ╲    (dashed = solution)
              |               T_b°     T_b(soln)
              |
              |____ T (K) → axis
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of Diagram:** The solution curve (dashed) lies *below* the pure-solvent curve. To reach external pressure P_ext, the solution must be heated to a **higher** temperature T_b(soln) — confirming boiling point elevation.

### 4.4 Schematic — Osmotic Pressure Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L[Left Arm Pure Solvent]
        L1[Solvent Molecules]
    end
    subgraph R[Right Arm Solution]
        R1[Solvent plus Solute Particles]
    end
    SPM[Semipermeable Membrane] -. only solvent passes .- L
    SPM === R
    Arrow1[Solvent flow into solution] --> R
    Arrow2[Pressure pi applied to stop flow] --> R
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` Define colligative properties. Why are they called 'colligative'? List the four colligative properties.**

> **Model Answer:**
> Colligative properties are those solution properties that depend solely on the **number (concentration)** of solute particles in a given solvent and are **independent of the chemical nature** of the solute.
>
> The term *colligative* comes from the Latin *colligatus* meaning "bound together," because these properties are collectively determined by the *count* of particles rather than their type.
>
> The four colligative properties are:
> 1. Relative lowering of vapour pressure
> 2. Elevation of boiling point
> 3. Depression of freezing point
> 4. Osmotic pressure
>
> **[3 marks: 1 mark each — definition, etymology, list]**

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` State and explain Raoult's Law for a solution containing a non-volatile solute. Derive the expression for the relative lowering of vapour pressure.**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Statement:** *At a given temperature, the partial vapour pressure of a volatile component in a solution equals the product of its mole fraction and the vapour pressure of the pure component.*
>
> $$P_1 = \chi_1 P_1^{\circ}$$
>
> **Derivation:** Using $\chi_1 + \chi_2 = 1$,
>
> $$P_1 = (1-\chi_2)P_1^{\circ}$$
> $$P_1^{\circ} - P_1 = \chi_2 P_1^{\circ}$$
> $$\dfrac{P_1^{\circ} - P_1}{P_1^{\circ}} = \chi_2$$
>
> This is the **relative lowering of vapour pressure**, equal to the mole fraction of the solute.
>
> **[3 marks: 1 statement + 2 derivation]**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per **KTU 2024 ESE regulation**, Module 3 contributes 14 marks with internal choice. Students answer *either* Question A *or* Question B.

---

#### ⭐ Question A — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the relationship between the **elevation of boiling point** and the molality of a dilute solution. Define the **ebullioscopic constant** and discuss its dependence on the solvent.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A solution of **0.30 g of a non-electrolyte solute in 30 g of water** boils at **100.05 °C**. Calculate the **molar mass** of the solute. Given $K_b(\text{water}) = 0.512 \text{ K·kg·mol}^{-1}$.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1** — State that boiling occurs when the solution's vapour pressure equals external atmospheric pressure.

**Step 2** — Apply Clausius–Clapeyron to the liquid–vapour equilibrium of the solvent:

$$\dfrac{d \ln P}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R T^2}$$

**Step 3** — Integrate between $(P_1^{\circ}, T_b^{\circ})$ and $(P_1, T_b)$:

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_1^{\circ}}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_b} - \dfrac{1}{T_b^{\circ}}\right)$$

**Step 4** — Substitute Raoult's Law, $P_1 = \chi_1 P_1^{\circ}$, and use $\ln(1-\chi_2) \approx -\chi_2$:

$$\chi_2 = \dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T_b}{(T_b^{\circ})^2}$$

**Step 5** — Express $\chi_2$ as molality: $\chi_2 \approx \dfrac{m \cdot M_1}{1000}$, where $M_1$ is in kg/kmol.

$$\Delta T_b = \underbrace{\dfrac{R(T_b^{\circ})^2 M_1}{1000 \, \Delta H_{\text{vap}}}}_{K_b} \cdot m$$

**Step 6** — Define $K_b$:

$$\boxed{K_b = \dfrac{R(T_b^{\circ})^2 M_1}{1000 \, \Delta H_{\text{vap}}}}$$

**Step 7 — Solvent Dependence:**
$K_b$ depends on the solvent via:
- **$T_b^{\circ}$** — higher boiling-point solvents give larger $K_b$.
- **$M_1$** — molar mass of solvent (e.g., benzene 78 g/mol vs water 18 g/mol).
- **$\Delta H_{\text{vap}}$** — solvents with *low* enthalpy of vaporisation have *high* $K_b$ (more sensitive).

Examples:
- Water: $K_b = 0.512 \text{ K·kg/mol}$
- Benzene: $K_b = 2.53 \text{ K·kg/mol}$
- Carbon tetrachloride: $K_b = 5.03 \text{ K·kg/mol}$

> **[7 marks: derivation 5 marks + $K_b$ definition 1 mark + solvent dependence 1 mark]**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Mass of solute $w_2 = 0.30 \text{ g}$
- Mass of solvent (water) $w_1 = 30 \text{ g} = 0.030 \text{ kg}$
- $\Delta T_b = 100.05 - 100.00 = 0.05 \text{ K}$ (or °C)
- $K_b = 0.512 \text{ K·kg·mol}^{-1}$

**Step 1 — Write the working formula:**

$$\Delta T_b = \dfrac{K_b \cdot w_2 \cdot 1000}{M_2 \cdot w_1(\text{in g})}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$0.05 = \dfrac{0.512 \times 0.30 \times 1000}{M_2 \times 30}$$

**Step 3 — Solve:**

$$M_2 = \dfrac{0.512 \times 0.30 \times 1000}{0.05 \times 30}$$

$$M_2 = \dfrac{153.6}{1.5} = 102.4 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$\boxed{M_2 \approx 102.4 \text{ g/mol}}$$

> **[7 marks: data 1 mark + formula 2 marks + substitution 2 marks + answer with units 2 marks]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Forgetting to convert $w_1$ correctly (use grams, NOT kg, in the denominator when numerator has the 1000 factor).
> - Reporting $T_b$ in °C instead of ΔT in K (numerically equal but units must be correct).
> - Missing units in the final answer (lose 1 full mark).

---

#### ⭐ Question B — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is **osmotic pressure**? Derive the **van't Hoff equation** for dilute solutions and explain the role of the **semipermeable membrane**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **5% aqueous solution** of a non-electrolyte biomolecule (mass by mass) exhibits an osmotic pressure of **6.84 atm at 27 °C**. Calculate its **molar mass**. ($R = 0.0821 \text{ L·atm·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$)

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Definition:** Osmotic pressure ($\pi$) is the *excess hydrostatic pressure* that must be applied on the solution side of a semipermeable membrane to prevent the spontaneous inflow of pure solvent from the solvent side.

**Step 1 — Condition for osmotic equilibrium:**

$$\mu_{\text{solvent}}(\text{solution side, } P+\pi) = \mu_{\text{solvent}}(\text{pure side, } P)$$

**Step 2 — Write each chemical potential:**

$$\mu_1^{\circ}(T, P) + RT \ln \chi_1 + \pi \, \bar{V}_1 = \mu_1^{\circ}(T, P)$$

**Step 3 — Apply dilute-solution approximation $\ln \chi_1 \approx -\chi_2 \approx -n_2/(n_1 + n_2) \approx -n_2 \bar{V}_1 / V$:**

$$-RT \dfrac{n_2}{V/\bar{V}_1} + \pi \bar{V}_1 = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for π:**

$$\boxed{\pi V = n_2 R T \quad \text{or} \quad \pi = \dfrac{n_2}{V} R T = M R T}$$

**Step 5 — Role of Semi-permeable Membrane:**
- Allows only **solvent molecules** to pass, blocking solute particles.
- Maintains a chemical-potential gradient that drives osmotic flow.
- Examples: cellophane, cellulose acetate (lab); plasma membrane, *aquaporins* (biology).
- Membrane must be **chemically inert** and mechanically robust to withstand pressure $\pi$.

> **[7 marks: definition 1 + derivation 4 + membrane role 2]**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- 5% w/w solution ⇒ 5 g solute in 95 g solvent (assume 100 g solution, density ≈ 1 g/mL ⇒ volume ≈ 0.1 L; for dilute approximation, V ≈ 100 mL ≈ 0.1 L)
- $\pi = 6.84 \text{ atm}$
- $T = 27 + 273 = 300 \text{ K}$
- $R = 0.0821 \text{ L·atm·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$

**Step 1 — Apply $\pi V = \dfrac{w_2}{M_2} R T$:**

$$M_2 = \dfrac{w_2 R T}{\pi V}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$M_2 = \dfrac{5 \times 0.0821 \times 300}{6.84 \times 0.1}$$

Numerator: $5 \times 0.0821 \times 300 = 123.15$

Denominator: $6.84 \times 0.1 = 0.684$

$$M_2 = \dfrac{123.15}{0.684} \approx 180.0 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$\boxed{M_2 \approx 180 \text{ g/mol}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistakes in this Numerical:**
> - Using total solution mass (100 g) as solvent mass — *this overestimates concentration*. Always subtract solute mass.
> - Forgetting to convert mL to L (a 1000× error in answer).
> - Using $R = 8.314$ instead of $0.0821$ — both are correct *if* units are matched; mixing them is the killer error.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist (Print & Pin)**

- **Definition:** Colligative properties depend on **number of solute particles, not their identity**.
- **Four properties:** (i) Vapour pressure lowering, (ii) Boiling point elevation, (iii) Freezing point depression, (iv) Osmotic pressure.
- **Raoult's Law:** $P_1 = \chi_1 P_1^{\circ}$; relative lowering = $\chi_2$.
- **Boiling point elevation:** $\Delta T_b = K_b \cdot m$; **Freezing point depression:** $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$.
- **Ebullioscopic constant:** $K_b = \dfrac{R(T_b^{\circ})^2 M_1}{1000 \, \Delta H_{\text{vap}}}$.
- **Cryoscopic constant:** $K_f = \dfrac{R(T_f^{\circ})^2 M_1}{1000 \, \Delta H_{\text{fus}}}$.
- **van't Hoff equation:** $\pi = M R T = \dfrac{n_2}{V} R T$.
- **van't Hoff factor (i):** Always multiply by *i* for electrolytes ($\text{NaCl} \to i \approx 2$, $\text{CaCl}_2 \to i \approx 3$).
- **Water constants:** $K_b = 0.512$; $K_f = 1.86$ (memorize!).
- **Molecular weight formula (from ΔT_f):** $M_2 = \dfrac{K_f \cdot w_2 \cdot 1000}{\Delta T_f \cdot w_1}$.
- **Assumption:** All formulas are for **dilute ideal solutions** (χ₂ → 0, Raoult's Law valid).
- **ΔT units:** Always report in **Kelvin**; numerically equal to °C differences.
- **Bio-applications:** Cell osmolarity, cryopreservation, MW of proteins by osmotic pressure.
- **Exam tip:** Always write the formula first, then substitute, then compute — KTU examiners reward stepwise working.
- **Common trap:** For strong electrolytes use *i*; for association in benzene, *i* < 1.

> **End of Module 3 — Colligative Properties | PEBBT414 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Thermodynamics in Biochemical Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biochemical Thermodynamics** is the application of the laws of thermodynamics to biological systems, focusing on the quantitative analysis of energy transformations, chemical equilibria, and the spontaneity of metabolic reactions occurring within living cells.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this subject is formally defined as the study of **energy flow (ΔE)**, **entropy changes (ΔS)**, **enthalpy changes (ΔH)**, and **Gibbs free energy changes (ΔG)** in biochemical reactions such as glycolysis, the TCA cycle, oxidative phosphorylation, photosynthesis, and protein-ligand binding.

### Key Thermodynamic Parameters in Biological Systems

| Parameter | Symbol | Biological Significance |
|-----------|--------|--------------------------|
| Standard Free Energy Change | ΔG°' | Determines spontaneity at pH 7 (biochemical standard state) |
| Actual Free Energy Change | ΔG | Determines spontaneity at cellular concentrations |
| Enthalpy Change | ΔH | Heat released or absorbed in biochemical reactions |
| Entropy Change | ΔS | Measure of disorder in biomolecular systems |
| Equilibrium Constant | Keq | Ratio of product to reactant concentrations at equilibrium |
| Standard Transformed Constant | K' | Equilibrium constant at pH 7 and specified pMg |

### Biochemical Standard State

> [!NOTE]
> In biochemistry, the **standard state (°')** is defined at **pH = 7.0** (not pH = 0 as in chemistry), **temperature = 298.15 K (25°C)**, **pressure = 1 atm (101.325 kPa)**, and **all solutes at 1 M concentration** (except H⁺ at 10⁻⁷ M).

This biochemical convention is **critical** because most cellular reactions occur near neutral pH. The prime symbol (′) in ΔG°' explicitly indicates this biochemically-adjusted standard state.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The "Cellular Battery" Analogy:**

Think of a living cell as a **rechargeable battery system** operating in a city called "Cytoplasm":

- **Glucose** is like **coal** (high-grade fuel) being fed into a power plant.
- The **Mitochondrion** is the **power plant** that converts coal into usable electricity.
- **ATP (Adenosine Triphosphate)** is the **electric current** distributed to all city machines (proteins, enzymes, transport systems).
- The **battery terminals** represent the **ΔG** of the reaction — the voltage difference between reactants and products.

Just as a battery cannot be used if the voltage difference (potential) is too low, a biochemical reaction cannot spontaneously proceed if its **ΔG is positive**. The cell cleverly **couples** unfavorable reactions (positive ΔG) with highly favorable ones (negative ΔG, such as ATP hydrolysis) to drive life processes — exactly like jump-starting a car by connecting it to another battery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Biological Constant:** The standard free energy of ATP hydrolysis under cellular conditions is **ΔG°' = −30.5 kJ/mol** (often approximated as −7.3 kcal/mol in older texts). This single value is the **energy currency exchange rate** of the cell.

---

## 1.3 Why Biochemical Thermodynamics is Unique

Unlike classical chemical thermodynamics, biochemical systems possess special features:

- **Isothermal and isobaric conditions** (cells operate at constant T and P)
- **Aqueous environment** at near-neutral pH
- **Highly coupled reactions** (one reaction drives another)
- **Steady-state, not equilibrium** (cells maintain non-equilibrium concentrations of ATP, ADP, etc.)
- **Catalyzed by enzymes** that only affect rates, **not** ΔG or Keq
- **Constant body temperature (37°C for humans)** for homeotherms, or ambient for poikilotherms

---

## 1.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Biotechnology & Bioprocess Engineering Relevance:**
> - Designing **microbial fuel cells** (MFCs) that exploit redox thermodynamics
> - **Metabolic engineering** to redirect flux toward desired products
> - Calculating **theoretical yields** of fermentation products
> - Designing **enzyme bioreactors** based on thermodynamic feasibility
> - **Biofuel production** (bioethanol, biohydrogen) using thermodynamic limits
> - **Pharmacokinetic modeling** of drug metabolism in the body
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Principles Applied to Biological Systems

### The First Law in Biochemical Context

The **First Law of Thermodynamics** states that energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed. In biological systems:

$$\Delta U = q + w$$

For biochemical reactions occurring at constant pressure (typical for cells):

$$\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta(PV)$$

At constant pressure, the heat exchanged equals the enthalpy change. For most biochemical reactions in aqueous solution, PV work is negligible, so:

$$\Delta H \approx q_p \text{ (heat at constant pressure)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biological Implication:** Living organisms are **open, non-equilibrium systems** that continuously exchange energy and matter with their environment. They maintain their highly ordered state by increasing the entropy of the surroundings (Second Law compliance).

### The Second Law and Spontaneity

For a biochemical reaction to be **spontaneous** (thermodynamically favorable):

$$\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S < 0$$

The sign of ΔG is the **master indicator** of reaction feasibility:

| ΔG Sign | Reaction Behavior | Biological Example |
|---------|-------------------|---------------------|
| ΔG < 0 (negative) | Spontaneous, exergonic | ATP hydrolysis, glucose oxidation |
| ΔG = 0 | At equilibrium | Reversible binding at saturation |
| ΔG > 0 (positive) | Non-spontaneous, endergonic | Protein synthesis from amino acids, photosynthesis light reactions |

---

## 2.2 The Gibbs Free Energy — The Heart of Biochemical Thermodynamics

### Standard vs. Actual Free Energy

**Standard free energy change (ΔG°'):** The free energy change when reactants and products are at **1 M concentration** (except H⁺ at 10⁻⁷ M) at **pH 7**, **25°C**, and **1 atm**.

**Actual free energy change (ΔG):** The real free energy change at **actual cellular concentrations**, calculated using:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ\prime} + RT \ln Q$$

Where:
- **R** = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **T** = Absolute temperature in Kelvin
- **Q** = Mass-action ratio (reaction quotient) at actual cellular conditions
- **ln** = Natural logarithm

### Relationship with Equilibrium Constant

At equilibrium, ΔG = 0 and Q = Keq, so:

$$\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -RT \ln K_{eq}$$

Or, in the more common base-10 form (using 2.303 × log):

$$\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -2.303 \, RT \log_{10} K_{eq}$$

At standard temperature **T = 298.15 K**:

$$\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -5.708 \, \log_{10} K_{eq} \text{ (in kJ/mol)}$$

### Magnitude Interpretation of ΔG°'

| ΔG°' (kJ/mol) | Keq | Biological Interpretation |
|----------------|-----|---------------------------|
| −30.5 | ~10⁵ | Strongly favorable (e.g., ATP hydrolysis) |
| −17.1 | ~10³ | Moderately favorable (e.g., glucose-6-P hydrolysis) |
| 0 | 1 | Equilibrium |
| +17.1 | ~10⁻³ | Moderately unfavorable |
| +30.5 | ~10⁻⁵ | Strongly unfavorable |

---

## 2.3 ATP — The Universal Energy Currency

### Structure and Thermodynamic Role

ATP consists of:
- **Adenine** (nitrogenous base)
- **Ribose** (5-carbon sugar)
- **Three phosphate groups** (α, β, γ)

The high-energy phosphoanhydride bonds (between α-β and β-γ phosphates) release large amounts of free energy upon hydrolysis due to:
1. **Electrostatic repulsion relief** between negatively charged phosphates
2. **Resonance stabilization** of the products (Pi and ADP)
3. **Hydration** of the products
4. **Proton dissociation** at physiological pH

### Key ATP Thermodynamic Equations

**ATP Hydrolysis:**

$$\text{ATP} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{ADP} + \text{P}_i \quad \Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -30.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**ATP Synthesis (reverse):**

$$\text{ADP} + \text{P}_i \rightarrow \text{ATP} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta G^{\circ\prime} = +30.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In living cells**, the actual ΔG for ATP hydrolysis is closer to **−50 to −60 kJ/mol** because the [ATP]/[ADP][Pi] ratio is maintained far from equilibrium, making ATP a much more powerful "energy currency" in vivo than ΔG°' suggests.

---

## 2.4 Energy Coupling — The Cellular Strategy

Cells drive thermodynamically unfavorable reactions (ΔG > 0) by coupling them to highly favorable ones (ΔG << 0), typically ATP hydrolysis.

### General Coupling Equation

$$\Delta G_{\text{total}} = \Delta G_1 + \Delta G_2 + \Delta G_3 + \cdots$$

For the reaction to proceed spontaneously:

$$\Delta G_{\text{total}} < 0$$

### Classic Example: Glucose Phosphorylation

**Unfavorable reaction:**

$$\text{Glucose} + \text{P}_i \rightarrow \text{Glucose-6-P} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta G^{\circ\prime} = +13.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Coupled with ATP hydrolysis:**

$$\text{ATP} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{ADP} + \text{P}_i \quad \Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -30.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Net (coupled) reaction:**

$$\text{Glucose} + \text{ATP} \rightarrow \text{Glucose-6-P} + \text{ADP} \quad \Delta G^{\circ\prime}_{\text{net}} = +13.8 + (-30.5) = -16.7 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

The reaction is now **spontaneous** because ΔG°'net is negative.

---

## 2.5 High-Energy Phosphate Compounds

Not all phosphate compounds are "energy-rich." The following table lists key biological phosphoryl compounds ranked by their ΔG°' of hydrolysis:

| Compound | ΔG°' of Hydrolysis (kJ/mol) | Energy Class |
|----------|------------------------------|--------------|
| Phosphoenolpyruvate (PEP) | −61.9 | Very high-energy |
| 1,3-Bisphosphoglycerate | −49.4 | Very high-energy |
| Acetyl phosphate | −43.1 | High-energy |
| Phosphocreatine | −43.1 | High-energy |
| ATP (→ ADP + Pi) | −30.5 | High-energy |
| ADP (→ AMP + Pi) | −30.5 | High-energy |
| Glucose-1-phosphate | −20.9 | Low-energy |
| Glucose-6-phosphate | −13.8 | Low-energy |
| Glycerol-3-phosphate | −9.2 | Low-energy |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Phosphate Group Transfer Potential":** Compounds above ATP transfer their phosphate groups *to* ADP to form ATP. Compounds below ATP receive phosphate groups *from* ATP. This is the essence of cellular energy transfer.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Comprehensive)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ | Gibbs free energy definition | J/mol |
| 2 | $\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ\prime} + RT \ln Q$ | Free energy at actual cellular conditions | J/mol |
| 3 | $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -RT \ln K_{eq}$ | Standard free energy–equilibrium relation | J/mol |
| 4 | $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -2.303 RT \log_{10} K_{eq}$ | Base-10 form (more practical) | J/mol |
| 5 | $\Delta G_{\text{coupled}} = \sum \Delta G_i$ | Additivity of free energies | J/mol |
| 6 | $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -nF \Delta E_0^{\prime}$ | Electrochemical free energy (redox) | J/mol |
| 7 | $\Delta S_{\text{universe}} = \Delta S_{\text{system}} + \Delta S_{\text{surroundings}} \geq 0$ | Second law | J/(K·mol) |
| 8 | $\Delta H^{\circ\prime} = \Delta G^{\circ\prime} + T\Delta S^{\circ\prime}$ | Enthalpy–free energy relation | J/mol |
| 9 | $\ln K_{eq} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ\prime}}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} + \frac{\Delta S^{\circ\prime}}{R}$ | van 't Hoff equation | dimensionless |
| 10 | $Q = \frac{[C]^c[D]^d}{[A]^a[B]^b}$ | Reaction quotient (mass-action ratio) | dimensionless |

**Critical Constants for KTU Examinations:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - R = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** = **1.987 cal/(mol·K)**
> - T (standard) = **298.15 K**
> - F (Faraday's constant) = **96,485 C/mol** = **96.485 kJ/(V·mol)**
> - 2.303 × R × T (at 298 K) = **5.708 kJ/mol**
> - 1 cal = **4.184 J** (exact)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship Between ΔG°' and Keq

### Starting Point

We begin with the fundamental relationship between free energy and the reaction quotient:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ\prime} + RT \ln Q$$

### Logical Progression

**Step 1:** Recognize the definition of equilibrium.

At equilibrium, the system has no further net change, meaning:
- $\Delta G = 0$ (no driving force remaining)
- $Q = K_{eq}$ (reactants and products at equilibrium concentrations)

**Step 2:** Substitute these equilibrium conditions into the fundamental equation.

$$\Delta G = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad Q = K_{eq}$$

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ\prime} + RT \ln Q$$

$$0 = \Delta G^{\circ\prime} + RT \ln K_{eq}$$

**Step 3:** Algebraically isolate $\Delta G^{\circ\prime}$.

$$\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -RT \ln K_{eq}$$

**Step 4:** Convert natural logarithm to base-10 logarithm.

Using the identity: $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$

$$\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -2.303 \, RT \log_{10} K_{eq}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute standard temperature T = 298.15 K and R = 8.314 J/(mol·K).

$$\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15 \times \log_{10} K_{eq}$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -5708 \, \log_{10} K_{eq} \text{ J/mol}$$

**Step 6:** Convert to kJ/mol for convenience (divide by 1000).

$$\boxed{\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -5.708 \, \log_{10} K_{eq} \text{ kJ/mol}}$$

### Numerical Verification

Let's verify with the ATP hydrolysis reaction where Keq ≈ 2 × 10⁵ at pH 7:

$$\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -5.708 \times \log_{10}(2 \times 10^5)$$

$$\log_{10}(2 \times 10^5) = \log_{10}(2) + 5 = 0.301 + 5 = 5.301$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -5.708 \times 5.301 = -30.25 \text{ kJ/mol} \approx -30.5 \text{ kJ/mol} \checkmark$$

This confirms our derivation matches the experimentally accepted value for ATP hydrolysis.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The van 't Hoff Equation

### Starting Point

The standard free energy change is related to enthalpy and entropy:

$$\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = \Delta H^{\circ\prime} - T \Delta S^{\circ\prime}$$

### Logical Progression

**Step 1:** Substitute this into the standard free energy – equilibrium relationship.

$$-RT \ln K_{eq} = \Delta H^{\circ\prime} - T \Delta S^{\circ\prime}$$

**Step 2:** Divide both sides by −RT.

$$\ln K_{eq} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ\prime}}{RT} + \frac{\Delta S^{\circ\prime}}{R}$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange into the linear form (y = mx + b):

$$\ln K_{eq} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ\prime}}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} + \frac{\Delta S^{\circ\prime}}{R}$$

**Step 4:** Identify the slope and intercept of this linear equation.

- **Slope** = $-\frac{\Delta H^{\circ\prime}}{R}$
- **Intercept** = $\frac{\Delta S^{\circ\prime}}{R}$
- **Independent variable** = $\frac{1}{T}$

**Step 5:** This is the famous **van 't Hoff equation**, which experimentally allows determination of ΔH°' and ΔS°' from the temperature dependence of Keq.

$$\boxed{\ln K_{eq} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ\prime}}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} + \frac{\Delta S^{\circ\prime}}{R}}$$

### Application: Two-Temperature Form

For measurements at two temperatures T₁ and T₂:

$$\ln\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ\prime}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

This is **extremely useful** for KTU problems asking "How does Keq change with temperature?"

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Electrochemical Free Energy (Redox Reactions)

### Starting Point

For oxidation-reduction (redox) reactions, the free energy change is related to the electromotive force (EMF):

$$-W_{electrical} = nFE$$

Where:
- $W_{electrical}$ = Electrical work done
- $n$ = Number of moles of electrons transferred
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol**
- $E$ = Cell potential (V)

### Logical Progression

**Step 1:** At constant T and P, the maximum useful work obtainable from a reaction equals −ΔG.

$$\Delta G = -W_{max} = -nFE$$

**Step 2:** For standard biochemical conditions, this becomes:

$$\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -nF \Delta E_0^{\prime}$$

**Step 3:** For actual cellular conditions:

$$\Delta G = -nF \Delta E$$

**Step 4:** Relate ΔE to ΔE₀' using the Nernst equation.

$$\Delta E = \Delta E_0^{\prime} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

**Step 5:** Substitute into the ΔG expression.

$$\Delta G = -nF \left[\Delta E_0^{\prime} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q\right]$$

$$\Delta G = -nF\Delta E_0^{\prime} + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 6:** Recognize that $-nF\Delta E_0^{\prime} = \Delta G^{\circ\prime}$, which confirms the general formula.

$$\boxed{\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ\prime} + RT \ln Q \quad \text{(unified result)}}$$

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Calculate ΔG°' for the reduction of NAD⁺ by FADH₂.

Given:
- $E_0'$ (FAD/FADH₂) = −0.22 V
- $E_0'$ (NAD⁺/NADH) = −0.32 V
- $n$ = 2 electrons

**Step 1:** Identify the half-reactions.

$$\text{NAD}^+ + 2e^- + \text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{NADH} \quad E_0' = -0.32 \text{ V (reduction)}$$

$$\text{FADH}_2 \rightarrow \text{FAD} + 2e^- + 2\text{H}^+ \quad E_0' = +0.22 \text{ V (oxidation)}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate ΔE₀'.

$$\Delta E_0' = E_0'(\text{acceptor}) - E_0'(\text{donor})$$

$$\Delta E_0' = (-0.32) - (-0.22) = -0.10 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate ΔG°'.

$$\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -nF\Delta E_0' = -(2)(96{,}485)(-0.10)$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = +19{,}297 \text{ J/mol} = +19.3 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Interpretation:** The reaction is **non-spontaneous** under standard conditions (positive ΔG°'), which is why this electron transfer is **not** a major pathway in mitochondria.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Metabolic Pathway Energetics

| Metabolic Pathway | Net ΔG°' (kJ/mol) | ATP Equivalent | Spontaneity |
|-------------------|---------------------|----------------|-------------|
| Glycolysis (glucose → 2 pyruvate) | −85 | 2 ATP produced | Highly favorable |
| TCA Cycle (per acetyl-CoA) | −40 | 3 NADH + 1 FADH₂ + 1 GTP | Highly favorable |
| Oxidative phosphorylation (per NADH) | −220 | ~2.5 ATP | Extremely favorable |
| Fatty acid β-oxidation (per palmitate) | −9790 | 106 ATP | Highly favorable |
| Gluconeogenesis (pyruvate → glucose) | +40 | 6 ATP consumed | Unfavorable, requires coupling |
| Photosynthesis (light reactions) | +1400 | 3 ATP + 2 NADPH | Highly unfavorable without light |

---

## 3.5 Worked Problem: Coupling in Biosynthesis

**Problem:** Calculate the ΔG°' for the synthesis of glutamine from glutamate and ammonia, given:

- Reaction 1: Glutamate + NH₄⁺ + ATP → Glutamine + ADP + Pi
- ΔG°' (ATP hydrolysis) = −30.5 kJ/mol
- ΔG°' (Glutamate + NH₄⁺ → Glutamine) = +14.2 kJ/mol (uncoupled)

**Solution:**

$$\Delta G^{\circ\prime}_{\text{overall}} = \Delta G^{\circ\prime}_{\text{glutamine synthesis}} + \Delta G^{\circ\prime}_{\text{ATP hydrolysis}}$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ\prime}_{\text{overall}} = (+14.2) + (-30.5)$$

$$\boxed{\Delta G^{\circ\prime}_{\text{overall}} = -16.3 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$

**Conclusion:** The reaction becomes **spontaneous** through coupling with ATP hydrolysis. This is why glutamine synthetase is a critical regulatory enzyme in nitrogen metabolism.

---

## 3.6 Advanced Problem: Actual ΔG Under Cellular Conditions

**Problem:** For the reaction: A + B → C + D

Given:
- ΔG°' = +5.0 kJ/mol
- [A] = 10⁻⁴ M, [B] = 10⁻⁴ M
- [C] = 10⁻² M, [D] = 10⁻² M
- T = 310 K (body temperature)
- R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)

Calculate the actual ΔG.

**Step 1:** Calculate the reaction quotient Q.

$$Q = \frac{[C][D]}{[A][B]} = \frac{(10^{-2})(10^{-2})}{(10^{-4})(10^{-4})} = \frac{10^{-4}}{10^{-8}} = 10^4$$

**Step 2:** Calculate RT ln Q at T = 310 K.

$$RT = 8.314 \times 310 = 2577.3 \text{ J/mol} = 2.577 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$RT \ln Q = 2.577 \times \ln(10^4) = 2.577 \times 9.210 = 23.74 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the fundamental equation.

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ\prime} + RT \ln Q$$

$$\Delta G = (+5.0) + (+23.74)$$

$$\boxed{\Delta G = +28.74 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$

**Interpretation:** Although ΔG°' is slightly positive, the actual cellular conditions make the reaction **strongly non-spontaneous** (highly positive ΔG). The cell would need to couple this with a much more exergonic reaction to drive it forward.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: ATP as the Universal Energy Currency (Concept Map)

This diagram illustrates how ATP couples catabolic (exergonic) reactions to anabolic (endergonic) reactions in the cell.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Catabolic Reactions<br>Glucose Oxidation<br>Lipid Breakdown"]
    nodeB["ATP Synthesis<br>ADP plus Pi to ATP<br>dG positive thirty point five kJ per mol"]
    nodeC["ATP Molecule<br>Universal Energy Currency<br>Cellular Concentration 2 to 5 mM"]
    nodeD["ATP Hydrolysis<br>ATP to ADP plus Pi<br>dG negative thirty point five kJ per mol"]
    nodeE["Anabolic Reactions<br>Protein Synthesis<br>DNA Replication"]
    nodeF["Active Transport<br>Ion Pumping<br>Mechanical Work"]
    nodeG["Heat Release<br>Maintenance of Body Temperature"]

    nodeA -->|Energy Released| nodeB
    nodeB -->|Produces| nodeC
    nodeC -->|Utilized by| nodeD
    nodeD -->|Drives| nodeE
    nodeD -->|Drives| nodeF
    nodeD -->|Generates| nodeG
```

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Energy Coupling Mechanism (Block Architecture)

This block diagram shows how an unfavorable reaction is driven by coupling with ATP hydrolysis through a common intermediate.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeReact["Reactant A<br>dG1 positive thirteen point eight kJ per mol"]
    nodeInt["Common Intermediate<br>Phosphorylated A"]
    nodeProd["Product B<br>Energy Stored"]
    nodeATP["ATP<br>Energy Donor"]
    nodeADP["ADP plus Pi<br>Energy Released<br>dG negative thirty point five kJ per mol"]

    nodeReact -->|Phosphorylation from ATP| nodeInt
    nodeATP -->|Provides Phosphate Group| nodeInt
    nodeInt -->|Dephosphorylation| nodeProd
    nodeProd -->|Releases Phosphate| nodeADP
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Hierarchy of Phosphate Group Transfer Potential

This diagram organizes biological phosphate compounds by their energy content relative to ATP.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeTop["Very High Energy Compounds<br>PEP minus sixty one point nine kJ per mol<br>1,3-BPG minus forty nine point four kJ per mol"]
    nodeMid["High Energy Compounds<br>Acetyl Phosphate minus forty three point one kJ per mol<br>Phosphocreatine minus forty three point one kJ per mol<br>ATP minus thirty point five kJ per mol"]
    nodeLow["Low Energy Compounds<br>Glucose one P minus twenty point nine kJ per mol<br>Glucose six P minus thirteen point eight kJ per mol<br>Glycerol three P minus nine point two kJ per mol"]

    nodeTop -->|Transfer Phosphate Downward| nodeMid
    nodeMid -->|Transfer Phosphate Downward| nodeLow
```

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStep1["Step 1: Hexokinase<br>Glucose to Glucose six P<br>Uses 1 ATP"]

    nodeStep1 --> nodeStep3
    nodeStep3 --> nodeStep7
    nodeStep7 --> nodeStep10
    nodeStep10 --> nodeFinal
```
