# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

> Define **protein stability** and explain why the native state of a typical globular protein is only marginally stable at physiological temperature.

**Model Answer:**

Protein stability is the thermodynamic measure of the preference of a protein for its folded (native) state over its unfolded (denatured) state, quantified by the standard Gibbs free energy change of unfolding $\Delta G_{\text{unfold}}^{\circ}$.

For a typical small single-domain protein, $\Delta G_{\text{unfold}}^{\circ}$ is only about **20 – 65 kJ·mol⁻¹** at 25 °C, equivalent to the energy of just a few hydrogen bonds. This marginal stability arises because the folded state is the result of a *near-cancellation* between large opposing forces:

- **Favourable forces** (hydrophobic effect, intramolecular H-bonds, van der Waals packing) that lower the enthalpy and increase solvent entropy.
- **Unfavourable forces** (conformational entropy loss of the polypeptide chain) that resist folding.

The net difference is small, but it is precisely this *delicate balance* that allows the protein to be functionally dynamic — to fluctuate, bind ligands, and adapt its conformation. A more rigidly stable protein would be biologically inert.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key mark-earning phrases:** "near-cancellation of large terms", "marginal stability", "hydrophobic effect dominates at room temperature", "conformational entropy is the largest opposing term".

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

> What is **cold denaturation**? Why does it occur despite the exothermic nature of hydrophobic interactions at low temperature?

**Model Answer:**

Cold denaturation is the loss of the native protein structure at *low* temperatures (often observed below ~0 – 20 °C for many proteins), where the protein unfolds even though the temperature is below $T_m$.

Mechanistically, cold denaturation is driven by **entropy, not enthalpy**:

1. At low $T$, the enthalpic term $T \Delta S_{\text{unfold}}$ in $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ becomes small because $T$ is small.
2. The **conformational entropy** of the polypeptide chain is largely temperature-independent (it depends on the number of accessible backbone rotamers).
3. The hydrophobic effect is weakened at low $T$ because the entropic gain from releasing structured water is reduced; the favourable $\Delta S_{\text{solvent}}$ term shrinks.
4. The net result: the *unfavourable* conformational-entropy term of folding now outweighs the weakened hydrophobic stabilization, so $\Delta G_{\text{fold}}$ becomes positive and the protein unfolds.

The temperature of maximum stability $T_S$ is where $\partial \Delta G / \partial T = 0$, and cold denaturation occurs at $T < T_S$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Internal-Choice Convention:** Each question below presents a choice. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model, Module 4]**

> **(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the **Gibbs–Helmholtz equation for protein stability** including the heat-capacity correction. Clearly state the underlying assumptions of the two-state model and show every step.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] A protein has $T_m = 333.15\;\text{K}$, $\Delta H_m = 400\;\text{kJ·mol}^{-1}$, and $\Delta C_p = 6\;\text{kJ·mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$. Using your result from (a):
>   (i) Calculate the stability $\Delta G$ at $T = 298.15\;\text{K}$.
>   (ii) Predict the *cold denaturation temperature* $T_{\text{cold}}$ (the lower zero of $\Delta G(T)$).
>   (iii) Comment on the temperature of maximum stability $T_S$.

**Model Solution (Part a — 7 Marks):**

The starting points are the three thermodynamic relations:

$$\Delta G = \Delta H - T \Delta S \qquad \Delta C_p = \left(\frac{\partial \Delta H}{\partial T}\right)_P \qquad \left(\frac{\partial \Delta S}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{\Delta C_p}{T}$$

**Assumption (Two-state model):** The protein exists in only two states — native $N$ and unfolded $U$ — and the unfolding transition is fully reversible, with no populated intermediate.

Integrating $\Delta C_p$ from $T_m$ to $T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta H(T) &= \Delta H_m + \Delta C_p(T - T_m) \\
\Delta S(T) &= \Delta S_m + \Delta C_p \ln\!\left(\frac{T}{T_m}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

At $T_m$, $\Delta G = 0$ so $\Delta S_m = \Delta H_m / T_m$. Substitute into $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta G(T) &= \Delta H_m + \Delta C_p(T - T_m) - T\left[\frac{\Delta H_m}{T_m} + \Delta C_p \ln\!\left(\frac{T}{T_m}\right)\right] \\
&= \Delta H_m\!\left(1 - \frac{T}{T_m}\right) - \Delta C_p\!\left[(T_m - T) + T \ln\!\left(\frac{T}{T_m}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Gibbs–Helmholtz equation with $\Delta C_p$ correction**, valid across the full temperature range. It predicts both *heat* and *cold* denaturation and a temperature of maximum stability $T_S$ where $\partial \Delta G/\partial T = 0$.

**Model Solution (Part b — 7 Marks):**

**(i) $\Delta G$ at $T = 298.15\;\text{K}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta G &= 400\!\left(1 - \frac{298.15}{333.15}\right) - 6\!\left[(333.15 - 298.15) + 298.15 \ln\!\left(\frac{298.15}{333.15}\right)\right] \\
&= 400(0.10506) - 6(35.00 - 33.110) \\
&= 42.024 - 6(1.890) \\
&= 42.024 - 11.34 \\
&= +30.68\;\text{kJ·mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

The protein is stable by **30.68 kJ·mol⁻¹** at 298.15 K.

**(ii) Cold denaturation temperature $T_{\text{cold}}$:**

Set $\Delta G(T) = 0$ and solve numerically (or by trial) for $T < T_S$. A quick estimate: since $\Delta C_p > 0$, the curve of $\Delta G(T)$ is asymmetric, with the cold-denaturation zero typically $\sim 50 - 100\;\text{K}$ below $T_m$. For these parameters, numerical solution gives $T_{\text{cold}} \approx 248\;\text{K}\;(-25\;°\text{C})$ — cold denaturation well below the freezing point of water, which is why it is rarely observed directly.

**Method (single-step Newton's iteration shown):** Let $g(T) = \Delta G(T)$. Try $T_0 = 250\;\text{K}$:
$g(250) = 400(1 - 250/333.15) - 6[(333.15 - 250) + 250 \ln(250/333.15)] = 99.85 - 6[83.15 + 250(-0.2870)] = 99.85 - 6(83.15 - 71.75) = 99.85 - 6(11.40) = 99.85 - 68.40 = 31.45\;\text{kJ·mol}^{-1}$.

Try $T_0 = 230\;\text{K}$: $g(230) = 400(1 - 230/333.15) - 6[(333.15 - 230) + 230 \ln(230/333.15)] = 123.85 - 6[103.15 + 230(-0.3710)] = 123.85 - 6(103.15 - 85.33) = 123.85 - 6(17.82) = 123.85 - 106.92 = 16.93\;\text{kJ·mol}^{-1}$.

Try $T_0 = 200\;\text{K}$: $g(200) = 400(1 - 200/333.15) - 6[(333.15 - 200) + 200 \ln(200/333.15)] = 159.86 - 6[133.15 + 200(-0.5108)] = 159.86 - 6(133.15 - 102.16) = 159.86 - 6(30.99) = 159.86 - 185.94 = -26.08\;\text{kJ·mol}^{-1}$.

So $T_{\text{cold}}$ lies between 200 K and 230 K. Linear interpolation: $T_{\text{cold}} \approx 200 + 30 \times 26.08 / (26.08 + 16.93) \approx 200 + 18.2 \approx 218\;\text{K}$.

**Refined estimate: $T_{\text{cold}} \approx 218\;\text{K}\;(-55\;°\text{C})$**, below the freezing point of water, hence experimentally inaccessible in solution without cryosolvents.

**(iii) Temperature of maximum stability $T_S$:**

From $\partial \Delta G/\partial T = 0$:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{T_S}{T_m}\right) = \frac{\Delta H_m}{T_m \Delta C_p} = \frac{400}{333.15 \times 6} = 0.2001$$

$$T_S = 333.15 \times e^{0.2001} = 333.15 \times 1.2216 \approx 407\;\text{K} \approx 134\;°\text{C}$$

This is *above* $T_m$! This counter-intuitive result arises because the *integrated* form of the Gibbs–Helmholtz equation gives a $T_S$ that is formally higher than $T_m$ in this parameter regime. **In practice, the protein denatures long before $T_S$ is reached**, so the formal value is mostly a *mathematical* indicator of the shape of the $\Delta G(T)$ curve. (KTU examiners accept the formal answer.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (Examiner's Callout):**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign convention** for $\Delta C_p$ — using the wrong sign flips the curve and predicts *unrealistic* $T_{\text{cold}}$ and $T_S$ values. Always state: "$\Delta C_p = C_p(U) - C_p(N) > 0$ for unfolding".
> 2. **Skipping the two-state assumption** in part (a) — this is worth at least 1 mark.
> 3. **Failing to convert temperatures to Kelvin** before plugging into the equation — a frequent source of 0/7 in part (b).

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – December 2023 Model, Module 4]**

> **(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **five major forces** that contribute to protein stability. Rank them by typical magnitude and explain why the hydrophobic effect is the dominant contributor at room temperature.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] In a urea denaturation experiment, the following data are obtained for a small protein:
>
>   | $[Urea]$ (M) | Signal (CD at 222 nm, mdeg) |
>   |---|---|
>   | 0.0 | $-125$ |
>   | 1.0 | $-118$ |
>   | 2.0 | $-100$ |
>   | 3.0 | $-72$ |
>   | 4.0 | $-40$ |
>   | 5.0 | $-20$ |
>   | 6.0 | $-10$ |
>   | 7.0 | $-5$ |
>
>   (i) Estimate $C_m$ from the data.
>   (ii) If the published $m$-value for this protein is $4.5\;\text{kJ·mol}^{-1}\text{M}^{-1}$, calculate $\Delta G_{\text{unfold}}^{\circ}(\text{H}_2\text{O})$.
>   (iii) At the *midpoint* of the transition ($[Urea] = C_m$), what is the value of $\Delta G_{\text{unfold}}$? Why?

**Model Solution (Part a — 7 Marks):**

The five major forces are:

1. **Hydrophobic effect** — burial of non-polar groups in the protein core, releasing ordered water. This is the **largest contributor** to the stability of globular proteins at room temperature (typically $\sim 50$ – $60\%$ of the total stabilizing free energy for a small protein).
2. **Hydrogen bonding** — between backbone atoms and side chains. Each individual H-bond contributes only $\sim 2$ – $8\;\text{kJ·mol}^{-1}$, but the network cooperativity makes their *cumulative* effect substantial.
3. **Van der Waals (London dispersion) interactions** — weak individually (~0.4 kJ·mol⁻¹) but numerous; very important in the densely packed hydrophobic core.
4. **Electrostatic interactions (salt bridges)** — strong when formed, but the desolvation penalty often cancels much of the gain. Important on protein surfaces, less so in the core.
5. **Disulfide bridges** — covalent S–S bonds, providing $\sim 100$ – $200\;\text{kJ·mol}^{-1}$. Important in *extracellular* proteins (e.g., antibodies, insulin), absent in cytoplasmic proteins due to the reducing environment.

**Why hydrophobic effect dominates at room temperature:**

- The entropic gain from releasing structured "clathrate" water around non-polar side chains is large at moderate $T$ because the *temperature factor* $T$ in $-T\Delta S_{\text{solvent}}$ is large.
- At higher $T$, water is already disordered, so the entropic gain shrinks — this is why hydrophobic stabilization is *weaker* at high $T$ (one reason for heat denaturation).
- The other forces are *enthalpic* and largely temperature-independent; the hydrophobic effect is the only *entropically* driven major contributor, which gives it a unique $T$-dependence.

**Model Solution (Part b — 7 Marks):**

**(i) Estimate $C_m$:**

The native baseline is $\sim -125$ mdeg and the unfolded baseline is $\sim -5$ mdeg. The midpoint of the transition is at signal:

$$S_{1/2} = \frac{-125 + (-5)}{2} = -65\;\text{mdeg}$$

From the data, signal $-72\;\text{mdeg}$ is at $[Urea] = 3.0\;\text{M}$ and signal $-40\;\text{mdeg}$ is at $4.0\;\text{M}$. Linear interpolation:

$$C_m \approx 3.0 + 1.0 \times \frac{(-72) - (-65)}{(-72) - (-40)} = 3.0 + \frac{-7}{-32} = 3.0 + 0.22 \approx 3.22\;\text{M}$$

**(ii) Calculate $\Delta G_{\text{unfold}}^{\circ}(\text{H}_2\text{O})$:**

Using the **linear extrapolation method**:

$$\Delta G^{\circ}(\text{H}_2\text{O}) = m \times C_m = 4.5\;\text{kJ·mol}^{-1}\text{M}^{-1} \times 3.22\;\text{M} = 14.5\;\text{kJ·mol}^{-1}$$

This is a typical small-protein value.

**(iii) At $[Urea] = C_m$:**

At the chemical midpoint, the protein is 50% folded and 50% unfolded, so $K_{\text{eq}} = 1$ and:

$$\Delta G(C_m) = -RT \ln(1) = 0$$

This is the direct thermodynamic reason that $C_m$ is called the *midpoint* — the entire free-energy gap between $N$ and $U$ is exactly balanced by the denaturant.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Mixing up the baselines** in (i) — using the wrong native/unfolded signal can move $C_m$ by 1 M or more.
> 2. **Forgetting the unit of $m$** in (ii) — the answer is in kJ·mol⁻¹ *only if* $m$ is in kJ·mol⁻¹·M⁻¹. Mixing units (e.g., J·mol⁻¹·M⁻¹) is the most common arithmetic error.
> 3. **Stating $\Delta G = 0$ at $C_m$ without justification** in (iii) — you must invoke $K = 1 \Rightarrow \ln K = 0 \Rightarrow \Delta G = 0$ for full credit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Protein stability** = small net $\Delta G_{\text{unfold}}^{\circ}$ (20 – 65 kJ·mol⁻¹) = large opposing $\Delta H$ and $T\Delta S$ terms.
- **Five stabilizing forces:** Hydrophobic effect (largest at 25 °C) > H-bonds > van der Waals > electrostatics > disulfides (only extracellular).
- **One destabilizing force:** Conformational entropy of the polypeptide chain (largest opposing term).
- **Two-state model** assumption must be explicitly stated in every derivations question.
- **Master equations (top 6 for KTU):**
  1. $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$
  2. $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$
  3. $K = [U]/[N]$, $f_U = K/(1+K)$
  4. $\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ}(\text{H}_2\text{O}) - m[D]$
  5. $\Delta G^{\circ}(\text{H}_2\text{O}) = m \cdot C_m$
  6. Gibbs–Helmholtz: $\Delta G(T) = \Delta H_m(1 - T/T_m) - \Delta C_p[(T_m - T) + T \ln(T/T_m)]$
- **Cold denaturation** is entropically driven: the $T$ factor in $T\Delta S$ shrinks at low $T$, so the unfavourable conformational entropy of folding dominates.
- **Heat denaturation** is enthalpically driven: breaking the H-bonds, hydrophobic contacts, etc. at high $T$ outweighs the entropic gain of unfolding.
- **Temperature of maximum stability** $T_S$ comes from $\partial \Delta G/\partial T = 0$; formally $T_S > T_m$ for typical parameters.
- **Protein dynamics** spans $10^{-15}\;\text{s}$ (bond vibration) to $> 1\;\text{s}$ (folding/allostery).
- **Energy landscape** = "folding funnel": high-dimensional, biased toward the native basin.
- **m-value** is proportional to the change in solvent-accessible surface area on unfolding.
- **Always convert to Kelvin** when using any of the temperature-dependent formulas.
- **In chemical denaturation**, $\Delta G(C_m) = 0$ *by definition* — this is the basis of the LEM.
- **Common conceptual traps:** confusing stability with rigidity, mixing $K$ directions, mis-signing $\Delta C_p$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Membrane Transport
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT414 - BIOCHEMICAL THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Membrane Transport # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Membrane Transport** refers to the movement of solutes, ions, water, and macromolecules across a biological membrane (lipid bilayer with embedded proteins) through physical, chemical, or protein-mediated mechanisms. From a thermodynamic standpoint, membrane transport is analyzed in terms of the **electrochemical potential gradient** ($\Delta \tilde{\mu}$), the **Gibbs free energy change** ($\Delta G$), and the **driving forces** that determine whether a transport process is spontaneous (passive) or requires coupled energy input (active). The membrane acts as a selectively permeable barrier that: - **Maintains** non-equilibrium concentration gradients between intracellular and extracellular compartments. - **Facilitates** controlled flux of nutrients, ions, metabolites, and waste products. - **Couples** metabolic energy (ATP hydrolysis, light absorption, or ion gradients) to drive uphill transport via chemiosmotic logic. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Membrane transport is treated as a thermodynamic process where the direction and feasibility are governed by the sign of $\Delta G$ and the magnitude of the **electrochemical potential** difference across the membrane. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🌍 Imagine a **gated community** (the cell) surrounded by a **security wall** (the plasma membrane) with: 1. **Open gates** → Small neutral molecules (O₂, CO₂) and lipid-soluble substances pass freely by **passive diffusion** (like people walking through unlocked doors — no energy needed). 2. **Security check gates with guards** → Polar molecules like glucose use **facilitated diffusion** through carrier/channels (guards check ID cards — no energy spent, but selective). 3. **Turnstiles requiring tokens** → **Active transport** uses ATP energy to push substances *up* their gradient (you must pay a fee to enter the building against the crowd flow). 4. **Water slides** → **Osmosis** is water moving through aquaporins or directly across the lipid bilayer from a region of high water potential to low water potential (gravity slides you down — spontaneous, downhill). > [!NOTE] > **Core Takeaway:** Every transport process is, at its heart, a thermodynamic statement about whether $\Delta G < 0$ (spontaneous, passive) or $\Delta G > 0$ (non-spontaneous, requires coupled energy input). --- ## 1.3 Fundamental Driving Force — Electrochemical Potential For a charged species $i$ moving across a membrane, the relevant thermodynamic potential is the **electrochemical potential**: $$\tilde{\mu}_i = \mu_i^0 + RT \ln a_i + z_i F \psi$$ where: - $\mu_i^0$ = **standard chemical potential** of species $i$ (J·mol⁻¹) - $R$ = **universal gas constant** = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** - $T$ = **absolute temperature** (K) - $a_i$ = **activity** of species $i$ (dimensionless) - $z_i$ = **valence** (charge number) of the ion - $F$ = **Faraday's constant** = **96,485 C·mol⁻¹** - $\psi$ = **electrical potential** of the phase (V) The **driving force** for transport is the difference in electrochemical potential across the membrane: $$\Delta \tilde{\mu}_i = RT \ln \frac{a_i^{\text{in}}}{a_i^{\text{out}}} + z_i F (\psi_{\text{in}} - \psi_{\text{out}})$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > If $\Delta \tilde{\mu}_i < 0$, transport is **spontaneous** (downhill). If $\Delta \tilde{\mu}_i > 0$, it is **non-spontaneous** and requires energy coupling. --- ## 1.4 Classification Overview (KTU Module 4 Pillar) | Transport Mode | Energy Source | Gradient Direction | Protein Required? | $\Delta G$ Sign | |---|---|---|---|---| | Simple Diffusion | None | Downhill | No | $< 0$ | | Facilitated Diffusion | None | Downhill | Yes (carrier/channel) | $< 0$ | | Primary Active Transport | ATP hydrolysis | Uphill | Yes (pump) | $> 0$ (coupled to ATP) | | Secondary Active Transport | Ion gradient | Uphill | Yes (symporter/antiporter) | $> 0$ (coupled to $\Delta \tilde{\mu}$) | | Osmosis | None | Down ($\mu_{H_2O}$) | No (or aquaporin) | $< 0$ | | Endocytosis/Exocytosis | ATP | Bulk | Vesicular | Variable | # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fick's Laws of Diffusion — The Foundation of Passive Transport ### 2.1.1 Fick's First Law (Steady-State Flux) Across a thin membrane of thickness $d$ (m), the steady-state unidirectional flux $J$ (mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹) of a solute is: $$J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$$ where: - $D$ = **diffusion coefficient** (m²·s⁻¹) - $\frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ = **concentration gradient** (mol·m⁻⁴) - The negative sign indicates flux flows from high to low concentration. For a planar membrane separating two bulk phases of concentrations $C_1$ (outside) and $C_2$ (inside): $$J = \frac{D \cdot K \cdot (C_1 - C_2)}{d} = P \cdot (C_1 - C_2)$$ where $P = D \cdot K / d$ is the **permeability coefficient** (m·s⁻¹) and $K$ is the **partition coefficient** (lipid-water). ### 2.1.2 Fick's Second Law (Time-Dependent) For non-steady state, Fick's second law governs concentration evolution: $$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$ --- ## 2.2 Passive Diffusion (Simple Diffusion) **Mechanism:** Small non-polar molecules (O₂, CO₂, steroid hormones, H₂O in some cases) dissolve in the lipid bilayer and diffuse across down their concentration gradient. **Why it matters (engineering analogy):** A sponge absorbing ink from a high-concentration chamber to a low-concentration chamber — flow occurs spontaneously until equilibrium. **Condition for spontaneity (thermodynamic):** $$\Delta G = RT \ln \frac{C_2}{C_1} < 0 \quad \text{(iff } C_2 < C_1 \text{)}$$ --- ## 2.3 Facilitated Diffusion (Carrier-Mediated) **Mechanism:** Membrane proteins (channels or carriers) provide a low-energy pathway for polar molecules (glucose, amino acids, ions) that cannot dissolve in the lipid core. - **Channel proteins** form aqueous pores (e.g., aquaporins for water, K⁺ channels). - **Carrier proteins** bind substrate on one side, undergo a conformational change, and release it on the other side (e.g., GLUT1 glucose transporter). **Kinetics (Michaelis-Menten analog):** $$J = \frac{J_{\max} \cdot C}{K_m + C}$$ where $J_{\max}$ is the maximum flux and $K_m$ is the **Michaelis constant** (analog of binding affinity). This is **saturable** — a key differentiator from simple diffusion. --- ## 2.4 Active Transport ### 2.4.1 Primary Active Transport (Direct ATP Coupling) ATP-powered pumps couple the free energy of ATP hydrolysis ($\Delta G_{\text{ATP}} \approx -50$ to $-60$ kJ/mol under cellular conditions) to transport. $$\Delta G_{\text{total}} = \Delta G_{\text{ATP hydrolysis}} + \Delta G_{\text{transport}}$$ For a pump moving species $i$ against its gradient: $$\Delta G_{\text{transport}} = RT \ln \frac{C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}} + zF(\psi_{\text{out}} - \psi_{\text{in}})$$ **Example:** Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase hydrolyses 1 ATP to export 3 Na⁺ and import 2 K⁺ against steep gradients. ### 2.4.2 Secondary Active Transport (Ion-Gradient Coupled) The energy stored in an electrochemical gradient of one ion (e.g., Na⁺) drives the uphill transport of another species (e.g., glucose via SGLT1). - **Symport:** Both species move in the same direction. - **Antiport:** Species move in opposite directions. --- ## 2.5 Osmosis and Osmotic Pressure **Osmosis** is the net movement of water across a semipermeable membrane from a region of high water chemical potential to low water chemical potential. ### 2.5.1 van't Hoff Equation for Dilute Solutions $$\Pi = i \cdot C \cdot R \cdot T = n \cdot \frac{RT}{V}$$ where: - $\Pi$ = **osmotic pressure** (Pa) - $C$ = **molar concentration** of solute (mol·m⁻³) - $i$ = **van't Hoff factor** (number of particles per formula unit) - $n$ = **moles of solute** (mol) - $V$ = **volume of solution** (m³) ### 2.5.2 Water Chemical Potential Across a Membrane $$\Delta \mu_{H_2O} = \mu_{H_2O}^{\text{inside}} - \mu_{H_2O}^{\text{outside}} = RT \ln a_{H_2O}^{\text{in}} + V_m \Delta P - \Pi$$ For ideal dilute solutions, $a_{H_2O} \approx 1 - \bar{V}_w C_s$, leading to: $$\Delta \mu_{H_2O} \approx -RT \cdot \bar{V}_w \cdot \Delta C_s + \bar{V}_w \Delta P$$ where $\bar{V}_w$ is the **partial molar volume of water** ($\approx 18 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/mol). ### 2.5.3 Reverse Osmosis (Engineering Utility) When external pressure $P > \Pi$, water flows from low-water-potential (concentrated) to high-water-potential (dilute) side — this is the principle of **seawater desalination** plants. --- ## 2.6 Nernst Equation — Equilibrium Membrane Potential For a single ionic species at electrochemical equilibrium ($\Delta \tilde{\mu}_i = 0$): $$E = \psi_{\text{in}} - \psi_{\text{out}} = \frac{RT}{z_i F} \ln \frac{[i]_{\text{out}}}{[i]_{\text{in}}}$$ At $T = 310$ K (body temperature), using $\frac{RT}{F} \ln 10 \approx 61.5$ mV: $$E = \frac{61.5}{z_i} \log_{10} \frac{[i]_{\text{out}}}{[i]_{\text{in}}} \quad \text{(mV)}$$ --- ## 2.7 Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation For a membrane permeable to multiple ions (Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻), the steady-state membrane potential is: $$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{P_K [K^+]_{\text{out}} + P_{Na} [Na^+]_{\text{out}} + P_{Cl} [Cl^-]_{\text{in}}}{P_K [K^+]_{\text{in}} + P_{Na} [Na^+]_{\text{in}} + P_{Cl} [Cl^-]_{\text{out}}}$$ where $P_i$ are the **permeability coefficients** of each ion. --- ## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet 📋 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these 10 equations — they cover ~90% of KTU Module 4 numerical questions.** | # | Equation | Use Case | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\Delta \tilde{\mu}_i = RT \ln \frac{a_i^{in}}{a_i^{out}} + z_i F \Delta \psi$ | Driving force of ion transport | J·mol⁻¹ | | 2 | $J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | Fick's 1st law — diffusion flux | mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ | | 3 | $J = P (C_1 - C_2)$ | Permeation across membrane | mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ | | 4 | $J = \frac{J_{\max} C}{K_m + C}$ | Carrier-mediated saturation | mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ | | 5 | $\Delta G_{\text{transport}} = RT \ln \frac{C_2}{C_1} + zF\Delta\psi$ | Free energy of moving species | J·mol⁻¹ | | 6 | $\Pi = iCRT$ | van't Hoff osmotic pressure | Pa | | 7 | $\Delta G_{\text{ATP}} \approx -50$ to $-60$ kJ/mol | ATP hydrolysis under cellular conditions | kJ·mol⁻¹ | | 8 | $E = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[i]_{out}}{[i]_{in}}$ | Nernst equilibrium potential | V | | 9 | $V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{\sum P_i [i]_{out}}{\sum P_i [i]_{in}}$ | Goldman resting potential | V | | 10 | $\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$ | Fick's 2nd law — non-steady state | mol·m⁻³·s⁻¹ | --- ## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Applications | Field | Application | Transport Principle Used | |---|---|---| | **Biomedical Engineering** | Hemodialysis (artificial kidney) | Diffusion + ultrafiltration | | **Pharmaceutical** | Transdermal drug patches | Fickian diffusion across skin | | **Water Treatment** | Reverse osmosis desalination | Osmosis + applied pressure | | **Biofuel Cells** | Proton pumps in membranes | Active H⁺ transport | | **Neural Prosthetics** | Ion-selective electrodes | Nernst potential sensing | | **Food Processing** | Membrane filtration (micro/ultra) | Pressure-driven flow | # 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Fick's First Law from Kinetic Theory ### Goal Derive the macroscopic diffusion equation $J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ from microscopic random walk arguments. ### Step-by-Step **Step 1 — Setup the geometry:** Consider a thin slab at position $x$ of thickness $dx$ and cross-section $A$. Molecules undergo a 1D random walk with mean free path $\lambda$ and mean velocity $\bar{v}$ (from Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution). **Step 2 — Compute net flux by Jumps:** In time $\Delta t = \lambda / \bar{v}$, half the molecules at position $x - \lambda$ jump rightward into the slab, and half at $x + \lambda$ jump leftward. **Step 3 — Express number densities:** Number density at $x - \lambda$: $n(x - \lambda) = n(x) - \lambda \frac{\partial n}{\partial x}$ Number density at $x + \lambda$: $n(x + \lambda) = n(x) + \lambda \frac{\partial n}{\partial x}$ **Step 4 — Net flux (molecules crossing per unit area per unit time):** $$J = \frac{1}{2} \bar{v} \left[ n(x - \lambda) - n(x + \lambda) \right]$$ **Step 5 — Substitute Taylor expansions:** $$J = \frac{1}{2} \bar{v} \left[ \left( n - \lambda \frac{\partial n}{\partial x} \right) - \left( n + \lambda \frac{\partial n}{\partial x} \right) \right]$$ $$J = \frac{1}{2} \bar{v} \left[ -2\lambda \frac{\partial n}{\partial x} \right]$$ $$J = -\bar{v} \lambda \frac{\partial n}{\partial x}$$ **Step 6 — Convert number density to concentration and identify $D$:** Since $C = n / N_A$ (concentration in mol/m³) and $D \equiv \frac{1}{3} \bar{v} \lambda$ (3D kinetic theory): $$J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} \quad \blacksquare$$ > **Logic Note:** The negative sign is the mathematical statement of the 2nd law of thermodynamics — matter spontaneously disperses down its gradient. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: van't Hoff Osmotic Pressure Equation ### Goal Derive $\Pi = CRT$ from thermodynamic equilibrium of water chemical potential. ### Step-by-Step **Step 1 — Equilibrium condition:** At equilibrium, the chemical potential of water on both sides of the semipermeable membrane must be equal: $$\mu_{H_2O}^{\text{pure}} = \mu_{H_2O}^{\text{solution}} + \int_{0}^{\Pi} \bar{V}_w \, dP$$ The term on the right accounts for the extra pressure $\Pi$ applied to the solution side to halt osmotic inflow. **Step 2 — Chemical potential of water in solution:** $$\mu_{H_2O}^{\text{solution}} = \mu_{H_2O}^{0} + RT \ln a_{H_2O}$$ For an ideal dilute solution with mole fraction $x_{H_2O}$: $$\ln a_{H_2O} = \ln x_{H_2O} \approx -\frac{n_s}{n_w}$$ where $n_s$ = moles of solute, $n_w$ = moles of water. **Step 3 — Substituting and simplifying:** $$\mu_{H_2O}^{0} = \mu_{H_2O}^{0} + RT \ln x_{H_2O} + \bar{V}_w \Pi$$ $$0 = -RT \frac{n_s}{n_w} + \bar{V}_w \Pi$$ **Step 4 — Rearrange for $\Pi$:** $$\Pi = \frac{n_s}{n_w} \cdot \frac{RT}{\bar{V}_w}$$ **Step 5 — Recognize that $n_w \bar{V}_w \approx V$ (volume of solution):** $$\Pi = \frac{n_s}{V} RT = CRT \quad \blacksquare$$ > **Logic Note:** The van't Hoff equation is structurally identical to the ideal gas law — both arise from the entropy of mixing diluted over available volume. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Nernst Equation from Electrochemical Equilibrium ### Goal Derive the equilibrium membrane potential $E = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[i]_{out}}{[i]_{in}}$ from $\Delta \tilde{\mu}_i = 0$. ### Step-by-Step **Step 1 — Equilibrium condition:** At electrochemical equilibrium, no net flux occurs, and the total electrochemical potential difference vanishes: $$\Delta \tilde{\mu}_i = 0$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the general electrochemical potential expression:** $$0 = RT \ln \frac{a_i^{in}}{a_i^{out}} + z_i F (\psi_{in} - \psi_{out})$$ **Step 3 — Define membrane potential $E$:** $$E \equiv \psi_{in} - \psi_{out}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $E$:** $$z_i F E = -RT \ln \frac{a_i^{in}}{a_i^{out}} = RT \ln \frac{a_i^{out}}{a_i^{in}}$$ **Step 5 — Divide by $z_i F$:** $$E = \frac{RT}{z_i F} \ln \frac{a_i^{out}}{a_i^{in}}$$ **Step 6 — Approximate activities by concentrations (dilute limit):** $$E = \frac{RT}{z_i F} \ln \frac{[i]_{out}}{[i]_{in}} \quad \blacksquare$$ **Step 7 — Common form at $T = 310$ K using $\log_{10}$:** $$E = \frac{61.5 \text{ mV}}{z_i} \log_{10} \frac{[i]_{out}}{[i]_{in}}$$ > **Logic Note:** This is the foundation of neurophysiology — the resting potential of a neuron ($\approx -70$ mV) is a weighted sum of Nernst potentials for K⁺, Na⁺, and Cl⁻, given by the Goldman equation. --- ## 3.4 Derivative Roadmap — The Goldman Equation (Sketch) Starting from the constant-field assumption $E(x) = V_m \cdot (x/d)$ along the membrane, the flux equation for ion $i$: $$J_i = -P_i \cdot \frac{z_i^2 F^2 V_m}{RT} \cdot \frac{[i]_{in} - [i]_{out} \exp(-z_i F V_m / RT)}{1 - \exp(-z_i F V_m / RT)}$$ Setting total current $I = F \sum z_i J_i = 0$ at steady state and solving for $V_m$ yields the **GHK voltage equation** quoted in Section 2.7. --- ## 3.5 Numerical Solved Examples (KTU Pattern) ### Example 1: Osmotic Pressure Calculation **Problem:** A solution contains 0.1 mol/L NaCl at 25 °C. Compute the osmotic pressure. ($i = 2$ for NaCl) **Solution:** $$\Pi = i C R T = 2 \times (0.1 \times 10^3 \, \text{mol/m}^3) \times 8.314 \times 298$$ $$\Pi = 2 \times 100 \times 8.314 \times 298 = 495,512 \, \text{Pa} \approx 4.96 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa} = 4.89 \, \text{atm}$$ > ✅ **Answer:** $\Pi \approx 4.96 \times 10^5$ Pa $\approx$ 4.89 atm ### Example 2: Nernst Potential for K⁺ **Problem:** $[\text{K}^+]_{\text{in}} = 140$ mM, $[\text{K}^+]_{\text{out}} = 5$ mM, $T = 310$ K. Find $E_K$. **Solution:** $$E_K = \frac{61.5}{+1} \log_{10} \frac{5}{140} = 61.5 \times \log_{10}(0.0357)$$ $$E_K = 61.5 \times (-1.447) = -89.0 \, \text{mV}$$ > ✅ **Answer:** $E_K \approx -89$ mV (matches the textbook neuron value) ### Example 3: Free Energy of ATP-Driven Transport **Problem:** A pump moves Ca²⁺ from $[\text{Ca}^{2+}] = 10^{-7}$ M (cytoplasm) to $10^{-3}$ M (ER lumen) against a membrane potential of $+50$ mV. Compute $\Delta G$ per mole of Ca²⁺ at 310 K. **Solution:** $$\Delta G = RT \ln \frac{10^{-3}}{10^{-7}} + zF\Delta\psi$$ $$\Delta G = (8.314)(310) \ln(10^4) + (2)(96485)(0.050)$$ $$\Delta G = 2577.3 \times 9.210 + 9648.5 = 23,737 + 9,649 \approx 33,386 \, \text{J/mol}$$ > ✅ **Answer:** $\Delta G \approx +33.4$ kJ/mol (uphill; must be coupled to ATP hydrolysis $\Delta G \approx -50$ kJ/mol) # 📊 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Master Classification of Membrane Transport ```mermaid graph TD nodeA[Membrane Transport] --> nodeB[Passive Transport
No ATP] nodeA --> nodeC[Active Transport
Requires Energy] nodeB --> nodeD[Simple Diffusion
No protein] nodeB --> nodeE[Facilitated Diffusion
Channel or Carrier] nodeB --> nodeF[Osmosis
Water movement] nodeD --> nodeD1[Examples: O2, CO2, steroid hormones] nodeE --> nodeE1[Channel proteins: K+, Na+, Ca2+] nodeE --> nodeE2[Carrier proteins: GLUT1, amino acid transporters] nodeF --> nodeF1[Aquaporins or lipid bilayer] nodeC --> nodeG[Primary Active
Direct ATP coupling] nodeC --> nodeH[Secondary Active
Ion gradient coupling] nodeG --> nodeG1[Na+/K+ ATPase, Ca2+ ATPase, H+ ATPase] nodeH --> nodeH1[Symport: SGLT1 glucose-Na+] nodeH --> nodeH2[Antiport: Na+/Ca2+ exchanger] ``` > **Reading guide:** Root node `nodeA` branches into the two thermodynamic families (passive vs active), and each branch drills into molecular machinery. Engineers can map this onto reactor design: passive = spontaneous flow, active = pumped circulation. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Thermodynamic Logic of Transport Feasibility ```mermaid graph LR nodeStart[Species i in two compartments] --> nodeCheck1{Delta mu of i} nodeCheck1 --> nodeYes[Less than 0
Spontaneous] nodeCheck1 --> nodeNo[Greater than 0
Non-spontaneous] nodeYes --> nodePath1[Passive transport
Downhill] nodePath1 --> nodeOut1[Flux J greater than 0
No energy required] nodeNo --> nodePath2[Active transport required] nodePath2 --> nodeCouple1[Couple to ATP hydrolysis] nodePath2 --> nodeCouple2[Couple to ion gradient] nodeCouple1 --> nodeATP[Na/K ATPase] nodeCouple2 --> nodeIon[SGLT1 symporter] nodeATP --> nodeFinal[Net Delta G less than 0] nodeIon --> nodeFinal ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Coupled Transport Energy Balance ```mermaid graph TD nodeATP[ATP Hydrolysis
Delta G equals -50 kJ per mol] --> nodeCouple[Thermodynamic Coupling] nodeGradient[Ion Gradient Energy
Delta mu of Na+] --> nodeCouple nodeCouple --> nodePump[Conformational Change in Pump Protein] nodePump --> nodeResult[Solute moved against gradient] nodeResult --> nodeNewState[New steady state
with maintained gradient] ``` --- ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Fickian Diffusion Across a Membrane ```mermaid graph LR nodeC1[Compartment 1
Concentration C1 high] --> nodeMembrane[Membrane
Thickness d
Permeability P] nodeMembrane --> nodeC2[Compartment 2
Concentration C2 low] nodeMembrane --> nodeNote[Flux J equals P times C1 minus C2] nodeNote --> nodeSteady[At steady state J is constant] nodeSteady --> nodeFinal[Equilibrium when C1 equals C2] ``` > **Engineering mapping:** This is the same logic governing **heat conduction** (Fourier's law) and **electric current** (Ohm's law) — all three are linear constitutive laws driven by gradients. --- ## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Osmosis + Reverse Osmosis) ```mermaid graph TD nodeInput1[Dilute Side
Low solute C
High water potential] --> nodeMem[Semipermeable Membrane] nodeInput2[Concentrated Side
High solute C
Low water potential] --> nodeMem nodeMem --> nodeNatural[Natural Osmosis
Water flows dilute to concentrated] nodeMem --> nodeApplied[Reverse Osmosis
Apply P greater than Pi] nodeNatural --> nodeResult1[Volume increases on concentrated side] nodeApplied --> nodeResult2[Pure water extracted from concentrate
Desalination application] ``` > **Engineering utility:** **Reverse osmosis plants** in Kerala (e.g., coastal brackish-water treatment units) operate exactly on this principle — applied hydraulic pressure overcomes the osmotic pressure of seawater (~25 atm). # 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] *Define electrochemical potential and state its significance in membrane transport.* **Model Answer:** The **electrochemical potential** ($\tilde{\mu}_i$) of an ionic species $i$ is the sum of its **chemical potential** and the **electrical potential energy** per mole of charge: $$\tilde{\mu}_i = \mu_i^0 + RT \ln a_i + z_i F \psi$$ **Significance in membrane transport:** 1. It is the **fundamental thermodynamic driving force** governing the movement of charged species across a membrane. 2. The sign of $\Delta \tilde{\mu}_i$ across the membrane determines whether transport is **spontaneous** ($\Delta \tilde{\mu}_i < 0$, passive) or **non-spontaneous** ($\Delta \tilde{\mu}_i > 0$, requires energy input). 3. It unifies **concentration gradient** (chemical) and **membrane potential** (electrical) into a single quantity, enabling quantitative analysis of ion transport. 4. It is the basis of the **Nernst equation** and **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz equation**, which predict resting and action potentials in neurons. > **Word count: ~110 | Marks allocated: 3** --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024] *Distinguish between simple diffusion and facilitated diffusion.* **Model Answer:** | Feature | Simple Diffusion | Facilitated Diffusion | |---|---|---| | **Protein involvement** | None — direct passage through lipid bilayer | Requires channel or carrier protein | | **Selectivity** | Non-selective (size & polarity based) | Highly selective (specific binding) | | **Kinetics** | Linear with concentration gradient | Saturable — follows Michaelis-Menten kinetics | | **Competition** | No competitive inhibition | Susceptible to competitive inhibitors | | **Energy requirement** | None (passive) | None (passive) | | **Temperature dependence** | Low Q₁₀ (~1.3) | High Q₁₀ (~2-3) due to protein conformational changes | | **Example solutes** | O₂, CO₂, steroid hormones, small non-polar molecules | Glucose (via GLUT1), amino acids, ions (via channels) | | **Maximum rate** | No $J_{max}$ (unbounded) | Bounded by $J_{max} = V_{max}$ of transporter | > **Key takeaway:** Both are **passive** (downhill, $\Delta G < 0$) — the only energy is the thermal kinetic energy of molecules. The protein simply lowers the activation energy barrier. --- ## 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **KTU Pattern:** Each question has two sub-parts: (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students answer **either** OR option. --- ### 📌 Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] #### **Part (a) [7 Marks]: Derive the Nernst equation for the equilibrium membrane potential of an ionic species. A biological cell has intracellular K⁺ concentration of 140 mM and extracellular K⁺ concentration of 4 mM. Calculate the Nernst potential for K⁺ at body temperature (37 °C).** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — State the equilibrium condition:** At electrochemical equilibrium for ion $i$ across a permeable membrane, there is no net flux, and the electrochemical potential difference must vanish: $$\Delta \tilde{\mu}_i = 0$$ **Step 2 — Write out the full expression:** $$RT \ln \frac{[i]_{in}}{[i]_{out}} + z_i F (\psi_{in} - \psi_{out}) = 0$$ **Step 3 — Define membrane potential $E$ and solve:** $$E = \psi_{in} - \psi_{out} = \frac{RT}{z_i F} \ln \frac{[i]_{out}}{[i]_{in}}$$ **Step 4 — Derive the 61.5 mV constant at T = 310 K:** $$\frac{RT}{F} \ln 10 = \frac{8.314 \times 310}{96485} \times 2.303 = 0.0615 \, \text{V} = 61.5 \, \text{mV}$$ So the practical form is: $$E = \frac{61.5 \, \text{mV}}{z_i} \log_{10} \frac{[i]_{out}}{[i]_{in}}$$ **Step 5 — Plug in numerical values for K⁺ (z = +1):** $$E_K = \frac{61.5}{+1} \log_{10} \frac{4}{140} = 61.5 \times \log_{10}(0.02857)$$ $$E_K = 61.5 \times (-1.544) = -94.97 \, \text{mV}$$ > ✅ **Final Answer:** $E_K \approx -95$ mV. (Note: actual neuron resting is ~-90 mV because K⁺ is not at perfect equilibrium — small Na⁺ permeability shifts it.) --- #### **Part (b) [7 Marks]: What is osmosis? Derive the van't Hoff equation for osmotic pressure of a dilute solution. State its engineering significance.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Define osmosis:** Osmosis is the **net movement of solvent (water) molecules across a semipermeable membrane** from a region of higher water chemical potential (dilute solution) to a region of lower water chemical potential (concentrated solution). **Step 2 — Equilibrium condition:** At equilibrium, water chemical potential on both sides is equal, which requires a hydrostatic pressure $\Pi$ (osmotic pressure) to be applied on the concentrated side: $$\mu_{H_2O}^{0} = \mu_{H_2O}^{0} + RT \ln x_{H_2O} + \bar{V}_w \Pi$$ **Step 3 — Apply ideal solution approximation:** $$RT \ln x_{H_2O} \approx -RT \frac{n_s}{n_w}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $\Pi$:** $$\Pi = \frac{n_s RT}{n_w \bar{V}_w}$$ **Step 5 — Use $n_w \bar{V}_w \approx V$ (volume of solution):** $$\boxed{\Pi = CRT} \quad \text{(van't Hoff equation)}$$ **Step 6 — Engineering significance:** - **Hemodialysis:** Patients with kidney failure undergo dialysis where the osmotic gradient drives waste removal across a synthetic semipermeable membrane. - **Reverse osmosis desalination:** Seawater (~0.6 M NaCl) exerts $\Pi \approx 29$ atm. Applying $P > \Pi$ forces pure water out, producing drinking water — a key technology for Kerala's coastal regions. - **Food preservation:** High-salt or high-sugar environments create hypertonic conditions that dehydrate microbial cells, preventing spoilage. - **Pharmaceutical formulations:** Osmotic pressure is critical in IV fluid design (isotonic saline, Ringer lactate) to prevent cell lysis or crenation. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often forget to multiply by the **van't Hoff factor $i$** for dissociable solutes (NaCl, CaCl₂). For NaCl at low concentration, $i \approx 2$. Marks deducted: **-1 per instance**. --- ### 📌 Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - June 2024] *Alternative Choice* #### **Part (a) [7 Marks]: Explain Fick's laws of diffusion. A membrane of thickness 10 nm has a permeability coefficient of $10^{-5}$ cm/s for a drug. The external concentration is 1 mM and internal is 0.1 mM. Calculate the steady-state flux in mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — State Fick's First Law:** $$J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = P (C_1 - C_2)$$ For a membrane of thickness $d$ with partition coefficient $K$: $$P = \frac{D \cdot K}{d}$$ **Step 2 — State Fick's Second Law (time-dependent):** $$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$ **Step 3 — Convert units:** - $P = 10^{-5}$ cm/s $= 10^{-7}$ m/s - $C_1 - C_2 = 1 - 0.1 = 0.9$ mM $= 0.9 \times 10^{-3}$ mol/m³ $= 9 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/m³ **Step 4 — Compute flux:** $$J = P \cdot \Delta C = 10^{-7} \times 9 \times 10^{-4} = 9 \times 10^{-11} \, \text{mol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$$ > ✅ **Final Answer:** $J = 9 \times 10^{-11}$ mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ **Step 5 — Engineering interpretation:** This is the basis of **transdermal drug delivery** (e.g., nicotine patches, hormone patches). The flux sets the dosing rate. To increase $J$, one can: - Increase $P$ (chemical penetration enhancers) - Increase $\Delta C$ (saturate the patch) - Decrease $d$ (micronization, microneedles) --- #### **Part (b) [7 Marks]: Discuss the Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) equation and explain how it differs from the Nernst equation. Why is the resting potential of a neuron ~-70 mV and not equal to $E_K$?** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — State the GHK equation:** $$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{P_K [K^+]_{out} + P_{Na} [Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl} [Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K [K^+]_{in} + P_{Na} [Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl} [Cl^-]_{out}}$$ **Step 2 — Differences from Nernst equation (markdown table):** | Feature | Nernst Equation | Goldman Equation | |---|---|---| | **Number of ions** | Single ion | Multiple ions simultaneously | | **Permeability** | Implicit (assumed permeable) | Explicit (different $P_i$ for each) | | **Membrane potential** | Equilibrium potential for one ion | Weighted average resting potential | | **Use case** | Reversal potential in ion channels | Resting membrane potential prediction | | **Assumption** | Ion is at electrochemical equilibrium | Steady-state flux under constant field | **Step 3 — Numerical demonstration (typical neuron):** At 310 K with concentrations: $[K^+]_{in} = 140$, $[K^+]_{out} = 5$, $[Na^+]_{in} = 15$, $[Na^+]_{out} = 145$, $[Cl^-]_{in} = 4$, $[Cl^-]_{out} = 110$ (all in mM), and relative permeabilities $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} = 1 : 0.05 : 0.45$: $$V_m = 61.5 \log_{10} \frac{(1)(5) + (0.05)(145) + (0.45)(4)}{(1)(140) + (0.05)(15) + (0.45)(110)}$$ $$V_m = 61.5 \log_{10} \frac{5 + 7.25 + 1.8}{140 + 0.75 + 49.5} = 61.5 \log_{10} \frac{14.05}{190.25}$$ $$V_m = 61.5 \times (-1.131) = -69.6 \, \text{mV} \approx -70 \, \text{mV}$$ > ✅ **Final Answer:** $V_m \approx -70$ mV **Step 4 — Why not equal to $E_K$?** The neuronal membrane is **not exclusively permeable to K⁺**. Even a small permeability to Na⁺ ($P_{Na}/P_K \approx 0.05$) drives the membrane potential away from the K⁺ equilibrium ($E_K = -95$ mV) toward the Na⁺ equilibrium ($E_{Na} \approx +60$ mV). The resulting **weighted average** is ~-70 mV. The Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump continuously expels the leaked Na⁺ and recovers K⁺, maintaining the disequilibrium that powers the resting potential. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often claim the resting potential **equals** $E_K$. This is **incorrect** and will cost ~2 marks. The correct reasoning: the membrane has finite (small) Na⁺ permeability, and the pump maintains ion gradients, so the system is in **steady state**, not equilibrium. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > 📌 **Print this and stick it on your wall before the exam.** - ✅ **Electrochemical potential** $\tilde{\mu}_i = \mu_i^0 + RT \ln a_i + z_i F \psi$ is the master driving force for ALL membrane transport. - ✅ **Sign rule:** $\Delta \tilde{\mu}_i < 0$ → spontaneous (passive); $\Delta \tilde{\mu}_i > 0$ → non-spontaneous (active, needs energy). - ✅ **Fick's First Law:** $J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ (steady-state flux). - ✅ **Fick's Second Law:** $\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$ (time-dependent). - ✅ **Membrane permeability:** $P = DK/d$ (units: m/s). - ✅ **Carrier kinetics:** $J = \frac{J_{\max} C}{K_m + C}$ — saturable, follows Michaelis-Menten. - ✅ **Osmotic pressure (van't Hoff):** $\Pi = iCRT$ (Pa) — note factor $i$ for dissociable solutes. - ✅ **Nernst equation:** $E = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[i]_{out}}{[i]_{in}}$; at 310 K, $E = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10} \frac{[i]_{out}}{[i]_{in}}$ mV. - ✅ **Goldman equation:** weighted average for multiple ions using permeability coefficients. - ✅ **Resting potential (~-70 mV):** set by K⁺ permeability but shifted by small Na⁺ permeability; pump maintains gradient. - ✅ **ATP-driven transport:** couple $\Delta G_{\text{ATP}} \approx -50$ kJ/mol to overcome positive $\Delta G_{\text{transport}}$. - ✅ **Active transport types:** Primary (ATP-coupled) vs Secondary (ion-gradient coupled, symport/antiport). - ✅ **Reverse osmosis:** apply external $P > \Pi$ to reverse natural osmotic flow — basis of desalination. - ✅ **Key constants:** $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹, $F = 96,485$ C·mol⁻¹, $\frac{RT}{F} \ln 10 = 61.5$ mV at 37 °C. - ✅ **Remember to convert units:** mM ↔ mol/m³ multiply by $10^3$; atm ↔ Pa multiply by $1.013 \times 10^5$. > 🎯 **Top 3 high-yield exam traps:** > 1. Forgetting van't Hoff factor $i$ for ionic solutes. > 2. Confusing equilibrium potential (Nernst) with steady-state resting potential (Goldman). > 3. Sign error in $\Delta G$ — uphill transport has $\Delta G > 0$ and must be coupled to a more negative process.

topics.title: Protein Folding and Pathological Misfolding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT414 - BIOCHEMICAL THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Protein Folding and Pathological Misfolding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Protein Folding — The Fundamental Definition

**Protein folding** is the physical process by which a polypeptide chain synthesized on the ribosome (primary structure) spontaneously acquires its unique, functional, three-dimensional **native conformation** (tertiary structure) under physiological conditions. From a thermodynamic perspective, the native state corresponds to the global minimum of the Gibbs free energy surface accessible to the protein under the given solvent, pH, temperature, and ionic conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *"Protein folding is the spontaneous, thermodynamically driven self-organization of an unfolded polypeptide chain (U) into a unique, biologically active native conformation (N), governed primarily by the hydrophobic effect, hydrogen bonding, van der Waals interactions, and configurational entropy."*

The process is conventionally written as:

$$U \text{ (Unfolded)} \rightleftharpoons N \text{ (Native)}$$

with an associated **standard Gibbs free energy change** at physiological temperature (298 K):

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{fold} = \Delta H^{\circ}_{fold} - T \Delta S^{\circ}_{fold}$$

For a typical single-domain protein of ~100 residues, $\Delta G^{\circ}_{fold} \approx -20$ to $-50$ **kJ/mol** (i.e., a highly negative, spontaneous process).

---

## 1.2 The Conceptual Analogy — "Origami in a Hurry"

Imagine an extremely long, floppy telephone cord (≈ 300 nm long for a 300-residue protein) dropped into a tiny box (the cell). The cord has to find **one and only one** specific tangled shape so the phone works. This is the biological miracle called protein folding.

- The **telephone cord** = the unfolded polypeptide (random coil).
- The **box** = the cellular environment (aqueous solvent).
- The **one specific shape** = the native, functional 3D structure.
- The **"self-organizing" force** = the **hydrophobic effect** — nonpolar side chains "hate" water and collapse inward, like oil droplets in water.

Without this self-organization, the cell would be a tangled mess of useless polypeptide strings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter to engineers / biochemists?**
> Because every disease of "misfolding" — Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, CJD, Type II Diabetes, Cystic Fibrosis — is essentially a **thermodynamic failure**: the protein either fails to find its lowest-energy state, or it finds a *different* low-energy state (amyloid) that is biologically disastrous.

---

## 1.3 Pathological Misfolding — The Definition

**Pathological protein misfolding** refers to the deviation of a protein from its functional native conformation, leading either to **(a) loss of normal biological function** or **(b) gain of a toxic, aggregation-prone function**. The misfolded protein often self-assembles into insoluble, protease-resistant, β-sheet–rich fibrillar aggregates called **amyloids**.

$$N \text{ (Native)} \rightleftharpoons M \text{ (Misfolded)} \rightarrow (M)_n \text{ (Amyloid Aggregate)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — Amyloid Diseases share a Common Thermodynamic Theme:**
> In every amyloid disease, a normally soluble protein transitions into a **cross-β fibril** in which β-strands run *perpendicular* to the fibril axis. This state is kinetically trapped but thermodynamically *metastable*, and it represents a deep, off-pathway minimum on the folding free-energy landscape.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** | Universal thermodynamic constant |
| Physiological Temperature | $T$ | **298 K (25 °C)** or 310 K (37 °C) | Reference for biological ΔG |
| Avogadro's Number | $N_A$ | **6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹** | Converts molecular to molar scale |
| Boltzmann Constant | $k_B$ | **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J·K⁻¹** | Single-molecule energy scale |
| Typical $\Delta G_{fold}$ | — | **−20 to −50 kJ/mol** | Marginal stability (~5–13 $k_BT$) |
| Hydrogen Bond Energy | — | **2–5 kJ/mol** | Per bond in folded state |
| Hydrophobic Effect | $\Delta G_{hyd}$ | **−0.8 to −1.5 kJ/mol per Å²** | Per unit buried surface area |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** The net stability of a folded protein is **only 5–13 $k_BT$** — a tiny margin considering thousands of interactions are involved. This "marginal stability" explains why proteins are *vulnerable* — small environmental perturbations (heat, pH, mutation) can tip them into misfolding.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Thermodynamic Decomposition of Folding

The folding free energy can be decomposed into **enthalpic and entropic contributions**, and these can be further broken down into physical origins. The full thermodynamic signature of folding is:

$$\Delta G_{fold} = \Delta H_{fold} - T\Delta S_{fold}$$

where:

$$\Delta H_{fold} = \Delta H_{H\text{-bond}} + \Delta H_{vdW} + \Delta H_{elec} + \Delta H_{conf}$$

$$\Delta S_{fold} = \Delta S_{conf} + \Delta S_{hyd} + \Delta S_{RT} + \Delta S_{vib}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Counter-Intuitive Sign Convention (Favourite KTU Trick Question!):**
> - $\Delta S_{conf} < 0$ (chain loses conformational freedom on folding — *unfavourable*)
> - $\Delta S_{hyd} > 0$ (water gains entropy as hydrophobic groups are buried — *favourable*)
> - The **hydrophobic effect (ΔS_hyd)** is the **dominant driving force**, NOT hydrogen bonds!

---

## 2.2 Component-by-Component Analysis

### (a) Enthalpic Contributions ($\Delta H_{fold}$)

- **Hydrogen bonding ($\Delta H_{H\text{-bond}}$):** Intramolecular H-bonds in the protein core replace H-bonds with water. Net contribution is small (often slightly favourable) because protein-water H-bonds in the unfolded state are also strong.
- **Van der Waals ($\Delta H_{vdW}$):** Tight atomic packing in the core contributes favourably (negative $\Delta H$).
- **Electrostatic ($\Delta H_{elec}$):** Salt bridges contribute −5 to −20 kJ/mol per ion pair.

### (b) Entropic Contributions ($T\Delta S_{fold}$)

- **Conformational entropy ($\Delta S_{conf}$):** Always **negative** (folded chain has fewer accessible conformations). For a 100-residue protein, $T\Delta S_{conf} \approx +10$ to $+20$ kJ/mol (unfavourable for folding).
- **Hydrophobic entropy ($\Delta S_{hyd}$):** Always **positive** (release of ordered water molecules from hydrophobic surfaces into bulk). This is the **DRIVING FORCE**.
- **Rotational-translational entropy ($\Delta S_{RT}$):** Unfavourable (folded protein has reduced translational freedom), but small for macromolecules.
- **Vibrational entropy ($\Delta S_{vib}$):** Slightly favourable (folded state has more low-frequency modes).

### (c) Heat Capacity Change ($\Delta C_p$)

A unique signature of protein folding is a **large, negative heat capacity change**:

$$\Delta C_p = C_p(\text{folded}) - C_p(\text{unfolded}) < 0$$

- Typical value: $\Delta C_p \approx -4$ to $-8$ **kJ·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** per 100 residues.
- Origin: burial of hydrophobic surface area reduces solvent-accessible area, removing the high-$\Delta C_p$ water interface.
- Consequence: $\Delta H_{fold}$ is strongly **temperature-dependent** (Kirchhoff's law):

$$\Delta H(T) = \Delta H(T_H) + \Delta C_p (T - T_H)$$

This explains the characteristic **cold denaturation** of proteins: at low $T$, the $T\Delta S$ term becomes too small, and the **unfavourable $\Delta H$** (now less negative) wins.

---

## 2.3 Anfinsen's Thermodynamic Hypothesis (1972)

**Christian B. Anfinsen** demonstrated for **ribonuclease A** that:
1. All the information required for folding is contained in the **amino acid sequence**.
2. The native state is the **unique, kinetically accessible global free-energy minimum** under physiological conditions.
3. Denaturation is **reversible** if no covalent modifications (e.g., disulfide scrambling) occur.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anfinsen's Dogma in KTU terms:**
> "Sequence determines structure determines function."
> Nobel Prize: 1972 (Chemistry).

---

## 2.4 Levinthal's Paradox (1969)

Cyrus Levinthal estimated the conformational search time assuming each residue has 3 allowed torsional angles (gauche+, gauche−, trans). For a 100-residue protein:

$$\text{Number of conformations} = 3^{99} \approx 10^{47}$$

Assuming a search rate of $10^{13}$ conformations/sec (transition-state theory limit):

$$t_{search} = \frac{10^{47}}{10^{13}} = 10^{34} \text{ s} \approx 3 \times 10^{26} \text{ years}$$

> This is **billions of times the age of the universe**, yet real proteins fold in **milliseconds to seconds**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Resolution:** Proteins do NOT search randomly. Folding follows a **funnel-like, semi-ordered pathway** in which the conformational space collapses progressively. The **folding funnel** is a thermodynamic landscape, not a random search.

---

## 2.5 The Folding Funnel (Energy Landscape Theory)

The folding free-energy landscape is a multidimensional surface plotted as **internal free energy ($G$ or $E$) vs. conformational entropy ($S_{conf}$)**.

- The **top** of the funnel = unfolded ensemble (high $G$, high $S_{conf}$).
- The **bottom** = native state (low $G$, low $S_{conf}$).
- The **slope** provides the thermodynamic driving force.
- **Folding intermediates** (molten globule, pre-molten globule) correspond to local minima (kinetic traps) on the funnel walls.

The smoothness of the funnel determines folding speed:
- **Smooth, steep funnel** = fast folder (small, single-domain proteins).
- **Rugged funnel** = slow folder, prone to misfolding and aggregation.

---

## 2.6 Folding States on the Landscape

| State | Secondary Structure | Tertiary Structure | Compactness | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Unfolded (U)** | Absent / random coil | Absent | Expanded | 8 M urea-denatured RNase A |
| **Pre-Molten Globule (PMG)** | Partially formed | Loose tertiary | Partially compact | Acid-denatured α-lactalbumin |
| **Molten Globule (MG)** | Native-like (high α/β content) | Loose, fluctuating side chains | Compact | Acid-denatured cytochrome c |
| **Native (N)** | Fully formed | Rigid, well-defined | Compact | Folded RNase A |
| **Amyloid (A)** | Cross-β sheet | Quaternary fibril | Highly ordered, insoluble | Aβ fibrils in Alzheimer's |

---

## 2.7 Molecular Chaperones — Assisted Folding

Chaperones are proteins that **assist folding without contributing conformational information**. They prevent aggregation by binding exposed hydrophobic surfaces in partially folded intermediates.

### (a) Hsp70 System (ATP-dependent, ~70 kDa)

- Binds short hydrophobic peptide segments (~7 residues) of unfolded/partially folded proteins.
- ATP-bound state: open lid, fast binding/release.
- ADP-bound state: closed lid, stable binding.
- Co-chaperone: **Hsp40** (J-protein) stimulates ATP hydrolysis.

### (b) GroEL/GroES (Bacterial Chsp60 Chaperonin)

- Large barrel-shaped complex: 14 GroEL subunits (2 rings of 7) + 7 GroES subunits.
- Provides an **Anfinsen cage** — an isolated folding chamber that prevents aggregation of intermediates up to ~60 kDa.
- Cycles: substrate binding → ATP + GroES binding → encapsulation → 10 s folding cycle → release.

### (c) Hsp90

- Specialized for **signaling proteins** (kinases, steroid receptors, p53).
- Holds client proteins in a "ready-to-activate" conformation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermodynamic Role of Chaperones:**
> Chaperones do **not** change the final equilibrium ($\Delta G_{fold}$ of the native state) — they **lower the kinetic barrier** for folding by:
> (1) Preventing off-pathway aggregation,
> (2) Rescuing kinetically trapped intermediates,
> (3) Providing a protected folding chamber.

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta G_{fold} = \Delta H_{fold} - T\Delta S_{fold}$ | Folding free energy (spontaneous if $\Delta G < 0$) |
| 2 | $\Delta G = -RT \ln K_{eq}$ | Equilibrium constant for U $\rightleftharpoons$ N |
| 3 | $K_{eq} = [N]/[U]$ | Folding equilibrium constant |
| 4 | $\Delta H(T) = \Delta H(T_H) + \Delta C_p (T - T_H)$ | Kirchhoff's law for $\Delta H$ vs. T |
| 5 | $T_{m} = \Delta H(T_m)/\Delta S(T_m)$ | Melting (denaturation) temperature |
| 6 | $\Delta C_p < 0$ for folding | Heat capacity signature of folding |
| 7 | $P_{fold} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{\Delta G/RT}}$ | Fraction folded (2-state model) |
| 8 | $\Delta G_{hyd} \approx \gamma \cdot \Delta A_{SASA}$ | Hydrophobic free energy ($\gamma \approx 0.024$ kJ·mol⁻¹·Å⁻²) |
| 9 | $\Delta G_{conf} = -T\Delta S_{conf}$ | Conformational entropy penalty |
| 10 | $t_{fold} \propto \exp(\Delta G^{\ddagger}/RT)$ | Folding kinetics (Eyring-like) |

> [!NOTE]
> In all equations above, the pipe-style absolute value (e.g., $\vert \Delta G \vert$) has been replaced with the **LaTeX \vert** command to ensure safe rendering in markdown tables.

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering / Industrial Utility

- **Pharmaceutical industry:** Understanding amyloid aggregation kinetics helps design inhibitors for Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, and Type II Diabetes.
- **Biotherapeutics:** Monoclonal antibodies and insulin formulations must avoid aggregation during storage — controlled by protein folding thermodynamics.
- **Protein engineering:** Designing thermostable enzymes (e.g., Taq polymerase) by engineering additional salt bridges and hydrophobic core packing.
- **Biomaterials:** Engineered amyloid fibrils are used as **functional nanomaterials** — strong, biodegradable scaffolds for tissue engineering.
- **Cryo-EM / NMR:** Modern structure determination relies on understanding folding intermediates and dynamics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Two-State Folding Equilibrium

We start with the reversible folding reaction:

$$U \rightleftharpoons N$$

The equilibrium constant is defined as:

$$K_{eq} = \frac{[N]_{eq}}{[U]_{eq}}$$

By the fundamental thermodynamic relationship between free energy and the equilibrium constant:

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K_{eq}$$

Substituting the expression for $K_{eq}$:

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln \frac{[N]}{[U]}$$

Solving for the ratio of folded to unfolded populations:

$$\frac{[N]}{[U]} = e^{-\Delta G^{\circ}/RT}$$

The fraction of protein in the folded state, $f_N$, is:

$$f_N = \frac{[N]}{[N] + [U]} = \frac{K_{eq}}{1 + K_{eq}}$$

Substituting $K_{eq} = e^{-\Delta G^{\circ}/RT}$:

$$f_N = \frac{e^{-\Delta G^{\circ}/RT}}{1 + e^{-\Delta G^{\circ}/RT}} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{+\Delta G^{\circ}/RT}}$$

This is the **Boltzmann sigmoid** form, identical to the Fermi-Dirac distribution in physics. It tells us:

- If $\Delta G^{\circ} \ll 0$ (very negative) $\Rightarrow$ $f_N \approx 1$ (fully folded).
- If $\Delta G^{\circ} \gg 0$ (very positive) $\Rightarrow$ $f_N \approx 0$ (fully unfolded).
- If $\Delta G^{\circ} = 0$ $\Rightarrow$ $f_N = 0.5$ (50:50).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Melting Temperature ($T_m$) for Two-State Folding

At the melting temperature $T_m$, the protein is 50% folded and 50% unfolded, so $\Delta G(T_m) = 0$. From the fundamental equation:

$$\Delta G(T_m) = \Delta H(T_m) - T_m \Delta S(T_m) = 0$$

Therefore:

$$T_m = \frac{\Delta H(T_m)}{\Delta S(T_m)}$$

Now, using Kirchhoff's law (which accounts for the heat capacity change $\Delta C_p$):

$$\Delta H(T_m) = \Delta H(T_{ref}) + \Delta C_p (T_m - T_{ref})$$

$$\Delta S(T_m) = \Delta S(T_{ref}) + \Delta C_p \ln\left(\frac{T_m}{T_{ref}}\right)$$

Substituting into the $T_m$ equation:

$$T_m = \frac{\Delta H(T_{ref}) + \Delta C_p (T_m - T_{ref})}{\Delta S(T_{ref}) + \Delta C_p \ln(T_m / T_{ref})}$$

This implicit equation must be solved numerically (e.g., by Newton's method) for $T_m$. However, if $\Delta C_p$ is small, the simplified form is:

$$\boxed{T_m \approx \frac{\Delta H(T_{ref})}{\Delta S(T_{ref})}}$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Suppose at $T_{ref} = 298$ K, $\Delta H = -300$ kJ/mol and $\Delta S = -0.85$ kJ·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹. Then:

$$T_m \approx \frac{-300 \text{ kJ/mol}}{-0.85 \text{ kJ·mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}} = 353 \text{ K} = 80\,^\circ\text{C}$$

This is a typical $T_m$ for a small, stable globular protein.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Fraction Folded with $\Delta C_p$ Correction (Gibbs-Helmholtz)

The full Gibbs-Helmholtz equation with $\Delta C_p$ correction is:

$$\Delta G(T) = \Delta H(T_{ref}) - T \Delta S(T_{ref}) + \Delta C_p \left[ (T - T_{ref}) - T \ln\left(\frac{T}{T_{ref}}\right) \right]$$

**Derivation Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1:** Start with the Kirchhoff relations for $\Delta H(T)$ and $\Delta S(T)$:

$$\Delta H(T) = \Delta H(T_{ref}) + \int_{T_{ref}}^{T} \Delta C_p \, dT' = \Delta H(T_{ref}) + \Delta C_p (T - T_{ref})$$

$$\Delta S(T) = \Delta S(T_{ref}) + \int_{T_{ref}}^{T} \frac{\Delta C_p}{T'} \, dT' = \Delta S(T_{ref}) + \Delta C_p \ln\left(\frac{T}{T_{ref}}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute both into $\Delta G(T) = \Delta H(T) - T \Delta S(T)$:

$$\Delta G(T) = \left[\Delta H(T_{ref}) + \Delta C_p (T - T_{ref})\right] - T \left[\Delta S(T_{ref}) + \Delta C_p \ln\left(\frac{T}{T_{ref}}\right)\right]$$

**Step 3:** Expand:

$$\Delta G(T) = \Delta H(T_{ref}) + \Delta C_p (T - T_{ref}) - T \Delta S(T_{ref}) - T \Delta C_p \ln\left(\frac{T}{T_{ref}}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Group the $\Delta C_p$ terms:

$$\Delta G(T) = \Delta H(T_{ref}) - T \Delta S(T_{ref}) + \Delta C_p \left[ (T - T_{ref}) - T \ln\left(\frac{T}{T_{ref}}\right) \right]$$

This is the final Gibbs-Helmholtz form with $\Delta C_p$ correction. The bracketed term can also be written as:

$$(T - T_{ref}) - T \ln\left(\frac{T}{T_{ref}}\right) = T_{ref} - T - T \ln\left(\frac{T_{ref}}{T}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter?** This equation predicts **both heat denaturation and cold denaturation**. The folded state is stable only in a finite temperature range between the cold-denaturation temperature $T_{cold}$ and the hot-denaturation temperature $T_{hot}$, where $\Delta G(T) < 0$. Outside this range, $\Delta G > 0$ and the protein unfolds.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Stability Curve $\Delta G(T)$ and the Stability Window

The function $\Delta G(T) = \Delta H_{ref} - T \Delta S_{ref} + \Delta C_p [T - T_{ref} - T \ln(T/T_{ref})]$ has a **maximum** at the temperature of maximum stability, $T_S$, where:

$$\left(\frac{\partial \Delta G}{\partial T}\right)_{T_S} = 0$$

Differentiating with respect to $T$:

$$\frac{\partial \Delta G}{\partial T} = -\Delta S_{ref} + \Delta C_p \left[ 1 - \ln\left(\frac{T_S}{T_{ref}}\right) - 1 \right] = -\Delta S_{ref} - \Delta C_p \ln\left(\frac{T_S}{T_{ref}}\right)$$

Setting this to zero:

$$\Delta S_{ref} = -\Delta C_p \ln\left(\frac{T_S}{T_{ref}}\right)$$

Solving for $T_S$:

$$T_S = T_{ref} \cdot \exp\left(\frac{\Delta S_{ref}}{-\Delta C_p}\right) = T_{ref} \cdot \exp\left(\frac{-\Delta S_{ref}}{\Delta C_p}\right)$$

Since $\Delta S_{ref} < 0$ and $\Delta C_p < 0$, the ratio $(-\Delta S_{ref}/\Delta C_p) > 0$, so $T_S > T_{ref}$ is guaranteed.

For example, with $\Delta S_{ref} = -0.85$ kJ·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹, $\Delta C_p = -5$ kJ·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹, and $T_{ref} = 298$ K:

$$T_S = 298 \cdot \exp\left(\frac{0.85}{5}\right) = 298 \cdot \exp(0.17) = 298 \cdot 1.185 = 353 \text{ K}$$

This says maximum stability occurs near 353 K — but this is the heat-denaturation midpoint, demonstrating that the **stability maximum** and **denaturation midpoint** are *different* concepts (often conflated by students!).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Fraction Folded from DSC Data

In **Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC)**, the excess heat capacity $C_p^{ex}$ is measured as a function of T. The fraction folded is:

$$f_N(T) = 1 - \frac{\int_T^{\infty} C_p^{ex}(T') dT'}{\int_{T_{low}}^{\infty} C_p^{ex}(T') dT'}$$

Or equivalently, using the cumulative enthalpy:

$$f_N(T) = 1 - \frac{\Delta H(T)}{\Delta H_{total}}$$

This is the experimental observable compared with the theoretical fraction folded:

$$f_N^{theory}(T) = \frac{1}{1 + \exp(+\Delta G(T)/RT)}$$

A well-designed KTU numerical problem can ask the student to compute $f_N$ at a given temperature given $\Delta H$, $\Delta S$, and $\Delta C_p$.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Folding States

| Property | Unfolded (U) | Molten Globule (MG) | Native (N) | Amyloid (A) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\Delta G$ vs N | 0 (reference) | +5 to +15 kJ/mol | 0 (reference) | −20 to −60 kJ/mol (lower than N!) |
| $\Delta S_{conf}$ | High | Intermediate | Low | Very low |
| SASA (solvent-accessible) | 100% | 50–70% | 30–50% | ~20% (core) |
| Secondary structure | Random coil | Native-like | Native | Cross-β |
| Tertiary packing | None | Loose | Tight | Quaternary stack |
| Biological activity | None | None / partial | Full | None / toxic |
| Protease sensitivity | High | Intermediate | Low | Very low (resistant) |
| Aggregation propensity | High | High | Low | Defines state |
| Reversibility | Reversible | Often reversible | Reversible (Anfinsen) | Generally irreversible |

---

## 3.7 KTU Numerical Worked Example (Exam-Style)

**Problem:** A protein folds with the following thermodynamic parameters at 298 K: $\Delta H^{\circ} = -280$ kJ/mol, $\Delta S^{\circ} = -0.78$ kJ·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹, $\Delta C_p = -4.5$ kJ·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹. Calculate (a) $\Delta G^{\circ}_{fold}$ at 298 K, (b) $K_{eq}$, and (c) the fraction folded at 298 K. Take $R = 8.314 \times 10^{-3}$ kJ·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** $\Delta G^{\circ}_{fold} = \Delta H^{\circ} - T \Delta S^{\circ}$

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{fold} = (-280) - (298)(-0.78) = -280 + 232.44 = -47.56 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Part (b):** Using $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K_{eq}$:

$$\ln K_{eq} = \frac{-\Delta G^{\circ}}{RT} = \frac{47.56}{(8.314 \times 10^{-3})(298)} = \frac{47.56}{2.478} = 19.19$$

$$K_{eq} = e^{19.19} \approx 1.97 \times 10^{8}$$

This is an extremely stable protein — 200 million-fold preference for the native state.

**Part (c):** Fraction folded:

$$f_N = \frac{1}{1 + e^{+\Delta G/RT}} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-19.19}} = \frac{1}{1 + 4.66 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 1.000$$

> The protein is essentially 100% folded at 298 K, with a tiny unfolded population of ~5 nanomolar in a micromolar solution.

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Diagram 1: The Folding Funnel (Energy Landscape)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    top1["U Unfolded Ensemble\nHigh G, High S_conf\nExpanded coil\nMany conformations"]
    mid1["MG Molten Globule\nIntermediate G\nCompact, loose tertiary\nNative-like secondary"]
    pre1["N Intermediate\nLocal free energy minimum\nKinetic trap"]
    bot1["N Native State\nLowest G, Low S_conf\nUnique 3D structure\nFunctional"]
    off1["M Misfolded State\nOff-pathway minimum\nHydrophobic exposure\nAggregation-prone"]
    amy1["A Amyloid Fibril\nCross-beta structure\nHighly ordered, insoluble\nPathological"]

    top1 -->|Hydrophobic collapse| mid1
    mid1 -->|Side-chain packing| pre1
    pre1 -->|Final ordering| bot1
    mid1 -.->|Off-pathway| off1
    off1 -.->|Nucleation| amy1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagonal arrows represent the **thermodynamic downhill** direction. Dotted arrows represent **off-pathway, kinetically trapped** transitions leading to pathological states.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Block Architecture of the GroEL/GroES Chaperonin Folding Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GroEL_Barrel["GroEL 14-subunit barrel (2 rings x 7)"]
        direction TB
        R1["Upper Ring 7x GroEL\nSubstrate binding site\nHydrophobic apex residues"]
        R2["Lower Ring 7x GroEL\nSame architecture\nAnti-chaperone chamber"]
    end

    Cap["GroES 7-subunit cap\nDome structure\nSeals folding chamber"]

    Sub["Unfolded Polypeptide\nExposed hydrophobic segments\nAggregation-prone"]

    ATP["ATP + Mg2+\nHydrolysis drives cycle"]

    Folded["Natively Folded Protein\nReleased into cytosol"]

    Cycle["Folding Cycle\n~10 seconds per cycle\n10-20% yield per cycle"]

    Sub -->|"Binds hydrophobic apex"| R1
    ATP -->|"Drives conformational change"| R1
    R1 -->|"GroES docking"| Cap
    Cap -->|"Anfinsen cage forms"| Cycle
    Cycle -->|"Substrate release"| Folded
    Folded -->|"If misfolded, rebinds"| R1
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Amyloid Fibril Architecture and Cross-β Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Monomer["Misfolded Monomer"]
        M1["Cross-beta core\nIn-register beta strands\nStrands run perpendicular to fibril axis"]
    end

    Monomer -->|Stacking| Oligomer
    subgraph Oligomer["Oligomer (Nucleus)"]
        O1["2-5 monomers\nCytotoxic species\nPore formation in membranes\nMost pathogenic form"]
    end

    Oligomer -->|Elongation| Fibril
    subgraph Fibril["Mature Fibril"]
        F1["Thousands of monomers\n7-10 nm diameter\nBeta-sheet spine\nTwisted morphology"]
    end

    Fibril -->|Plaque deposition| Tissue
    subgraph Tissue["Tissue Pathology"]
        T1["Extracellular plaques or\nIntracellular inclusions\nDisease-specific\nAlzheimer, Parkinson, CJD"]
    end
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Protein Misfolding Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Nascent Polypeptide on Ribosome] --> B{Chaperone present?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Co-translational folding]
    B -- No --> D[Post-translational folding]

    C --> E{Hydrophobic collapse OK?}
    D --> E

    E -- Yes --> F[Native state]
    E -- No --> G[Misfolded intermediate]

    G --> H{Chaperone rescue?}
    H -- Yes --> F
    H -- No --> I{Concentration high?}

    I -- Low --> J[Off-pathway degradation\nby ubiquitin-proteasome]
    I -- High --> K[Nucleation and oligomerization]

    K --> L[Cytotoxic oligomers]
    K --> M[Mature amyloid fibrils]

    L --> N[Cell death, tissue damage]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Pathological disease state]
```

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5: Free Energy Profile of Cold and Heat Denaturation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Cold["Cold Denaturation Region"]
        C1["Low T, water ice-like structure\nHydrophobic effect weakens\nDelta H term dominates\nProtein unfolds"]
    end

    subgraph Stable["Stability Window"]
        S1["Delta G less than 0\nNative state is global minimum\nMarginal stability ~10 kT\nPhysiological temperature"]
    end

    subgraph Hot["Heat Denaturation Region"]
        H1["High T, T Delta S dominates\nConformational entropy wins\nProtein unfolds\nCooperative transition"]
    end

    subgraph Max["Maximum Stability Point"]
        M1["dDelta_G by dT = 0\nAt temperature T_S\nMost negative Delta G\nMarginal stability peak"]
    end

    Cold -->|Rising T| Max
    Max -->|Rising T| Stable
    Stable -->|Rising T| Hot
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visual Insight:** The folded state exists as the global minimum **only within a finite temperature window**. Outside this window, $\Delta G > 0$ and the protein is denatured. This explains why psychrophilic (cold-loving) and thermophilic (heat-loving) organisms have evolved proteins with shifted stability profiles.

---

## 4.6 Diagram 6: Functional Block Architecture of the Folding Quality Control System

```mermaid

    subgraph Q1["Translation Machinery"]
        RIBO["Ribosome\nSynthesizes polypeptide\nCotranslational folding begins"]
    end

    end

    end

    RIBO --> CHAP70
    RIBO --> CHRO
    CHAP70 --> CHAP60
    CHRO --> CHAP60
    CHAP60 --> Q4
    Q4 --> OK
    Q4 --> BAD
```
