## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Thermodynamic Cycle of DNA–Protein Binding

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Free Protein P aq"]
    D1["Free DNA D aq with condensed counterions and water"]
    Int["Dehydrated / Deionized Interface PD*"]
    PD1["Final Bound Complex PD aq"]
    P1 -->|deltaG hyd| Int
    D1 -->|deltaG pe| Int
    Int -->|deltaG int| PD1
    PD1 -->|deltaG conf coupled folding and bending| P1
```

This block diagram shows that the net free energy of binding is the *sum* of: dehydration ($\Delta G_{hyd}$), counterion release ($\Delta G_{pe}$), direct interaction ($\Delta G_{int}$), and conformational change ($\Delta G_{conf}$).

### 4.2 Free Energy Decomposition Bar Chart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Total deltaG bind = -50 kJ per mol"]
    A --> B1["deltaG int = -120 kJ per mol FAVORABLE"]
    A --> B2["deltaG hyd = +20 kJ per mol UNFAVORABLE"]
    A --> B3["deltaG pe = -25 kJ per mol FAVORABLE"]
    A --> B4["deltaG conf = +75 kJ per mol UNFAVORABLE penalty"]
```

This illustrates the **enthalpy–entropy compensation** idea: the *direct interaction* is highly favorable ($-120$ kJ/mol), but most of it is "spent" paying the *conformational* and *hydration* penalties, leaving a net of only $-50$ kJ/mol.

### 4.3 Hill Plot Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L["log Concentration of free Protein P"]
    Y["log Y divided by 1 minus Y"]
    L -->|slope equals nH| Y
    Y --> N1["nH = 1 independent sites"]
    Y --> N2["nH greater than 1 positive cooperativity"]
    Y --> N3["nH less than 1 negative cooperativity"]
```

### 4.4 Salt-Dependence (Record–Lohman) Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Salt["Monovalent salt concentration M+"]
    Kobs["log K observed"]
    Salt -->|slope equals number of ions released| Kobs
    Kobs --> Res1["Steep negative slope: many phosphate contacts"]
    Kobs --> Res2["Shallow slope: mostly non ionic contacts"]
```

### 4.5 Conceptual Architecture: Direct vs Indirect Readout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Recog["DNA Protein Recognition"]
    Recog --> Direct["Direct Readout"]
    Recog --> Indirect["Indirect Readout"]
    Direct --> D1["H bond donor acceptor pairing"]
    Direct --> D2["van der Waals shape complementarity"]
    Direct --> D3["Watson Crick edge recognition"]
    Indirect --> I1["DNA bending flexibility"]
    Indirect --> I2["Groove width variations"]
    Indirect --> I3["Sequence dependent deformability"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**  
Define the biochemical standard free energy change ($\Delta G^{\circ\prime}$). Why is the prime symbol used in the context of DNA–protein binding studies?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The biochemical standard free energy change $\Delta G^{\circ\prime}$ is the change in Gibbs free energy when reactants and products are at unit activity ($1$ M), **pH 7**, $298$ K, and $10^5$ Pa. **(1 mark)**
- The prime distinguishes the **biochemical** standard state (constant $\text{[H}^+\text{]} = 10^{-7}$ M) from the **chemical** standard state (constant $\text{[H}^+\text{]} = 1$ M, pH 0). **(1 mark)**
- In DNA–protein binding, the reacting species are often polyionic (DNA phosphate groups, charged side chains of Lys/Arg), so the proton activity must be fixed at pH 7 to keep $\Delta G^{\circ\prime}$ meaningful. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**  
What is meant by *cooperative binding* in DNA–protein interactions? Give one example.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- Cooperative binding refers to the phenomenon in which the binding of one protein molecule to a DNA site *changes* the affinity of subsequent binding events at neighboring sites. **(2 marks)**
- Example: the binding of the *lac* repressor (tetramer) to the auxiliary operator $O_2$ and primary operator $O_1$ of the *lac* operon, mediated by DNA looping, displays strong positive cooperativity with Hill coefficient $n_H \approx 2$. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Thermodynamics of Repressor–Operator Binding

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the standard free energy of binding and the dissociation constant. A transcription factor binds its operator with $K_d = 5 \times 10^{-11}$ M at $25^{\circ}$C. Calculate $\Delta G^{\circ\prime}$ and comment on the strength of binding. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the **Record–Lohman polyelectrolyte theory**, show that the salt-dependence of $K_{obs}$ can be used to determine the number of counterions released during binding. The observed binding constant of a protein–DNA complex is $10^{9}$ M⁻¹ at $50$ mM NaCl and $10^{7}$ M⁻¹ at $200$ mM NaCl. Estimate $n$. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

Starting from the reaction $P + D \rightleftharpoons PD$ with $K_a = 1/K_d$:

$$
\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -RT \ln K_a = RT \ln K_d
$$

Substitute values ($R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹, $T = 298$ K, $K_d = 5 \times 10^{-11}$ M):

$$
\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = (8.314)(298)\ln(5 \times 10^{-11}) \text{ J·mol}^{-1}
$$

$$
\ln(5 \times 10^{-11}) = \ln 5 + \ln 10^{-11} = 1.609 - 25.328 = -23.719
$$

$$
\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = 2478 \times (-23.719) \approx -58{,}780 \text{ J·mol}^{-1} = -58.8 \text{ kJ·mol}^{-1}
$$

**Comments:** $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -58.8$ kJ·mol⁻¹ is a strongly favorable, very tight binding — typical of high-affinity transcription factors (compare to random-sequence DNA: $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} \approx -20$ to $-30$ kJ·mol⁻¹). The $\sim 30$ kJ·mol⁻¹ *extra* stabilization represents the *specificity* of sequence recognition. **(7 marks distributed across derivation, numerical substitution, and interpretation.)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

Record–Lohman analysis starts with the equilibrium:

$$
P^{z+} + D^{N-} \rightleftharpoons [PD]^{(z-N)+} + n\,M^{+}
$$

The observed association constant in terms of bulk concentrations is:

$$
K_{obs} = \frac{[PD][M^+]^n}{[P][D]}
$$

Hence:

$$
\log K_{obs} = \log K_{int} + n \log [M^+]
$$

For the two data points:

$$
\log K_{obs}^{(1)} = \log(10^{9}) = 9 \text{ at } \log(0.050) = -1.301
$$

$$
\log K_{obs}^{(2)} = \log(10^{7}) = 7 \text{ at } \log(0.200) = -0.699
$$

The slope is:

$$
n = \frac{9 - 7}{-1.301 - (-0.699)} = \frac{2}{-0.602} \approx -3.32
$$

The magnitude $|n| \approx 3.3 \approx 3$ counterions released (the negative sign simply reflects the convention that $K_{obs}$ decreases with increasing salt). **(7 marks — equation setup 2, data analysis 3, interpretation 2.)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Cooperativity and Hill Analysis

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Pattern]**

**(a)** What is the Hill equation? Derive it from a binding isotherm. A dimeric protein binds to a 20-bp palindromic site with $K_d = 10^{-8}$ M. Calculate the fraction bound at $[P] = 10^{-8}$ M assuming (i) independent sites, and (ii) perfect cooperativity with $n = 2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a derivation how van 't Hoff analysis can be used to obtain $\Delta H$ and $\Delta S$ of a protein–DNA interaction. The binding constants at $20^{\circ}$C and $35^{\circ}$C are $5 \times 10^{9}$ M⁻¹ and $2 \times 10^{9}$ M⁻¹ respectively. Compute $\Delta H^{\circ\prime}$ and $\Delta S^{\circ\prime}$. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

The Hill equation for a macromolecule with $n$ equivalent binding sites is:

$$
Y = \frac{[P]^n}{K_d + [P]^n}
$$

For a non-cooperative case ($n = 1$):

$$
Y_{(i)} = \frac{10^{-8}}{10^{-8} + 10^{-8}} = 0.5
$$

For perfect cooperativity ($n = 2$):

$$
Y_{(ii)} = \frac{(10^{-8})^2}{(10^{-8})^2 + 10^{-8}} = \frac{10^{-16}}{10^{-16} + 10^{-8}} \approx 10^{-8}
$$

**Interpretation:** With perfect cooperativity at $[P] = K_d$, the fractional saturation drops nearly to zero — the *Hill model* predicts ultrasensitive (switch-like) behavior. Real biological systems (e.g., *lac* repressor) lie between $n = 1$ and $n = 2$.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

From $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ and $\Delta G = -RT \ln K_a$:

$$
\ln K_a = -\frac{\Delta H}{R}\cdot\frac{1}{T} + \frac{\Delta S}{R}
$$

At $T_1 = 293.15$ K, $K_{a1} = 5 \times 10^{9}$:

$$
\ln K_{a1} = \ln 5 + 9 \ln 10 = 1.609 + 20.723 = 22.332
$$

At $T_2 = 308.15$ K, $K_{a2} = 2 \times 10^{9}$:

$$
\ln K_{a2} = \ln 2 + 9 \ln 10 = 0.693 + 20.723 = 21.416
$$

The van 't Hoff equation in two-point form:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{K_{a2}}{K_{a1}}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)
$$

Compute the right-hand prefactor:

$$
\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{308.15} - \frac{1}{293.15}
$$

$$
= 0.003245 - 0.003411 = -1.66 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}
$$

And the left side:

$$
\ln(K_{a2}/K_{a1}) = \ln(2/5) = \ln 0.4 = -0.9163
$$

Therefore:

$$
-0.9163 = -\frac{\Delta H}{R}(-1.66 \times 10^{-4})
$$

$$
\Delta H = \frac{0.9163 \times 8.314}{1.66 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 45{,}900 \text{ J·mol}^{-1} \approx +45.9 \text{ kJ·mol}^{-1}
$$

Now $\Delta S$ from $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ at $T_1$:

$$
\Delta G_1 = -RT_1 \ln K_{a1} = -(8.314)(293.15)(22.332) = -54{,}440 \text{ J·mol}^{-1}
$$

$$
\Delta S = \frac{\Delta H - \Delta G_1}{T_1} = \frac{45{,}900 - (-54{,}440)}{293.15} = \frac{100{,}340}{293.15} \approx 342 \text{ J·mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}
$$

**Interpretation:** $\Delta H^{\circ\prime} = +45.9$ kJ·mol⁻¹ (unfavorable, endothermic) is *overcome* by a large favorable $\Delta S^{\circ\prime} = +342$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹. This is a classic **entropy-driven binding**, dominated by the release of water and counterions. **(7 marks — derivation 3, calculation 3, interpretation 1.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall / Mark-Loss Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse $\log$ with $\ln$.** $K_a$ calculations use $\ln$, but $pH$, $pK_a$ use $\log_{10}$. Mismatched bases are an instant 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Always quote $\Delta G^{\circ\prime}$ with the prime.** A "$\Delta G$" without the prime in a DNA-binding problem is a partial-loss-of-mark error in KTU valuation.
> 3. **van 't Hoff plot requires $\Delta C_p = 0$.** If the data show curvature, the simple two-point form is *invalid* — students must mention $\Delta C_p$.
> 4. **Salt-dependence slope sign:** The slope is *negative* ($\log K_{obs}$ vs $\log [M^+]$) but $n$ (the *number* of ions) is reported as a positive integer. Don't write a negative "n" in the final answer.
> 5. **Don't forget units.** kJ·mol⁻¹ for $\Delta G$, $\Delta H$; J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ for $\Delta S$. Unitless $K_a$ is a KTU-red-flag for careless work.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -RT \ln K_a = RT \ln K_d$ — the *master equation* for binding energetics. **(HIGH PRIORITY)**
- Always use the **biochemical** standard state: pH 7, $298$ K, 1 M species.
- $K_d$ values for tight DNA-binders span $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-12}$ M, giving $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} \approx -50$ to $-70$ kJ·mol⁻¹.
- **Record–Lohman theory:** $\log K_{obs} = \log K_{int} + n \log [M^+]$; slope = $-n$ = number of counterions released.
- **Hill equation:** $Y = \dfrac{[P]^n}{K_d + [P]^n}$; $n_H > 1$ → positive cooperativity.
- **van 't Hoff plot:** $\ln K_a$ vs $1/T$ gives $\Delta H$ from slope, $\Delta S$ from intercept.
- **Enthalpy–entropy compensation** is the rule, not the exception; net $\Delta G$ is the small difference of large opposing terms.
- **Three major experimental methods:** ITC (gives $\Delta H$ directly), EMSA (gives $K_d$), SPR (kinetics + $K_d$).
- **DNA deformation** (bending, kinking) costs $\Delta G_{conf}$ and is paid out of $\Delta G_{int}$ — a key feature of *indirect readout*.
- **Specific vs non-specific binding:** Specific binding has $\Delta G_{int}$ much more negative (extra H-bonds), while non-specific relies on electrostatics.
- **Molecular crowding** (in vivo) *enhances* binding affinity by 10–100×, an effect often omitted in textbook analysis but vital in cellular context.
- **Salt bridge / phosphate contact number ≈ counterion release number** — this is the central experimental check on any proposed binding geometry.
- **Heat capacity change $\Delta C_p \neq 0$** signals a large negative $\Delta S_{hyd}$ contribution from ordered water release.
- **For KTU Part B derivations**, show every algebraic step and quote *all* constants ($R$, $T$, $K_d$) explicitly — partial-credit relies on visible working.
